**June 24-27, 2025**
**Chattanooga, Tennessee**

**FIFTY-SECOND GENERAL ASSEMBLY ACTIONS**

**SENT DOWN TO PRESBYTERIES**

Proposed amendments to _BCO_ :
8-4, 20-1, 21-1, 21-12, and 23-1; 12-3; 15-5.c; 18-2;

24-1 (two separate amendments); 31-10; 35-9;

36-4; 36-5; 36-8 and 42-2; 41-3; 57-2

June 22-26, 2026
Louisville, Kentucky

**FUTURE GENERAL ASSEMBLIES:**

June 14-18, 2027
Milwaukee, Wisconsin

June 26-30, 2028

Dallas, Texas

Double underlining in Committee of Commissioners (CoC) recommendations
indicates that the Committee of Commissioners added or changed the wording
from the original Permanent Committee or Agency Recommendation. Where
the original Permanent Committee or Agency Report has proposed a change
in the _BCO_ or _RAO_, that change is indicated by single underlining.

PART IV

MINUTES OF THE FIFTY-SECOND

GENERAL ASSEMBLY

**First Session—Tuesday Evening**

**June 24, 2025**

52-1 Assembly Called to Order and Opening Worship

The Fifty-Second General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America
gathered for the opening worship service at 6:30 p.m. on Tuesday, June 24, 2025,
at the Chattanooga Convention Center in Chattanooga, Tennessee. Outgoing
Moderator, RE Steve Dowling, called the Assembly to order for worship and
observance of the Lord’s Supper.

Following worship, at which RE Dowling gave an exhortation on Revelation 3:1422, the Assembly recessed at 8:05 p.m. to reconvene in 15 minutes.

52-2 Assembly Reconvened and Declaration of Quorum and Enrollment

The Assembly reconvened at 8:25 p.m. At the Moderator’s request, Mr. Jonathan
Calloway briefed the Assembly on the use of the voting devices.

Upon receiving the enrollment numbers from the Provisional Stated Clerk John
Bise, the Moderator declared a quorum to be present with 1605 TEs and 609 REs
for a total of 2214 commissioners present.

52-3 Election of Moderator

The Moderator opened the floor for nominations for Moderator of the 52 [nd] General
Assembly. TE Jason Helopoulos **nominated** TE Kevin DeYoung. With no further
nominations, the Moderator declared nominations closed. TE Kevin DeYoung was
**elected** by acclamation.

TE DeYoung assumed the chair and addressed the Assembly briefly.

<a id="ga52-p18"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=20 printed_page=18 -->

RE Danny McDaniel, Chairman of the Administrative Committee, presented a
plaque to retiring Moderator Dowling in token of the Assembly’s appreciation for
his year of service as Moderator of the 51 [st] General Assembly.

The Provisional Stated Clerk gave some announcements.

52-4 Docket

On behalf of the Provisional Stated Clerk, the Moderator presented the second draft
of the Docket (p. 1337), which he declared **adopted** without objection.

52-5 Election of Clerks and Committee of Thanks

On the recommendation of the Provisional Stated Clerk to the Moderator, TE Paul
L. Bankson, TE Hoochan Paul Lee, and TE Kenneth A. McHeard were **elected** as
recording clerks without objection.

On the recommendation of the Provisional Stated Clerk to the Moderator, TE
Matthew Bradley was **elected** timekeeper/assistant clerk without objection.

**OVERTURES COMMITTEE**

**PARTIAL REPORT**

**Recommendations:**
34. That **Overture 34** from Savannah River Presbytery (“Amend _RAO_ 16 to Require
Reporting on Session and Diaconate Membership and Duties”) be answered in
the **affirmative as amended** . **93-32-1**

**Be it resolved** the Savannah River Presbytery respectfully overtures the 52 [nd]
General Assembly of the PCA to protect the constitutional practice of ordaining
a Diaconate by adding the following to _RAO_ 16-4.c.1 (single underlining and
single strikethrough for original overture; double underline and double strike
through for OC amendment):

- A current Directory of Presbytery, including (1) a list of all teaching
elders, with their addresses and their status; and of all churches and
missions with the address of the church, and the name and address of
the Clerk of Session, (2) the list of persons who comprise the Session
and the Diaconate of each church arranged by their ordained or given
titles; and if no Diaconate exists, a report from the Session as to why it
has not been established and whether or not the duties have devolved
upon the ruling elders, according to _BCO_ 9-2 unordained diaconal
assistants, or any other committee or member of the church, or
whether they are being assisted by unordained diaconal assistants, or
any other committee or member of the church, (23) a listing of all
officers and committee members of the regular committees of
presbytery, and (34) a list identifying all candidates under care,

<a id="ga52-p21"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=23 printed_page=21 -->

interns, and licentiates of the presbytery with their addresses and their
status.

**Be it further resolved** to add the following to _RAO_ 16-6.c.1:

1) Exceptions of substance: Apparent violations of the Scripture or serious
irregularities from the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in
America, actions out of accord with the deliverances of the General
Assembly, and matters of impropriety and important delinquencies,
and any non-compliance with _RAO_ 16-3.e.5-6 and _RAO_ 16-4.c.1
should shall be reported under this category.

43. That **Overture 43** from Calvary Presbytery (“Amend _RAO_ 4-11 regarding Data
on Age and Ethnicity”) be answered in the affirmative as amended. **76-28-3**

**Be it resolved** that Calvary Presbytery hereby overtures the 52 [nd] General
Assembly to amend _RAO_ 4-11 by adding the following to the third paragraph
(single underline and single strikethrough for original overture; double underline
and double strike through for OC amendment):

**4-11.** The budget for each permanent Committee and Agency shall be
submitted to the Administrative Committee of General Assembly, which
shall independently evaluate the proposed budget of each permanent
Committee and Agency and report to the Assembly its considered opinion
on the adoption. To protect the ministry and fiduciary responsibilities of the
Committees and Agency Boards, the financial coordination and
independent evaluation of the budgets by the Administrative Committee
shall be for review, discussion, and recommendation to the Assembly.

Should modifications in budgets be deemed necessary by the Assembly,
special care shall be taken that changes not be made in such a way as to
threaten the continuity or effectiveness of the Committee’s or Agency’s
ministry. Budgets of permanent Committees and Agencies that are agreed
upon by the Administrative Committee may be changed only by a twothirds vote of the Assembly commissioners present and voting at the time
the budget is submitted for adoption. In the event the Administrative
Committee disagrees with the budget submitted by one of the Committees
or Boards, in whole or any item thereof, the General Assembly may adopt
either the Committee’s or Board’s budget or the Administrative Committee’s
recommended budget by simple majority. The requirement of a two-thirds
vote of the General Assembly applies only to changes not recommended
by the Administrative Committee, initiated on the floor of the Assembly.
With respect to the Committees and Agencies, financial coordination is
through the Administrative Committee to the Assembly and from the
General Assembly to the Committees and Agencies. The Administrative
Committee is a “filter upward” and has no downward authority.

The Administrative Committee shall annually provide and publish
guidelines for the Partnership Share Giving Program of the PCA, including
the Ministry Ask. These guidelines shall include statistical information

<a id="ga52-p22"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=24 printed_page=22 -->

expressed both in per capita giving terms and in proportional (percentage)
giving terms to demonstrate the giving necessary to attain full funding for
budgets of the General Assembly Committees and Agencies. Such statistical
information shall not include data pertaining to age or ethnicity.

The Committees and Agencies receiving support through the PCA
Partnership Share Giving Program shall present their financial needs and
their Ministry Ask to the Administrative Committee for review and
recommendation in the course of the regular budgeting process. Each
Committee and Agency shall then be free to request from the PCA churches
and constituency the contributions necessary to fund its approved budget.

While all churches remain free to give their contributions as they by God’s
guidance determine, all churches are encouraged to give, as they are able,
at least ten percent (10%) of their tithes and offerings to the PCA causes of
presbyteries and General Assembly.

The Administrative Committee shall maintain a manual containing the
Affirmation of Core Financial Standards. This information, by which each
PCA General Assembly ministry (Committee or Agency) shall function and
by which the budget process is governed for each Committee and Agency,
shall be available to all constituencies.

Respectfully submitted,
/s/ RE Melton L. Duncan, Chairman /s/ TE Eddie Lim, Secretary

TE Daniel Jarstfer raised a **POINT OF ORDER** that the Assembly exceeded the order
of the day to adjourn. The Moderator ruled the point of order **not well taken** as the
time in the docket was an aspirational time and not an order of the day.

(Appendix D, p 267), including a video highlighting the work of the CDM. TE
Estock invited TE Alan Strange (OPC), the President of the Board of Great
Commission Publications to address the Assembly. The CoC Chairman presented
the CoC report (below).

**R** **ECOMMENDATIONS** **2-5** **adopted** in gross without objection

**R** **ECOMMENDATION** **1** (approval of the minutes)
**adopted as amended by the CoC** without objection

**R** **ECOMMENDATION** **6** (thanking the staff) **adopted** without objection

**R** **ECOMMENDATION** **7** (election of coordinator)
**adopted** without objection

**R** **ECOMMENDATION** **10** (2026 budget) was **referred** to the Committee of

Commissioners on the Administrative Committee ( _RAO_ 14-6.j).

**R** **ECOMMENDATIONS** **8, 9, and 11-13** (advice on overtures) were
**referred** to the Overtures Committee.

The CoC Chairman closed the report in prayer.

**REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE OF COMMISSIONERS ON**

**COMMITTEE ON DISCIPLESHIP MINISTRIES**

**I.** **Business Referred to the Committee**
A. CDM Permanent Committee Report
B. CDM Permanent Committee Minutes from:

September 12-13, 2024
March 13-14, 2025
May 20, 2025 (CoC Addition)
C. CDM 2025 Proposed Budget
D. 2024 CDM Audit
E. CDM Permanent Committee Recommendations

**II. Statement of Major Issues Discussed**

A. CDM Permanent Committee Report
B. CDM Permanent Committee Minutes from:

September 12-13, 2024
March 13-14, 2025

<a id="ga52-p32"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=34 printed_page=32 -->

May 20, 2025 (CoC Addition)
C. CDM 2025 Proposed Budget
D. 2024 CDM Audit: In accordance with _RAO_ 14-7.h, the CoC notes that the
audit was received and all necessary actions on any recommendations are
being taken.
E. CDM Permanent Committee Recommendations

**III. Recommendations**

1. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the meetings of the
Permanent Committee for the Committee on Discipleship Ministries on
September 12-13, 2024 without exception, and approve the minutes of the
March 13-14, 2025 and the May 20, 2025 meetings of the Permanent
Committee for CDM with an exception of substance.

Exception: In both the March and May meetings of the Permanent
Committee there was a commission to review minutes that included
non-committee members. **40-1-0**
_adopted as amended by the CoC_
2. That the General Assembly encourage churches and individuals to
contribute generously to the “ **Love Gift Legacy** ” ( **pcacdm.org/wm-love-**
**gift** ). For 2024, CDM used the funds to develop and promote resources and
training for CDM's Women's Ministry International (WMI), which includes
partnerships with MTW and ministries such as the UK Partnership and City
to City Latin America. For 2025, CDM will join with Geneva Benefits and
use the funds to expand the “Wives of Elders” (WE) ministry. The expansion
will focus on providing counseling, training, and encouragement to wives
of elders in their unique role in the life of the church. **40-1-0**
_adopted_
3. That the General Assembly encourage individuals, local churches, and
Presbyteries to utilize the many free resources available on the CDM
websites ( **pcacdm.org** [especially resources for leaders];
**children.pcacdm.org** [children]; **women.pcacdm.org** [women];
**pcanextgen.org** [youth]; **pcacdm.org/ministries/older-adult-ministry**

[older adults]; **pcabookstore.com** ). These resources are developed by PCA
leaders for PCA leaders. **43-0-0**
_adopted_
4. That the General Assembly encourage local churches to consider and use
**Reachout** **Adventures** from CDM for Summer programming
( **reachoutadventures.com** ). CDM is working with PCA leaders to revise this
reformed and covenantal curriculum. The 2025 theme is _Upward Bound_,
which considers the journey of faith as presented in the Gospel of Luke.
The 2026 theme will be _Space Mission_, which explores the good news
found in Genesis. **40-2-0**
_adopted_
5. That the General Assembly encourage individuals and local churches to
use the excellent resources for all ages from Great Commission Publications
( **www.gcp.org** ), our official curriculum publisher. _Show Me Jesus_ is Biblebased, Christ-centered curriculum that teaches the unfolding story of
redemption from Genesis to Revelation and builds on God’s truth in ageappropriate ways. In fall 2025, GCP launches a new Preschool curriculum
for 4- and 5-year-olds is designed to:

<a id="ga52-p33"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=35 printed_page=33 -->

Spark Wonder: Creatively teaching Bible stories to discover our BIG
God.
Teach Heart to Heart: Discipling our kids in relationship—together
growing in the grace and knowledge of Christ.
Discover Jesus: Glorifying God forever as we discover the unfolding
story of salvation through Jesus.
This new _Show Me Jesus_ Preschool nurtures little hearts to build lifelong
faith in Christ. **43-1-0**
_adopted_
6. That the General Assembly give thanks to TE Tom Harr, Ms. Sherry
Kendrick (advisory), RE Lightsey Wallace, and RE Jeremy Whitley for their
faithful service as members of the Permanent Committee. **43-0-0**
_adopted_
7. That the General Assembly re-elect TE Stephen Estock to serve as the
Coordinator for the Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM). **44-0-0**
_adopted_
8. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly
answer **Overture 9** from James River Presbytery “Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to Make
AC Members Appointed by Committees and Agencies Non-voting Advisory
Members" in the **NEGATIVE** . **31-10-1**

_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:
CDM believes the overture is unnecessary and an unhelpful change to
the constitution. The rationale for the change (as indicated in the
“whereas” clauses) fails to adequately acknowledge the “grass roots”
nature of the current voting membership of the AC. The CDM
representative on the AC is elected by the permanent committee from
those who are elected by the General Assembly through the _BCO_ 14
nomination and election process. Ordinarily, the AC representative is
one of the CDM officers (elected by the committee) since those men
are most familiar with the breadth of CDM’s ministry. Moreover, the
proposed change disenfranchises the representatives of the committees
and agencies from voting on issues that directly affect the ministry of
the committee or agency (e.g., budgets and the docket of the General
Assembly), as well as participation on the PCA Board of Directors. The
current structure dates back to the 16 [th] General Assembly (1988) and
the work of an ad-interim committee chaired by RE W. Jack
Williamson. He, along with other forefathers of the denomination,
shaped the “grass roots” system mentioned in the Overture. Apart from
a specific reason that necessitates a change, it is unwise to depart from
the “grass roots” system our PCA forefathers delivered to us.
9. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly
answer **Overture 11** from South Texas Presbytery “To Study Incorporating
Bitcoin into Session, Presbytery, and Committee & Agency Balance Sheets"
in the **NEGATIVE** . **44-0-0**

_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:
The Overture is unnecessary and unwise. It is historically
unprecedented to direct the collective attention of the Church to a
specific investment—not just an investment category (cryptocurrency)
but a specific product or brand (Bitcoin)—to be included ina well

<a id="ga52-p34"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=36 printed_page=34 -->

balanced portfolio. Moreover, the U. S. Department of Labor
determined in 2022 that cryptocurrency investments fail to satisfy the
“prudent investor” rule, which is the standard used for fiduciary boards
in the United States. Finally, the study called for by the Overture could
distract the Church from its true mission to make disciples through the
proclamation of the gospel.
10. That the General Assembly approve the 2026 CDM budget as presented by
the Administrative Committee.
_referred to the CoC on the AC (_ RAO _14-6.j)_
11. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly
answer **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery “Erect Ad Interim
Committee to Receive Suggestions for Permanent Committee and Agency
Operations, Resources, and Expenses" in the **NEGATIVE.** **43-0-1**
_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:
The Overture is unnecessary. The annual budgets of every Committee
and Agency are reviewed thoroughly and audited annually ( _RAO_ 147h). Members of the permanent committee are elected by the
Assembly to exercise primary fiduciary responsibility for their
committee ( _RAO_ 4-11). The budget they approve is submitted to the
Administrative Committee, where it is examined by a subcommittee
and sent to the Assembly by the full committee. At the Assembly, the
Committee/Agency budget is reviewed by the Committee of
Commissioners for the Committee/Agency, as well as the Committee
of Commissioners for the Administrative Committee, before being sent
to the Assembly for approval. All of the budgets are included in the
initial Assembly Commissioner Handbook, thus providing every
commissioner an opportunity to review and offer questions to the
appropriate Committee of Commissioners. In the absence of evidence
that 1) the current procedures to provide transparency and
accountability are inadequate, or 2) the recommendations o fan ad
interim committee would result in greater support for the Committee
and Agencies, the ad interim committee described by the Overture
would not be a wise use of funds.
12. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly
answer **Overture 30** from Houston Metro Presbytery “Amend _BCO_ 8-4, 201, 21-1, 21-12, and 23-1 re the Calling and Dissolution of TE Relationships
for Needful Works" by **REFERRING** it back to Houston Metro Presbytery or
to the 53 [rd] General Assembly in 2026 without prejudice. **43-0-0**
_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds
The Overture calls for changes to the wording of many paragraphs in
the _BCO_, including the ordination vows of elders. As the
Administrative Committee noted, “[t]his Overture potentially affects
the legal employment conditions and relationships of TE personnel in
all Committee and Agencies, as well as the relationship of such men
to their Presbyteries—and the relationship of those Presbyteries and the
General Assembly to civil authorities.” The potential impact to the
operations of the Committees and Agencies demand more research
and advice from legal counsel to possibly refine changes before
sending them to the Presbyteries for advice and consent.

<a id="ga52-p35"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=37 printed_page=35 -->

13. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly
answer **Overture 49** from Pacific Northwest Presbytery “Erect an Ad Interim
Committee on AI" by **DEFERRING** action on this Overture to the 53 [rd]
General Assembly in 2026. **41-2-0**
_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds
The use of AI technology is growing exponentially. As with any
emerging trend, elders would benefit from careful reflection on the
theological and moral issues involved, including unintended
consequences. Several excellent books, papers, and even classes and
policies are already available to help members understand this
technology. More preparation would be helpful before forming an Ad
Interim Committee.

**REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE OF COMMISSIONERS ON**

**COVENANT COLLEGE**

**I.** **Business Referred to the Committee**
A. CC Report to General Assembly
B. CC Permanent Committee Minutes from:

August 1 2024, October 17-18, 2024; March 20-21, 2025
C. CC 2023 Audit
D. CC 2025-2026 Proposed Budget
E. CC Permanent Committee Recommendations

<a id="ga52-p37"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=39 printed_page=37 -->

**II. Statement of Major Issues Discussed**

A. CC Report to General Assembly
B. CC Permanent Committee Minutes from:

August 1 2024, October 17-18, 2024; March 20-21, 2025
C. CC 2023 Audit: In accordance with _RAO_ 14-7.h, the CoC notes that the
audit was received and all necessary actions on any recommendations are
being taken.
D. CC 2025-2026 Proposed Budget
E. CC Permanent Committee Recommendations

**III.** **Recommendations**
1. That the General Assembly thank and praise God for the excellent work
and faithfulness of the Board of Trustees, faculty, and staff of Covenant
College in serving the Presbyterian Church in America by shaping students
for lives of service in the Kingdom of God. **46-0-0**
_adopted_
2. That the General Assembly encourage congregations of the PCA to support
the ministry of Covenant College through encouraging prospective students
to attend, through contributing the Partnership Shares approved by the
General Assembly, and through prayer. **46-0-0**
_adopted_
3. That the proposed budget for 2025–26 for Covenant College be approved.
_Referred to the CoC on the AC (_ RAO _14-6.j)_
4. That the minutes of the Board of Trustees of Covenant College for August
1, 2025, October 17-18, 2024, and March 20-21, 2025 be approved
without exception. **45-0-1**
_adopted_
5. That the General Assembly receive as information the foregoing Annual
Report, recognizing God’s gracious and abundant blessing and
commending the College in its desire to continue pursuing excellence in
higher education for the glory of God. **46-0-0**
_adopted_
6. That the General Assembly designate Sunday, October 19, 2025, as a
Lord’s Day on which churches of the denomination are encouraged to
highlight Covenant College's ministry and pray specifically for the College
in its mission and ministry. **46-0-0**
_adopted_
7. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly
answer **Overture 9** from James River Presbytery “Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to Make
AC Members Appointed by Committees and Agencies Non-voting Advisory
Members" in the **NEGATIVE** with reference to the recommendation of the
Administrative Committee. **29-14-3**

_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:
In addition, Covenant College notes that the Administrative
Committee’s “unique role as a service committee to the General
Assembly and the entire denomination” ( _RAO_ 5-4) includes the
responsibility for evaluation and reporting its opinion to the General
Assembly of the budgets of each permanent Committee and Agency.
( _RAO_ 4-11) That important administrative function requires a clear

<a id="ga52-p38"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=40 printed_page=38 -->

understanding of each of the proposed budgets which understanding
requires close coordination and input from representatives of the
permanent Committees and Agencies, acting as full participating and
voting members of the Administrative Committee.
8. That **Overture 11** from South Texas Presbytery, “To Study Incorporating
Bitcoin into Session, Presbytery, and Committee & Agency Balance Sheets,”
be answered in the **NEGATIVE** with reference to the recommendation of
the Administrative Committee. **46-0-0**

_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:
The courts, permanent Committees and Agencies of the denomination
have the responsibility and right to independently consider and
evaluate the appropriateness of any legal investment vehicle that its
donors and supporters desire to utilize. To call out not only one type
of investment vehicle but one specific type of a class of investment
(i.e., Bitcoin is a single example of the broader class of
cryptocurrencies) is not necessary and unduly limits the consideration
of appropriate investment vehicles and strategies.
9. That **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery (“Erect Ad Interim
Committee to Receive Suggestions for Permanent Committee and Agency
Operations, Resources, and Expenses”) be answered in the **NEGATIVE** with
reference to the recommendation of the Administrative Committee.

**46-0-0**

_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:
An ad interim committee may only be erected to “handle particular
matters of business” pursuant to _RAO_ 9-1. Overture 27 provides the
proposed committee only very generalized direction with no clear
indication of expected outcomes or deliverable results. Rather, the
committee is instructed to solicit and receive recommendations that
are then to be “collated, evaluated, and reported” with no criteria for
such evaluation and reporting specified. Therefore, the Overture fails
to identify any “particular matters of business.” In addition, although
the receipt of the Overture was timely, it was received very close to
the deadline and does not provide Covenant College sufficient time to
fully consider having been submitted well after the last meeting of the
agency board prior to the 52 [nd] General Assembly.
10. That the 52 [nd] General Assembly **REFER** **Overture 30** back to Houston Metro
Presbytery or to the 53 [rd] General Assembly in 2026. **46-0-0**
_referred to the Overtures Committee_
11. That the 52 [nd] General Assembly **DEFER** action on **Overture 49** from Pacific
Northwest (“Erect ana d interim committee on AI”) to the 53 [rd] General
Assembly in 2026 with reference to the rationale provided by the
Committee on Discipleship Ministries. **46-0-0**
_referred to the Overtures Committee_

G. Overture 49

**III. Recommendations**

1. That the General Assembly give thanks to God for the ministry of Covenant
Theological Seminary; for its faithfulness to the Scriptures, the Reformed
faith, and the Great Commission; for its students, graduates, faculty, staff,
and trustees; and for those who support the Seminary through their prayers
and gifts. **44-0-0**
_adopted_
2. That the General Assembly encourage the congregations of the Presbyterian
Church in America to support the ministry of Covenant Theological
Seminary by contributing the Partnership Shares approved by the Assembly,
and by recommending Covenant Seminary to prospective students.
**44-0-0**
_adopted_
3. That the General Assembly ask the Lord’s blessing on Covenant Seminary’s
President, Rev. Dr. Thomas C. Gibbs, and grant him and the Seminary’s
leadership team, faculty, and Board of Trustees great wisdom, biblical
faithfulness, and clear vision as they lead the institution forward in training
fruitful pastors and other ministry leaders. **44-0-0**
_adopted_
4. That the General Assembly ask God to guide Covenant Seminary’s ongoing
efforts at recruiting new students, evaluating and strengthening our
programs, and seeking to make the Seminary a greater resource for the
church both locally and globally. **44-0-0**
_adopted_
5. That the General Assembly ask God’s blessing on the Seminary’s planning
and fundraising efforts, and on its attempts to recruit a new generation of
dedicated pastor-scholars to train new generations of leaders for Christ’s
church and kingdom. **44-0-0**
_adopted_
6. That the General Assembly praise God for his provision of faithful donors
and supporters who continue to give generously toward our mission, and
for the many ways that he has enabled Covenant Seminary’s students,
faculty, and staff to continue building effectively on the solid foundation
laid by our forebears in the faith. Praise him for the gift of resilient and
creative people who enable the institution to function so well during
challenging times. **44-0-0**
_adopted_
7. That the General Assembly pray for unity among the brethren of the PCA
and ask the Lord to work in all our hearts to foster a deeper desire to engage
with one another and the world in compassionate and gospel-centered
ways, and that we might bear strong witness to the truth and power of God’s
redeeming grace. **44-0-0**
_adopted_

<a id="ga52-p42"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=44 printed_page=42 -->

8. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the stated and called
meetings of the Seminary’s Board of Trustees and Executive Committee of
the Board of Trustees for 2024–2025 without exception as follows:

- _Stated Board Meetings:_ April 26, 2024; September 27, 2024;
January 24, 2025

- _Called Board Meetings:_ None

- _Stated Executive Committee Meetings:_ April 25, 2024; September
17, 2024; December 5, 2024; January 23, 2025

- _Called Executive Committee Meetings:_ January 9, 2025
**47-0-0**
_adopted_
9. That the proposed budget for fiscal year 2025–2026 for Covenant
Theological Seminary be approved.
_Referred to the CoC on the AC (_ RAO _14-6.j)_
10. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 9** from James River Presbytery
“Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to Make AC Members Appointed by Committees and
Agencies Non-voting Advisory Members” in the **NEGATIVE**, with reference
to the recommendation of the Administrative Committee. **33-12-3**

_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:
Covenant Theological Seminary recognizes the PCA has assigned the
Administrative Committee a special “role as a service committee to the
General Assembly and the entire denomination” ( _RAO_ 4-2; 5-4). This
service specifically includes the responsibility for “financial
coordination and independent evaluation of the budgets…for review,
discussion, and recommendation to the General Assembly” ( _RAO_ 411). Given this important administrative and fiduciary function it is
critical that the Administrative committee have a clear understanding
of each of the proposed budgets, which requires the close
coordination, input and expertise of the representatives of the
permanent Committees and Agencies, acting as full participating and
voting members of the Administrative Committee ( _RAO_ 4-11; 5-1).
11. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 11** from South Texas
Presbytery “To Study Incorporating Bitcoin into Session, Presbytery, and
Committee & Agency Balance Sheets” in the **NEGATIVE** with reference to
the recommendation of the Administrative Committee. **45-1-2**

_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:
The courts and Permanent Committees and Agencies of the PCA
already have the freedom and responsibility to consider and evaluate
the appropriateness of any legal investment that its constituents wish
to utilize. It is historically unprecedented to direct the collective
attention of the Church to a specific investment – not just an
investment category (cryptocurrency) but a particular product or brand
(Bitcoin) – to be included ina well-balanced portfolio, is not necessary,
and unduly limits the consideration of appropriate vehicles and
strategies.
12. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia
Presbytery “Erect Ad Interim Committee to Receive Suggestions for
Permanent Committee and Agency Operations, Resources and Expenses”

<a id="ga52-p43"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=45 printed_page=43 -->

in the negative with reference to the recommendation of Covenant College.
**46-0-0**

_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:
Covenant Theological Seminary further notes that prior to their
approval by the Assembly, the budgets of the Permanent Committees
and Agencies are reviewed and approved by multiple bodies
comprised of Assembly-elected representatives that are obligated by
certain fiduciary guidelines ( _RAO_ 4-11): Permanent Committee or
Agency Board, Committees of Commissioners (CoC) of said committee
or board, the PCA Administrative Committee (AC) and the CoC of the
AC, therefore, this overture is unnecessary. Similarly, Permanent
Committee and Agency actions are supervised by GA-elected
Committees and Boards and, then, approved through minutes
reviewed and approved annually by GA Committees of
Commissioners and the GA itself. Additionally, given that this overture
lacks a funding plan, which would require a 2/3 vote of the Assembly
for GA approval ( _RAO_ 9-3), its execution would be an unbudgeted
mandate for the Administrative Committee.
13. That **Overture 30** from Houston Metro Presbytery, “Amend _BCO_ 8-4, 201, 21-1, 21-12, and 23-1 re: the Calling and Dissolution of TE Relationships
for Needful Works” be deferred without prejudice until the 53 [rd] General
Assembly in 2026. **46-0-0**
_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:
This overture was received at a late date, after all but two of the
Permanent Committees and Agencies (CTS & AC) had held their stated
spring meetings and so were unable to offer an opinion, and for the
purpose of hearing from all Permanent Committees and Agencies so
that this overture, which potentially affects the legal employment
relationships of all, might be adequately considered, evaluated with
legal counsel, and perfected for service to the Church.
14. That **Overture 49** from Pacific Northwest Presbytery, “Erect an Ad Interim

Committee on AI” be answered in reference to the recommendation from
the Overtures Committee. **46-0-0**

_referred to the Overtures Committee_

TE David Coffin filed the following dissent.

Dissent ( _BCO_ 45-1, -2)

I dissent from the decision of the Assembly in the matter of the MNA report,
concerning the election of the Coordinator. The Assembly allowed debate in the
election, including debate that criticized the actions of the Coordinator. However,
such a recommendation is not to be debated under regular order, but is rather a
“nominating speech”, that must be considered under RONR, 46:27, -28, “Debate on
Nominations.”

As such, RONR, 46:28, sets forth the parameters of such debate: “…speakers must
exercise caution to avoid making any personal criticism of [candidates] in debate.
Rather than attacking a nominee, a speaker may advocate the election o fa rival
candidate.” However, as there were no rival candidates with respect to this election,
speakers should have exercised caution to avoid making any personal criticism of
the candidate for Coordinator. This failed to happen.

The Moderator reminded the Assembly that nominations from the floor were due
by the Assembly’s recess at 4:30 p.m. Provisional Stated Clerk John Bise gave his
advice that the provisions of _RAO_ 4-5 do not apply to Special Committees ( _RAO_
8).

Below is the RUF Permanent Committee's advice to the Overtures
Committee on four overtures referred to the RUF Permanent Committee for
its advice to the Overtures Committee. The CoC Chairman ruled that these

were not items of business for the RUF CoC. ( _RAO_ 11.5.b. All overtures
proposing amendment to the Constitution shall be referred to the Overtures
Committee. The Stated Clerk may also refer such overtures to other
Committees of Commissioners, other Permanent Committees or Agencies,
other ad interim or special committees _for advice only to the Overtures_
_Committee_ . [emphasis added])

4. That GA answer **Overture 9** from James River "Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to Make AC
Members Appointed by Committees and Agencies Non-Voting Advisory
Members” in the **NEGATIVE** .
5. That the GA answer **Overture 11** from South Texas "To Study Incorporating
Bitcoin into Committee and Agency Balance Sheets" be answered in the
**NEGATIVE** .
7. That the GA answer **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia (“Erect Ad Interim
Committee to Receive Suggestions for Permanent Committee and Agency
Operations, Resources, and Expenses”) in the **NEGATIVE** .
_8._ That the GA answer **Overture 30** from Houston Metro “to Amend _BCO_ 8-4, 201, 21-1, 21-12, and 23-1 re the Calling and Dissolution of TE Relationships for
Needful Works” by **DEFERRING** without prejudice until the 53 [rd] General
Assembly in 2026.

_referred to the Overtures Committee_

**RUF Permanent Committee's Grounds** **for answering Gulf Coast Overture 18 in the**
**Negative:**

- RUF does not use a "Campus Ministry Manual." The 1979 "manual" referenced
in the overture was a document drafted when ap ca campus ministry was i nits
infancy and under the auspices of the GA's Ministry to the US. It was primarily
initiated and run by local presbyteries. Twenty-four years ago, when RUM
became a GA Permanent Committee, maintaining or creating such a manual
was not required. If the GA had wanted such a manual, the logical time to have
codified such a requirement would have been in 2000 at the 28 [th] GA in Tampa
or in 2001 at the 29 [th] GA in Dallas, or at least shortly thereafter.

- There has never been a requirement, per se, for RUF to have such a manual.
This is clear from _RAO_ 4-21 "Guidelines for Keeping Minutes of Permanent
Committees and Agencies of the General Assembly" below.

j.An up-to-date copy of the bylaws and manual of the Committee or
Agency, _if such exist_, should be kept with the minutes. (emphasis added)

- Even if RUF used such a manual, the _RAO_ does not expect the GA to approve
its contents. That would require an _RAO_ amendment through a two-thirds vote
of the Assembly. It cannot be accomplished simply through an overture asking
the GA to "direct" a permanent committee to create and submit a manual for
review and approval. For example, the _RAO_ stipulates that the GA approves the
SJC Manual ( _RAO_ 17-5), and the _RAO_ contains operating rules for its Committee
on Review of Presbytery Records ( _RAO_ 16). However, there is no requirement,
for example, for ga to approve ana c Manual. _RAO_ 10-1 says “The Assembly’s
Administrative Committee and the Local Arrangements Committee shall operate

<a id="ga52-p52"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=54 printed_page=52 -->

under a Manual approved by the Administrative Committee.” The same applies
to the GA Nominating Committee, which uses a manual that does not require
GA approval.

- The _RAO_ already stipulates that the minutes of a Permanent Committee "shall
include":

A recording of information sufficient to demonstrate the Committee's or
Board’s implementation of instructions received from the General Assembly
and of _material_ policies and _material_ policy changes adopted by the
Committee or Board _in that year_ . ( _RAO_ 4-21.d.4; emphasis added)
The GA-elected RUF Permanent Committee believes the abovementioned _RAO_
4-21 paragraph is sufficient. Whenever the RUF Permanent Committee changes
various protocols, those actions are reported in Committee Minutes that are
reviewed annually by the GA Committee of Commissioners. And exceptions
taken to those minutes would be reported through that Committee of
Commissioners. Last year, the RUF Permanent Committee reported a
recommendation to the Assembly that the GA approve a revised Affiliation
Agreement. The RUF Permanent Committee will continue to adhere to the
obligations of _RAO_ 4-21.d.4 unreservedly.

- Requiring the RUF Permanent Committee to produce a lengthy operating
manual that must be reviewed and approved by an Assembly of over 2,000
commissioners would be unnecessary and unwieldy. it would beak into
requiring sessions to recommend annual church budgets, church employee
manuals, child protection procedures, etc. to the congregation for scrutiny and
approval by the members. That's why a congregation elects its elders. And that's
why permanent committee members are nominated by presbyteries, reviewed
by the GA Nominating Committee, and then elected by the Assembly.

<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=56 printed_page=54 -->

**REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE OF COMMISSIONERS ON**

**RIDGE HAVEN**

**I.** **Business Referred to the Committee**
A. RH Report to General Assembly
B. RH Board of Directors Minutes:

March 12-13, 2024
September 16-18, 2024
March 10-12, 2025
C. RH 2026 Proposed Budget
D. RH Permanent Committee Recommendations

**II. Statement of Major Issues Discussed**

A. RH Report to General Assembly
B. RH Board of Directors Minutes:

March 12-13, 2024
September 16-18, 2024
March 10-12, 2025
C. RH 2026 Proposed Budget
D. RH Permanent Committee Recommendations

**III. Recommendations**

1. That the proposed budget for 2026 for Ridge Haven be approved.
_referred to the CoC on the AC (_ RAO _14-6.j)_
2. That the minutes of the Board of Directors of Ridge Haven for March 1113, 2024, and September 16-18, 2024, be approved without exceptions.
**42-0-0**
_adopted_
3. That February 15, 2026, be a day for our churches to pray for the ministries
of Ridge Haven. **42-0-0**
_adopted_
4. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly
answer **Overture 9** from James River Presbytery, “Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to
Make AC Members Appointed by Committees and Agencies Non-voting
Advisory Members,” in the **NEGATIVE** . **38-1-3**
_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:
We believe that the overture fails to make its case that the current
membership of the AC undermines or is contrary to a grassroots
Presbyterianism. Every member of the AC, whether at-large or
appointed by the Committees and Agencies, has been duly elected by
the General Assembly.

We also fail to see how the disenfranchisement of nine members
of the AC, each of whom has been duly elected by the General
Assembly, better serves the corporate mission of our beloved church.
To the contrary, we believe this change fails to take into account the
vital importance of unity in our mission.
The unity of our mission governs the division of labor among our
committees and agencies. As _BCO_ 14-1.3 states, “The work of the
Church as set forth in the Great Commission is one work, being
implemented at the General Assembly level through equally essential

<a id="ga52-p55"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=57 printed_page=55 -->

committees.” Dr. Morton Smith highlights the priority of unity in his
commentary on this section of the _BCO_ when he states,

In this chapter of the Book, which provides for the
erection of several different committees of the Assembly
to assist it in carrying out the Great Commission, the
unity of the Church's work is affirmed. This unity is at
times overlooked as individuals get involved with one or
another aspect of the Church's work. Realizing this
danger, this affirmation is made, before a division of
labor is made at the Assembly level. To reinforce this
concept of the unity of the work of the Church, the last
part of the sentence asserts that the work of the Assembly
is to be implemented through equally essential
committees. This concept is all too often neglected, as
one committee is elevated above others in the minds of
the people. [1]

We believe that the unity of our mission is uniquely expressed in
the Administrative Committee in our current polity, both in the scope
of its work and in the makeup of its membership. To remove the voting
rights of those members appointed by the Committees and Agencies,
therefore, is to diminish the unity of our mission. It also serves, contrary
to the design of the Overture, to practically elevate the AC even further
above the other committees and agencies, whose budgets the AC

approves.
It should be noted that Dr. Smith, a champion of the grassroots
nature of the PCA, makes no critical comments in his commentary
regarding the makeup of the AC. While arguments from silence do not
carry much weight, Smith is explicit and firm in his concerns
elsewhere. This makes his silence regarding the AC slightly more
audible.
It also highlights what is of fundamental importance in our polity
with regard to committees and agencies. Among Dr. Smith’s stated
concerns in _BCO_ 14-1 is that the committees understand and operate
according to their relationship to the General Assembly. He rightly
states, with regard to _BCO_ 14-1.7, that “a committee is always the
servant of the appointing body,” i.e., the General Assembly. The
Overture implies that by allowing appointed members both voice and
vote, the AC is less directly accountable to the General Assembly. To
borrow Smith’s phrase, it renders the AC less of a “servant of the
appointing body.” But every member of the AC—whether at-large or
appointed—is accountable to the General Assembly. And each
appointed member is additionally accountable to the Committee or
Board that appointed him. Thus, while we are sympathetic with the
concern for undue power and influence on any committee, we are not
convinced of the nature of the problem outlined in the Overture, nor
of the solution offered.

1 Morton H. Smith, _Commentary on the Book of Church Order of the Presbyterian Church in America_, 6 th ed.,
(Taylors, SC: Presbyterian Press, 2007), 139.

<a id="ga52-p56"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=58 printed_page=56 -->

**5.** That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly
answer **Overture 11** from South Texas Presbytery, “To Study Incorporating
Bitcoin into Session, Presbytery, and Committee & Agency Balance Sheets”
in the **NEGATIVE** . **42-0-0**

_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:
This overture is not necessary and seems to lie outside the scope of the
mission entrusted to us. Further, the overture’s focus on one particular
investment/asset is peculiarly narrow.
6. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly
answer **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery, “Erect Ad Interim
Committee to Receive Suggestions for Permanent Committee and Agency
Operations, Resources, and Expenses” in the **NEGATIVE** . **42 -0-0**
_referred to the Overtures Committee_

Grounds:
Given that an Ad Interim Committee may only be erected to “handle
particular matters of business” ( _RAO_ 9-1); and, given that PCA
Committees and Agencies are already required to submit their annual
budgets to the Administrative Committee for evaluation and
recommendation to the General Assembly ( _RAO_ 4-11); and, given that
the description of the work of the proposed committee is expressed in
language which leaves many open questions; Therefore, the Ridge
Haven Board of Directors recommends that Overture 27 from
Northwest Georgia Presbytery be answered in the NEGATIVE.
7. Regarding **Overture 49** from Pacific Northwest Presbytery “Erect an Ad
Interim Committee on AI,” we believe this is a worthy topic of study, but
have no specific advice to the OC as to whether or not the PCA should
establish a study committee on it. **42-0-0**
_referred to the Overtures Committee_

**III. Recommendations**

1. That the General Assembly urge churches to set aside the month of
November 2025 as a month of prayer for global missions, asking God to
send many more laborers into His harvest field. (MTW will offer a 30 Days
of Prayer Calendar, which your church can download from mtw.org/30dop
in the fall as well as other resources). **49-0-1**
_adopted_
2. That the General Assembly urge churches to set aside a portion of their
giving for the suffering peoples of the world; to that end, be it recommended
that a special offering for relief and mercy (MTW Compassion offering) be
taken during 2025 and distributed by MTW (MTW offers bulletin inserts by
mail, as well as a digital version that can be downloaded at
mtw.org/compassion). **49-0-1**
_adopted_
3. That the General Assembly urge churches to set aside Sunday, November
2, 2025, as a day of prayer for the persecuted church worldwide. **49-0-1**
_adopted_
4. Having performed an annual review of our coordinator, we give all praise,
honor, and glory to God and thank Him for Dr. Lloyd Kim and his gracious,
humble, and effective servant-leadership. CMTW unanimously
recommends to the General Assembly that Dr. Lloyd Kim be re-elected
coordinator of Mission to the World. **49-0-1**
_adopted_
_5._ That the proposed budget of MTW, as presented through the Administrative
Committee, be approved; **50-0-0**
_referred to the CoC on the AC (_ RAO _14-6.j)_
6. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the meetings of the
Permanent Committee on Mission to the World dated March 13-14, 2024;
September 18-19, 2024; March 12-13, 2025; April 21, 2025; May 14, 2025
without exception. **50-0-0**
a _dopted as amended by the CoC_
7. That **Overture 9** from James River Presbytery regarding, “Amend _BCO_ 141 to Make AC Members Appointed by Committees and Agencies Nonvoting Advisory Members,” be answered in the **NEGATIVE**
**AFFIRMATIVE.** **29-21-0**

_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:

As the overture states, “the General Assembly’s Administrative
Committee exercises tremendous influence over the PCA’s national
operations…the preparation of budgets for permanent committees and
agencies; arranging for the General Assembly to meet each year…”
These functions directly affect committees and agencies. The present
system allows for committees and agencies to speak and vote on
matters that directly affect them. This overture would disenfranchise
committees and agencies.

<a id="ga52-p60"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=62 printed_page=60 -->

The committees and agencies not only vote to approve their
representatives to the AC, but also these representatives have all been
duly elected by the General Assembly to carry out the duties of the
committees and agencies, including (for some) representing the
committees and agencies on the Administrative Committee. Therefore,
in our current practice, 100% of the voting members serving on the AC
committee have been elected by the General Assembly. This overture
is trying to solve a problem that does not exist.

The CoC believes that Overture 9 provides a helpful corrective to
the current voting makeup of the AC. While it is vital that members of
the MTW Permanent Committee (and others) are present for
consultation, our CoC concluded that this can be achieved while at
the same time ensuring increased grassroots input and participation in
the deliberations of the AC.
8. That **Overture 11** from South Texas Presbytery regarding, “To Study
Incorporating Bitcoin into Session, Presbytery, and Committee & Agency
Balance Sheets,” be answered in the **NEGATIVE.** **49-0-1**
_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:
The overture is outside the purview of the General Assembly. The
study or not of a specific investment product (Bitcoin) is best
determined by each session, presbytery, committee and agency, not
the general assembly.
Secondly, this overture would require time and energy at every
level of the church that may be better used in carrying out the mission
of the church. These decisions should be left at the most local level
rather than dictated by the General Assembly.
9. That **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery regarding, “Erect Ad
Interim Committee to Receive Suggestions for Permanent Committee and
Agency Operations, Resources, and Expenses,” be answered in the
**NEGATIVE** . **49-0-1**

_referred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:

The ad interim committee proposed overture is not given clear
direction of expected outcomes or deliverable results, nor is it given
criteria on how solicited recommendations would be evaluated or
reported. According to RAO 9-1 ad interim committees may only be
erected to “handle particular matters of business”. The proposed
overture fails to meet RAO 9-1 requirements.

The ad interim committee proposal is redundant and confusing.
Lower courts already send qualified elders to serve on our Permanent
Committees and Agencies who have the responsibility of evaluating
and overseeing the operations, resources, and expenses of the
committees and agencies. The committees and agencies also use
external auditing firms to do impartial and thorough yearly audits of
operations and expenses. c mt w has a subcommittee that does a
thorough review of the audit before it is approved at the committee
level. Furthermore, each committee and agency’s work are reported,
reviewed and approved by the CoC of each committee or agency, AC
Committee (in relation to the respective budgets), and the General
Assembly. Introducing another committee to do the same work of our
existing committees and agencies and communicated through different

<a id="ga52-p61"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=63 printed_page=61 -->

channels (website, ad interim committee report) would be confusing
and potentially lead to inconsistent decision making, results, and
actions on behalf of the denomination. In addition, this overture could
violate donor intent.

Finally, this overture, by its proposed means of collecting and
distributing “recommendations for improvement, optimization, and
greater transparency” creates a platform with the potential to divide.
10. That **Overture 30** from Houston Metro Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 8-4, 201, 21-1, 21-12, and 23-1 re the Calling and Dissolution of TE Relationships
for Needful Works”) be **REFERRED** back to Houston Metro Presbytery
without prejudice. **49-0-1**
_referred to the Overtures Committee_

TE David Senters rose to a **PARLIAMENTARY INQUIRY** : based on
_RAO_ 4-11, what would occur in the case of the Assembly’s rejection
of an agency’s budget? The Moderator gave his opinion that should
the budget not be approved, it would go back to the permanent
committee whose budget is affected. TE Zack Byrd rose to a
**PARLIAMENTARY INQUIRY** : if the budget goes back to the permanent
committee, will they bring it back to the Assembly before the end of
the Assembly? The Moderator opined that it would be unlikely. TE
Byrd then asked, how will be able to approve their next year’s budget
at this Assembly? The Moderator gave his opinion that if a budget is
not approved, it would revert to the current year’s budget—that is, the
last budget approved by the Assembly.

The Moderator invited TE Larry Roff to lead the Assembly in the singing of Psalm
5 acapella.

_The Administrative Committee report continues at 52-37, p. 69._

52-35 Special Order: Nominating Committee Report

At 9:30 a.m. a Special Order was recognized by the Assembly. TE Matthew Dallas
Adams, Chairman, led in prayer and presented the report of the Nominating
Committee (Appendix L, p. 403).

Hearing no objection, the Moderator declared all uncontested nominees **elected** .

The following contested nominees were elected by the Assembly (see the
Supplemental Report, Appendix L, p. 438).

**Administrative Committee**, _Class of 2029_
TE Ligon Duncan. Mississippi Valley (1074-717)
RE Melton Ledford Duncan, Calvary (888-881)
**Administrative Committee**, _Alternate_
TE Brian Salter, Tennessee Valley (1002-758)

<a id="ga52-p67"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=69 printed_page=67 -->

TE Travis Marsh raised a **POINT OF ORDER** that the vote for RE for the class of

2029 was very close and some voting devices did not seem to register. TE Wilson
Van Hooser raised a **POINT OF ORDER** that the previous point of order was out of
order as it was a speech against floor nominees. The Moderator ruled the point of
order **not well taken** as the original point of order was a question about voting
devices not working. The Moderator ruled the point **not well taken** as there is no
way to go back and revote if someone’s voting device was not working.

TE David Senters **moved to reconsider** the election for RE for the class of 2029

for the AC, declaring he had voted with the prevailing side. The Moderator ruled
that the motion **out of order** since an election becomes final immediately if the
candidate is present and cannot be reconsidered (RONR [12 [th] ed.] 37:9.2.g; 46:46).

TE James Kessler asked the Moderator at what point would a call for division be
too late? The Moderator ruled that it was too late now as the election had been

declared. TE Kessler requested that the Moderator give an opportunity for division
to be called on close votes as there was concern about whether all the voting
devices are registering. the moderator reminded the assembly that a division is a
non-counted standing vote (RONR [12 [th] ed.] 4:52), which would be less accurate
than the voting devices.

**Administrative Committee**, _Alternate_
RE Rick Owens, North Texas (944-834)

**Committee on Constitutional Business**, _Class of 2029_
RE Joshua Torry, South Texas (967-826)

**Committee on Constitutional Business**, _Alternate_
TE Randy Schlichting, Metro Atlanta (921-887)

TE K.J. Drake raised a **POINT OF INFORMATION** that the Theological Examining
Committee has no RE nominees. The Chairman responded that there were no floor
nominees and so the Committee nominees were uncontested.

**Board of Trustees of Covenant College**, _Class of 2029_
TE Bradley Barnes, Southern New England (1043-700-125)

**Committee on Discipleship Ministries**, _Class of 2030_

52-36 Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) Vows and Declaration to be
Assembly’s Judicial Commission

The newly elected members of the SJC who were present (TE David Coffin, TE
Paul Bankson, TE Stephen B. Tipton, TE Paul Kooistra, RE Steve Dowling, and
RE Frederick Jay Neikirk) took their vows before the Assembly. The Moderator
**declared** the Standing Judicial Commission to be the Judicial Commission of this
Assembly in accord with _BCO_ 15-4.

52-37 Administrative Committee Report continued from 52-34

**R** **ECOMMENDATION** **20** (2026 MNA budget) **adopted** (1278-595)
RE Kenneth Wynne **moved to amend** by reducing the line item for
the MNA Coordinator’s compensation by 10% from the proposed
budget but not to affect the benefits line. A **POINT OF ORDER** was
raised that the Assembly cannot amend the Committee’s
recommendation. The Moderator responded that the point of order was
**not well taken** as _RAO_ 4-11 allows the Assembly to amend, but it
requires a 2/3 vote. TE Matthew Capone **moved all pending**
**questions**, which was **adopted** with at least a 2/3 majority by voice
vote. The motion to amend **failed** (764-1100). **R** **ECOMMENDATION**
**20** was **adopted** (1278-595).

**R** **ECOMMENDATION** **25** (2026 Partnership Shares & Ministry Asks)
**adopted** by voice vote

**R** **ECOMMENDATION** **30** (2026 GA Registration Fee)
**adopted as amended by the CoC** by voice vote

**R** **ECOMMENDATION** **35** (approval of minutes) **adopted** by voice vote
The CoC Chairman yielded to the Permanent Committee Chairman
who yielded to his designee TE Per Almquist to present the Permanent
Committee’s position. The CoC Chairman’s designee, TE Stephen
O’Neill, presented the substitute. TE Almquist responded for the
Permanent Committee. **R** **ECOMMENDATION** **35** as amended by the
CoC **failed** (704-820). TE Fred Greco raised a **POINT OF ORDER** that
when there is a difference between the Permanent Committee’s

recommendation and the CoC’s recommendation, the CoC’s
recommendation is a substitute for the main. The Moderator ruled the

point of order **well taken** . **R** **ECOMMENDATION** **35** as recommended
by the Permanent Committee (to approve the minutes without
exception) was **adopted** by voice vote.

The Chairman closed the report with prayer.
