**REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE OF COMMISSIONERS ON**

**ADMINISTRATIVE COMMITTEE**

**I.** **Business Referred to the Committee**
A. AC Permanent Committee Report to GA
B. Minutes of the 2024 meetings of the AC and Board of Directors
1. AC **–** April 18, 2024, June 11, 2024, October 3, 2024, March 11, 2025,
and May 1, 2025
2. BD – April 18, 2024, June 11, 2024, October 3, 2024
C. Budgets for the Permanent Committees and Agencies
D. Overtures Referred to the AC
E. Recommendations of the AC Committee
F. Review of Auditor’s Report for 2024

**II. Statement of Major Issues Discussed**

A. AC Permanent Committee Report to GA
B. Minutes of the 2024 meetings of the AC and Board of Directors
1. AC **–** April 18, 2024, June 11, 2024, October 3, 2024, March 11, 2025,
and May 1, 2025
2. BD – April 18, 2024, June 11, 2024, October 3, 2024
C. Budgets for the Permanent Committees and Agencies
D. Overtures Referred to the AC
E. Recommendations of the AC Committee
F. Review of Auditor’s Report for 2024: In accordance with _RAO_ 14-7.h, the
CoC notes that the audit was received and all necessary actions on any
recommendations are being taken.

**III. Recommendations**

1. Rescinded
2. That the Assembly commend the entire staff of the Administrative
Committee for faithful and effective labors for the Lord with skillful and
joyful service to the ministers, churches, Presbyteries, permanent
committees, agencies, and General Assembly in promoting the unity,
purity, and mission of the Presbyterian Church in America in the past year.
**46-0-0**
_adopted_
3. That the Overtures Committee receive the following advice on **Overture 3**
from Arizona Presbytery, “Erect Ad Interim Committee on Christian
Nationalism,” and **Overture 4** from South Texas Presbytery, “Erect Ad
Interim Committee on Christian Nationalism.” **46-0-0**

_referred to the Overtures Committee_
The Administrative Committee offers no opinion per se on the
wisdom of the proposed study committee. In the event the Assembly
answers Overtures 3 and 4 in the affirmative, approving the
establishment of an ad interim committee on the study of Christian
Nationalism, the budget of such committee be set at a maximum of
$10,000, provided solely by designated gifts to the AC. The AC notes:
“Funding should not be the burden primarily of the members of the ad
interim committee nor the AC, but of those requesting the ad interim
committee. Any overture proposing an ad interim committee should

<a id="ga52-p71"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=73 printed_page=71 -->

ordinarily include a plan for how sufficient, designated funds for the
ad interim committee will be raised.” ( _RAO_ 9-3)

The AC further notes “only two (2) ad interim committees may be
appointed or continued in any given year, unless additional ones are
approved by a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the Assembly” ( _RAO_ 9-3) Any
study committee approved without a funding plan requires a two-thirds
(2/3) vote of the General Assembly as an amendment to the AC budget
( _RAO_ 9-3, 4-11). Since the AC can only administer funds that have
been designated and received for an ad interim study committee ( _RAO_
9-3), funds will be distributed by the AC only as they are made
available in the project account.
4. That the Overtures Committee recommend that **Overture 9** from James
River Presbytery, “Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to Make AC Members Appointed by
Committees and Agencies Non-voting Advisory Members,” be answered in
the **NEGATIVE** . **26-17-5**

r _eferred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:

The current structure of the Administrative Committee is grounded
in PCA history with grass roots concerns addressed by our founding
fathers. The PCA tried having only at-large members on the AC at the
beginning of its history but found that personalities and politics made
such an arrangement counter-productive for the consistent mission
and progress of the church. So, in 1984, the General Assembly
assigned (and later approved the recommendations of) an Ad Interim
Study Committee composed of our founders and key leaders (e.g., Jack
Williamson, Bob Canada, and Morton Smith) to develop a system of
checks and balances for the AC. Those founders and leaders worked
in an Ad Interim Committee for five years to design a structure to
protect our grass roots governance through the representation of atlarge members as well as the representation of those elected to regular
oversight of the General Assembly’s Committees and Agencies.

The overture’s wording may seem to indicate that some
Administrative Committee members are elected while others are only
“appointed” to their roles. This wording may give an impression
inconsistent with our actual practice. Just as the General Assembly
elects at-large members of the AC, it also elects the Committee and
Agency members to their office. Every elder who represents a
Permanent Committee or Agency on the AC has been elected to that
responsibility first by a church, then a presbytery, then General
Assembly, and then by the Committee or Agency whose members vote
to approve their representative for the AC.

If Overture 9 were to pass, no Committee or Agency could vote
on its own budget, nor vote on the budgets of other Committees and
Agencies in ways that enhance cooperation and connectionalism
between them. Budgets of many millions of dollars, developed through
months of financial planning and inter-agency cooperation would be
determined solely by at-large AC members who meet for a few hours
each Spring.

The structure designed by the 5-year study of the Ad Interim
Committee composed of founding leaders replaced a system that was

<a id="ga52-p72"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=74 printed_page=72 -->

not working. We believe it is in the best interests of the PCA’s mission
to maintain the current structure.
5. That the Administrative Committee’s advice on **Overture 11** be answered
with reference to the answer provided by the Board of Directors of the PCA
Foundation. **46-0-0**

r _eferred to the Overtures Committee_
6. That the Overtures Committee receive the following advice on **Overture**
**26** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery, “Erect Ad Interim Committee to
Revise the Directory for Worship for Authoritative Use.” **46-0-0**
r _eferred to the Overtures Committee_
The Administrative Committee offers no opinion per se on the
wisdom of the proposed study committee’s purpose. In the event the
Assembly answers Overture 26 in the affirmative, approving the
establishment of an ad interim committee to revise the directory for
worship for authoritative use, the budget of such committee be set at a
maximum of $15,000, provided solely by designated gifts to the AC.
The AC notes: “Funding should not be the burden primarily of the
members of the ad interim committee nor the AC, but of those
requesting the ad interim committee. Any overture proposing an ad
interim committee should ordinarily include a plan for how sufficient,
designated funds for the ad interim committee will be raised.” ( _RAO_ 93)

The AC further notes that “only two (2) ad interim committees may
be appointed or continued in any given year, unless additional ones
are approved by a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the Assembly” ( _RAO_ 9-3)
any study committee approved without a funding plan requires a two thirds (2/3) vote of the General Assembly as an amendment to the AC
budget ( _RAO_ 9-3, 4-11). Since the AC can only administer funds that
have been designated and received for an ad interim study committee
( _RAO_ 9-3), funds will be distributed by the AC only as they are made
available in the project account.
7. That the Overtures Committee recommend that **Overture 27** from
Northwest Georgia Presbytery, “Erect Ad Interim Committee to Receive
Suggestions for Permanent Committee and Agency Operations, Resources,
and Expenses,” be answered in the **NEGATIVE** or **REFERRED** to the 53 [rd]
General Assembly. **46-0-0**
r _eferred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:

Having no details on how the proposed ad interim committee
would research, evaluate, or coordinate with Permanent Committees
and Agency Boards already elected to supervise operations, resources,
and expenses the AC offers no opinion per se on the wisdom of the
proposed study committee. In the event the Assembly would answer
Overture 27 in the affirmative, approving the establishment of an ad
interim committee on the study of Operations, Resources, and
Expenses, then the budget of such committee be set at a maximum of
$10,000, provided solely by designated gifts to the AC. The AC notes:
“Funding should not be the burden primarily of the members of the ad
interim committee nor the AC, but of those requesting the ad interim
committee. Any overture proposing an ad interim committee should

<a id="ga52-p73"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=75 printed_page=73 -->

ordinarily include a plan for how sufficient, designated funds for the
ad interim committee will be raised.” ( _RAO_ 9-3)

The AC further notes that “only two (2) ad interim committees may
be appointed or continued in any given year, unless additional ones
are approved by a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the Assembly” ( _RAO_ 9-3)
any study committee approved without a funding plan requires a two thirds (2/3) vote of the General Assembly as an amendment to the AC
budget ( _RAO_ 9-3, 4-11). Since the AC can only administer funds that
have been designated and received for an ad interim study committee
( _RAO_ 9-3), funds will be distributed by the AC only as they are made
available in the project account.
8. That the Overtures Committee recommend that **Overture 30** from Houston
Metro Presbytery, “Amend _BCO_ 8-4, 20-1, 21-1, 21-12, and 23-1 re the
Calling and Dissolution of TE Relationships for Needful Works,” be
**REFERRED** back to Houston Metro Presbytery or to the 53 [rd] General
Assembly. **46-0-0**
r _eferred to the Overtures Committee_
Grounds:

This overture was not received by the AC in time for eight of the
ten Permanent Committees or Agencies to consider prior to the 52 [nd]
General Assembly in 2025. This overture potentially affects the legal
employment conditions and relationships of TE personnel in all
Committee and Agencies, as well as the relationship of such men to
their Presbyteries—and the relationship of those Presbyteries and the
General Assembly to civil authorities. Significant time and care are
needed with legal counsel for the Permanent Committees and
Agencies adequately to consider effects and implications related to our
practices, liabilities, and polity.

We are grateful that the author of the overture has consented to
receive input from the Permanent Committees and Agencies but, at this
late date, little can be offered prior to this General Assembly.

The Committee on Constitutional Business has advised that this
overture is in conflict with the PCA Constitution.
9. That **Overture 35** from Pacific Northwest Presbytery, “Request AC to Study
and Report on GA Meeting Locations,” be answered in the **AFFIRMATIVE** .
**46-0-0**
_adopted_
Grounds:

Post-Covid costs, recent historically high GA attendance, and
even higher commissioner registration numbers required to break
even, make it imperative that the Administrative Committee research
General Assembly arrangements as they bear on future attendance,
costs, and fees that affect our GA’s ability to function as a truly
representative body responsible for determining the doctrinal fidelity
and gospel mission of the larger PCA.
10. That **Overture 41** from Calvary Presbytery, “Direct the AC to Collect &
Report Each Congregation’s Public Worship Service Times,” be answered
in the **NEGATIVE** . **46-0-0**
_adopted_

<a id="ga52-p74"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=76 printed_page=74 -->

Grounds:

The Church Directory page of the Administrative Committee
website already allows users to find and click on each church’s name.
Then, the church’s website opens with all the information the church
desires to share. Such websites are far more likely to be up to date
regarding current service time information than what could be
collected and maintained with Administrative Committee personnel
and resources.

For additional information, please note on the PCA Administrative
[Committee website—https://www.pcaac.org/ there are resources that](https://www.pcaac.org/)
include a Church Directory with an interactive map showing all of the
churches within the PCA:

**MAP VERSION:**

Each of those churches is linked with specific church information.

<a id="ga52-p75"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=77 printed_page=75 -->

If the church has a website, a link is provided to go directly to the most
current information about that local congregation.

**TEXT-ONLY VERSION:**

The AC office is available to answer questions about various church
information as well. They may be reached at [ac@pcanet.org or by](mailto:ac@pcanet.org)
phone at 678-825-1000.

<a id="ga52-p76"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=78 printed_page=76 -->

11. That **Overture 42** from Calvary Presbytery, “Direct the Stated Clerk Not to
Collect Statistical Data Pertaining to Age or Ethnicity,” be answered in the
**NEGATIVE** **AFFIRMATIVE as amended** . **30-18-2**
a _dopted as amended by the CoC_

“Therefore, be it resolved that Calvary Presbytery hereby overtures the
52nd General Assembly to direct the Office of the Stated Clerk through
the Administrative Committee not to collect statistical data pertaining
to age or ethnicity in annual statistical reports.”

Grounds:

We are sympathetic with the original overture’s rationale, but
recognize the value of collecting age related data for the purposes of
age appropriate denominational ministries and publications.

Soliciting ethnic data, however, raises a number of difficulties. In
the discussion of the Committee of Commissioners, speeches assumed
differing and contradictory interpretations of what the term ethnicity
means. When collecting data for statistical purposes, the term needs to
be consistently understood and applied, but ethnicity can be defined
in relation to skin color, language, cultural heritage, ora variety of
combinations of the same.

We recognize the need for collecting statistical data related to
language, given the number of PCA churches holding worship services
in non-English languages. Yet, this illustrates one of the many
difficulties of the term “ethnicity,” which conflates cultural or national
heritage with spoken or preferred language.

Age data is important for developing and deploying resources for
catechizing, parenting, evangelizing, leadership training, and
providing appropriate care for people of all ages. Most churches
already identify grade levels for children to plan nurture and
instruction. National surveys estimate that PCA leadership is among
the oldest for evangelical denominations. Aggregating such age data is
a responsible way for the church to consider where resources and
training are most needed to maintain our mission of being "true to the
scriptures and reformed faith, and obedient to the great commission"
across generations.

The AC is not originating concern for collection of data regarding
ethnicities in our churches. The directives of the 46 [th] General
Assembly (responding to the Overtures of 39 different presbyteries
seeking to further racial reconciliation in our churches) instructed
Mission to North America (MNA) “to budget and plan for renewing the
research with LifeWay Research Services[done in 2018], or some other
research service, to report back to the 51 [st] General Assembly (2023) in
order to establish a longitudinal study of our denomination on the issue
of racial reconciliation” (see _M46GA_, 2018, MNA Report
Recommendation 3, p. 628, emphasis added). As the Study Committee
reported to the 45 [th] GA, the primary research objective was “to obtain
a quantitative baseline measure of where the Presbyterian Church in
America stands on racial reconciliation.” Subsequent study is required

<a id="ga52-p77"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=79 printed_page=77 -->

to make the baseline meaningful for longitudinal analysis whose goal
is to observe changes and trends over a period of time.

To collect this longitudinal data in the most efficient and costeffective way, MNA and CDM leaders have discussed with
Administrative Committee leadership utilizing the annual statistical
reports already requested of local churches. The Stated Clerk included
a note in this year’s statistical data forms that such data would be
requested in the future to give churches an opportunity to respond
about how best to report such information. Assumptions about how
difficult such reporting will be are premature and forbidding all such
data collection is counter to the instruction of previous Assemblies.

The Report of the Ad Interim Committee on Racial and Ethnic
Reconciliation that was the basis of the recommendations approved by
the 46 [th] GA also explained why such data collection is important to
our gospel witness:

This is what the son of David will do – effect redemption
and bring the nations into God's own people so that there will
be diversity within the unity of God's people .... He does not do
away with our ethnic particularity in this world; rather, he
honors us as the "nations" even as he includes us in his "people."
He will rule over us in his kingdom, the Kingdom of God ....

This does not do away with ethnicity or race; but this one
new humanity points us in the direction of Revelation 7 in
which people "from every nation, from all tribes and peoples
and languages" (cf. Gen. 10:5, 20, 31) are brought together by
the blood of Jesus to sing his praise. The particularity and
individuality of the nations, tribes, peoples, and languages is
not done away with; rather, the diversity-in-unity of the praise,
each in their own language, will redound to the splendor and
praise of the God who redeemed his people with the blood of
his Son, Jesus. And even at the end of the biblical story, "the
kings of the nations" will bring their glory into the new city-all
that is good as a result of our diversity will be present, not in a
"color-blind" fashion, but precisely in the rich diversity that God
has made (Rev. 21 :24-26; cf. Is. 60) ....

God's mission does not call our church to become “colorblind," the negation of race or ethnicity; rather, it calls to
become "one new humanity," in which that diversity remains
diverse and yet finds a new and true unity (not uniformity)
through the blood of Jesus (Eph. 2:13-18)."

Necessary reasons for evaluating our progress in this gospel
mission were beautifully expressed in Overture 55 from Mississippi
Valley Presbytery that was enfolded into the final recommendations of
the 44 [th] GA:

Whereas, The Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley
recognizes that the sins of racism and racial injustice—not
only toward African Americans, but also toward other racial
minorities—are not simply past sins, but continue to be sins
with which our churches and congregants wrestle….

Whereas, God has once more given the PCA a gracious
opportunity to show the beauty, grace, and power of the

<a id="ga52-p78"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=80 printed_page=78 -->

Gospel of Jesus Christ through confession and through the
fruits of repentance: such as, … renewing our church’s
commitment to develop minority leadership at the
congregational, presbytery, and denominational levels; and
encouraging a denomination-wide vision for and
commitment to a more racially and ethnically diverse
church; …

Be it finally resolved, that The Presbytery of the
Mississippi Valley humbly asks the General Assembly of the
Presbyterian Church in America to urge all the congregations
and presbyteries of the Presbyterian Church in America … to
confess their own particular sins and failures regarding racial
injustice as may be appropriate, and to seek to bring forth
fruits of repentance for the Gospel’s sake within their own
local communities.
Please note that our godly Mississippi brethren urged “studying,
evaluating and implementing” the fruits of repentance “at the
congregational, presbytery, and denominational levels,… encouraging
a denomination-wide vision for and commitment to a more racially
and ethnically diverse church.”

These humble but direct exhortations underscore the importance
of evaluating and displaying the “fruits of repentance.”
12. That the Overtures Committee recommend that **Overture 43** from Calvary
Presbytery, “Amend _RAO_ 4-11 regarding Data on Age and Ethnicity,” be
answered in the **NEGATIVE** **AFFIRMATIVE as amended** . **30-18-2**

r _eferred to the Overtures Committee_

Therefore, be it resolved that Calvary Presbytery hereby overtures the
52nd General Assembly to amend _RAO_ 4-11 by adding the following
to the third paragraph (underlining for additions): “Such statistical
information shall not include data pertaining to age or ethnicity.”

Grounds:

We are sympathetic with the original overture’s rationale, but
recognize the value of collecting age related data for the purposes of
age appropriate denominational ministries and publications.

Soliciting ethnic data, however, raises a number of difficulties. In
the discussion of the Committee of Commissioners, speeches assumed
differing and contradictory interpretations of what the term ethnicity
means. When collecting data for statistical purposes, the term needs to
be consistently understood and applied, but ethnicity can be defined
in relation to skin color, language, cultural heritage, ora variety of
combinations of the same.

We recognize the need for collecting statistical data related to
language, given the number of PCA churches holding worship services
in non-English languages. Yet, this illustrates one of the many
difficulties of the term “ethnicity,” which conflates cultural or national
heritage with spoken or preferred language.

The overture appears confused about the basis by which
partnership shares are determined (i.e., the subject of _RAO 4-11_,
paragraph 3 being amended by the proposed overture). Partnership
shares are based either on a dollar figure multiplied by the per capita

<a id="ga52-p79"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=81 printed_page=79 -->

count of a local church’s communicant members or on the
proportional (percentage) need of the Committees and Agencies being
supported by the church’s benevolence/mission budget. Ages and
ethnicities have nothing to do with the statistics determining
partnership shares.

As to the appropriateness of the Administrative Committee
requesting age and ethnicity information from churches in their annual
reports, the AC notes the following.

Age data is important for developing and deploying resources for
catechizing, parenting, evangelizing, leadership training, and
providing appropriate care for people of all ages. Most churches
already identify grade levels for children to plan nurture and
instruction. National surveys estimate that PCA leadership is among
the oldest for evangelical denominations. Aggregating such age data is
a responsible way for the church to consider where resources and
training are most needed to maintain our mission of being "true to the
scriptures and reformed faith, and obedient to the great commission"
across generations.

The AC is not originating concern for collection of data regarding
ethnicities in our churches. The directives of the 46 [th] General
Assembly (responding to the Overtures of 39 different presbyteries
seeking to further racial reconciliation in our churches) instructed
Mission to North America (MNA) “to budget and plan for renewing the
research with LifeWay Research Services[done in 2018], or some other
research service, to report back to the 51 [st] General Assembly (2023) in
order to establish a longitudinal study of our denomination on the issue
of racial reconciliation” (see _M46GA_, 2018, MNA Report
Recommendation 3, p. 628, emphasis added). As the Study Committee
reported to the 45 [th] GA, the primary research objective was “to obtain
a quantitative baseline measure of where the Presbyterian Church in
America stands on racial reconciliation.” Subsequent study is required
to make the baseline meaningful for longitudinal analysis whose goal
is to observe changes and trends over a period of time.

To collect this longitudinal data in the most efficient and costeffective way, MNA and CDM leaders have discussed with
Administrative Committee leadership utilizing the annual statistical
reports already requested of local churches. The Stated Clerk included
a note in this year’s statistical data forms that such data would be
requested in the future to give churches an opportunity to respond
about how best to report such information. Assumptions about how
difficult such reporting will be are premature and forbidding all such
data collection is counter to the instruction of previous Assemblies.

The Report of the Ad Interim Committee on Racial and Ethnic
Reconciliation that was the basis of the recommendations approved by
the 46 [th] GA also explained why such data collection is important to
our gospel witness:

This is what the son of David will do – effect redemption
and bring the nations into God's own people so that there
will be diversity within the unity of God's people .... He does
not do away with our ethnic particularity in this world; rather,
he honors usa s the "nations" even as he includes us in his

<a id="ga52-p80"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=82 printed_page=80 -->

"people." He will rule over us in his kingdom, the Kingdom
of God ....

This does not do away with ethnicity or race; but this
one new humanity points us in the direction of Revelation 7
in which people "from every nation, from all tribes and
peoples and languages" (cf. Gen. 10:5, 20, 31) are brought
together by the blood of Jesus to sing his praise. The
particularity and individuality of the nations, tribes, peoples,
and languages is not done away with; rather, the diversity-inunity of the praise, each in their own language, will redound
to the splendor and praise of the God who redeemed his
people with the blood of his Son, Jesus. And even at the end
of the biblical story, "the kings of the nations" will bring their
glory into the new city-all that is good as a result of our
diversity will be present, not in a "color-blind" fashion, but
precisely in the rich diversity that God has made (Rev. 21 :2426; cf. Is. 60) ....

God's mission does not call our church to become
“color-blind," the negation of race or ethnicity; rather, it calls
to become "one new humanity," in which that diversity
remains diverse and yet finds a new and true unity (not
uniformity) through the blood of Jesus (Eph. 2:13-18)."
Necessary reasons for evaluating our progress in this gospel
mission were beautifully expressed in Overture 55 from Mississippi
Valley Presbytery that was enfolded into the final recommendations of
the 44 [th] GA:

Whereas, The Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley
recognizes that the sins of racism and racial injustice—not
only toward African Americans, but also toward other racial
minorities—are not simply past sins, but continue to be sins
with which our churches and congregants wrestle….

Whereas, God has once more given the PCA a gracious
opportunity to show the beauty, grace, and power of the
Gospel of Jesus Christ through confession and through the
fruits of repentance: such as, … renewing our church’s
commitment to develop minority leadership at the
congregational, presbytery, and denominational levels; and
encouraging a denomination-wide vision for and
commitment to a more racially and ethnically diverse
church; …

Be it finally resolved, that The Presbytery of the
Mississippi Valley humbly asks the General Assembly of the
Presbyterian Church in America to urge all the congregations
and presbyteries of the Presbyterian Church in America … to
confess their own particular sins and failures regarding racial
injustice as may be appropriate, and to seek to bring forth
fruits of repentance for the Gospel’s sake within their own
local communities.

Please note that our godly Mississippi brethren urged “studying,
evaluating and implementing” the fruits of repentance “at the
congregational, presbytery, and denominational levels,… encouraging

<a id="ga52-p81"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=83 printed_page=81 -->

a denomination-wide vision for and commitment to a more racially
and ethnically diverse church.”

These humble but direct exhortations underscore the importance
of evaluating and displaying the “fruits of repentance.”
13. That **Overture 44** from Pee Dee Presbytery, “Transition byFaith to a PressRelease-Based Publication,” be answered in the **NEGATIVE** **AFFIRMATIVE**
**AS AMENDED** . **30-18-4**
_adopted as amended by the CoC_
Amend O44 “Therefore, be it resolved that the 52 [nd] General Assembly
of the Presbyterian Church in America directs the Administrative
Committee to examine and evaluate the structure and editorial policies
of byFaith magazine, and bring either recommendations for changes
or rationale for no changes to the 54th GA.”

Grounds:
The committee recognizes the concerns expressed about byFaith in the
original overture, while also recognizing the value of a robust
denominational publication that is more than just a press release
agency. This recommendation ensures that the AC assess the structure
and editorial policies of byFaith, which appear not to have been
updated since they were established in 2003. The AC will have
sufficient time for the new Stated Clerk to have a role in this process,
and the recommendations they will bring to the 54 [th] General Assembly
will ensure that the Assembly itself ultimately decides the editorial
policy and structure of the denomination’s publication.

The General Assembly authorized the Administrative Committee
(AC) to establish byFaith in 2003 with this mission mandate:

“The mission of the PCA news magazine is to draw readers into
the full life of the church by informing, edifying, and encouraging
them with the news of God’s kingdom. Through feature stories,
news reports, editorial opinions, readers’ comments, practical
information, and relevant biblical and spiritual discussion, the
magazine motivates and equips readers to serve Christ in the
Presbyterian Church in America and in His kingdom.”

While the magazine clearly serves as a source of information for
denominational news, it was envisioned to be much more, a
publication rich with various styles of journalism to encourage and
equip God’s people for their mission in God’s world.

The minutes of the Assembly further note that the magazine would
publish content, “examining difficult issues: personal, social, cultural,
and theological. Helping readers think and speak about them
biblically, always equipping them with a biblical world-and-life view.”

In other words, it was understood that every piece of content
would not share unanimous agreement by every member, officer, or
court of the church. The readership of the denomination holds diverse
perspectives regarding what constitutes essential news, shaped by
factors including, but not limited to, regional differences,
socioeconomic status, education level, denominational history, and
theological tradition. Given this diversity within the PCA, it is
beneficial to note how the GA desired for byFaith to help us remember

<a id="ga52-p82"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=84 printed_page=82 -->

our shared foundation in the gospel of Jesus Christ and our common
pursuit of the peace, purity, and mission of the church.

Importantly, it was understood that every piece of content would
not be received or treated as official church policy, unless it was
specified as such.

In this era, when different websites or social media outlets seek to
build or serve particular constituencies within our church by
circulating criticisms, speculations, or allegations with no church
oversight, it is important to have a media source under the authority of
the General Assembly’s AC to inform and inspire God’s people with
how the PCA is progressing, or being hindered in, Christ’s mission.

A common misconception is that the Stated Clerk directs byFaith’s
editorial process or choices. In fact, the GA sought to protect the Clerk
and the publication from inappropriate associations by building in
other oversight mechanisms. Members of the AC, who are elected by
the General Assembly, receive reports on the progress and content of
byFaith at every regular meeting. In addition, the AC elects an
oversight committee for more regular review of byFaith.

Through productive use of writing resources expressed through
digital means, byFaith’s publishing output i satan all-time high: since
the summer of 2024 byFaith has produced one piece of original
content every weekday, resulting in almost 200 articles to date in the
new format.

With more content and new editorial leadership, the website is
experiencing vastly increased readership throughout our
denomination. As shown in the table below, the number of visitors and
page views on the website have more than doubled in the past year.
Further, views (i.e., times when a web page is loaded or reloaded ina
browser) are 33 times higher, and clicks (i.e., times when a user clicks
on links or ads in search results) have increased 17 times over the same
period.

**1)** **WEBSITE ACTIVITY**
July 2023-Feb 2024 July 2024-Feb 2025
Visitors 77,000 175,000
Pageviews 161,000 356,000

**2)** **GOOGLE SEARCH ENGINE**
Jan-Feb 2024 Jan-Feb 2025
Views 10,300 339,000
Clicks 630 10,500

Not only is byFaith being increasingly well received by PCA
readers, the recognized quality of the articles is allowing the PCA to
serve the wider church and world. Recent articles from byFaith have
been referenced by the _Washington Post_, _Wall Street Journal_, _Atlanta_
_Journal-Constitution_, _U.S. News and World Report_, Reuters,
_Christianity Today_, The Gospel Coalition, _World Magazine_, Challies,
and Ministry Watch.

Despite these increases in production and operational quality, the
budgeted operating expenses of byFaith have dropped from $650K in

<a id="ga52-p83"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=85 printed_page=83 -->

2024 to just over $400K in 2025. Those expenses are expected to
further drop to $312K in 2026. Considering budgeted contributions
and advertising revenues, the expected actual impact on the AC budget
is $159K. This is the best budgeted operating outcome in over a decade
while development and advertising plans continue to be made to
increase revenue to further improve the bottom line in the years ahead.

Today—as a fully digital publication—byFaith honors its GAdesignated mission even as it has _grown_ its article production and
reach while dramatically _cutting costs._

30. That the 2026 General Assembly registration fee be set at $575 for Teaching

Elders from churches with $150,000 or more in annual tithes and offerings,
as well as for other TEs not called to churches. Of that amount, $400 will
be allocated toga costs, $125 to the Standing Committees for expenses,
including the Standing Judicial Committee, and $50 to Statistics and
Publications.

Further, the AC recommends that the 2026 fee for all Ruling Elders and for
Teaching Elders from churches with under $150,000 in annual income be
$350. The entire amount will be allocated to General Assembly costs.

The registration fee for honorably retired TEs and REs will remain $150.
**26-18-1**
_adopted as amended by the CoC_
That the 2026 General Assembly registration fee be set at $600 for Teaching
Elders from churches with $150,000 or more in annual tithes and offerings
as well as for other TEs not called to churches. Of that amount, $425 will
be allocated toga costs, $125 to the Standing Committees for expenses
including the Standing Judicial Committee, and $50 to Statistics and
Publications. Starting in 2027, this fee is proposed to be annually inflationadjusted.

Further, the AC recommends that the 2026 fee for _all_ Ruling Elders and
Teaching Elders from churches with _under_ $150,000 in income be $425.
The entire amount will be allocated to General Assembly costs. Starting in
2027, this fee is proposed to be annually inflation-adjusted.

In 2028, the AC recommends that the fee for Ruling Elders from churches
with $150,000 or more in annual income be increased to match the fee
paid by Teaching Elders from churches with $150,000 or more in annual
tithes and offerings.

(Honorably retired TEs and REs are not included in the analysis or proposed
changes and would continue to pay $150.)

Grounds:

Following a change in price structure in 2023 when all Ruling
Elder registration fees were set at a lowered rate of $300, we did not
see a corresponding increase in registrations—Ruling Elders comprised
approximately 30% of the total commissioners both before and after
the fee change. As a result, after the 2023 increase to Teaching Elder
registration fees, every two TEs paying $75 more each only offset one
Ruling Elder paying $150 less. In sum, the fee change neither increased

<a id="ga52-p86"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=88 printed_page=86 -->

Ruling Elder attendance at General Assembly nor increased revenue to
cover General Assembly costs.

**City and Year** **TE Attendance** **RE Attendance**

St. Louis 2021 1499 (71%) 615 (29%)

Birmingham 2022 1634 (69%) 751 (31%)

Memphis 2023 1607 (70%)

694 (30%)

Richmond 2024 1459 (69%) 654 (31%)

Further, the GA registration fee has not kept pace with inflation.
This proposed change catches up on past inflation and sets a course to
adjust to inflation in real time.

From a historical perspective, the TE and RE fee was $400 in 2006,
and it was raised to $450 (a $50 increase) for both TEs and REs in 2013.
In 2023, the TE and RE fees were rebalanced (with TEs—2/3s of the
total increased by $75, and REs—1/3 of the total decreased by $150).
So, the net effect is that total fees for the AC have only increased $50
(which was done in 2013) since 2006 when both TEs and REs were
raised last at $400. In other words, over the past 20 years the net
increase to GA fees has been just $50.

36. Regarding **Overture 47** from Great Lakes Presbytery, “Erect Ad Interim
Committee on Christian Nationalism,” the Administrative Committee offers
**NO OPINION** per se on the wisdom of the proposed study committee. In
the event the Assembly answers Overtures 3, 4 and 47 in the affirmative,
then the AC notes that approving the establishment of such an ad interim
committee on the study of Christian Nationalism would have a budget set
a ta maximum of $10,000, provided solely by designated gifts to the AC.
The AC further notes: “Funding should not be the burden primarily of the
members of the ad interim committee nor the AC, but of those requesting
the ad interim committee. Any overture proposing an ad interim committee
should ordinarily include a plan for how sufficient, designated funds for the
ad interim committee will be raised.” ( _RAO_ 9-3)

any study committee approved without a funding plan requires a two-thirds
(2/3) vote of the General Assembly as an amendment to the AC budget
( _RAO_ 9-3, 4-11). Ordinarily the AC can only administer funds that have
been designated and received for an ad interim study committee ( _RAO_ 93); funds will be distributed by the AC only as they are made available in
the project account.

The AC also notes “only two (2) ad interim committees may be appointed
or continued in any given year, unless additional ones are approved by a
two-thirds (2/3) vote of the Assembly” ( _RAO_ 9-3). Overtures 3, 4, 26, 27,
47, 48 and 49 are all requesting an ad interim committee to the 52 [nd]
General Assembly. **46-0-0**
37. That **Overture 48** from Tennessee Valley Presbytery “Erect an Ad Interim
Committee for a Pastoral Letter on Christian Citizenship and Church-State
Relations” be answered in the **NEGATIVE** . **46-0-0**
_adopted_
Grounds:

The overture stipulates that the proposed ad interim committee
shall be unfunded. The establishment of an ad interim committee,
necessitating resources for its effective operation, without provision for
requisite funding, constitutes a fiscally imprudent measure.

<a id="ga52-p88"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=90 printed_page=88 -->

any study committee approved without a funding plan requires a
two-thirds (2/3) vote of the General Assembly as an amendment to the
AC budget ( _RAO_ 9-3, 4-11). Ordinarily the AC can only administer
funds that have been designated and received for an ad interim study
committee ( _RAO_ 9-3); funds will be distributed by the AC only as they
are made available in the project account.

The AC further notes that “only two (2) ad interim committees may
be appointed or continued in any given year, unless additional ones
are approved by a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the Assembly” ( _RAO_ 9-3).
Overtures 3, 4, 26, 27, 47, 48 and 49 are all requesting an ad interim
committee be approved by the 52nd General Assembly.

The overture itself acknowledges the existence of "substantial,
biblical guidance concerning Christian citizenship and the relationship
of the church to the state" within the Westminster Standards, as
adopted by this body, and the Preface to the Book of Church Order of
the Presbyterian Church in America. These foundational documents
provide a sufficient and authoritative framework for the instruction of
the Church on these matters, rendering the creation of a new ad interim
committee for this purpose unnecessary at this time.

In the event the Assembly would answer Overture 48 in the
affirmative, then the AC notes: “Funding should not be the burden
primarily of the members of the ad interim committee nor the AC, but
of those requesting the ad interim committee. Any overture proposing
an ad interim committee should ordinarily include a plan for how
sufficient, designated funds for the ad interim committee will be
raised.” ( _RAO_ 9-3)
38. That **Overture 49** from Pacific Northwest Presbytery “Erect an Ad Interim
Committee on AI” be answered in the **NEGATIVE** . **46-0-0**
_adopted_
Grounds:

While acknowledging the importance of engaging with the
implications of artificial intelligence for the Church, the Administrative
Committee recommends a negative response to this overture for the
following reasons. We firmly believe that the Church, through its
grounding in established biblical principles and Reformed
understanding, as articulated in the Westminster Confession of Faith
concerning creation ( _WCF_ 4), humanity's covenant with God ( _WCF_ 7),
and the nature of the Church ( _WCF_ 25), already possesses the
foundational ethical guidance necessary to apply general principles for
the use or limits of artificial intelligence technology.

Absence of a Comprehensive Funding Plan: While the overture
proposes an ongoing, annual budget of $15,000 to be funded by gifts
to the Administrative Committee, most study committees are capped
at $10,000. Further, no funding source is guaranteed for even one year,
and additional years could well prove to be more costly given the
potentially extensive scope of the proposed study, including the need
to consult theological, technical, medical, and legal specialists. A
study of this magnitude warrants a more clearly defined and dedicated
funding strategy to ensure its thorough and effective execution.

Regarding even regular study committees, the Administrative
Committee (AC) notes: "Any overture proposing an ad interim

<a id="ga52-p89"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=91 printed_page=89 -->

committee should ordinarily include a plan for how sufficient,
designated funds for the ad interim committee will be raised. Funding
should not be the burden primarily of the members of the ad interim
committee or the AC, but of those requesting the ad interim committee"
( _RAO_ 9-3). Any study committee approved without a funding plan
requires a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the General Assembly as an
amendment to the AC budget ( _RAO_ 9-3, 4-11). Funds would only be
distributed by the AC as they are made available in the project
account.

Premature Nature Given Current LLM Development: The overture
seeks to address complex issues related to artificial intelligence, while
specific Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly evolving. Because
of the rate of change, it is premature to establish a study committee
tasked with providing definitive guidance and principles for the
Church at this time. The strengths, weaknesses, and capabilities of
today’s LLMs are still in flux, and any report produced now risks
becoming quickly outdated or based on an incomplete understanding.
These are matters more appropriately addressed by scholars in our
research and theology institutions as this field develops.

Unrealistic Reporting Timeline for Committees and Agencies: The
overture directs all General Assembly committees, commissions, and
agencies to submit a report on their current and proposed AI usage
within six months (by December 31, 2025). Given the rapid evolution
of AI and the varying levels of expertise and established protocols
among these diverse church bodies and institutions, producing a
comprehensive or definitive report within this timeframe presents
significant challenges for the realistic ability to gather the in-depth
insights required for a complex and ongoing study.

Summary: In summary, while acknowledging the importance of
addressing AI's implications, the Administrative Committee
recommends answering this overture in the negative. This decision is
based on the absence o fa comprehensive funding plan for such a
significant study, the premature nature of establishing definitive
guidance given the rapidly evolving landscape of Large Language
Models, and the unrealistic reporting timeline proposed for
committees and agencies. Crucially, the Administrative Committee
recognizes that established ethical guidance, rooted in biblical
principles and our Reformed understanding of humanity and the
Church ( _WCF_ 4, 7, 25), already exists for navigating new technologies.
Furthermore, the overture's broad language carries inherent risks
concerning intellectual property infringement and the ethical use of
copyrighted denominational materials within AI, an area that requires
careful, ongoing discernment rather than a single, time-bound report.

The AC further notes that “only two (2) ad interim committees may
be appointed or continued in any given year, unless additional ones
are approved by a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the Assembly” ( _RAO_ 9-3).
Overtures 3, 4, 26, 27, 47, 48, and 49 are all requesting an ad interim
committee be approved by the 52nd General Assembly.

<a id="ga52-p90"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=92 printed_page=90 -->

<a id="ga52-p95"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=97 printed_page=95 -->

TE David Coffin filed the following dissent.

Dissent (BCO 45-1, -2)

I dissent from the decision of the General Assembly in the approval of Overture 24,
to amend _BCO_ 24-1 to clarify a Session’s role in examining officer nominees. The
overture would allow a session contemplating the election of officers to render a
decision on Christian experience at any point in the nomination process, and based
on that decision, may judge the nominee, whether previously ordained or not,
ineligible for that election. In my judgment this allows a Session, in a legislative act,
to—effectively—indict, convict and censure an ordained elder. this i sana ct of
discipline without due process, and thus permits actions that are profoundly unjust.
Such acts are properly without any force whatever, since unjust, since incapable of
the approbation of an impartial public, and since it will never have the countenance
and blessing of the great Head of the Church. (Preliminary Principles 8)

**REPORT OF THE**

**OVERTURES COMMITTEE**

**I.** **Business Referred to the Committee**

37 Overtures: 1, 3-16, 19, 22-27, 29-34, 37-40, 43, 47-50. Five overtures (3,
4, 26, 47, 48) were referred to the AC as well as to the OC. Twenty-five overtures
(1, 5-8, 10, 12-16, 19, 22-25, 29, 31-34, 37-40) were referred to CCB as well as
to the OC. Two overtures (9, 30) were referred to CCB, AC, CC, CDM, CTS,
Geneva, MNA, MTW, PCAF, RH, and RUF as well as to the OC. Three overtures
(11, 27, 49) were referred to AC, CC, CDM, CTS, Geneva, MNA, MTW, PCAF,
RH, and RUF as well as to the OC.

Twelve overtures, referred exclusively to other Committees or Agencies, were
not considered by the OC (35, 41, 42, 44 to AC; 2, 17, 20, 28, 36, 45, 46 to
MNA; 18 to RUF). One overture (21) was withdrawn by Presbytery.

**II. Statement of Major Issues Discussed**

Each overture was discussed, and recommendations were made. If the OC
recommended no amendment to an overture, then that overture is not reprinted
here, and we have included only the Clerk’s Summary Title. In instances where
the OC proposed amendments to an overture, the Presbytery’s proposed action
is reprinted, noting the changes proposed by OC (single underline and single
strikethrough for original overture; double underline and double strike through
for OC amendment).

<a id="ga52-p98"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=100 printed_page=98 -->

The full text of the Overtures is found on pp. 35-157 of this Commissioner
Handbook [Appendix U, p. 1163 of these _Minutes_ ]. OC Recommendation
numbers in this report correspond to the Overture numbers.
