**IV. Recommendations**

1. That **Overture 1** from Northern New England Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 32-2 to
Clarify that a Court May Investigate Whether Charges Should Be Filed”, p. 35) be
answered in the **negative.** **109-14-2**

**Grounds:** This Overture muddies the clear distinction between a court taking action
against an accused following a formal investigation ( _BCO_ 31-2) and a person
pursuing an offender in accordance with Matthew 18 ( _BCO_ 32-2). These two
processes in the _BCO_ are parallel and distinct; this Overture would do away with the
right o fa person to pursue a matthew 18 claim against another with an aim toward
restoration and repentance. This Overture would also prevent a church member from
challenging a decision o fa pres byte ry to decline to institute process against a
Teaching Elder.

2. [Overture 2 was referred by the Stated Clerk to MNA.]

3. That **Overture 3** from Arizona Presbytery (“Erect Ad Interim Committee on Christian
Nationalism”, p. 39) be answered with **reference to the answer to Overture 47.**
**113-7-2**

4. That **Overture 4** from South Texas Presbytery (“Erect Ad Interim Study Committee on
Christian Nationalism”, p. 40) be answered **reference to the answer to Overture 47.**
**104-6-3**

5. That **Overture 5** from Calvary Presbytery (“Grant _BCO_ 60-63 Full Constitutional
Status”, p. 42) be answered with **reference to the answer to Overture 26.** **112-7-2**

6. That **Overture 6** from Rocky Mountain Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 18-2 to Allow
Flexibility on Applications to Come Under Care”, p. 43) be answered in the
**affirmative as amended.** **116-6-1**

**Be it resolved:** That _BCO_ 18-2 be amended by the current language as follows (single
underline and single strikethrough for original overture; double underline and double
strike through for OC amendment):

**18-2** . Every applicant for the ministry must put himself under the care of
Presbytery, which should ordinarily be the Presbytery that has jurisdiction of the
church of which he is a member. The endorsement of his Session must be given to
the Presbytery, consisting of testimonials regarding his Christian character and
promise of usefulness in the ministry. The endorsement should also describe the
activities of ministry the applicant has participated in with brief evaluation.

Every applicant for care shall be a member of the congregation whose
session provides an endorsement for at least six months before filing his application,
except in those cases deemed extraordinary by the Presbytery.

Every applicant must should file his application with the clerk of the
Presbytery at least one month before the meeting of the Presbytery (unless a
Presbytery has a different stated application deadline). An applicant for care may not
be received under care and examined for ordination at the same meeting of the
Presbytery, since he must serve a period of at least one year of internship prior to
ordination (see _BCO_ 19-7 and 21-4). An applicant for internship is obliged to be

<a id="ga52-p100"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=102 printed_page=100 -->

under care and may be licensed to preach the Gospel; further, one who is not already
under care may be taken under care, be licensed to preach the Gospel, and become
an intern at the same meeting of Presbytery.

7. That **Overture 7** from Missouri Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 34-1 Regarding Advisors
and Original Jurisdiction in Process Against a TE”, p. 45) be answered in the **negative.**
**113-10-2**

**Grounds:** The challenges of _BCO_ 34-1 are not adequately answered by this Overture.
Further, the language of “failed to uphold the Constitution” is both vague and subject
to being triggered by very minor violations of the Constitution.

8. That **Overture 8** from Nashville Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 24-1 to Require REs and
Deacons to State Confessional Differences”, p. 49) be answered in the **affirmative as**
**amended.** **97-31-0**

**Be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] General Assembly amend _BCO_ 24-1 to include the
following two paragraphs (single underline and single strikethrough for original
overture; double underline and double strike through for OC amendment):

**24-1.** Every church shall elect persons to the offices of ruling elder and deacon in
the following manner: At such times as determined by the Session, communicant
members of the congregation may submit names to the Session, keeping in mind that
each prospective officer should be an active male member who meets the
qualifications set forth in 1 Timothy 3 and Titus 1. Nominees for the office of ruling
elder and/or deacon shall receive instruction in the qualifications and work of the
office.

a. Each nominee shall be examined in:
1. his Christian experience, especially his personal character and family
management (based on the qualifications set out in 1 Timothy 3:1-7 and
Titus 1:6-9),
2. his knowledge of Bible content,
3. his knowledge of the system of doctrine, government, discipline contained
in the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America ( _BCO_ Preface III,
The Constitution Defined),
4. the duties of the office to which he has been nominated, and
5. his willingness to give assent to the questions required for ordination. ( _BCO_
24-6).

b. In the examination of the nominee’s personal character, the Session shall give
specific attention to potential notorious concerns. Careful attention must be
given to his practical struggle against sinful actions, as well as to persistent sinful
desires. The nominee must give clear testimony of reliance upon his union with
Christ and the benefits thereof by the Holy Spirit, depending on this work of
grace to make progress over sin (Psalm 103:2-5, Romans 8:29) and to bear fruit
(Psalm 1:3, Gal. 5:22-23). While imperfection will remain, when confessing sins
and sinful temptations publicly, the nominee must exercise great care not to
diminish the seriousness of those sins in the eyes of the congregation, as though
they were matters of little consequence, but rather should testify to the work of
the Holy Spirit in his progress in holiness (1 Cor. 6:9-11).

<a id="ga52-p101"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=103 printed_page=101 -->

c. Notwithstanding the above, the Session may render a decision on Christian
experience at any point in the process, and based on that decision, may judge
him ineligible for that election.

d. While our Constitution does not require the nominee’s affirmation of every
statement and/or proposition of doctrine in our _Confession of Faith_ and
_Catechisms_, it is the right and responsibility of the Session to determine if the
nominee is out of accord with any of the fundamentals of these doctrinal
standards and, as a consequence, may not be able in good faith sincerely to
receive and adopt the _Confession of Faith_ and _Catechisms_ of this Church as
containing the system of doctrine taught in the Holy Scriptures (cf. _BCO_ 21-5,
Q.2; 24-6, Q.2).

e. Therefore, in examining a nominee for ordination, the Session shall inquire not
only into the nominee’s knowledge and views in the areas specified above but
also shall require the nominee to state the specific instances in which he may
differ with the _Confession of Faith_ and _Catechisms_ in any of their statements
and/or propositions. The court may grant an exception to any difference of
doctrine only if in the court’s judgment the nominee’s declared difference is not
out of accord with any fundamental of our system of doctrine because the
difference is neither hostile to the system nor strikes at the vitals of religion. The
Session shall record in its records ( _BCO_ 12-7) such declared differences (or that
there were no differences) and its assessment of each declared difference in
accordance with the categories set forth in _RAO_ 16-3.e.7.

f. If there are candidates eligible for the election, the Session shall report to the
congregation those eligible, giving at least thirty (30) days prior notice of the
time and place of a congregational meeting for elections.

g. If one-fourth (1/4) of the persons entitled to vote shall at any time request the
Session to call a congregational meeting for the purpose of electing additional
officers, it shall be the duty of the Session to call such a meeting on the above
procedure. The number of officers to be elected shall be determined by the
congregation after hearing the Session’s recommendation.

9. That **Overture 9** from James River Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to Make AC
Members Appointed by Committees and Agencies Non-voting Advisory Members”,
p. 52) be **referred back to the Presbytery.** **75-51-1**

**Grounds:** This Overture included several amendments which increased the risk of
unintended consequences and general unease of the Committee. After much debate
and inquiry regarding whether or not commentary from the Stated Clerk or CCB was
appropriate, it was decided to refer it back to the Presbytery for clarification,
perfection, and possible resubmission.

10. That **Overture 10** from Central Florida Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 22 to Affirm the
‘Consent of the Governed’ Regarding Assistant Pastors”, p. 55) be **referred back to**
**the Presbytery.** **119-7-2**

<a id="ga52-p102"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=104 printed_page=102 -->

**Grounds:** This Overture raises a significant question about the issue in our polity of
Assistant Pastors. After significant debate and attempts to perfect the Overture, the
decision was made to refer it back to the Presbytery for clarification, perfection, and
possible resubmission.

11. That **Overture 11** from South Texas Presbytery (“To Study Incorporating Bitcoin into
Session, Presbytery, and Committee & Agency Balance Sheets”, p. 57) be answered
in the **negative.** **100-15-2**

**Grounds:** This Overture was voted in the negative without debate.

12. That **Overture 12** from Providence Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 58-5 to Specify Only
Officers May Distribute the Lord’s Supper”, p. 59) be answered in the **affirmative.**
**78-49-1**

13. That **Overture 13** from Great Lakes Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 25-1 to Specify Only
Members in Good Standing May Vote”, p. 63) be answered in the **negative. 96-22-4**

**Grounds:** The Overture seeks to solve a problem which does not actually exist. The
two provisions of the _BCO_ (24-3 and 25-1) deal with different matters, the difference
between members in good standing and those under discipline ( _BCO_ 24-3) and the
difference between communing and non-communing members (25-1).

14. That **Overture 14** from Great Lakes Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 38-1 to Indicate Ways
a Court May Interact with an Offended Party”, p. 64) be answered in the **negative.**
**104-3-4**

**Grounds:** The current language of _BCO_ 38-1 gives sufficient flexibility for courts to
have contact with offended persons. Further, courts could adopt a particular method
of contacting offended persons as a part of their bylaws or rules of operation.

15. That **Overture 15** from Mississippi Valley Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 36-4 to Add
Language for the Administration of Definite Suspension from Office”, p. 65) be
answered in the **affirmative as amended.** **124-0-1**

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 36-4 be amended by adding the following language (single
underline and single strikethrough for original overture; double underline and double
strike through for OC amendment):

**36-4.** Definite suspension from office should be administered in the presence of
the court alone or in open session of the court, as it may deem best, and public
announcement thereof shall be at the court’s discretion. When the court has resolved
to pass this sentence, the moderator shall address the offending brother to the
following purpose:

**Whereas, you, ___________________ (here describe the brother as a**
**teaching elder, ruling elder, or deacon) are convicted by sufficient proof (or are**
**guilty by your own confession) of the sin of ______________ (here insert the**
**offense), and have satisfied the court as to your repentance by the grace of God;**
**yet for the credit of religion, the honor of Christ, and your own good we the**

<a id="ga52-p103"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=105 printed_page=103 -->

**_______________________ (Presbytery (or Church Session) in the name and by the**
**authority of the Lord Jesus Christ, do now declare you suspended from the exercise**
**of your office, and all the functions thereof, for __________________ (here state the**
**definite period of time of the suspension). (here state the dates on which the**
**suspension begins and ends).**

To this shall be added such advice or admonition as may be judged necessary, and
the whole shall be concluded with prayer to almighty God that He would follow this
act of discipline with His blessing.

16. That **Overture 16** from Mississippi Valley Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 36-5 to Conform
the Language to _BCO_ 37-3 regarding Suspension from Office”, p. 67) be answered
in the **affirmative as amended.** **124-0-0**

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 36-5 be amended by adding “/or” to the word “and” in “(and
from the exercise of your office)” (single underline and single strikethrough for original
overture; double underline and double strike through for OC amendment):

**Whereas, you, ___________________ (here describe the person as a**
**teaching elder, ruling elder, deacon, or private member of the church) are**
**convicted by sufficient proof (or are guilty by your own confession) of the sin of**
**___________________ (here insert the offense), we the ___________________**
**(Presbytery (or Church Session) in the name and by the authority of the Lord Jesus**
**Christ, do now declare you suspended from the Sacraments of the Church (and/or**
**from the exercise of your office), until you give satisfactory evidence of repentance.**

17. [Overture 17 was referred by the Stated Clerk to MNA.]

18. [Overture 18 was referred by the Stated Clerk to RUF.]

19. That **Overture 19** from Eastern Carolina Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 57-5 to Revise the
Membership Vows”, p. 71) be answered in the **negative.** **117-7-2**

**Grounds:** The current questions of membership are time-tested, Biblical, and
Trinitarian. There is no need to revise them at this time.

20. [Overture 20 was referred by the Stated Clerk to MNA.]

21. [Overture 21 was withdrawn by the Presbytery.]

22. That **Overture 22** from Pacific Northwest Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 20-3, 24-3, and
25-1 to clarify “Regular Standing” regarding minimum voting age”, p. 75) be
answered in the **negative.** **88-36-3**

**Grounds:** This Overture would create a distinct subclass of communing member
without all of the privileges of other communing members.

23. That **Overture 23** from Pacific Northwest Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 41-3 to Allow
Supplemental Judges for a Session Trial”, p. 94) be answered in the **affirmative.**
**64-49-8**

<a id="ga52-p104"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=106 printed_page=104 -->

24. That **Overture 24** from Evangel Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 24-1 to Clarify a Session’s
Role in Examining Officer Nominees”, p. 96) be answered in the **affirmative as**
**amended.** **103-12-2**

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 24-1 be amended by the deletion of one word (“him” in the
paragraph beginning with “Notwithstanding the above…”) and the addition of one
phrase (in the same paragraph), as follows (single underline and single strikethrough
for original overture; double underline and double strike through for OC amendment):

**24-1.** Every church shall elect persons to the offices of ruling elder and deacon in the
following manner: At such times as determined by the Session, communicant
members of the congregation may submit names to the Session, keeping in mind that
each prospective officer should be an active male member who meets the
qualifications set forth in 1 Timothy 3 and Titus 1. Nominees for the office of ruling
elder and/or deacon shall receive instruction in the qualifications and work of the
office. Each nominee shall be examined in:

a. his Christian experience, especially his personal character and family
management (based on the qualifications set out in 1 Timothy 3:1-7 and
Titus 1:6-9),

- **b.** his knowledge of Bible content,
- **c.** his knowledge of the system of doctrine, government, discipline contained in the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America ( _BCO_ Preface III, The Constitution Defined),
- **d.** the duties of the office to which he has been nominated, and
- **e.** his willingness to give assent to the questions required for ordination. ( _BCO_

24-6).
In the examination of the nominee’s personal character, the Session shall give
specific attention to potential notorious concerns. Careful attention must be given to
his practical struggle against sinful actions, as well as to persistent sinful desires. The
nominee must give clear testimony of reliance upon his union with Christ and the
benefits thereof by the Holy Spirit, depending on this work of grace to make progress
over sin (Psalm 103:2-5, Romans 8:29) and to bear fruit (Psalm 1:3, Gal. 5:22-23).
While imperfection will remain, when confessing sins and sinful temptations
publicly, the nominee must exercise great care not to diminish the seriousness of
those sins in the eyes of the congregation, as though they were matters of little
consequence, but rather should testify to the work of the Holy in his progress in
holiness (1 Cor. 6:9-11).

Notwithstanding the above, the Session may render a decision on Christian
experience at any point in the process, and based on that decision, may judge him
the nominee him, whether previously ordained or not, ineligible for that election.

If there are candidates eligible for the election, the Session shall report to the
congregation those eligible, giving at least thirty (30) days prior notice of the time
and place of a congregational meeting for elections.

If one-fourth (1/4) of the persons entitled to vote shall at any time request the
Session to call a congregational meeting for the purpose of electing additional
officers, it shall be the duty of the Session to call such a meeting on the above
procedure. The number of officers to be elected shall be determined by the
congregation after hearing the Session’s recommendation.

<a id="ga52-p105"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=107 printed_page=105 -->

25. That **Overture 25** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 46-4 to Add a
Class of Associate Membership for Honorably Retired TEs”, p. 98) be **referred back**
**to the Presbytery. 114-2-1**

**Grounds:** The Committee on Constitutional Business advised that the Overture is in
conflict with the Constitution (specifically _BCO_ 13-1 and 13-2), and therefore we
refer it back to the Presbytery for clarification, perfection, and possible resubmission.

26. That **Overture 26** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery (“Erect Ad Interim Committee
to Revise the Directory for Worship for Authoritative Use”, p. 100) be answered in
the **affirmative as amended.** **82-40-0**

**Be it resolved** that the Northwest Georgia Presbytery hereby overtures the 52 [nd]
General Assembly to (single underline and single strikethrough for original overture;
double underline and double strike through for OC amendment):

1. Form an ad hoc interim committee to finish the work of revising revise propose
revision to portions of the “Directory for the Worship of God” not yet given full
authority, in accordance with Scripture and the _Westminster Standards_, for
authoritative use in the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) according to the
rules of the _Book of Church Order_,

a. Utilizing the various NAPARC denominations’ Directories of Public
Worship as a guide

- **b.** Including Scriptural and confessional References
- **c.** Presenting various chapters to the General Assembly for adoption and/or amendment in accord with the provisions set forth in _BCO_ 262 until their work is complete.

2. Authorize the Moderator to appoint the seven voting members of this
Committee, per _RAO_ 9-4 and _RAO_ 19-1 ( _Robert's Rules_, RONR, 11th ed., pp.
174-175, pp. 495-496, and p. 579).

- **a.** These members shall be PCA teaching elders, or and PCA ruling elders, and the Committee shall include at least three of each. (Anyone may suggest names to the Moderator.) The Moderator may to make his appointments after the Assembly adjourns.
- **b.** The Stated Clerk shall serve as a non-voting advisor of the committee.

Committee budget shall be $15,000 $10,000. This expense shall be included in the
Administrative Committee budget solely from designated gifts. Presbyteries,
churches, and individuals are highly encouraged to contribute funds to the AC,
designated for this work.

27. That **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery (“Receive Suggestions for
Permanent Committee and Agency Operations, Resources, and Expenses”, p. 103)
be answered in the **negative.** **111-7-1**

**Grounds:** In light of the Committee's recommendation of the establishment of two
other study committees, it was the opinion of the Committee that it would not be
wise to present this study committee recommendation to the assembly for
consideration, especially in light of _RAO_ 9-3.

28. [Overture 28 was referred by the Stated Clerk to MNA.]

<a id="ga52-p106"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=108 printed_page=106 -->

29. That **Overture 29** from Houston Metro Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 15-5.c to Clarify
the Handling of an SJC Minority Decision by GA”, p. 107) be answered in the
**affirmative as amended.** **116-5-4**

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 15-5.c be amended as follows (single underline and single
strikethrough for original overture; double underline and double strike through for OC
amendment):

c.(1) If, within twenty-four (24) hours of the time of adjournment of a Standing
judicial commission meeting at which a final decision was rendered ina case, at
least one-third (1/3) of the voting members of the Standing Judicial Commission
voting o na particular final decision file written notice of their intention to file a
minority decision with the Stated Clerk of the General Assembly, and within twenty
(20) days from the adjournment do file such a minority decision, such minority
decision shall be considered a minority report and shall be referred, with the report
of the Standing Judicial Commission, to the General Assembly. In each instance “file”
shall be understood as defined by the _Operating Manual for Standing Judicial_
_Commission_ .
(2) No such reference* from the Standing Judicial Commission shall be considered
by the General Assembly unless the report of the Standing Judicial Commission, and
the minority report, the Record of the Case, and all briefs timely filed by the parties
have been distributed in electronic form mailed to the clerk of Session of each church
and published in the _Commissioner Handbook_ at least thirty (30) days prior to the
meeting of the General Assembly.
(3) The Assembly shall act upon such a reference* from the Standing Judicial
Commission, in each case without question, discussion, debate, or amendment, as
follows:

(a) The Standing Judicial Commission shall have 30 minutes to present its
decision to the Assembly.
(b) The minority shall have 30 minutes to present its decision to the Assembly.
(c) The Standing Judicial Commission shall have 10 minutes to reply to the
minority report.
(d) The decision of the minority shall be proposed, and the General Assembly
shall, without question, discussion, debate, or amendment, approve or
disapprove of the minority report.
(e) If the General Assembly approves the minority report, it shall be the

decision of the General Assembly and printed in its minutes. There may be
no complaint or appeal from such a final decision of the General Assembly.
(f) If the General Assembly disapproves the minority report, the General
Assembly shall take up the decision of the Standing Judicial Commission
and without question, discussion, debate, or amendment, approve or
disapprove of the decision of the Standing Judicial Commission.
(4) If the General Assembly approves of a proposed the Standing Judicial
Commission’s decision, it shall be the decision of the General Assembly, and printed
in its minutes. There may be no complaint or appeal from such a final decision of
the General Assembly. If the General Assembly finally disapproves of both proposed
decisions, it must set the case for hearing before the General Assembly or a special
commission of six (6) Teaching Elders and six (6) Ruling Elders appointed by the
Moderator of the General Assembly it, and in either instance the case shall be tried
on the record as delivered to the Stated Clerk. Any such special commission shall
then proceed to consider the case and shall report its decision, in like manner, to the

<a id="ga52-p107"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=109 printed_page=107 -->

General Assembly for its approval or disapproval. In any event, the full record of the
case, including written testimony of witnesses, all documents, exhibits and papers
shall be delivered to the Stated Clerk for permanent preservation.

30. That **Overture 30** from Houston Metro Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 8-4, 20-1, 21-1,
21-12, and 23-1 regarding the Calling and Dissolution of TE Relationships for
Needful Works”, p. 111) be answered in the **affirmative as amended.** **112-4-7**

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 8-4, 20-1, and 21-1 be amended as follows (single underline
and single strikethrough for original overture; double underline and double strike
through for OC amendment):

**8-4.** As the Lord has given different gifts to men and has committed to some special
gifts and callings, the Church is authorized to call and appoint some to labor as
teaching elders in such works as may be needful to the Church ( _cf_ . _BCO_ 21-12).
When a teaching elder is called to such needful work, it shall be incumbent upon
him to make full proof of his ministry by disseminating the Gospel for the edification
of the Church. He shall make a report to the Presbytery at least once each year.

**20-1** . Before a candidate, or licentiate, can be ordained to the office of the
ministry, he must receive a call to a definite work. Ordinarily the call must come
from a church, Presbytery, a permanent Committee or Agency (see _BCO_ 14-1.12), or
the General Assembly of this denomination. If the call comes from another source,
the Presbytery shall always make a record of the reasons why it considers the work
t ob ea valid christian ministry. (See also _BCO_ 8-7 and 21-1).
A proper call must be written and in the hands of the Presbytery prior to
being acted upon by a Presbytery. It must include financial arrangements (such as
salary, vacation, insurance, retirement, etc.) between those calling and the one
called, and assurance that the definite work will afford the liberty to proclaim and
practice fully and freely the whole counsel of God, as contained in the Scriptures
and understood in the _Westminster Confession of Faith_ . It shall be in accord with the

_BCO_ 8.

**21-1.** No minister, licentiate or candidates hall receive a call from a church ora
call to a needful work ( _BCO_ 21-12) but by the permission of his Presbytery. When a
call has been presented to the Presbytery, if found in order and the Presbytery deems
it for the good of the Church, they shall place it in the hands of the person to whom
it is addressed.
Ordinarily a candidate or licentiate may not be granted permission by the
Presbytery to move on to the field to which he has been called, prior to his
examination for licensure or ordination. Likewise an ordained minister from another
Presbyterian Church in America Presbytery or another denomination, ordinarily shall
not move on to the field to which he has been called until examined and received
by Presbytery.

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 21 be amended by adding a new paragraph, _BCO_ 21-12 as
follows (single underlining and single strikethrough for original overture; double
underline and double strike through for OC amendment):

**21-12.** **Needful Works and Calls from Outside the PCA** .

<a id="ga52-p108"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=110 printed_page=108 -->

a. A minister, licentiate, or candidate that receives a call from a body other than
ap ca church shall be considered to have a call to a needful work ( _cf._ _BCO_ 84 if the call is approved by pPresbytery). Such calls shall include (without
limitation): (i) calls from the General Assembly or a Ppermanent Committee or
Agency, (ii) chaplaincy calls from a pPresbytery, MNA, other Ppermanent
Committee or Agency, or other body, (iii) missionary calls from MTW or other
missionary society, (iv) campus minister calls from RUF, (v) calls from outside
the PCA, and (vi) calls t ob ea teacher ina school of divinity or to give instruction
in the doctrines and duties of religion in a school, college, or university.

b. **Ordination to a Needful Work** . The day appointed for the ordination having
come, and the Presbytery being convened, a sermon suitable for the occasion
shall be preached by a person appointed or invited by the Presbytery. The
Presbytery member appointed to preside shall afterwards briefly recite from the
pulpit the proceedings of the Presbytery preparatory to the ordination; he shall
point out the nature and importance of the ordinance, and endeavor to impress
the audience with a proper sense of the solemnity of the transaction.

**Questions for Ordination**

Then, addressing himself to the candidate, he shall propose to him the following
questions:

**1. Do you believe the Scriptures of the Old and New Testaments, as originally**
**given, to be the inerrant Word of God, the only infallible rule of faith and**
**practice?**

**2. Do you sincerely receive and adopt the** Confession of Faith **and the**
Catechisms **of this Church, as containing the system of doctrine taught in the**
**Holy Scriptures; and do you further promise that if at any time you find**
**yourself out of accord with any of the fundamentals of this system of doctrine,**
**you will on your own initiative, make known to your Presbytery the change**
**which has taken place in your views since the assumption of this ordination**
**vow?**

**3. Do you approve of the form of government and discipline of the Presbyterian**
**Church in America, in conformity with the general principles of Biblical polity?**

**4. Do you promise subjection to your brethren in the Lord?**

**5. Have you been induced, as far as you know your own heart, to seek the**
**office of the holy ministry from love to God and a sincere desire to promote**
**His glory in the Gospel of His Son?**

**6. Do you promise to be zealous and faithful in maintaining the truths of the**
**Gospel and the purity and peace and unity of the Church, whatever**
**persecution or opposition may arise unto you on that account?**

**7. Do you engage to be faithful and diligent in the exercise of all your duties**
**as a christian and a minister of the Gospel, whether personal or relational,**
**private or public; and to endeavor by the grace of God to adorn the profession**

<a id="ga52-p109"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=111 printed_page=109 -->

**of the Gospel in your manner of life, and to walk with exemplary piety before**
**the flock of which God shall make you overseer?**

**8. Do you now undertake this Needful Work, and do you promise, in reliance**
**on God for strength, to be faithful in the discharge of all the duties incumbent**
**on you as a minister of the Gospel of the Lord Jesus Christ?**

The candidate having answered these questions in the affirmative, the presiding
minister shall propose to the calling entity representatives from the entity extending
the call the following questions:

**1. Do you, the people of this calling entity** r **epresentatives from the entity**
**extending the call, continue to profess your readiness to receive**
_________________, **whom you have called to this Needful Work?**

**2. Do you promise to receive the word of truth from his mouth with meekness**
**and love?**

**3. Do you promise to encourage him in his labors, and to assist his endeavors**
**for your instruction and spiritual edification?**

**4. Do you engage to continue to him while he is in this call that competent**
**worldly maintenance which you have promised, and to furnish him with**
**whatever you may see needful for the honor of religion and for his comfort**
**among you?**

The people of the calling entity representatives from the entity extending the call
having answered these questions in the affirmative, by holding up their right hands,
the candidate shall kneel, and the presiding minister shall, with prayer and the laying
ono f hands of the Presbytery, according to the apostolic example, solemnly set him
apart to the holy office of the Gospel ministry. Prayer being ended, he shall rise from
his knees; and the minister who presides shall first, followed by all members of the
Presbytery, take him by the right hand, saying, in words to this effect:

**We give you the right hand of fellowship, to take part in this ministry with us.**

The presiding minister shall then say:

**I now pronounce and declare that __________________ has been regularly**
**elected, and ordained, agreeable to the Word of God, and according to the**
**Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America; and that as such he is**
**entitled to all support, encouragement, honor, and obedience in the Lord: In**
**the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost. Amen.**

After which the minister presiding, or some other teaching or ruling elder appointed
for the purpose, shall give a solemn charge to the minister to persevere in the
discharge of his duties, and then after prayer and the singing of a psalm, or hymn,
the congregation shall be dismissed with the benediction. The Presbytery shall duly
record its proceedings.

<a id="ga52-p110"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=112 printed_page=110 -->

**Installation to a Needful Work.** In the installation of an ordained minister to a
Needful Work, the following questions are to be substituted for those addressed to a
candidate for ordination, namely:

**Questions for Installation**

**1. Are you now willing to take charge of this Needful Work, agreeable to**
**your declaration in accepting the call?**

**2. Do you conscientiously believe and declare, as far as you know your**
**own heart, that, in taking upon you this charge, you are influenced by a**
**sincere desire to promote the glory of God and the good of the Church?**

**3. Do you solemnly promise that, by the assistance of the grace of God,**
**you will endeavor faithfully to discharge all the duties of this Needful**
**Work, and will be careful to maintain a deportment in all respects**
**becoming a minister of the Gospel of Christ, agreeable to your ordination**
**engagements?**

The candidate having answered these questions in the affirmative, the presiding
minister shall propose to the calling entity the following questions:

**1. Do you, the people of this calling entity representatives from the entity**
**extending the call, continue to profess your readiness to receive**
**_________________, whom you have called to this Needful Work?**
**2. Do you promise to receive the word of truth from his mouth with**
**meekness and love?**
**32. Do you promise to encourage him in his labors, and to assist his**
**endeavors for your in instruction and spiritual edification for those to**
**whom he is called to minister?**
**45. Do you engage to continue to him while he is in this call that**
**competent worldly maintenance which you have promised, and to furnish**
**him with whatever you may see needful for the honor of religion and for**
**his comfort among you?**

c. **Transfer of ma nina needful Work.** when a man who has a call to a needful
Work desires to move from one field of labor to another, if the field of labor is
in another Presbytery, the minister must may be examined and approved by the
that Presbytery to labor in the field to which he is being transferred, and must
may be released for transfer by his present Presbytery (see _BCO_ 13-6), or the
procedures set forth in _BCO_ 13-2 (sentence two) and _BCO_ 19-1 shall be
followed.

**Be it further resolved** that _BCO_ 23-1 be amended by adding the following at the end
of the current section as a new paragraph (single underlining and single strikethrough
for original overture; double underline and double strike through for OC amendment):

In the case of a needful work ( _BCO_ 21-12), when any minister shall tender the
resignation of his call to his Presbytery, the Presbytery shall cite the calling such an
entity to appear, to show cause why the Presbytery should or should not accept the

<a id="ga52-p111"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=113 printed_page=111 -->

resignation. If the calling such an entity fails to appear, or if its reasons for retaining
the minister be deemed insufficient, his resignation shall be accepted and the relation
dissolved. If any calling entity desires to be relieved of the minister called to the
particular needful work, a similar procedure shall be observed. In any case, the
minister must not physically leave the field until the Presbytery or its commission
empowered to handle uncontested requests for dissolution has dissolved the relation.

31. That **Overture 31** from Houston Metro Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 31-10 to Require
Vote of the Entire Court to Suspend an Officer’s Official Functions during Process”,
p. 121) be answered in the **affirmative as amended.** **110-12-2**

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 31-10 is amended as follows (single underline and single
strikethrough for original overture; double underline and double strike through for OC
amendment):

**31-10** . when a member o fa church court i sunder process, all his official functions
may be administratively suspended at the court’s discretion; but this shall never be
done in the way of censure, and this requires a two-thirds (2/3) majority of the voting
members present and voting (not counting abstentions) at the meeting of the entire
court Session or Presbytery, even if the process is being conducted by a commission.

32. That **Overture 32** from Houston Metro Presbytery (“Amend _BCO_ 36 to Provide for
Elevation of a Censure without an Entirely New Trial”, p. 122) be answered in the
**affirmative as amended.** **102-17-7**

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 36 be amended to add a new paragraph, _BCO_ 36-8, and
_BCO_ 42-2 be amended as follows (single underline and single strikethrough for
original overture; double underline and double strike through for OC amendment):

**36-8** . When members of a court wish to consider the elevation of an indefinite
suspension, whether from office or from the Sacraments ( _BCO_ 36-5) to the censure
of deposition or excommunication respectively, it shall not be required to initiate
process _de novo_ according to _BCO_ 31-2 or _BCO_ 32-2. The indefinitely suspended
person is not in good standing and has already been found impenitent, resulting in
indefinite suspension ( _BCO_ 30-3). However, the indefinitely suspended person has
aright to a hearing in the matter. The court supervising the indefinite suspension may
consider a motion to find him “incorrigible and contumacious” ( _cf_ . _BCO_ 30-4) and
present evidence to that effect. The court shall give at least ten (10) thirty (30) days’
notice of such a motion to the indefinitely suspended person and permit him the
privilege of the floor to plead and give a defense evidence of his repentance before
the original trial court having jurisdiction ( _BCO_ 33-1, 34-1) and not any trial
commission. The court, upon completing its hearing, shall vote to determine whether
or not to elevate the indefinite suspension to deposition or excommunication. A
person whose suspension is so elevated has the right of Appeal ( _BCO_ 42-2).

**42-2.** The only parties entitled to an appeal are those who have submitted to a regular
trial, those appealing a censure ina _BCO_ 38-1 case without process, those appealing
the elevation of a censure under _BCO_ 36-8, and those appealing a _BCO_ 34-10
divestiture without censure.

<a id="ga52-p112"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=114 printed_page=112 -->

33. That **Overture 33** from TE Fred Greco (“Amend _BCO_ 57-2 regarding the Examination
of Young Persons for Admission to the Sealing Ordinances”, p. 124) be answered in
**the affirmative.** **101-23-4**

34. That **Overture 34** from Savannah River Presbytery (“Amend _RAO_ 16 to Require
Reporting on Session and Diaconate Membership and Duties”, p. 125) be answered
in the **affirmative as amended.** **93-32-1**

**Be it resolved** the Savannah River Presbytery respectfully overtures the 52 [nd] General
Assembly of the PCA to protect the constitutional practice of ordaining a Diaconate
by adding the following to _RAO_ 16-4.c.1 (single underline and single strikethrough
for original overture; double underline and double strike through for OC amendment):

A current Directory of Presbytery, including (1) a list of all teaching elders, with their
addresses and their status; and of all churches and missions with the address of the
church, and the name and address of the Clerk of Session, (2) the list of persons who
comprise the Session and the Diaconate of each church arranged by their ordained
or given titles; and if no Diaconate exists, a report from the Session as to why it has
not been established and whether or not the duties have devolved upon the ruling
elders, according to _BCO_ 9-2 unordained diaconal assistants, or any other committee
or member of the church, or whether they are being assisted by unordained diaconal
assistants, or any other committee or member of the church, (23) a listing of all
officers and committee members of the regular committees of presbytery, and (34)
a list identifying all candidates under care, interns, and licentiates of the presbytery
with their addresses and their status.

**Be** **it** **further** **resolved** to add the following to _RAO_ 16-6.c.1:
Exceptions of substance: Apparent violations of the Scripture or serious irregularities
from the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America, actions out of accord
with the deliverances of the General Assembly, and matters of impropriety and
important delinquencies, and any non-compliance with _RAO_ 16-3.e.5-6 and _RAO_
16-4.c.1 should shall be reported under this category.

43. That **Overture 43** from Calvary Presbytery (“Amend _RAO_ 4-11 regarding Data on
Age and Ethnicity”, p. 142) be answered in the **affirmative as amended.** **76-28-3**

**Be it resolved** that Calvary Presbytery hereby overtures the 52 [nd] General Assembly
to amend _RAO_ 4-11 by adding the following to the third paragraph (single underline
and single strikethrough for original overture; double underline and double strike
through for OC amendment):

**4-11.** The budget for each permanent Committee and Agency shall be submitted
to the Administrative Committee of General Assembly, which shall independently
evaluate the proposed budget of each permanent Committee and Agency and report
to the Assembly its considered opinion on the adoption. To protect the ministry and
fiduciary responsibilities of the Committees and Agency Boards, the financial
coordination and independent evaluation of the budgets by the Administrative
Committee shall be for review, discussion, and recommendation to the Assembly.
Should modifications in budgets be deemed necessary by the Assembly,
special care shall be taken that changes not be made in such a way as to threaten
the continuity or effectiveness of the Committee’s or Agency’s ministry. Budgets of
permanent Committees and Agencies that are agreed upon by the Administrative
Committee may be changed only by a two-thirds vote of the Assembly
commissioners present and voting at the time the budget is submitted for adoption.
In the event the Administrative Committee disagrees with the budget submitted by

<a id="ga52-p114"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=116 printed_page=114 -->

one of the Committees or Boards, in whole or any item thereof, the General Assembly
may adopt either the Committee’s or Board’s budget or the Administrative
Committee’s recommended budget by simple majority. The requirement of a twothirds vote of the General Assembly applies only to changes not recommended by
the Administrative Committee, initiated on the floor of the Assembly. With respect
to the Committees and Agencies, financial coordination is through the Administrative
Committee to the Assembly and from the General Assembly to the Committees and
Agencies. The Administrative Committee is a “filter upward” and has no downward
authority.
The Administrative Committee shall annually provide and publish
guidelines for the Partnership Share Giving Program of the PCA, including the
Ministry Ask. These guidelines shall include statistical information expressed both in
per capita giving terms and in proportional (percentage) giving terms to demonstrate
the giving necessary to attain full funding for budgets of the General Assembly
Committees and Agencies. Such statistical information shall not include data
pertaining to age or ethnicity.
The Committees and Agencies receiving support through the PCA
Partnership Share Giving Program shall present their financial needs and their
Ministry Ask to the Administrative Committee for review and recommendation in the
course of the regular budgeting process. Each Committee and Agency shall then be
free to request from the PCA churches and constituency the contributions necessary
to fund its approved budget.
While all churches remain free to give their contributions as they by God’s
guidance determine, all churches are encouraged to give, as they are able, at least
ten percent (10%) of their tithes and offerings to the PCA causes of presbyteries and
General Assembly.
The Administrative Committee shall maintain a manual containing the
Affirmation of Core Financial Standards. This information, by which each PCA
General Assembly ministry (Committee or Agency) shall function and by which the
budget process is governed for each Committee and Agency, shall be available to all
constituencies.

45. [Overture 45 was referred by the Stated Clerk to MNA.]

46. [Overture 46 was referred by the Stated Clerk to MNA.]

47. That **Overture 47** from Great Lakes Presbytery (“Erect ana d interim committee on
Christian Nationalism”, p. 150) be answered in the **affirmative as amended.**
**73-50-0**

**Be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] PCA General Assembly erect and convene an Ad Interim
Study Committee on Christian Nationalism ( _RAO_ 9-1), to study the relationship
between Christian Nationalism, Ethno-Nationalism, and related teachings. Further,
the committee shall advise on whether these teachings and formulations are in
conformity with the system of doctrine taught in the _Westminster Standards_ or where
they may diverge from the system of doctrine (single underline and single
strikethrough for original overture; double underline and double strike through for OC
amendment).

<a id="ga52-p115"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=117 printed_page=115 -->

**Further**, the committee shall write a report that gives pastoral guidance when
addressing congregations, new members, and future officers of the PCA.

**Be it further resolved** that the General Assembly’s moderator be empowered as a
commission to populate the ad interim committee with three (3) Teaching Elders and
four (4) Ruling Elders.

**Be it further resolved** that the budget for the study committee be set at $10,000/year
and that funds be derived from gifts to the AC designated for that purpose, with the
General Assembly appealing to the various Presbyteries to fund this initiative via such
gifts ( _RAO_ 9-3).

**Be it further resolved** that the Great Lakes Presbytery is committed to offer a $1,000
gift, Arizona Presbytery is committed to offering a $2,500 gift, and that members of
South Texas Presbytery committed to offer $5,000 per year to assist in funding the
work of this Ad Interim Study Committee.

48. That **Overture 48** from Tennessee Valley Presbytery (“Erect an Ad Interim Committee
for a Pastoral Letter on Christian Citizenship and Church-State Relations”, p. 152) be
answered in the **affirmative as amended.** **113-8-1**

**Therefore, be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church
in America direct its moderator to appoint an unfunded ad interim committee to
produce a pastoral letter [1] or short paper applying the doctrine of the Constitution of
the Presbyterian Church in America to questions of Christian citizenship and churchstate relations for the edification of the Church. The committee will be composed of
four teaching elders and three ruling elders and will set its own procedures and
timelines.

That the 52 [nd] General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America does hereby
join with the Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church (221 [st] General Synod) and
with the Reformed Presbyterian Church in North America (193 [rd] Synod) in
condemning without distinction any theological or political teaching which posits a
superiority of race or ethnic identity born of immutable human characteristics, and
does call to repentance any who would promote or associate themselves with such
teaching, either by commission or omission.

49. That **Overture 49** from Pacific Northwest Presbytery (“Erect an Ad Interim Committee
on AI”, p. 153) be **referred to the 53** **[rd]** **GA.** **113-6-0**

**Grounds:** While the Committee valued the substance of the request, with two study
committees already before the Assembly, it seemed wise to postpone consideration
of this matter to the 53 [rd] General Assembly.

Compassion regarding Immigrants”, p. 155) be answered to be **postponed**
**indefinitely.** **84-29-1**

**Grounds:** While the Committee valued the substance of the request, considerations

**MINORITY REPORT ON**

**OVERTURE 9**

Fathers and Brothers: Overture 9 is an important measure, which if passed, would
substantially reform the governance of the PCA General Assembly. The
Administrative Committee is one of special importance, and as currently constituted,
45% of the voting members are appointed by the Assembly’s program committees
and agencies rather than being directly elected by the assembly.

Overture 9 is a modest proposal that would amend the _BCO_ and the corresponding
_RAO_ provisions to make the 9 members of the AC appointed by the permanent
committees non-voting, advisory members of the AC. The votes would be reserved
for the 11 members of the AC directly elected by the Assembly.

This proposal is not a criticism of the character of anyone on the AC or any of the
program committees or agencies. Good governance is put in place to protect
everyone involved and to help promote good outcomes. The GA meets for only a
few days a year, and between meetings, much business is conducted by its
Administrative Committee. This includes making arrangements for the next meeting
of the assembly itself and arranging for the many meetings of groups like RPR, CCB,
nominating committee, and the SJC. The AC hires and retains a number of full-time
staff, and significantly, it makes an annual nomination for who should be our stated
clerk. It’s work is critical to the Assembly.

The minority believes this reform should be made because it will make the
Administrative Committee more directly accountable to the Assembly. This
improvement is very much in keeping with grass roots Presbyterianism.

During the course of debate in the Overtures Committee, Overture 9 was amended
to include substantial additional changes to the way permanent committee budgets
are handled in advance of the meeting of the General Assembly each year. This
caused debate to be bogged down and created confusion. We have chosen, for our
report, to put before the Assembly the original language of Overture 9 as it was
submitted by James River Presbytery. As proposed, Overture 9 is a very simple
change that will, we believe, produce big results for the Assembly and for our
beloved church, and we ask that the Assembly adopt it. The text of the overture
follows.

Respectfully Submitted,

RE Matthew Fender TE Cody Hooper
TE Stephen Spinnenweber RE Tim Shorey
TE Matt Giesman TE Skip Gillikin
RE John Barry TE Allan Bledsoe
TE David Barry TE Mark Herzer
RE Gregory Miseyko TE Derrick Brite
TE Joshua Walker TE Kenny Silva
RE David Auman RE Donald Kooy
TE Ray Heiple RE Rob Looper
RE Tim Carlson TE John Irwin
RE Chris Shoemaker RE Daniel Nicholas

<a id="ga52-p120"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=122 printed_page=120 -->

RE Ned Currie RE Lance Kinzer
TE Sean Morris RE Terry Carnes
TE Levi Bakerink RE Scott Wulff

###

##### OVERTURE 9 from the James River Presbytery (to CCB, OC, AC, CC, CDM, CTS,

Geneva, MNA, MTW, PCAF, RH, RUF)
“Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to Make AC Members Appointed by Committees and
Agencies Non-voting Advisory Members”

**Whereas** the permanent committees and agencies of the General Assembly exist to
serve the church and to promote the Great Commission ( _BCO_ 14-1.5);

**Whereas** the PCA has, since its founding, been committed to a grass roots system of
church government;

**Whereas** the General Assembly’s Administrative Committee exercises tremendous
influence over the PCA’s national operations, including the nomination of the
Stated Clerk; the preparation of budgets for the permanent committees and
agencies; arranging for the General Assembly to meet each year; and the hiring,
retention, and dismissal of numerous employees;

**Whereas**, as presently constituted in _BCO_ 14-1.12 and _RAO_ Article V, the
Administrative Committee consists of eleven (11) members elected at large by
the General Assembly ( _BCO_ 14-1.12.a; _RAO_ 5-1.a) and nine (9) members
appointed by the permanent committees and agencies ( _BCO_ 14-1.12.b, _RAO_ 51.b);

**Whereas** this means that 45% of the votes on the Administrative Committee, as
presently constituted, are cast by men appointed to the Committee rather than
elected;

**Whereas**, per _BCO_ 14-1.12 and _RAO_ 5-3, “The chief administrative officers of the
program Committees and Agencies may attend any meeting of the
Administrative Committee. They shall be entitled to the privilege of the floor,
but shall not have a vote and must be excluded when an executive session is
called;” and

**Whereas** the PCA will be better served if the voting members of the Administrative
Committee are directly elected by the General Assembly, making the Committee
more directly accountable to the will of the Assembly; now, therefore,

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 14-1.12 is amended as follows (underlining for additions):

By inserting the words “non-voting, advisory” into the stem of _BCO_ 14-1.12.b
between the word “One” and the word “member” and by inserting the following
sentence at the end of the second paragraph: “The non-voting, advisory members
appointed to the Administrative Committee pursuant to subparagraph (b) above shall
have the privilege of the floor but shall not be entitled to vote and must be excluded

<a id="ga52-p121"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=123 printed_page=121 -->

when an executive session is called.” These changes shall result in an amended _BCO_
14-1.12 that reads:

12. The Assembly permanent committees are the Administrative Committee
of General Assembly, Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee
on Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the World, and
Committee on Reformed University Fellowship.

The Administrative Committee of General Assembly (AC) shall
consist of twenty (20) members:

- **a.** Eleven members in classes elected through the standard nomination and election procedure,
- **b.** One non-voting, advisory member each from the following program committees or agencies:

1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM);
2. Covenant College (CC);
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS);
4. Mission to North America (MNA);
5. Mission to the World (MTW);
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF);
7. Geneva Benefits Group, Inc. (Geneva);
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF);
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH).

The eleven members at large shall serve a term of four years. The
chairman of the Administrative Committee shall be one of its members at
large. The non-voting, advisory members appointed to the Administrative
Committee, pursuant to subparagraph (b) above, shall have the privilege
of the floor but shall not be entitled to vote and must be excluded when
an executive session is called.

Each program committee and agency shall designate its member
each year at the last meeting of the committee or board before the meeting
of General Assembly. The chief administrative officers of the program
committees and agencies may attend any meeting of the Administrative
Committee. They shall be entitled to the privilege of the floor but shall not
have a vote and must be excluded when an executive session is called.

Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on Mission to
North America, Committee on Mission to the World, and Committee on
Reformed University Fellowship shall consist of fifteen (15) men divided
into five classes of three men each, with two men being TEs and one RE
or two men being REs and one TE on alternate years, elected to serve fiveyear terms. Committees on Discipleship Ministries, Mission to North
America, Mission to the World, and Reformed University Fellowship shall
have one ruling and one teaching elder as alternates to fill any vacancy
that may occur during the year.

Persons who have served for a full term, or for at least two years of
a partial term, on one of the Assembly’s permanent committees or
agencies shall not be eligible for re-election to an Assembly committee
until one year has elapsed. (Exceptions may be permitted in agency bylaws
approved by the Assembly.)

**Be it further resolved** that in order to harmonize the language in the _Rules of_
_Assembly Operation_ with this amendment to the _Book of Church Order_, at the

<a id="ga52-p122"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=124 printed_page=122 -->

time that this amendment is finally and fully adopted by the General Assembly,
having met with the approval of 2/3 of the presbyteries, _RAO_ 5-1.b is amended
as follows (underlining for additions):

By inserting the words “non-voting, advisory” in the first line between the words

“One” and “member” such that an amended _RAO_ 5-1 reads:

**5-1.** The Administrative Committee of General Assembly (AC) shall consist
of twenty (20) members:

- **a.** Eleven members in classes elected through the standard nomination and election procedure,
- **b.** One non-voting, advisory member each from the following program committees or agencies:

1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM);
2. Covenant College (CC);
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS);
4. Mission to North America (MNA);
5. Mission to the World (MTW);
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF);
7. Geneva Benefits Group, Inc. (Geneva);
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF);
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH).

**Be it further resolved** that in order to harmonize the language in the _Rules of_
_Assembly Operation_ with this amendment to the _Book of Church Order_, at the
time that this amendment is finally and fully adopted by the General Assembly,
having met with the approval of 2/3 of the presbyteries, _RAO_ 5-2 is amended by
adding the following sentence at the end the end of the paragraph, “For
avoidance of doubt, the non-voting advisory members appointed to the
Administrative Committee pursuant to _RAO_ 5-1.b shall have the privilege of the
floor but shall not be entitled to vote and must be excluded when an executive

session is called.” This amendment will result in an amended _RAO_ 5-2 that reads
(underlining for additions):

**5-2.** The eleven members at large shall serve a term of four years. The
chairman of the Administrative Committee shall be one of its members at
large. The non-voting, advisory members appointed to the Administrative
Committee, pursuant to _RAO_ 5-1.b, shall have the privilege of the floor
but shall not be entitled to vote and must be excluded when an executive
session is called.

_Adopted by the James River Presbytery at its stated meeting, January 18, 2025._
_Attested by J. Christopher Rohde, stated clerk_

<a id="ga52-p123"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=125 printed_page=123 -->

**MINORITY REPORT ON**

**OVERTURE 12**

We, the minority, appeal to the assembly to vote to answer Overture 12 in the
negative.
The overture lacks proper Scriptural warrant.
The overture goes beyond our confessional standards
The overture contravenes the historical practice of the church.
The overture abandons settled commitments of our polity.

**The overture lacks proper Scriptural warrant**
Most importantly, the change proposed by Overture 12 lacks compelling
Scriptural warrant. Preliminary Principle 7 reminds us the Holy Scriptures are the
only rule of faith and practice. The Overture does an effective job of asserting its
position, but it does a poor job of proving it. Outside of a passing reference to
Matthew 16:19, the overture makes no Biblical case.
The New Testament passages that outline proper celebration of the Lord’s
Supper (1 Corinthians 11:23-34; Matthew 26:26-29; Luke 22:14-23; Mark 14:22-25)
focus on the institution of the Supper and the giving of the body and blood of Christ
to the faithful gathered to worship. They do not command, describe, or demand by
necessary implication the specific circumstances through which the church is to
distribute the elements.
We are reliant on Scripture alone in worship, in both what we are bound
to and where we are free. What Scripture states relevant to the Lord’s Supper is that
it is instituted/administered by a minister. If we truly want to practice the Regulative
Principle of Worship, if we truly want to be mastered by the Word in our worship,
we must not only avoid adopting elements God does not command, we must also
avoid instituting and universalizing rules concerning form that God has not seen fit
to command. As important as it is to abide by each positive prescription for worship
in Scripture, we must equally avoid adding requirements, even those we prefer.
Where Scripture permits a diversity of practice, so must we. As the Apostle
admonishes in Revelation 22:18-19: “I warn everyone who hears the words of the
prophecy of this book: if anyone adds to them, God will add to him the plagues
described in this book, and if anyone takes away from the words of the book of this
prophecy, God will take away his share in the tree of life.”
When it comes to legislating the circumstances for the distribution of the
elements of the Lord’s Supper, the burden of Scriptural proof lies with the majority.
And they have not provided it.

**The overture goes beyond our confessional standards**
Secondly, the overture goes beyond our confessional standards. Part of this
over-reach results from terminological confusion. Throughout the overture various
verbs are used to describe the Supper: administer, serve, pass, give. The discussion
on the floor of the overtures committee introduced still more: place, hand, etc. This
imprecision with language leads to the improper conflation of administration and
distribution and creates unnecessary confusion. This confusion stems directly from
moving beyond the guidance of our confessional standards.
The PCA Constitution, in concert with the broader Reformed tradition,
makes a vital distinction between the administration of the sacrament and the
distribution of the elements. The failure to recognize this distinction is the
fundamental flaw in the argument of Overture 12. Administration refers to the
authoritative, consecratory, and non-delegable acts performed by the Teaching Elder

<a id="ga52-p124"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=126 printed_page=124 -->

as a minister of the Word. Distribution, in contrast, refers to the subsequent logistical
act of passing the consecrated bread and wine to the communicants.
WCF 29.3 and WLC 169 outline aspects of the ordinance of the Lord’s
Supper. The teaching elder is responsible for its administration: declare the words of
institution, pray and bless the elements (thereby setting them apart), and to take the
bread and cup and give them to the communicants. Notice how the specific
instructions relate to the administering and instituting the Supper and not to the
circumstances of distribution. In fact, no further instruction exists other than “give.”
That the key feature of the administration is the setting apart by prayer and
thanksgiving, and by the Biblical words of institution is further confirmed by BCO
58-5 which has remained the same (other than the removal of one word) since our
denomination began in 1973. Those are the acts of authority, limited to ordained
teaching elders. What follows is the distribution (“Here the bread is to be
distributed”), Regarding the circumstances or mode of the distribution, the BCO–like
the Confession and like the Scripture—is silent.
The majority would have you believe that BCO 58-5 “implies (or assumes)”
only the officers can distribute the elements. That implication or assumption simply
does not exist in the BCO. Even if it did, it does not exist in Scripture.

**The overture contravenes the historical practice of the church.**
The overture itself states that “not since the beginning of Presbyterianism
has the administering of the sacraments been in the hands of the non-ordained
membership.” It then goes on to assert (without compelling evidence) that the
distribution of the sacraments has likewise never been in the hands of the nonordained. This is simply false.
A diversity of practice has existed in the Reformed church on the manner of
distribution from the beginning. For instance, the Westminster Directory for the
Public Worship of God states, “the communicants may orderly sit about it, or at it.”
This provision expressly preserved the diversity of distribution between the Scottish
and English church. The Scots would have congregants come to sit at long tables and
serve one another, while the English allowed the elements to be passed to the
congregation.
An attempt now to limit the distribution to ordained officers counters the longstanding allowable practice of the church. Neither the original Directory of Worship
of 1645, nor the 1839 update, mentions ruling elders at all when it comes to the
distribution of communion or any role in communion at all. They only mention the
"minister" and "the communicants."
Overture 12’s historical claims are also not consistent with the fifty-plus
year history of the PCA. The General Assembly has considered legislating distribution
of the Lord’s Supper before. In 1994, there was an overture from Eastern Carolina
Presbytery seeking to amend the Directory for Worship to specify how the Lord’s
Supper was to be distributed. That overture specifically recommended that teaching
and ruling elders distribute the elements; and that failing to have enough elders to
do so, deacons might also distribute the elements. That overture went on to declare:
“Lay persons are not to participate in distributing this sacred ordinance, other than
passing the elements along a row where walking is impractical.” In its wisdom, the
22nd Assembly denied that overture by an overwhelming majority (M22GA, 1994,
247-48).
The historical record contains no General Assembly or CCB ruling against
the practice of using non-ordained members for distribution in a manner approved

<a id="ga52-p125"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=127 printed_page=125 -->

by the Session of the Church, implying a long-standing acceptance of this as a matter
of local discretion under the authority of the Session.

**This overture abandons the settled principles of our polity.**
Throughout the history of the church the assembly has been reluctant to
legislate on this matter out of faithfulness to Preliminary Principle 1 of our polity.
Specifically, “God alone is Lord of the conscience and has left it free from any
doctrines or commandments of men (a) which are in any respect contrary to the
Word of God, or (b) which, in regard to matters of faith and worship, are not
governed by the Word of God.” Applied in this overture, to demand a certain manner
and mode for distribution is to bind the conscience, which only the Lord through
Scripture can do.
Furthermore, WCF 1.6 states there are “circumstances concerning the worship of
God, and government of the church, common to human actions and societies, which
are to be ordered by the light of nature, and Christian prudence.”
Additionally, BCO 11-2 states, “[Church Courts] have power to establish rules for the
government, discipline, worship, and extension of the Church, which must be
agreeable to the doctrines relating thereto contained in the Scriptures, the
circumstantial details only of these matters being left to the Christian prudence and
wisdom of Church officers and courts.” Then BCO 12-5.e specifies that the Session
“exercises authority over… the administration of the sacraments.” Therefore, the
jurisdiction over circumstantial matters regarding the distribution of the elements is
left to the Sessions of individual churches as part of their maintenance of the spiritual
government of the church.
Our polity states that after the authoritative institution of the Supper by the minister,
the elements are to be given (i.e., distributed) to the church. How they are given is a
circumstance rightly ordered by Christian prudence and the wisdom of each Session.
We, the minority, fully uphold the authority of a church’s ordained elders
in overseeing the proper distribution of the Lord’s Supper, particularly in its
connection to authoritative preaching of the Word, faithful shepherding of the flock,
and discipline within the family of God. We simply dispute the way this overture
wields such authority. In its efforts to protect the authority of the elders in the
sacrament, the overture actually impinges upon the authority of Sessions; authority
outlined and protected in both the Confession and the BCO.
No one in the minority—and no one in the Assembly, we assume–is
doubting the authoritative responsibility of the Session in the church and especially
in relation to their oversight of the sacraments. The elders are, indeed, “stewards of
the mysteries of God” (1 Corinthians 4:1). The argument that non-ordained
distribution of the elements is a threat to, or impingement upon, the office of elder is
misguided. Ministerial authority vested in the ordained office is enacted in the
administration of the table (including its proper fencing and its attendant pastoral
oversight) and not in the circumstances of distribution.

**Conclusion**
We, the minority, specifically wish to appeal to those of you who on first blush might
have no issue with this overture. This overture may not have any effect on your
church’s practice of the Lord’s Supper, just as it would not have an effect on the
practice of many of our churches. That is not why you should oppose this Overture.
You should oppose it because it lacks Scriptural warrant, it goes beyond our
confessional standards, it conflicts with the historical practice of the church, and it
abandons the settled principles of our polity.

<a id="ga52-p126"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=128 printed_page=126 -->

We are convinced that the majority has failed to prove their case. From the
arguments offered in Overtures Committee and here today, no compelling rationale
has been offered to demonstrate Scriptural warrant, confessional evidence, historical
witness, or polity consistency that demands that we must prohibit non-ordained
members from participating in distribution of the elements under the authority of the
Session. What has been offered are defenses of the authority of the elders in the
church around the sacrament and its connection to proper discipline in the church—
both ideas that we are just as eager to maintain. Authority rightly stems from the
Word and the Spirit (in our elders’ ordination, in the proper preaching of the word,
and in the proper institution of the Supper). That right authority becomes suspect and
thin when it is insisted upon in ways not supported by Scripture and affirmed by our
confessional documents.
Let us not neglect the wisdom of our forefathers who designed our Directory
of Worship with forethought that balances the proper administration of the sacrament
with sessional freedom to decide the circumstances of its distribution.
The current wording of BCO 58-5 i snot a flaw to be corrected but a feature
to be preserved, one that trusts Sessions to shepherd their flocks and order their
worship in a manner that is decent, orderly, and edifying. In no way does it promote
liturgical innovation; rather it preserves the established constitutional heritage and
precious liberty of the Presbyterian Church in America.
Therefore, we urge the 52nd General Assembly to answer Overture 12 in the
negative.

TE Erik Bonkovsky, James River
For the Minority

TE Erik Bonkovsky RE Trevor Laurence
TE Alex Dean RE Arlen Biersgreen
TE Lindsey Williams RE Albert Leavengood
TE Billy Boyce RE Jim Wert
TE Ben Shelton RE John Pickering
TE Chris Polski RE Aaron Collier
TE Ethan Smith RE Drew Massengill
TE Brad Lucht RE William (Bill) Taft
TE Jeremy King RE Jason Greene
TE Kevin Timmons RE Horace Lamb

TE Peter Green
TE Aaron Baker
TE Joshua Reitano TE Joseph Patrick Allen
TE Michael Chhangur TE Daniel Herron

The Moderator ruled that **R** **ECOMMENDATIONS** **36-38** ( pp. 87-89 ) were now **moot**
due to the actions on the Overtures Committee report.

52-48 Committee of Thanks Report

RE Mel Duncan led the Assembly in prayer and gave the report. The **Resolution**
**on Thanks** was **adopted** by acclamation.

**RESOLUTION OF THANKS**

“To you i lift up my eyes, O you who are enthroned in the heavens! Behold, as the
eyes of servants look to the hand of their master, as the eyes of a maidservant to the
hand of her mistress, so our eyes look to the LORD our God, till he has mercy upon
us” (Psalm 123:1-2).

On the banks of the Tennessee River, under the shadow of Lookout Mountain, the
52nd General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America has gathered together
in the Scenic City of Chattanooga. We gather in gratitude for our triune God’s
faithfulness through the past, His mercies in the present, and His hope for the future.

We thank God for the missionary spirit of the Presbyterian evangelist Cyrus
Kingsbury, the Father of Missions in these Indian territories, who over two centuries
ago in 1817, started the Brainard Mission among the Cherokee Nation along
Chickamauga Creek, before heading to Mississippi the following year to start the
Elliott and Mayhew Missions among the Choctaw Nation.

We thank God that in June of 1840, some of the missionaries from the Brainard
Mission founded the First Presbyterian Church of Chattanooga so graciously pastored
today by Dr. Gabe Fluhrer. We remember the Reverend Thomas Hooke McCallie,
also Pastor of First Chattanooga who in 1892 served as the chairman of the Home
Mission Committee for Knoxville PCUS Presbytery when Lookout Mountain
Presbyterian Church was planted, who in 1905 gave his sons Park and Spencer
$2000 and the family’s 40-acre plot of land on Missionary Ridge to begin McCallie
School, and whose daughter Grace helped to found the Girls Preparatory School.

We thank God for the reviving work of the Holy Spirit in 1975 at a lay renewal
weekend at Lookout Mountain Presbyterian Church, which led not only to many
conversions, but to the congregation joining the PCA, and millions of dollars given
through the MacLellan Family Foundation to local ministry, global missions, and
seminary scholarships.

We thank God for the ministry of Covenant College, it iss o good to be in the home
of the Scots, we appreciate the work of the Chalmers Center, which is changing how
the church globally serves the poor in the name of Jesus.

We thank God for the urban and rural church planting vision of the Tennessee Valley
Presbytery, stretching from the North Georgia mountains through the heart of the
Smokies. We thank God for the churches of this founding PCA presbytery including
New City Fellowship and Pastor Kevin Smith, Covenant Oak Ridge and Dr Nick
Wilborn and the hundreds of officer bearers and dozens of local churches who bring
the ordinary means of grace week after week throughout the Tennessee Valley.

We thank God for the labors of Host Committee Chairman Andy Jones and the
outstanding VOLS who have served the GA so well. We are thankful for the work of
the Administrative Committee in planning and putting on the Assembly, for Dr. Bryan
Chapell’s ministry as Stated Clerk, and for the work of Provisional Stated Clerk John
Bise and for the skillful moderating of the Rev. Dr. Kevin DeYoung.

<a id="ga52-p128"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=130 printed_page=128 -->

We thank God for the ministry of the word from Mr. Steve Dowling, the Rev. Mr.
Dennis Louis, and the Rev. Dr. Dennis Johnson, and for all who blessed the Lord and
us with musical gifts in the worship services.

By His grace may we take root downward and bear fruit upward, remaining faithful
to the Scriptures, true to the Reformed faith, and obedient to the Great Commission.

Mr. Moderator, if this motion receives a second… I ask it be adopted with reformed
acclamation and Christian thanksgiving.

TE Jon Medlock, Northern California
Chairman

RE Melton L. Duncan, Calvary
Secretary

_The Thanks Committee wish to extends its appreciation to TE Caleb Cangelosi of the_
_Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley for his considerable assistance in framing this_
_recommendation._

RE Mel Duncan closed the report in prayer.

52-49 Adjournment and Benediction

Upon the acclamation of the Assembly, the Moderator declared the 53 [rd] General
Assembly adjourned at 9:52 p.m. to reconvene in Louisville, KY on June 22, 2026.
TE Larry Roff led the Assembly in singing Psalm 133. The Moderator led in prayer
and pronounced the Apostolic Benediction (2 Corinthians 13:14).
