**REPORT OF THE**

**ADMINISTRATIVE COMMITTEE**

_See 52-34 (p. 65) and 52-37 (p.69) for the report of the Committee of_

_Commissioners and the Assembly’s action_

As our church works together for Christ’s sake, we send representatives to our
General Assembly to “superintend the affairs of the whole church” ( _BCO_ 146). The General Assembly in turn entrusts the Administrative Committee (AC)
to serve the whole denomination at every level by helping local churches,
Presbyteries, as well as our committees and agencies carry out Christ’s
mission. The AC serves by coordinating and communicating matters of
finance, legality, polity, and corporate responsibility to help maintain and
further the peace, purity, and mission of the whole church.

Because we are committed to serving the whole church, the AC staff serve as
frontline responders to the needs of every Session, Presbytery, and committee
of the General Assembly. Through communications, resources, and
arrangements, the AC supports every aspect of the nurture, mission, and work
of the PCA. In all we do, our goal is to promote the priorities of the gospel and
to honor the importance and significance of the work being done by the whole
church in all of its parts.

**M** **EETINGS OF THE** **A** **DMINISTRATIVE** **C** **OMMITTEE** **(AC)** **AND** **B** **OARD OF**
**D** **IRECTORS OF THE** **P** **RESBYTERIAN** **C** **HURCH IN** **A** **MERICA** **(** **A CORPORATION** **)**

The Administrative Committee handles the ecclesiastical matters committed

to it by the General Assembly ( _BCO_ 14-1.12; _RAO_ 4-2; V). The twentymember AC includes eleven members elected at large from the General
Assembly and nine members who have been elected to represent the
Permanent Committees and Agencies of the General Assembly. Coordinators
and Presidents of the General Assembly Committees and Agencies may attend
AC meetings but have no vote.

The Administrative Committee of the General Assembly also serves as the
Board of Directors of the Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation)

[PCA “Corporate Bylaws,” Article II Section 2]. “The purpose of the

<a id="ga52-p134"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=136 printed_page=134 -->

corporation is to engage in any lawful act or activity for which corporations
may be organized under the general Corporation Law of Delaware” (PCA
Certificate of Incorporation). Matters requiring civil actions are handled by the
PCA Board of Directors. The Board of Directors meets immediately following
the stated meetings of the Administrative Committee to deal with civil actions
and activities. The stated and called meetings of the AC from June 2024 to
May 2025 were:
June 11, 2024—General Assembly, Richmond, Virginia
October 3, 2024—MTW Building, Lawrenceville, Georgia
March 11, 2025—Called Meeting, Zoom
May 1, 2025—MTW Building, Lawrenceville, Georgia

**S** **UMMARY OF THE** **A** **CTIONS OF THE** **B** **OARD OF** **D** **IRECTORS**

These actions of the Board of Directors are reported to the General Assembly.
No action of the General Assembly is required on the following items:

1) All required corporate filings of the Presbyterian Church in America

(A Corporation) have been filed in the relevant states. The Presbyterian
Church in America (A Corporation) is a registered Delaware
corporation. The Presbyterian Church in America (A Corporation) is
currently registered as a foreign corporation in Georgia, Missouri,
Mississippi, and Washington.
2) All required corporate filings of the corporations of the Reformed

Presbyterian Church, Evangelical Synod (acquired in the Joining and
Receiving of 1982) have been filed in the relevant states. **Delaware**
**Corporations** : World Presbyterian Missions, Inc.; National
Presbyterian Missions, Inc.; Christian Training, Inc. **Michigan**
**Corporation** : Board of Home Ministries. **Pennsylvania Corporation** :
Reformed Presbyterian Church, Evangelical Synod.
3) The current Officers of the Corporation (through the end of this

Assembly) are: **President** : RE E. R. (Danny) McDaniel; **Secretary**
**and Treasurer** : Dr. Bryan Chapell (Stated Clerk); **Assistant**
**Secretaries/Treasurers** : Dr. Dixie Zietlow (Business Administrator),
Ms. Heidi Harrison (Operations Manager), Ms. Angela Nantz (Meeting
Planner) [ _RAO_ 3-2.o, PCA “Corporate Bylaws,” Article IV].
4) The Stated Clerk updated the Board of Directors on our current legal

status and concerns. The PCA is not currently party to any lawsuits.
5) The proposal of the Board of Directors of MNA Disaster Response, Inc.

was approved. This approval amends the MNA Disaster Response

<a id="ga52-p135"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=137 printed_page=135 -->

bylaws to allow corporate oversight of MNA Disaster Response to be
exercised by MNA rather than the PCA Board of Directors (MNA’s
incorporation makes this now possible and advisable).

**A** **DMINISTRATIVE** **C** **OMMITTEE** **(AC)** **O** **FFICERS FOR THE** **2025-2026** **A** **SSEMBLY** **Y** **EAR**
At its 2025 spring meeting, the Administrative Committee elected the
following men to serve as the 2025-2026 AC officers:

Chairman: RE Danny McDaniel
Vice-Chairman: RE E. J. Nusbaum

Secretary: TE Mike Hearon
Planning and Management Subcommittee Chairman: TE Jason Helopoulos
Budget and Finance Subcommittee Chairman: RE Alan Walters

**F** **UTURE** **A** **SSEMBLIES**
2026—Louisville, Kentucky, the location for the 53 [rd] General Assembly

hosted by Ohio Valley Presbytery
2027—Milwaukee, Wisconsin, the location for the 54 [th] General Assembly

hosted by Wisconsin Presbytery, Chicago Metro Presbytery, Korean
Central Presbytery, and the Presbytery of Northern Illinois

**L** **EGAL** **M** **ATTERS**

The AC navigates legal matters at the denominational level for the PCA. We
joyfully report that the PCA is currently not named in any lawsuit.

Further, we continue to work to make our churches and Presbyteries aware of
the wisdom and importance of pursuing all avenues for their legal protection.
In the fall, we expanded and updated the information in the _Presbytery Clerk’s_
_Handbook_ related to incorporation and Directors and Officers liability
insurance for PCA churches and Presbyteries. Our updated information was
reviewed by the PCA’s insurance agent and legal counsel and thereafter
presented to the Presbytery clerks at their annual conference in December.

**F** **INANCIAL** **M** **ATTERS**

The Lord continues to bless and provide financially primarily through
churches and individual donors. We are completely dependent on His care
working through you.

Total revenue through December 31, 2024, was $3,434,224.54; and total
expenditures were $3,313,780.59, providing income from Operations of
$120,443.95. We are grateful to God for this, especially after a couple of

<a id="ga52-p136"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=138 printed_page=136 -->

deficit years related, in part, to the impact of the 50 [th] Anniversary celebration!
We now are keeping a close eye on all meeting costs as the impact of inflation
and dynamics in the post-Covid hospitality industry significantly affect our
expenditures. For example, the General Assembly cost center ran an $80K
deficit in FY2024.

Our thanks go out to our 2024 Development Team led by Karen Cook and
implemented by Paul Kooistra, Wayne Herring (unexpectedly called to his
eternal reward in March), and Craig Wilkes, along with several other AC staff
and Committee members who contributed to our development needs. We
rejoice in the number of churches adding the AC to their giving but remain
aware that roughly half of all PCA churches do not give any denominational
support.

- The annual audits of the AC and the PCA Office Building were

completed as required ( _RAO_ 14-7.h).

- The PCA Committees and Agencies have submitted their 2026

proposed budgets for approval by the 52 [nd] General Assembly (these
may be found on p. 401 of this _Handbook_ ). [p. 169 of these _Minutes_ ]

- The AC evaluated the Committee and Agency Chief Administrative

Officer compensation guidelines as required ( _BCO_ 14-1.13). The
Committees and Agencies state CAO compensation as separate line
items or notes in their respective proposed budgets presented to the
Assembly. The AC annually reviews compensation guidelines.
Every four years the Committees and Agencies do an in-depth study
of comparable CAO compensations. We completed an in-depth
study in the fall of 2021.

- The AC reviewed the General Assembly Commissioner’s

Registration fee as required ( _RAO_ 9-4). [1] (Please be aware that
scholarships are available for those with qualifying need.)

1 Commissioners should note that the General Assembly Registration fees do not fully
cover all the costs associated with the General Assembly, that not all commissioners
pay the full fee, and that the AC Funding Plan adopted by the General Assembly in
2012 recommended that the General Assembly Registration fees more realistically
cover costs. About one-third of the costs of the Assembly’s annual meeting are
underwritten by Exhibiters. The larger costs to commissioners are usually not the
registration fee but travel, hotel, and meal expenses.

<a id="ga52-p137"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=139 printed_page=137 -->

- The 2024 true cost of unfunded mandates was $668.42 per

commissioner, which significantly exceeds the current $525 TE and
$300 RE Assembly Registration Fee.

- The AC reviewed the required contributions of other General

Assembly Committees and Agencies to the AC ( _RAO_ 5-4.a) and is
making appropriate recommendations.

- The AC reviewed the requested Annual Administrative Fee paid by

ministers to the AC ( _RAO_ 5-4.c).

- as a point of information, 2026 building occupancy costs were

discussed.

- The AC approved auditors for the various Committees and Agencies

as requested and is making appropriate recommendations to the
Assembly.

- “ Certificate of Compliance” forms were signed by AC members and

collected for the file (as part of the Conflict of Interest Policy, per
_M21GA_, 1993, 21-64, pp. 174ff).

**P** **ERSONNEL**

We give thanks for the faithful and diligent service of all of the staff of the
Administrative Committee/Office of the Stated Clerk. We praise God for the
genuine faith and committed hearts of the men and women who serve the
administrative and legal responsibilities of the entire PCA with sacrificial
dedication and great expertise. Additional responsibilities of the AC are
fulfilled by the PCA Historical Center (see Attachment 1) and byFaith.

The AC staff presently includes Bryan Chapell, Dixie Zietlow, Heidi Harrison,
Paul Kooistra, Angela Nantz, Wayne Sparkman, Andy Jones, Margaret Baker,
Karen Cook, Ashley Davis, Erika Derr, Anna Eubanks, Karen Frey, Larry
Hoop, Monica Johnston, Alyssa Cho, Billy Park, Craig Wilkes, and Per
Almquist. Several staff members work remotely and/or part-time.

The AC evaluated the job performance of Stated Clerk Bryan Chapell as
required by _RAO_ 3-3.d and unanimously recommends the election of Dr.
Bryan Chapell as Stated Clerk of the PCA for the Assembly year 2025-2026.

**O** **VERTURE** **2023-20**

At its October 2023 meeting, the AC took the following action in response to
a 2023 overture seeking a two-year study and implementation of increased
digitization of General Assembly functions and responsibilities:

<a id="ga52-p138"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=140 printed_page=138 -->

**PM 10/23-10** To establish a subcommittee under the Planning and
Management Committee of the AC to consider, explore, and
recommend means to edify the church and improve the efficiency of
operations both at the General Assembly and around the denomination
through the use of technology. This subcommittee will be appointed
and directed by the Business Administrator of the AC. Further, the AC
considers this motion as our response to Overture 20 from the 50 [th]
General Assembly.
**MSP**

The following individuals serve on the IT Subcommittee:

Dixie Zietlow, AC Business Administrator, Chair
Heidi Harrison, AC Operations Manager, Secretary
TE Per Almquist, AC Communications and Polity Specialist
Jonathan Calloway, IT Specialist, General Assembly Tech Support
Volunteer

RE Frank Cohee, IT Specialist, AC Member
TE Steve Jeantet, AC Member
RE Eric Manthei, IT Specialist, General Assembly Tech Support
Volunteer

Angela Nantz, AC Meeting Planner

The committee meets regularly to set IT priorities and direction.

No additional resources were provided to the AC for study and implementation
of digitization and electronic record keeping. Even so, AC staff made the
following significant digital upgrades since the 50 [th] General Assembly:

 SJC Records of the Case are now submitted and decisions distributed

online in a fully digitized format, with _The_ _Operating Manual for_
_Standing Judicial Commission_ ( _OMSJC_ ) updated to reflect this
change


byFaith transitioned from print to a fully digital format
 The _Book of Church Order_ is newly available in HTML format in
addition to pdf


The Nominating Committee’s manual was updated to reflect the
creation of a digital submission option for committee and agency
nominee biographies
 An official _BCO_ amendment tracker is now available on the AC

webpage, updated as Presbytery votes are reported to the AC

<a id="ga52-p139"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=141 printed_page=139 -->


Progress in providing better online visibility for contracted hotel
availability at General Assembly is ongoing


A digital-first approach to many AC publications was implemented
for publications such as the _Book of Church Order_ and General
Assembly Minutes*


The following additional PCA Administrative Committee resources
remain digitally available:

`o` PCA Church Directory
`o` PCA Church Statistics—submission of stats is available

online, as are statistical reports
`o` General Assembly _Commissioner Handbook_
`o` Nominations Resources for Presbyteries
`o` Presbytery Clerk Resources


Real-time AI translation software from English to Korean was tested
at a recent AC-sponsored meeting


A church resources webpage is under development, which will
include, among other things, an electronic version of the membership
and dismission certificate


Updates to the Clerk of Session Handbook have been initiated and are
ongoing

*In consultation with Historical Center Director Wayne Sparkman, and
recognition that both digital and print records are vulnerable to corruption over
time, key documents such as General Assembly minutes, will be maintained
in hard copy at this time ( _RAO_ 3-2 h).

**R** **ECOMMENDATIONS**

1. That the General Assembly elect Dr. Bryan Chapell for another term as

Stated Clerk.

_Note: Rescinded. See the AC Supplemental Report, p. 234._

2. That the Assembly commend the entire staff of the Administrative

Committee for faithful and effective labors for the Lord with skillful and

joyful service to the ministers, churches, Presbyteries, permanent
committees, agencies, and General Assembly in promoting the unity,
purity, and mission of the Presbyterian Church in America in the past year.

3. That the Overtures Committee receive the following advice on **Overture**

**3** from Arizona Presbytery, “Erect Ad Interim Committee on Christian

<a id="ga52-p140"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=142 printed_page=140 -->

Nationalism,” and **Overture 4** from South Texas Presbytery, “Erect Ad
Interim Committee on Christian Nationalism.”

The Administrative Committee offers no opinion per se on the
wisdom of the proposed study committee. In the event the
Assembly answers Overtures 3 and 4 in the affirmative, approving
the establishment of an ad interim committee on the study of
Christian Nationalism, the budget of such committee be set at a
maximum of $10,000, provided solely by designated gifts to the
AC. The AC notes: “Funding should not be the burden primarily
of the members of the ad interim committee nor the AC, but of
those requesting the ad interim committee. Any overture
proposing an ad interim committee should ordinarily include a
plan for how sufficient, designated funds for the ad interim
committee will be raised.” ( _RAO_ 9-3)

The AC further notes “only two (2) ad interim committees
may be appointed or continued in any given year, unless additional
ones are approved by a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the Assembly”
( _RAO_ 9-3) Any study committee approved without a funding plan
requires a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the General Assembly as an
amendment to the AC budget ( _RAO_ 9-3, 4-11). Since the AC can
only administer funds that have been designated and received for
an ad interim study committee ( _RAO_ 9-3), funds will be
distributed by the AC only as they are made available in the
project account.

4. That the Overtures Committee recommend that **Overture 9** from James

River Presbytery, “Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to Make AC Members Appointed
by Committees and Agencies Non-voting Advisory Members,” be
answered in the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

The current structure of the Administrative Committee is

grounded in PCA history with grass roots concerns addressed by
our founding fathers. The PCA tried having only at-large members
on the AC at the beginning of its history but found that
personalities and politics made such an arrangement counterproductive for the consistent mission and progress of the church.
So, in 1984, the General Assembly assigned (and later approved
the recommendations of) an Ad Interim Study Committee
composed of our founders and key leaders (e.g., Jack Williamson,
Bob Canada, and Morton Smith) to develop a system of checks

<a id="ga52-p141"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=143 printed_page=141 -->

and balances for the AC. Those founders and leaders worked in an

Ad Interim Committee for five years to design a structure to
protect our grass roots governance through the representation of
at-large members as well as the representation of those elected to
regular oversight of the General Assembly’s Committees and
Agencies.

The overture’s wording may seem to indicate that some
Administrative Committee members are elected while others are

only “appointed” to their roles. This wording may give an
impression inconsistent with our actual practice. Just as the
General Assembly elects at-large members of the AC, it also elects
the Committee and Agency members to their office. Every elder
who represents a Permanent Committee or Agency on the AC has
been elected to that responsibility first by a church, then a
presbytery, then General Assembly, and then by the Committee or
Agency whose members vote to approve their representative for
the AC.

If Overture 9 were to pass, no Committee or Agency could
vote on its own budget, nor vote on the budgets of other
Committees and Agencies in ways that enhance cooperation and
connectionalism between them. Budgets of many millions of
dollars, developed through months of financial planning and interagency cooperation would be determined solely by at-large AC
members who meet for a few hours each Spring.

The structure designed by the 5-year study of the Ad Interim
Committee composed of founding leaders replaced a system that
was not working. We believe it is in the best interests of the
PCA’s mission to maintain the current structure.

5. That the Administrative Committee’s advice on **Overture 11** be answered

with reference to the answer provided by the Board of Directors of the
PCA Foundation.

6. That the Overtures Committee receive the following advice on **Overture**

**26** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery, “Erect Ad Interim Committee to
Revise the Directory for Worship for Authoritative Use.”

The Administrative Committee offers no opinion per se on the
wisdom of the proposed study committee’s purpose. In the event
the Assembly answers Overture 26 in the affirmative, approving
the establishment of an ad interim committee to revise the

<a id="ga52-p142"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=144 printed_page=142 -->

directory for worship for authoritative use, the budget of such
committee be set at a maximum of $15,000, provided solely by
designated gifts to the AC. The AC notes: “Funding should not be
the burden primarily of the members of the ad interim committee
nor the AC, but of those requesting the ad interim committee. Any
overture proposing an ad interim committee should ordinarily
include a plan for how sufficient, designated funds for the ad
interim committee will be raised.” ( _RAO_ 9-3)

The AC further notes that “only two (2) ad interim committees
may be appointed or continued in any given year, unless additional
ones are approved by a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the Assembly”
( _RAO_ 9-3) Any study committee approved without a funding plan
requires a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the General Assembly as an
amendment to the AC budget ( _RAO_ 9-3, 4-11). Since the AC can
only administer funds that have been designated and received for
an ad interim study committee (RAO 9-3), funds will be
distributed by the AC only as they are made available in the
project account.

7. That the Overtures Committee recommend that **Overture 27** from

Northwest Georgia Presbytery, “Erect Ad Interim Committee to Receive
Suggestions for Permanent Committee and Agency Operations,
Resources, and Expenses,” be answered in the **NEGATIVE** or
**REFERRED** to the 53 [rd] General Assembly.

Grounds:

Having no details on how the proposed ad interim committee
would research, evaluate, or coordinate with Permanent
Committees and Agency Boards already elected to supervise
operations, resources, and expenses the AC offers no opinion per
se on the wisdom of the proposed study committee. In the event
the Assembly would answer Overture 27 in the affirmative,
approving the establishment of an ad interim committee on the
study of Operations, Resources, and Expenses, then the budget of
such committee be set at a maximum of $10,000, provided solely
by designated gifts to the AC. The AC notes: “Funding should not
be the burden primarily of the members of the ad interim
committee nor the AC, but of those requesting the ad interim
committee. Any overture proposing an ad interim committee
should ordinarily include a plan for how sufficient, designated
funds for the ad interim committee will be raised.” ( _RAO_ 9-3)

<a id="ga52-p143"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=145 printed_page=143 -->

The AC further notes that “only two (2) ad interim committees
may be appointed or continued in any given year, unless additional
ones are approved by a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the Assembly”
( _RAO_ 9-3) Any study committee approved without a funding plan
requires a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the General Assembly as an
amendment to the AC budget ( _RAO_ 9-3, 4-11). Since the AC can
only administer funds that have been designated and received for
an ad interim study committee ( _RAO_ 9-3), funds will be
distributed by the AC only as they are made available in the
project account.

8. That the Overtures Committee recommend that **Overture 30** from

Houston Metro Presbytery, “Amend _BCO_ 8-4, 20-1, 21-1, 21-12, and 231 re the Calling and Dissolution of TE Relationships for Needful Works,”
be **REFERRED** back to Houston Metro Presbytery or to the 53 [rd] General
Assembly.

Grounds:

This overture was not received by the AC in time for eight of
the ten Permanent Committees or Agencies to consider prior to
the 52 [nd] General Assembly in 2025. This overture potentially
affects the legal employment conditions and relationships of TE
personnel in all Committee and Agencies, as well as the
relationship of such men to their Presbyteries—and the
relationship of those Presbyteries and the General Assembly to
civil authorities. Significant time and care are needed with legal
counsel for the Permanent Committees and Agencies adequately
to consider effects and implications related to our practices,
liabilities, and polity.

We are grateful that the author of the overture has consented
to receive input from the Permanent Committees and Agencies
but, at this late date, little can be offered prior to this General
Assembly.

The Committee on Constitutional Business has advised that

this overture is in conflict with the PCA Constitution

<a id="ga52-p144"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=146 printed_page=144 -->

9. That **Overture 35** from Pacific Northwest Presbytery, “Request AC to

Study and Report on GA Meeting Locations,” be answered in the
**AFFIRMATIVE** .

Grounds:

Post-Covid costs, recent historically high GA attendance, and
even higher commissioner registration numbers required to break
even, make it imperative that the Administrative Committee
research General Assembly arrangements as they bear on future
attendance, costs, and fees that affect our GA’s ability to function
as a truly representative body responsible for determining the
doctrinal fidelity and gospel mission of the larger PCA.

10. That **Overture 41** from Calvary Presbytery, “Direct the AC to Collect &

Report Each Congregation’s Public Worship Service Times,” be answered
in the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

The Church Directory page of the Administrative Committee
website already allows users to find and click on each church’s
name. Then, the church’s website opens with all the information
the church desires to share. Such websites are far more likely to
be up to date regarding current service time information than what
could be collected and maintained with Administrative

Committee personnel and resources.

For additional information, please note on the PCA
[Administrative Committee website - https://www.pcaac.org/ there](https://www.pcaac.org/)
are resources that include a Church Directory with an interactive
map showing all of the churches within the PCA:

**MAP VERSION:**

<a id="ga52-p147"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=147 printed_page=null -->

If the church has a website, a link is provided to go directly to the
most current information about that local congregation.

11. That **Overture 42** from Calvary Presbytery, “Direct the Stated Clerk Not

to Collect Statistical Data Pertaining to Age or Ethnicity,” be answered in
the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

Age data is important for developing and deploying resources
for catechizing, parenting, evangelizing, leadership training, and
providing appropriate care for people of all ages. Most churches
already identify grade levels for children to plan nurture and
instruction. National surveys estimate that PCA leadership is
among the oldest for evangelical denominations. Aggregating
such age data is a responsible way for the church to consider
where resources and training are most needed to maintain our
mission of being "true to the scriptures and reformed faith, and
obedient to the great commission" across generations.

The AC is not originating concern for collection of data
regarding ethnicities in our churches. The directives of the 46 [th]
General Assembly (responding to the Overtures of 39 different
presbyteries seeking to further racial reconciliation in our
churches) instructed Mission to North America (MNA) “to budget
and plan for renewing the research with LifeWay Research
Services[done in 2018], or some other research service, to report
back to the 51 [st] General Assembly (2023) in order to establish a
longitudinal study of our denomination on the issue of racial
reconciliation” (see _M46GA_, 2018, MNA Report
Recommendation 3, p. 628, emphasis added). As the Study

<a id="ga52-p148"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=150 printed_page=148 -->

Committee reported to the 45 [th] GA, the primary research objective
was “to obtain a quantitative baseline measure of where the
Presbyterian Church in America stands on racial reconciliation.”
Subsequent study is required to make the baseline meaningful for
longitudinal analysis whose goal is to observe changes and trends
over a period of time.

To collect this longitudinal data in the most efficient and costeffective way, MNA and CDM leaders have discussed with
Administrative Committee leadership utilizing the annual
statistical reports already requested of local churches. The Stated
Clerk included a note in this year’s statistical data forms that such
data would be requested in the future to give churches an
opportunity to respond about how best to report such information.
Assumptions about how difficult such reporting will be are
premature and forbidding all such data collection is counter to the
instruction of previous Assemblies.

The Report of the Ad Interim Committee on Racial and Ethnic
Reconciliation that was the basis of the recommendations
approved by the 46 [th] GA also explained why such data collection
is important to our gospel witness:

This is what the son of David will do – effect

redemption and bring the nations into God's own
people so that there will be diversity within the
unity of God's people .... He does not do away
with our ethnic particularity in this world; rather,
he honors usa s the "nations" even as he includes

us in his "people." He will rule over us in his
kingdom, the Kingdom of God ....

This does not do away with ethnicity or race;
but this one new humanity points us in the
direction of Revelation 7 in which people "from
every nation, from all tribes and peoples and
languages" (cf. Gen. 10:5, 20, 31) are brought
together by the blood of Jesus to sing his praise.
The particularity and individuality of the nations,
tribes, peoples, and languages is not done away
with; rather, the diversity-in-unity of the praise,
each in their own language, will redound to the
splendor and praise of the God who redeemed his

<a id="ga52-p149"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=151 printed_page=149 -->

people with the blood of his Son, Jesus. And even
at the end of the biblical story, "the kings of the
nations" will bring their glory into the new cityall that is good as a result of our diversity will be
present, not in a "color-blind" fashion, but
precisely in the rich diversity that God has made
(Rev. 21 :24-26; cf. Is. 60) ....

God's mission does not call our church to

become “color-blind," the negation of race or
ethnicity; rather, it calls to become "one new
humanity," in which that diversity remains
diverse and yet finds a new and true unity (not
uniformity) through the blood of Jesus (Eph.
2:13-18)."

Necessary reasons for evaluating our progress in this gospel
mission were beautifully expressed in Overture 55 from
Mississippi Valley Presbytery that was enfolded into the final
recommendations of the 44 [th] GA:

Whereas, The Presbytery of the Mississippi
Valley recognizes that the sins of racism and racial
injustice—not only toward African Americans, but
also toward other racial minorities—are not simply
past sins, but continue to be sins with which our
churches and congregants wrestle….

Whereas, God has once more given the PCA a
gracious opportunity to show the beauty, grace, and
power of the Gospel of Jesus Christ through
confession and through the fruits of repentance: such
as, … renewing our church’s commitment to develop
minority leadership at the congregational, presbytery,
and denominational levels; and encouraging a
denomination-wide vision for and commitment to a

more racially and ethnically diverse church; …

Be it finally resolved, that The Presbytery of the
Mississippi Valley humbly asks the General
Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to
urge all the congregations and presbyteries of the
Presbyterian Church in America … to confess their

<a id="ga52-p150"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=152 printed_page=150 -->

own particular sins and failures regarding racial
injustice as may be appropriate, and to seek to bring
forth fruits of repentance for the Gospel’s sake within
their own local communities.

Please note that our godly Mississippi brethren urged
“studying, evaluating and implementing” the fruits of repentance
“at the congregational, presbytery, and denominational levels,…
encouraging a denomination-wide vision for and commitment to
a more racially and ethnically diverse church.”

These humble but direct exhortations underscore the

importance of evaluating and displaying the “fruits of
repentance.”

12. That the Overtures Committee recommend that **Overture 43** from Calvary

Presbytery, “Amend _RAO_ 4-11 regarding Data on Age and Ethnicity,” be
answered in the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

The overture appears confused about the basis by which
partnership shares are determined (i.e., the subject of _RAO 4-11_,
paragraph 3 being amended by the proposed overture).
Partnership shares are based either on a dollar figure multiplied by
the per capita count of a local church’s communicant members or
on the proportional (percentage) need of the Committees and
Agencies being supported by the church’s benevolence/mission
budget. Ages and ethnicities have nothing to do with the statistics
determining partnership shares.

As to the appropriateness of the Administrative Committee
requesting age and ethnicity information from churches in their
annual reports, the AC notes the following.

Age data is important for developing and deploying resources
for catechizing, parenting, evangelizing, leadership training, and
providing appropriate care for people of all ages. Most churches
already identify grade levels for children to plan nurture and
instruction. National surveys estimate that PCA leadership is
among the oldest for evangelical denominations. Aggregating
such age data is a responsible way for the church to consider
where resources and training are most needed to maintain our
mission of being "true to the scriptures and reformed faith, and
obedient to the great commission" across generations.

<a id="ga52-p151"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=153 printed_page=151 -->

The AC is not originating concern for collection of data
regarding ethnicities in our churches. The directives of the 46 [th]
General Assembly (responding to the Overtures of 39 different
presbyteries seeking to further racial reconciliation in our
churches) instructed Mission to North America (MNA) “to budget
and plan for renewing the research with LifeWay Research
Services[done in 2018], or some other research service, to report
back to the 51 [st] General Assembly (2023) in order to establish a
longitudinal study of our denomination on the issue of racial
reconciliation” (see _M46GA_, 2018, MNA Report
Recommendation 3, p. 628, emphasis added). As the Study
Committee reported to the 45 [th] GA, the primary research objective
was “to obtain a quantitative baseline measure of where the
Presbyterian Church in America stands on racial reconciliation.”
Subsequent study is required to make the baseline meaningful for
longitudinal analysis whose goal is to observe changes and trends
over a period of time.

To collect this longitudinal data in the most efficient and costeffective way, MNA and CDM leaders have discussed with
Administrative Committee leadership utilizing the annual
statistical reports already requested of local churches. The Stated
Clerk included a note in this year’s statistical data forms that such
data would be requested in the future to give churches an
opportunity to respond about how best to report such information.
Assumptions about how difficult such reporting will be are
premature and forbidding all such data collection is counter to the
instruction of previous Assemblies.

The Report of the Ad Interim Committee on Racial
and Ethnic Reconciliation that was the basis of the
recommendations approved by the 46 [th] GA also explained why
such data collection is important to our gospel witness:

This is what the son of David will do – effect

redemption and bring the nations into God's own
people so that there will be diversity within the
unity of God's people .... He does not do away
with our ethnic particularity in this world; rather,
he honors usa s the "nations" even as he includes

<a id="ga52-p152"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=154 printed_page=152 -->

us in his "people." He will rule over us in his
kingdom, the Kingdom of God ....

This does not do away with ethnicity or race;
but this one new humanity points us in the
direction of Revelation 7 in which people "from
every nation, from all tribes and peoples and
languages" (cf. Gen. 10:5, 20, 31) are brought
together by the blood of Jesus to sing his praise.
The particularity and individuality of the nations,
tribes, peoples, and languages is not done away
with; rather, the diversity-in-unity of the praise,
each in their own language, will redound to the
splendor and praise of the God who redeemed his
people with the blood of his Son, Jesus. And even
at the end of the biblical story, "the kings of the
nations" will bring their glory into the new cityall that is good as a result of our diversity will be
present, not in a "color-blind" fashion, but
precisely in the rich diversity that God has made
(Rev. 21 :24-26; cf. Is. 60) ....

God's mission does not call our church to

become “color-blind," the negation of race or
ethnicity; rather, it calls to become "one new
humanity," in which that diversity remains
diverse and yet finds a new and true unity (not
uniformity) through the blood of Jesus (Eph.
2:13-18)."

Necessary reasons for evaluating our progress in this gospel
mission were beautifully expressed in Overture 55 from
Mississippi Valley Presbytery that was enfolded into the final
recommendations of the 44 [th] GA:

Whereas, The Presbytery of the Mississippi
Valley recognizes that the sins of racism and
racial injustice—not only toward African
Americans, but also toward other racial
minorities—are not simply past sins, but continue
to be sins with which our churches and

congregants wrestle….

<a id="ga52-p153"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=155 printed_page=153 -->

Whereas, God has once more given the PCA a
gracious opportunity to show the beauty, grace, and
power of the Gospel of Jesus Christ through
confession and through the fruits of repentance: such
as, … renewing our church’s commitment to develop
minority leadership at the congregational, presbytery,
and denominational levels; and encouraging a
denomination-wide vision for and commitment to a

more racially and ethnically diverse church; …

Be it finally resolved, that The Presbytery of the
Mississippi Valley humbly asks the General
Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to
urge all the congregations and presbyteries of the
Presbyterian Church in America … to confess their
own particular sins and failures regarding racial
injustice as may be appropriate, and to seek to bring
forth fruits of repentance for the Gospel’s sake within
their own local communities.

“studying, evaluating and implementing” the fruits of repentance
“at the congregational, presbytery, and denominational levels,…
encouraging a denomination-wide vision for and commitment to
a more racially and ethnically diverse church.”

importance of evaluating and displaying the “fruits of
repentance.”

13. That **Overture 44** from Pee Dee Presbytery, “Transition byFaith to a

Press-Release-Based Publication,” be answered in the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

The General Assembly authorized the Administrative
Committee (AC) to establish byFaith in 2003 with this mission
mandate:

“The mission of the PCA news magazine is to
draw readers into the full life of the church by
informing, edifying, and encouraging them with
the news of God’s kingdom. Through feature
stories, news reports, editorial opinions, readers’
comments, practical information, and relevant

<a id="ga52-p154"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=156 printed_page=154 -->

biblical and spiritual discussion, the magazine
motivates and equips readers to serve Christ in
the Presbyterian Church in America and in His
kingdom.”

While the magazine clearly serves as a source of information
for denominational news, it was envisioned to be much more, a
publication rich with various styles of journalism to encourage and
equip God’s people for their mission in God’s world.

The minutes of the Assembly further note that the magazine
would publish content, “examining difficult issues: personal,
social, cultural, and theological. Helping readers think and speak
about them biblically, always equipping them with a biblical
world-and-life view.”

In other words, it was understood that every piece of content
would not share unanimous agreement by every member, officer,
or court of the church. The readership of the denomination holds
diverse perspectives regarding what constitutes essential news,
shaped by factors including, but not limited to, regional
differences, socioeconomic status, education level,
denominational history, and theological tradition. Given this
diversity within the PCA, it is beneficial to note how the GA
desired for byFaith to help us remember our shared foundation in
the gospel of Jesus Christ and our common pursuit of the peace,
purity, and mission of the church.

Importantly, it was understood that every piece of content
would not be received or treated as official church policy, unless
it was specified as such.

In this era, when different websites or social media outlets
seek to build or serve particular constituencies within our church
by circulating criticisms, speculations, or allegations with no
church oversight, it is important to have a media source under the
authority of the General Assembly’s AC to inform and inspire
God’s people with how the PCA is progressing, or being hindered
in, Christ’s mission.

A common misconception is that the Stated Clerk directs
byFaith’s editorial process or choices. In fact, the GA sought to
protect the Clerk and the publication from inappropriate
associations by building in other oversight mechanisms. Members

<a id="ga52-p155"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=157 printed_page=155 -->

of the AC, who are elected by the General Assembly, receive
reports on the progress and content of byFaith at every regular
meeting. In addition, the AC elects an oversight committee for
more regular review of byFaith.

Through productive use of writing resources expressed
through digital means, byFaith’s publishing output i satan all-time
high: since the summer of 2024 byFaith has produced one piece
of original content every weekday, resulting in almost 200 articles
to date in the new format.

With more content and new editorial leadership, the website
is experiencing vastly increased readership throughout our
denomination. As shown in the table below, the number of visitors
and page views on the website have more than doubled in the past
year. Further, views (i.e., times when a web page is loaded or
reloaded in a browser) are 33 times higher, and clicks (i.e., times
when a user clicks on links or ads in search results) have increased
17 times over the same period.

**1) WEBSITE ACTIVITY**
July 2023-Feb 2024 July 2024-Feb 2025
Visitors 77,000 175,000
Pageviews 161,000 356,000

**2) GOOGLE SEARCH ENGINE**
Jan-Feb 2024 Jan-Feb 2025

Views 10,300 339,000
Clicks 630 10,500

Not only is byFaith being increasingly well received by PCA
readers, the recognized quality of the articles is allowing the PCA
to serve the wider church and world. Recent articles from byFaith
have been referenced by the _Washington Post_, _Wall Street_
_Journal_, _Atlanta Journal-Constitution_, _U.S. News and World_
_Report_, Reuters, _Christianity Today_, The Gospel Coalition, _World_
_Magazine_, Challies, and Ministry Watch.

Despite these increases in production and operational quality,
the budgeted operating expenses of byFaith have dropped from
$650K in 2024 to just over $400K in 2025. Those expenses are
expected to further drop to $312K in 2026. Considering budgeted

<a id="ga52-p156"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=158 printed_page=156 -->

contributions and advertising revenues, the expected actual impact
on thea c budget is $159K. This is the best budgeted operating
outcome in over a decade while development and advertising
plans continue to be made to increase revenue to further improve
the bottom line in the years ahead.

Today—as a fully digital publication—byFaith honors its
GA-designated mission even as it has _grown_ its article production
and reach while dramatically _cutting costs._

PCA Historical Center Report

Attachment 1

**PCA HISTORICAL CENTER**
**Annual Report to the Administrative Committee**

Many years ago, I heard someone’s offered wisdom that “people don’t
really care about the history of an institution until it is at least fifty years old.”
Though we now approach our 52 [nd] General Assembly, it is still of course too
early to measure any real change of attitude or a broader, deepening
appreciation of those whose shoulders we stand on. But we pray for that day
when the Historical Center takes on a more prominent place in the life of the
denomination, and I think we are moving in that direction.
We pray too for the Lord’s provision as we fast approach maximum
capacity in our current facility. Since our start in 1985, the PCA Historical
Center has had its home in the Buswell Library at Covenant Theological
Seminary. They have been congenial and ever helpful hosts. But we need to
find a way to continue to grow our collection and we’re running out of what
room the Buswell Library can provide. Something of a pipe dream (for now)
would be the donation of some historic mansion in good repair. A home like
that, preferably here in St. Louis, would provide a place to host special talks
and small conferences, while also providing room to expand our collections,
together with suitable places for displays. It's a vision forth e future and i ask
others to join me in that prayer.

**Recent news from early 2025:**
The David Bays Calhoun Manuscript Collection: 9 boxes of
documents (9.0 cu. ft.) have been donated by Mrs. David B. Calhoun (Anne),
with the transfer of these boxes graciously facilitated by their son Allen
Calhoun. This new received material will be merged with the 3 boxes already
here on the shelf.

The research library aspect of our work has been bolstered by an
additional seventy-six “new” titles added thus far in 2025, with twenty of those
works being scarce titles from the 19 [th] century.

**Collection Development: Highlights on what was received in 2024:**
PCA Administrative Committee records, 13 cu. ft., plus an artifact—
the time-keeping lights as built by RE Walter Lastovica, built to assist him in
his work as timekeeper in the early years of the PCA General Assembly.

<a id="ga52-p163"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=165 printed_page=163 -->

Standing Judicial Commission, completed case files, 2020-03 to 2022
20, 4.0 cu. ft.
Review of Presbytery Records, Presbytery minutes from the 51 [st] GA

(2024), 3.0 cu. ft.
Central Florida Presbytery (PCA) records, 9.0 cu. ft., submitted by TE

Don Mountan.

Records of the Southwest Presbytery (PCA), 7.0 cu. ft.
Addition to the Robert G. Rayburn Manuscript Collection, donated by

TE Rob S. Rayburn, 0.7 cu. ft.
Research files on the life and ministry of Arthur Perkins, donated by

TE Brian DeJong.
China Mission (PCUS) Newsletter, 1933-1939. A rare publication.
Minutes of the Presbytery of Kanawha (PCUS), 1929-1950 and the

Synod of West Virginia (PCUS), 1940-1952. (we try to add
PCUS Presbytery minutes when we can find them)
Documents pertaining to the Justification Controversy at Westminster

Theological Seminary, 1979-1981, 0.25 cu. ft., donated by
Larry Edison.
Rev. R.G. Schwanebeck Jr. manuscript collection, 0.5 cu. ft., donated

by Joseph Moore.
Thomas E. Martin Jr. Collection of original 19 [th] c. books, sermons and

addresses by William Buell Sprague (1795-1876), 3.0 cu. ft.
W. Wilson Benton Manuscript Collection, 10 cu. ft. of documents,

primarily sermon manuscripts. Donated by Mrs. Pam Benton;
delivered on 10/17/24 by Mrs. David Filson.

Lastly, we have recently added two commemorative Calvin
medallions to our collection.

**Patronage – Visits & Requests:**
I estimate that about 120 requests were received in 2024, most arriving
by email, some by phone and a few by in-person visits. One Zoom meeting
was held to discuss transfer of Presbytery and Session records. And Joshua
Ryo Nelson was the only Ph.D. level researcher to arrive for in-person research
in 2024, working to write an article on Japan missions conducted by the
Southern Presbyterian Church.
We keep a record of all patron requests, together with our replies, on
an Excel sheet. As the years roll on we expect that record will itself become a

<a id="ga52-p164"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=166 printed_page=164 -->

useful guide to the knowledge and information accumulated in assisting
patrons.
Late last year we added a new email address for patron use—
archivist@pcanet.org. This new address serves several purposes: (1) It
removes my own email address somewhat from public view; (2) makes it
easier for timely replies by whichever staff member might be available; and
(3) facilitates transition for when I do eventually retire. Lord willing, that
retirement date is at least a handful of years away.

**Web Site Development**
We continue to implement the valuable work which Claire Wilkinson
accomplished for us during the academic year 2023-2024, updating the basic
appearance of the site, adding drop-down links and making it easier to navigate
through the entire site. Finishing that implementation will take much of this

year.
The PCAHC website has recently been moved to a new host location,
one that currently serves other Administrative Committee websites. Along
with that move we will also be adding a page where people will be able to
make financial donations to support the work of the Historical Center. I do
want to thank RE Rich Leino for his generosity in hosting the Center’s website
over the past five years. He was a tremendous help to us in this regard.

**Research Library**

Presenting the holdings of our research library on the Web turned out
to be something o fa problem in 2024. The subscription-based software used
for our online catalog became too expensive, but a comparable solution was
found at about 1/4 [th] the cost. Still, moving our holdings over to the new site
has taken and continues to take a good bit of time. Roughly half of our holdings
(about 2800 titles) are now posted on this online catalog, lovingly nicknamed
[“Warfield” : https://www.libib.com/u/warfield](https://www.libib.com/u/warfield)

In 2024, holdings in the Center’s research library grew by over 170
volumes. Notable additions include:

Six books on Korean Presbyterian missions, authored by Priscilla
Welbon Ewy, daughter of Henry Garner Welbon, whose manuscript collection
is housed here at the Center

[[https://www.pcahistory.org/mo/welbon/welbon.html]](https://www.pcahistory.org/mo/welbon/welbon.html)

_The Presbyterial Critic_, two bound volumes of this 19 [th] century
periodical. These volumes appear to have been owned by one of the
authors of that journal, Thomas Ephraim Peck.

<a id="ga52-p165"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=167 printed_page=165 -->

_Catalogue of the McAlpin Collection_, five volumes; an important
index of British history & theology, 1501-1640.
_The Sunday School Times._ This was a prominent periodical, with
coverage here for the years 1924 and 1935-1967. These issues were originally
owned by Dr. Allan A. MacRae, whose Papers (about 100 cu. ft., still our
largest collection) were received here late in 2002. Articles by such notables
as Wade C. Smith and Robert McQuilkin regularly appeared on the pages of
this journal. Our collection of this periodical totals about 1603 issues.
In closing this section, a thought to ponder, on the value of our
research library and why it is an important part of our collection:
**“Pre-AI books are potentially worth their weight in gold. These**
**are permanent physical records of human knowledge before it could have**
**been tainted by AI text generators.”**

[source: www.howtogeek.com/generative-ai-could-make-old-paperbooks-more-valuable-than-ever/]

**The Work Ahead in 2025 – “It’s going to be a very busy year.”**
As a general schedule for the posting of finding aids, in the first half
of 2025 we will be working on:

1. The Papers of the first three PCA Stated Clerks. The finding aids
for Dr. L. Roy Taylor’s collection have now all been posted. Work has moved
on to posting the Morton H. Smith and Paul R. Gilchrist collections.

(a.) Rev. Dr. L. Roy Taylor Manuscript Collection, 23 cu. ft.

(23 cartons)
[https://pcahistory.org/mo/taylorLR/index.html](https://pcahistory.org/mo/taylorLR/index.html)
(b.) Rev. Dr. Morton Howison Smith Manuscript Collection,

24 cu. ft. (26 cartons)
[https://pcahistory.org/mo/smithmh/index.html](https://pcahistory.org/mo/smithmh/index.html)
(posting in April)
(c.) Rev. Dr. Paul Rowland Gilchrist Manuscript Collection,

44 cu. ft. (44
[cartons)https://pcahistory.org/mo/gilchristPR/index.](https://pcahistory.org/mo/gilchristPR/index.html)
html (posting in May-July)
2. Allan A. MacRae Collection, 100 cu. ft. (103 cartons)

[https://pcahistory.org/mo/macrae/index.html](https://pcahistory.org/mo/macrae/index.html)
The MacRae collection should begin to post in May and then
conclude by September.
3. William Shirmer Barker II Manuscript Collection, 28 cu. ft. (28
cartons)

<a id="ga52-p166"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=168 printed_page=166 -->

[https://pcahistory.org/mo/barker/index.html](https://pcahistory.org/mo/barker/index.html)
Some of the Barker collection will post in June and then finish
up by October.

4. James Mack (“Buck”) Hatch Jr. Manuscript Collection. 3.0 cu. ft.
(3 cartons)

The Hatch collection is expected to post in June or July.

Lastly, in the latter half of 2025, we will be doing the work of
arrangement and description for the David Bays Calhoun, Floyd E. Hamilton
and George Peter Hutchinson Manuscript Collections.

**(2.) Infrastructure**
Our main goal here is to refresh, correct and update all of our print
copies of finding aids. Generally, these were last updated 20 years ago. We
will also be exporting the updated finding aids from Word over to Portable
Document Format (PDF) for local use on our office computers and also posting
these same PDF files to the Web in place of the previous html format. The
main reason for the latter change is that multiple PDF files, when first saved
to a common folder, can all be searched at the same time under a single search
command.

Work also continues to build out and complete the “Compendium,”
our Excel file with its dozen or so sheets covering the many operations of the
Center’s work. Among these, for example, is a sheet for the Shelf List, listing
every box on every shelf in the facility (and we have about a mile of shelving!)

**(3.)** **Exhibit at the 52** **[nd]** **PCA General Assembly:**
Work is underway for the PCA Historical Center’s exhibit display at
the 52 [nd] PCA General Assembly. Our display will focus on two collections:
(1.) William Buell Sprague collection; (2.) W. Wilson Benton Sermon
Collection.

**(4.) Publications**
I plan to start sending brief quarterly updates to the Subcommittee and
we plan to again issue an annual or perhaps even semi-annual newsletter to
better tell of the work of the PCA Historical Center.

**(5.) Projects for the Coming Year**
(1.) Tracking the development of the Southern Presbyterian _Book of_
_Church Order_, as reported on the pages of _The Christian Observer_, 1865-1880.

<a id="ga52-p167"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=169 printed_page=167 -->

this project is a holdover from last year but still one which would make fora
very useful compilation. It will involve digitizing the many newspaper
columns that reported on the debates and development of the PCUS _Book of_
_Church Order_, 1861-1879. With the text of many paragraphs in our PCA _BCO_
still either unchanged or closely similar to what was found in the old PCUS
_BCO_, that nineteenth-century coverage and debate should be interesting and
useful.

(2.) A second project, now largely complete, we’ve titled “Dear Folks:
Eyewitness to the Modernist Controversy.” This project pulled excerpts from
all the many letters that Allan MacRae wrote to his parents circa 1925-1940.
The resulting compilation is over 100 pages in length, and we plan to present
the project soon to a potential publisher.

**Personal:**

My health is good. In keeping with both personal and professional
growth, i have a goal of reading 50 pages per day this year. I’m also working
on the memorization of Psalm 119 and trying to refresh my Hebrew. Already
this year I’ve had the opportunity to teach one segment of a Sunday School
class on Francis Schaeffer’s early ministerial career. And a road trip in May of
2025 to Pennsylvania is looking likely, for the purpose of acquiring new
materials for the Historical Center.

Evan Kvale is now in his second year of a four-year program at
Covenant Seminary. He has done an excellent job of taking up the work at the
Historical Center, working specifically as a project archivist, while also
learning many of the other duties and skills required in the larger scope of
things here.
As project archivist, in 2024 Evan has worked on the Barker and Hatch
collections and is most recently focusing on the MacRae collection. Earlier in
2024, Mr. Kvale prepared an author-title index for the early years of _The_
_Independent Board Bulletin_, (1935-1944). This was the official house organ
of the Independent Board for Presbyterian Foreign Missions (IBPFM), an
agency formed under the efforts of J. Gresham Machen and for which ministry
Machen was later defrocked by the PCUSA. This author-title index is part of
a long-standing effort at the PCA Historical Center, whereby we have sought
to lift such publications out of obscurity by making their content more visible
through these indexes. For links to similar indexes, see
[https://www.pcahistory.org/HCLibrary/index.html](https://www.pcahistory.org/HCLibrary/index.html)

<a id="ga52-p168"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=170 printed_page=168 -->

Respectfully submitted,
/s/ RE Wayne Sparkman, Th.M., C.A., and Director of the PCA
Historical Center

<a id="ga52-p169"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=171 printed_page=169 -->

Proposed Budgets

Attachment 2

**PROPOSED BUDGETS**

**PCA COMMITTEES AND AGENCIES**

**ADMINISTRATIVE COMMITTEE**

**2026 PROPOSED BUDGET**

**I.** **Economic Considerations and General Ministry Factors**

The budget is built primarily on the job description of the Stated Clerk
in the _RAO_, which determines the services that are to be provided by
the Office of the Stated Clerk to churches, presbyteries, Committees
and Agencies, and to the General Assembly. The General Assembly
has also placed the Historical Center and byFaith Magazine under the
administrative oversight and in the budget of the AC, as well as the
Standing Committees and the SJC.

Many of the activities and responsibilities of the Administrative
Committee are directly affected by the activity and growth of the PCA.
Based on data from reporting churches, the number of communicant
members in the PCA grew 2.6% between 2023 and 2024. We rejoice
in this blessing and look forward to the Lord’s continued work in our
branch of Christ’s church.

The budgets are presented in a format to comply with the standards
for not-for-profit organizations adopted by the Financial Accounting
Standards (FASB). The FASB standards provide a definition of
“supporting activities” which they call “management and general.”
Therefore, compensation for the stated Clerk and his staff is allocated
according to the estimated time spent by each person in “program,”
administration, and fund-raising areas.

The 2026 proposed budget of $ 3,731,961 represents a less than $30K
increase over the approved 2025 budget. The increase is minimal
because of a reduction in the staff salaries and benefits line item due

<a id="ga52-p170"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=172 printed_page=170 -->

to multiple retirements of long-term employees and new efficiencies
in office operations. The 2026 budget assumes 17 FTE positions.

The occupants at the 1700 N. Brown Road Lawrenceville, GA office
expect to pay $16/sq f tin rent in 2026. Please see the 2023 AC minutes
for recent actions on rent amounts (BF 04/23-22.1, BF 04/23-22.2, BF
10/23-7)

**II.** **Major Changes in Budget**

The main change in the budget is due to staff transitions.

**III.** **Income Streams and Development Plans**

The PCA AC Development plans are increasing in man hours and in
effectiveness as we advance the ministries of the whole PCA. Earned

income and individual donor streams are expected to grow gradually,
therefore the support of each PCA church is needed.

**IV.** **Major Ministry Not Implemented in the Past Year**

There was no “Major Ministry” unimplemented in 2024.

**V.** **Present & Future**

The Spanish _BCO_ is completed and the Portuguese _BCO_ is translated
and awaiting review. We continue Korean communications and
translations.

**VI.** **Notes to Line Items**

Staff salaries and benefits are increasing by 3%.

“Telephone” and “maintenance” are being removed as line items in
Operating Expenses due to obsolescence and inactivity.

“Short-term rentals” is a new expense line item.

“Professional services” is redefined from previous financials

<a id="ga52-p171"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=173 printed_page=171 -->

<a id="ga52-p177"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=179 printed_page=177 -->

**COMMITTEE ON DISCIPLESHIP MINISTRIES**

**2026 PROPOSED BUDGET**

**Economic Considerations and General Ministry Factors**

The attached budget represents the anticipated financial activities associated
with the ministry to connect and equip those in the PCA involved in
discipleship ministry. The staff of CDM works to consult with and train
practitioners in the local church, particularly through national, regional, local
and virtual training events. Participants include ministers of discipleship,
elders, Bible teachers, small group leaders, Sunday school teachers, and the
staff and volunteers who work in ministries to children, youth, and adults.
CDM also offers resources for those in the local church by providing helpful
materials on the CDM website, publishing the work of PCA members,
recommending resources available in the broader Church, and operating the
PCA Bookstore.

The _Book of Church Order_ states, “It is the responsibility of every member
and every member congregation to support the whole work of the
denomination as they be led in their conscience held captive to the Word of
God” ( _BCO_ 14-4). Currently, 28% of particular PCA churches contribute to
CDM and the permanent committee and staff are grateful for the generosity
shown by these churches. We pray more will join them in support of CDM’s
essential ministry.

Underlying budget assumptions include:

- a tapering rate of inflation—nevertheless persistent higher costs due
to recent years’ cumulative inflation.

- health insurance premiums are projected to increase 15% from the
actual paid in 2025.

- moderate growth in giving from churches and greater need for gifts
from individuals to supplement churches that do not give according to
the Ministry Ask.

- CDM anticipates needing 13.3 FTE employees, supplemented with
several part-time contract consultants, to accomplish the anticipated
ministry. Because of the persistent inflation of the previous three
years, CDM still needs to increase employee compensation
approximately 10% to maintain “real” wage.

<a id="ga52-p178"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=180 printed_page=178 -->

- CDM continues to provide mailroom and technology services to the
committees and agencies in the building and is reimbursed
accordingly.

**Major Changes in Budget:**

Generally, the 2026 budget represents a ministry program very similar to that
undertaken in 2025. Consequently, the net increase to the proposed budget is
only 4.9%. The primary drivers of this increase are: 1) increased Bookstore
sales of the new ESL curriculum produced in cooperation with MNA, 2)
expanding the children’s ministry training and conference activities, and 3)
general inflationary pressures on expenses such as compensation, travel, and
conference expenses.

**Income Streams:**

CDM has four revenue streams: 1) church contributions, 2) individual
contributions, 3) revenues from the sale of resources, and 4) registration fees
for conferences and training. CDM’s primary source of unrestricted income
for the ministry is contributions from 28% of particular PCA churches. In light
of the ministry responsibilities given to CDM by the General Assembly, the
“Ministry Ask” is set at $7 per communicant member. If every PCA
congregation were to give at this level, CDM would be fully funded and able
to accomplish what the Assembly has directed.

Since a majority of PCA congregations do not contribute to the ministry of
CDM, and others are unable to give the $7 “Ministry Ask,” the staff of CDM
works to solicit donations from individuals, local church women’s groups, and
the PCA Foundation. Additionally, the staff seeks to find creative ways to
enhance revenue through sales of products, attendance at events, and receipt
of fees for services provided to churches and the other committees and
agencies.

CDM desires to be a wise steward of the funds God has provided and offer the
very best service to PCA churches. As CDM engages with and receives
feedback from leaders in churches, the staff is convinced a percentage request
is more helpful to those who contribute to General Assembly committees.
Consequently, CDM asks churches to give a minimum of .20% (.0020) of the
congregation’s total tithes and offerings, excluding capital budget items.

<a id="ga52-p179"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=181 printed_page=179 -->

**Major Ministry Not Implemented in the Past Year:**

In an effort to bring the scope of the ministry of CDM in line with the giving
from PCA churches, the staff and permanent committee have focused on the
areas of ministry to women, children, and resource development. CDM is
attempting to provide more support for leaders in ministry to students and older
adults as funds allow. Due to financial constraints, these ministries are forced
to operate below budget by forgoing ministry opportunities, using volunteers
wherever possible and limiting staff compensation. CDM continues to seek
funding from churches and individuals to grow and fully staff ministries to
youth, men, and older adults in the denomination.

**Notes to Budget “line items”:**

- **Contributions and Support** (Budget Comp., line 1) represent all
donated funds by churches, individuals, and organizations.

- **Other Revenue** (Budget Comp., line 2) consists of book and
curriculum sales, conference and training fees, reimbursements for
postage and other services, and the net change in CDM’s investments
held at the PCA Foundation. This line item is projected to increase,
representing anticipated growth in book and curriculum sales, greater
participation in conferences and training opportunities, and modest
projection in earnings on CDM’s investments.

- **Training and Certification** (Budget Comp., line 3) reflects a sixth
year of the Children’s Ministry Certification program and third full
year of a NextGen Certification program.

- The **Women’s Ministry** (Budget Comp., line 4) is budgeted to
increase 3.4% due to greater participation in annual and regional
conferences as well as growth in the WE and International Women’s
Ministries programs.

- CDM continues albeit, in a limited way, to help local churches that request
assistance in developing **Men's Ministries** (Budget Comp., line 5)

- **NextGen Ministry** (Budget Comp., line 6) represents the cost of the
CDM coordinator working with two part-time consultants, the
ministry team, and a national conference.

- **Children's Ministry** (Budget Comp., line 7) is projected to increase
as a second LEAD conference is planned in addition to the annual
event held at Ridge Haven. It is important to note that a portion of the

<a id="ga52-p180"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=182 printed_page=180 -->

Children’s Ministry Coordinator’s compensation is temporarily
shifted to **Children’s Worship and Prayer** (Budget Comp, line 8)
initiative. This line item presents CDM’s third year of activity
provided for by a five-year, $1.25 million grant.

- **Older Adult Ministry** (Budget Comp, line 9) indicates expenses
associated with a third year o fa functioning ministry teamwork ing to
develop training and resources for churches desiring to minister to
their older adults and more effectively involving them in its ministry.

- **Publications and Curriculum** (Budget Comp., line 10) includes the
costs associated with developing and producing between eight and
twelve studies and curricula.

- The increase of budgeted expenses for the **Bookstore** (Budget Comp., line
11) corresponds to the projected increase in sales revenue (line 2).
Bookstore sales have continue to increase as it supports CDM’s various
ministries and now carries the new _Neighbor to Neighbor_ ESL curriculum.

- **Reachout Adventures** (Budget Comp, line 12) is projected to remain
essentially the same as 2025 as the final of four modules will undergo
a complete revision to offer both print and digital resources.

- **Management and General** (Budget Comp., line 13) In addition to
staff costs, this line item includes the **Audit Fees** (Proposed, line 26),
and CDM’s share of **Liability Insurance** (Proposed, line 17) as well
as fees that are mandated to CDM by the General Assembly such as
Nominating Committee and Administrative Committee fees. See
**General Assembly Shared Expenses** (Proposed, line 25)

- The line-item **Committee/Agency Services** (Budget Comp., line 14)
represents mailroom and technology services provided to the other
committees and agencies and are reimbursed 100% to CDM. This is
expected to decline as mailroom and technology services decrease due
to two committees operating remotely.

- **Fund Raising** (Budget Comp., line 17) represents the costs associated
with contacting churches, presbyteries and individuals and informing
them about the ministry of CDM and their potential role in supporting
the ministry. This item includes 20% of the CDM Coordinator and his
associated expenses as well as two part-time staff assistants.

- The Coordinator, his part time assistant and related expenses are
allocated to the various expense categories as follows: Training and
Certification 10%, Fund Raising 20%, Administration 15%,
Bookstore 5%, Women’s Ministry 10%, Youth Ministry 10%,
Children’s Ministry 10%, and Publications and Curriculum 20%.

**COVENANT COLLEGE**

**PROPOSED BUDGET**
**FISCAL YEAR ENDING JUNE 30, 2026**

**I.** **Economic Considerations and General Ministry Factors**
General Comments

Covenant College operates as an institution of higher education in an
increasingly complex and competitive market. As the college is largely
dependent on tuition and auxiliary fees for its revenues, attracting qualified
students is essential to sustainable operations. In recent years, the cost of
higher education has come under increased scrutiny, and changing enrollment
and demographic patterns have created challenges for many institutions,
including Covenant College.

The college is working hard to grow enrollment and is seeing encouraging
results in recruiting efforts. The most recent freshman class was the largest in
over 10 years, and we are anticipating an even bigger class in the fall of 2025

For the past number of years, the college has worked diligently to mitigate the
effects of inflation. Again this year, the college is only allowing for nonnegotiable budget increases.

Economic Assumptions Used in Budgeting for the next fiscal year

- 330 new students entering in fall 2026

- Continued expense increases due to inflationary pressures

- A draw from the endowment of 5% of the weighted three-year
average value

**II.** **Major Changes in Budget**

Covenant College is committed to sound financial planning and good
stewardship of its resources. The attached budget proposes a 4% increase in
tuition and a 4% increase in room and board fees for the coming year. These
increases, along with the budgetary adjustments, allow the college to maintain
its low student-to-faculty ratio of 14:1 and to provide high quality residential

programs.

<a id="ga52-p185"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=187 printed_page=185 -->

The proposed budget does not provide for any significant changes in programs
or program spending. The budget does however provide for a significant
increase in capital spending to care for the physical plant.

The budget that is presented to the General Assembly includes $3.4 million in
depreciation, which is a non-cash expense. While the college is committed to
living within its means, in the coming fiscal year we are expecting to
experience a small deficit, which is a $1.5 million improvement from the prior
year. This deficit will be funded from free cash flow resulting from our noncash depreciation expense, Covenant College Foundation support, and by
drawing upon our line of credit. This line of credit is secured by board
designated endowment funds which were generated from surpluses in previous
fiscal years.

**III.** **Income Streams**

Tuition and fees charged to students, gifts from donors (individuals and
churches), fees for services, and gains from investing the college and
foundation endowments constitute the four primary streams of income for the
college.

The majority of college costs are paid by the students and their families, who
are the direct beneficiaries. The college works with each family in an attempt
to find an affordable path to attendance. The attraction and retention of
students is essential to the financial health of the college, and our retention
rates remain above industry norms.

Restricted and unrestricted gifts for scholarships and operations, received from
churches and individuals, make up roughly $3 million dollars of the operating
budget. Churches historically have given about $1 million of that amount each
year. Churches that participate in the Church Scholarship Promise program are
able to realize an additional scholarship benefit for their students.

The college provides other services for fees as well. Offering housing in its
residential rental properties, operating the college bookstore, and delivering
conference services provide for a modest income stream that nets about
$300,000 each year before allocation of facilities costs.

<a id="ga52-p186"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=188 printed_page=186 -->

Finally, the combined endowment of the college and the Covenant College
Foundation provide resources directly to the annual operating budget of the
college for general operations and student scholarships. Due to the growth of
the endowment, we anticipate drawing over $2 million for operations and
scholarships.

**IV.** **Major Ministry Not Implemented in the Past Year**

There were no major ministry items not implemented in the last year.

**V.** **Notes to Budget “line items”**

Accounting Format & Other Notes

The college uses the NACUBO (National Association of College and
University Business Officers) definitions of revenue and expense
categories. This ensures that the college will be able to directly compare
various ratios with other colleges and assess our effectiveness in accordance
with our assessment systems. While the categories do not exactly parallel the
definitions used by the Accounting and Financial Reporting Guide for
Christian Ministries, there is some similarity. NACUBO categories including
Instructional, Academic Support, Library, Student Services, Public Service
and Student Aid, and Auxiliary services (Room and Board) are considered
"Program Services."

- Budget Comparison Note 1: Net Tuition & Fees increasing
from the prior year budget due to a significant increase in the
size of the student body as well as tuition increases.

- Budget Comparison Note 2: Room and Board income
increasing due to projected increase in total students living in
campus housing and to increase in fees.

- Budget Comparison Note 3: Unrestricted Gifts increasing to
align with historical precedent and giving projections/goals.

- Budget Comparison Note 4: Benefits increasing to account
for the employee educational benefit.

- Budget Comparison Note 5: The reductions in budgets, year
over year, are the result of cost cutting efforts to identify and
reduce expenditures not essential to the execution of the
mission of the college.

<a id="ga52-p187"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=189 printed_page=187 -->

- Budget Comparison Note 6: Contracts and Leases are
increasing due largely to increased food costs and an
increased student population.

- Budget Comparison Note 7: Maintenance costs are increasing
with anticipated utilities increases as well as strategic
investments in non-capital repairs and maintenance.

- Budget Comparison Note 8: Capital spending is budgeted to
increase by $800,000 to allocate resources to care for the
aging campus.

- Budget Comparison Note 9: Year over year, we are projecting
an improvement, on a cash basis approaching $1,500,000.

<a id="ga52-p188"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=190 printed_page=188 -->

<a id="ga52-p191"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=193 printed_page=191 -->

**COVENANT THEOLOGICAL SEMINARY**

**2025-2026 PROPOSED BUDGET**

**Economic Considerations and General Ministry Factors**

**Regular Operations** : Covenant Theological Seminary is an accredited
graduate-level education ministry of the Presbyterian Church in America
training pastors, counselors, and others who seek to minister in Christ’s
church. Its purpose is to glorify the triune God by training his servants to walk
in God’s grace, minister God’s Word, and equip God’s people— all for God’s
mission.

**General Ministry Factors:** The Seminary uses data on past and current trends
combined with qualitative information about the conditions believed to drive
trends to determine its two main income streams, tuition and donations.
Budgets for its other important revenue sources, endowment income, use of
restricted funds, and housing income are based on the fair market value of
investments, restricted fund balances and fundraising initiatives, and rent and
occupancy rates.

**Major Changes in Budget**

This year there were no major changes in the budget.

**Income Streams**

**Net Tuition:** The Seminary uses credit hours sold (CHS) as its primary
financial measure of enrollment. Expected CHS for FY25 is 7,600. Based on
prior experience our FY26 budget anticipates recruiting 150 students in fall
2025 and 6% growth in CHS to 8,050.

**Gifts and Donations:** The unrestricted gifts and donations budget of
$2,937,246 i san increase of 17.5% over the FY25 budget of $2,500,000. This
increase is based on actual fundraising results in FY23 and FY24.

**Endowment Draw:** The endowment draw percentage continues to be 5% of
the endowment’s average fair market value.

<a id="ga52-p192"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=194 printed_page=192 -->

**Major Ministry Not Implemented in the Past Year**

We did not have a major ministry that was not implemented in the past year.

**Notes to Budget Line Items**

The Seminary described in its explanatory notes last year, its plan improve
enrollment through energetic engagement with denominational leaders and
partners. While the recruitment landscape is challenging for seminaries,
Covenant is continuing to see improved enrollment and continues to build
toward a CHS goal of 11,000. At the Seminary’s current level of CHS, it is
managing operating losses through the controlled use of unrestricted reserve
funds. The loss of $463,947 budgeted for FY26 is a significant improvement
over the budgeted loss of $866,725 for FY25. After FY26, the Seminary’s
unrestricted reserves are expected to be $1,319,791.
