**GENEVA BENEFITS, INC.**

**2026 PROPOSED BUDGET**

**I.** **Economic Considerations and General Ministry Factors**
A. Geneva Vision: We believe the church thrives and the gospel
advances when men and women who serve PCA ministries grow
spiritually and financially healthy.

B. Geneva Mission: We guide PCA pastors and ministry workers
through the complexities of financial planning and employee
benefits so they and their families are able to live generously in
every season of ministry.

C. This budget reflects the costs incurred to administer the trusts for
Geneva Benefits Group. This budget does not reflect the financial
activity in those trusts. (Geneva's Annual Report provides
complete financial activity in the trusts, including unaudited
financial statements.)

II. **Major Changes in Budget**
A. The 2026 budget reflects a 6.5% increase from the prior year, or
$412,983 (Budget Comparisons – Total Support & Revenue). This
change is funded by a 10.5% increase in trustee fees. (Budget
Comparisons – Lines 1-3).

B. Program income declined by 77.5% following the conclusion of a
long-term grant in 2025. (Budget Comparisons – Line 6)

C. Administration expenses, which include payroll costs, increased
by 5% or$278,462 (Budget Comparisons – Line 9).

D. The total number of staff budgeted for 2026 is 28.75 FTE (FullTime Equivalent). This number includes three planned new hires
in 2025: a Marketing Coordinator, a Financial Planner, and a
Financial Paraplanner.

E. The Retirement portion of Support and Revenue increased by
4.7%, or $162,485, as higher expenses increased the overall

<a id="ga52-p197"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=199 printed_page=197 -->

Trustee Fees allocated to Retirement and Insurance. (See details
above at II. A.; Budget Comparisons – Line 1).

F. The insurance portion of Support and Revenue increased by 4.7%,
or $81,243, as higher expenses increased the overall trustee fees
allocated to retirement and insurance. (See details above at II. A.;
Budget Comparisons – Line 2).

G. The Relief portion of Support and Revenue shows a 52.2%
increase, or $375,136. The fee increase is driven by the launch of
anew pastor support program and the conclusion o fa long-term
grant, which is set to end in 2025. (See details above at II.A.;
Budget Comparisons – Line 3 and Line 6).

H. The Insurance TPA income portion of Support and Revenue
reflects fee income assessed by Geneva for in-house
administration of the Insurance plan. Historically, there was a
preference to isolate this income stream. Starting in 2025, this
income was incorporated into the Insurance Trustee Fees
calculation. (Budget Comparisons – Line 4).

I. The Retirement Fee Income portion of Support and Revenue
represents administrative fees from the self-directed investment
accounts in the Retirement Plan (reclassification/phase-out of this
income began in 2023) and correction fees related to retirement
account services. (Budget Comparisons – Line 5).

J. The Other Income portion of Support and Revenue reflects
estimated registration income for the annual Ministerial Relief
Golf Tournament and investment income from invested cash

reserves. (Budget Comparisons – Line 7)

K. The 2026 budget reflects $60,000 for capital expenditures: office
improvements, scheduled equipment replacement, and new hire
computer and equipment purchases. (Proposed Budget – Line 26)

L. Please note that 2024 actuals are unaudited as of the drafting of
the Budget Package. The 2024 audit is expected to be completed

<a id="ga52-p198"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=200 printed_page=198 -->

in May of 2025. (Budget Comparisons and Five-Year
Comparison).

**III.** **Income Stream**

Geneva's primary budgeted revenue sources are as follows:
1) Trustee fees charged to:

a. PCA Health and Welfare Benefit Trust for Insurance

- **b.** Geneva Benefits Group Charitable Relief Trust
- **c.** PCA 403(b)(9) Retirement Plan and Other Plans Trust

2) Program Income

**IV.** **Major Ministry Not Implemented in the Past Year**
All budgeted ministries were implemented in the past year.

**V.** **Notes to Budget Line Items**
A. Occupancy expense for the shared facility is expected to increase
from $15 to $16 per square foot (Proposed Budget – Line 15).

B. All fundraising activities are related to the Ministerial Relief
department through our development activities, including annual
Relief Offering, appeals through PCA Foundation, and
advertising in denominational publications (Proposed Budget –
Fundraising Column). The 2026 fundraising budget includes a
portion of the salary and benefits expenses for the Director of
Philanthropy and Donor Relations Manager.

**MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA**

**2026 PROPOSED BUDGET**

**Economic Considerations and General Ministry Factors**
The Committee on Mission to North America (MNA) is a Permanent
Committee of the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA), serving PCA
churches and presbyteries under the nonprofit corporation of the PCA. Per
_Rules of Assembly Operations_ 6-2, “The affairs of the church involved in its
extension in the United States and Canada are assigned to the Committee on
Mission to North America.”

MNA accomplishes its mission to cultivate kingdom advancement through the
PCA in North America through its various ministries. For over 50 years, MNA
has served as the denominational committee tasked with church planting and
resourcing.

MNA provides expertise with excellence to all PCA churches, presbyteries,
and networks in North America by offering a suite of practical ministry
resources – training, equipment, and assisting in missional purpose, serving
communities to advance God’s kingdom.

MNA serves the PCA by focusing on Church Vitality and Church Planting.
This focus means (is defined by) supporting PCA churches, presbyteries, and
networks in all facets of planting new churches and strengthening existing
churches.

As the denominational committee tasked with church planting and resourcing,
MNA deploys a variety of ministries to resource particular areas of the Church,
its members, and their respective areas of service within the broader kingdom.

MNA’s 10-year vision is to see the church increase within the PCA and
through this effort, see the world gripped, changed, and set on fire by the
gospel. The church, denomination, nation, and world will be blessed as the
church grows and thrives.

By resourcing our churches for kingdom impact, by 2033, MNA will facilitate
the multiplication of PCA churches in the U.S. and Canada from 1,932 to 3,000

<a id="ga52-p203"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=205 printed_page=203 -->

congregations. By the grace of God, our church will prayerfully accomplish
this by:

- Planting new churches

- Reducing church closures

- Expanding the diversity of the PCA

- Partnering with denominational committees and agencies

- Enfolding existing congregations into the PCA

**Major Changes in Budget**
MNA is committed to good stewardship of its resources. While being good
stewards of resources, MNA also understands the realities of the cost of
launching a bold 10-year vision as explained above. Therefore, the proposed
budget includes a healthy growth rate increase to facilitate the strategic plan
associated with the vision.

_**Transitioning Bent Tree, Coaching Services, LAMP, and SecondCareer**_
As a part of the ongoing refined focus, in 2024 MNA assessed ministry
alignment around church planting and vitality (outward facing mobilization of
church members). The result being that four of MNA’s ministries transitioned
to a ministry partner/recommended ministry status and will not be listed as an
MNA ministry. Those ministries are Bent Tree, Coaching Services, LAMP
Seminary, and SecondCareer. We believe that these ministries are important
and needed in our church and we are committed to helping them flourish.
These changes, though hard, are the culmination of our multi-year trajectory
and we believe are necessary for MNA to grow in faithfulness to our charge
from the General Assembly for the expansion of the kingdom in the United
States and Canada.

**Income Streams**

MNA’s main income streams come through constituent donations, partnership
share giving, and investment income. MNA’s fundraising team continues to
grow and be equipped for increased development efforts as it relates to
implementation of MNA’s new vision.

**Major Ministry Not Implemented in the Past Year**
All budgeted ministries were implemented in the past year.

**Notes to Budget Line Items**

<a id="ga52-p204"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=206 printed_page=204 -->

MNA is submitting a proposed 2026 budget that is an increase of almost 7%
from the 2025 budget. Due to an increase in church planter project accounts
and growth in permanent staff ministry development in conjunction with
MNA’s overall vision, we believe this is a reasonable Total Expense Budget
for 2026.

Per Capita Calculation: The 2026 Proposed Total Expense Budget of $ is
adjusted using the following formula:

2026 Proposed Total Expense Budget $ 32,345,343
2026 Proposed Church Planters Support (18,375,000)
Subtotal 13,970,343

2026 Budgeted Investment Income (476,816)
2026 Budgeted Conference and Other Revenue (531,568)
Total Net Partnership Share Fund $ 12,961,959

The _per capita_ calculation of the Partnership Share Fund will be $12,961,959
divided by the number of PCA members. The MNA Ministry Ask figure is
$28 for 2026.

An overall net increase of approximately 10% in salaries and benefits is
assumed. That is an aggregate of cost of living, merit increases and health
insurance costs.

The cost being charged for office space in the PCA building located at 1700
N. Brown Road, Lawrenceville, GA increased during 2024. A rate of $16 per
square foot is assumed for the 2026 budget projection.

<a id="ga52-p208"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=210 printed_page=208 -->

**MISSION TO THE WORLD**

**PROPOSED CONSOLIDATED 2026 BUDGET**

**I. Economic Considerations and General Ministry Focus:**
The proposed 2026 budget is based on an analysis of key factors that influence
the income and expenses of Mission to the World as it operates in a global
context with a rapidly changing global economy. We start by reviewing the
results of 2024 and extend these indicators into 2025 and 2026.

The stock market had a good year in 2024! The S&P 500 index went up by
over 26% and the NASDAQ jumped by 32%. This growth was mainly due to
the robust company earnings and increased popularity of new technology like
artificial intelligence. The U.S. economy also did well. Unemployment was
low at 4.1%, inflation went down a bit, and the Federal Reserve cut interest
rates for the first time in years.

Due to the solid performance of the market, MTW’s investments experienced
a positive return in 2024. Analysts predict that the stock market will continue
to perform well in 2025, although there may be some volatility. Therefore, we
are budgeting accordingly with modest increases in endowment and
investment income. Also, inflation predictions for 2025 suggest that inflation
rates may trend slightly upward compared to the end of 2024. Compensation
for home office staff and missionaries were adjusted for inflation in 2024 and
increases are budgeted for the following two years.

Remembering that the entire program of MTW is by the grace of God, we want
to give God praise for a positive year. In 2024, giving from our churches
increased by 2% and MTW saw a 4% increase in giving to our missionaries.
We rejoice that we were able to see our ministries fully funded and the faithful
support of our donors.

**II. Major Changes in Budget:**
changes in budget reflect current economic conditions and a desire t ob ea
good steward of the resources God gives us through His people. We carefully
worked with each department to reach a balanced budget in the home office.
Several adjustments helped reach the proposed budget. The outcome should
allow us to continue to give full support to our missionaries while helping them
to advance ministry.

<a id="ga52-p209"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=211 printed_page=209 -->

In 2025, we plan to continually increase our engagement with national partners
at a strategic level and emphasize partnerships with PCA churches and other
agencies to advance church planting around the world. We will seek to open
new ministries with an emphasis on church planting and other support
ministries tied closely to the churches with which we work.

In 2024, we experienced a decrease of 34 long-term missionaries which was
mainly attributed to the retirement of our aging missionaries. Due to the
modification of the missionary selection process, the category of short-term
missionaries will phase out in the following years which explains the decrease
of 16 short-term missionaries over 2023. Praise God, we welcomed 36 new
initial term missionaries in 2024. At the end of their initial term and if

approved, these missionaries will transition to long-term missionaries. We also
experienced an increase of eight interns and an increase of 250 one-to-threeweek trip volunteers.

**Ministry Personnel Plans** **2022** **2023** **2024 2025 Plan 2026 Plan**
Long-Term Missionaries 570 543 509 510 515

Short-Term Missionaries 66 51 35 20 5

Initial Term Missionaries - 35 71 120 130

Intern Missionaries 75 92 100 100 105

Volunteers 1026 818 1068 900 950

Efforts of the Partner Relations Department will continue to focus on raising
endowment funds and increasing planned giving that will help mitigate the
administrative fee for long-term missionaries and provide funding for the
Partner Relations Department’s strategic initiatives. The last quarter of 2024
was one of the busiest quarters that the Partner Relations Department had in
the number of estate and giving presentations and the number of attendees.
Additionally, a new Donor Advised Fund portal for our partners will be
implemented in the first quarter of 2025.

The U.S. Operations team has undergone some significant changes due to the
regional mobilization centers (hubs) not showing the desired results of
mobilizing new missionaries and project funds supporting the hubs being
exhausted in the end of 2024. U.S. Ops has changed its focus to equipping
churches for engagement in global missions and sending missionaries, in
addition to engaging more volunteers.

<a id="ga52-p210"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=212 printed_page=210 -->

The launch of a new mtw.org website and donor portal was delayed in 2024
due to third-party vendors who introduced a process change that was not in the
original timeline and who also introduced untested processes that could impact
the donor experience on the new donations portal. The Information
Technology Department is currently searching for a new donor platform that
is within MTW budget and can support our needs.

**III. Income Streams:**

Projections have been made regarding the number of missionaries, office
personnel, annual income, and annual expenses. In making these projections,
the following assumptions have been used:

- We anticipate that continued efforts to recruit missionaries in 2025
will show positive results during 2025. We praise God because we
have seen an increase in individuals applying to become MTW
missionaries. MTW continues to focus on its goal of mobilizing
churches to send out at least 1% of their adult members for world

missions.

- We plan to put home office staff hiring on hold in 2025 to support the
strategic initiative to control the growth of administrative fees. Any
additions will be directly related to new ministry that will generate
needed income.

- With 2025 projections of inflation to increase and other global
economic factors to be unstable, we anticipate an increase in ministry
costs. Therefore, we find it will be necessary to take specific steps to
keep income and expenses in balance.

- Income projections have assumed a gradual increase in donor giving.
We have projected the support requirements of missionaries, adjusted
the numbers for inflation, and balanced this with future income and
growth projections. For expense projections, we analyzed the historic
and economic trends and adjusted operating expenses accordingly.

- Missionary support accounts with deficit balances increased in 2024
and our Resource Team continues to collaborate closely with each
missionary account in deficit or trending toward deficit to address their
ongoing support needs.

- Partnership share giving for the home office indicated an increase in
2024 and is projected to grow steadily in the coming years.

<a id="ga52-p211"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=213 printed_page=211 -->

- Project and team income are calculated by reviewing active and
planned special projects. There was an increase in project and team
income in 2024 and we expect increases in the coming years. Our
Ambassadors program continues to provide major funding for new
fields, church planting, training nationals, and mercy ministry.

- Investment income projections assume continued volatility in the
stock market and modest growth in the following two years. The fixed
monthly administrative assessment charge per missionary remains the
same from 2024. With controlled or specially funded costs in the home
office, we expect to keep the general fund in balance.

- In 2025, estimated medical and pharmaceutical costs are expected to
increase by 8%-12% in the United States. As a result, the monthly
health insurance premium for missionaries and office staff were
increased in 2025 by 8%. Due to delayed processing and payment of
medical claims in 2024 by our new third-party administrator, we are
anticipating an influx of 2024 medical claims in the first half of 2025.

**IV. Major Ministry Not Implemented in the Past Year**
All major ministry items were implemented.

**V. Notes to Budget**
The following three tables show the consolidated income and expense budget
proposed for 2026. The first table shows the 2026 budget broken down into
major components. The second table presents a historical perspective showing
2024 unaudited actual information and budget approved at General Assembly,
2025 modified budget, 2026 proposed budget and the changes in budget from
2025 to 2026. The third table shows a five-year history of income and

expenses.

**PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

**FOUNDATION, INC**
**2026 PROPOSED BUDGET**

**I. Economic Considerations and General Ministry Factors**

The PCA Foundation (PCAF) is organized for the “purposes… of the Church
of Jesus Christ, including the carrying out of the Great Commission, the
support of churches, presbyteries, and other committees and agencies of the
Presbyterian Church in America, and distributions to or for the use of
organizations… that minister to man’s spiritual, physical, emotional, and
intellectual powers.” (Article IV, PCAF Articles of Incorporation)

The purpose of the PCAF is accomplished primarily by making grants of funds
contributed by individuals and families. The PCAF encourages contributions
by providing information and education about, and facilitating, generous, taxefficient contributions, and makes effective grants by enabling donors to
recommend grants and providing information to donors about charitable
endeavors worthy of support.

The PCAF offers the following charitable giving-granting programs: donoradvised funds, including Advise and Consult Funds, Increase Funds, and
Single Charity Funds, Charitable Remainder Trusts, Charitable Lead Trusts,
Endowments, Designated Funds for churches and other PCA entities, gifts of
appreciated non-cash property, bequests, and presentation of information
about strategic giving and granting to individuals, and churches, presbyteries,
and other groups.

The PCAF has experienced significant growth in recent years, which has
provided increased funds for grants and support and education activities. As
of December 31, 2024, its assets totaled $407.4 million. Compared to
December 31, 2019, total assets of $104.0 million, total assets have increased
$303.4 million over the last five years. In addition to substantially increased
giving, improving financial markets over that period, with 2022 being an
exception, and increased awareness of the PCAF’s charitable services have
been significant contributors to the PCAF’s growth.

<a id="ga52-p216"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=218 printed_page=216 -->

The PCA Foundation’s proposed Operating and Capital Budget for 2026 of
$2,710,893 represents a $307,948 or 12.8% increase from the General
Assembly approved budget for 2025 of $2,402,945.

**II. Major Changes in Budget**

The major changes in budgeted revenue included in the proposed 2026 Budget
compared to 2025 Budget are increased balanced-based revenue from Increase
Funds and similar funds and trusts, and a lesser payout from the Advise &
Consult Fund earnings. The PCAF anticipates $476,000 in increased balancedbased and other account charges, and $96,000 less in payout from Advise &
Consult Fund earnings.

The major changes in budgeted expenses included in the proposed 2026
Budget compared to 2025 Budget are net increased wages and benefits due to
two new staff positions added in 2025 and loss of one employee in 2025 not
to be replaced until part-way through 2026, and the addition of new SaaS
(software-as-a-service) applications. The PCAF anticipates costs of $91,725
for increases in existing staff wages and benefits and $100,750 for increases
in the cost of SaaS agreements.

**III. Income Streams**

The PCAF is self-supported. It does not participate in the PCA’s Partnership
Shares Program, nor does it rely on the financial support of churches to help
underwrite its operating expenses.

Approximately 61% of the PCAF’s total 2026 budgeted operating revenue is
interest/earnings generated on its Advise and Consult Funds®, the PCAF
Endowment (consistent with prior years) and several other invested assets.
Trustee fees and administrative-cost charges imposed on Increase Funds,
Charitable Trusts, Endowments and other charged accounts are expected to be
36% of 2026 budgeted operating revenue. Direct charitable contributions for
operational support (primarily from a small number of individuals and Board
members) account for the remaining 3%. Income streams budgeted for 2026
are higher than those budgeted for in the prior year due to an increase in
projected complex gifts.

<a id="ga52-p217"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=219 printed_page=217 -->

The sources described above are attainable and sufficient to provide the 2026
budgeted operating revenues.

**IV. Major Ministry Not Implemented in the Past Year**

There were no new major ministry plans of the PCA Foundation scheduled for
implementation during 2024.

**V. Notes to Budget “line items”**

**General Comments**
The 2026 Operating and Capital Budget of $2,710,893 represents a $307,948
or 12.8% increase compared to the 2025 Budget of $2,402,945.

**Support & Revenue**
The 2026 Budget for Support and Revenue is $2,800,320, the amount needed
to fund the 2026 Operating and Capital Budget.

Undesignated Earnings (line 1) – These payouts are from funds held by the
PCA Foundation, mainly from Advise & Consult Funds and the PCAF
Endowment, which help underwrite the Foundation’s operating expenses. The
payout percentages are set annually by the PCA Foundation’s Board and
generally are somewhat correlated to the expected investment returns of the
accounts. However, during times when the expected investment returns may
be lower than the payout amounts needed to fund operations, reserves in these
accounts are significantly more than adequate to compensate for the
difference. The 2026 Budget of $1,704,000 represents a reduction of $96,000
or 5.3% less than the 2025 Budget amount of $1,800,000.

Account Charges (line 2) – 2026 account charges are fees and balanced-based
charges on funds held for long term administration such as Increase Funds,
Charitable Remainder Trusts, Charitable Lead Trusts, Endowments, and
Designated Funds. The 2026 Budget amount of $1,015,320 compares to the
2025 Budget amount of $539,500, an increase of $475,820 or 88.2%.
Balanced-based and other account charges are projected to be $974,000, pergrant charges are projected to be $21,320, and per-gift transaction charges for
complex gifts are projected to be $20,000.

<a id="ga52-p218"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=220 printed_page=218 -->

Contributions (line 3) – Gifts primarily from a small number of individuals
and Board members help underwrite the Foundation’s Operating Budget.
Contributions budgeted for 2026 are $57,500, compared to $57,500 in the 2025
Budget.

**Operations Expenses**
The 2026 amount budgeted for operating expenses is $2,698,643, compared to
$2,419,270 budgeted for 2025, an increase of $279,373 or 11.6%.

Staff Wages & Benefits (lines 5, 6 and 7) – 2026 is budgeted at $1,799,377,
representing an increase of 5.4% or $91,725 from the 2025 Budget amount of
$1,707,652. The increase results from hiring a new associate business manager
in 2025, and a new accounting assistant in 2025, and budgeted cost-of-living
and work performance salary increases.

The 2026 Budget for Staff Wages and Benefits of $1,799,377 represents an
increase of $340,043 or 23.3% over the 2024 Actual expenditure of
$1,459,334. However, of this increase, $139,425 is due to hiring an associate
business manager in 2025 and $42,632 is due to hiring a new accounting
assistant in 2025. The remaining increase of $157,986 is due primarily to
timing of actual hire dates compared to forecasted hire dates in previous
budgets, and inflation-required increases in wage, payroll tax and retirement
plan contribution expenses.

All Other Operating Expenses (lines 8-23) – All other operating expenses for
the 2026 Budget are $899,266, compared to $711,618 in the 2025 Budget, an
increase of $187,648 or 26.4%.

**Capital Expenditures**
Capital Expenditures (line 24) – The 2026 Budget of $49,750 consists
primarily of computer hardware and office furnishings for operations and
donor relations and a new vehicle.

**REFORMED UNIVERSITY FELLOWSHIP**

**2026 PROPOSED BUDGET**

**The RUF Mission:**

The mission of Reformed University Fellowship is to build the church now
and for the future by reaching students for Christ and equipping students to
serve both in the United States and globally.

**I.** **Economic Considerations and General Ministry Factors**

- This budget reflects our continuing growth as we develop new
RUF works on campuses worldwide. For 2026, we project to
have over 200 campus ministries worldwide.

- The proposed 2026 budget for the entire ministry is
$64,331,663.

- There is a net increase in ministry expenses of 8% from the 2025
approved budget and a 6% increase over 2024 actuals.

- There is an overall net increase of 9% in salaries and related
adjustments to benefits for all existing staff positions from the
approved 2025 budget and a net increase of 7.5% from 2024
actuals.

**II.** **Major Changes in Budget**

- The only significant change to the budget in 2026 is the
continued impact of the execution of the 50-year Capital
Campaign.

**III.** **Income Streams**

- Income for the 2026 budget is projected to come from
contributions (88%), medical fund (7%), conference revenues
(4%) and other revenue (0.3%).

<a id="ga52-p223"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=225 printed_page=223 -->

- There is a net increase in ministry income of 9% from the 2025
approved budget and a 4% increase over 2024 actuals.

**IV.** **Major Ministry Items Not Implemented**

- The only major ministry item planned for 2025 is a continuing
capital campaign.

**V.** **Notes to Budget Line Items**

All other categories are projected at a moderate 7.5%.

<a id="ga52-p227"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=229 printed_page=227 -->

**RIDGE HAVEN**

**BREVARD, NC—CONO, IA**

**2026 PROPOSED BUDGET**

**I.** **Economic Considerations and General Ministry Factors**

The Lord’s protection was uniquely felt this year, especially during
the fall amidst Hurricane Helene. Our staff and their homes were safe

and in general our buildings on campus did not suffer significant
damage. Dealing with hundreds of trees down and no electricity or cell
service for overawe ek was a small price to pay for our safety.
Unfortunately, almost 1,000 guests canceled, and additional funds
were reallocated to cover the expenses incurred brought on by the
storm and its lasting damage. Staff and volunteers spent several
months working to remove trees and debris, repair damages, in order
to reopen campus safely for our guests. We focused our cleanup efforts
on the main campus so that we were able to reopen once the power
was turned back on just under two weeks from the initial outage. We
are continuing to fervently work on cleanup and repairs of the rest of
the campus as we prepare for a full summer of ministry.

Even with Helene we once again had the privilege of hosting over
13,000 guests with well over 200 unique churches and ministries in
2024.

We are actively working to complete our Cono activity pool in Iowa
this summer after a year's delay due to State Agency turnover and our
pool company going out of business. We are working with a new pool
company that should have the pool ready for this summer. In Brevard
we have hopes to complete our new chapel atop Keyes Summit and
the building of four new family cabins as soon as possible to
accommodate the increase in campers this summer. Additionally, we
anticipate breaking ground on our new lake this spring which has been
a pressing need for numerous summers now.

Cono has seen immense growth over the past year, and we are honored
to be able to serve our churches in the Midwest. We are pleased to
announce that we have five Presbyteries holding camps with us this

<a id="ga52-p228"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=230 printed_page=228 -->

summer and several more weeks full of overnight camps. Our hope is
to continue finding ways to serve the entire PCA and one aspect of this
is the avenue we have for raising and training youth leaders for the
church. We continue to have increased opportunities for ministry
internships, starting as early as High School, which includes our
Explorer’s Project, as well as increasing our full-time staff positions.

**II.** **Major Changes in Budget**

We anticipate continued growth in ministry along with the
infrastructure and capital needed to support it. As noted below, we will
be announcing a new Campaign this fall that will include new adult
housing, family cabins, new lake and camp area in Brevard along with
continued expansion at Cono in Iowa which is reflected in our 2026
budget.

**III.** **Income Streams**

Ridge Haven receives support/revenue from the following sources:

1. Camp, conference, and retreat fees (includes food service and

camp store/bookstore revenue).
2. Facility, house rentals, and Cono School and farmland rental.
3. Contributions (includes partnership shares and direct
contributions).
4. Minor sources of revenue, which include resident fees

(water/sewer fees, road assessments, etc.), and interest-bearing
bank accounts.

**IV.** **Major Ministry Not Implemented in the Past Year**

As noted above, we unfortunately were unable to complete our activity
pool at Cono due to numerous unforeseen circumstances.
Additionally, several projects in Brevard including the chapel, family
cabins and new lake were expected to be completed by summer of
2025 however Hurricane Helene delayed us starting on these projects
by three months and limited us financially.

<a id="ga52-p229"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=231 printed_page=229 -->

We had hoped to announce a new 6M Capital Campaign this year to
accommodate our growing ministry opportunities, but Helene caused
us to delay this until fall. The storm not only hindered us financially,
but we willingly encouraged our construction and contractor
companies to spend the remainder of 2024 helping those in our area
with greater needs.

The delayed start to clearing for the new lake and Family Camp
Complex is now expected to begin in the next few months, so it is
expected to be ready for the summer of 2026. The Complex will have
its own gym, dining hall, lake and cabins all near our South Rec
activity area. Eventually this complex will be able to accommodate
another 250-300 guests in this self-contained area. This will be great
for Family Camps, Church-wide retreats, Presbytery gatherings, and
College ministries.

The final aspects of the campaign will hopefully see the completion of
adult housing and further staff housing in Brevard, while Cono
upgrades will include opening additional housing opportunities and a
State mandated upgrade to our septic system to keep up with the
increasing demand.

**V.** **2026 Budget Line Items Notes**

Notes refer to all three inserts below.

**Line 2**, Property – This line item includes revenue from lot
maintenance fees, water hookups, water usage fees, and road
maintenance fees from 18 lots which are contiguous to our main
campus. The amount budgeted each year reflects the predictable
aspects of this revenue, i.e. the principal and interest being paid on lot
leases being bought over time, the annually collected lot lease
maintenance fees, water usage fees, and a portion of the road
maintenance fees. This line item also includes our farmland rental and

rental houses at Cono.

**Line 3 & 4**, Contributions – related to expected upcoming projects.

<a id="ga52-p230"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=232 printed_page=230 -->

**Line 6**, Miscellaneous – Includes refund of state sales tax,
amortization of lot leases, Right-of-Way land sales, staff rentals, and
interest revenue. In 2021, this also included our Payroll Tax Refund.

**Line 7**, Payroll and Benefits – Includes payroll and benefits for 28
year-round employees including the Executive Director, part-time
staff, plus over 100 seasonal staff both for Ridge Haven Brevard, and
Ridge Haven Cono. Speakers, and musicians’ honorariums are
included in this category, as well as payroll taxes and workers’
compensation insurance. The Executive Director’s Salary is projected
at $69,000.00 along with on-campus housing and benefits
($17,889.12).

**Line 9**, Office and Administrative – Includes major expense items,
including commercial insurance, telephone fees, office and
housekeeping supplies, loan interest and bank fees, and audit and legal
fees.

**Line 11**, Facilities & Maintenance – Includes all maintenance, upkeep,
repairs, deferred maintenance, real estate taxes, and refuse expenses.
Also includes vehicle parts and service, fuel costs, and equipment
leases. Increased due to added capital buildings along with need to
outsource more ongoing projects.

**Line 13**, Ministry – Includes ministry supplies, registration software
fees, and travel and other expenses associated with our camp for innercity kids, which we underwrite 100%.

**Line 14**, Recruitment & Development – Includes all printing costs,
promotional ads and media productions, recruitment initiatives, trips, and
Development Officers and Recruiters salaries.

Supplemental Report of the AC

**SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF THE**

**ADMINISTRATIVE COMMITTEE**

Following its called meeting June 5, 2025, the Administrative Committee
presents the following amended, added, and rescinded recommendations to the
52 [nd] General Assembly:

**RECOMMENDATIONS**
**Recommendation 1** to the 52 [nd] General Assembly was rescinded.

The AC accepted the retirement of TE Bryan Chapell as Stated Clerk at a
called meeting on June 5, 2025. Given the timing of his retirement, the AC
cannot fulfill its duty to nominate a new clerk at the 52 [nd] General
Assembly. We plan to employ RE John Bise as a provisional replacement
( _RAO_ 4-17) pending his approval by TEC. We intend to bring a nominee
for Stated Clerk to the 53 [rd] General Assembly.

**Recommendation 30** to the 52 [nd] General Assembly was amended as follows:

30. That the 2026 General Assembly registration fee be set at $600 for

Teaching Elders from churches with $150,000 or more in annual tithes and
offerings as well as for other TEs not called to churches. Of that amount,
$425 will be allocated toga costs, $125 to the Standing Committees for
expenses including the Standing Judicial Committee, and $50 to Statistics
and Publications. Starting in 2027, this fee is proposed to be annually
inflation-adjusted.

Further, the AC recommends that the 2026 fee for _all_ Ruling Elders
and Teaching Elders from churches with _under_ $150,000 in income be
$425. The entire amount will be allocated to General Assembly costs.
Starting in 2027, this fee is proposed to be annually inflation-adjusted.

In 2028, the AC recommends that the fee for Ruling Elders from
churches with $150,000 or more in annual income be increased to match
the fee paid by Teaching Elders from churches with $150,000 or more in
annual tithes and offerings.

(Honorably retired TEs and REs are not included in the analysis or
proposed changes and would continue to pay $150.)

<a id="ga52-p235"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=237 printed_page=235 -->

Grounds:

Following a change in price structure in 2023 when all Ruling
Elder registration fees were set at a lowered rate of $300, we did
not see a corresponding increase in registrations—Ruling Elders
comprised approximately 30% of the total commissioners both
before and after the fee change. As a result, after the 2023 increase
to Teaching Elder registration fees, every two TEs paying $75
more each only offset one Ruling Elder paying $150 less. In sum,
the fee change neither increased Ruling Elder attendance at
General Assembly nor increased revenue to cover General
Assembly costs.

City and Year TE Attendance RE Attendance
St. Louis 2021 1499 (71%) 615 (29%)
Birmingham 2022 1634 (69%) 751 (31%)

Memphis 2023 1607 (70%) 694 (30%)

Richmond 2024 1459 (69%) 654 (31%)

Further, the GA registration fee has not kept pace with
inflation. This proposed change catches up on past inflation and
sets a course to adjust to inflation in real time.

From a historical perspective, the TE and RE fee was $400 in
2006, and it was raised to $450 (a $50 increase) for both TEs and
REs in 2013. In 2023, the TE and RE fees were rebalanced (with
TEs—2/3s of the total increased by $75, and REs—1/3 of the total
decreased by $150). So, the net effect is that total fees for the AC
have only increased $50 (which was done in 2013) since 2006
when both TEs and REs were raised last at $400. In other words,
over the past 20 years the net increase to GA fees has been just
$50.

36. Regarding **Overture 47** from Great Lakes Presbytery, “Erect Ad Interim

Committee on Christian Nationalism,” the Administrative Committee
offers **NO OPINION** per se on the wisdom of the proposed study
committee. In the event the Assembly answers Overtures 3, 4 and 47 in
the affirmative, then the AC notes that approving the establishment of such
an ad interim committee on the study of Christian Nationalism would have
a budgets eta ta maximum of $10,000, provided solely by designated gifts
to the AC. The AC further notes: “Funding should not be the burden

<a id="ga52-p236"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=238 printed_page=236 -->

primarily of the members of the ad interim committee nor the AC, but of
those requesting the ad interim committee. Any overture proposing an ad
interim committee should ordinarily include a plan for how sufficient,
designated funds for the ad interim committee will be raised.” ( _RAO_ 9-3)

any study committee approved without a funding plan requires a two thirds (2/3) vote of the General Assembly as an amendment to the AC
budget ( _RAO_ 9-3, 4-11). Ordinarily the AC can only administer funds that
have been designated and received for an ad interim study committee
(RAO 9-3); funds will be distributed by the AC only as they are made
available in the project account.

The AC also notes “only two (2) ad interim committees may be
appointed or continued in any given year, unless additional ones are
approved by a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the Assembly” ( _RAO_ 9-3).
Overtures 3, 4, 26, 27, 47, 48 and 49 are all requesting an ad interim
committee to the 52 [nd] General Assembly.

37. That **Overture 48** from Tennessee Valley Presbytery “Erect an Ad Interim

Committee for a Pastoral Letter on Christian Citizenship and Church-State
Relations” be answered in the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

The overture stipulates that the proposed ad interim
committee shall be unfunded. The establishment of an ad interim

committee, necessitating resources for its effective operation,
without provision for requisite funding, constitutes a fiscally
imprudent measure.

Any study committee approved without a funding plan
requires a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the General Assembly as an
amendment to the AC budget ( _RAO_ 9-3, 4-11). Ordinarily the AC
can only administer funds that have been designated and received
for an ad interim study committee ( _RAO_ 9-3); funds will be
distributed by the AC only as they are made available in the
project account.

The AC further notes that “only two (2) ad interim committees
may be appointed or continued in any given year, unless additional
ones are approved by a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the Assembly”
( _RAO_ 9-3). Overtures 3, 4, 26, 27, 47, 48 and 49 are all requesting
an ad interim committee be approved by the 52nd General
Assembly.

The overture itself acknowledges the existence of
"substantial, biblical guidance concerning Christian citizenship

<a id="ga52-p237"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=239 printed_page=237 -->

and the relationship of the church to the state" within the
Westminster Standards, as adopted by this body, and the Preface
to the Book of Church Order of the Presbyterian Church in
America. These foundational documents provide a sufficient and
authoritative framework for the instruction of the Church on these

matters, rendering the creation of a new ad interim committee for
this purpose unnecessary at this time.

In the event the Assembly would answer Overture 48 in the
affirmative, then the AC notes: “Funding should not be the burden
primarily of the members of the ad interim committee nor the AC,
but of those requesting the ad interim committee. Any overture
proposing an ad interim committee should ordinarily include a
plan for how sufficient, designated funds for the ad interim
committee will be raised.” ( _RAO_ 9-3)

38. That **Overture 49** from Pacific Northwest Presbytery “Erect an Ad

Interim Committee on AI” be answered in the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

While acknowledging the importance of engaging with the
implications of artificial intelligence for the Church, the
Administrative Committee recommends a negative response to
this overture for the following reasons. We firmly believe that the
Church, through its grounding in established biblical principles
and Reformed understanding, as articulated in the Westminster
Confession of Faith concerning creation ( _WCF_ 4), humanity's
covenant with God ( _WCF_ 7), and the nature of the Church ( _WCF_
25), already possesses the foundational ethical guidance necessary
to apply general principles for the use or limits of artificial
intelligence technology.

Absence of a Comprehensive Funding Plan: While the
overture proposes an ongoing, annual budget of $15,000 to be
funded by gifts to the Administrative Committee, most study
committees are capped at $10,000. Further, no funding source is
guaranteed for even one year, and additional years could well
prove to be more costly given the potentially extensive scope of
the proposed study, including the need to consult theological,
technical, medical, and legal specialists. A study of this magnitude
warrants a more clearly defined and dedicated funding strategy to
ensure its thorough and effective execution.

<a id="ga52-p238"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=240 printed_page=238 -->

Regarding even regular study committees, the Administrative
Committee (AC) notes: "Any overture proposing an ad interim
committee should ordinarily include a plan for how sufficient,
designated funds for the ad interim committee will be raised.
Funding should not be the burden primarily of the members of the
ad interim committee or the AC, but of those requesting the ad
interim committee" ( _RAO_ 9-3). Any study committee approved
without a funding plan requires a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the
General Assembly as an amendment to the AC budget ( _RAO_ 9-3,
4-11). Funds would only be distributed by the AC as they are made
available in the project account.

Premature Nature Given Current LLM Development: The
overture seeks to address complex issues related to artificial
intelligence, while specific Large Language Models (LLMs) are
rapidly evolving. Because of the rate of change, it is premature to
establish a study committee tasked with providing definitive
guidance and principles for the Church at this time. The strengths,
weaknesses, and capabilities of today’s LLMs are still in flux, and
any report produced now risks becoming quickly outdated or
based on an incomplete understanding. These are matters more
appropriately addressed by scholars in our research and theology
institutions as this field develops.

Unrealistic Reporting Timeline for Committees and
Agencies: The overture directs all General Assembly committees,
commissions, and agencies to submit a report on their current and
proposed AI usage within six months (by December 31, 2025).
Given the rapid evolution of AI and the varying levels of expertise
and established protocols among these diverse church bodies and
institutions, producing a comprehensive or definitive report within
this timeframe presents significant challenges for the realistic
ability to gather the in-depth insights required for a complex and
ongoing study.

Summary: In summary, while acknowledging the importance
of addressing AI's implications, the Administrative Committee
recommends answering this overture in the negative. This
decision is based on the absence of a comprehensive funding plan
for such a significant study, the premature nature of establishing
definitive guidance given the rapidly evolving landscape of Large
Language Models, and the unrealistic reporting timeline proposed

<a id="ga52-p239"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=241 printed_page=239 -->

for committees and agencies. Crucially, the Administrative
Committee recognizes that established ethical guidance, rooted in
biblical principles and our Reformed understanding of humanity
and the Church ( _WCF_ 4, 7, 25), already exists for navigating new
technologies. Furthermore, the overture's broad language carries
inherent risks concerning intellectual property infringement and
the ethical use of copyrighted denominational materials within AI,
an area that requires careful, ongoing discernment rather than a
single, time-bound report.

The AC further notes that “only two (2) ad interim committees
may be appointed or continued in any given year, unless additional
ones are approved by a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the Assembly”
( _RAO_ 9-3). Overtures 3, 4, 26, 27, 47, 48, and 49 are all requesting
an ad interim committee be approved by the 52nd General
Assembly.

Respectfully submitted,

RE Danny McDaniel
Chairman

<a id="ga52-p240"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=242 printed_page=240 -->

Commission for Korean Southwest

**MINUTES OF THE**

**COMMISSION FOR THE REVIEW OF RECORDS**

**FROM KOREAN SOUTHWEST PRESBYTERY**

July 19,2024

10 AM Called to order by the Convener TE Stephen O’Neill.

Present: TE Stephen O’Neill, RE Jay Neikirk, TE Jason Piland, RE Flynt
Jones, TE Paul Lee, and RE Paul Goodrich.

A quorum was declared.

TE O’Neill opened with Prayer,

M/S/P To elect TE O’Neill as chair and RE Goodrich as secretary.

**Suggested Categories for exceptions for 2023 to be classified:**

I. Exceptions of Form – Keep in report to Presbytery

II. Exceptions of substance to which presbytery **does not** need to respond.

III. Exceptions of substance to which Presbytery **does** need to respond and

correct their minutes where possible.

M/S/P to accept these categories for classification of 2023 new exceptions.

The committee discussed requirements and details to determine whether
response was or wasn’t required.

**Suggested Categories for responses to prior years exceptions to be**
**classified:**

I. Fully Satisfied

II Unsatisfactory but no response required.

III Unsatisfactory – response required and correct record where possible.

<a id="ga52-p241"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=243 printed_page=241 -->

M/S/P to accept these categories for classification of prior years’ responses.

The committee reviewed the prior years responses and placed each one in one
of the three categories. The committee also found 4 exceptions that were not
responded to by presbytery.

A draft report reflecting the options is attached.
Next Meeting 7/26/24 at 3:45 to review draft reports. Make any needed edits
and produce final reports for assembly and clerk.

_[Note: No additional meetings were held._
_The final report was approved via email.]_

Need to determine proper method to submit report to the proper parties.

12:15 Adjourned with Prayer from RE Goodrich

**Respectfully Submitted**

_Paul D Goodrich_

**______________________________**
Commission Secretary

The Board of Directors of the Presbyterian Church in America (a corporation)

Lawrenceville, Georgia. President Bob Brunson called the meeting to order at
2:15 p.m.

Calvary, Alternate

The following items were presented for action:

**BD-6/24-1** That the following resolutions on Fifth Third Bank’s Deposit

Account Resolution Document be approved:

RESOLVED, that Fifth Third Bank, National Association (“Bank”) is
designated a depository of the Company, with full authority to accept
deposits made at any time, by any person, and in any form to the credit of

<a id="ga52-p246"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=248 printed_page=246 -->

this Company in accounts with Bank, in accordance with the written or
verbal instructions of the person(s) presenting the funds for deposit or of
any document accompanying said deposits and subject to the rules and
regulations of Bank.

RESOLVED FURTHER, that Bank is authorized to pay or otherwise
honor or apply without inquiry and without regard to the application of the
proceeds all checks, drafts, and other orders for the payment, transfer and
withdrawal of money from any and all accounts maintained by this
Company with Bank, including those drawn to the individual order of a
signer, when signed, accepted or endorsed by any of the following officers
or employees of this Company:

Bryan Chapell, Stated Clerk
Dixie Zietlow, Business Administrator
Heidi Harrison, Operations Manager
Angela Nantz, Meeting Planner

RESOLVED FURTHER, that any of the above named officer(s),
person(s), member(s), or partner(s) is/are hereby authorized on behalf of
this Company to (i) execute the necessary documents to conduct, if
applicable, wire transfer services, automated clearing house transactions,
electronic data interchange, sweep services, deposit management services,
and lockbox services; and (ii) authorize outgoing wire transfer requests
and transfer to and from the accounts of this Company using the funds
transfer system of the automated clearing house.

RESOLVED FURTHER, that any of the above named officer(s),
person(s), member(s), or partner(s) is/are hereby authorized on behalf of
this Company to delegate to another person or persons, as evidenced in
a form provided by Bank and executed by any of the above named
officer(s), person(s), member(s), or partner(s), the authority to (i) serve
as a Channel Administrator as that term is defined under the Bank’s

Online Channel Access Agreement that governs _Channel Services_, such
as Fifth Third Direct; and (ii) initiate electronic funds transfers through
use of a Channel Service or through Direct Send File Transfer.

RESOLVED FURTHER, that a facsimile signature of any or all of
the above-named signers shall constitute the signature of said

<a id="ga52-p247"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=249 printed_page=247 -->

signer, regardless of by whom or by what means the actual or
purported facsimile signature may have been affixed, if such
facsimile signature resembles the facsimile specimen(s) (if any)
filed with Bank by the secretary or other officer of this Company.

RESOLVED FURTHER, that the Bank is authorized to rely upon the
foregoing resolution until receipt by Bank of written notice of any change
or revocation.

**MSP**

**BD-6/24-2** That the Board of Directors receive notice of staff’s intent to pursue
opening a line of credit in the amount of $400,000, and that the Officers
Committee be granted authority to approve its specific terms.
**MSP**

**BD-6/24-3** That the Administrative Committee staff be authorized to apply for
commercial credit cards at Fifth Third Bank for staff with a combined total
credit limit of $111,000 for authorized ministry expenses.
**MSP**

**MSP** to adjourn

The meeting adjourned with prayer by Chairman Bob Brunson at 11:55 a.m.
Respectfully Submitted,

/signature on file/ /signature on file/
TE Robert Brunson, President TE Bryan Chapell, Secretary/Treasurer

Appendix C: CCB Report

**REPORT OF THE**

**COMMITTEE ON CONSTITUTIONAL BUSINESS**

_See 52-12 (p. 22) for the report to the Assembly_

**I. Introduction**

The Committee on Constitutional Business (CCB) met prior to the 52 [nd]
General Assembly at the PCA Administrative Offices in Lawrenceville,
GA on April 28, 2025.

Attendance at the meeting was as follows:

Teaching Elders Ruling Elders
Scott Phillips – Present Matthew Fender (Chairman) – Present
Jason Piland (Secretary) – Present Chris Shoemaker – Present
David Strain – Present Bryce Sullivan – Present
Stephen Tipton – Present John Ward Weiss – Absent

T. David Gordon (Alternate) – Present Joshua Torrey (Alternate) – Present

Also present were TE Bryan Chapell, the Stated Clerk of the General
Assembly, and TE Per Almquist, Communications and Polity Specialist
for the Stated Clerk’s office.

RE Joshua Torrey was seated as a voting member of the Committee for
the April 28 meeting.

**II. Advice on Proposed Changes to the Constitution**

The Stated Clerk referred the following Overtures to the Committee:

**A.** Overture 1 from Northern New England Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 32-2 to
Clarify that a Court May Investigate Whether Charges Should Be Filed”

<a id="ga52-p252"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=254 printed_page=252 -->

In the opinion of the CCB Overture 1 is **in conflict** with the Constitution in the
following ways:

- The proposed amendment creates a conflicting constitutional
threshold regarding the nature of an offense that is subject to judicial
process. Specifically, Overture 1 requires that “the alleged offense
rises to a level warranting judicial process,” but, according to _BCO_ 291, the “proper object of judicial process” is “anything in the doctrines
or practice of a Church member professing faith in Christ which is
contrary to the Word of God”;

- The proposed amendment creates a conflicting constitutional
threshold regarding the standard of proof required to commence
judicial process when charges are presented by a voluntary prosecutor.
Specifically, Overture 1 requires that there must be “a strong
presumption of guilt” (proposed _BCO_ 32-2.e.) and (assuming the list
is conjunctive) that “it is reasonable to expect the offense can be
proven through judicial process” ( _BCO_ 32-2.f.), but _BCO_ 31-9 only
requires “probable cause” for charges from a voluntary prosecutor;
and

- The proposed amendment removes agency from someone seeking to
become a voluntary prosecutor. Specifically, the logic of the Rules of
Discipline presumes that individuals have the right to bring charges
and prosecute them voluntarily (see, e.g., _BCO_ 31-6, -7, -9), but
Overture 1 places an absolute investigatory and high moral burden on
the court in every case where charges are laid before the court by an
individual.

Additionally, in the opinion of CCB, Overture 1 is **ambiguous** in that it is
unclear whether the items listed are conjunctive or disjunctive.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**B.** Overture 5 from Calvary Presbytery: “Grant _BCO_ 60–63 Full Constitutional
Status”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 5 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 7-1-0_

**C.** Overture 6 from Rocky Mountain Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 18-2 to Allow
Flexibility on Applications to Come Under Care”

<a id="ga52-p253"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=255 printed_page=253 -->

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 6 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**D.** Overture 7 from the Missouri Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 34-1 Regarding
Advisors and Original Jurisdiction in Process Against a TE”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 7 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 7-0-1_

**E.** Overture 8 from the Nashville Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 24-1 to Require
REs and Deacons to State Confessional Differences”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 8 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**F.** Overture 9 from the James River Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to Make
AC Members Appointed by Committees and Agencies Non-voting Advisory
Members”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 9 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**G.** Overture 10 from the Central Florida Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 22 to
Affirm the ‘Consent of the Governed’ Regarding Assistant Pastors”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 10 is **ambiguous** in that it does not address
how to treat men who currently serve as Assistant Pastors when the
amendment is adopted.
_Adopted 7-0-1_

**H.** Overture 12 from Providence Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 58-5 to Specify
Only Officers May Distribute the Lord’s Supper”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 12 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**I.** Overture 13 from the Great Lakes Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 25-1 to Specify
Only Members in Good Standing May Vote”

<a id="ga52-p254"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=256 printed_page=254 -->

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 13 is consistent with the language of
current _BCO_ 20-3 and 24-3, but it does not address a possible conflict between
those parts of the _BCO_ and _BCO_ 25-7 and 25-11.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**J.** Overture 14 from the Great Lakes Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 38-1 to
Indicate Ways a Court May Interact with an Offended Party”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 14 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**K.** Overture 15 from the Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley: “Amend _BCO_
36-4 to Add Language for the Administration of Definite Suspension from
Office”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 15 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**L.** Overture 16 from the Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley: “Amend _BCO_
36-5 to Conform the Language to _BCO_ 37-3 re Suspension from Office”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 16 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**M.** Overture 19 from the Eastern Carolina Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 57-5 to
Revise the Membership Vows”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 19 is **not in conflict** with the _BCO_ ;
however, CCB advises careful attention to the theological questions raised in
light of the _Westminster Standards_ .
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**N.** Overture 22 from the Pacific Northwest Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 20-3,
24-3 and 25-1 to clarify ‘Regular Standing’ re minimum voting age”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 22 is **in conflict** with Preliminary
Principle 6 and _BCO_ 6-4, in that PP6 affirms that “the power to elect persons
to the exercise of authority in any particular society resides in that society” and
_BCO_ 6-4 acknowledges that every communicant member is “entitled to all the

<a id="ga52-p255"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=257 printed_page=255 -->

rights and privileges of the church,” while the proposed _BCO_ 25-1 grants
congregations the right to remove a communicant member’s right and
privilege to vote.

CCB notes that adopting this change would create collateral consequences
regarding the quorums specified in _BCO_ 25-2, -3.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**O.** Overture 23 from the Pacific Northwest Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 41-3 to
Allow Supplemental Judges for a Session Trial”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 23 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 7-0-1_

**P.** Overture 24 from the Evangel Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 24-1 to Clarify a
Session’s Role in Examining Officer Nominees”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 24 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**Q.** Overture 25 from the Northwest Georgia Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 46-4
to Add a Class of Associate Membership for Honorably Retired TEs”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 25 is **in conflict** with _BCO_ 13-1 and 132 in that it applies permissive language to a Teaching Elder holding
membership in his Presbytery; further, it creates a constitutional conflict by
using mandatory language requiring all Honorably Retired Teaching Elders to
hold associate membership in a local congregation. To the extent the language
of the proposed amendment is deemed to apply to existing Honorably Retired
Teaching Elders, it appears to be in conflict with Preliminary Principle 2.
Finally, the Overture uses the phrase “communicant fellowship” which is a
category undefined by our Constitution.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**R.** Overture 29 from the Houston Metro Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 15-5.c to
Clarify the Handling of an SJC Minority Decision by GA”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 29 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

<a id="ga52-p256"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=258 printed_page=256 -->

**S.** Overture 30 from the Houston Metro Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 8-4, 20-1,
21-1, 21-12, and 23-1 re the Calling and Dissolution of TE Relationships for
Needful Work”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 30 is **in conflict** with our Constitution in
that it purports to give Presbyteries authority over the installation of Teaching
Elders to calls outside of churches of the Presbytery and the PCA as well as
the dissolution of such calls.

Additionally, in the opinion of the CCB, Overture 30 is **ambiguous** regarding
the relation between a “needful work” ( _BCO_ 8-4) and “a work not under the
jurisdiction of his Presbytery” ( _BCO_ 13-2; cf. _BCO_ 13-5), which is often called
an “out of bounds call.” it is unclear whether a “needful work” encompasses
all “out of bounds calls,” or whether there is still a category of “out of bounds
calls” that are different from “needful works.”

_Adopted 8-0-0_

**T.** Overture 31 from the Houston Metro Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 31-10 to
Require Vote of the Entire Court to Suspend an Officer’s Official Functions
during Process”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 31 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.

CCB notes that the word “administratively” is not otherwise constitutionally
defined and may therefore create an ambiguity.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**U.** Overture 32 from the Houston Metro Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 36 to
Provide for Elevation of a Censure without an Entirely New Trial”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 32 is **ambiguous** in that it is not clear
whether the “hearing” in the added paragraph is governed by other provisions
in the Rules of Discipline, such as _BCO_ 32-10, -13, -15, -16, -17, -18; and _BCO_
35, and whether the accused has the right to Appeal an elevated suspension.

CCB notes that the phrase “original trial court” is potentially ambiguous in
that it is not clear if the phrase refers to the exact commission or group of
presbyters who heard the evidence at the original trial; whether the phrase

<a id="ga52-p257"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=259 printed_page=257 -->

refers simply to the Session or Presbytery in which the trial was held; or
whether the phrase could include another court that has assumed jurisdiction
under _BCO_ 37-7.

CCB also notes that this overture is in conflict with Overture 38.

_Adopted 8-0-0_

**V.** Overture 33 from TE Fred Greco: “Amend _BCO_ 57-2 re the Examination
of Young Persons for Admission to the Sealing Ordinances”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 33 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**W.** Overture 34 from the Savannah River Presbytery: “Amend _RAO_ 16 to
Require Reporting on Session and Diaconate Membership and Duties”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 34 is **in conflict** with the Constitution in
that if a church does not have Deacons, _BCO_ 9-2 requires that the duties of the
office “shall devolve upon the ruling elders” of that church but the amendment
as presently written suggests that the duties of the diaconate may devolve upon
other persons or entities other than the ruling elders.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**X.** Overture 37 from the Southeast Alabama Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 12-3
so that a Session May Elect One of Its Members as Moderator in Judicial
Cases”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 37 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**Y.** Overture 38 from the Southeast Alabama Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 30-4
to Require a New Case with Process for Elevation of Indefinite Suspension to
Excommunication”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 38 is **in conflict** with the Constitution in
that _BCO_ 30-4 refers to the infliction of an initial censure of excommunication

after the judgment o fa court and i snot intended to offer a definition of
excommunication per se, therefore this overture is incompatible with those

<a id="ga52-p258"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=260 printed_page=258 -->

parts of the _BCO_ which require excommunication for acts not necessarily
categorized as “gross crime or heresy” such as _BCO_ 32-6, 33-2, -3, and 34-4.

CCB also notes that this overture is in conflict with Overture 32

_Adopted 8-0-0_

**Z.** Overture 39 from the Southeast Alabama Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 34-8
and add _BCO_ 33-5 to Clarify the Process for Elevating Suspension from Office
to Deposition”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 39 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 5-0-3_

**AA.** Overture 40 from the Southeast Alabama Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 359 to Require Recording All Parts of a Trial”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 40 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 7-0-1_

**BB.** Overture 43 from Calvary Presbytery: “Amend _RAO_ 4-11 to Specify the
Scope of Statistical Reporting by the Administrative Committee”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 43 is **not in conflict** with the Constitution.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**III. Advice to the Stated Clerk**

The CCB received two requests for advice from the Stated Clerk.

**A.** the c cb received a request for advice from the stated clerk ina
communication dated May 31, 2024:

[W]hether future _RAO_ changes originating from any source should be
required to have CCB review and Overtures Committee approval
before being voted upon by the General Assembly.

It was moved and carried to adopt the following advice to the Stated Clerk:

_Rules of Assembly Operations_ 8-2.b.3 requires that the Committee on
Constitutional Business “[p]rovide advice concerning the effect of any

<a id="ga52-p259"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=261 printed_page=259 -->

proposed amendment to the Constitution or to the _Rules of Assembly_
_Operations_ .” Therefore, all amendments to the _RAO_, no matter the
source, must be reviewed by CCB. However, no provision in our
Constitution or _RAO_ requires the Overtures Committee to approve
_RAO_ amendments that arise from CRPR or the SJC (cf. _RAO_ 15-1).
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**B.** the c cb received a request for advice from the stated clerk ina
communication dated April 25, 2025, and in person at the meeting:

Should overtures received on or by April 25 be officially handled for
this year’s General Assembly by the Stated Clerk’s office?

It was moved and carried to adopt the following advice to the Stated Clerk:

Overtures received on or by April 25 should be deemed timely for this
year’s General Assembly, having arrived within the 60-day deadline
according to _RAO_ 11-6.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**IV. Non-Judicial References**

The CCB received two non-judicial references.

**A.** On December 6, 2024, the Highlands Presbytery submitted the following
questions to the General Assembly for advice only ( _BCO_ 41-3) regarding the
Presbytery’s compliance with SJC Case No. 2023-16:

1. Does the SJC have the remit from the General Assembly to address
a question not raised by a complainant? ( _BCO_ 39-3.1)
2. Does the SJC have the remit from the General Assembly to rule in
any other way than to either: (a) uphold the complaint, (b) deny the
complaint, or (c) refer the case back to the Presbytery? ( _BCO_ 39-3.1,
43-10; _OMSJC_ 14.4.c)
3. Does the SJC’s ruling constitute an injunction contrary to the 50th
General Assembly’s approval of Highlands Presbytery’s minutes?
( _M50GA_, pp.564–565)
4. If the SJC gives a lower court an unlawful injunction (cf., _BCO_ 402), what options are available to the lower court in its response?

<a id="ga52-p260"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=262 printed_page=260 -->

The CCB declined to accede to the reference and provided the following

comment:

The decisions of the SJC are final decisions of the GA ( _BCO_ 15-5).
The CCB does not render opinions on previous SJC decisions.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**B.** On February 12, 2025, the Platte Valley Presbytery submitted the following
question to the General Assembly for advice only ( _BCO_ 41-3) regarding the
Presbytery’s review of Session records:

Must the Minutes of Congregational Meetings be submitted as part of
the Records of the Session that are to be reviewed by Presbytery?

The CCB acceded to the reference and adopted the following response:

Not only a court’s minutes but the entirety of its “records” are subject
to review by a higher court on an annual basis ( _BCO_ 40-1). A higher
court has discretion regarding what records it requires from the lower
court in order for the higher court to fulfill its responsibilities under
_BCO_ 40. At a minimum, the higher court has the authority to require
a lower court to submit for review any document that the Constitution
requires the lower court to keep. While the _BCO_ does not expressly
require Sessions to keep the minutes of congregational meetings, it is
clear by implication that such records must be maintained in order to
demonstrate whether such substantial matters as the election of

officers have been properly conducted. Sessions are required to
receive congregational meeting minutes ( _BCO_ 25-5). Therefore, if a
Presbytery requires congregational meeting minutes as part of its
review of Session records, then Sessions in that Presbytery are
required to submit congregational meeting minutes to the Presbytery.
_See_ _Michelson v. Northwest Georgia Presbytery_, SJC Case No. 202112, _M49GA_ (2022), 822–825, 823.
_Adopted 8-0-0_

**V. Advice to the Overtures Committee**

The CCB received no requests for advice from the Overtures Committee.

<a id="ga52-p261"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=263 printed_page=261 -->

**VI. Minutes of the Standing Judicial Commission**

The CCB examined the minutes of the Standing Judicial Commission
meetings on July 22, 2024; October 17, 2024; February 19, 2025; and
March 6, 2025, and no exceptions were found.

**VII. Proposed Amendment to the** _**Rules of Assembly Operations**_

The CCB received a proposed amendment to the _RAO_ being recommended
to the 52 [nd] General Assembly from the Standing Judicial Commission.

In the opinion of the CCB, the SJC’s proposed amendment to _RAO_ 17-3 is **not**
**in conflict** with the Constitution.

_Adopted 8-0-0_

Chairman – TE Jason Piland

Secretary – RE Chris Shoemaker

Supplemental Report

**SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF THE**

**COMMITTEE ON CONSTITUTIONAL BUSINESS**

**I. Introduction**

The Committee on Constitutional Business (CCB) met prior to the 52 [nd]
General Assembly on Wednesday, June 4, 2025, at 2:00 p.m. via Microsoft
Teams.

Attendance at the meeting was as follows:

Teaching Elders Ruling Elders
Scott Phillips – Present Matthew Fender (Chairman) – Present
Jason Piland (Secretary) – Present Chris Shoemaker – Present
David Strain – Present Bryce Sullivan – Present
Stephen Tipton – Present John Ward Weiss – Present

T. David Gordon (Alternate) – Absent Joshua Torrey (Alternate) – Present

**II. Advice to the Stated Clerk**

the c cb received a request for advice from the stated clerk ina
communication dated June 2, 2025, on behalf of the Administrative
Committee:

1. If the AC didn’t make a nomination for Stated Clerk, could the AC
CoC make a substitute motion along the lines of, “That Mr. X be
nominated for Stated Clerk.” I.e., can they make a substitute fora
motion that (in our mind) never existed?

2. Further if the AC didn’t make a nomination for Stated Clerk, would
“additional nominations” from the floor ( _RAO_ 3-4) be allowed?

The CCB adopted the following responses to the requests from the Stated
Clerk:

1. In the opinion of the CCB, no. _RAO_ 14-6.d., h. give a Committee of
Commissioners power only to Amend a recommendation presented to

<a id="ga52-p263"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=265 printed_page=263 -->

it, with a substitute recommendation being a form of motion to amend
( _see_ RONR [12th ed.] 12:8). A Committee of Commissioners may not
take up new business itself, except a recommendation to commend the
committee or staff ( _RAO_ 14-6.k.). This precludes the Committee of
Commissioners for the Administrative Committee from itself

recommending the nomination o fa stated clerk if the matter o fa
Stated Clerk does not arise in recommendations of the Administrative

Committee.

_Adopted 8-0-0_

2. In the opinion of the CCB, yes. The _RAO_ does not expressly
contemplate a set of facts where the Administrative Committee does
not bring a nominee for the office of Stated Clerk. _RAO_ 3-4 states:
“The Administrative Committee of General Assembly has the
_responsibility_ of nominating to the General Assembly the person to
fill the office of the Stated Clerk” (emphasis added). Further, _RAO_ 49 states: “The Administrative Committee _shall_ nominate annually a
Stated Clerk for election by the General Assembly” (emphasis added).
_RAO_ 3-4 and _RAO_ 4-9 require the Administrative Committee to bring
a nominee. The second sentence of _RAO_ 3-4 simply assumes the
Committee is bringing a nominee and provides an additional method
for presentation of nominees.

In the event that the Administrative Committee declines to fulfill its

duty under _RAO_ 4-9, the second sentence of _RAO_ 3-4 should be
interpreted as acknowledging the General Assembly’s right to select a
Stated Clerk of its own choosing, regardless of the nominee (or lack
of nominee) put forward by the Administrative Committee. A contrary
reading would result in the Assembly having no mechanism under the
_Rules_ (without suspension thereof) to elect a Stated Clerk when the
Administrative Committee does not proffer a nominee. The phrase
“additional nominations” has been read in an analogous way regarding
floor nominations when the Nominating Committee does not proffer
a nominee for any position ( _RAO_ 8-4.i.).

Further, absent a recommendation from the Administrative
Committee, the election of a Stated Clerk will not come before the
Assembly in the ordinary course, and the election may only be taken
up as new business under _RAO_ 13.

<a id="ga52-p264"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=266 printed_page=264 -->

Finally, no nominee can be placed before the General Assembly unless
and until he has been approved by the Theological Examining
Committee ( _BCO_ 14-1.14). We observe that this constitutional
requirement is mandatory and cannot be suspended.

_Adopted 8-0-0_

Submitted by:
RE Matt Fender, Chairman TE Jason Piland, Secretary

<a id="ga52-p265"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=267 printed_page=265 -->
