Appendix D: CDM Report

**REPORT OF THE**

**COMMITTEE ON DISICPLESHIP MINISTRIES**

_See 52-22 (p. 31) for the report of the Committee of Commissioners_

_and the Assembly’s action_

In Luke’s version of the Great Commission, we read, “But you will receive
power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you will be my witnesses
in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth.” (Acts
1:8 ESV ). The same Spirit fuels believers today as we make disciples by
proclaiming “the glorious deeds of the L ORD ” (Ps. 78:4).

The mission of the Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM) is to help
leaders in the PCA make disciples in the local church by connecting them to
the people and resources they need. CDM's motto is "making disciples by
connecting people to people and people to resources." Below is a brief report
on the ways CDM staff members and ministry partners have been stewards of
the opportunities God has provided over the last year.

**Connecting People to People – National Training Conferences**

To serve the PCA, CDM provides opportunities for leaders in discipleship
ministry to come together for training and encouragement. Since last summer,
attendance at all events exceeded our expectations, as participants come from
churches of all sizes around the denomination. In August 2024, CDM again
offered the GROW virtual training conference, specifically designed to help
children’s ministry (CM) directors in smaller churches provide training for
volunteers. On the launch weekend, we had individuals and churches from 25
states and 4 countries registered.

Our CM in-person LEAD retreat in April 2025 at Ridge Haven reached fullcapacity with 160 leaders. The “Power Up” preconference also reached
capacity as we focused on equipping churches to incorporate children in the
worship service. Randall Goodgame, who wrote a new song for the CDM
_Exploring the Elements of Worship_ curriculum, provided great insight and

<a id="ga52-p268"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=270 printed_page=268 -->

helped attendees consider how to use the new series of songs in the _Scripture_
_Hymnal_ (Rabbit Room Press, 2024). In response to the growth of the annual
CM leadership gathering at Ridge Haven, the CM Team will add another event
to meet in Indianapolis in early November 2026.

The PCA Nextgen Conference had a great second year as we met in October
2024 at chapel gate pc in Baltimore. The group of 60+ leaders were
encouraged and equipped by Rev. Thurman Williams and a slate of workshops
designed by the CDM Student Ministry (SM) Team. Chapelgate agreed to host
again, and plans are underway for the October 2025 event.

CDM hosted another large group for the February 2025 Women's Ministry
Leadership Training (LT) in Atlanta, GA. Over 450 women from around the
world gathered to consider how God’s story of redemption shapes women’s
ministry in the local church. One attendee remarked, “I’ve been to 5 LTs, and
this was the best so far. I really liked the cohesion between the keynotes and
the workshops.” As in the past, many attendees commented on how this event
enables them to connect with and learn from others around the PCA.

**Connecting People to People** – **"Made for More" Regional Conferences**

In September 2024 CDM launched a series of 8 regional gatherings designed
for women from age 9 to 99+. These conferences were a partnership between
CDM WM and CM (including Teach Us to Worship), a local PCA church,
Harvest USA, and Created for a Purpose. The Friday-Saturday format
provided an opportunity for multi-generational connection around the topics
of creational design and gospel identity. The response and feedback have been
overwhelmingly positive. Almost 4000 women from over 500 churches
participated. Of the total, approximately 1500 were younger than 30 yrs. old.

**Location** **Date** **Attendance** **# Churches** **# < 30 yrs.**
Birmingham, AL Sep. 13-14, 2024 789 91 344
San Diego, CA Sep. 27-28, 2024 241 37 94
Colorado Springs, Oct. 18-19, 2024 242 40 87
CO

Naples, FL Nov. 8-9, 2024 519 64 188
Annapolis, MD Nov. 15-16, 2024 680 91 219
Charlotte, NC Jan. 31- Feb.1, 2025 736 98 268
Dallas, TX Apr. 4-5, 2025 360 52+ 140+
Chicago, IL May 2-3, 2025 300+ 47+ 130+
**TOTALS** **3867+** **520+** **1470+**

<a id="ga52-p269"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=271 printed_page=269 -->

**Connecting People to People** – **Leadership Development**

In addition to the large group gatherings, CDM also provides opportunities for
leaders to develop in smaller groups with more focused training. The **CDM**
**Certification** program continues to serve churches well. The permanent
committee certified 30 students in the 2024 CM class. Currently, more than
200 people have completed this program, including a few from churches
outside the United States. A small initial group of SM certification students
completed the program and were also certified (see Attachment 1). Both the
CM and SM Teams are working to improve and expand these programs based
on participant feedback.

For WM leaders, CDM partnered with Village 7 PC in Colorado Springs, CO,
for the W4 (Women Working with Women) gathering for part-time and fulltime WM staff members. Ms. Karen Hodge, CDM WM Coordinator,
continued offering a cohort-based mentoring program for younger women to
prepare a new generation of WM leaders (ages 20-40).

Over the past year, the Wives of Elders (WE) Ministry and Women’s Ministry
International (WMI) have seen more growth. The WE Ministry is the recipient
of the 2025 Love Gift Legacy as Ms. Meaghan May seeks to connect over
2000 women who have expressed interest in the program. Ms. Connie Miller
has formed a team to help her respond well to the expanding opportunities for
WMI. CDM is partnering with Ms. Kathy Clow of MTW to equip PCA
missionaries. Also, in coordination with MTW, WMI is working with PCA
congregations in the U. S. who want to serve as WM mentors for churches
around the world, particularly in Africa and Europe.

**Connecting People to Resources – Blogs and Podcasts**

The CDM websites provide an avenue to the experience of ministry team
members and ministry partners. Blog posts for CM and WM provide practical
help and ministry encouragement. The WM enCourage blog has 22 regular
authors providing weekly posts. IN 2024, the blog had over 365,000 readers
with approximately 31,000 downloads per month.

The WM enCourage podcast continues to expand. The listener base has grown
to almost 3,000 per week. In addition to offering content from CDM Bible
studies and conference workshops, the podcasts also explore practical issues

<a id="ga52-p270"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=272 printed_page=270 -->

WM leaders face every day. The PCA Nextgen podcast launched a new live
podcast every Friday at 11:00 a.m. Eastern on the CDM YouTube channel
(“Encourage and Equip Christian Leaders”). Three members of our SM Team
invite guests to join them for a discussion about common issues in Nextgen
ministry and how leaders address those issues to create a Word-based,
relationally-driven discipleship culture.

**Connecting People to Resources – Teach Us to Worship**

The CDM Teach Us to Worship (TUTW) ministry initiative entered the second
year of the five-year grant from the Lilly Endowment, Inc. In January 2025,
the TUTW staff gathered 40+ leaders to discuss a template to help PCA
churches develop a philosophy of children in worship. The group was an
incredible variety of pastors, ruling elders, and lay leaders. Though they came
with different convictions about worship style, the resulting template
(available at **teachustoworship.org** ) was an amazing expression of our
common commitment to covenant children. The TUTW Team is also

beginning to offer the new _Exploring the Elements of Worship_ curriculum that
has replaced the _Teach Me to Worship_ curriculum (still available for pdf
download at the PCA Bookstore).

**Connecting People to Resources – ESL Curriculum**

By September 2025, CDM will offer all 5 levels of the _Neighbor to Neighbor—_
_Uniting through English_ curriculum developed in a partnership with MNAESL. This curriculum was written by PCA leaders from MNA especially for
PCA churches; therefore, it aligns with our biblical convictions. Each level
offers a teacher book and student books at affordable prices. In 2024,
thousands of copies of the Introductory and Beginner Levels (1-2) were used.
Now with the Intermediate Levels (3-4) complete, churches will be able to
have a unified curriculum for their ESL program.

**Connecting People to Resources – PCA Bookstore and Reachout**
**Adventures**

The PCA Bookstore ( **pcabookstore.com** ) provides a wide variety of
resources. Books offered on the website are recommended by PCA leaders as
being useful not only to PCA members but also those in the broader
evangelical church. Consequently, not all resources would conform fully to the

<a id="ga52-p271"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=273 printed_page=271 -->

confessional standards of the PCA, but each item is considered for its potential
value to PCA members. Some customers have expressed gratitude for a place
where they can focus their search for resources and escape the somewhat
overwhelming results from queries on the Internet. Additionally, through the
PCA Bookstore, CDM passes along the discounts we receive to provide
churches with greater discounts, especially for group orders (5 or more books).

**Recent CDM Resources and Publications**

_Made for More Study_ conference team topical Bible study on gospel identity
_Mission Possible_ Mariah Cunningham 12 lessons on missions for children
_Daughters of the Covenant_ Susan Hunt, _et al_ . covenant theology and identity
_Colossians & Philemon_ Sarah Ivill 8 lesson Bible study
_Road Talk_ (vol. 3) John Kwasny family devotional guides for the car
_Meal Talk_ (vol. 2) John Kwasny family devotional guide for mealtimes
_Neighbor to Neighbor_ MNA ESL ESL curriculum for PCA churches
_God’s Plan for Development_ Zoe Jones 8 lessons on development for families
_Nurtured_ Annalisa Smith 9 topical lessons for new mothers

This year CDM continued to update the Reachout Adventures curriculum,
used primarily for VBS programs ( **reachoutadventures.com** ). The four
rotating themes provide a unique and engaging way to study Joshua, Luke,
Genesis, and Jonah. The 2025 theme “Upward Bound" was redesigned
according to feedback from actual and potential users. Feedback continues to
be positive regarding the _a la carte_, portal-based format. The resources are
primarily digital, more flexible, and easier to use. After all four themes are
revised, CDM staff will continue to work with children’s ministry leaders to
discover ways to better serve the current programs of local churches.

In the CDM budget, the PCA Bookstore and Reachout Adventures are treated
as separate cost centers designed to operate on at least a “break-even” basis
(i.e., the cost of inventory, staff salaries, floor space, storage, postage, etc., is
covered by sales revenue). Sales of the 2024 Reachout _Olympion_ theme
tracked slightly lower than the previous year but essentially broke-even after
the costs of updating the curriculum. Bookstore sales continued strong. In
2024, the PCA Bookstore covered expenses and contributed over $50,000 to
the overall CDM ministry.

**CDM Response to the 51** **[st]** **General Assembly (2024)**

In its response to Overture 33, the 51 [st] General Assembly instructed CDM to
provide a brief three-part report to the 52 [nd] General Assembly (2025) on

<a id="ga52-p272"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=274 printed_page=272 -->

CDM’s relationship with the _Jesus Calling_ book. The CDM members
discussed the content of Overture 33, the GA tasking, and an appropriate
response at the September 2024 and March 2025 stated meetings. CDM
members and staff are preparing a final version of the response fora called
virtual meeting on May 20, 2025. At that time, the members will approve the
CDM response for inclusion in an on-site supplement to the 52 [nd] General
Assembly Commissioner Handbook.

**Financial Review**

The ministry of CDM is funded through a combination of revenue sources:

1. giving from churches ( _BCO_ 14-1.4),
2. giving from individuals,
3. revenue from the sale of resources,
4. revenue from conference fees, and
5. various reimbursements for ministry expenses.

CDM finished 2024 with a net increase of $180,000. Also, the investment
accounts are approximately $2.6 million due to the funds received from the
Lilly Endowment, Inc., and the transfer of floor space to Geneva Benefits.
Many of these funds are already committed to pay expenses for the TUTW
ministry and offset the increased rental rate approved by the Administrative
Committee. Unrestricted contributions from churches increased by $25,397
(5.3%) and from individuals by $6,093 (8.5%). The members of the permanent
committee, including those who are “hard to kidnap,” celebrate the
overwhelming support of the committee members and staff in the financial
needs of the CDM ministry. We are grateful for the variety of ways God has
provided.

Since the beginning of the denomination, CDM’s primary source of
unrestricted income has been support from PCA congregations (BCO 14-1).
However, in 2024 only **456** ( **28%** ) PCA particular churches contribute
financially to the ministry of CDM. The staff and committee members are
grateful for those churches who partner with CDM, but the large number of
PCA churches who choose not to financially support the ministry is
discouraging. Because of the limited support from PCA congregations, CDM
has been hindered in providing a full range of resources for churches,
especially in ministry to youth, men, and older adults.

<a id="ga52-p273"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=275 printed_page=273 -->

**Conclusion to the CDM Report**

The Committee on Discipleship Ministries strengthens the church by
connecting leaders to the people and resources they need for discipleship
ministry. We are thankful for the opportunity to help PCA churches develop
discipleship ministries that are Word-based and relationally-driven as we
strive to tell the glorious deeds of the L ORD to the ends of the earth.

**R** **ECOMMENDATIONS**

1. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the meetings of the

Permanent Committee for the Committee on Discipleship Ministries on
September 12-13, 2024, and March 13-14, 2025 without exception.

2. That the General Assembly encourage churches and individuals to

contribute generously to the “ **Love Gift Legacy** ” ( **pcacdm.org/wm-love-**
**gift** ). For 2024, CDM used the funds to develop and promote resources
and training for CDM's Women's Ministry International (WMI), which
includes partnerships with MTW and ministries such as the UK
Partnership and City to City Latin America. For 2025, CDM will join with
Geneva Benefits and use the funds to expand the “Wives of Elders” (WE)
ministry. The expansion will focus on providing counseling, training, and
encouragement to wives of elders in their unique role in the life of the
church.

3. That the General Assembly encourage individuals, local churches, and

Presbyteries to utilize the many free resources available on the CDM
websites ( **pcacdm.org** [especially resources for leaders];
**children.pcacdm.org** [children]; **women.pcacdm.org** [women];
**pcanextgen.org** [youth]; **pcacdm.org/ministries/older-adult-ministry**

[older adults]; **pcabookstore.com** ). These resources are developed by
PCA leaders for PCA leaders.

4. That the General Assembly encourage local churches to consider and use

**Reachout Adventures** from CDM for Summer programming
( **reachoutadventures.com** ). CDM is working with PCA leaders to revise
this reformed and covenantal curriculum. The 2025 theme is _Upward_
_Bound_, which considers the journey of faith as presented in the Gospel of
Luke. The 2026 theme will be _Space Mission_, which explores the good
news found in Genesis.

<a id="ga52-p274"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=276 printed_page=274 -->

5. That the General Assembly encourage individuals and local churches to

use the excellent resources for all ages from Great Commission
Publications ( **www.gcp.org** ), our official curriculum publisher. _Show Me_
_Jesus_ is Bible-based, Christ-centered curriculum that teaches the
unfolding story of redemption from Genesis to Revelation and builds on
God’s truth in age-appropriate ways. In fall 2025, GCP launches a new
Preschool curriculum for 4- and 5-year-olds is designed to:

Spark Wonder: Creatively teaching Bible stories to discover our BIG
God.

Teach Heart to Heart: Discipling our kids in relationship—together
growing in the grace and knowledge of Christ.
Discover Jesus: Glorifying God forever as we discover the unfolding
story of salvation through Jesus.
This new _Show Me Jesus_ Preschool nurtures little hearts to build lifelong
faith in Christ.

6. That the General Assembly give thanks to TE Tom Harr, Ms. Sherry

Kendrick (advisory), RE Lightsey Wallace, and RE Jeremy Whitley for
their faithful service as members of the Permanent Committee.

7. That the General Assembly re-elect TE Stephen Estock to serve as the

Coordinator for the Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM).

8. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly

answer **Overture 9** from James River Presbytery “Amend _BCO 14-1_ to
Make AC Members Appointed by Committees and Agencies Non-voting
Advisory Members" in the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

CDM believes the overture is unnecessary and an unhelpful
change to the constitution. The rationale for the change (as
indicated in the “whereas” clauses) fails to adequately
acknowledge the “grass roots” nature of the current voting
membership of the AC. The CDM representative on the AC is
elected by the permanent committee from those who are elected
by the General Assembly through the BCO 14 nomination and
election process. Ordinarily, the AC representative is one of the
CDM officers (elected by the committee) since those men are
most familiar with the breadth of CDM’s ministry. Moreover, the
proposed change disenfranchises the representatives of the
committees and agencies from voting on issues that directly affect
the ministry of the committee or agency (e.g., budgets and the

<a id="ga52-p275"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=277 printed_page=275 -->

docket of the General Assembly), as well as participation on the
PCA Board of Directors. The current structure dates back to the
16 [th] General Assembly (1988) and the work of an ad-interim
committee chaired by RE W. Jack Williamson. He, along with
other forefathers of the denomination, shaped the “grass roots”
system mentioned in the Overture. Apart from a specific reason
that necessitates a change, it is unwise to depart from the “grass
roots” system our PCA forefathers delivered to us.

9. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly

answer **Overture 11** from South Texas Presbytery “To Study
Incorporating Bitcoin into Session, Presbytery, and Committee & Agency
Balance Sheets" in the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

The Overture is unnecessary and unwise. It is historically
unprecedented to direct the collective attention of the Church to a
specific investment—not just an investment category
(cryptocurrency) but a specific product or brand (Bitcoin)—to be
included in a well-balanced portfolio. Moreover, the U. S.
Department of Labor determined in 2022 that cryptocurrency
investments fail to satisfy the “prudent investor” rule, which is the
standard used for fiduciary boards in the United States. Finally,
the study called for by the Overture could distract the Church from
its true mission to make disciples through the proclamation of the
gospel.

10. That the General Assembly approve the 2026 CDM budget as presented

by the Administrative Committee.

<a id="ga52-p276"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=278 printed_page=276 -->

GCP Report

Attachment 2

**R** **EPORT FROM THE** **P** **UBLISHING** **M** **INISTRY OF**

**G** **REAT** **C** **OMMISSION** **P** **UBLICATIONS** **(GCP)**

**Great Commission Publications** ( **GCP** ) is the official publishing ministry of
the PCA and the Orthodox Presbyterian Church (OPC). GCP publishes highquality Reformed and covenantal curriculum for all ages. These include Bible
curriculum, catechetical curriculum and worship resources. Everything GCP
publishes is based solely upon the Bible as the Word of God and conforms to
the Westminster Standards.

gc pis governed by a twelve-member board of trustees comprised of teaching
and ruling elders. Six are elected by the PCA CDM and six by the OPC
Committee on Christian Education (CCE). Additionally, the PCA CDM
Coordinator and the OPC CCE General Secretary serve as ex-officio, nonvoting trustees. The president of the board of trustees for 2024 was PCA
teaching elder David Stewart and the president for 2025 is OPC teaching elder
Dr. Alan Strange. (This position alternates annually between the PCA and
OPC.)

This year marks a milestone in the life of GCP as we celebrate the 50 [th]
anniversary of this joint venture between the OPC and PCA. When the PCA
formed in 1973, it was without a suitable curriculum for the training and
instruction of its growing number of children and young people. While, on the
other hand, the OPC had invested significant resources over the years in
creating a bible curriculum and was looking fora partner to enhance its
viability. So, in God’s good providence, the leadership of both denominations
joined hands in 1975 and entered a partnership that has been integral to
discipling our covenant children for [now] half a century.

Today, by God’s grace, GCP’s reach extends beyond the two parent
denominations. Approximately 40% of sales are made to other denominations
and churches that value a curriculum that faithfully teaches the Bible and
consistently presents Christ throughout. GCP leads with its five distinctives
which set it apart as a trusted resource.

<a id="ga52-p279"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=281 printed_page=279 -->

**Our Sovereign God** . Our focus is on God as our Sovereign Creator,
Redeemer and King. He is the author of the Bible and its main subject
and character. Our purpose is to glorify and enjoy him forever.

**Show Me Jesus** . All of Scripture is the unfolding story of salvation
and Jesus is its focus. We show how Jesus, revealed throughout the
Bible, came to seek and save the lost.

**God’s Grace in the Covenant** . From Genesis to Revelation, God’s
covenant is the thread that ties the Bible together. We build on that
truth to teach students of all ages the consistent faithfulness of God.

**Redemptive-Historical Approach** . This Reformed approach to the
Bible is essential to understanding God’s Word as the unified counsel
of God. Therefore, we interpret each portion of God’s Word in context
of the whole.

**Church-Family Connection** Church and home: working together to
point covenant children to Christ. We believe parents are key in the
spiritual training of their children. We provide resources for families
to reinforce the solid teaching children receive at church.

_Show Me Jesus_ is GCP’s foundational curriculum. This Bible-based

curriculum teaches God’s story of redemption, showing how Jesus is the
fulfillment of his covenant promises. it is taught i nan age-appropriate manner
through four departments: Toddler (2- to 3-year-olds), Preschool (4- to 5-yearolds), Younger Elementary (1st and 2nd grades), and Middle Elementary (3rd
and 4th grades).

GCP is pleased in this anniversary year to introduce the newly revised _Show_
_Me Jesus_ Preschool curriculum. It maintains the same treasured stories and

lessons but is now based on the English Standard Version (ESV) of the Bible.
It also contains new biblical artwork, additional resources to assist parents in
connecting with the Sunday School, and various digital downloads and
resources for use by teachers.

_G2R (Genesis to Revelation) Bible Survey_ for preteens is an exciting journey
through every book of the Bible, helping students integrate its stories through

<a id="ga52-p280"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=282 printed_page=280 -->

a historical-redemptive study of Scripture. _G2R God’s Promises_ (grades 7–9)
zeroes in on God’s unfolding covenant promises.

_So What?_ youth Bible studies offer 10 study series focusing on books of the
Bible (John, Romans, Philippians) and topics such as a biblical worldview, the
character of God, the Bible, the church, worship, and more.

_Kids’ Quest Beginning_ and _Elementary_ curricula teach _First Catechism_ —basic
Bible truths in a Q/A format. A free Summer Download Kit with planning
guides, social media, t-shirt logos, and more is available to serve churches.
_First Catechism_ is also available in two parallel language editions: EnglishKorean and English-Spanish—excellent for our churches in bilingual contexts
and outreach.

_Digging Deeper,_ teaches the Shorter Catechism from a format designed
primarily for youth, but also ideal for discipling adults who are new to the
Christian faith and/or Reformed doctrine. This two-volume series is ideal for

anyone who wants to go deeper in the Reformed faith through such crucial
biblical truths as what to believe about God and how to live out the duties God

requires.

_Pilgrim’s Progress_ teaches the gospel to children using the classic adventure
story. The curriculum includes a fully illustrated chapter hardback book,
audiobook with character voices and sound effects, music, teacher manual, and
student activities for elementary ages.

GCP also publishes the _Trinity Hymnal,_ the treasured hymnal that enriches
worship with its collection of over 700 hymns and psalms. It includes the
Westminster Confession of Faith and the Shorter Catechism, as well as the
Apostles’ and Nicene Creeds. Orchestrations are available for all of the hymns
and new digital editions are now available for download. GCP also serves as
the exclusive distributor of the _Trinity Psalter Hymnal,_ a joint publication of
the OPC and the United Reformed Churches of North America (URCNA).

For samples of any materials or to place an order, visit **[www.gcp.org](http://www.gcp.org/)** . You also
may call 800-695-3387 to speak to a curriculum specialist.

<a id="ga52-p281"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=283 printed_page=281 -->

Supplemental Report of CDM

**SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF THE**

**COMMITTEE ON DISCIPLESHIP MINISTRIES**

**CDM RESPONSE TO THE 51** **[ST]** **GENERAL**

**ASSEMBLY**

**REGARDING THE** _**JESUS CALLING**_ **BOOK**

In its response to Overture 33 from TE Benjamin Inman, the 51 [st] General
Assembly requested a brief report from CDM to the 52 [nd] General Assembly
on the _Jesus Calling_ book ( _M51GA_, 2024, pp. 96-98). The permanent
committee discussed a draft response at the Fall 2024 and Spring 2025 stated
meetings. At a called meeting on May 20, 2025, the permanent committee
approved a three-part response to address the three issues highlighted by the
Assembly.

**Part 1**

_Report to "examine the history of the CDM's relationship with the book and_
_outline its reasons for withdrawing the book from its inventory previously and_
_not offering it for sale since."_

Integrity Publishing first published _Jesus Calling_ by Sarah Young in 2004.
Thomas Nelson Publishing acquired Integrity Publishers in 2006. In 2012,
HarperCollins acquired Thomas Nelson. Consequently, various editions of the
_Jesus Calling_ book(s) may have different publishing information.

In 2014, Coordinator TE Estock received complaints about _Jesus Calling_ and,
for pastoral reasons, directed the PCA Bookstore staff to remove the title from
the website. The Bookstore no longer sells _Jesus Calling_ . CDM staff
conducted a thorough search of available records and discovered the following
facts:

- There are no references to _Jesus Calling_ in the CEP/CDM [1] Minutes.

1 At the 42 nd General Assembly in 2014, the Assembly changed the name of
the permanent committee from “Committee on Christian Education and

<a id="ga52-p282"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=284 printed_page=282 -->

- In 2013, there was a chart comparing bestselling Christian titles from
various outlets with those sold by the CEP Bookstore ( _M41GA_, 2013,
p. 227). _Jesus Calling_ and _Jesus Today_ were referenced as top sellers
according to the Association for Christian Retail (CBA).

- The PCA Bookstore sold 202 copies of _Jesus Calling_ and its
associated _Journal_ between 2011-2014.

**Part 2**

_Report to "assess the book's appropriateness for Christians in general and_
_PCA members and congregations in particular with special regard for its_
_doctrine and method."_

The Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM) is a program committee of
the Assembly tasked to conduct the affairs of the church in the areas of
Christian education and publications ( _RAO_ 4-1, 2; 6-1). As a committee, CDM
does not direct any Church judicatory ( _BCO_ 14-1.7) or seek to
replace/undermine the pastoral responsibility of local church elders to their
members. CDM staff members may occasionally respond to questions related
to resources based on information from pastors, ministry leaders, and PCA
members, and their own personal perspectives. To serve the whole of the PCA
well, neither CDM nor its staff recommend _Jesus Calling_ .

**Part 3**

_Report to "provide recommendations (if needed) for remedial materials,_
_advisory statements, or General Assembly actions concerning_ Jesus Calling _."_

Since the devotional life and spiritual maturity of each person are unique, local
church elders are best suited to evaluate the spiritual needs and maturity of
those considering the book. Elders should converse with members and
recommend alternative or remedial devotional materials as needed. CDM staff

members and ministry partners stand ready to help leaders connect members
to Word-based devotional resources.

Publications” (CEP) to “Committee on Discipleship Ministries” (CDM).
Hereafter, “CDM” refers to the permanent committee under either name.
Similarly, c. 2014 the CDM staff changed the name of the bookstore affiliated
with CDM from “CEP Bookstore” to “The PCA Bookstore.” Hereafter, “PCA
Bookstore” refers to both.

<a id="ga52-p283"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=285 printed_page=283 -->

**Additional Recommendations for the 52** **[nd]** **General Assembly**

At the May 20, 2025, called meeting, the permanent committee approved the
following recommendations for the Assembly:

11. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly

answer **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery “Erect Ad
Interim Committee to Receive Suggestions for Permanent Committee and
Agency Operations, Resources, and Expenses" in the **NEGATIVE.**

Grounds:

The Overture is unnecessary. The annual budgets of every
Committee and Agency are reviewed thoroughly and audited
annually ( _RAO_ 14-7h). Members of the permanent committee are
elected by the Assembly to exercise primary fiduciary
responsibility for their committee ( _RAO_ 4-11). The budget they
approve is submitted to the Administrative Committee, where it is
examined by a subcommittee and sent to the Assembly by the full
committee. At the Assembly, the Committee/Agency budget is
reviewed by the Committee of Commissioners for the
Committee/Agency, as well as the Committee of Commissioners
for the Administrative Committee, before being sent to the
Assembly for approval. All of the budgets are included in the
initial Assembly Commissioner Handbook, thus providing every
commissioner an opportunity to review and offer questions to the
appropriate Committee of Commissioners. In the absence of
evidence that 1) the current procedures to provide transparency
and accountability are inadequate, or 2) the recommendations of
an ad interim committee would result in greater support for the
Committee and Agencies, the ad interim committee described by
the Overture would not be a wise use of funds.

12. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly

answer **Overture 30** from Houston Metro Presbytery “Amend _BCO_ 8-4,
20-1, 21-1, 21-12, and 23-1 re the Calling and Dissolution of TE
Relationships for Needful Works" by **REFERRING** it back to Houston
Metro Presbytery or to the 53 [rd] General Assembly in 2026.

Grounds

<a id="ga52-p284"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=286 printed_page=284 -->

The Overture calls for changes to the wording of many paragraphs
in the _BCO_, including the ordination vows of elders. As the
Administrative Committee noted, “[t]his Overture potentially
affects the legal employment conditions and relationships of TE
personnel in all Committee and Agencies, as well as the
relationship of such men to their Presbyteries—and the
relationship of those Presbyteries and the General Assembly to
civil authorities.” The potential impact to the operations of the
Committees and Agencies demand more research and advice from
legal counsel to possibly refine changes before sending them to
the Presbyteries for advice and consent.

13. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly

answer **Overture 49** from Pacific Northwest Presbytery “Erect an Ad
Interim Committee on AI" by **DEFERRING** action on this Overture to
the 53 [rd] General Assembly in 2026.

Grounds

The use of AI technology is growing exponentially. As with any
emerging trend, elders would benefit from careful reflection on
the theological and moral issues involved, including unintended
consequences. Several excellent books, papers, and even classes
and policies are already available to help members understand this
technology. More preparation would be helpful before forming an
Ad Interim Committee.

<a id="ga52-p285"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=287 printed_page=285 -->

**REPORT OF THE**

**COOPERATIVE MINISTRIES COMMITTEE**

_See 52-16 (p. 23) for the report to the Assembly_

The Cooperative Ministries Committee (CMC) is designed to unite the
leaders of our various agencies and institutions with the elected leaders
of the General Assembly in coordinated ministry to advance the mission
oft hep ca as a whole. The simple goal is to have all the horses in the
harness pulling in the same direction. That goal is achieved by relational
camaraderie as well as by ministry strategizing, denominational
assessment, and shared insights regarding best practices and resources
for advancing Christ’s mission in our challenging times.

The Cooperative Ministries Committee met on January 22, 2025.

The committee rejoices to report that many examples of interagency
cooperation, communication, and collaboration were shared ( _RAO_ 7-3).

The CMC advised the Administrative Committee to only consider
scheduling an Assembly-wide seminar when needed and that Assembly
business begin at 9:00 am Wednesday morning during the General
Assemblies where Assembly-wide seminars do not take place.

The Stated Clerk’s office is continuing to work on creating a DASA
manual to help churches deal with abuse cases. Stated Clerk Bryan
Chapell gave an update on extracted materials from the DASA Report.

The committee discussed proposed Overtures to the 52 [nd] General
Assembly, which has implications for all Committees and Agencies and
their Committees of Commissioners when a proposed amendment
impacting them is considered by the General Assembly.

<a id="ga52-p286"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=288 printed_page=286 -->

Members of the CMC are the chairmen and chief administrative officers

of the General Assembly permanent Committees and Agencies. The
current Moderator and the immediate past five moderators of the
General Assembly are advisory members, having six-year terms.

Respectfully submitted,

/s/ RE Steve Dowling /s/ TE Bryan Chapell
Moderator 51 [st] General Assembly Stated Clerk of the PCA
Secretary of the CMC

<a id="ga52-p287"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=289 printed_page=287 -->

**REPORT OF**

**COVENANT COLLEGE**

_See 52-23 (p. 36) for the report of the Committee of Commissioners_

_and the Assembly’s action_

Greetings in the name of Jesus Christ,

I offer this annual report of the 2023-2024 academic year on behalf of the
Board of Trustees and the Covenant College community. In October of 2023,
the Board of Trustees graciously and unanimously appointed me as the seventh
president of Covenant College. It was a blessing and privilege to celebrate the
Lord’s 70 years of faithfulness to Covenant College at the March 2024 board
meeting as I was inaugurated. The 2023-2024 academic year was one in which
we experienced God’s blessings and provision in rich ways.

**Trustee Candidate Recommendations**
One of the ways God continues to bless Covenant College is with gifted trustee
candidates who meet particular needs on the board, who are committed to
Reformed liberal arts education, and who have demonstrated a desire to
support the work of the college. This year is no exception in that regard. The
following elders have been screened and recruited by the Covenant College
Board of Trustees and have been recommended unanimously as candidates for
election to the Class of 2029 of the Covenant College Board of Trustees. They
have all been nominated by their presbyteries. We are deeply grateful for these
men, for their willingness to serve, and for how their gifts and skill sets meet
the present needs of the board. Full biographies are available in the Rainbow
Book.

**Bradley Barnes** – TE, Southern New England
Bradley serves as the lead pastor at Christ the King Newton in Newton, MA.
He received an M.Div. from Gordon-Conwell Theological Seminary and was
ordained as a minister in 2004. Bradley and his wife, Meda, served with
Reformed University Fellowship at Harvard University from 2004-2009.
Bradley is the grandson of Covenant College’s second president, Dr. Marion

<a id="ga52-p288"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=290 printed_page=288 -->

Barnes, and has served one term on the Covenant College Board of Trustees
(2021-2025).

**Alexander Brown** – TE, Savannah River
Alex has served as the pastor of Golden Isles Presbyterian Church on St.
Simons Island, GA, for the past 15 years. Originally from Scotland, Alex
studied at the Free Church College (now Edinburgh Theological Seminary)
before moving to the United States. he is married to a covenant "Scot," Sara,
who graduated from Covenant College in 2001, and has three children. Alex
has served as the moderator of the Savannah River Presbytery (2024) and has
served on the SRP's MTW and MNA Committees. He has represented the SRP
on the Review of Presbytery Records Committee (2012-2014), the Overtures
Committee (2013), the MTW Committee of Commissioners (2016), and the
Covenant College Committee of Commissioners (2015, 2017). He has served
one term on the Covenant College Board of Trustees (2021-2025).

**Robert Curtis** – RE, Southwest Florida
Bob earned his B.S. in commerce from the University of Virginia and works
in real estate development and investment in the Tampa area. He has been
involved with the PCA for 30 years. Bob and his wife, Laurie, are members of
Redeemer PCA in Riverview, FL, and have five children, all of whom are
either graduates of Covenant or currently attending. Bob brings with him years
of experience, having previously served on the Covenant College Board of
Trustees from 2015-2024, and currently serves on the Covenant College
Foundation Board.

**Mark Griggs** – RE, Tennessee Valley
Mark is a retired attorney who now teaches entrepreneurship at Chattanooga
Christian School. Mark earned a B.S. in business economics from Auburn

University, a J.D. from University of Georgia Law School, and an LL.M in
estate planning from the University of Miami Law School. Mark and his wife,
Beth, are members of Grace+Peace Church in Ooltewah, TN, a newer church
plant. They have four children, one of whom graduated from Covenant
College. Mark has been involved in the PCA for over 30 years and has 12 years
of experience serving on the Covenant College Board of Trustees.

**Drew Jelgerhuis** – RE, Great Lakes
Drew serves as business development manager at Scherdel Medtec NA. He
earned a B.A. in mechanical engineering from Dordt University. Drew has

<a id="ga52-p289"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=291 printed_page=289 -->

been a church plant commissioner for three successful church plants in the
Great Lakes Presbytery. He currently serves as the church planting committee
chair at Christ Church PCA in Grand Rapids, MI. He and his wife, Jane, have
three children, all of whom graduated from Covenant. Drew has served one
term on the Covenant College Board of Trustees (2021-2025).

**Dennis Louis** – TE, Tennessee Valley
Rev. Dennis Louis has been a pastor for 12 years, serving as the senior pastor
of Chattanooga Valley Presbyterian Church for four of those years and as
pastor of discipleship at McIlwain Presbyterian in Pensacola, FL, prior to that.
He has chaired the Examinations Committee in the Gulf Coast Presbytery and
served on numerous other committees. Currently, he serves on the Candidates
and Licensure Committee for the Tennessee Valley Presbytery. Dennis was
born in the Bahamas and lived there until college. He graduated from
Pensacola Christian College with a B.A. and an M.A. in biblical studies and
then pursued his ministerial training at Reformed Theological Seminary in
Jackson, MS. He and his wife Theresa have four children, and enjoy
shepherding and getting to know the many Covenant students who attend
church at Chattanooga Valley Presbyterian Church.

**Martin Moore** – RE, Georgia Foothills
Marty has previously served the Covenant College Board of Trustees as its
chairman of the board, the treasurer, and chair of the Finance and Audit
Committees. He also serves as the representative of the College on the PCA
Administrative Committee. he is a graduate of ohio University. Marty attends
Chestnut Mountain Presbyterian Church, where he serves on the session,
chairs various committees, and teaches adult Sunday School. Marty is a retired
chief financial officer, having worked primarily in the private equity segment
and in multiple industries. He and his wife, Susan, have two grown children
and five grandchildren.
