**Admissions**

In the fall of 2023, we welcomed 263 new students to Covenant’s campus.
Each one of these students was prayed for as they began their Covenant
academic career. During the 2023-24 academic year, we laid the groundwork
for our biggest incoming class in ten years who would arrive in the fall of 2024.
During the 2023-24 academic year, we welcomed 1770 prospective students
from 46 states and 13 countries and 246 students interviewed for competitive
scholarships during our two on-campus Scholarship Weekends. We were

<a id="ga52-p293"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=295 printed_page=293 -->

delighted to host visitors overnight on campus over our sneakPEAK visit
weekends and allow them to be fully immersed in the Covenant community
and experience life inside classes and residence life. wear e so encouraged by
each student who the Lord brings to the mountain, and it allows us to extend
biblical hospitality as we show them how we do life together.

**Campus & Facilities**
In 2023–2024, Facilities Management completed numerous renovations and
upgrades across campus. These projects included enhancements to rental
apartments, stormwater improvements, exterior renewals for Founders and
Andreas, interior room updates in Belz, student apartment roof replacements,
and resolving lingering chapel roof issues. Additionally, mechanical and
electrical equipment replacements were carried out in Barnes and Founders.
Student Impact initiatives included upgrades to the dining space in The Blink,
renovations to student apartment kitchens, new classroom tables, and the
creation of a dedicated space in the library to serve campus commuters.
Routine maintenance efforts, such as parking lot repairs, resealing, and
dormitory hall carpet replacements, were also completed.
Following the January 2024 windstorm, Facilities Management promptly
completed necessary repairs, including constructing new team shelters at
Scotland Yard. Campus planning initiatives included partial replacements of
the library and chapel roof systems and preparations for the renovation of
Probasco Hall.

A new Capital Expenditures (CapEx) Planning Committee was established to
enhance strategic planning and resource allocation. It identifies and prioritizes
current and future capital needs to facilitate cross-departmental budget
coordination and resource sharing. The committee aims to foster trust, achieve
consensus on expenditures, and ensure alignment with budget constraints and
institutional priorities set by President Brad Voyles.

**Development & Finances**
We are thankful for our generous donors who gave over $8.5 million to the
college during the 2023-2024 fiscal year, including gifts to endowment,
student scholarships, the annual fund, and athletics. We saw another year of
strong participation in giving from our churches and were blessed to receive
gifts from 469 churches, with 202 partnering with us in our Church
Scholarship Promise program. In total, the college received roughly $968,000
from churches.

<a id="ga52-p294"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=296 printed_page=294 -->

The fiscal year ending June 30, 2024, saw a strong performance compared to
the prior year. Consolidated with the foundation, the college experienced a
positive change in net assets over $7 million. From an operating perspective,
the college continues to operate on tight liquidity but ended the year without
any debt.

**Student Life**
During the 2023-24 academic year, the New Scots Orientation Program
allowed first-year students to begin exploring their new home, learning about
the college’s academic and spiritual formation process, and building new
friendships. Stephanie Formenti became the new Vice President of Student
Development in February 2024. Student Senate and the Campus Activities
Board worked with the President’s Office to host a Spring Inaugural Ball for
President Voyles. The Conversations about Culture and Race Series provided
its second annual experiential learning trip to Birmingham and Montgomery,
AL, in partnership with Briarwood, Birmingham Theological Seminary, and
Urban Hope Community Church. Residence Life continued to serve emerging
adults, training all Resident Directors, Resident Assistants, and students living
in residence halls to build stronger community engagement through
discipleship.

The Covenant College Athletic Department experienced sustained success in
its third full year of competition within the Collegiate Conference of the South.
The mission of our department is to explore and express the preeminence of
Christ in intercollegiate athletics by cultivating disciples and pursuing
excellence. Academically, we achieved a fall average GPA of 3.41 and a 3.36
spring average GPA across all sports. Athletically, Volleyball, Men's Soccer,
and Men's Tennis won the conference championship. Additionally, five other
sports finished in the top four of the conference. We are thankful for the Lord‘s
provision over each of our programs and eagerly await what He has in store
for all of them in the future.

The Chapel Department at Covenant College has the privilege of walking
alongside and shepherding our students in their love of Jesus Christ and his
gospel. We have chapel three times a week, where we encounter Jesus together
in the community. We also oversee numerous group and individual
discipleship opportunities at the college. As a part of our regular chapel
services, we have two main conferences each year: The Francis Schaeffer

<a id="ga52-p295"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=297 printed_page=295 -->

Conference on True Spirituality and the Kingdom Life Conference. Our
student ministry coordinators help facilitate our Fall and Spring Days of
Prayer, the weekly Public Reading of Scripture, small group Bible studies in
dormitories, prayer hikes, and weekly prayer and praises on residence halls.
Please pray for wisdom and faithfulness as we seek to serve our students and
glorify our Father in heaven.

**Conclusion**
We are grateful for the oversight of the PCA as we seek to remain faithful to
our mission to explore and express the preeminence of Jesus Christ in all
things. Thank you for your continued partnership in God's work through
Covenant College.

Yours in Christ,

Dr. Brad Voyles
President

**R** **ECOMMENDATIONS**

1. That the General Assembly thank and praise God for the excellent work

and faithfulness of the Board of Trustees, faculty, and staff of Covenant
College in serving the Presbyterian Church in America by shaping
students for lives of service in the Kingdom of God.

2. That the General Assembly encourage congregations of the PCA to

support the ministry of Covenant College through encouraging
prospective students to attend, through contributing the Partnership Shares
approved by the General Assembly, and through prayer.

3. That the proposed budget for 2025–26 for Covenant College be approved.

4. That the minutes of the Board of Trustees of Covenant College for October

17-18, 2024, and March 20-21, 2025 be approved without exception.

5. That the General Assembly receive as information the foregoing Annual

Report, recognizing God’s gracious and abundant blessing and
commending the College in its desire to continue pursuing excellence in
higher education for the glory of God.

6. That the General Assembly designate Sunday, October 19, 2025, as a

Lord’s Day on which churches of the denomination are encouraged to

<a id="ga52-p296"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=298 printed_page=296 -->

highlight Covenant College's ministry and pray specifically for the
College in its mission and ministry.

7. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly

answer **Overture 9** from James River Presbytery “Amend BCO 14-1 to
Make AC Members Appointed by Committees and Agencies Non-voting
Advisory Members" in the **NEGATIVE** with reference to the
recommendation of the Administrative Committee.

Grounds:

In addition, Covenant College notes that the Administrative
Committee’s “unique role as a service committee to the General
Assembly and the entire denomination” ( _RAO_ 5-4) includes the
responsibility for evaluation and reporting its opinion to the
General Assembly of the budgets of each permanent Committee
and Agency. ( _RAO_ 4-11) That important administrative function
requires a clear understanding of each of the proposed budgets
which understanding requires close coordination and input from
representatives of the permanent Committees and Agencies,
acting as full participating and voting members of the
Administrative Committee.

8. That **Overture 11** from South Texas Presbytery, “To Study Incorporating

Bitcoin into Session, Presbytery, and Committee & Agency Balance
Sheets,” be answered in the **NEGATIVE** with reference to the
recommendation of the Administrative Committee.

Grounds:

The courts, permanent Committees and Agencies of the
denomination have the responsibility and right to independently
consider and evaluate the appropriateness of any legal investment
vehicle that its donors and supporters desire to utilize. To call out
not only one type of investment vehicle but one specific type of a
class of investment (i.e., Bitcoin is a single example of the broader
class of cryptocurrencies) is not necessary and unduly limits the
consideration of appropriate investment vehicles and strategies.

9. That **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery (“Erect Ad Interim

Committee to Receive Suggestions for Permanent Committee and Agency
Operations, Resources, and Expenses”) be answered in the **NEGATIVE**
with reference to the recommendation of the Administrative Committee.

Grounds:

<a id="ga52-p297"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=299 printed_page=297 -->

An ad interim committee may only be erected to “handle
particular matters of business” pursuant to RAO 9-1. Overture 27
provides the proposed committee only very generalized direction
with no clear indication of expected outcomes or deliverable
results. Rather, the committee is instructed to solicit and receive
recommendations that are then to be “collated, evaluated, and
reported” with no criteria for such evaluation and reporting
specified. Therefore, the Overture fails to identify any “particular
matters of business.” In addition, although the receipt of the
Overture was timely, it was received very close to the deadline
and does not provide Covenant College sufficient time to fully
consider having been submitted well after the last meeting of the
agency board prior to the 52nd General Assembly.

10. That the 52 [nd] General Assembly **REFER** **Overture 30** back to Houston

Metro Presbytery or to the 53 [rd] General Assembly in 2026.

<a id="ga52-p298"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=300 printed_page=298 -->

Supplemental Report

**SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF**

**COVENANT COLLEGE**

Covenant College submits the following Recommendation as a supplemental
report to the 52 [nd] General Assembly.

**R** **ECOMMENDATION**

11. That the 52 [nd] General Assembly **DEFER** action on **Overture 49**
from Pacific Northwest (“Erect ana d interim committee on AI”)
to the 53 [rd] General Assembly in 2026 with reference to the
rationale provided by the Committee on Discipleship Ministries.

<a id="ga52-p299"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=301 printed_page=299 -->

**REPORT OF**

**COVENANT THEOLOGICAL SEMINARY**

_See 52-24 (p. 40) for the report of the Committee of Commissioners_

_and the Assembly’s action_

**Strengthening Pastoral Formation, Growing the Church, and**

**Resourcing Leaders—in the PCA and Beyond**

_But I will stay in Ephesus until Pentecost, for a wide door for effective_
_work has opened to me, and there are many adversaries._
_– 1 CORINTHIANS 16:8–9_

In Paul’s concluding comments of his first letter to the church at Corinth, he
highlights how ministry opportunity often coincides with ministry challenge.
Rather than being cause for frustration or retreat, however, Paul frames his
challenge as just one more reason to persevere in his calling. Paul chooses to
stay in Ephesus because a “wide door for effective work has opened” and
“there are many adversaries.” To be sure, the adversaries Paul most likely had
in mind were antagonists to his efforts to proclaim the gospel, but it seems
likely he would not object to applying this insight to all kinds of difficult
circumstances and ministry setbacks.

Stepping back and considering the mission of Covenant Theological
Seminary, we can embrace the wisdom Paul expresses here. Without a doubt,
we are witnessing God opening doors and answering prayers as we put our
shoulders into the daily work of the Seminary, and more broadly, to the tasks
outlined in our Strategic Plan titled Centered on Christ’s Mission to Train the
Next Generation, which we have been implementing over the last year. (You
can see the full Strategic Plan on our website at
https://www.covenantseminary.edu/strategic-plan.) Nevertheless, challenges
remain. As with Paul, both blessings and battles inspire our confidence that
Christ, through his Spirit, is advancing his mission among us.

Relying on him and the grace he gives us, we move boldly forward into the
blessings and challenges that await us—but wed o not dos o alone. Rather, the

<a id="ga52-p300"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=302 printed_page=300 -->

Seminary can only function well and accomplish the tasks before us with the
support, encouragement, and assistance of our sister PCA agencies and
committees. To this end, a recurring theme for us as we think about the future
is to further expand on existing relationships to forge strategic partnerships
that benefit both the Seminary and our sister organizations, for the purpose of
strengthening the churches of our denomination—and the broader church
beyond the PCA.

From our perspective, the three-pronged goal of such partnerships in the
context of our mission is to (1) strengthen pastoral formation and training to
sustain church leaders for the long term, (2) grow Christ’s church through
intentional and robust recruitment and preparation of a new generation of
church planters, and (3) provide resources for churches and ministry leaders
that enable them to serve the Lord more effectively.

As this report shows, these three threads run through the fabric of all our
ministry efforts and intertwine in countless ways with the six main pillars of
our current Strategic Plan (prioritize pastor-minded students; strengthen
theological preparedness; renew focus on spiritual formation; strengthen local,
intercultural, and global mission; steward our relationship with the PCA; and
foster financial stability) as we seek to carry out our mission efficiently and
effectively for the eternal glory of God.

**Reaffirmation of Accreditation Status**
As we have been making progress in each of the main pillars of the Strategic
Plan, we have also been working to further evaluate and strengthen the base of
our academic foundation in conjunction with our accrediting agencies. To this
end, last October the Seminary hosted a comprehensive evaluation visit for
reaffirmation of its accreditation by the Association of Theological Schools
(ATS) Commission on Accrediting. Evaluators from peer institutions visited
the campus to verify that the school has met all applicable Commission
Standards of Accreditation (available at https://www.ats.edu/files/galleries/
standards-of-accreditation.pdf). ATS also solicited comments in advance from
those with relevant knowledge regarding how well the school met those
standards and/or generally demonstrated educational quality. These comments
were shared with the on-site evaluation committee, which met extensively with
students, faculty, board, and staff before reporting its findings.

<a id="ga52-p301"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=303 printed_page=301 -->

The visit went very well, and the team recommended that Covenant’s
accreditation be reaffirmed for a period of ten years, until March 31, 2034. All
current degree programs were approved. In addition, the team cited the
following distinctive strengths of the Seminary:

- Dynamic and collaborative institutional leadership that has renewed
relationships with stakeholders, strengthened denominational trust,
and led to a process that resulted ina strategic plant hat is bold,
measurable, and realistic.

- A well-qualified faculty who match personal warmth with the truth of
their convictions in ways that foster an environment for student
learning and formation, helping to share a distinct Covenant Seminary
ethos that marks graduates in identifiable and winsome ways.

- An attentive and relational student services team who are student
focused, data-informed, and proactive in fostering an environment
where students have the support needed to flourish throughout their
educational journey.

- the implementation o fa shared governance modelled by a policy driven board that incorporates diverse voices through an advisory
board and that works together with the administration and the faculty
to advance the school’s mission.

- Library services and staff who are extending the capacity of the
school’s resources to support both the residential and online student
communities.

Please rejoice with us in this news of reaffirmation of accreditation! This
represents the culmination of more than a year’s worth of labor by many staff
and faculty members. Special thanks go to Shannon Hathaway, our Director
of Accreditation; Jay Sklar, Vice President of Academics; and Jessie Swigart,
Dean of Academic Administration, who guided us excellently through this
process. I also want to extend my thanks to those trustees and advisors who
met with the Peer Review Team.

In addition to ATS, Covenant is also accredited by The Higher Learning
Commission (HLC) and is a member of the North Central Association of
Colleges and Schools (NCACS). The institution has been approved under
Commission policy to offer all its degree programs. Additionally, the
Seminary’s 75-credit hour Master of Arts in Counseling is fully accredited by
CACREP (Council for the Accreditation of Counseling and Related

<a id="ga52-p302"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=304 printed_page=302 -->

Educational Programs), which enables graduates with the 75-credit hour
degree (since 2022) to enjoy the portability of a CACREP counseling degree
when seeking licensure in states other than Missouri. For full details on all of
the Seminary’s various accreditations, see the Legal Information page of our
website at https://www.covenantseminary.edu/legal-information.

**ATS Moving Forward in Mission Grant—$25,000 to Further Church**
**Planting Recruitment and Training**
We were pleased this year to receive a $25,000 Moving Forward in Mission
Grant from the Association of Theological Schools (ATS) to further the
Seminary’s goal of recruiting and training more pastor-minded church
planters, as well as church planting team members, for the Presbyterian Church
in America and beyond.

The grant, part of the ATS Organizational and Educational Models Project,
provides additional support for Covenant’s recruiting efforts in part through
the creation and distribution of enhanced print, audio/visual, and digital
marketing materials specifically for its Church Planting Track. Additionally,
the grant enables the Seminary to explore new recruiting pipelines by
supporting attendance at national conferences dedicated to church planting, as
well as hosting such a conference on the Seminary campus.

Dr. Robert Kim, Associate Professor of Applied Theology and Church
Planting, and Philip and Rebecca Douglass Chair of Church Planting and
Christian Formation at Covenant, noted, “Preparing church planters has long
been a part of Covenant’s pastoral training mission. A good number of
qualified graduates each year end up planting churches through Mission to
North America (MNA), the PCA’s church-planting agency, or with other
similar organizations or ministries. But the need for new churches—and welltrained planters to lead them—is great. To help address this need, the Seminary
a few years ago instituted the Church Planting Track, a specialized series of
courses and related internships for the Master of Divinity (MDiv) and Master
of Arts (Biblical and Theological Studies) (MABTS) degree programs.
Covenant is one of only two ATS-accredited Reformed seminaries to have
such a track. The ATS grant will significantly enhance our ability to recruit
appropriately gifted and interested students specifically for church planting.”

The grant not only helps the Seminary fulfill part of its overall purpose to
“glorify the triune God by training his servants to walk in God’s grace, minister

<a id="ga52-p303"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=305 printed_page=303 -->

God’s Word, and equip God’s people—all for God’s mission,” but also
dovetails nicely with several priorities of our Strategic Plan. One of these
priorities is to increase enrollment of pastor-minded students by building on
relationships with influencers, alumni, and prospective students. This grant
increases the Seminary’s ability to reach and recruit students with an
intentional focus on church planting. Other strategic priorities are to strengthen
local, intercultural, and global mission, and to steward well the Seminary’s
relationship with our denomination. The grant helps here by fostering deeper
partnerships with sister PCA Agencies, other church planting agencies and
networks, and global mission ministries to see church planters prepared and
sent around the United States and the world. The grant also helps further
institutional financial sustainability, central to which is growing our student
body base, especially in the MDiv and MABTS program, to which the Church
Planting Track is connected. In all of these ways, the grant will help the
Seminary keep moving forward in mission. We are immensely grateful for the
work of ATS and for this grant, and we look forward to seeing how the Lord
will continue to build his church through it.

As part of the implementation of the grant, we aim to host a church planting
gathering on our campus to provide encouragement, resources, and additional
training opportunities for church planters. Watch our blog and other social
media outlets for more information on this and about other resources and

stories related to church planting.

**Lilly Endowment Pathways for Tomorrow Grants**
As of this past spring, the Seminary was actively applying for a new round of
funding announced by the Lilly Endowment, Inc., called The Pathways for
Tomorrow Initiative. In Lilly Endowment’s words, “The aim of this initiative
is to help theological schools strengthen and sustain their institutional
capacities to prepare and support pastoral leaders for Christian churches.” The
grants are aimed at helping institutions like Covenant to:

- Explore the leadership needs of Christian congregations as well as
emerging challenges and opportunities facing their own institutions.

- Gain clarity about their mission in light of these challenges and
opportunities and in relationship to the leadership needs of their
constituent churches.

<a id="ga52-p304"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=306 printed_page=304 -->

- Assess the effectiveness of their financial and educational strategies
as well as the long-term viability of their overall operations for
educating and supporting pastoral leaders.

- Design and implement plans to launch new and/or strengthen current
projects to address their key challenges and opportunities, and to make
their institutions more educationally effective and financially viable
for the continued preparation and support of pastors and
congregational lay ministers into the future.

We believe that the focus of these grants perfectly aligns with priorities already
expressed in our current Strategic Plan and are actively working to apply to
both the $1 million grant for individual schools and the larger collaborative
grant in the amount of $10 million. If awarded, these grants would enable us
to expand our efforts to recruit and train gifted and qualified pastor-minded
students both through renewed emphasis on some of our tried-and-true
pathways and pipelines, and through new partnerships with sister PCA
agencies and other ministries that may provide new channels for recruitment.

To learn more, visit the grant page at the Lilly Endowment site
( _https://lillyendowment.org/ pathways-for-tomorrow-initiative/_ ) and watch
our own news and social media for further word from us.

**Dr. Richard Joe Named to Paul D. Kooistra Chair of World Missions**
We are pleased to announce that Dr. Richard Joe will be joining our faculty
over the summer as Associate Professor of World Missions and the first

occupant of the Paul D. Kooistra Chair of World Missions. Dr. Joe comes to
us from Mission to the World, where he has served for several years as Persian
Global Ministries Regional Coordinator. He also was the Persian Regional
Director for Mosaix Academy.

Born and raised in Philadelphia, Dr. Joe has had a calling to serve Persians
ever since his first trip to the Middle East in 1993. After pastoring a PCA
church in California for several years, he and his family moved to Europe in
2006, where he pastored a Persian church, following which they returned to
the US for further study and then moved to the Middle East in 2013 to teach
and train international church leaders. In 2107 he returned to the US for PhD
studies. In addition to his teaching at Covenant Seminary, Dr. Joe will continue
to teach and train international church leaders in Europe, the Middle East, and
other regions of the world. He holds a PhD in Intercultural Studies from Trinity

<a id="ga52-p305"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=307 printed_page=305 -->

Evangelical Divinity School, a Master of Professional Studies in Persian from
the University of Maryland, a Master of Divinity from Westminster
Theological Seminary, and BA in International Studies from Muhlenberg
College.

President Rev. Dr. Thomas C. Gibbs said of the appointment, “Dr. Joe’s many
years of experience in global missions and his heart for reaching the unreached
with the message of the gospel make him an ideal choice to be the first to fill
the Kooistra Chair, named in honor of Dr. Paul D. Kooistra, former President
of Covenant Seminary, former Coordinator of Mission to the World, and
former President of Erskine College, whose influence on our institution and
on the PCA and beyond has been enormous. Dr. Joe’s appointment gives the
Seminary our first full-time missiology professor in more than a decade. We
are immensely grateful to have him with us.”

MTW Coordinator Dr. Lloyd Kim noted, “We are thrilled for Covenant
Seminary to name Dr. Joe as its Paul Kooistra Chair of World Missions.
Richard and his wife, Hyeyoung, have served faithfully as missionaries with
Mission to the World for many years in some challenging contexts. He will
bring not only academic expertise to this role, but also invaluable field
experience. We are excited for the seminary and the many students Dr. Joe
will equip, train, and inspire for global missions.”

Please pray with us for the Lord’s continued blessing on Dr. Joe and his
ministry as he helps prepare a new generation of mission-minded pastors,
church planters, and missionaries.

**New Graduate Certificate in Leading Bible Studies**
As part of our commitment to providing training and resources for the local
church, we have developed a new Graduate Certificate in Leading Bible
Studies, designed to teach believers to study the Bible well and to lead others
in studying it well. Coursework for this 11-credit-hour program will engage
students in the content and structure of the Bible, the process of interpreting
the Bible, and in designing lesson plans and talks to faithfully communicate
the Bible. The GCLBS is open to both men and women and will be especially
helpful for Men’s or Women’s Ministry leaders, church elders, or anyone who
simply wants to learn how to read and understand Scripture better.

<a id="ga52-p306"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=308 printed_page=306 -->

The certificate can be done residentially or 100% online, with the credits
potentially being transferable to the MA in Ministry (MAM) or MA in
Theological Studies (MATS) degree programs if students wish to pursue
further study. The program will be available to start online in summer 2025,
or to start residentially in fall 2025. For more information, contact our
Admissions team at https://connect.covenantseminary.edu/register/inquire.

**Newly Restructured Church Planting Scholarship**
Covenant’s Church Planting Track Scholarship, initiated several years ago, has
recently been restructured to better equip Church Planting Track students in
the MDiv and MABTS degree programs with the ministry tools necessary to
plant thriving, gospel-preaching churches.

- _Full-time MDiv students in the Church Planting Track_ in the first year
of their program receive a scholarship of up to 50% of tuition. The
award increases each year and caps at up to 80% in year four.

- _Students in the Church Planting Track of the MABTS program_ may
receive an up-to-25% tuition award for every year in the program.

The goal of the scholarship is to help launch future church planters and church
planting team members into the harvest field with as few financial burdens as
possible. To learn more, visit _covsem.co/cptscholarship_ .

**Compelling Preaching Grant Update**
Last year we were pleased to tell you about our receipt of a $1.19 million
Compelling Preaching Initiative Grant from Lilly Endowment for the purpose
of equipping future preachers. This grant is designed to help further develop
an emphasis on preaching as a primary means of pastoral ministry for the
Seminary’s Master of Divinity students. One special benefit of the grant was
that it enabled us to move Dr. Thurman Williams, our Director of Homiletics,
from part-time to full-time faculty status, where he has continued to do an
excellent job teaching and overseeing the preaching classes for our students.
He has also been busy with other aspects of implementing the grant, which
include:

- Developing preaching cohort groups to better facilitate peer growth in
preaching.

<a id="ga52-p307"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=309 printed_page=307 -->

- Developing preaching conferences and workshops that will expand on
the success of our existing annual Covenant Seminary Preaching
Lectures.

- Developing preaching resources and a related website that can be used
to encourage and revitalize seasoned preachers, and better equip and
support young preachers and those aspiring to preach.

- Developing opportunities for preachers of all experience levels, ages,
backgrounds, and ethnicities to learn from each other, including bivocational pastors who may not have access to seminary-level
education due to lack of accessibility and/or affordability.

We are in various stages of developing each aspect of the grant, and you can
look forward to hearing more about them as more information is available.

**Capital Campaign Preparation**
After much discussion and consideration with our staff and Board, we have
begun preparations for launching a new capital campaign later this year. Under
the leadership of VP of Advancement James Dickson, we have been refining
our ideas and are in the midst of planning for the components of the campaign
and working to create an initial draft of the campaign case to begin sharing
with donors for feedback and further refinement. The campaign would build
on the success of our $43 million _Hope for the Future_ campaign of a few years
ago by focusing on further enhancing our ability to equip a new generation of
leaders for a thriving church. Look for more information as we get closer to
the official launch of the campaign.

**Encouraging Enrollment News**
We are pleased to report that the past academic year has continued the general
upward trend in enrollments in key programs. Our FY24 final Credit Hours
Sold of 7,834 exceeded the budget by 1.74%. This represented a 6.2% Year
Over Year increase from the previous year’s 7,375 Credit Hours Sold.

We experienced robust numbers in three critical degree programs. Our pastorminded MDiv-Ordination Track program grew by 18% compared to the
previous year with 40 new students enrolled. The incoming classes in the MAC
and MABTS degree programs were almost identical to last year. Along with
this, the spring 2025 semester saw a strong enrollment of 53 new students.
Among these, 2 enrolled in the residential MDIV, 14 in the hybrid MDIV, and
9 in the residential MAC degree. This is very encouraging, given that these

<a id="ga52-p308"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=310 printed_page=308 -->

positive results occurred during the spring semester, which is typically a time
of lower enrollments for us.

Given the strong performance in our MDiv, MABTS, and MAC programs, it’s
clear that we are making progress in the right areas. For example, when we
step back and compare the fall 2024 enrollment to the fall of 2021, in the
MDiv-Ordination Track and the MABTS degree we performed 85% better.
Even so, overall enrollment growth has not yet outpaced the lean entering
classes of both 2021 and 2022. Consequently, though we still believe an
overall Credit Hours Sold level of 11,000 is attainable, this will be achieved
over a longer timeline.

Though we have not yet reached our goals, we are encouraged. Progress in
enrollment remains our top institutional priority. Given the annual cycle in
academic institutions, this progress can sometimes seem to go at a snail’s pace,
but we are hopeful that with patience, hard work, and much prayer, we can
continue to bring these numbers up. We are grateful for the wonderful work
being done by our tireless Admissions team, under the direction of VP of
Enrollment Rev. John Chung, in identifying and recruiting new students.
Please join us in congratulating them, and in praying for their efforts, and
please pray that even now the Lord would be preparing the hearts of those
whom he would bring to Covenant in the coming year.

**Financial Outlook and Donor Engagement**
The Seminary’s overall financial performance and donor engagement through
the first half of FY25 was encouraging, and at this writing we expect the
second half to follow suit. Unrestricted donations in the amount of $1,166,814
were right on pace with recent performance. When restricted giving, _Bridge_
_the Gap_ campaign gifts, and gifts to complete the Paul D. Kooistra Chair of
World Missions are considered, all gifts received to the Seminary ending
December 31, 2024, totaled $2.57 million, which mirrors the strong results of
the past three years. Additionally, due to strong donor support, continued
attention to revenue streams, and expense forecasting, we reduced our
anticipated deficit for FY25 to $807,000, a 23% reduction, which will reduce
stress on our still healthy financial reserves.

Our FY26 budget was approved by our Board of Trustees in January of this
year, and we recommend it to the General Assembly for adoption. The budget
forecasts 8,050 Credit Hours Sold, a 6% increase over the expected FY25

<a id="ga52-p309"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=311 printed_page=309 -->

CHS. Our recruitment goal for FY26 is to reproduce our performance in FY24,
which saw a 6% Year Over Year increase. An additional important element in
the FY26 budget is a significant increase in our Annual Fund target from $2
million to $2.5 million. The AF has not seen a substantial increase in several
years, and in light of recent past performance, this increase seems strategic and
reachable.

**North Outer Forty Road Property Sale**
In a related budgetary matter, we have been working diligently over the last
year to finalize the sale of property owned by the Seminary along North Outer
Forty Road just south of our campus. This property had previously been used
for a pastoral retreat center and other purposes but has been expensive to
maintain and has not been used much in recent years. The property has been
under contract and last July a rezoning request for it was approved by both
municipalities with jurisdiction. Closing on the sale took place in March 2025
for a contracted price of $2.95 million. The sale provides additional needed
relief for our budget.

_**Onboard Covenant**_ **Orientation Process**

Strengthening pastoral formation and training also means strengthening and
caring well for our faculty and staff. To that end, our Human Resources team
this past year developed a new orientation and training module called _Onboard_
_Covenant_ to help new faculty and staff members get quickly acclimated to the
mission and ministry of the Seminary. This process provides an overview of
our institutional and denominational history, a sense of our vision and values,
and an introduction to the ways in which we do community life together as we
carry out our mission. We see this as a useful tool that can be easily modified
and updated as needed. Responses from recent new hires to the onboarding
process have been encouraging.

**Formational Seminary Events, Activities, and Resources**
Other ways in which we are fulfilling our mission and addressing the various
pillars of our Strategic Plan come through the many gospel-centered,
pastorally focused, and spiritually formative events and activities we hold
throughout each year on our campus. Some highlights from the past year are
included here.

- **Dedication of the Garden of Lament and Hope**

<a id="ga52-p310"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=312 printed_page=310 -->

Seminary does not insulate us from the hardships common to life. The
challenges of juggling classes, family life, work, ministry
opportunities, and anything else life throws out there can be daunting.
Sometimes, it can be too much. Hardships strike us all now and then,
and grief can be a specter that broods over us. A campus family
struggles with the loss of a child, the death of a parent, personal illness,
depression. A faculty member fights an ongoing battle with cancer.
An enthusiastic friend to many suddenly ends his own life and the
community is left reeling. A long-ago incident of horrific murder casts
shadows that haunt many lives even today. Stories of lament and grief
like this are as much a part of life as stories of praise and hope—and
God uses all of them to shape his people for the ministries to which he
calls them. In recognition of these hard stories in our lives—and of the
fact that even in lament we have cause for hope in Jesus Christ—the
Seminary, as part of our annual Chapel Service of Lament in March,
dedicated an area near our library as a memorial Garden of Lament
and Hope. When completed sometime in the next few months, the
garden will be a place of memorial and meditation where anyone in
the campus community can come to remember and reflect on the griefs
we all share and seek rest in the hope we all claim in Christ.

- **The 2024 Francis Schaeffer Institute Lectures—** _**Christ and All His**_
_**Hallows: Evangelism in a Disenchanted World**_ **.** Our annual FSI
Conference in September brought in pastor and apologist Dr. Gavin
Ortlund, award-winning author of _What it means to be Protestant_, and
Mr. Jake Meador, editor-in-chief of Mere Orthodoxy, to discuss the
challenges and opportunities of evangelism in a world that has become
jaded and disenchanted with the claims of Christianity. Drawing on
the works and ideas of philosopher Charles Taylor, the presentations
explored some of the causes for the current cultural situation and
offered ways in which believers can embody and convey a dynamic
experience of the risen Christ that breaks through the cultural
hardening with the truth of the gospel. The fall 2025 FSI Lectures will
take place September 19 and 20 on the Seminary campus. Theme and
speaker details will be announced in the next few months.

- **2024 Covenant Seminary Preaching Lectures Focused on** _**No**_
_**Greater Calling: The Life of the Preacher**_ . Rev. Dr. Trent Casto,
Senior Pastor of Covenant Church (PCA) in Naples, Florida, was the

<a id="ga52-p311"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=313 printed_page=311 -->

featured speaker for the 2024 edition of the Covenant Seminary
Preaching Lectures. Noting that Dr. D. Martyn Lloyd-Jones once
described preaching as “the highest, the greatest, and the most glorious
calling to which anyone can ever be called,” Dr. Casto delved into the
profound responsibility of those who are set apart by God to proclaim
his Word. He explored three key aspects of the preacher's life: the craft
of preaching itself, the common pitfalls that can undermine a
preacher’s ministry, and the essential pursuit of personal holiness. The
goal was to equip those called to this sacred task with the zeal,
diligence, and reliance on the Holy Spirit necessary to fulfill their
calling. The 2025 edition of the Lectures will take place October 28;
theme and speaker are yet to be determined.

- **2024 Lifetime of Ministry Conference Highlighted** _**Renewed Hope**_
_**in Kingdom Ministry: Sustaining Grace for a Life of Ministry**_ **.** The
recently re-instituted Lifetime of Ministry (LOM) Conference is
designed to help pastors and church leaders explore and navigate the
unique challenges of pastoral work, and to provide a place of rest,
retreat, and encouragement for pastors and their spouses with a view
to equipping them for the next season of ministry. The 2024 edition of
LOM took place in October with Dr. Dan Doriani, Professor of
Biblical and Systematic Theology, and Dr. Thomas C. Gibbs,
President and Associate Professor of Applied Theology, as the
featured speakers. Their focus was on helping those in ministry renew
a sense of hope and enthusiasm amid the challenges of ministry life,
and on ways of sustaining that renewal for the long term. The 2025
edition of LOM will take place in November; theme and speaker
details are yet to be determined.

- **The 14th Annual Theological Fellowship Conference Featured Dr.**
**Jack Collins.** This year’s edition of our annual student-led theology
conference took place in January 2025 and featured plenary speaker
Dr. Jack Collins, Professor of Old Testament, who presented on the
topic _“The Testimony of the Lord is Sure,” but What About Everyone_
_Else’s?—The Shape of Sola Scriptura: The Place of Extra-Biblical_
_Material in Biblical Thinking_ . (See elsewhere in this report for more
about Dr. Collins and his many contributions to the Seminary.) Hosted
by The Theological Fellowship at Covenant Seminary, a student group
devoted to theological discussion, the conference also featured

<a id="ga52-p312"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=314 printed_page=312 -->

presentations of papers from Covenant students on a variety of
theological topics, with an award given for the best paper as judged by
the group. The Theological Fellowship also brings in other speakers
during the year for the benefit of the student body.

- **The 2025 David C. Jones Theology Lectures Featured Dr. Fred**
**Sanders on the Doctrine of the Son of God.** The second installment

of our recently revitalized David C. Jones Theology lectures took
place in March and featured Dr. Fred Sanders, Professor at the Torrey
Honors College of Biola University and a faculty member of the Los
Angeles Bible Training School, who focused on the doctrine of the
Son of God, exploring the trinitarian context of Christology and the
modes of the Son’s presence in the history of salvation—specifically
considering his sending in terms of invisible mission and visible
mission. Dr. Sanders is the author of several books, including _Union_
_with Christ and the Life of Faith_ (Baker Academic, 2025), _The Holy_
_Spirit: An Introduction_ (Crossway, 2023), and _The Triune God_
(Zondervan, 2016). Named for the late Dr. David C. Jones, long-time
professor of systematic theology and ethics at Covenant Seminary, the
Jones Lectures honor Dr. Jones’s teaching and influence on
generations of our students and highlight the Seminary’s Reformed
theological heritage by focusing each year on a particular area of
theological study with presentations by a contemporary academic
authority in that area. The date, theme, and speaker for next year’s
lectures are yet to be determined.

- **The 2025 Austin Harrington Counseling Lectures Featured Dr.**
**Gary Moon.** To assist counselor, pastors, and ministry leaders in
growing in their work, and to help them help others grow in their lives
and faith, our Counseling Department annually offers the Austin
Harrington Counseling Lectures. The 2025 edition in March featured
Dr. Gary Moon, distinguished Professor of Psychology and Spiritual
Formation at Richmont Graduate University and founding Executive
Director of the Martin Institute for Christianity and Culture and the
Dallas Willard Center for Christian Spiritual Formation at Westmont
College. Dr. Moon spoke on the main theme of _Stimulating Spiritual_
_Formation: Helping One Another Thrive_ by looking at how spiritual
formation shapes us as persons, and answering seven important
questions related to the practical implications of formation for

<a id="ga52-p313"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=315 printed_page=313 -->

counseling and ministry. The dates, theme, and speaker for the 2026
edition of the Harrington Lectures have not yet been determined.

**Spiritual Formation and Gospel-Centered Community**
Covenant has long recognized that “spirituality” encompasses all matters
within the scope of concern and activity of the Holy Spirit through whom we
are conformed more and more to the image of our Lord and Savior, Jesus
Christ. Within that broad area, the Seminary prioritizes the elements of
spiritual formation essential to all believers as they grow in their love for Jesus
and especially those fundamental for individuals called to lead others in
ministry. Thus, our goal is for students not only to know the Bible better but
to be transformed through a deeper relationship with Jesus Christ—and the
deepest transformation occurs within gospel-centered community. Many of
our alumni point to their time in community at Covenant as one of the greatest
and most formative times of their lives. Our Student Life team seeks to

cultivate this transformative personal and spiritual formation in a variety of
important ways. Campus life is geared toward nurturing growth in gospelcentered community through many avenues that bring students, faculty, staff,
and families together in creative, challenging, and life-changing ways.

- _**Weekly Chapel Services**_ . One of the primary ways the Lord shapes
our campus community for ministry is through worship. Here at
Covenant that comes through our weekly Wednesday Chapel services.
For the last couple of years, we have enjoyed a renewed emphasis on
this weekly time of gathering to hear the Word of God preached by a
faculty member or guest preacher. Under the leadership of Drs. Robert
Kim and Robbie Griggs, chapel services are more focused and have
been better attended by students, staff, and faculty. Growing out of
Covenant’s statement of purpose, our chapel speakers the last few
years have been reflecting on and unpacking the meaning of some of
the themes and phrases that are at the core of the Seminary’s ethos.
The fall 2024 theme, for example, was “Glorifying the Triune God,”
and for spring 2025 it has been “Walking in God’s Grace.” In addition
to Covenant professors, speakers for this academic year have included
a variety of pastors from PCA, EPC, and other churches (some local,
some from other parts of the country), as well as several leaders of our
denomination, such as Dr. Irwyn Ince, Dr. Ed Dunnington, Dr. Scotty
Smith, Rev. S. J. Lim, and others. Please join us in giving thanks for

<a id="ga52-p314"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=316 printed_page=314 -->

this renewed interest in the worship of our triune God and in praying
that Chapel services may flourish even more.

- _**Fireside Chats with the President**_ . Begun at the invitation of our
Student Council, these informal chats with President Dr. Thomas C.
Gibbs provide a time when students can bring any questions they may
have about current happenings at the Seminary or plans for the future,
share concerns or praises, and simply take time to build relational
capital between the administration and the student body. These regular
semesterly events have become very popular with our students.

- _**Ministry Lunches**_ . Multiple times throughout each semester
representatives from many different church, ministry organizations,
and PCA agencies and committees host lunch-time gatherings to
inform students about internship and ministry opportunities, provide
helpful insights on ministry life, share experiences, and offer
encouragement. This past academic year featured two Special
Ministry Lunches of particular note:

o In October 2024, renowned award-winning Christian

recording artist and Covenant alumna **Laura Story** visited
our campus to lead music during a Chapel service and stayed
to have lunch and lead a discussion on the ins and outs of

leading worship music.
o In March 2025, Coordinator for Women’s Ministry with the

PCA’s Committee for Discipleship Ministries **Karen Hodge**
joined us for an enriching lunchtime conversation with the
women of Covenant Seminary about our shared commitment
to serving Christ’s church through the vital work of women’s
ministry.

- _**Seminary Picnics**_ . Each fall and spring Student Life organizes
Seminary-wide picnics as times when the entire Covenant community
can gather to share good food, enjoy fun games, and just hang out
together and get to know one another better.

- _**Parents’ Morning/Night Out**_ . Our Family Nurture program offers
childcare during select Seminary events and also provides specific
times during each semester when parents may drop their kids off for
fun and games at the Log Cabin on our campus while Mom and Dad
enjoy some much-needed time off.

- _**Day of Prayer**_ . Each semester we host a Day of Prayer during which
all classes are cancelled so everyone can gather for a morning of

<a id="ga52-p315"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=317 printed_page=315 -->

intensive prayer and worship, followed by a lunch prepared by our
International Student Fellowship.

- _**Cohort and Covenant Groups**_ . First-year students in several degree
programs are required to participate in Cohort Groups. These groups
of eight to ten students facilitated by a faculty or staff member meet
weekly throughout the semester and are designed to promote
sustainability for a lifetime of ministry by helping students grow in
emotional and spiritual health, relational skills, and wise conflict
resolution. By reflecting on each person’s own story within the group
context, students learn how they fit into the community of faith and
into the larger biblical story that God invites them to be part of. The
groups are aimed at helping students become more like Christ—a
crucial element for leading Christ’s church well. The Seminary also
offers a variety of non-required Covenant Groups that students are
encouraged to participate in as they are able. These groups are usually
focused on a particular theme or interest and aim at deepening
relationships and skills that help to foster personal growth in grace.

- _**A Servant-Leader Mindset**_ . At new student orientation, our incoming
students are told, “You are not the end of your own education.” The
fact is, a deepening relationship with Christ is not for oneself alone but
for the benefit of those whom we will be called upon to lead and serve
in every area of life and ministry. Every aspect of community life at
Covenant is geared toward fostering this mindset of servant-leadership
and nurturing a heart of grace in men and women who will one day be
the leaders and influencers in Christ’s church and kingdom.

- _**Whole-Person Formation**_ . In addition to worship, relationships are at
the heart of the Christian life and the basis of effective gospel ministry.
The Student Life team continues to pursue each student individually—
whether male, female, residential student or online student—through
personal contacts and ongoing check-ins and follow-ups throughout
their time at Covenant. Faculty also set aside specific hours each week
to meet with and mentor students in ways that go beyond classroom
teaching. The Seminary also provides access to a limited number of
free counseling sessions for those who may be dealing with issues that
require deeper biblical insight.

**Stewarding Partnerships with the PCA**
We greatly value our connections with our sister PCA agencies and
committees and continue to pursue mutually beneficial partnership

<a id="ga52-p316"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=318 printed_page=316 -->

opportunities that enhance the various aspects of our denominational calling
to be “faithful to the Scriptures, true to the Reformed faith, and obedient to the
Great Commission.” Some examples include:

- _**We continue to work with MNA**_ as we further develop our recruitment
strategies for the Church Planting Track and Church Planting
Scholarships. We host a Church Planting Summit in conjunction with
MNA and have for several summers hosted the full MNA Church

Planter Assessment Center on our campus. We look forward to doing
so again this year. We work closely with MNA and the Missouri
Presbytery MNA Committee to identify and place students in strategic
internships in local church plants and other local missions endeavors.

- _**We also continue a close partnership with RUF**_, holding potentialstudent recruitment events for RUF interns and staff each year and
hosting RUF ministry assessment opportunities for students interested
in campus ministry. We also provide a scholarship specifically for
current or former RUF interns, campus staff, or students actively
involved with an RUF ministry.

- _**Our ties with MTW help to foster a mission-oriented mindset**_ in our
students by exposing them to the stories and experiences of
missionaries who serve all over the world, many of them in “closed
countries” or other places where their ministries face severe
opposition or even persecution. These missionaries often speak in our
Chapel services or at Ministry Lunches hosted on campus where
students can interact with them directly. The big news in this area is
our hiring of Dr. Richard Joe to be the first occupant of the Paul D.
Kooistra Chair of World Missions (see details earlier in this report).
We are grateful for the input and guidance of MTW Coordinator Dr.
Lloyd Kim and his staff as we sought the right person for this position.

- _**We often consult with and work with CDM as we consider what**_
_**resources may be beneficial**_ to our churches and church leaders. One
such resource is CDM’s Children’s Ministry Certification, for which
the Seminary partners with CDM to provide a continuing education
component called Children’s Ministry Certification Plus. This enables
Children’s Ministry Certificate holders to enhance their training by
auditing our Educational Foundations course together as a distinct
cohort on our campus or online during the fall hybrid residency week.
Covenant benefits from the wisdom and experience of these faithful
and trained practitioners, and it is our prayer that those who participate

<a id="ga52-p317"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=319 printed_page=317 -->

benefit from further study in the biblical and theological foundations
of educational ministries, teaching and learning methods, discussion
of human development, and curriculum planning. Further deepening
our partnership with CDM, this year Dr. Thomas C. Gibbs and his
wife, Tara, were featured speakers at the committee’s Women’s
Ministry Leadership Training (LT) Conference in February and their
Leading, Equipping, and Discipling (LEAD) Children’s Ministry
Training Conference in March. Both organizations benefit greatly
from this ongoing close relationship.

- _**Our Admissions staff is in close contact with Covenant College**_ and
other important feeder institutions that serve as the essential pipelines
for potential Covenant Seminary students. As noted elsewhere in this
report, we hope, with the assistance of some of the grants we have
recently received or applied for, to expand our recruiting efforts in
several ways, most notably with regard to church planting.

- _**In addition, President Dr. Thomas C. Gibbs travels frequently both**_
_**nationally and internationally**_ to meet with PCA and other church
leaders to forge ongoing bonds of friendship and partnership. He visits
alumni serving as pastors, church planters, or missionaries, and he
teaches and preaches often in conjunction with many of our
missionary partners both in the US and abroad.

**The Blessing of People: Faculty, Staff, and Board Updates**
The Lord’s greatest gift to Covenant Seminary is the people who serve here as
faculty, staff, or trustees. We have been particularly blessed in this regard.
Notable staffing updates or changes for the past year are listed below.

- _**Dr. C. John “Jack” Collins to Retire**_ . One of our longest-serving
faculty members, Professor of Old Testament Dr. C. John “Jack”
Collins, retired from Covenant Seminary at the end of the 2024–2025
academic year after 32 years of faithful service. Our Board of
Trustees, administration, faculty, staff, students, and thousands of
alumni across the country and around the world are grateful for Dr.
Collins’s ministry among us and to us, and we look forward to seeing
how the Lord will work through him and his wife, Diane, in the next
chapter of their life.

Rev. Dr. Thomas C. Gibbs, President of Covenant Seminary, noted
that “Jack’s teaching and many other professional and personal

<a id="ga52-p318"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=320 printed_page=318 -->

contributions to the life of this institution have had a profound impact
on generations of our students and alumni, as well as the broader
church. I personally am deeply grateful for Jack’s influence on my
own ministry journey and for the blessing of knowing him as a friend
and colleague. I am grateful as well for his wife, Diane, who has been
an integral part of Jack’s life and ministry, and of the life and ministry
of Covenant. We’ll miss having them around campus as often, but we
look forward to staying in touch as they plan to remain in the area after
Jack’s retirement.”

Dr. Collins joined Covenant’s faculty in 1993 after several years as a
church planter in Spokane, Washington, serving first as Assistant
(1993–1995), then as Associate (1995–2000), and finally as Professor
of Old Testament (2000–2025). He has chaired the Old Testament
department since 2005. His many books and journal articles focusing
on themes of science and faith and how to read the Bible well have

help many to better understand the Scriptures, especially the early
chapters of Genesis. His work as chair of the Old Testament
committee for the ESV translation of the Bible and as general editor
for the Old Testament portion of the ESV Study Bible helped produce
one of the most important and influential modern Bible versions.

Dr. Collins’s background in science (he holds a BS and an MS from
MIT, in addition to his MDiv from Faith Evangelical Lutheran
Seminary and a PhD in Hebrew from the University of Liverpool) and
his deep interest in linguistics and hermeneutics have informed his
teaching ministry from the beginning. This, combined with his
reputation as a thoughtful and careful scholar, has resulted in several
notable opportunities along the way: a Templeton Foundation grant
for developing and teaching a graduate-level course on science and
faith, a Research Fellowship from the Discover Institute’s Center for
the Renewal of Science and Culture, and a stint as a senior research
Fellow at the Carl Henry Center for Theological Understanding at
Trinity International University. Among the abundant fruit of these
opportunities were some of Dr. Collins’s best-known books: _Science_
_and Faith: Friends or Foes?_ (Crossway, 2003), _The God of Miracles:_
_An Exegetical Examination of God’s Action in the World_ (IVP, 2000),
and _Reading Genesis Well: Navigating History, Poetry, Science, and_
_Truth in Genesis 1–11_ (Zondervan, 2018). In addition, the countless

<a id="ga52-p319"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=321 printed_page=319 -->

seminars, conferences, and other speaking and teaching engagements
he has been part of over his long career have been major blessings to
the church.

Dr. Jay Sklar, Vice President of Academics for Covenant, summed up
nicely the feelings of many who have known and worked with or
studied under Dr. Collins: “I have been teaching alongside of Jack for
going on twenty-five years now. Throughout that time, he has been a
faithful mentor and friend. And “faithful” is indeed the word to use,
for if there’s one thing Jack has modelled for me it is his faithful care
and commitment to his friends. I’m so grateful for that friendship and
for the many, many ways he has taught me to read the Bible better.
God bless you, brother!”

Please pray with us in gratitude for Jack and Diane, and for the Lord’s
continued blessing on them.

**Other Faculty/Staff Updates**

- _**Dr. Tasha Chapman**_, formerly Professor of Educational Ministries,
**stepped down from her position in December 2024** after 22 years
of faithful teaching. Alongside her significant teaching in the
educational ministries program, Dr. Chapman’s resume includes a
long list of contributions, including key roles in the Doctor of Ministry
program, Covenant’s distance education program, the international
students’ groups, and the Center for Ministry Leadership, the last of
which led to the publication of two co-authored books: _The Politics of_
_Ministry: Navigating Power Dynamics and Negotiating Interests_
(IVP, 2019) and _Resilient Ministry: What Pastors Told Us About_
_Surviving and Thriving_ (IVP, 2013). We are deeply grateful for Tasha
and for the ways in which these contributions, and many more, have
shaped our students and helped graduates all across the country and
around the world in their ongoing service to Christ and his Kingdom.
Please join us in giving thanks to God for Tasha’s rich legacy and her
impact on students, alumni, and the broader church.

- _**Rev. John Chung**_ was named **Vice President of Enrollment** for
Covenant Seminary in July 2024. Rev. Chung had been serving as
Senior Director of Enrollment Management for Covenant since April
2022. In his expanded capacity, he continues to oversee and coordinate

<a id="ga52-p320"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=322 printed_page=320 -->

the functions of the Admissions, Financial Aid, and Institutional
Retention teams, while providing strategic leadership and direction for
all the Seminary’s initiatives related to student recruitment,
enrollment, and retention. Rev. Chung’s experience as a pastor, his
expertise in team leadership and staff development, his extensive work
with nonprofit organizations, and his extensive relationships in the
Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) and beyond have all enabled
him to guide the Seminary’s enrollment team forward in exciting ways
over the last few years. Under his leadership the Seminary’s
enrollment has increased significantly, with the most recent incoming
class (for the 2023–2024 academic year) up 43%. Enrollment
continues to rise in all degree programs, especially those particularly
aimed at pastor-minded students, such as the residential Master of
Divinity (MDiv), the hybrid MDiv, and the MA (Biblical and
Theological Studies) (MABTS). Prior to his role at Covenant, Rev.
Chung worked with the PCA’s Mission to the World and as Institute
Director for Next (formerly Global Youth and Family Institute). He
served as a Youth Minister and Pastor with the Korean Presbyterian
Church of St. Louis (PCA); as Executive Pastor of Christ Central
Presbyterian Church (PCA) in Centreville, Virginia; as an Associate
Pastor at The Journey–Tower Grove (Acts 29) in St. Louis; and as a
Visiting or Adjunct Professor at Covenant. he holds a ba in speech
communication from the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign
and an MDiv from Covenant Seminary. We are grateful for John’s
tireless efforts to promote the Seminary and recruit new students, and
for the steadfast commitment to excellence and camaraderie he brings
to his job.

- _**Dr. Dan Doriani**_, Professor of Biblical and Systematic Theology, who
has been teaching for us part time over the last year while serving as
Interim Senior Pastor for Briarwood Presbyterian Church in
Birmingham, Alabama, will **return to full-time teaching** at Covenant
later this year. We are grateful for the opportunity Dr. Doriani has had
to provide pastoral leadership and stability for this flagship church of
our denomination following the unexpected passing of long-time
Senior Pastor Dr. Harry Reeder in May 2023. Even in the midst of his
busy schedule, Dr. Doriani has continued to be a source of blessing
not only to our students but also to the broader church through his
Work with Dan Doriani podcasts for the Center for Faith and Work

<a id="ga52-p321"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=323 printed_page=321 -->

St. Louis, for which he serves as Founder and President, and through
his publications (most recently the book _The Death of the Deadly Sins:_
_Embracing the Virtues That Transform Lives_, which he edited for
P&R Publishing), and his many speaking and teaching engagements.
(See “Faculty Publications and Kingdom Service” below for more.)

- _**Dr. Aaron Goldstein**_, Associate Professor of Old Testament and
Director of Online Learning, will **step into a full-time teaching role**
in our Old Testament department beginning July 2024. Dr. Goldstein
began in 2015 as a Visiting Instructor in Old Testament and took on
additional responsibilities in helping to administrate Covenant’s
online program in 2018. In 2021, Dr. Goldstein joined the faculty as
Assistant Professor of Old Testament and also became the Director of

Online Learning. He holds a PhD in biblical studies from Concordia
Seminary in St. Louis, where he wrote a dissertation on the narrative
role of John the Baptist as eschatological Elijah in Matthew’s Gospel.
Prior to coming aboard full time at Covenant, Dr. Goldstein served for
eight years as a pastor at Cornerstone Church (EFCA), a local
congregation in St. Louis, and taught as an Adjunct Professor of
Religion at Lindenwood University. He has written several articles
and book reviews and was a contributor to the _ESV Men’s Devotional_
_Bible_ (2015). He also serves as an Associate Pastor at Trinity
Presbyterian Church (PCA) in Kirkwood, Missouri.

- _**Dr. Robbie Griggs**_, Associate Professor of Systematic Theology and
Director of the ThM Program, will be **assuming the role of Dean of**
**Faculty** as Dr. Brad Matthews moves from that role to Vice President
of Academics (see below). He will continue to oversee the ThM
program. Dr. Griggs joined our faculty in 2016 and has been an
invaluable player on the Covenant team in many ways. He serves as
Faculty Advisor for the student-led Theological Fellowship at
Covenant Seminary (along with Dr. Bob Yarbrough and Dr. Brian
Aucker), facilitator of the Systematic Theology Reading Group for
students, a member of the board of the student-produced _The Common_
_Table_ magazine, a member of the Seminary’s Chapel steering
committee, oversees the revitalized David C. Jones Theology
Lectures, and is active in ministry at the local church and presbytery
levels. His PhD in New Testament is from Durham University, and he
also holds an MDiv from Covenant Seminary and a BA in philosophy

<a id="ga52-p322"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=324 printed_page=322 -->

and a BS in finance from the University of Missouri–Columbia. An
expert in New Testament theology and early Jewish theology, his
research focuses on Paul’s theology of grace in Galatians in
comparison with contemporary Jewish theologies of God as “giver.”
This research takes him to the heart of several key historic and
contemporary debates in Pauline and systematic theology. Dr. Griggs
served as a pastor at Central Presbyterian Church in St. Louis for eight

years.

- _**Dr. Joel Hathaway**_, Director of Alumni and Career Services, and
Director of the Doctor of Ministry Program, **recently published the**
**book** _**Find and Get Your Ideal Ministry Job**_ (J. Hathaway, 2025).
Based on 20 years of experience coaching pastoral candidates and
consulting with church search committees and adapted from material
originally taught in Dr. Hathaway’s seminary class Candidating and
Transitioning Into Ministry, the step-by-step guide is designed to help
candidates find the best ministry positions, navigate search
committees, ace interviews, and understand church dynamics. It also
assists candidates in refining their unique ministry approach, crafting
a standout resume, and becoming an intentional worker. The book is
available through Amazon.com.

- _**Dr. Richard Joe**_ joins our faculty this summer as Associate Professor
of World Missions and the first occupant of the **Paul D. Kooistra**
**Chair of World Missions** . See earlier in this report for details.

- _**Dr. Brad Matthews**_, Associate Professor of New Testament and Dean
of Faculty, will step into the role of **Vice President of Academics**
starting in July of this year when Dr. Jay Sklar, currently in the VP
role, shifts back to full-time teaching and writing (see below). Dr.
Matthews joined our faculty in the summer of 2008. He began his
professional life as a mechanical engineer before coming to Covenant
Seminary to complete an MDiv degree and discerned the Lord’s
calling to pursue PhD studies. He completed his thesis on the nature
of Christian maturity at the University of Durham, England, which
involved an interdisciplinary evaluation of modern perspectives and
theories using a biblical theology of maturity. He has published on the
topics of Christian maturity and interpretive issues in Colossians and
is currently working o na popular version of his thesis a swell a work

<a id="ga52-p323"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=325 printed_page=323 -->

on the topic of Christian authority. Dr. Matthews’s deep love for the
church led him to serve in several different capacities throughout his
ministry: as a pastoral intern over youth ministries and director of
campus ministry, as an interim pastor and now Assistant Pastor at
South City Church in St. Louis, and as a frequent speaker at retreats,
conferences, and Sunday school classes.

- _**Dr. Jay Sklar**_, currently Vice President of Academics and Professor
of Old Testament, has, after nearly a decade in the VP role, decided to
**shift back to full-time teaching and writing** as of the end of the
current academic and fiscal year. He also served as Dean of Faculty
from 2012 to 2020. Dr. Sklar’s doctoral research at the University of
Gloucestershire was completed under Dr. Gordon Wenham and
focused on the sacrificial system in Exodus through Numbers,
resulting in the book _Sin, Impurity, Sacrifice, Atonement: the Priestly_
_Conceptions_ . He has continued to focus his work on the Bible’s first
five books, writing commentaries on Numbers (Story of God,
Zondervan) and two on Leviticus (Zondervan Exegetical Commentary
on the Old Testament; Tyndale/IVP Old Testament Commentary), coauthoring a Leviticus Bible study, and written supplementary books
on Leviticus ( _Additional Notes on Leviticus_ ) and Numbers ( _Additional_
_Notes on Numbers_ ). He serves as one of three general editors for
Crossway’s ESV Expository Commentary series (for which he has
written commentaries on Exodus and Jonah) which he wrote the
commentary on Jonah) and offers a helpful collection of resources for
preaching and teaching on the Pentateuch at
_preachandteachthebible.com_ . Dr. Sklar’s ministry experience ranges
from leading youth and children’s ministries in both church and camp
settings in his native Canada to teaching, writing, speaking at church
and college events, and helping with theological education overseas.
He has been a frequent teacher at Faculté Jean Calvin in France. We
are grateful indeed for Dr. Sklar’s years of dedicated service in his
many roles at Covenant and excited that he will be able to give even
more of his time, attention, and expertise to teaching and training our
students for ministry.

- _**Dr. Jessie Swigart**_, Assistant Professor of Educational Ministries and
Dean of Academic Administration, **recently defended her**
**dissertation and earned her PhD** through Trinity Evangelical

<a id="ga52-p324"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=326 printed_page=324 -->

Divinity School. Completed under Dr. Donald Guthrie, Dr. Swigart's
dissertation explored professional identity formation in early career
helping professionals. Dr. Swigart joined the staff of Covenant
Seminary as Academics Coordinator in 2009 after completing her
MAEM at CTS in 2008. She was appointed Associate Dean of
Academics in 2013 and Dean of Academic Administration in 2016. In

this role she works with the Vice President of Academics to manage
and evaluate the academic processes of the Seminary. Dr. Swigart
teaches in the Educational Ministries department. She is passionate
about creating challenging and compassionate spaces in which
learners can grow together in the knowledge of our savior Jesus Christ.
Please join us in celebrating this academic milestone with Jessie.

- **Mr. Nate Whitfield (MDiv ’23)** joined our staff in spring 2025 as our
**Admissions Recruiter** . Nate grew up and went to elementary school
in the Northern Virginia Suburbs of Washington, DC, but moved to
St. Louis for middle and high school, attending Twin Oaks
Presbyterian Church. He is the twenty-second person in his extended
family to have graduated from Wheaton College, where he earned a
BS in Computer Science. After Wheaton, Nate worked in consulting
and sales and relationship management in Maine and St. Louis, then
came to Covenant Seminary to study for his MDiv, which he received
in 2023. While at Covenant, Nate also worked in student ministry at
the Kirk of the Hills Presbyterian Church. We are grateful for Nate’s
enthusiasm for the Seminary and for the valuable addition he makes
to our Admissions team.

**Additional Faculty Searches in Progress**

- _**New Systematic Theology Professor**_ . We are pleased to announce that
we are making progress on our search for a new professor to serve in
our department of Systematic Theology and have identified a likely
candidate. Our goal as of this writing is to present the candidate at our
April 2025 Board meeting for review and approval.

- _**Francis Schaeffer Chair of Apologetics**_ . We are still seeking a
professor to serve in the Schaeffer Chair and pray for the Lord’s
guidance and providence in this regard.

<a id="ga52-p325"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=327 printed_page=325 -->

**Farewell to Old Friends**

- _**Rev. Dr. W. Wilson Benton Jr. (1941–2025): Celebrating a Life of**_
_**Service to the Lord**_ . With our brothers and sisters in the Presbyterian
Church in America, Covenant Seminary mourned the passing of Rev.
Dr. W. Wilson Benton Jr., who went to be with the Lord on January
26, 2025, following a long battle with Alzheimer’s disease. Our hearts
are with Dr. Benton’s beloved wife, Pam, his children and
grandchildren, and their extended family and friends as they grieve his
loss, but we rejoice as well that Dr. Benton is now in the presence of
the glorious Savior he loved so much and served so well. We praise
God for Dr. Benton’s many years of pastoral ministry, twenty of which
were at Kirk of the Hills Presbyterian Church in St. Louis, not far from
the Seminary. We are grateful for his love and support of Covenant
over the decades, from sharing his wisdom and expertise as a preacher
and pastor with our students as an adjunct homiletics professor and
Doctor of Ministry instructor, to serving as a faithful friend and mentor
for countless Covenant faculty, staff, and alumni. His involvement
with and support for the Seminary’s Center for Ministry Leadership in
the early 2000s helped refine and sharpen the institution’s focus on
preparing strong pastors and other leaders for Christ’s church. Dr.
Benton’s influence on Covenant and on our denomination have been

profound.

Of his own experience of Dr. Benton, Covenant Seminary President
Dr. Thomas C. Gibbs, said, “As a student, i wa sin awe of Dr. Benton’s
command of Scripture and his gift for delivering sermons that stirred
our minds and moved our hearts. Over the years, though, what I came
to most appreciate about Wilson and Pam was their warm hospitality
and commitment to faithfully shepherding those the Lord put in their
path. Wilson was a giant in the PCA, and he will be missed by all of
us.”

- _**Eleanor “Ellie” Brown (1943–2025), former Financial Aid Director**_
_**and Registrar at the Seminary**_ . Many of our alumni and longer-term
staff and faculty remember Ellie Brown, who passed away in January
2025 in DeSoto, Missouri, at the age of 81. Ellie served at the
Seminary for many years after an already full career in health care
services. After her retirement from Covenant, she managed the
Paradise Valley Resort in Cassville, Missouri, for a few more years.

<a id="ga52-p326"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=328 printed_page=326 -->

Ellie loved working and serving the students whom the Lord sent her
way, and many still recall fondly her kindness and expertise in helping
them get the financial aid they needed to pursue their seminary studies.
Ellis is survived by her children, grandchildren, siblings, and a large
contingent of friends and extended relations.

**Board/Advisory Board Updates**

- _**Board of Trustees Additions for 2024–2025**_

o **Mr. David Duren**, Chairman, CEO, and Founder, Circon

Energy, LLC, Houston, TX. Joined the Board in 2024 after
serving a term on Advisory Board.
o **Rev. Brian Habig**, Senior Pastor, Downtown Presbyterian

Church, Greenville, SC. Returned to Board in 2024 (which he
first joined in 2014) after serving a term on Advisory Board.
o **Mr. Brewster “Bruce” Harrington**, Principal (Retired),
