**Future Trustees (Class of 2029) – Endorsed by the Board of Trustees and**
**recommended to the Nominating Committee of the General Assembly**
The Board of Trustees of Covenant Theological Seminary is pleased once
again to present to the Nominating Committee of the PCA our
recommendations for the next class of Seminary Board members. We remain
thankful for the General Assembly’s ruling nearly two decades ago which
allows the Seminary to communicate directly with the Nominating Committee
to endorse specific candidates for our Board, as long as their names have first
been submitted by their respective presbyteries. These men provide key
strengths and experience we believe the Seminary needs for the years ahead.

_**Covenant Seminary hereby endorses the following ruling elders**_ as
candidates for its Board of Trustees for the Class of 2029:

o **Mr. William “Bill” Bennett**, Senior Vice President, Cadence

Bank, Birmingham, AL. Currently serving on Advisory
Board.

o **Dr. C. Scott Shidemantle**, Professor of Biblical Studies and

Bible Core Coordinator, Geneva College, Beaver Falls, PA.
Joined the Board in 2022; nominated for second term.
o **Mr. Lee Tyner**, General Counsel, Texas Christian

University, Fort Worth, TX. Currently serving on Advisory
Board.

o **Mr. Frank Wicks**, Executive Vice President and President of

Applied Markets, a Division of Sigma-Aldrich (Retired), St.
Louis, MO. Currently serving on Advisory Board.

_**Covenant Seminary hereby endorses the following teaching elders**_ as
candidates for its Board of Trustees for the Class of 2029:

<a id="ga52-p328"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=330 printed_page=328 -->

o **Rev. Dr. Hugh Barlett**, Senior Pastor, Chesterfield

Presbyterian Church, St. Louis, MO. Currently serving on
Advisory Board.
o **Rev. Dr. Brian H. Cosby**, Senior Minister, Wayside

Presbyterian Church, Signal Mountain, TN. Joined the Board
in 2022; nominated for second term.

_The Board of Trustees of Covenant Theological Seminary humbly submits the_
_above endorsements with gratitude that the General Assembly encourages us_
_to voice our particular needs and concerns in this way._

**Faculty Publications and Kingdom Service**
Our faculty’s influence reaches far beyond the classroom: professors mentor
and disciple students, participate in the life of local congregations, share their
teaching and preaching abilities with the larger church in the U.S. and around
the world, and engage with our culture and the pressing issues of our day from
a biblically sound and gospel-centered perspective. Here is a sampling of their
publications and other kingdom service since our last report.

- _**Dr. Brian Aucker**_, Professor of Old Testament

o Subject of Faculty Profile in _Covenant_ magazine, spring 2024.
o Faculty Advisor for the student-led Theological Fellowship at

Covenant Seminary (along with Dr. Bob Yarbrough and Dr.
Robbie Griggs).
o Mentored numerous students and served as Faculty Advisor

for several ThM and PhD students.

- _**Prof. Suzanne Bates**_, Assistant Professor of Counseling

o Served on the Seminary’s Title IX Committee and Diversity

Committee.

o Facilitated Beautiful Community Covenant Group primarily

for CTS students of color, and Women of Wisdom group for
student women over the age of 40.

Archaeology

o Published the entries “Anaphora Pilati,” “Paradosis Pilati,”

“Pilate Cycle,” and “Vindicta Salvatoris” in _Brill_
_Encyclopedia of Early Christianity_, ed. P. van Geest, B. J. L.
Peerbolte, and D. Hunter (Leiden: Brill, 2024).

<a id="ga52-p329"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=331 printed_page=329 -->

_Oxford Bibliographies in Biblical Studies_, ed. Christopher

original publication in 2018.

Institute for Biblical and Archaeological Studies on CTS’s

**Gratitude for Our Denomination and Our Role Within It**

We conclude this report as we do each year with humble gratitude to our
denomination for continuing to support and encourage us, and to the Lord for
allowing us to be part of his gracious work in bringing the good news of Jesus
Christ to people everywhere as we train pastors, counselors, and other ministry
leaders for the PCA and beyond. May God’s hand of blessing b eon us, and on
our sister PCA organizations, as we work together to bring honor and glory to
Jesus’s name. May he guide us and keep us all steadfast in our devotion to him,
so that we may stay the course that he has set for us and hear at last, when we
reach our final destination, those beautiful and blessed words, “Well done,
good and faithful servants.”

<a id="ga52-p335"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=337 printed_page=335 -->

Respectfully submitted,

_**Rev. Dr. Thomas C. Gibbs**_

President

**R** **ECOMMENDATIONS**

1. That the General Assembly give thanks to God for the ministry of

Covenant Theological Seminary; for its faithfulness to the Scriptures, the
Reformed faith, and the Great Commission; for its students, graduates,
faculty, staff, and trustees; and for those who support the Seminary through
their prayers and gifts.

2. That the General Assembly encourage the congregations of the

Presbyterian Church in America to support the ministry of Covenant
Theological Seminary by contributing the Partnership Shares approved by
the Assembly, and by recommending Covenant Seminary to prospective
students.

3. That the General Assembly ask the Lord’s blessing on Covenant

Seminary’s President, Rev. Dr. Thomas C. Gibbs, and grant him and the
Seminary’s leadership team, faculty, and Board of Trustees great wisdom,
biblical faithfulness, and clear vision as they lead the institution forward
in training fruitful pastors and other ministry leaders.

4. That the General Assembly ask God to guide Covenant Seminary’s

ongoing efforts at recruiting new students, evaluating and strengthening
our programs, and seeking to make the Seminary a greater resource for the
church both locally and globally.

5. That the General Assembly ask God’s blessing on the Seminary’s planning

and fundraising efforts, and on its attempts to recruit a new generation of
dedicated pastor-scholars to train new generations of leaders for Christ’s
church and kingdom.

6. That the General Assembly praise God for his provision of faithful donors

and supporters who continue to give generously toward our mission, and
for the many ways that he has enabled Covenant Seminary’s students,
faculty, and staff to continue building effectively on the solid foundation
laid by our forebears in the faith. Praise him for the gift of resilient and

<a id="ga52-p336"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=338 printed_page=336 -->

creative people who enable the institution to function so well during
challenging times.

7. That the General Assembly pray for unity among the brethren of the PCA

and ask the Lord to work in all our hearts to foster a deeper desire to engage
with one another and the world in compassionate and gospel-centered
ways, and that we might bear strong witness to the truth and power of
God’s redeeming grace.

8. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the stated and called

meetings of the Seminary’s Board of Trustees and Executive Committee
of the Board of Trustees for 2024–2025 without exception as follows:

- _Stated Board Meetings:_ April 26, 2024; September 27,
2024; January 24, 2025

- _Called Board Meetings:_ None

- _Stated Executive Committee Meetings:_ April 25, 2024;
September 17, 2024; December 5, 2024; January 23, 2025

- _Called Executive Committee Meetings:_ January 9, 2025

9. That the proposed budget for fiscal year 2025–2026 for Covenant

Theological Seminary be approved.

10. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 9** from James River

Presbytery “Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to Make AC Members Appointed by
Committees and Agencies Non-voting Advisory Members” be answered
in the **NEGATIVE**, with reference to the recommendation of the
Administrative Committee.

Grounds:

Covenant Theological Seminary recognizes the PCA has assigned
the Administrative Committee a special “role as a service
committee to the General Assembly and the entire denomination”
( _RAO_ 4-2; 5-4). This service specifically includes the
responsibility for “financial coordination and independent
evaluation of the budgets…for review, discussion, and
recommendation to the General Assembly” ( _RAO_ 4-11). Given
this important administrative and fiduciary function it is critical
that the Administrative committee have a clear understanding of
each of the proposed budgets, which requires the close
coordination, input and expertise of the representatives of the
permanent Committees and Agencies, acting as full participating

<a id="ga52-p337"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=339 printed_page=337 -->

and voting members of the Administrative Committee ( _RAO_ 411; 5-1).

11. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 11** from South Texas

Presbytery “To Study Incorporating Bitcoin into Session, Presbytery, and
Committee & Agency Balance Sheets” be answered in the **NEGATIVE**
with reference to the recommendation of the Administrative Committee.

Grounds:

The courts and Permanent Committees and Agencies of the PCA
already have the freedom and responsibility to consider and
evaluate the appropriateness of any legal investment that its
constituents wish to utilize. it is historically unprecedented to
direct the collective attention of the Church to a specific
investment – not just an investment category (cryptocurrency) but
a particular product or brand (Bitcoin) – to be included ina well balanced portfolio, is not necessary, and unduly limits the
consideration of appropriate vehicles and strategies.

12. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia

Presbytery “Erect Ad Interim Committee to Receive Suggestions for
Permanent Committee and Agency Operations, Resources and Expenses”
be answered in the negative with reference to the recommendation of
Covenant College.

Grounds:

Covenant Theological Seminary further notes that prior to their
approval by the Assembly, the budgets of the Permanent
Committees and Agencies are reviewed and approved by multiple
bodies comprised of Assembly-elected representatives that are
obligated by certain fiduciary guidelines ( _RAO_ 4-11): Permanent
Committee or Agency Board, Committees of Commissioners
(CoC) of said committee or board, the PCA Administrative
Committee (AC) and the CoC of the AC, therefore, this overture
is unnecessary. Similarly, Permanent Committee and Agency
actions are supervised by GA-elected Committees and Boards
and, then, approved through minutes reviewed and approved
annually by GA Committees of Commissioners and the GA itself.
Additionally, given that this overture lacks a funding plan, which
would require a 2/3 vote of the Assembly for GA approval ( _RAO_
9-3), its execution would be an unbudgeted mandate for the
Administrative Committee.

<a id="ga52-p338"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=340 printed_page=338 -->

13. That **Overture 30** from Houston Metro Presbytery, “Amend _BCO_ 8-4, 20
1, 21-1, 21-12, and 23-1 re: the Calling and Dissolution of TE
Relationships for Needful Works” be deferred without prejudice until the
53 [rd] General Assembly in 2026.

Grounds:

This overture was received at a late date, after all but two of the
Permanent Committees and Agencies (CTS & AC) had held their
stated spring meetings and so were unable to offer an opinion, and
for the purpose of hearing from all Permanent Committees and
Agencies so that this overture, which potentially affects the legal
employment relationships of all, might be adequately considered,
evaluated with legal counsel, and perfected for service to the
Church.

<a id="ga52-p339"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=341 printed_page=339 -->

**REPORT OF THE**

**GENEVA BENEFITS GROUP**

_See 52-33 (p. 62) for the report of the Committee of Commissioners_

_and the Assembly’s action_

We are pleased to present our Report to the General Assembly on behalf of the
Board of Directors and staff of Geneva Benefits Group.

**President’s Report**
The start of every new year is a wonderful opportunity for me to be reminded
of why Geneva exists and what a privilege it is to serve our denomination. I
can’t get out of Genesis without coming face-to-face with the great story of
God’s provision for the whole world through Joseph’s planning and
stewardship. Then, it is not far into Exodus when Moses supports Joshua’s
victory over the Amalekites through the raising of his hands. Both passages
shape our office’s calling and work. We are here to serve the church and
support her in her work such that our pastors and ministry workers can focus
on the mission of the church.

Each year at this time, as I begin my Bible reading plan through the Scriptures,
I look forward to reading both accounts. They lead me to pray more earnestly
for our churches and the future of our pastors and ministry workers. They lead
me to ask questions like, “Is Geneva doing all we can do to help prepare our
church for the years ahead? Are there more things we can do to come alongside
our churches to assist them in their work of ministry? Are there needs our
churches will have in the future that we can help with?” What I love about
Geneva is that I am not the only one asking these questions. Our entire
leadership team is preoccupied with thinking of how best to serve our church,
our pastors, and our ministry workers.

_Richer Services_

This past year marked another step in the direction of expanding our services
to our churches. In 2024, we continued to provide sabbatical matching grants
for pastors serving in under-resourced churches. We also laid the groundwork
for pastoral cohorts, which we launched in January 2025. this i sin addition to

<a id="ga52-p340"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=342 printed_page=340 -->

our Cherish counseling program, our health insurance assistance program, and
all the other financial assistance programs we have provided the denomination
for years. We continue to provide our employee benefits of Life Insurance,
Disability Insurance, as well as Vision and Dental, and are always looking for
other services we could provide the church.

_Richer Tools_

We know that the greatest employee benefit need of our churches is health
insurance. We also know that the cost of health insurance is becoming too
onerous for many of our churches to keep providing it. Over the past three
years we have explored different solutions that could serve our churches, and
we think we may have found a plan that would help many of our pastors and
ministry workers. Our efforts to enrich our services to the denomination
included improvements to our retirement plan investment line-up and
exploring new services to help our churches. One such initiative is looking at
creating a savings vehicle for churches so they came earn some return on their
cash reserves.

In 2024 the board conducted a search for anew recordkeeper. Our primary
motivation in doing so was to provide our pastors and ministry workers with
better tools to help you prepare for retirement. We know that the greatest single
thing anyone can do to improve their financial future is to increase their
savings, so we are seeking to find tools that will help you do just that.

_Richer Team_

While we have slowed our pace of hiring in the past year, we continue to add
staff. The addition of TE Will Chang as our Investment Specialist in January
of 2024 has been a wonderful blessing for us as well as our pastors and ministry
workers. We hired Mike Copening to serve as our IT manager while also
adding another team member to our Benefit Services team, Sarah Joiner. Both
Sarah and Mike have been excellent additions to our team. Their love for the

local church has made them excellent members of the Geneva staff. They
encapsulate the heart each of our staff members has for our churches, pastors,
and ministry workers.

**Market Update**
2024 proved to be another strong year for US Equities as the S&P 500 posted
gains of over 20%. This marks the second consecutive year of +20% returns
driven by the magnificent 7 (accounting for more than half of the return and

<a id="ga52-p341"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=343 printed_page=341 -->

includes Microsoft, Apple, Alphabet, Meta, Amazon, Nvidia, and Tesla) and
large-cap growth more broadly. Coming into the more volatile fourth quarter,
however, the rally slowed as there were two rate cuts of .25%, the election
outcome in November that provided a brief tailwind for stocks, and most
notably, a change intone by fed chair jerome powell who indicated a more
cautious approach in the months ahead. As a result, rate cut expectations for
2025 were reduced from four to two .25% reductions following the recent
strength of the US economy. December inflation remained sticky (not falling
as quickly as expected) as the Consumer Price Index (CPI) came in at 2.9%,
and December nonfarm payrolls increased by 256,000, well surpassing the
expected 153,000 jobs.

International equities underperformed the US markets due to a stronger dollar
and the outperformance of US mega caps. Potential tariffs under the Trump
administration could result in further uncertainty in global regions. As Brian
[Swint states, “Currency traders are taking the president-elect’s tariff plans](https://barrons.cmail19.com/t/j-l-wliihtd-dkdttikdx-h/)
seriously. Treasury secretary nominee Scott Bessent said…a 10% tariff should
[cause the greenback to appreciate 4%—about the amount it has gained since](https://barrons.cmail19.com/t/j-l-wliihtd-dkdttikdx-k/)
the election. That’s bad for stocks since it reduces the value of any company
earnings abroad and makes U.S. exports more expensive.” [1]

The strength of the economy, coupled with reduced rate cut expectations for
2025, led to a selloff in rates (rates go up, and prices go down) as falling bond
prices pulled the Bloomberg Aggregate Index into negative territory in the
fourth quarter. The sell-off was enough to essentially erase all the gains from
the first three quarters of the year. On a relative basis, high yield continued to
outperform as credit was rewarded given the strength of the economy. The
Bloomberg Aggregate Index returned -3.06% for the quarter, while the
Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Index returned .17%. With the sharp rise
in yields, longer maturity bonds suffered the most, given their greater duration
risk. The Bloomberg Long Treasury Index (maturity greater than ten years)
returned -8.62% for the quarter.

As we move into 2025, there remains much uncertainty about the impact of
the new administration. What will be of importance is the timing and
magnitude of policy implementation, particularly related to tariffs,
immigration, tax cuts, and deregulation. The S&P 500 continues to be

1 The Barrons Daily, Jan 17, 2025.

<a id="ga52-p342"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=344 printed_page=342 -->

concentrated and more expensive, with its current price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio
trading at 28x. Yet, the economy continues its resilience as labor markets are
strong, inflation is trending down overall, and projected earnings growth
remains positive. Perhaps it is even more prudent now to remain well-balanced
and well-diversified, given the uncertainty of the upcoming year.

**Summary of 2024 Operations**
The audit was received and there were no actions or recommendations

required.

Total 403(b) retirement plan assets under management increased by 12.1%
from $947,717,693 to $1,044,330,158. This increase can be attributed to
comparative market performance over the prior year and plan contributions
surpassing plan distributions. The retirement plan continues to be Geneva’s
largest employee benefit plan, with over 10,000 retirement accounts at yearend 2024.

Participation also increased for the combined group insurance benefit plans,
exceeding 5,000 insured employee lives.

With over 5,000 participants, life insurance plans experienced no plan design
changes in 2024. Offered through MetLife, the plans include features such as
will preparation, portability, estate services, and accelerated benefit offerings,
all with limited (or no) medical underwriting for new employees.

Over 4,000 participants have disability insurance, including long-term and
short-term disability. Offered through Unum, the long-term disability plan
provides elements of top-tier group disability benefits, such as retirement
income protection, cost-of-living adjustments, and claims payment until Social
Security’s full retirement age. Geneva’s short-term plan covers the loss in
income for the period between the loss and when long-term coverage begins.

The two newest plan offerings of dental and vision insurance protect almost
400 families. In 2024, there were no changes to either plan. Both plans promote
tax-efficient employee benefit offerings and boast large national networks.

Geneva has recommended a property, casualty, and liability insurance carrier
for organizations since 2000, and since 2017, has recommended Brotherhood
Mutual. Brotherhood specializes in providing insurance to Christian

<a id="ga52-p343"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=345 printed_page=343 -->

organizations and seeks to provide PCA organizations with special groupaffiliated pricing. Brotherhood Mutual and related companies also provide
commercial auto, worker’s compensation, mission travel insurance, legal
assistance, and payroll processing.

Since 1973, the Geneva Relief Fund has provided pastors and their widows
with critical financial assistance and support. We give thanks to God for his
provision that met the increased needs of our pastors, widows, and ministry
workers. In 2024, we provided over $824,000 in financial assistance to PCA
church servants through 454 financial assistance awards. Through our Cherish
program, wives of PCA teaching elders were able to meet with a qualified
Christian counselor. We invested $68,000 in this much-needed program to
provide pastors’ wives with access to 750 counseling sessions.

We are grateful to report that as needs increased, so did generosity. In 2024,
generous individuals, churches, and private foundations gave over $1.75
million to the Relief Fund. We thank every church that supports this ministry,
whether through gifts or participating in our special Look After offering. We
invite all churches to prayerfully consider sharing about the Relief Fund with
your congregations and inviting them to participate in a special offering in
support of our retired pastors, widows, and ministry workers in need. We will
provide you with everything you need.

**Regulatory Limits for 2025**
The cost-of-living and required statutory limit adjustments applicable to the
403(b) Retirement Plan for 2025 are as follows:

Employee elective deferral limit $23,500 ($500 increase)

Catch-up contribution limit (ages 50-59 and 64+) $ **7**,500 ($0 increase)

Catch-up contribution limit (ages 60-63) $11,250 (new)

Retirement Plan contribution limit $70,000 ($1000 increase)

<a id="ga52-p344"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=346 printed_page=344 -->

**Staff**

The Geneva staff is thankful to the Lord for His faithfulness and everlasting
love to his Church this past year and eagerly awaits the opportunities and
challenges in store for our future. We believe that God will continue to bless
our ministry to others as we remain faithful to Him. We welcome the prayers
and partnership of participants and churches this year and into the future. It is
our privilege to serve those who minister in the Presbyterian Church in
America.

Andrew Field Rev. Ed Dunnington, CFP [®]
Chairman, Board of Directors President

**R** **ECOMMENDATIONS**

1. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly

answer **Overture 9** from James River Presbytery “Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to
Make AC Members Appoint-ed by Committees and Agencies Non-voting
Advisory Members" in the **NEGATIVE**, and grant TE Ed Dunnington
permission to speak to the Overtures Committee at General Assembly on
behalf of the Geneva Board of Directors;

Grounds:

The Overture is contrary to our history, practice, wisdom, and
Christian charity.

First, the overture is contrary to our history. Since 1988 our
forefathers arranged for leaders who have been elected to
positions which make them regularly responsible to supervise the
mission of the PCA’s Committees and Agencies should have a
voice and a vote on Administrative Committee decisions affecting
their ability to fulfill their Assembly-assigned duties ( _BCO_ 14-12;
_RAO_ 5-3). The AC functions “as a service committee to the
General Assembly and the entire denomination” ( _RAO_ 4-2; 5-4).

Second, the overture is contrary to our practice. The
overture’s wording suggests that some Administrative Committee
members are elected while others are only “appointed” by their
respective Committees or Agencies. This is simply inaccurate.
The General Assembly elects the eleven at-large members of the
AC and their respective committee or agency elected the members
to their office on the Committees and Agencies. Disenfranchising
such elders is contrary to our forefathers’ intention that we fulfill
our corporate mission through a “grass roots” principle of
government.

Third, the overture is contrary to wisdom: it is unwise to give
decision-making responsibility for our corporate administration

<a id="ga52-p346"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=348 printed_page=346 -->

exclusively to those who have no regular involvement in the
governance of our Committees and Agencies. Specifically as it
relates to the budgets of our Committees and Agencies. This
would prohibit every Committee and Agency from voting on their
OWN budget.

Finally, the overture is contrary to Christian charity. This
overture has come without any conversation or consultation with
affected elders, churches, presbyteries, Committees, or Agencies.
It seeks to disenfranchise brothers and fathers who have been

elected to fulfill responsibilities for our corporate mission. If there
are perceived problems to work through, Christian charity
requires us to first talk together as brothers in order to seek
understanding that helps us be mutually faithful to our vows and
to our Savior ( _BCO_ 8-3; Ordination Vow #6).

2. That **Overture 11** from South Texas Presbytery, “To Study Incorporating

Bitcoin into Session, Presbytery, and Committee & Agency Balance
Sheets,” be answered in the **NEGATIVE**, and grant TE Ed Dunnington
permission to speak to the Overtures Committee at General Assembly on
behalf of the Geneva Board of Directors;

Grounds:

The Overture is contrary to our history, practice, wisdom, and
Christian charity.

The proposed advice, that sessions, presbyteries, committees
and agencies study Bitcoin, is unusual in its narrow administrative
focus, and outside the mission of the Church and the General
Assembly. It is historically unprecedented to direct the collective
attention of the Church to a specific investment—not just an
investment category (cryptocurrency) but a specific product or
brand (Bitcoin)—to be included ina well-balanced portfolio. Not
only could such study detract local churches from proclaiming the
gospel, what property a church or presbytery should hold is
completely outside the purview of the General Assembly. The
Book of Church Order states churches “shall of have sole title to

its property, real, personal, or mixed, tangible or intangible” ( _BCO_
25-8), and “shall hold, own, and enjoy” ( _BCO_ 25-9) said property,
indicating churches are free to pursue any [lawful] investment or
not, without outside or top-down inducements.

As to committees and agencies of the Assembly, this would
represent an unwise, expensive, and highly intrusive impediment

<a id="ga52-p347"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=349 printed_page=347 -->

to the agencies’ work of supporting churches as they proclaim the
gospel. Further, how best to fund and carry out their mission under
policies determined by the Assembly—in this case, what specific
investment products to be purchased, and what to be studied or not
studied—should be left to the committees and the agency boards
the Assembly has elected. In fact, those elected members on
permanent committees and agency boards, particularly PCAF and
Geneva Benefits, offer the expertise necessary to determine and
oversee investment strategies, best financial practices, legal
compliance in these areas, particularly compliance with fiduciary
duties, and any research required to support those activities. Those
actions so moved and approved (along with business transacted)
at permanent committee and board meetings are already required
to be recorded in the minutes ( _RAO_ 4-21.d.3).

Further, there is the likelihood of legal liability for failing to
follow the “lawful commands” of the civil magistrate if Geneva
Benefits and other PCA agencies were to not consider the “just
and legal authority” ( _WCF_ 23, IV) of the civil magistrate. To
point, the Department of Labor (DOL) has clearly stated, in a
notice on March 10, 2022, that cryptocurrency investments fail to
satisfy the “prudent investor” rule which is the standard used for
fiduciary boards. Such a failure would result in the Employee
Benefits Security Administration conducting, “an investigative
program aimed at plans that offer participant investments in
cryptocurrencies and related products, and to take appropriate
action to protect the interests of plan participants and beneficiaries
with respect to these investments.” Such an investigation has the
great potential of resulting in DOL violations and legal liability
for local churches, the PCA Foundation, Geneva Benefits Group,
and even this General Assembly.

Lastly, in reference to Geneva, the PCA Corporate Bylaws
state (Article VI, section 1) that “No action of the General
Assembly shall conflict with the provisions of the…{Trust
Agreement(s)}”…the investment provisions of which are
overseen by counsel from fiduciary advisors, under guidance
provided by accounting & legal authorities, and ultimately
governed by the General Assembly-elected officers of the board
of Geneva Benefits Group, Inc.

<a id="ga52-p348"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=350 printed_page=348 -->

We, therefore, recommend that the overture be answered in
the negative.

3. That the minutes of the Geneva Benefits Group Board of Directors

meetings for dates September 26, 2024, September 27, 2024, November
15, 2024, and March 21, 2025 be approved without exception;

4. That the General Assembly approve the appointment of RE Eric

Burkholder to the Geneva Benefits Board of Directors to complete RE
Huey L. Townsend’s remaining term in the class of 2027;

Grounds:

The PCA Corporate Bylaws VI-I lays out provisions
specifically related to Geneva. It says, "A vacancy on the Board
of Directors because of death, resignation, removal,
disqualification, or otherwise, may be filled by the directors for
the unexpired portion of the term subject to the approval of the
next General Assembly."

5. That the General Assembly encourage PCA Presbyteries, churches, and

related ministries to review and utilize the PCA Call Package Guidelines
in creating compensation packages for Teaching Elders;

6. That the General Assembly encourage member churches to participate in

an annual offering to Geneva Relief or to budget regular benevolence
giving to support relief activities through the Geneva Relief Fund;

7. That the proposed budget for 2026 Geneva Benefits Group be approved;

8. That the Trustee Fee Agreements for the Retirement Plan Trust, the Health

and Welfare Benefit Trust, and the Charitable Relief Trust for 2026 be
approved.

**REPORT OF THE**

**COMMITTEE ON MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA**

_See 52-25 (p. 44) for the report of the Committee of Commissioners_

_and the Assembly’s action_

_The glory that you have given me I have given to them, that they may be one_
_even as we are one, I in them and you in me, that they may become perfectly_
_one, so that the world may know that you sent me and loved them even as you_
_loved me._ John 17:22-23

**Growing Together for the Harvest**
_**One Church, One Mission: It Takes All of Us**_

In 2024, Mission to North America (MNA) witnessed significant strides
toward our 10-year vision of facilitating the growth of the PCA to 3,000
congregations by 2033. Through the dedication of our presbyteries, churches,
and congregants, the tireless efforts of our staff, and the grace of God, we have
successfully helped to plant new churches, strengthen existing ones, and
expand the diversity of the PCA.

This report highlights the key data points and stories that demonstrate the
impact of your generous contributions in 2024.

- **40 new churches planted**

- **150+ future church planters trained**

- **22 churches joined new Chinese church planting network**

- **1,500+ volunteers mobilized in response to disasters in FL, GA,**
**NC, SC, TN**

**54 new English as a Second Language (ESL) Ministries started**

- **333 Chaplains endorsed through MNA Chaplain Ministries**

**8,000 Ministry to State (MTS) ministry activities with government**
**officials and staff**

<a id="ga52-p356"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=358 printed_page=356 -->

These are just a few examples of the many ways MNA is working to serve the
PCA for its continued growth in faithfulness to reach their communities and
the world for Christ.

**Refined Focus on Church Planting and Vitality in 2024**
MNA’s charge from the General Assembly is found in _Rules of Assembly_
_Operation_ ( _RAO_ ) 6-2, “The affairs of the church involved in its extension in
the United States and Canada are assigned to the Committee on Mission to
North America, whose duties and authority shall be designated by the General
Assembly.”

Over the past three years, in coordination with our Permanent Committee,
MNA has been on a steady and deliberate trajectory of bringing clarity and
sharpened focus to our purpose and mission based on _RAO_ 6-2. We believe
that in our secular age it is crucial for MNA to help our church engage the
gospel opportunity presented by the current cultural climate.

Below is an abbreviated timeline of that process:

_**September 2021 – June 2022**_ _: Focused on unifying purpose, mission, and_
_values for MNA._

**MNA Purpose** : Cultivating Kingdom Advancement through the PCA in
North America

**MNA Mission** : MNA provides expertise with excellence to all PCA
churches, presbyteries, and networks in North America by offering a suite
of practical ministry resources – training, equipping, and assisting in
missional purpose, serving communities to advance God’s kingdom.

**MNA Core Values** : Service, Collaboration, Clarity, Adaptability

_**September 2022 – June 2023**_ _: Focused on vision to serve the PCA for growth_
_and clarify the “two-pronged” priority for MNA._

**MNA Vision** : By resourcing our churches for kingdom impact, MNA will
facilitate the growth of the PCA to 3,000 congregations in 2033. By the
grace of God our church will prayerfully accomplish this by planting new
churches, reducing church closures, expanding the diversity of the PCA,

<a id="ga52-p357"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=359 printed_page=357 -->

partnering with denominational committees and agencies, and enfolding
existing congregations into the PCA.

**Strategic Priorities** :

1. Ongoing Focus and Commitment to Church Vitality: As our
churches grow in health and the rhythm of renewal through the
means of grace, our heart for the lost and commitment to neighbor
love in Jesus’s name will also grow.
2. Multiplying Church Planting: The multiplication of churches that
will be faithful to the Scriptures, true to the Reformed faith, and
obedient to the Great Commission will take the prayer-saturated,
collaborative effort of our whole denomination.
(See CMNA Report _Minutes of the 50_ _[th]_ _General Assembly_ )

_**September 2023 – June 2024**_ _: Refined focus on church planting and church_
_vitality._

1. **Church Planting** . To increase church planting across the
denomination, MNA is focused on recruiting church planters,
assessing church planters, and equipping church planters.

a. Added Rural Church Planting Initiative and City Lights
Chinese Ministry.

2. **Church Vitality** . To equip the church to thrive, MNA is seeking to
expand the presence of our ministries within every presbytery to
ensure that churches have access to ministry resources that will
support and build each congregation. This effort to serve our
churches for their health is intended to help to reduce the number of
church closures the PCA experiences each year and to enable existing
churches to grow in missional health as they proclaim the Gospel to
their neighbors.
(See CMNA Report _Minutes of the 51_ _[st]_ _General Assembly_ )

As a part of the ongoing refined focus, in 2024 we assessed ministry
alignment around church planting and vitality (outward facing
mobilization of church members). This resulted in some of our ministries
transitioning to a recommended ministry status and not being listed as an
MNA ministry. Those ministries included Bent Tree, Coaching Services,

<a id="ga52-p358"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=360 printed_page=358 -->

LAMP Seminary, and SecondCareer. We believe that these ministries are
important and needed in our church and we are committed to helping
them flourish. These changes, though hard, are the culmination of our
multi-year trajectory and we believe are necessary for MNA to grow in
faithfulness to our charge from the General Assembly for the expansion
of the kingdom in the U.S. and Canada. Please pray for all our ministries
and leaders, those transitioning out and those moving forward under the
MNA umbrella. Pray that Christ would be glorified, and the church would
be blessed by all that we do.

**Impact in 2024**

_**1.**_ _**Church Planting.**_ To catalyze and increase church planting across
the denomination, MNA is focused on assessing, equipping,
supporting and resourcing church planters and church planting
churches.

- **a.** In 2024, 40 new churches were planted and 150+ future church planters were trained. The number of new churches planted in 2024 may be higher because not every presbytery provides its church planting information.
- **b.** MNA’s Church Planter Assessment Center held five church

planter assessments, meeting in Lakeland, FL, Charlotte, NC,
St. Louis, MO, Columbus, OH, and Denver, CO. 30
couples/individuals were assessed in 2024.
c. 2024 was the first year for MNA’s new Church Planter
Preparation (CPP) Training. This preparation event is
designed for church planters and aspiring church planters.
Participants are able to select one of six tracks for training, (1)
discerning the call to church planting, (2) preparing to plant a
church, (3) establishing a presence to gathering a launch team,
(4) gathering a team to launching a plant, (5) launching a plant
to particularization, and (6) growing from particularization to
reproduction. The March 2024 CPP was held at Trinity
Presbyterian Church in Lakeland, FL and the November 2024
event in Atlanta, GA. Particularly notable was that the
November CPP was the first joint MNA and MTW
collaboration on church planting.
d. Our church planting team is conducting a thorough evaluation
of the Assessment Center. This work is also being done with

<a id="ga52-p359"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=361 printed_page=359 -->

MTW as we focus on greater collaboration between the two
Committees.

e. MNA City Lights Chinese Ministries helped to establish two
new Chinese fellowships and grew the network to 22 churches
and 50 pastors.
_**2.**_ _**Church Vitality.**_ To equip the church to thrive, MNA is seeking to
expand the presence of our ministries within every presbytery to
ensure that churches have access to ministry resources that will
support and build each congregation. A few of the ministry highlights
are below.

a. The PCA Unity Fund raised $76,588.63 to support the raising
up of ethnic minority leaders in the PCA. This meant an
increase in scholarships for theological education and General
Assembly commissioner registration fees.

- **b.** MNA African American Ministries continued to grow its virtual cohorts to support the raising up of future leaders and church planters.
- **c.** MNA Chaplain Ministries serves our church in obedience to the Great Commission by providing ordained Teaching Elders to serve as chaplains in military and civilian organizations. In

2024, 333 PRCC chaplains were endorsed and 170 chaplains
and their wives attended the annual chaplain training held
prior to the opening of the 51 [st] General Assembly.
d. MNA Disaster Response coordinated the PCA response to
Hurricanes Helene and Milton. MNA Disaster Response
teams have been the tip of the spear in the response; firstresponse teams were deployed as soon as access was granted
to each area as the storms moved from the Gulf, through FL,
GA, SC, NC, and into TN. At the end of 2024 major recovery
operations were still ongoing in GA, NC, and TN. Many PCA
congregations and communities served by the PCA were
affected; recovery is slow. Many PCA physical plants have
extensive issues such as flooding, roof damage, residual
mold/mildew damage from a combination of water intrusion
and disrupted electrical service. Some PCA pastors have
major damage to their homes. Many PCA communing
members and regular attenders have suffered loss. In response
to these events in NC, TN, GA and FL over 1,500 volunteers
were mobilized through the end of 2024. Over 500 PCA

<a id="ga52-p360"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=362 printed_page=360 -->

congregations provided financial resources, volunteer
mobilization, and prayer support during these responses.
Additionally, the 2024 Thanksgiving Offering gifts went to
MNA Disaster Response.

- **e.** MNA English as a Second Language (ESL) Ministry equips churches to reach immigrants, international college students and refugees with the Gospel of Jesus Christ in both word and deed. In 2024, 54 new ESL Ministries were started in local churches and 66 ESL trainings were conducted.
- **f.** MNA Ministry to State (MTS) ministers to those serving in government. 8,000 ministry activities were held with government officials and staff. 720 congregations were impacted through training and equipping with MTS.
- **g.** MNA Native American & First Nations Ministries was able to helpfully fund a ministry resource center ina native community. Two church building projects for Native outreach were fully funded without taking on loan debt.

**MNA Senior Leadership Growth:**
In November 2024, Mrs. Angela Pacey was appointed as the new MNA
Refugee and Immigrant Ministry (RIM) Director. Prior to her appointment to
this role, Angela served as the team leader from Christ Covenant Church in
Matthews, NC as they stepped out in faith and obedience to serve the Afghan
refugee community that had settled in their area. That team expanded to over
400 volunteers serving 120 Afghan families in the greater Charlotte area.
Angela holds multiple graduate degrees, including a master’s degree in
religion from Gordon Conwell Theological Seminary. angela is a wife of 36
years, a mother of four adult children, and a proud grandmother.

Pat Hatch, who was the pioneer of MNA Refugee and Immigrant Ministries in
2014 has transitioned to MNA Refugee and Immigrant Ministry (RIM)
Director Emeritus. We extend our immense gratitude to Pat for her many years
of faithful leadership of this ministry.

**MNA Resources for Church Planting & Vitality:**
MNA continues to be committed to pursuing a heart for gospel impact through
the PCA in every region, community, and people group of North America. We
long for the Spirt to continue the good work of MNA and are excited to press

<a id="ga52-p361"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=363 printed_page=361 -->

forward in that direction. For the details of MNA Ministries, services, and
resources available for church planting and vitality visit: www.pcamna.org.

For the list of PCA church planters placed on the field in 2024, see attachment
1.

**What Are We Asking You to Do?**

1. **Pray** . We invite every church, presbytery, committee, and agency
to join us in concerted prayer that the Lord grant us the unity,
collaboration, love, sacrifice, and perseverance that’s required to
pursue this vision.
2. **Give** . The programs, staff, and projects required to grow to 3,000
churches will take the generosity of God’s people, united in
pursuit of this vision. Our budget for this vision is $4.6M. We are
inviting you to give to this good work. Please consider giving to
the MNA General Fund, our ministries, and the PCA Unity Fund.
To give online, go to www.pcamna.org and select the “Give”
button. You may also mail donations to: MNA, PO Box 890233,
Charlotte, NC 28289-0233.
3. **Engage** . Reach out to us. Let us serve you for church vitality and
planting by making use of the resources we have available for that
purpose. MNA’s various ministries are wonderful resources for
established PCA churches and church plants. We help equip and
train churches and congregants for faithful kingdom mission. We
invite you to engage with MNA ministries, enabling us to come
alongside you as you seek to faithfully love neighbors in Jesus’

name.

**We are One Church. We have One Mission. And it will take all of us.** May
Christ be glorified through our efforts! And may he bless us to be unified as
we bear witness to his might and love in this world!

TE Irwyn L. Ince, Jr.
MNA Coordinator

<a id="ga52-p362"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=364 printed_page=362 -->

A. **MNA Stewardship and Finances: 2024 Progress**

**Ministry Ask/Askings Giving:**

MNA was supported in 2024 by 952 churches giving $5,715,075 and
4,930 individual donors giving $5,691,435. MNA requests that
churches give the Ministry Ask of $28 per member, if giving o nape r
capita basis. If all churches gave $28 per member, all projects would
be funded without individual fundraising by project leaders.

MNA requests that churches give to all PCA Committees and
Agencies at the Ministry Ask level. Because many churches do not
contribute at the Ministry Ask level, MNA senior staff members seek
designated support for their personal support and programs. Churches
have responded generously to these additional requests for support,
providing significantly greater resources for ministry. Contact MNA
Executive Coordinator TE Murray Lee <mwlee@pcanet.org or 678825-1250> or MNA Church Relations Director RE Stephen Lutz
<slutz@pcanet.org or 828-242-1440> for further information on
financial support for MNA.

B. **Funding for Church Planting and Other Projects:**

- All church planters are supported by gifts designated for their
particular church planting projects.

- Church planters who do not have a strong personal PCA network
require a special priority for project support as we trust God for
much greater ministry among the many people groups of North
America. MNA strongly encourages churches to give a high
priority to church planters who do not have a background in the
PCA.

- Five Million Fund for Church Buildings: providing interest-free
loans of up to $100,000, this fund continues to be a helpful source
for churches as they put together funding packages for their initial
building programs. This is a revolving fund, supported by loan
repayments, a swell as by donations.

C. **Thanksgiving Offering:** MNA is grateful to the Lord for more than

$71,500 given to the 2024 Thanksgiving Offering and commends all
PCA **who gave in support of MNA Disaster Response** in aiding those
impacted by Hurricane Helene and Hurricane Milton.

<a id="ga52-p363"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=365 printed_page=363 -->

**R** **ECOMMENDATIONS**

1. That having reviewed the work of the MNA Coordinator during 2024

according to the General Assembly guidelines, **the MNA Permanent**
**Committee commends TE Irwyn Ince for his excellent leadership,**
**with thanks to the Lord for the good results in MNA Ministry during**
**2024 and recommends his re-election as MNA Coordinator for**

**another year.** Attachment 2 provides a complete list of MNA staff; see
the GA Directory for the list of MNA Permanent Committee members.

2. That the proposed budget for 2026 for MNA Permanent Committee be

approved.

3. That the minutes of Mission to North America for March 6-7, 2024, May

13, 2024, and October 30-31, 2024, be approved without exception.

4. That **RE Maj Gen Bentley B Rayburn**, USAF, Ret. and **TE Ch (Lt Col)**

**Chad Steven Montgomery**, USAF, be appointed to serve as PCA
members of the Presbyterian and Reformed Commission on Chaplains and
Military Personnel (PRCC) for the Class of 2029.

5. That the MNA Committee recommends to the General Assembly that

##### Overture 2 from South Florida, “Expand Boundaries of South Florida

Presbytery," effective July 1, 2025, be answered in the **AFFIRMATIVE** .

6. That the MNA Committee recommends that the General Assembly

approves the United Reformed Churches North America (URCNA)
application for full membership on the Presbyterian and Reformed
Commission on Chaplains and Military Personnel (PRCC).

7. That the MNA Committee recommends to the General Assembly that

##### Overture 9 from James River Presbytery , “ Amend BCO 14-1 to Make

AC Members Appointed by Committees and Agencies Non-voting
Advisory Members,” be answered in the **AFFIRMATIVE** .

8. That the MNA Committee recommends to the General Assembly that

##### Overture 17 from Mississippi Valley Presbytery, “Change the Boundaries

of Covenant and Mississippi Valley Presbyteries,” and **Overture 20** from
Covenant Presbytery, “Change the Boundaries of Covenant and
Mississippi Valley Presbyteries,” be answered in the **AFFIRMATIVE** .

9. That **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery, “Erect Ad Interim

Committee to Receive Suggestions for Permanent Committee and Agency
Operations, Resources, and Expenses,” be answered in the **NEGATIVE** .

<a id="ga52-p364"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=366 printed_page=364 -->

Grounds:

Pursuant to _RAO_ 9-1 ad interim committees may be erected
solely to “handle particular matters of business as designated by
the Assembly.” The ad interim committee proposed in the
overture only provides generalized directives, not specific
deliverables or outcomes, and does not speak to “particular
matters of business.” The proposed committee would be
instructed to solicit and receive recommendations with no criteria

for the source of those recommendations, how they will be
solicited, evaluated, or reported. It is also already the case that any
presbytery, session, officer of the church, or congregant who has
suggestions for a Permanent Committee or Agency is already
welcomed to send suggestions to said Committee or Board.
Further, per _RAO_ 4-11, the budget of MNA is reviewed and
approved by the Permanent Committee and the Administrative
Committee, whose members have been elected by the General
Assembly. That budget is then reviewed by the MNA Committee
of Commissioners, along with the annual audit (per _RAO_ 14-7)
before being presented for approval by the General Assembly. The
constitutional checks and balances are already in place, and the
proposed ad interim committee would add inefficiency and
confusion.

10. That **Overture 28** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery, “Demand Mission

to North America (MNA) to Apologize and Terminate Personnel,” be
answered in the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

1. We unanimously reaffirm the public apology of our
Coordinator Irwyn Ince from Feb 12, 2025 which said:

Mission to North America (MNA) serves the
PCA for the advancement of Christ’s kingdom through
our churches in the U.S. and Canada. We do this

through a two-pronged focus on church planting and
church vitality. Our church planting efforts facilitate
starting and supporting new PCA churches. Our
church vitality efforts provide ministry resources that
enable our churches to grow in their outward facing
missional engagement in their communities, loving
neighbors as our Lord commands (Matt 22:39). One

<a id="ga52-p365"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=367 printed_page=365 -->

such long-standing MNA ministry is our Refugee and
Immigrant Ministry (RIM).

Recently, we posted links on our webpage to
external organizations offering a wide range of
recommendations and frequently asked questions on
immigration. MNA’s leadership erroneously allowed
the posting of content that advised undocumented
persons on ways to avoid being detained by
authorities. We affirm that it is our Christian duty to
obey the lawful commands of the civil magistrate and
be subject to their authority (WCF 23.4; Romans 13:14; 1 Peter 2:13-14). to counsel otherwise is a sin. We
confess that we fell short of our Biblical and

Confessional standard. We repent and apologize. We
also apologize for causing confusion and consternation
in our church. We have removed all the previously
posted information from our website.

MNA’s Refugee & Immigrant Ministry, along
with other MNA ministries, seeks to serve our
churches with resources that enable them to faithfully
follow our Lord’s call to love the stranger. However,
MNA does not engage in providing legal advice,
political campaigning or partisan positions. The
information we post going forward will provide
ecclesiastical resources in line with our Confession for
guidance on the matter.
2. Toward the end of online postings consistent with our
standards, an internet conduct policy was
unanimously adopted by the MNA Permanent
Committee at the 2025 MNA spring meeting. This
new policy will be reviewed for ratification by the 52 [nd]
General Assembly in June 2025.
3. With a new internet conduct policy and expectations
for MNA staff coming, the MNA Permanent
Committee still desires to express our deep
appreciation for MNA leadership, MNA staff and

<a id="ga52-p366"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=368 printed_page=366 -->

ministry leaders, and their commitment to kingdom
and mercy ministries on behalf of the Presbyterian
Church in America.

11. That **Overture 36** from the Georgia Foothills Presbytery, "Change the

Boundaries of Georgia Foothills and Metro Atlanta Presbyteries," be
answered in the **AFFIRMATIVE** pending the corresponding overture
from Metro Atlanta Presbytery.

<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=369 printed_page=367 -->
