<a id="ga52-p463"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=465 printed_page=463 -->

**REPORT OF THE**

**PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

**FOUNDATION, INC.**

_See 52-21 (p. 28) for the report of the Committee of Commissioners_

_and the Assembly’s action_

The PCA Foundation is pleased to report that, by God’s grace, the PCA
Foundation engaged in 2024 in significant work of the Church as set forth in
the Great Commission. We are pleased to see how the Lord continued to
enable the PCA Foundation to advance and manifest His Kingdom.

Total gifts to the PCA Foundation during 2024 were $100.9 million. These
gifts include a number of gifts of appreciated non-cash property (real estate, S
corporation stock, partnership interests, etc.).

We are pleased to report that the PCA Foundation distributed, or granted to
ministry, $71.7 million during 2024. Distributions to PCA churches were
$23.2 million, distributions to PCA Committees and Agencies were $4.2
million, and distributions to other Christian ministries were $44.3 million.

We continue to look for opportunities to work with PCA churches and their
members, and other individuals and families, to increase giving to advance and
reveal the Kingdom. Among other initiatives, we intend to continue and
enhance the free Smart Giving Workshops for, and offer smart giving
resources such as a planned giving website to, PCA churches in 2025.

The 2024 distributions and grants to ministry by the PCA Foundation were as
follows:

12/31/2024
Mission to the World $ 542,602
Mission to North America 591,669
Committee on Discipleship Ministries 21,152
Administrative Committee 517,562
Geneva Benefits Group 74,160

<a id="ga52-p464"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=466 printed_page=464 -->

Reformed University Fellowship 2,098,775
Covenant College 154,561
Covenant Theological Seminary 169,949
PCA Foundation 33,616
Ridge Haven 43,083
_Total Committees & Agencies_ $ 4,247,129

PCA Churches 23,152,505
Other Charitable Organizations 44,267,200

_Total_ $ 71,666,834

The PCA Foundation’s total assets were $407.4 million as of December 31,
2024, an increase of $150.6 million over the December 31, 2023 total assets
of $256.8 million. The primary reasons that total assets increased $150.6
million during 2024 were that gifts to the PCA Foundation to fund accounts
exceeded grant distributions by $29.1 million and asset valuation increased
$121.5 million due to financial market gains, including the increase in value
o fa certain kind of non-cash asset given in multiple installments over the years
2020 through 2024. Much of the funds contributed to the Foundation go out as
grants within a relatively short period of time — often within the same or the
following year. Therefore, the PCA Foundation may realize significant
amounts as both contributions and grants in a given year, meaning that total
assets may stay about the same from year to year; however, in some years the
Foundation may experience substantial increases or decreases.

The PCA Foundation has and continues to make significant progress in serving
with members and friends of the PCA. Over the last 20 years, total assets have
grown from $34.3 million to $407.4 million. These total assets provide a sound
base for future financial support of PCA Churches, PCA Committees and
Agencies, and other charitable organizations.

Coincident with the growth of its assets over the last 20 years, the PCA
Foundation has received $662.6 million in gifts, and made distributions to
charities totaling $317.4 million.

During 2024, the PCA Foundation continued its increased intentional outreach
to individuals and families, churches, Presbyteries and ministries.

<a id="ga52-p465"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=467 printed_page=465 -->

While focused primarily in 2024 on technology projects to achieve cost
savings and greater service to donors, creation of full Smart Giving Workshop
materials, and improvements in and streamlining of operations, the president
and development staff of the Foundation continued outreach to donors and
prospects and presented the Foundation’s work and services to churches and
individual prospects and donors. In 2025, the Foundation will continue
presenting Smart Giving Workshops, both live and via Zoom, sponsored by
individual PCA churches.

In response to the COVID-19 pandemic in early 2020, the Foundation
introduced an online giving program for PCA churches. For each church that
chooses to participate, the PCA Foundation provides the church with a link to
a secure online giving page to which it may direct its members and donors via
the church’s website or email or other notification methods. The PCA

Foundation imposes a very small charge to defray part of the costs of the
service, but no charge to churches that also maintain a church designated fund
with a substantial balance. Since the program’s inception, the PCA Foundation
has processed over $39.0 million in grants to PCA churches resulting from
over 110,000 donations.

The PCA Foundation does not participate in the PCA’s Partnership Shares or
Ministry Ask Programs, nor does it rely on the financial support of churches
to help underwrite its operating budget. Rather, its operations are funded
primarily by account charges, earnings on investment accounts, and by
charitable contributions from individuals, including current and former PCA
Foundation Board Members.

Because the main focus of the PCA Foundation is not on raising funds for its
own operations, or for any other particular ministry, it has a unique opportunity
and niche within the PCA. We assist Christians to carry out their charitable
desires, working with them to provide for and make substantial, effective
grants that support the propagation of the Gospel and reveal the work of the
Gospel in the hearts of believers. By suggesting and facilitating tax-efficient
giving strategies such as appreciated non-cash giving and accelerated giving,
we enable donors to convert significantly more income tax into Kingdom
funding. Our most popular giving-granting tools are the Advise & Consult
Fund® and Increase Fund, both of which are donor-advised funds. We also

<a id="ga52-p466"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=468 printed_page=466 -->

create, manage, and receive charitable-purpose funds, endowments, charitable
trusts, bequests, and church, presbytery, and PCA-ministry designated funds.

The PCA Foundation is “donor driven,” which means that it engages donors
to identify and recommend the grants it makes. This is grass-roots,
democratized grant-making. But the Foundation also works increasingly to
make those donors better grant advisors by informing them of charitable
endeavors worthy of support, and to provide opportunities for donors to give
for the support of foreign missions work, the education of seminary students,
and poor and distressed individuals and families. We provide information to
individuals without pressuring them to give to the PCA Foundation for its own
operations, or to any other particular ministry.

The PCA Foundation will continue to strive to effectively serve with its present
and future donors and the churches, presbyteries, committees, and agencies of
the PCA to carry on the work of the PCA as set forth in the Great Commission.
By God’s grace, the PCA Foundation will be able to do so.

Please continue to pray that I will be faithful as the Foundation president and
will experience and live out the love of Christ in this calling. Also, please pray
for the board and growing staff of the Foundation as they seek to continue
leading the PCA Foundation successfully into the future. The PCA Foundation
is financially sound and is postured well for continued growth.

**T** **HE** **PCA** **F** **OUNDATION** **’** **S** **R** **ECOMMENDATIONS ARE** **:**

1. That the General Assembly approve the proposed 2026 Budget of the PCA

Foundation, Inc. with the understanding that it is a spending plan and will
be modified as necessary by the PCA Foundation’s Board of Directors to
accommodate changing circumstances during the year.

2. That the Minutes of Board meetings of September 27, 2024, and April 2,

2025, be approved without exception.

3. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 9** from James River

Presbytery, “Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to Make AC Members Appointed by
Committees and Agencies Non-voting Advisory Members,” in the
**NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

Removing voting rights from Committees and Agencies bars
them from defending their budgets from potentially unreasonable

<a id="ga52-p467"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=469 printed_page=467 -->

or unfair impositions and directives by a central authority that
potentially is uninterested or self-interested, or unknowledgeable.
Moreover, so removing voting rights fails to diffuse power over
and increase accountability of the Committees and Agencies, and
in fact serves to concentrate such power and insulate from such
accountability.

The Administrative Committee has the power through its
influence on the General Assembly to exact from the other
Committees and Agencies payments to itself. Such exactions by
an ecclesiastical authority in which the payors of the exactions
have no representation is inherently unfair, and investing an
authority bereft of such representation with the power to make
them has proven historically to be unwise. Similarly, the
Administrative Committee has penultimate authority, below the
General Assembly, to approve the budgets of the Committees and
Agencies, which may not submit their own budgets. The
Administrative Committee, despite its sincere devotion to all the
ministries of the General Assembly, naturally is not as interested
in the ministry of any Committee or Agency as are the committee
men or directors of that Committee or Agency, and certainly not
as interested as it is in its own particular ministry. Likewise, AC
members are not as knowledgeable about how a Committee or
Agency operates, and what it needs to conduct its ministry, as are
the men elected by the General Assembly to govern that
Committee or Agency. Under Overture 9’s proposal, a bare
majority of AC members excluding representatives of the diverse
Committees and Agencies would be empowered to seek the
Administrative Committee’s particular interests, and act on their
own limited knowledge of Committee and Agency operations and
needs.

The Committees and Agencies have diverse interests, needs,
and judgments under their common interest in advancing the work
of the Great Commission. Naturally, then, they do not vote on the
Administrative Committee in lockstep. Moreover, the General
Assembly elects the men who compose the Committees and
govern the Agencies, men from every corner of the PCA. In
consequence of these facts, removing the vote of the Committees
and Agencies would neither diffuse power over them nor increase
their accountability to the elders of the PCA in General Assembly.

<a id="ga52-p468"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=470 printed_page=468 -->

Rather, removing their vote would concentrate power in a smaller
number of governors less representative of the entire Church,
more uniformly interested and knowledgeable, and more easily
co-opted by a single staff.

4. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 11** from South Texas

Presbytery, “To Study Incorporating Bitcoin into Session, Presbytery, and
Committee & Agency Balance Sheets,” in the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

The proposed directive or advice, that sessions, presbyteries,
committees and agencies study Bitcoin, is unusual in its narrow
administrative focus, and outside the mission of the Church and
the General Assembly. It is historically unprecedented to direct
the collective attention of the Church to a specific investment—
not just an investment category (cryptocurrency) but a specific
product or brand (Bitcoin)—to be included ina well-balanced
portfolio. What property a church or presbytery should hold is
completely outside the purview of the General Assembly. As to
committees and agencies of the Assembly, how best to fund and
carry out their mission under policies determined by the
Assembly—in this case, what specific investment products to be
purchased, and what to be studied or not studied—should be left
to the committees and the agency boards the Assembly has
elected. In fact, those elected members on permanent committees
and agency boards, particularly PCAF and Geneva, offer the
expertise necessary to determine and oversee investment
strategies, best financial practices, legal compliance in these areas,
particularly compliance with fiduciary duties, and any research
required to support those activities. Those actions so moved and
approved (along with business transacted) at permanent
committee and board meetings are already required to be recorded
in the minutes ( _RAO_ 4-21.d.3).

For example, the Geneva board already has considered
investment in cryptocurrency and determined that it would be a
violation of governing legal rules. The Department of Labor
(DOL) has clearly stated, ina notice on march 10, 2022, that
cryptocurrency investments fail to satisfy the “prudent investor”
rule which is the standard used for fiduciary boards. Such a failure
would result in the Employee Benefits Security Administration
conducting, “an investigative program aimed at plans that offer

<a id="ga52-p469"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=471 printed_page=469 -->

participant investments in cryptocurrencies and related products,
and to take appropriate action to protect the interests of plan
participants and beneficiaries with respect to these investments.”

Importantly, the study of this particular Bitcoin technology
called for by the overture would draw time and energy at every
level of the Church away from the mission of the Church,
proclaiming the Gospel of Christ. We believe that the overture
itself similarly diverts the time and attention of the General
Assembly away from the proclamation of that Gospel, which is its
express mission and sacred trust.

5. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia

Presbytery, “Erect Ad Interim Committee of Operations, Resources, and
Expenses,” in the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

The overture does not clearly apply to the PCA Foundation
since it proposes an ad interim committee to solicit and receive
recommendations “for all funded General Assembly works,” and
the Foundation is not funded by the General Assembly, nor is it
funded by lower courts. To the extent the overture in fact does
apply to the Foundation, an ad interim committee is unnecessary
since the General Assembly already has charged the Foundation
board of directors that it elects to solicit and receive

recommendations for improvement, optimization, and greater
transparency. That board of directors communicates to the
General Assembly through its committee of commissioners for the
Foundation the improvement actions it has required Foundation
management to take.

6. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 30** from Houston Metro

Presbytery, “Amend _BCO_ 8-4, 20-1, 21-1, 21-12, and 23-1 re the Calling
and Dissolution of TE Relationships for Needful Works,” with reference
to the recommendation of the Administrative Committee.

7. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 49** from Pacific Northwest

Presbytery, “Erect ana d interim committee on AI,” with reference to the
recommendation of the Administrative Committee.

<a id="ga52-p470"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=472 printed_page=470 -->

Respectfully Submitted,
Timothy W. Townsend

President

<a id="ga52-p471"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=473 printed_page=471 -->

**REPORT OF THE**

**COMMITTEE ON REFORMED UNIVERSITY**

**FELLOWSHIP**

_See 52-26 (p. 49) for the report of the Committee of Commissioners_

_and the Assembly’s action_

**Introduction**

Ministry to colleges represents an essential part of the mission of the
Presbyterian Church in America. The college years have increasingly become
a time for questioning authority, scrutinizing absolutes, throwing out old
premises, and reinventing the self. Reformed University Fellowship delivers
the good news of Jesus Christ in the face of these unfolding campus narratives
to reach students for Christ and equip them to serve. The passion and vigor of
college students have proved, over the last 200 years, to affect the Church
globally, significantly engaging the world with mission and purpose. The story
of redemption playing out is more significant than any story we can imagine.

Reformed University Fellowship goes to the campus with a fixed theology and
a flexible methodology that allows us to carry the timeless truths of
Christianity to students across all demographics. We are not limited in how
and where we preach the Gospel, and we know that a large percentage of
people come to faith in Jesus between the age of 18 and 25. To engage the
current academic culture, Reformed University Fellowship sends ordained
PCA ministers to serve on the college campus, preach the Gospel of Christ,
build Christ’s Church, and ultimately prepare students to live all of life under
the Lordship of Christ. This is a concrete expression of our commitment to our
covenant children and our obedience to the Great Commission to reach

students for Christ and equip them to serve. The Permanent Committee for
Reformed University Fellowship wishes to thank all our churches,
presbyteries, and the General Assembly for their oversight, financial support,
prayers, and encouragement for our campus ministers, staff, and interns, who
have served on 188 campuses worldwide.

<a id="ga52-p472"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=474 printed_page=472 -->

**Reformed University Fellowship**
Reformed University Fellowship (RUF) offers the truth of God’s Word both
to covenant children, as well as students who might be searching for this truth.
Working within the context of the Church, we follow Christ’s lead as He builds
His Kingdom. Students are instructed in Evangelism and Missions, Growth in
Grace, Fellowship and Service, and a Biblical World- and-Life View. An
ordained PCA minister leads each RUF, actively working to accomplish goals
in these four major areas. RUF strengthens the Church by reaching students
who may not know Christ, as well as equipping those who know Him to serve,
and to love Jesus for a Lifetime.

**Reformed University Fellowship**
RUF is currently on 160 campuses and added 3 new works in 2024, Saint Louis
University, Tennessee State and Marquette. Lord willing, we anticipate the
launch of four new RUF campuses in the fall of 2025 including University of
North Georgia, Colorado School of Mines, Cal Poly San Luis Obispo, and
potentially Troy University.

**Reformed University Fellowship International**
RUF-I is currently on 26 campuses including a new work at the University of
Alabama. There is significant interest from the Pacific Northwest Presbytery
in having RUF-I at the University of Washington as our next start up.

**Reformed University Fellowship Global**
RUF-G continues to partner with MTW in coming alongside missionaries who
are seeking to reach and equip college students on campuses across the globe.
We currently have 8 RUF-G ministries with the prospect of more missionaries
partnering with us this year. In February, we held the first ever RUF Staff
Training week outside of the United States in Cape Town, South Africa. There
were 35 people in attendance including 3 current RUF-G campus ministers in
Africa, 3 potential RUF-G campus ministers in Africa, as well as African
pastors and MTW workers. There were six African countries represented. RUF
sent Pat Roach (Western Area Coordinator), Chuck Askew (RUF CM at NC
State), Matt Trexler (RUF CM at UCLA), and Chad Brewer (RUF-I/G
Assistant Coordinator) to provide the training.

**Ministry Distinctives**
Weekly large groups, small groups, and one-on-one staff-student meetings
provide the structure for campus ministry. Each type of meeting is essential in
ministering to college students. In large group meetings, students gather to
sing, pray, and hear the good news of Jesus taught from the scriptures. Small

<a id="ga52-p473"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=475 printed_page=473 -->

groups focus on study, prayer, and fellowship, and many are led by junior and
senior students under the direction of the campus minister, campus staff, and
interns. One-to-one meetings between students and staff members offer indepth discipleship, evangelistic encounters, accountability in trust- confidence
relationships, and counseling. RUF emphasizes the development of a biblical
world-and-life view. As students learn to think biblically, they will make a
lasting difference in the Church and the world. A key distinctive of RUF is its
connection to the Church. Through exhortation by their campus minister,
attendance with friends at local churches, involvement in the campus
community, and exposition of Biblical truth, college students learn to love the
Church and develop a lifelong commitment to involvement with God’s people.
RUF provides a bridge for maintaining (or establishing) a connection to the
Church as students transition from home to college to work and family life.
RUF does not exist to perpetuate a campus ministry but to grow the Church.

**Campus Interns, Staff, & Ministry Fellows**
Launched in 1980, the Intern Program has trained over 900 interns. In the last
30 years, the program has grown at a rate of 13% per year. Emily Nixon,
Assistant Vice President of Internships, continues to help lead the department,
and this year, we have 158 full-time and Two part-time young men and women
(all recent college graduates) who currently work directly with a campus
minister and receive on-the-job training in evangelism, small group leadership,
and one-on-one ministry. While interns minister to college students, they also
participate in a study program on biblical and theological training. After their
internship with Reformed University Fellowship, interns move into vocational
ministry and the broader marketplace with a deepened understanding of God’s
Word and experience in His service. The campus intern and campus staff are
equipped to be “an instrument for noble purposes, made holy, useful to the
Master and prepared to do any good work” (II Timothy 2:21). The Campus
Staff department within RUF exists to provide a long-term vocational ministry
position for women who desire to work on the college campus. While most of
our Campus Staff come directly from serving two years as an RUF intern, we
have begun expanding outside of that channel to bring in qualified candidates
to meet the growing demand on campus.

The RUF Campus Staff department held its annual assessment in January of
2025. Ten candidates were assessed, and the department hopes to hire 10 for
the campus staff position starting in June 2025. Casey Cockrum, Assistant
Vice President of Campus Staff, led the three-day assessment and had nine
other staff join her to help determine the candidate’s fit for the department. We
are so encouraged by these women joining the campus staff ranks. Their

<a id="ga52-p474"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=476 printed_page=474 -->

addition will bring the anticipated campus staff number to 80 across the
ministry. The Lord continues to bring us amazing women to serve in the
campus staff role, and we’re grateful for the continued growth.

The RUF Fellows Program is designed to provide financial support to current
and former RUF Campus Interns pursuing a seminary degree for future
vocational ministry. The program is intended to reward interns who have
demonstrated a passion for and have exhibited an ability to excel in ministry.
Like the RUF internship program, The Fellows Program encourages
participants to learn about ministry more so than to accomplish it. The Program
is open to all interns regardless of their current or prospective employment
relationship with RUF. Started in June of 2022, the RUF Fellows Program
currently has 39 participants and hopes to add 10 more starting in June.

**Summer Conference**
Summer Conference May 2025:
We are so excited to be back at Laguna Beach Christian Resort for SuCo2025!
We will be welcoming RUF students from all over the country for four weeks
this summer, with Florida Atlantic University Campus Minister Jeff Lee
serving as the Summer Conference Director.
WEEK 1 – May 5-10
WEEK 2 – May 12-17
WEEK 3 – May 18-23

**Wives Retreat**

We are incredibly grateful for the role wives play in RUF’s ministry to college
students, and Wives Weekend plays a vital role in encouraging them in their
marriages and ministry. We were glad to be together in Nashville, TN, January
16-19, 2025 for all Wives Weekend and Palm Spring February 6-9, 2025 for
West Wives Weekend.

**Staff Training**
December training in Denver for Campus Ministers and Campus staff had 253
people in attendance. RUF training attends to the development of the entire
person to create healthy ministers and ministries. The stated purpose of
training is “to train pastors/staff, so they learn to understand and do ministry
faithfully and fruitfully.” To that end, Gary Purdy (Senior Minister of North
Cross Church, Cornelius, NC) spent a day teaching on Glorification. We also
had sessions on Cross Cultural Preaching & Teaching, Pastoral Leadership
with Sessions and Churches and Personality in Ministry/Meta Thinking. RUF

<a id="ga52-p475"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=477 printed_page=475 -->

I staff training took place the first week of January in Denver, Dan Lim taught
the staff and focused on Evangelism and Outreach.

**RUF Assessment**

On Feb 11-13th, RUF held a winter assessment in St. Louis, MO on Covenant
Seminary’s campus, with 5 campus minister candidates. RUF continues to
collaborate with area coordinators and national staff to enhance the necessary
hard skills and emotional and cultural intelligence elements of assessment.
Pre-screening protocol has helped to say “no” in the application stage and
reduced unnecessary assessments’ costs and labor. We will hold our second
regional assessment July 8-10 in Atlanta, GA and anticipate several
candidates. We are building an assessor list of current and former campus
ministers and wives to help with the increased staffing needs of regional
assessments.

**RUF Cross-Cultural Advancement**

Over the past several months, RUF has continued to make meaningful strides
in cross-cultural development, focusing on integrating our theological vision/
Philosophy of ministry with practical ministry efforts on campus. We have
continued refining our big picture approach to training while also looking at
the more granular level of particular offerings to ensure that our staff are being
equipped with both theological depth and practical tools for a faithful, wellrounded ministry to the whole campus. These refinements have been well
received by our staff. Our growth may be slower than we desire, but we’re
moving together as a ministry- a sign of health and faithfulness.

Additionally, recruitment and retention efforts have been strengthened through
deeper collaboration with partner seminaries and campus ministers. We have
connected with graduating students, identified several promising candidates
for RUF internships and staff roles, and we have facilitated regional
recruitment events. Looking ahead, our focus is on a continual refinement of
our wide array of POM training modules, creative recruitment, and caring for
the staff we hope to retain for the longer-term ministry of RUF. We remain
committed to reaching the full diversity of our campuses with the gospel and
ask for your continued prayers as we work to strengthen and beautify Christ’s
church through these efforts. Thank you for your partnership in RUF’s
mission.

**Growth**
Income Growth – Overall Ministry

<a id="ga52-p476"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=478 printed_page=476 -->

<a id="ga52-p477"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=479 printed_page=477 -->

**Conclusion**
God is at work through the ministry of RUF. RUF strives to engage the
culture and carry out the kingdom’s priorities of the Church. God brings
together students and ministers from many different walks of life to
accomplish His purposes. We hope that each person influenced by
Reformed University Fellowship will, in turn, affect many other people
in the course of his or her life. The Church is strengthened as students
learn to love and seek out the Church and are trained to serve as future

church leaders.

**R** **ECOMMENDATIONS FOR THE** **2025** **G** **ENERAL** **A** **SSEMBLY VIA THE** **RUF**

**C** **OMMITTEE OF COMMISSIONERS**

1. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the Permanent

Committee on Reformed University Fellowship meetings on August 19,
2024, September 23, 2024, October 2, 2024, March 18 [th] and 19 [th], 2025,
and April 28 [th], 2025 without exception.

2. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the executive session

meetings of the Committee on Reformed University Fellowship on
October 2, 2024 and March 19, 2025 without exception.

3. That the proposed budget for 2026 for Reformed University be approved.

4. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 9** from the James River

Presbytery "To Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to Make AC Members Appointed by
Committees and Agencies Non-Voting Advisory Members” in the
**NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

The Overture contradicts our history, practice, wisdom, and
commitment to brotherhood.

- Our historical practices clearly show that our forefathers
believed that leaders who were elected to positions that
require regular responsibility for assessing the needs and
overseeing the work of the PCA’s Committees and
Agencies should have both voice and vote in decisions of
the Administrative Committee (AC) that impact their
ability to fulfill Assembly-assigned duties ( _RAO_ 5-3). For
the AC to carry out its mandate to “serve the General
Assembly and the whole denomination” ( _RAO_ 4-2; 5-4),
it is essential that those elected by the General Assembly
to oversee the work of Committees and Agencies retain

<a id="ga52-p478"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=480 printed_page=478 -->

voting power on matters related to the administration of
our corporate mission.

- The overture’s language creates the misleading
impression that some members of the AC are elected,
while others are only “appointed” by their respective
Committees or Agencies. This portrayal conflicts with
both our principles and actual practices. The General
Assembly elects not only the eleven at-large members of
the AC but also all the members representing Committees
and Agencies. Specifically, each elder who represents a
Committee or Agency on the AC has been elected to
represent the church at various levels:

o Every elder represents their local congregation
when elected to serve o na session ora sa pastor.
o any ruling elder o na committee or agency has
been elected by their presbytery to represent that
level of the church.

o Ruling and teaching elders on Committees or
Agencies are nominated and approved by their
presbytery for these roles.
o Finally, every ruling or teaching elder
representing a Committee or Agency on the AC
has been elected by the entire General Assembly
for that responsibility ( _RAO_ 5-3).
Disenfranchising these elders would also disenfranchise the
congregations and communities they represent, which runs
counter to our forefathers’ intention to uphold a "grassroots" form
of government in fulfilling our corporate mission.

5. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 11** from South Texas

Presbytery "To Study Incorporating Bitcoin into Committee and Agency
Balance Sheets" be answered in the **NEGATIVE.**

Grounds:

The proposal to direct sessions, presbyteries, committees, and
agencies to study Bitcoin is an unusual and narrow administrative
focus that lies outside the mission of the Church and the General

Assembly. It is historically unprecedented for the Church to direct
its collective attention to a specific investment, not merely an
investment category (cryptocurrency) but a particular asset
(Bitcoin), for inclusion in a balanced financial portfolio. Decisions

<a id="ga52-p479"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=481 printed_page=479 -->

regarding what property a church or presbytery should hold fall
entirely outside the scope of the General Assembly’s authority.

For the Assembly's committees and agencies, the
determination of how best to fund and carry out their mission
under policies set by the Assembly—including which specific
investment products to consider and whether to study or not study
them—should be left to the discretion of the committees and

agency boards that the Assembly has elected. The elected
members of permanent committees and agency boards, especially
those of PCAF and Geneva, possess the necessary expertise to
determine and oversee investment strategies, ensure best financial
practices, maintain legal compliance (including fiduciary duties),
and conduct any necessary research to support those activities.
Actions taken and approved in these areas, as well as business
transacted, are already required to be documented in the minutes
of permanent committee and board meetings ( _RAO_ 4-21.d.3).

Furthermore, the focus on studying Bitcoin, as proposed by
this overture, would divert time and energy from the core mission
of the Church—proclaiming the Gospel of Christ. We believe that
this overture itself shifts the attention of the General Assembly
away from its express mission, which is to faithfully uphold the
sacred trust of the Gospel.

6. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 18** from Gulf Coast

Presbytery "To Direct RUF to Update the 'Campus Ministry Manual' for
GA Approval" be answered in the **NEGATIVE.**

Grounds:

- RUF does not use a "Campus Ministry Manual." The
1979 "manual" referenced in the Overture was a
document drafted when a PCA campus ministry was in its
infancy and under the auspices of the GA's Ministry to the
US. It was primarily initiated and run by local
presbyteries. Twenty-four years ago, when RUM became
a GA Permanent Committee, maintaining or creating such
a manual was not required. If the GA had wanted such a
manual, the logical time to have codified such a
requirement would have been in 2000 at the 28th GA in
Tampa or in 2001 at the 29th GA in Dallas, or at least
shortly thereafter.

- There has never been a requirement, per se, for RUF to
have such a manual. This is clear from _RAO_ 4-21

<a id="ga52-p480"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=482 printed_page=480 -->

"Guidelines for Keeping Minutes of Permanent
Committees and Agencies of the General Assembly"
below.

j. An up-to-date copy of the bylaws and
manual of the Committee or Agency, _if_
_such exist_, should be kept with the
minutes. (emphasis added)

- Even if RUF used such a manual, the _RAO_ does not expect
the GA to approve its contents. That would require an
_RAO_ amendment through a two-thirds vote of the
Assembly. It cannot be accomplished simply through an
overture asking the GA to "direct" a Permanent
Committee to create and submit a manual for review and

approval. For example, the _RAO_ stipulates that the GA
approves the SJC Manual ( _RAO_ 17-5), and the _RAO_
contains operating rules for its Committee on Review of
Presbytery Records ( _RAO_ 16). However, there is no
requirement, for example, for ga to approve ana c
Manual. _RAO_ 10-1 says “The Assembly’s Administrative
Committee and the Local Arrangements Committee shall
operate under a Manual approved by the Administrative
Committee.” The same applies to the GA Nominating
Committee, which uses a manual that does not require GA
approval.

- The _RAO_ already stipulates that the minutes of a
Permanent Committee "shall include":

A recording of information sufficient to
demonstrate the Committee's or Board’s

implementation of instructions received
from the General Assembly and of
_material_ policies and _material_ policy
changes adopted by the Committee or
Board _in that year_ . ( _RAO_ 4-21.d.4;
emphasis added)
The GA-elected RUF Permanent Committee believes the

abovementioned _RAO_ 4-21 paragraph is sufficient.
Whenever the RUF Permanent Committee changes
various protocols, those actions are reported in
Committee Minutes that are reviewed annually by the GA
Committee of Commissioners. And exceptions taken to
those minutes would be reported through that Committee

<a id="ga52-p481"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=483 printed_page=481 -->

of Commissioners. Last year, the RUF Permanent
Committee reported a recommendation to the Assembly
that the GA approve a revised Affiliation Agreement. The
RUF Permanent Committee will continue to adhere to the

obligations of _RAO_ 4-21.d.4 unreservedly.

- Requiring the RUF Permanent Committee to produce a
lengthy operating manual that must be reviewed and
approved by an Assembly of over 2,000 commissioners
would be unnecessary and unwieldy. it would beak into
requiring sessions to recommend annual church budgets,
church employee manuals, child protection procedures,
etc. to the congregation for scrutiny and approval by the
members. That's why a congregation elects its elders. And
that's why permanent committee members are nominated
by presbyteries, reviewed by the GA Nominating
Committee, and then elected by the Assembly.

7. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia

Presbytery (“Erect Ad Interim Committee to Receive Suggestions for
Permanent Committee and Agency Operations, Resources, and
Expenses”) in the **NEGATIVE** _._

Grounds:

According to _RAO_ 9-1, an ad interim committee may only be
erected to “handle particular business matters.” The Overture does
not provide specific deliverables to be developed, only provides
generalized directives, and does not speak to “particular business
matters.” The committee would be instructed to receive

recommendations with no criteria for such results. According to
_RAO_ 4-11, the budgets of RUF (and other Committees and
Agencies) are reviewed and approved by the Permanent
Committee (elected members) and the Administrative Committee
(elected members) that already have a fiduciary responsibility
placed on them by the _Rules of Assembly Operation_ ( _RAO_ ). CRUF
reviews this through a subcommittee of the CRUF, the Committee
of Commissioners, and the Administrative Committee, before
being presented to the Assembly. The _RAO_ (14-7h) requires each
Committee of Commissioners to ensure the Committees and

Agencies provide an annual audit for review. Therefore, there are
already transparency and constitutional checks and balances in
place. Furthermore, our current ecclesial structure offers clear
means for lower courts to provide recommendations for

<a id="ga52-p482"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=484 printed_page=482 -->

improvement and optimization of program committees and
agencies.

8. That the General Assembly answer **Overture 30** from Houston Metro

Presbytery “Amend _BCO_ 8-4, 20-1, 21-1, 21-12, and 23-1 re the Calling
and Dissolution of TE Relationships for Needful Works” **by**
**DEFERRING without prejudice until the 53** **[rd]** **General Assembly in**
**2026.**

Grounds:

CRUF recognizes the needed clarity that this overture pursues.
This overture was received after our spring Permanent Committee
meeting and, although we were able to call a meeting to
preliminarily discuss it, we have not had sufficient time for needed
consultation around potential implication of issues addressed by
the overture. Where ‘needful works’ involve both ecclesial and

legal employment relationships we would appreciate the
opportunity to seek legal counsel so that RUF can best serve the
PCA. We are grateful that the author of the overture has consented
to receive input from the Committees and Agencies and RUF
would like to participate in that preparation for the 53 [rd] General
Assembly in 2026.

9. That the General Assembly receive Coordinator RE Will Huss’

resignation with gratitude and thanksgiving to God for the many ways He
has used Will to bless RUF, the PCA, and the Church at large.

10. That the General Assembly elect RE Andrew Rein as Coordinator of

Reformed University Fellowship for the 2025/2026 term.

<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=485 printed_page=483 -->

**VI. Recommendations concerning received reports alleging important**
**delinquency and/or grossly unconstitutional actions.**

**2025-01** _(See VIII.76.e)_ _(54-4-6)_
Find that the 30 September 2024 letter from TE Lupton, RE
Nusbaum and TE Eide is not a report of an “important
delinquency or grossly unconstitutional proceedings” ( _BCO_
40-5).

**2025-02** ( _See VIII.55.e_ ) _(_ 58-0-2 _)_
Find that the April 14, 2025, letter from Mr. Crouse does not
present a report o fan “important delinquency or grossly
unconstitutional proceedings” ( _BCO_ 40-5).

**2025-03** _(See VIII.43.e)_ _(_ 60-0-2 _)_
Find that the May 23, 2025, email from RE Dockery is not a
report of an “important delinquency or grossly
unconstitutional proceedings” ( _BCO_ 40-5).

**2025-04** _(See VIII.43.f)_ _(_ 60-0-2 _)_

<a id="ga52-p507"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=509 printed_page=507 -->

Find that the May 23, 2025, letter from RE Isbell is not a
report of an “important delinquency or grossly

**VII. General Recommendations**

1. Thank Dr. Bryan Chapell, Dr. Per Almquist, Alyssa Cho, Karen Cook,
Ashley Davis, Karen Frey, Heidi Harrison, Margie Mallow, Angela
Nantz, TE Billy Park and the rest of the AC staff who covered the
needs and requests of RPR in addition to their other duties. In
particular, we commend them for their attentive support, friendly
welcome, and support to the officers. _(61-0-0)_

2. Thank the staff of Mission to the World who allowed the Committee

to use their building, and who endured disruption in their workplace
during the meeting of the Committee. _(61-0-0)_

3. Commend the 2025 Committee on Review of Presbytery Records
officers: TE Jon Anderson, TE Steve Tipton, TE Jacob Gerber, and
TE Thomas Rickard for their work before and during the meeting of
the Committee. Without that work the Committee would not have

been able to accomplish its tasks. _(61-0-0)_

4. Commend TE Thomas Rickard and Ms. Alyssa Cho for their
tremendous labors in gathering and uploading the reports of the Read
Teams and developing a draft report of the Committee. Their work
dramatically increased the Committee’s efficiency. _(61-0-0)_

5. The General Assembly ask Presbyteries to take note of the following
issues that created problems for the Committee and for Presbyteries
during the 2025 review: _(59-2-0)_

- **a.** Failure to apply _BCO_ 38-3 when ate moves to a non-NAPARC call and/or failure to demonstrate whether or why presbytery used part a or part b of that paragraph.
- **b.** Failure to correct previous minutes (where possible) as part of

Presbytery’s response to a previous exception of substance ( _RAO_
16-10.b.1)
c. Appointing one or two men to fill positions on Committees of
Commissioners of the General Assembly in apparent violation of
_RAO_ 14-2 and _BCO_ 15-2.

<a id="ga52-p508"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=510 printed_page=508 -->

6. The General Assembly remind the brethren that the appropriate course
of action for dealing with alleged unconstitutional proceedings is
ordinarily through appeals ( _BCO_ 42) and complaints ( _BCO_ 43), rather
than invoking the standard required under _BCO_ 40-5. ( _61-0-0_ )

7. The Stated Clerk of the General Assembly send a letter to the
Presbyteries through the stated clerks of the Presbyteries to include the
following points: ( _61-0-0_ )

- **a.** Commend clerks for their hard work to create, keep, and submit their records, and every Presbytery who sent delegates to serve on the Committee on Review of Presbytery Records.
- **b.** Urge those presbyteries who failed to send delegates to do so in the future, noting the great learning opportunities that exist through participation in the review process. Note that 72 of 87 presbyteries had representatives appointed to the committee this year, with 65 attending the meeting (including 19 REs and 46

TEs).
c. Encourage presbyteries and clerks to review carefully the
“Guidelines for Keeping Presbytery Minutes” from _RAO_ 16-3
and the “Guidelines for Responding to General Assembly” from
_RAO_ 16-10, and to be sure that the requirements included in those
sections are reflected in their minutes.

d. Note the issues raised in recommendations 5 and 6.

e. Note the following list of the 8 most common exceptions of
substance found during the 2025 review process (which matters
accounted for over 50% of all exceptions), and urge clerks and
presbyteries to be particularly attentive to addressing these issues
in their minutes, including, if necessary, correction of minutes
from meetings in 2025 that occurred prior to the reception of this
letter.

i. Call-related exceptions (congregational/sessional approval,
arrangements, dissolutions)
ii. Incomplete examination records (candidates, licensure,
ordinations, transfers)
iii. Missing meeting minutes (executive, commission,
referenced attachments, etc.)
iv. No record of annual reports (interns, candidates, Out of
Bounds TEs)

<a id="ga52-p509"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=511 printed_page=509 -->

v. No or incomplete review of Session records
vi. Missing record of required voting thresholds
vii. Missing required administrative items (six-month
membership, ministerial obligation forms)
viii. No record of judgment of stated differences
f. Encourage presbytery stated clerks to attend the annual
Presbytery Clerks’ Meeting in December.

8. Ask the Stated Clerk to provide RPR, each year, a list of all SJC
decisions reported to Presbyteries in the year of the minutes being
reviewed, as well as those reported late enough in the prior year such
that it would be unlikely that a Presbytery could have recorded it in its
minutes) so compliance with _OMSJC_ 17.1.f can be monitored.
_(61-0-0)_

9. Amend _RAO_ but adding a new section in the _RAO_, numbered _RAO_ 1611: ( _28-25-0_ )
During a meeting of the Committee on Review of Presbytery
Records, a motion to _Limit or Extend Limits of Debate_ shall
not be permitted, but there shall be permitted a motion to order
the _Previous Question_, which motion shall be adopted upon
three-quarters (3/4) of the members present and voting.

10. Amend _RAO_ by adding the following at the end of _RAO_ 16-3.b:
_(59-1-1)_
Neither this official copy of the minutes nor any electronic
copy submitted to the General Assembly for review may
contain any material that must be accessed outside the
documents submitted. This includes material that must be

accessed through external links, websites, QR codes, filesharing services, or other external digital platforms.

The full text of _RAO_ 16-3.b, would be as follows,
The minutes should be neat and legible. Minutes should be
dated, and the pages numbered. An official copy of the
minutes should be printed and either bound or kept in a good
binder. Neither this official copy of the minutes nor any
electronic copy submitted to the General Assembly for review
may contain any material that must be accessed outside the

<a id="ga52-p510"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=512 printed_page=510 -->

documents submitted. This includes material that must be

accessed through external links, websites, QR codes, filesharing services, or other external digital platforms.

**Rationale:**

In recent years, many Presbyteries have moved to using
links, Dropbox, or QR codes to allow Presbyters to access
documents.

Sometimes the minutes of Presbytery then reference
documents that are part of the minutes simply by including
those links or codes.

When RPR is tasked with reviewing those minutes,
however, its members do not have access to those links or
codes. As a result, key parts of a Presbytery’s minutes are not
accessible for review.

Further, even if RPR is provided with those links or codes,
there is no way to ascertain whether the documents thus
accessed, assuming they are even still available at the link, are
the same as the documents that were to be part of the official
minutes.

This amendment simply ensures that RPR, and future
readers of the minutes, will have access to all the documents
Presbytery intended to include in the minutes.

11. Amend _RAO_ 16-3.e by adding a new item 6 and renumbering current
items 6-9 accordingly, with the new item to read as follows: _(58-1-0)_
Minutes of Presbytery examinations for ordination ( _BCO_ 214) and/or transfer ( _BCO_ 13-6) must record that Presbytery
accomplished the requirements of _BCO_ 21-4.e. A statement
such as “The examination in experiential religion ( _BCO_ 214.c.(1)(a)) covered all the items specified in section e of _BCO_
21-4” would be sufficient.

**Rationale:**

The _RAO_ (16-3.e.5 and 16-3.e.6) already requires that
Presbytery minutes list “all specific requirements and trials”
for licensure, ordination, and transfer ( _BCO_ 21-4.a through d),
as well as specifics as to how Presbytery dealt with a man’s
stated differences ( _BCO_ 21-4.f and g). Given that, it seems

<a id="ga52-p511"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=513 printed_page=511 -->

reasonable that the minutes of Presbyteries should
demonstrate compliance with _BCO_ 21-4.e).

The PCA spent several years developing the language of
_BCO_ 21-4 as a means of dealing with a concern that was
troubling the denomination. Given that importance it is
reasonable that the General Assembly should have a
mechanism for making sure presbyteries are complying with
the provision.

The intent is not to prescribe _how_ Presbyteries accomplish
the requirements of _BCO_ 21-4.e)

Unlike _RAO_ 16-3.e.5, the proposed language for the new
_RAO_ 16-3.e.6 does not require the minutes to list the
accomplishment of every specific requirement of _BCO_ 214.e. Thus, the proposed provision does not mean the minutes
must list every item covered in _BCO_ 21-4.e or demonstrate
how Presbytery carried out its responsibility to give “careful
attention” to those issues.

**VIII. Report Concerning the Minutes of Each Presbytery**
_Ruling Elder Jay Neikirk and Teaching Elder Paul Lee, members of the_
_Standing Judicial Commission, abstained from voting on all portions of the_
_reports for Blue Ridge Presbytery, Central Georgia Presbytery, Highlands_
_Presbytery, and Ohio Valley Presbytery. While they generally refrained from_
_participating in discussion, they did so specifically because one or more_
_exceptions pertained to constitutional matters or were related to cases_
_presently or previously before the Commission._

**d.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**satisfactory:**
**2023-08: Apr 29, 2022; Aug 26, 2022** ( _BCO_ 15-2) —
Presbytery’s “Administrative Commission” does not meet the
minimum requirement for a commission.
**Response:** Error noted and corrected at 24 August
meeting with adoption of change to Standing Rules and
addition of required RE to the AC.
**Rationale:** Change to standing rules does not result
in a constitutional quorum.
**Response:** Agree. The previous Standing Rules
amendment did not ensure a constitutional quorum.
Presbytery has been operating by a constitutional quorum
since the previous amendment; September Presbytery voted
to amend Standing Rules again to ensure AC permanently
fulfills the requirements of 15-2.
**2023-09: Apr 29, 2022; Aug 26, 2022** ( _RONR_ (12 [th] ed.) 8:110; 9:31-36; Standing Rules) — Presbytery’s “Administrative
Commission” conducted business and effectively approved a
summary of multiple electronic videoconference sessions
over a several month span, but without adhering to the rules
of an official meeting.
**Response:** Actions taken outside the confines of
deliberative meetings on those dates ratified at 24 August
Presbytery.
**Rationale:** The corrective action (pg 75, Item 7) did
not ratify specific actions, but sought to legitimize the

<a id="ga52-p513"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=515 printed_page=513 -->

meetings. Response did not address how the presbytery will
remedy this going forward.
**Response:** Agree. September Presbytery ratified
specific actions of AC on both dates, as noted in its minutes.
Presbytery and its AC will conduct future meetings IAW the
_BCO_ rules and the _RONR_ .
**2023-10: Apr 29, 2022; Aug 26, 2022** ( _BCO_ 15-1) — No
record of quorum for commission meetings.
**Response:** Actions taken outside the confines of
deliberative meetings on those dates ratified at 24 August
Presbytery.
**Rationale:** The corrective action (pg 75, Item 7) did
not ratify specific actions, but sought to legitimize the
meetings. Response did not address how the presbytery will
remedy this going forward.
**Response:** Agree. September Presbytery ratified
specific actions of AC on both dates, as noted in its minutes.
Presbytery and its AC will conduct future meetings IAW the
_BCO_ rules and 15-1.

**2024-01: General 2023** ( _BCO_ 13-12; _RAO_ 16-3.c.1) —
Notice for called meeting not in order (call not recorded in
minutes).
**Response:** Agree. Notice and minutes of called
meetings included at September Presbytery. Omission of
these items to RPR was due to oversight of Stated Clerk, who
promises to do better.
**2024-02: Mar 30, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4.c; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Incomplete record of ordination exam.
**Response:** Disagree. 30 March 2023 called meeting
was for the purpose of examining a transfer candidate. The 30
March 2023 minutes reflect he was examined in all areas

required by _BCO_ 13-6. Error on this matter was due to the
negligence of the Stated Clerk, who did not include the
minutes to RPR. He promises to be more diligent in his
submissions to RPR.

**unsatisfactory, therefore new responses shall be submitted**
**to the following GA:**

<a id="ga52-p514"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=516 printed_page=514 -->

**2023-16: Aug 26, 2022** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —

26 August 2022 did not properly reflect that. In the future,
Presbytery will be more detailed in our Presbytery minutes.
**Rationale:** Response does not indicate that the
record has been corrected, nor does it explain how a
committee can fulfill the examination requirement.

**2024-01: Apr 28, 2023** ( _BCO_ 18-2) — No record of sixmonths membership for candidate.
**Response:** Blue Ridge Presbytery agrees with this
exception and will seek to be more careful in the future, We
will correct our minutes accordingly.
**2024-02: Apr 29, 2023** ( _BCO_ 12-1) — Presbytery improperly
cited the session of [Church Name Omited] for “failure to vote
to allow TE [Name Omited], Assistant Pastor, to attend
Session meetings.” The report of the Ad-Hoc Committee on
Review of Sessional Records appended to the minutes states
as follows: “TE [Name Omited] was not approved to sit on
Session meetings. As an assistant pastor he is not a member
of the Session”.

**Response:** Blue Ridge Presbytery disagrees with this
citation. _BCO_ 12-1 states that the members of the Session are

the pastor, associate pastor (s) (if any) and ruling elders of the
church, All others must be invited to attend as they are not
members.
**2024-03: Apr 29, 2023** ( _BCO_ 18-2) — No record of sixmonths membership for candidate.
**Response:** Blue Ridge Presbytery agrees with this
exception and will seek to be more careful in the future, We
will correct our minutes accordingly.

**c.** Be approved with exception of substance:
**2025-01: Mar 1, 2024** ( _BCO_ 21-4; RAO16-3.e.5) — Stated

**d.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**satisfactory:**
**2022-01: Mar 05–06, 2021** ( _BCO_ 21-4.f; 40-2) — Presbytery
granted a doctrinal exception that may be hostile to our system
of doctrine ( _WCF_ 1.1-10). More information or clarity is
needed on the exception.
**Response:** The Candidates expressed view is that, the
gifts of tongues and prophecy, though no longer normative,
still occur in a manner that can be defined by such terms.
There have been individuals who have reportedly received, in
a miraculous manner, the gift of knowing (speaking and/or
reading) a language they have had no previous experience or
training in. He believes these gifts can be given as a means of
grace from God and would categorize this in the realm of the
“gift of tongues”. Also, individuals may at times be blessed
with extraordinary insight on a certain issue for which they
may have not previously demonstrated any particular
knowledge, expertise or have no training in. The Candidate
defines such situations as being in the realm of the “gift of
prophecy”, using its wider definition. “Prophecy” being
looked at as per 1 Corinthians 14:3-4 as speaking to people
for their upbuilding and encouragement and consolation…
(to) build up the church.” It would be defined as: “the gift of
communicating and enforcing revealed truth”. Though such
situations could also come under the definition of being gifts
of “wisdom”, “knowledge”, and/or “discernment”, the
definition of “prophecy” as an over-arching term for such is
also acceptable for such circumstances. The Candidate agrees
that “prophecy” defined as new revelation from God to a
person that is “extra-Biblical” or outside of God’s revealed
and complete Word, does not exist.
**Rationale:** Presbytery does not acknowledge any
biblical support for the continuation of such gifts, nor does it

<a id="ga52-p517"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=519 printed_page=517 -->

provide any rationale as to how a person can meaningfully
differentiate between modern day “prophecy” and Scripture.
**Response:** The candidate affirmed that the supreme
judge by which all controversies of religion are to be
determined, and all decrees of councils, opinions of ancient
writers, doctrines of men, and private spirits, are to be
examined, and in whose sentence we are to rest, can be no
other but the Holy Spirit speaking in the Scripture (Mat 22:29;
Mat 22:31; Eph 2:20; Act 28:25).
**Rationale:** Presbytery did not address the issue the
Assembly took with its previous response. Presbytery
provides no “biblical support for the continuation of such
gifts, nor does it provide any rationale as to how a person can
meaningfully differentiate between modern day “prophecy”
and Scripture,” in its response. Further, its response is merely
a citation of _WCF_ 1.10, and so provides the Assembly with no
new information, as the candidate stated no difference with
that portion of the Standards.
**Response:** We agree that TE [Name Omitted]’s
views in this matter are concerning and contrary to our
Standards. We regret approving this exception and we resolve
not to grant such an exception in the future as we believe this
exception to be hostile to our system of doctrine. TE [Name
Omitted] has been encouraged to transfer his ministerial
credentials to a more suitable ecclesiastical body, as he is
currently “without call” and we are shepherding him along in
the process. We expect that this will be done before the Fall
2025 stated meeting of the Canada West Presbytery.
**2024-05: General 2023** ( _BCO_ 13-9.b; _BCO_ 40-3) — Results
of review of records of church Sessions not stated.

**Response:** The Administrative sub-committee did
report on all the churches minutes that were received. There
were no exceptions of substance taken with any of the
received minutes. There were exceptions of form taken with
some sets of minutes received. The minutes of the Presbytery
have been corrected to show the results of the sub-committee's

report in this regard.

<a id="ga52-p518"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=520 printed_page=518 -->

**2024-11: General 2023** ( _BCO_ 15-1; _RAO_ 16-3.e.4) —
Minutes of commission not entered in Presbytery minutes,
and these must be presented for review next year.
**Response:** The Commission reports for the
installation of [Names Omitted] were reported to Presbytery
and inadvertently left out of the minutes. The minutes of each
of these Commissions are included in the the Fall 2024 CWP

Stated Meeting minutes.
**2024-01: Mar 4, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Incomplete record of ordination exam.
**Response:** The Minutes did not record the full extent
of the examination for the candidate, not withstanding their
previous sustaining of Licensure examination at a previous
Stated Meeting. The Candidate sustained examination in all
areas required for Ordination, including Christian experience
and sense of call to the ministry, scholastic credentialing,
Bible Content, Theology, Sacraments, Church History,
History of the PCA, the _BCO_, and principles of church
government and discipline. The minutes have been corrected
to address this oversight.
**2024-02: Mar 4, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Incomplete record of ordination exam.
**Response:** The Minutes did not record the full extent
of the examination for the candidate, not withstanding their
previous sustaining of Licensure examination at a previous
Stated Meeting. The Candidate sustained examination in all
areas required for Ordination, including Christian experience
and sense of call to the ministry, scholastic credentialing,
Bible Content, Theology, Sacraments, Church History,
History of the PCA, the _BCO_, and principles of church
government and discipline. The minutes have been corrected
to address this oversight.
**2024-03: Oct 13, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Incomplete record of ordination exam.
**Response:** The Minutes did not record the full extent
of the examination for the candidate. The Candidate sustained

examination in all areas required for Ordination, including
Christian experience and sense of call to the ministry,
scholastic credentialing, Bible Content, Theology,

<a id="ga52-p519"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=521 printed_page=519 -->

Sacraments, Church History, History of the PCA, the _BCO_,
and principles of church government and discipline. The
minutes have been corrected to address this oversight.
**2024-04: Mar 4, 2023** ( _BCO_ 23-1) — No record that
Congregation concurred with dissolution of pastoral relation.
**Response:** The congregation called TE [Name
Omitted] to be the Lead Pastor of their congregation in order
to allow the current lone Pastor of the congregation, TE

[Name Omitted], to receive a call as an Associate Pastor at the
church. His call as associate Pastor was also made by the same
congregation. Nevertheless, the congregational vote to
dissolve the Pastor relationship was overwhelmingly
approved by the congregation, in addition to the
overwhelming approval to call the same Pastor as an
Associate Pastor instead and to call TE [Name Omitted] as the
new Lead Pastor. The Clerk regrets the error in the reporting
of the procedure and has corrected the minutes to show the
approval of the dissolution of the Pastoral call by the
congregation.
**2024-06: Mar 4, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4.g; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Presbytery did not require the candidate to state the specific

has amended the formatting to show the Standards article in

**2024-07: Mar 4, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4.g; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Presbytery did not require the candidate to state the specific
instances in which he may differ with the Confession of Faith
and Catechisms in any of their statements and/or propositions.

minutes, the exception did not stand out in any way. The Clerk

<a id="ga52-p520"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=522 printed_page=520 -->

**2024-08: Oct 13, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4.g; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Presbytery did not require the candidate to state the specific
instances in which he may differ with the Confession of Faith
and Catechisms in any of their statements and/or propositions.

**2024-09: Oct 13, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4) — No record of requiring
statement of differences with our Standards.

**Response:** The Candidate's exception to the
Standards was included in the minutes without the record of

asking for such declaration. The minutes have been corrected
regarding the process.
**2024-10: Oct 13, 2023** ( _BCO_ 19-16; _BCO_ 21-2) — 3/4 vote
for waiving internship requirement not recorded.
**Response:** The candidate did indeed serve an
internship of one year in the OPC (in which church he became
fully credentialed prior to coming to Woodgreen). The
internship was deemed acceptable to our Credentials
Committee and approved by the Presbytery. The internship
and OPC credentialing was discussed in his Christian
experience and sense of call but inadvertently left out of the
reporting. The Clerk regrets the error of not including this
information in the minutes and has corrected that.
**2024-12: Oct 13, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4.g; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Presbytery did not require the candidate to state the specific
instances in which he may differ with the Confession of Faith
and Catechisms in any of their statements and/or propositions.
**Response:** The lines being referred to are written as
responses to an exception taken by RPR from previous
minutes in 2022. The exceptions being included in these 2023
minutes are the response to that (proper) exception taken, not
a reporting o fa Candidate's ordination exam. The ordination
exam itself, occurring at a 2022 Stated meeting, had asked the
candidate for exceptions taken to the Standards but the
minutes errantly omitted to include them. The exceptions

<a id="ga52-p521"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=523 printed_page=521 -->

were then included in these 2023 minutes to document them

and to respond to the RPR exception taken.
**2024-13: Oct 13, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4.g) — Presbytery granted a
doctrinal exception that appears to be out of accord with the
fundamentals of our doctrinal standards. Candidates stated

difference, " _WLC_ 177 – worthy participation of the Lord’s
Supper I am more than willing to submit in teaching and
practice to the confessional position of requiring a public
profession of faith from covenant children before admitting
them to the Lord's Table. I am strongly against Federal Vision
and I am intentionally distancing myself from such distinctive
teachings in the CREC (to which I never agreed). However, it
seems to me that, within the context of faithful family nurture
and given the place of covenant children in both the old and
new covenants, we should expect that our children already
possess the prerequisite faith from the earliest age, and that
they will gradually, organically, display this faith in an ageappropriate manner. While we do not know whether our
children are elect or not, and we cannot peer infallibly into
their hearts, I think we should treat them with the judgment of
charity expressed, for instance, in Canons of Dordt 1.17 and
Luke 18:15ff, teaching them to pray "Our Father in heaven"
and to sing "Jesus loves me" in the full sense of the words.
Nevertheless, as I said, I am very happy to abide by the
confessional requirements of a public profession of faith
( _WLC_ 177; _DFW_ 56- 4.j) and have already spoken with my
own children about doing so. To administer the Supper and
fence the table in this way would pose no harm to my
conscience whatsoever.".

**Response:** TE [Name Omitted] has submitted to and
upheld our constitutional standards in every way, both in
teaching and in practice, and we are confident that he will
continue to do so in keeping with his ministerial vows. His
private views in this matter are a commonly held exception
within the PCA and he holds them with the utmost humility
and discretion. We believe that, in granting such an exception
and requiring him not to teach or practice his views, we have
done nothing contrary to the longstanding precedent and
current practice of the PCA.

<a id="ga52-p522"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=524 printed_page=522 -->

**2024-14: Mar 4, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4.b) — Minutes show the
Presbytery examined the candidates in biblical counseling.
While this may be prudent, it is not an area of examination for
ordination according to the _BCO_, nor does Presbytery include
it in its Standing Rules.
**Response:** In recent years, the Presbytery has
adopted the practice of having its Credentials Committee
examine candidates in the area of Christian Counseling. We
recognize that it is not a required area of examination, but
because it is done it is reported as a part of our minutes. In the
future the minutes will clarify that such exam is extracurricular to the _BCO_ requirements and are not a part of the
Ordination qualifications.
**2024-15: Oct 13, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4.b) — Minutes show the
Presbytery examined the candidates in biblical counseling.
While this may be prudent, it is not an area of examination for
ordination according to the _BCO_, nor does Presbytery include
it in its Standing Rules.
**Response:** In recent years, the Presbytery has
adopted the practice of having its Credentials Committee
examine candidates in the area of Christian Counseling. We
recognize that it is not a required area of examination, but
because it is done it is reported as a part of our minutes. In the
future the minutes will clarify that such exam is extracurricular to the _BCO_ requirements and are not a part of the
Ordination qualifications.

6 . That the Minutes of **Catawba Valley Presbytery** : _63-0-0_
**a.** Be approved without exception: **General 2024; Jan 27, 2024;**
**Sep 28, 2024.**
**b.** Be approved with exception of form: **None.**
**c.** Be approved with exception of substance: **None.**
**d.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**satisfactory:**
**2024-01: May 20, 2023** ( _BCO_ 23-1) — No record that
congregation concurred with the dissolution of pastoral
relations.

**Response:** CVP acknowledges that the minutes of the
called meeting of [Church Name Omitted] that it had voted

<a id="ga52-p523"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=525 printed_page=523 -->

unanimously in a congregational meeting on 3/19/23 to accept
the TE’s resignation was inadvertently omitted from the
Shepherding Committee report that it had acted as a
commission to dissolve the pastoral relationship. The minutes
of that congregational meeting as well as a letter from the
resigning TE have now been submitted to presbytery by the
Shepherding Committee. CVP voted on Sept 28, 2024 to
correct this action by adding the May 19, 2023 congregational
minutes and letter of resignation as an appendix to the May
20, 2023 CVP Minutes, and CVP promises to be more diligent
to include such documentation in our future minutes.

**2024-06: Nov 11, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) — Stated
differences not judged with the prescribed categories.
**Response:** CVP acknowledges that it failed to follow
the prescribed wording of RAO16-3.e.5 with regard to these
stated differences to the Standard, though our minutes showed
clearly that the intent of _RAO_ 16.3 was followed in that the
exception “was not out of accord with any fundamental of our
system of doctrine.” Regardless, CVP approved on Sept 28,
2024 to correct the minutes of Nov 11, 2022 and May 20, 2023
to express that the candidate’s stated difference was “more
than semantic, but not out of accord with any fundamental of
our system of doctrine” ( _BCO_ 19-2; 21-4). CVP also promises
to be more diligent to use the prescribed wording in dealing
with exceptions.
**e.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**unsatisfactory, therefore new responses shall be submitted**
**to the following GA:**
**2023-01: Jan 22, 2022** ( _BCO_ 43-8) — Four timely and
orderly complaints dismissed without a hearing.
**Response:** CVP responds by noting that these four
complaints were in fact adjudicated by a Commission but the
Commission was unable to proceed after reviewing the
complaints, finding all four complaints to be “out of order
administratively,” in that they were not filed against an action
of a court ( _BCO_ 43-1). CVP was also notified by the
Complainant’s civil attorney that the Complaiant desired to
withdraw his complaints and to receive no further interaction
with CVP concerning these matters.

<a id="ga52-p524"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=526 printed_page=524 -->

**Rationale:** No record that Presbytery corrected its
actions ( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1). Presbytery has not rescinded the
unconstitutional denial of the complaints. The complaints
were, in fact, against actions of a court ( _BCO_ 43-1), and it was
improper to dismiss the complaints as “out of order
administratively” on the stated grounds without affording the
Complainant the right of a hearing. A purported subsequent
withdrawal o fa complaint could dispose o fa complaint only
after an unconstitutional action is rescinded.

**Response:** CVP responds by acknowledging that it
erred in that it should first have determined that the matter was

“administratively out of order” before forming a commission
( _BCO_ 43.8). The commission also erred in that it was
improper to dismiss one complaint as “out of order
administratively” on the stated grounds given. The wording in
the commission’s report admits to some adjudication of the
complaint, which was out of order and was not a strict
judgment based on _BCO_ 43:1. CVP affirms to the General
Assembly that it will follow the order found in _BCO_ 43:8, and
it will follow the order and steps used by the SJC found in
_OMSJC_ 9.1 and 9.2. Moreover, CVP affirms that it will seek
to provide the proper grounds for any ruling in future cases.
CVP adds that it was notified by the Complainant’s civil
attorney that the Complainant has withdrawn his complaints
and desires no further interaction with CVP concerning these
matters. The Complainant is no longer a member of any
congregation of the PCA. This has the effect of rendering the
Complaints administratively out of order in the same fashion
as the SJC acted in case SJC 2022-15 ( _RE Terry Murdock_ vs.
_South Florida Presbytery_ ) and SJC 2022-15 ( _Mr. Peter_
_Benyola_ vs. _Central Florida Presbytery_ ).
**Rationale:** No record that Presbytery corrected its
actions ( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1). Presbytery has not rescinded the
unconstitutional denial of the complaints. The complaints
were made by a member in good standing of the PCA at the
time of filing and at the time of the adjudication of the
complaints. Furthermore, the complaints were against actions
of a court ( _BCO_ 43-1). Therefore, it was improper to dismiss
the complaints as “out of order administratively” on the stated

<a id="ga52-p525"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=527 printed_page=525 -->

grounds without affording the Complainant the right of a
hearing. a purported subsequent withdrawal o fa complaint
could dispose of a complaint only after an unconstitutional
action is rescinded. (See the Response of Northwest Georgia
Presbytery to exception of substance 2023-01 for a
satisfactory response to the same error.)

**d.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**unsatisfactory, therefore new responses shall be submitted**
**to the following GA:**
**2024-01: Jan 17, 2023** ( _BCO_ 20-8) — No record
commissioners appointed by the church presented and
prosecuted the call of a TE before Presbytery.
**Response:** Agreed that there is no explicit record of
named commissioners actually presenting and prosecuting the
call. But there is record that the call was reviewed and the
terms were approved, which surely assumes it was presented
and prosecuted. Previous “Guidelines to Exceptions” do not
list the failure to explicitly state that named commissioners
presented and prosecuted the call as an “exception of
substance.” In the future, will this level of detail qualify under
_RAO_ 16-6.c.1) as an “Exception of Substance”? Will this be
considered a “serious irregularity” or “important
delinquency”?
**Rationale:** Presbytery must respond following the
pattern of _RAO_ 16-10.b.

<a id="ga52-p526"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=528 printed_page=526 -->

**2024-02: Apr 18, 2023** ( _BCO_ 20-8) — No record
commissioners appointed by the church presented and
prosecuted the calls of TEs before the Presbytery.
**Response:** Agreed that there is no explicit record of
named commissioners actually presenting and prosecuting the
call. But there is record that the call was reviewed and the

terms were approved, which surely assumes it was presented
and prosecuted. Previous “Guidelines to Exceptions” do not
list the failure to explicitly state that named commissioners
presented and prosecuted the call as an “exception of
substance.” In the future, will this level of detail qualify under
_RAO_ 16-6.c.1) as an “Exception of Substance”? Will this be
considered a “serious irregularity” or “important
delinquency”?
**Rationale:** Presbytery must respond according to the
pattern of _RAO_ 16-10.b.
