**d.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**satisfactory:**
**2024-01: Feb 3, 2023** ( _BCO_ 20-1; _RAO_ 16-3.e.6) — Specific
arrangements of call not shown to be approved.
**Response:** The specific arrangements of the calls in
question were reviewed and approved, though the record is
not as clear as it could have been that this was the case. We

will amend the record, and be more complete in the future.
**2024-02: Feb 3, 2023** ( _BCO_ 23-1) — No record that
Congregation concurred with dissolution of pastoral relations.
**Response:** The minutes as presented include record
of the full requirements for dissolving pastoral relations. A
church may be relieved of its pastor without the concurrence
of that pastor ( _BCO_ 23-1 ¶2), and a pastor may be relieved of
his pastoral charge without the concurrence of the
congregation ( _BCO_ 23-1 ¶1). As such dissolution of the
pastoral relations does not require mutual concurrence, it
cannot be a requirement that the presbytery demonstrate that

<a id="ga52-p644"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=646 printed_page=644 -->

the mutual concurrence of both parties has been obtained. The
presbytery may show such concurrence if it chooses, but must
show (1) that either the pastor or the congregation has
requested the dissolution of the pastoral relation, and (2) that
the presbytery concurred with that request.
**2024-03: Apr 12, 2023** ( _BCO_ 13-12; RONR (12th ed.) 25:10)
— Notice for called meeting not in order (10-day notice,
verbatim meeting call, etc., not indicated/recorded).
**Response:** The call was sent to the body with the
requisite 10-day notice, and the only agenda items were those
contained in the call. However, these calls and their timing
were not completely recorded in the minutes. We will correct
the record and be more complete in the future.
**2024-04: May 5, 2023** ( _BCO_ 23-1) — No record that
Congregation concurred with dissolution of pastoral relations.
**Response:** The minutes as presented include record
of the full requirements for dissolving pastoral relations. A
church may be relieved of its pastor without the concurrence
of that pastor ( _BCO_ 23-1 ¶2), and a pastor may be relieved of
his pastoral charge without the concurrence of the
congregation ( _BCO_ 23-1 ¶1). As such dissolution of the
pastoral relations does not require mutual concurrence, it
cannot be a requirement that the presbytery demonstrate that
the mutual concurrence of both parties has been obtained. The
presbytery may show such concurrence if it chooses, but must
show (1) that either the pastor or the congregation has
requested the dissolution of the pastoral relation, and (2) that
the presbytery concurred with that request.
**2024-05: May 5, 2023** ( _BCO_ 23-1) — No record that
Congregation concurred with dissolution of pastoral relations.
**Response:** The minutes as presented include record
of the full requirements for dissolving pastoral relations. A
church may be relieved of its pastor without the concurrence
of that pastor ( _BCO_ 23-1 ¶2), and a pastor may be relieved of
his pastoral charge without the concurrence of the
congregation ( _BCO_ 23-1 ¶1). As such dissolution of the
pastoral relations does not require mutual concurrence, it
cannot be a requirement that the presbytery demonstrate that
the mutual concurrence of both parties has been obtained. The

<a id="ga52-p645"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=647 printed_page=645 -->

presbytery may show such concurrence if it chooses, but must
show (1) that either the pastor or the congregation has
requested the dissolution of the pastoral relation, and (2) that
the presbytery concurred with that request.
**2024-06: May 5, 2023** ( _BCO_ 23-1) — No record that
Congregation concurred with dissolution of pastoral relations.
**Response:** The minutes as presented include record
of the full requirements for dissolving pastoral relations. A
church may be relieved of its pastor without the concurrence
of that pastor ( _BCO_ 23-1 ¶2), and a pastor may be relieved of
his pastoral charge without the concurrence of the
congregation ( _BCO_ 23-1 ¶1). As such dissolution of the
pastoral relations does not require mutual concurrence, it
cannot be a requirement that the presbytery demonstrate that
the mutual concurrence of both parties has been obtained. The
presbytery may show such concurrence if it chooses, but must
show (1) that either the pastor or the congregation has
requested the dissolution of the pastoral relation, and (2) that
the presbytery concurred with that request.
**Rationale:** _BCO_ 23-1 requires the Presbytery to cite
the church (or Session) to appear by its commissioners to
report the church’s (Session’s) disposition toward the
dissolution of the pastoral relationship (whether concurring or
dissenting) following a called meeting of the congregation
(Session) for the purpose of ascertaining that disposition. The
Presbytery’s response does not acknowledge the error or
attempt to correct the record
**2024-07: May 5, 2023** ( _BCO_ 23-1) — No record that
Congregation concurred with dissolution of pastoral relations.
**Response:** The minutes as presented include record
of the full requirements for dissolving pastoral relations. A
church may be relieved of its pastor without the concurrence
of that pastor ( _BCO_ 23-1 ¶2), and a pastor may be relieved of
his pastoral charge without the concurrence of the
congregation ( _BCO_ 23-1 ¶1). As such dissolution of the
pastoral relations does not require mutual concurrence, it
cannot be a requirement that the presbytery demonstrate that
the mutual concurrence of both parties has been obtained. The
presbytery may show such concurrence if it chooses, but must

<a id="ga52-p646"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=648 printed_page=646 -->

show (1) that either the pastor or the congregation has
requested the dissolution of the pastoral relation, and (2) that
the presbytery concurred with that request.
**Rationale:** _BCO_ 23-1 requires the Presbytery to cite
the church (or Session) to appear by its commissioners to
report the church’s (Session’s) disposition toward the
dissolution of the pastoral relationship (whether concurring or
dissenting) following a called meeting of the congregation
(Session) for the purpose of ascertaining that disposition. The
Presbytery’s response does not acknowledge the error or
attempt to correct the record
**2024-08: May 5, 2023** ( _BCO_ 20-1; _RAO_ 16-3.e.6) — Specific
arrangements of call not shown to be approved.
**Response:** The specific arrangements of the calls in
question were reviewed and approved, though the record is
not as clear as it could have been that this was the case. We

will amend the record, and be more complete in the future.
**2024-09: Aug 2, 2023** ( _BCO_ 13-12; _RONR_ (12th ed.) 25:10)
— Notice for called meeting not in order (10-day notice,
verbatim meeting call, etc., not indicated/recorded).
**Response:** The call was sent to the body with the
requisite 10-day notice, and the only agenda items were those
contained in the call. However, these calls and their timing
were not completely recorded in the minutes. We will correct
the record and be more complete in the future.

53. That the Minutes of **Northern Illinois Presbytery:** _56-0-0_
**a.** Be approved without exception: **Dec 5, 2024.**
**b.** Be approved with exception of form: **None.**

**2025-01: General 2024** ( _BCO_ 18-6) — No record that reports
on candidates's progress were received from candidates'
instructors.

**2025-02: General 2024** ( _BCO_ 18-6) — No record of annual
report(s) of candidates.
**2025-03: Jan 13, 2024** ( _BCO_ 15-1; _RAO_ 16-3.e.4.) —
Minutes of commission not entered in Presbytery minutes.

<a id="ga52-p647"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=649 printed_page=647 -->

**2025-04: Jan 13, 2024** ( _RAO_ 14-2) — Election of
commissioners requires action by presbytery or a commission
of presbytery. .
**2025-05: May 14, 2024** ( _BCO_ 18-3) — No record candidates
examined on motives for seeking ministry.
**2025-06: May 14, 2024** ( _BCO_ 18-2) — No record of
endorsement by candidate's Session.
**2025-07: May 14, 2024** ( _BCO_ 18-2) — No record of sixmonths membership for candidates.
**2025-08: Sep 14, 2024** ( _BCO_ 18-2) — No record of sixmonths membership for candidates.
**2025-09: Sep 14, 2024** ( _BCO_ 18-2) — No record of
endorsements by candidates' sessions.
**d.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**satisfactory:**
**2024-01: General 2023** ( _BCO_ 13-2) — No record of annual
report of ministers without call.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception and
promises to be more careful in the future. The PNI Credentials
Committee particularly has taken note and will be diligent to
be keep up with annual reports as required in _BCO_ 13-2.
**2024-02: General 2023** ( _BCO_ 8-4) — No record of annual
report of minister appointed to a needful work.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception and
promises to be more careful in the future. The PNI Credentials
Committee particularly has taken note and will be diligent to
be keep up with annual reports as required in _BCO_ 8-4.
**2024-03: Jan 14, 2023** ( _BCO_ 23-1) — No record that Session
concurred with dissolution of pastoral relations.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception and
corrects its record. Presbytery had, in fact, received testimony
from the Session of [Church Name Omitted]. That fact should
have been recorded; the record will be corrected to reflect the
Session’s concurrence.

**2024-04: Jan 14, 2023** ( _BCO_ 8-7) — No record that the
Presbytery is assured that an out-of-bounds TE will have full
freedom to maintain and teach the doctrine of our Church.

**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception and
corrects its record. Presbytery had, in fact, received

<a id="ga52-p648"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=650 printed_page=648 -->

correspondence from Interim Pastors Ministries that TE

[Name Omitted] would have full freedom to maintain and
teach the doctrine of our Church. That correspondence should
have been recorded; the record will be corrected to reflect
IPM’s correspondence.
**2024-05: Jan 14, 2023** ( _BCO_ 22-6) — No record that the
Session requested the renewal of Stated Supply.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception. The
church that TE [Name Omitted] was serving was not a PCA
church and had simply communicated its desires for the
extension through TE [Name Omitted] in order that they
might have time install a new pastor. Presbytery should have
communicated directly with the board of elders at the Chenoa
church. Presbytery promises to be more careful in the future.
**e.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**unsatisfactory, therefore new responses shall be submitted**
**to the following GA:**
**2024-06: Feb 23, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Incomplete record of ordination exam.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception and
will correct its record. The summary on pg. 66 “The
Committee had received all necessary documentation and
papers from the candidate,” was intended to cover all that had
been done in Credentials Committee. All necessary details
should have been explicitly enumerated. Presbytery promises
to be more careful in the future.
**Rationale:** No record that Presbytery corrected its
record ( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1). Presbytery has not corrected the
record and enumerated all required details.
**2024-07: Feb 23, 2023** ( _BCO_ 20-1; _RAO_ 16-3.e.6) — Specific
arrangements of call not shown to be approved.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception.
Presbytery attempted to handle the Call Letter within the
Credentials Committee in honor of the church’s request, and
thus did not distribute the Call Letter, but approved it upon the
Credentials Committee’s recommendation that the call was in

order. Presbytery will be more careful in the future with
regard to the approval of Call Letters.

<a id="ga52-p649"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=651 printed_page=649 -->

**Rationale:** No record that Presbytery corrected its
record ( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1). Presbytery has not corrected the
record to show that presbytery approved the arrangements of
the call.

**2024-08: Feb 23, 2023** ( _BCO_ 15-1; _RAO_ 16-3.e.4) — Minutes
of commission not entered in Presbytery minutes, and these
must be presented for review next year.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception and
will append those minutes to the record and will supply the
minutes of the commission for review by the Review of
Presbytery Records of the 52nd General Assembly.
**Rationale:** No record that Presbytery corrected its
record ( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1). Presbytery has not provided the
minutes of the commission for review (TE [Name Omitted]).
**2024-09: May 9, 2023** ( _BCO_ 18-7) — Candidates dismissed
from roll without record of informing former candidates.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception and
will correct the record to show clearly that the former
candidates were informed.
**Rationale:** No record that Presbytery corrected its
record ( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1). Presbytery has not corrected record.
**2024-10: May 9, 2023** ( _BCO_ 15-1; _RAO_ 16-3.e.4) — Minutes
of commission not entered in Presbytery minutes, and these
must be presented for review next year.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception and
will append those minutes to the record and will supply the
minutes of the commission, including the referenced Report
from the Commision and Letter of Confession, for review by
the Review of Presbytery Records of the 52nd General
Assembly.
**Rationale:** No record that Presbytery corrected its
record ( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1). No record Presbytery took action at
a presbytery meeting in 2024 to append their commission's
minutes to the appropriate presbytery meeting in 2023.
**2024-11: Jul 25, 2023** ( _BCO_ 38-1; _RAO_ 16-3.e.8) — Record
of Case without process not recorded in minutes.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception and
will append the Record of the Case without Process to the
record and will supply the Record of the Case for review by

<a id="ga52-p650"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=652 printed_page=650 -->

the Review of Presbytery Records of the 52nd General
Assembly.
**Rationale:** No record that Presbytery corrected its
record ( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1). No record Presbytery took action on
their commission's recommendations (commission meeting
5/9/23, appendix, nos. 1, 2).
**2024-12: Sep 9, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Incomplete record of ordination exam.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception
regarding the incomplete record of the ordination exam and
will correct the Record. The approval of the knowledge of
Greek and Hebrew and the examination of his personal
character were handled in the Credentials Committee, which
should have been explicitly enumerated in the Record.
However, the candidate stated no difference with Standards
(pg 103 of Record), so the Presbytery is uncertain about
RPR’s citation on views.
**Rationale:** No record that Presbytery corrected its
record ( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1). Presbytery has not corrected the
record and enumerated the required details of the licensure
exam that were not included in the existing Record.
**2024-13: Sep 9, 2023** ( _BCO_ 23-1) — No record of four fifths
(4/5) majority vote of congregation on petition to request that
an associate pastor succeed the senior pastor.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception and
will correct the record. Presbytery’s intention in recording that
“The church brings the petition in accord with _BCO_ 23-1,”
was to speak to the 4/5 requirement. This should have been
explicitly stated. The Presbytery will be more careful in the
future.
**Rationale:** No record that Presbytery corrected its
action or record ( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1). Presbytery should correct
the record to reflect the church's satisfaction of the

supermajority requirement.

**d.** **That the following responses to the 52nd GA be found**
**satisfactory:**
**2022-01: Apr 13, 2021** ( _BCO_ 43-8) — i fa complaint is
properly filed, it shall be heard by the Presbytery. A complaint

<a id="ga52-p652"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=654 printed_page=652 -->

may not be denied without a hearing except on narrow
administrative grounds.
**Response:** At the April 13, 2021 meeting of
Presbytery, there was an indication that the complainant
intended to withdraw his complaint and that he would not
pursue it any further nor elevate it to a higher court. The
complainant communicated such in an email to Presbytery’s
Stated Clerk on April 16, 2021. The Stated Clerk regrets the
failure to record this in the minutes.
**Rationale:** No record that Presbytery corrected its
actions ( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1). Presbytery has not rescinded the
unconstitutional denial of the complaint.
**Response:** The Northwest Georgia Presbytery agrees
with the exception. A complaint, timely filed and in order,
must be given a hearing ( _BCO_ 43-8). The Presbytery therefore
erred in denying the complaint without a hearing. However, it
is not possible for the Presbytery to correct its error since the
complaint has been withdrawn by the complainant. This
action to withdraw the complaint was taken by the
complainant on April 16, 2021 in a letter emailed to the Stated
Clerk for circulation to the NWGP (and submitted to the
CRPR as a separate PDF attachment titled “Crouse
Withdrawal of Complainant”). There is therefore no
complaint before the Presbytery to take up or otherwise
correct. Nevertheless, the Presbytery regrets its error and
promises to adjudicate all complaints it may receive in the
future in strict adherence to the provisions of the _Book of_
_Church Order_ . (The Presbytery also adopted an apology to the
complainants in the above cases and directed its Stated Clerk
to deliver the apology to them. The apology is submitted to
the CRPR as a separate PDF attachment titled “NWGP Ltr to
Complainants 1.24.24”)
**Rationale:** No record that Presbytery corrected its
actions ( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1). The Presbytery serves as an example
to others in the way it apologized to the Complainants for its
incorrect actions, but Presbytery has not rescinded the
unconstitutional denial of the complaint. Acknowledgement
of abandonment or withdrawal should only come after the
unconstitutional action has been rescinded because the alleged

<a id="ga52-p653"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=655 printed_page=653 -->

abandonment or withdrawal occurred after the

unconstitutional dismissal by the Presbytery.
**Response:** The Presbytery agrees with the exception.
The denial of the complaint has been rescinded and the
complaint ruled judicially moot. Presbytery regrets its error
and promises to adjudicate all complaints it may receive in the
future in strict adherence to the provisions of the _Book of_
_Church Order_ .
**2023-01: Apr 5, 2022** ( _BCO_ 43-8) — Timely and orderly
complaints (3) denied without a hearing.
**Response:** The Northwest Georgia Presbytery agrees
with the exception. A complaint, timely filed and in order,
must be given a hearing ( _BCO_ 43-8). The Presbytery erred in
adopting the decision of its judicial commission in the instant
case because the judicial commission made its
recommendation without holding a hearing. However, it is not
possible for the Presbytery to correct this error since the case
(one which consolidated three complaints) has already been
appealed to and ruled upon by the SJC in two separate
decisions (SJC 2022-16 _Michelson_, et al. v. _NWGP_, & SJC
2022-05 _Crouse_ et al. v. _NWGP_ ). Even if the Presbytery could
rehear a case subsequently decided by the higher court, the
complaint is now moot (as the SJC ruled in both above cases)
since the underlying issue in the consolidated cases was
resolved when the Midway Session dismissed all charges
against the accused Ruling Elders and ended the judicial
process. Nevertheless, the Presbytery regrets its error and
promises to adjudicate all complaints it may receive in the
future in strict adherence to the provisions of the Book of
Church Order. (The Presbytery also adopted an apology to the
complainants in the above cases and directed its Stated Clerk
to deliver the apology to them. The apology is submitted to
the CRPR as a separate PDF attachment titled “NWGP Ltr to
Complainants 1.24.24”)
**Rationale:** No record that Presbytery corrected its
actions ( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1). The Presbytery serves as an example
to others in the way it apologized to the Complainants for its
incorrect actions, but Presbytery has not rescinded the
unconstitutional denial of the complaints. Acknowledgement

<a id="ga52-p654"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=656 printed_page=654 -->

of judicial mootness should only come after the
unconstitutional action has been rescinded because the action

of the higher court occurred after the unconstitutional
dismissal by the Presbytery.
**Response:** The Presbytery agrees with the exception.
The denial of the complaints has been rescinded and the
complaints ruled judicially moot. Presbytery regrets its error
and promises to adjudicate all complaints it may receive in the
future in strict adherence to the provisions of the Book of
Church Order.

**2024-01: Apr 4, 2023** ( _BCO_ 15-1) — No record commission
submitted “complete minutes” of its meetings.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the exception and
apologizes for failing to record the complete minutes of its
Shepherding Commission. The Stated Clerk will correct the
record and submit the minutes to RPR.

**e.** **That the 52nd General Assembly:**

Find that the April 14, 2025, letter from Mr. Crouse
does not present a report of an “important delinquency or
grossly unconstitutional proceedings” ( _BCO_ 40-5).

**Rationale:** The report by Mr. Crouse reports the
handling of the request of a _BCO_ 31-2 investigation of a
Teaching Elder in NWGP. While the report appears credible,
the 52 [nd] General Assembly finds no sufficient evidence that
NWGP’s actions rise to the level of an important delinquency
or a grossly unconstitutional proceeding under _BCO_ 40-5.

57. That the Minutes of **Ohio Valley Presbytery:** _47-5-3_
**a.** Be approved without exception: **General 2024; Jan 16, 2024**
**(CMP); Jan 25, 2024; Mar 19, 2024; Mar 19, 2024 (CMP);**
**Apr 30, 2024; Apr 30, 2024 (CMP); May 25, 2024; May 25,**
**2024 (ES); Jul 27, 2024; Sep 14, 2024; Oct 15, 2024; Dec**
**18, 2024.**
**b.** Be approved with exception of form: **None.**
**c.** Be approved with exception of substance:
**2025-01: Jan 16, 2024** ( _BCO_ 20-1; _BCO_ 23) — Presbytery
did not approve change of call.
**2025-02: May 7, 2024** ( _BCO_ 21-4.f; _BCO_ 57-1; 57-2) —
Presbytery granted a doctrinal exception that appears to be
hostile to the system of doctrine or strike at the vitals of
religion. In the candidates own words, he says, “As regards
who may partake of the Lord’s Supper, I differ with the idea
that the New Testament requires covenant children to make a
profession of faith (an idea not explicitly stated in the
Standards) or mature to the point of self-examination prior to
being admitted to the Table. I believe covenant children
should be welcomed to the Table of the Lord more or less

when they join the table in their homes. Three biblical
arguments persuade me of this view: the OT pattern of
Passover and other sacrificial feasts, the dubious
universalizing of 1 Corinthians 11:28, and the unity of
signification between the Sacraments.1
First, it is clear that children partook, and were
commanded to partake, of the Passover meal and other
sacrificial feasts in the OT (Exod 12:24-27; Deut 12:11-12;
16:10-11), which are forerunners to the Lord’s Supper much
as circumcision is to baptism. The unity of the Covenant of
Grace is a hallmark of Reformed thought. The Confession
says that “The sacraments of the old testament, in regard of
the spiritual things thereby signified and exhibited, were, for

<a id="ga52-p656"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=658 printed_page=656 -->

substance, the same with those of the new” ( _WCF_ 28.5).
Given these points, there ought to be a presumption of
continuity as to the recipients of the sacraments of
nourishment (holy feasts/Lord’s Supper) just as there is for
the sacraments of initiation (circumcision/baptism).
Second, the chief warrant for seeing discontinuity at
this point is Paul’s injunction in 1 Corinthians 11:28: “Let a
person examine himself, then, and so eat of the bread and
drink of the cup.” It is argued that in this verse Paul issues a
universal prerequisite for all worthy partaking in the Lord’s
Supper. However, it is dubious whether this verse can be used
in this way. The NT letters are occasional documents (1
Corinthians especially so), responding to concrete
circumstances in first-century churches. Paul is clear in what
he is addressing—exclusionary, factious, and elitist behavior
toward fellow church members while ostensibly celebrating
the Lord’s Supper (1 Cor 11:18-20). Schismatic behavior that
tears at the unity of the body profanes the unity of the bread
and the participation in the body of Christ that partaking in the
bread signifies (1 Cor 10:14-17). Lots of legitimate
application can be made along these lines to the Church today.
But to universalize the application of this passage is to take it
too far. To deduce from it that Paul intended to set a minimum

maturity threshold for participation in the Supper is, in my
view, to stretch it beyond the breaking point. Moreover, even
if Paul’s injunction to self-examination is taken as broadly as
possible, the fact remains that there is a universal injunction
to repent and believe prior to baptism for adults which
(contrary to Baptists) we do not require of covenant children.
Third, Scripture teaches and our Standards affirm
that baptism and the Lord’s Supper fundamentally agree in
their significance: “the spiritual part of both is Christ and his
benefits; both are seals of the same covenant” ( _WLC_ 176).
Oddly to my mind, Bavinck, and the Majority Report of the
PCA on this subject with him, use this reality to assert the
insignificance of withholding the Supper from young
children. The children have baptism, the reasoning goes, so
it’s not a big deal if they don’t get the Supper yet. But to my
mind this point cuts precisely the other way. Since by baptism

<a id="ga52-p657"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=659 printed_page=657 -->

they already bear Christ’s name, how can they be justly denied
his bread? By what token are they granted the one-time sign
of union with Christ while being denied the recurring sign of
union with Christ? The unity of signification between the
Sacraments compels me to think that the reasons for which we
baptize our covenant children hold for the question of whether
or not to commune our children as well.”

**2025-03: May 7, 2024** ( _BCO_ 13-1; _BCO_ 32-2; 38-2) —
Presbytery, in dealing with a very difficult church situation,
makes statements regarding sins on the part of one or more
persons without having instituted process.
**d.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**

:Amended May 2021 Minutes to reflect vote

**2024-01: Jan 17, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Incomplete record of ordination exam.

to reexamine the licentiate as required in _BCO_ 19-5 in Bible
content or church government. CMP did not record an exam
of the licentiate’s seminary training, knowledge of Greek and
Hebrew, or of his theological and exegetical papers, as
required by _BCO_ 21-4c. We apologize for this oversight. As
part of Ohio Valley Presbytery since July 2024, a complete
exam always will be performed in the future.
**2024-03: Nov 21, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4; RAO16-3.e.5) —
Incomplete record of ordination exam. No record of candidate
being examined thoroughly in Bible, Theology, and Church
Government. no record of examination in languages. No
record of exegetical and theology papers.
**Response:** We disagree with this exception in part.
The candidate was examined in Bible (designated in the
minutes as Bible Knowledge), Theology (designated in the
minutes as Bible Doctrine), and Church Government as part
of his licensure exam recorded in the September 19, 2023,
stated meeting minutes. CMP failed to examine the candidate
in his knowledge of Greek and Hebrew, or on his theological
and exegetical papers, as required by _BCO_ 21-4c. We
apologize for this oversight. As part of Ohio Valley

<a id="ga52-p659"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=661 printed_page=659 -->

Presbytery since July 2024, a complete exam always will be
performed in the future.

candidate has the endorsement of his session.

**d.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**satisfactory:**
**2024-01: General 2023** ( _BCO_ 13-9.b; _BCO_ 49-3) —
Incomplete record of review of records of church Sessions.
**Response:** We acknowledge that the lack of a report
by the Review of Session Records Committee at the October
3, 2023, Stated Meeting left it unclear as to where we were in
the process of the review of minutes submitted, exceptions
cited, responses to exceptions received, and approval of such
responses, as reflected in the January 28, 2023, Stated
Meeting minutes and in the May 2, 2023, Stated Meeting
minutes.

<a id="ga52-p661"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=663 printed_page=661 -->

We apologize for this failure and commit ourselves
to avoiding this mistake in the future. We regret that we did
not give a report at our final meeting stated meeting of 2023,
and we are up to date on our current tallies of minutes
submitted and reviewed, exceptions cited, responses to
exceptions received, and responses approved. Further, our
2024 records will reflect that we are current in our actions.

**2024-02: Jan 28, 2023** (PP 6) — No record that members of
tempory session were called by the congregation.
**Response:** We recognize that we were in breach of
Preliminary Principle 6 and the obtaining of the concurrence
of congregations for whom presbytery has appointed a
temporary session. We commit ourselves to avoiding this
error in the future.

We further report for the record, that, though not
recorded, and though [Church Name Omitted] no longer
exists as it was merged with [Church Name Omitted],
effective January 1, 2024, former members can confirm that
the congregation of [Church Name Omitted] did desire and
concur with presbytery’s establishment o fa commission to
serve as their temporary session. We regret and apologize for
the lack of complete action, clarity, and recording.
**2024-03: Nov 29, 2023** (PP 6) — No record that members of
tempory session were called by the congregation.
**Response:** We recognize that we were in breach of
Preliminary Principle 6 and the obtaining of the concurrence
of congregations for whom presbytery has appointed a
temporary session. We commit ourselves to avoiding this
error in the future.

We further report that as the established commission
continues at the present time to serve as the temporary session
of [Church Name Omitted], they do so with the concurrence
of the congregation, as reported by the Clerk of the
Commission in the minutes of a congregational meeting held
by [Church Name Omitted] on Sunday, January 19, 2025, at
which they expressed their desire to have the commission
serving as their temporary session. We regret and apologize
for this error.”

<a id="ga52-p662"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=664 printed_page=662 -->

**d.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**satisfactory:**
**2022-01: Feb 5, 2021** ( _BCO_ 20, 21) — No record that TE,
whose call changed from Assistant to Associate, was installed
as Associate Pastor.

**Response:** Presumably the citation refers to the
excerpt below from PNW's 2021 Records Package (p. 30):
"Recommendation: To approve TE [name omitted] change
from Assistant to Associate Pastor at [church name omitted].
Adopted."TE [name omitted] was installed as Associate
Pastor at [church name omitted] on Sunday morning, April 18,
2021. The Commission included TEs [name omitted] and

[name omitted], along with REs [name omitted] and [name
omitted]. We have attached those Commission minutes to our
October 2022 Minutes as well as to this Response.
**Rationale:** Response says that the Commission
minutes were attached to their October 2022 Minutes and also

attached to this report. Minutes were not included for review.
( _RAO_ 16-10.b.1)
**Response:** PNW second response, to 51 [st] GA: Our
Records indicate TE [Name Omitted] was properly installed
as Associate Pastor at [Church Name Omitted] in Bellevue
Washington on April 18, 2021 by Presbytery's Commission of

<a id="ga52-p663"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=665 printed_page=663 -->

TE [Name Omitted], TE [Name Omitted], RE [Name
Omitted] and RE [Name Omitted].
**Rationale:** Minutes for a commission to install must

be submitted for review. If the Commission minutes are

missing, they need to be recreated.
**Response:** We acknowledge the citation regarding
the absence of the commission minutes for the installation of

TE [Omitted] as Associate Pastor at [Omitted]
Upon review, we have recreated the commission
minutes based on records from Hope PCA and the service
bulletin. These minutes have been amended to the minutes and

are attached.

Moving forward, we will ensure that all commission
minutes are promptly recorded and included in Presbytery
records.

**2024-01: Feb 2, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Incomplete record of ordination exam.
**Response:** We agree with the citation regarding the
February 3, 2023, minutes, which noted an incomplete record
of TE [Name Omitted]’s ordination examination. The proper
actions took place but were incorrectly recorded. To address
this issue, the Presbytery has taken the following actions:

1. Amendment of the Minutes: The minutes from

February 2, 2023, have been amended to include
the actions that took place.
2. Future Precautions: We will ensure that records

accurately reflect the actions of the Pby.
The Presbytery regrets the error.
[Clerk’s note: Motion H, attached]
**2024-02: Feb 2, 2023** ( _BCO_ 19-16) — 3/4 vote for waiving
internship requiremetns not recorded.
**Response:** We acknowledge the citation regarding
the February 2, 2023, minutes, which noted that the 3/4 vote
required for waiving internship requirements under _BCO_ 1916 was not explicitly recorded. Upon review, we confirm that
the required 3/4 supermajority vote was properly taken and
achieved during the meeting; however, this detail was
inadvertently omitted from the minutes.

<a id="ga52-p664"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=666 printed_page=664 -->

To address this issue, the Presbytery has taken the
following actions:

1. Amendment of the Minutes: The minutes from

February 2, 2023, have been amended to explicitly
state that the motion to waive the internship
requirements was passed by the required 3/4
supermajority vote, in accordance with _BCO_ 19-16.
2. Future Precautions: We will ensure that all actions

requiring a supermajority vote are explicitly recorded
in the minutes, specifying both the threshold required
and confirmation that it was met.

We regret this oversight and appreciate the
Committee’s attention to ensuring our records comply with
the standards of the _BCO_ . The Presbytery is committed to
maintaining accurate and complete minutes in the future.
**2024-03: May 19, 2023** ( _BCO_ 43.8) — Timely and orderly
complaint dismissed without a hearing.
**Response:** Respectfully, the Pacific Northwest
Presbytery does not agree that this is an exception of
substance. The complaint in question was ruled out of order
by the Session, and the Presbytery similarly ruled it out of
order. The matter was subsequently carried to the Standing
Judicial Commission (SJC). Presbytery does not believe this
constitutes “an apparent violation of Scripture, a serious
irregularity from the Constitution of the PCA, an action out of
accord with a deliverance of the GA, a matter of impropriety,
an important delinquency, or non-compliance with _RAO_ 163.e.5-6.

1. Timeliness and Order of Complaint:

RPR concluded that the complaint was “timely
and orderly” without reviewing the Record of the
Case. It is unclear how this determination could

have been made without access to the full record,
particularly when Presbytery ruled the complaint
out of order.

2. _BCO_ 43-8 Does Not Apply:

There is no constitutional requirement for a
higher court to hold a hearing i fit deems a
complaint out of order. Additionally, there is no

<a id="ga52-p665"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=667 printed_page=665 -->

requirement to adopt a reason for ruling a
complaint out of order. Past SJC decisions have
demonstrated that complaints can be ruled out of
order without citing reasons. Even if the
complaint had been heard, _BCO_ 43-9 permits a
court to deny a complaint without providing
reasons. _BCO_ 43 does not require the higher court
to produce a decision equivalent to the standards
of the _SJC Manual_, §17.1.
Furthermore, _BCO_ 43-8 specifically applies only
after the higher court has determined that a
complaint is timely and in order for it to be heard.
Since the Presbytery deemed the complaint out of
order, _BCO_ 43-8 is not applicable in this case.
3. Judicial Matters Should Not Be Reviewed by
RPR:

According to _RAO_ 16-4.e.9, “Minutes of
presbytery dealing with judicial cases shall not be
dealt with by Committee on Review of
Presbytery Records when notice of appeal or
complaint has been given the lower court ( _BCO_
40-3).” This complaint was carried to the SJC as
Case 2023-11, and a decision was rendered by the
SJC on March 7, 2024. Members of the RPR who
serve on the SJC, as well as the Pacific Northwest
Presbytery representative, could have informed
the RPR of this fact in May 2024.
For these reasons, the Pacific Northwest Presbytery
maintains that the citation is unwarranted, and further
attention to this matter is unnecessary.
**2024-05: May 19, 2023** ( _BCO_ 23-1) — No record that
congregation concurred with dissolution of pastoral relation.
**Response:** We acknowledge the citation regarding
the May 19, 2023, minutes, which noted the absence of a
record that the congregation concurred with the dissolution of
Rev. [Name Omitted]’s call, as required by _BCO_ 23-1.
Upon review, we confirm that the congregation of [Church
Name Omitted], Issaquah, formally concurred with the
dissolution of Rev. [Name Omitted]’s call in a called

<a id="ga52-p666"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=668 printed_page=666 -->

congregational meeting held on May 7, 2023. The
congregation voted unanimously (48-0) in favor of the
dissolution, as recorded in the minutes of the congregational
meeting. This action was communicated to the Presbytery,
and the Ministers & Churches Committee, acting as a
commission, subsequently approved the dissolution in
accordance with Presbytery Standing Rule 4.56.e.
At its most recent meeting, the Presbytery approved an
amendment to the May 19, 2023, minutes to include this
information, ensuring compliance with _BCO_ 23-1. The
amended minutes now reflect that the congregation formally
concurred with the dissolution, and that this concurrence was
a part of the Presbytery’s decision-making process.
We regret the original omission and are committed to
ensuring that all future minutes fully document the required
steps in similar cases.
**2024-06: May 19, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) — Stated
differences not recorded in the canidate’s own words.

**Response:** We acknowledge the citation regarding
the May 19, 2023, minutes, which noted that the stated
differences of the candidate were not recorded in his own
words, as required by _BCO_ 21-4 and _RAO_ 16-3.e.5. Upon
review, it was discovered that the candidate’s statement was
inadvertently omitted from the minutes.
To address this omission, the Presbytery has taken the
following corrective actions:

1. Amendment of the Minutes: The minutes have

been amended to include the candidate’s full

statement of differences (or the absence of
differences, as applicable) in his own words.
The minutes of the Feb. 2025 meeting at which
the May 2023 minutes were amended are
attached.

2. Future Precautions: Moving forward, we will
ensure that all candidates’ stated differences are

fully and accurately recorded in their own words,
as required by _BCO_ 21-4 and _RAO_ 16-3.e.5, and
that such documentation is carefully reviewed
before the minutes are finalized.

<a id="ga52-p667"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=669 printed_page=667 -->

We apologize for this oversight and are committed to
maintaining the integrity of our records in compliance with
the _BCO_ and _RAO_ standards.
**2024-07: May 19, 2023** ( _BCO_ 19-6) — 3/4 vote for waiving
internship requiremetns not recorded.
**Response:** We acknowledge the citation regarding
the May 19, 2023, minutes, which noted that the 3/4 vote
required for waiving internship requirements under _BCO_ 196 (note: the citation says 19-6 but we presume the reference is
19-16) was not explicitly recorded in the minutes. While the
vote was properly taken and the super-majority threshold was
met, the minutes failed to specify that the motion passed by at
least a 3/4 vote as required.
To address this oversight, the Presbytery has taken the
following corrective actions:

1. Amendment of the Minutes: The minutes have

been amended to explicitly state that the motion
to waive the internship requirements was passed
with a 3/4 super-majority vote in compliance with
_BCO_ 19-16. The minutes of the Feb. 2025
meeting at which the May 2023 minutes were
amended are attached.

2. Future Precautions: Moving forward, we will
ensure that all actions requiring a super-majority
vote are explicitly recorded in the minutes,
including both the percentage required and
confirmation that the threshold was met, or a
notation of a unanimous vote.

We apologize for this omission and are committed to
maintaining complete and accurate records in compliance
with the _BCO_ .

**2024-08: May 19, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Incomplete record of ordination exams.
**Response:** Upon review, we recognize that this
omission occurred because the May 2023 minutes referenced
Rev. [ ] as being licensed in October 2022, and noted that the
exam being given was “ordination part 2,” which is a wellunderstood phrase in the PNWP, but, absent context, did not
make that clear in the minutes. The minutes of the October

<a id="ga52-p668"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=670 printed_page=668 -->

2022 meeting show that he was given the first part of his
ordination examination, in which he was “carefully
examined” in the areas listed in _BCO_ 21-4.c.1.a, c, d, and h.
These areas include:

1. His Christian experience, especially personal
character and family management (21-4.c.1.a),
2. Theology (21-4.c.1.c),
3. The Sacraments (21-4.c.1.d),
4. Church government (21-4.c.1.h).
These examinations fulfilled the ordination

examination requirements in the above-listed areas. However,
theological and exegetical papers were not recorded as part of
the examination during this stage, and it is unclear as to
whether they were ever requested or received. As his
ordination is now complete, the Presbytery asks the GA to
forgive the oversight.
To address this oversight:
1. We have amended the May 19, 2023, minutes to
explicitly state the areas of examination from
October 2022 that pertain to the requirements of
_BCO_ 21-4.

2. Moving forward, we will ensure that all parts of
the ordination examination—including the
requirement for theological and exegetical
papers—are fully documented in the Presbytery
minutes as they occur.
We appreciate the Committee’s diligence in reviewing our
records and will ensure full compliance with _BCO_ 21-4 and
_RAO_ 16-3.e.5 going forward.

**2024-12: Oct 12, 2023** ( _BCO_ 23-1) — No record that
congregation concurred with dissolution of pastoral relations.
**Response:** We acknowledge the citation regarding
the October 12, 2023, minutes, which noted the absence of a
record that the congregation of [Church Name Omitted],
Seattle, concurred with the dissolution of TE [Name
Omitted]’s pastoral relationship, as required by _BCO_ 23-1.
While it is true that the October 12, 2023, minutes did
not explicitly state that [Church Name Omitted] requested the
dissolution, we would note that, as TE [Name Omitted] was
an assistant pastor, congregational concurrence is not required
for the dissolution of his call. Per _BCO_ 23-1, the dissolution
of an assistant pastor’s call is governed by the will of the
Session and does not require congregational involvement.
However, we did confirm that [Church Name
Omitted], Seattle, did request the dissolution of TE [Name
Omitted]’s call as assistant pastor. The Ministers and
Churches Committee, acting as a commission of Presbytery
under Standing Rule 4.56.e, approved the dissolution in
response to this request. However, the request from the
Session of [Church Name Omitted] was inadvertently omitted
from the minutes.

To address this oversight, the Presbytery has
amended the October 12, 2023, minutes to explicitly state that
the dissolution was at the request of [Church Name Omitted],
ensuring compliance with _BCO_ 23-1. Moving forward, we
will ensure that all such requests and actions are explicitly
recorded in the Presbytery minutes to avoid any confusion.

with _BCO_ 13-2. Moving forward, we will ensure all such

minutes.

Those minutes are attached.

**2024-14: Oct 12, 2023** ( _BCO_ 19-2; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) — All
specific requirements of licensure exam not recorded.
**Response:** We acknowledge the citation regarding

specific requirements of the licensure exam for Mr. [ ]. Upon
review, we confirm that all specific requirements of _BCO_ 192 were administered during Mr. [ ]’s licensure exam,
including the requirement to preach a sermon. However, these
elements were not recorded in the minutes.

outlined in _BCO_ 19-2: Christian experience, theology, English
Bible, church government, and the preaching of a sermon. The
relevant minutes of the Feb. 7, 2025 meeting at which we
approved this amendment are attached.
We regret the omission and appreciate the
Committee’s diligence in ensuring compliance with the _BCO_ .
Moving forward, we will ensure that all examinations are
thoroughly documented in the minutes.

**e.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**unsatisfactory, therefore new responses shall be submitted**
**to the following GA:**
**2024-15: General 2023** ( _BCO_ 15-1; _RAO_ 16-3.e.4) —
Minutes of commission not entered in Presbytery minutes,
and these must be presented for review next year.
**Response:** We have received and reviewed the
citation regarding the General 2023 minutes, which noted that
the minutes of a commission were not entered into the
Presbytery minutes, as required by _BCO_ 15-1 and _RAO_ 163.e.4.

However, the citation does not specify which
commission is being referred to. We respectfully request that
the Committee on Review of Presbytery Records clarify
which commission and associated minutes are in question so
that we can investigate and take the appropriate corrective
action.

We remain committed to maintaining accurate and
complete records and will gladly address this matter once the
specific details are provided.
**Rationale:** Minutes of standing commission to
approve Presbytery’s minutes not recorded.
**2024-04: May 19, 2023** ( _PP_ 6) — No record that members of
interim/provisional Session were called by congregation.
**Response:** We respectfully disagree with the citation
that there is “no record that members of the

interim/provisional Session were called by the congregation.”
Provisional Sessions are appointed by the Presbytery and do
not require congregational approval. Specifically:

1. _BCO_ 5-8 assigns the authority to the Presbytery
to appoint a provisional Session for mission
churches or other churches unable to function

autonomously due to a lack of sufficient
leadership. The church at hand had one ruling
elder and no pastor.
2. _BCO_ 12-1 requires that a Session consist of at
least one Teaching Elder and one or more Ruling
Elders, and so this church had no session. This
meant that it is the Presbytery’s responsibility to

<a id="ga52-p673"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=675 printed_page=673 -->

ensure proper oversight, either by appointing a
provisional Session or through other means of
support.
3. The congregation’s role does not include calling
or approving members of a provisional Session
under the provisions of _BCO_ 5-8 or _BCO_ 13-1, as
these actions fall under the jurisdiction of the
Presbytery.
In this particular instance, the Presbytery
acted within its authority to address the
governance needs of the church by appointing
members to an interim/provisional Session.
While we acknowledge the importance of clearly
documenting such actions in Presbytery minutes
for the sake of clarity, the absence of
congregational approval in this process is not a
procedural error, as it is not required by the _BCO_ .
We appreciate the Committee’s diligence in
reviewing our records and will endeavor to ensure that future
minutes explicitly document the Presbytery’s actions in
appointing provisional Sessions for clarity and completeness.
**Rationale:** Presbytery did not address the issue
raised by Preliminary Principle 6, which indicates that a
presbytery does not have the power to elect persons to
exercise authority in any particular church. See also _BCO_ 162.

**d.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**satisfactory:**

<a id="ga52-p674"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=676 printed_page=674 -->

**2022-01: Feb 21, 2021** ( _BCO_ 15-3; 31-10; 34; 36) — Censure
inflicted by a judicial commission without process being
instituted or a judgment approved by the Presbytery.
**Response:** In the judicial case concerning T. E. [name
omitted] he consented to the recommendation made by the
Judicial Committee for definite suspension. He has pleaded
neither guilty nor not guilty to the charges made; the
Presbytery is waiting for the decision of the civil court before
it deals decisively with this case. However, Presbytery should
have made clearer in its minutes of the executive session that

“MSC” means that the motion was made, seconded, and
carried by Presbytery.
**Rationale:** Presbytery’s response does not address
the issue. a pres byte ry may suspend a minister from office
following the conclusion of judicial process; or, under 31-10,
he may be suspended from his official functions while under
process. Presbytery has not indicated that the minister is under

process.
**Response:** The minister in question had already
requested and been granted suspension from office by an act
of Presbytery on 2/11/2021 Minutes, Attachment E) and
remained under suspension from office, meaning that he could
not rightly exercise any of the functions of an ordained
teaching elder. The judicial process in our Presbytery had
begun with this suspension and was intensified when further
evidence surfaced of his offenses; the Presbytery deemed it
wise not to further the process until the teaching elder’s case
had been adjudicated in the civil court. The civil case took an
unusual amount of time for the adjudication to take place, and
in the end the teaching elder took an Alford plea which is
allowed in civil cases by the Supreme Court of the U.S. but
not in our church courts. At the specific request of and with
the approval of the teaching elder, the church trial was
postponed until after the civil case had been settled. The civil
case was concluded and the Presbytery in its meeting of May
11, 2023, resumed process against this teaching elder.
Arraignment was set for the August 10, 2023 meeting of
Presbytery. The teaching elder did not appear, and a motion
was made empowering the Stated Clerk to appoint a Judicial

<a id="ga52-p675"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=677 printed_page=675 -->

Commission to bring the matter to a conclusion and report
back to the Presbytery. Furthermore, it was moved, seconded,
and carried in the meeting of Palmetto Presbytery on
November 9, 2023 that this action of the presbytery on
11/9/23 be added to this response to GA/RPR by March 15,
2024.
**Rationale:** Presbytery has not adequately shown that
the TE was suspended under one of only two methods
prescribed in the _BCO_ : (1) as a censure or (2) as an
administrative act toward a teaching elder already under
process ( _BCO_ 31–10). The _BCO_ does not permit a presbytery
to suspend a teaching elder because he requests to be
suspended, nor as the first act of process.
**Response:** Please excuse our misunderstanding and
lack of clarification on the issues at hand in previous
responses. Palmetto Presbytery did institute process on
February 11, 2021 (minutes submitted), and presbytery acted
to administratively suspend TE [name omitted] (per _BCO_ 3110) from official functions, while presbytery awaited
resolution of the pending civil trial. TE [name omitted]
consented to this administrative suspension.
**2024-01: May 11, 2023** ( _BCO_ 20-8) — No record
commissioners appointed by the church presented and
prosecuted the call of a TE before Presbytery.
**Response:** Palmetto Presbytery agrees with the 20241: May 11, 2023 ( _BCO_ 20-8) cited exception of substance and
apologizes for the error of omission. The commissioner
appointed by the church to call TE [Name Omitted] as
Assistant Pastor was (TE [Name Omitted]) who was present
at the meeting. He presented and prosecuted the call of the
Church [Name Omitted] through the Presbytery’s Church &
Ministerial Health Committee (C&MH). The committee
reviewed the call and recommended approval of the call at the
May 11, 2023 meeting of Palmetto Presbytery. The
Presbytery unanimously approved the call. The Presbytery
regrets and apologizes for not fully recording this information
in its minutes to show that the _BCO_ 20-8 requirements were
fulfilled in this case and we will seek to do so in the future.

<a id="ga52-p676"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=678 printed_page=676 -->

**2024-02: Aug 10, 2023** ( _BCO_ 23-1) — No record Presbytery
cited the church to appear by its commissioners to show cause
why the resignation of a TE should be accepted.
**Response:** Palmetto Presbytery agrees with the 2024
RPR finding 2024-2: Aug 10, 2023 ( _BCO_ 23-1) and regrets
the error of omission. The RPR inquiry into an alleged
exception of substance regarding the resignation of a Palmetto
Presbytery Teaching Elder is correct that the Presbytery did
not specifically “cite the church to appear by its
commissioners to show cause why the resignation of a TE
should be accepted.” However, three Ruling Elders from the
church were present at this meeting and did show ample cause
why the resignation of TE (Name Omitted) should be
accepted. The church’s Clerk of Session also reported the
congregation’s vote of 163-4 to accept his resignation. These
facts were noted in the minutes of Palmetto Presbytery of
August 10, 2023 (pages 12-13).

**d.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**satisfactory:**
**2024-01: Jan 26, 2023** ( _BCO_ 31-2; _BCO_ 12-4(a)) — In
adopting recommendation No 1 of the commission, the
presbytery constituted an investigative commission under
_BCO_ 31-2 to investigate several matters, including “to
investigate matters surrounding a scandalous incident
involving an officer and two other members of [Church Name
Omitted].” Absent accepting a reference from the session, the

<a id="ga52-p677"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=679 printed_page=677 -->

presbytery has no authority to conduct a 31-2 investigation
into anyone other than a TE.
**Response:** Pee Dee Presbytery acknowledges to the
Assembly our error in the investigation of the church under
_BCO_ 31.2. This was not in the purview of the presbytery and
presbytery will not take this action in the future. Our minutes
have been noted that acknowledges this error.

**Response:** That the Philadelphia Presbytery direct

**Response:** Motion from the CT: That Presbytery

**Response:** That the Philadelphia Presbytery direct the Stated

examined the candidate in the areas prescribed but did not

**e.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**unsatisfactory, therefore new responses shall be submitted**
**to the following GA:**
**2023-03: Jan 15, 2022** ( _BCO_ 13-9.b; 40-1) — Presbytery did
not fulfill its constitutional duty to review session records
when it declined to review 2019 and 2020 minutes.
**Response:** The Philadelphia Presbytery has been
reviewing past Session Records, and will approve a new
process at the January 2024 meeting that will ensure Session
Records are reviewed in an expedited manner.

<a id="ga52-p679"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=681 printed_page=679 -->

**Rationale:** No evidence the Presbytery has been
reviewing these minutes.
**Response:** That the Philadelphia Presbytery direct
the Stated Clerk to amend the minutes of the January 15, 2023
as previously adopted to include the following: The
Philadelphia Presbytery agrees that we have not yet read and
approved the 2019 and 2020 minutes, but our inaction does
not mean that we are refusing to approve them. We have been
overwhelmed with several time-consuming disciplinary cases
over the past year that has occupied our time and manpower.
The Presbytery is committed to processing these minutes as
directed by RPR. The Presbytery thus directs the Stated Clerk
to make a marginal notation at the point of error in the original
minutes, directing readers to where they may find the
correction. Also, at the March 15, 2025 Stated Meeting the
following motion was passed: Whereas the PCA General
Assembly has cited the Philadelphia Presbytery for failing to
review its Sessions’ Record for the years 2018 and 2020,
2021, and 2023 Whereas the Book of Church Order ( _BCO_ )
40-1 requires the Presbytery to review the records of every
session subject to its jurisdiction yearly, Whereas the
Philadelphia undertook a review of the records of its sessions
for the years 2019 and 2023 and that these reviews were made
by two persons for each session and considered matters of
form and substance, The Coordinating Team proposes that it
create a committee of elders to review the session records for

the years 2019, 2020, 2021 and 2022 in the following manner:
Each Session’s records for these years would be reviewed by
only one reviewer. The reviewers would focus on identifying
possible instances of gross non-compliance with our
constitutional standards, not on exceptions of form or minor
exceptions of substance which may have been reported in the
2023 review of Session records. The committee is asked to
issue its report to the Presbytery by October 31, 2025. The
committee would consist of TE[s] [Names Omitted]’s name
was added. M/s/a by unanimous vote.
**Rationale:** Committee recognizes the challenges
each Presbytery faces but timely and thorough review of the
records of church Sessions are required, essential and an

<a id="ga52-p680"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=682 printed_page=680 -->

important distinction of the Presbyterian Church in America.
Presbytery must also record in their minutes that they
reviewed Session Minutes for years 2019 and 2020.
**2024-02: General 2023** ( _BCO_ 13.9.b; _BCO_ 40-1) — No
record of review of records of church sessions.
**Response:** From the March 15, 2025 Stated Meeting:

Whereas the PCA General Assembly has cited the
Philadelphia Presbytery for failing to review its Sessions’
Record for the years 2018 and 2020, 2021, and 2023 Whereas
the Book of Church Order ( _BCO_ ) 40-1 requires the Presbytery
to review the records of every session subject to its
jurisdiction yearly, Whereas the Philadelphia undertook a
review of the records of its sessions for the years 2019 and
2023 and that these reviews were made by two persons for
each session and considered matters of form and substance,
The Coordinating Team proposes that it create a committee of
elders to review the session records for the years 2019, 2020,
2021 and 2022 in the following manner: Each Session’s
records for these years would be reviewed by only one
reviewer. The reviewers would focus on identifying possible
instances of gross non-compliance with our constitutional
standards, not on exceptions of form or minor exceptions of
substance which may have been reported in the 2023 review
of Session records. The committee is asked to issue its report
to the Presbytery by October 31, 2025. The committee would
consist of TE[s] [Names Omitted]’s name was added. M/s/a
by unanimous vote.
**Rationale:** Each Presbytery is required under _BCO_
40-1 to review all Session minutes at least on a yearly basis.
The continued failure to fully review Session minutes for the
years 2019, 2020, 2021 and 2022 reflects a serious dereliction
of duty. The solution suggested by the Presbytery does not
appear to reflect the intent and spirit of the _BCO_ 40-2 which
requires a thorough examination of Session minutes and not
simply reviewing only for gross non compliance of
constitutional standards.

**f.** **That the following responses shall be submitted to the**
**following GA as no responses were received in 2025:**

<a id="ga52-p681"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=683 printed_page=681 -->

**2024-01: Mar 18, 2022** ( _BCO_ 43-3) — Complaint from lower
court (church Session) not included in regular meeting
minutes or executive session minutes.

**Response:** This was an oversight. These Executive
Session Minutes will be included in the 2024 Executive
Session packet for the 52 [nd] General Assembly.
**Rationale:** A clerical error occurred in the

preparation of the response form: the Presbytery was provided
with an incorrect exception entry. The Presbytery submitted a
thoughtful and timely response to the item as presented, but it
did not correspond to the actual exception noted in the report
approved by the 51 [st] General Assembly. The Secretary of the
CRPR apologizes for the mistake and regrets the confusion
caused. The Presbytery shall submit a response to the correct
exception to the 53 [rd] General Assembly.

64. That the Minutes of **Piedmont Triad Presbytery:** _47-11-1_
**a.** Be approved without exception: **May 30, 2024; Aug 10,**
**2024; Aug 10, 2024 (ES); Sep 19, 2024; Oct 3, 2024; Nov 9,**
**2024; Nov 9, 2024 (ES).**
**b.** Be approved with exception of form: **General 2024.**
**c.** Be approved with exception of substance:
**2025-01: Feb 10, 2024** ( _BCO_ 20-8) — No record
commissioners appointed by the church presented and
prosecuted call before presbytery.
**2025-02: Feb 10, 2024** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.6) —
Presbytery incorrectly judged a candidate’s stated difference
as merely semantic. Minute reads: " I would take more of the
continental reformers view that part of the Sabbath can be
spent in recreation. After church, going to a game, or taking a
trip to the mountains, etc.”.

<a id="ga52-p682"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=684 printed_page=682 -->

**2025-03: May 11, 2024** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.6) —
Presbytery incorrectly judged a candidate’s stated differences
as merely semantic.
First minute read: “I have a perceived difference with
the _WCF_ 4.1 (cf. also _WLC_ 15; _WSC_ 9) which states: “It
pleased God the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost, for the
manifestation of the glory of His eternal power, wisdom, and
goodness, in the beginning, to create or make of nothing the
world, and all things therein, whether visible or invisible, in
the space of six days, and all very good.” The Confession here
appears to teach that creation took place in the span of six,
literal 24 hour periods. I do not believe an orthodox view of
creation necessitates the “Calendar Day Interpretation” of
Genesis 1-2. That being said, I believe God called all things,
both invisible and visible, out of nothing (ex nihilo) into being
by his voice, as the Belgic Confession 12 states without
reference to the span of time: “We believe that the Father,
when it seemed good to him, created heaven and earth and all
other creatures from nothing, by the Word—that is to say, by
the Son.”

Second minute reads: “I have a perceived difference
with the _WCF_ 21.8 (cf. also _WLC_ 1117, 119; _WSC_ 60), which
states: “This Sabbath is to be kept holy unto the Lord when
men, after a due preparing of their hearts, and ordering of their
common affairs beforehand, do not only observe an holy rest
all the day from their own works, words, and thoughts about
their worldly employments and recreations; but also are taken
up the whole time in the public and private exercises of His
worship, and in the duties of necessity and mercy.” The
Confession here appears to teach that personal, private
activities for pleasure are not appropriate on the Sabbath. I
believe that New Covenant Christians keep the Sabbath holy
primarily by worshiping God weekly on the First Day (i.e.
Sunday). We rest from our work and ordinary labors in order
to facilitate our worship of God. Recreational activities that
do not hinder our ability to worship are still permissible. This
view is expressed in the Heidelberg Catechism 103: “Q. What
is God’s will for you in the fourth commandment? A. First,
that the gospel ministry and education for it be maintained,

<a id="ga52-p683"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=685 printed_page=683 -->

and that, especially on the festive day of rest, I diligently
attend the assembly of God’s people to learn what God’s
Word teaches, to participate in the sacraments, to pray to God
publicly, and to bring Christian offerings for the poor.”” Both
statements depart from the Standards’ teaching on the relevant
topics, and thus are more than semantic.
**2025-04: May 11, 2024** ( _BCO_ 21-4; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5) —
Presbytery incorrectly judged a candidate’s stated differences
as merely semantic. Both stated differences actually differ
from the Confession of Faith.

The first difference reads: “I have a perceived
difference with the _WCF_ Chapter 21, Section 8: “The Sabbath
is then kept holy unto the Lord, when men, after a due
preparing of their hearts, and ordering of their affairs
beforehand, do not only observe an holy rest, all the day, from
their own, works, words, and thoughts about their worldly
employments, and recreations, but also are taken up the whole
time, in the public and private exercises of his worship, and in
the duties of necessity, and mercy.” though i do believe it is
important to keep the Sabbath as the Lord’s Day and to
worship on the first day of the week, when Christ rose. As the
pattern laid out in scripture. I do not think we have to literally
rest from all worldly matters to keep this. As humans, we are
to set aside a day of the week to worship and rest in the Lord.
I think the proper way to observe the Sabbath day is to gather
together as the body and worship our God and King. (Matt.
28, Acts 2) I do think it is important to set aside the Lord’s
Day to be purposeful in your reflection on God and gather
with the body in your local church. This way you hear the
Word proclaimed, pray/reflect on the week. And partake of
the Lord’s Supper in order to nourish your faith. Though I do
not think per the confession, you can do nothing on the
Sabbath. I do think doing things with your family, recreation,
yardwork, etc. can all be means of rest and bring glory to God.
This is part of our living our lives daily as Christians seeking
to honor and serve Christ daily. In other words, as Christians
we shouldn’t only think about Christ and his word on Sunday.
Rather we are to live our daily lives praising him and serving

<a id="ga52-p684"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=686 printed_page=684 -->

him daily in our various callings. The Sabbath is the day set
aside to prepare to live out this calling.”
The second difference reads: “I have perceived
difference with _WCF_ chapter 4 section 1: “It pleased God the
Father, Son, and Holy Ghost, and for the manifestation of the
glory of his eternal power, wisdom, and goodness in the
beginning, to create, or make of nothing, the world, and all
things therein whether visible or invisible, in the space of six
days; and all very good.” My view of creation would be the
standard day age view. The ‘six days’ are understood in the
same sense as “in that day” of Isaiah 11:10-11 —that is, as
periods of indefinite length and not necessarily of 24 hours
duration. There are other similar uses of the Hebrew word for

“day” (yôm) in Scripture to support this view of periods
longer than 24 hours including that in the very context of
Genesis 2:4. Another argument for this approach is that the
seventh day in Genesis 1 is not concluded with the boundary
phrase, “and there was evening, and there was morning” as
with the other days, and therefore it continues, as indicated by
Hebrews 4:1-11's quotation of Psalm 95:11.”
**d.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**satisfactory:**
**2024-01: Feb 11, 2023** ( _BCO_ 18-2) — No record of sixmonths membership for candidate.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the General
Assembly. We regret this oversight and will be more careful
in the future to make sure the six-month membership
requirement is noted. The candidate in question did, indeed,
meet that requirement.
**2024-02: May 13, 2023** ( _BCO_ 18-2) — No record of sixmonths membership for candidate.
**Response:** Presbytery respectfully disagrees with the
General Assembly. The six-month membership requirement
for this candidate is noted in Appendix M of the minutes,
where the Session endorsement reads: “[Candidate] has been
a member of [our church] since Sept 2022” (which was more
than six-months). There is no _BCO_ requirement that it be
recorded in the body of the meeting minutes, and RPR noted
that it did read the endorsement in the appendix. With that

<a id="ga52-p685"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=687 printed_page=685 -->

said, presbytery will do its best to record the six-month
membership in the body of the meeting minutes from now on
for the sake of clarity.
**2024-03: Aug 12, 2023** ( _BCO_ 15-1; _RAO_ 16-3.e.4) —
Minutes of commission not entered in Presbytery minutes,
and these must be presented for review next year.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees that it failed to include
the commission minutes. That was an error on the part of the
Stated Clerk, not the commission. The commission report is
now included in the minutes of the November 9, 2024, Stated
Meeting. Please forgive the Stated Clerk’s oversight.
**2024-04: Feb 11, 2023** ( _RAO_ 15-2) — Presbytery allowed an
the same RE to serve on Overtures Committee for more than

two consecutive assemblies without two-thirds vote of

Presbytery.
**Response:** Presbytery agrees with the General
Assembly. We did not record that the vote to empower the
Moderator to fill any openings for General Assembly was
unanimous, which meets the 2/3 threshold required by the
_BCO_ when sending a commissioner to the Overtures
Committee after more than two consecutive years. Given that
the _BCO_ has this special vote threshold in order to protect the
minority in cases where more than one candidate is nominated
for Overtures, we do not believe the action of our Moderator
to appoint a commissioner fora fourth consecutive year was
unjustified given that there was an opening and no minority
was prevented from sending another delegate. We regret the
oversight and will be more careful to record our specific
actions in the future.

<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=688 printed_page=686 -->

**2025-02: Oct 19, 2024** ( _BCO_ 24-1) — Presbytery approved a

**d.** **That the following responses to the 52** **[nd]** **GA be found**
**satisfactory:**
**2023-01: May 31, 2022** ( _BCO_ 21-4.f) — Presbytery judged a
stated difference to be “merely semantic,” and that decision
needs clarification in light of the candidate’s own words as
recorded in the minutes.

**Response:** This is the exception to which the General
Assembly exception has been taken: “It is my view that the
Second Commandment’s prohibition against images is always
in the context of worship. Therefore, I believe that images in
a worship context (e.g. stained glass windows or icons
depicting Jesus in a church sanctuary) are a violation of the
Second Commandment. But I do not believe that images of
Jesus used in a pedagogical context (e.g. The Jesus Storybook
Bible or Sunday School curriculum for children) are a
violation of the Second Commandment. I also believe _WLC_

109’s prohibition against images of Christ “in our mind” goes
further than what Scripture requires and seems to me to be
unrealistic in practice as we read the Gospel accounts in the
New Testament. I believe that to worship any images of the
Triune God (whether external or mental) violates the Second
Commandment.” (Bold emphasis stated clerk’s). Pittsburgh
Presbytery had ruled this was merely semantic. In discussing
the highlighted sentence, presbyters agreed that it does seem
to raise the exception beyond “merely semantic” to the level
of “more than semantic, but not out of accord with any
fundamental of our system of doctrine.” It was m/s/c to adopt
the following response by Pittsburgh Presbytery: Upon further
reflection, the presbytery does agree that the words “goes
further than what the Scripture requires” does move the
exception to the level of “more than semantic, but not out of
accord with any fundamental of our system of doctrine,” and
the minutes have been notated accordingly.
**2024-01: Jan 28, 2023** ( _BCO_ 19-2.e-f.; _RAO_ 16-3.e.5(a-d))
— No record of if candidates states any differences to the

<a id="ga52-p687"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=689 printed_page=687 -->

Westminister Standards, and if any, presbytery’s judgment of
these ( _RAO_ 16-3.e.5(a-d)).
**Response:** Concerning 2024-1, the licentiate in
question was finishing an exam in January 2023 that began in
the October 2022 stated meeting, where his differences were
listed, categorized and approved (Minutes of Pittsburgh
Presbytery 2022, 22-69.5, p. 188).

interns’ updates.

**2024-03: Apr 1, 2023** ( _BCO_ 19-12) — Incomplete review of

**Response:** Concerning 2024-2,3,4,5, regarding the

(Minutes of Pittsburgh Presbytery 2024, 24-05.5, p. 86), and

required.
**2024-04: Jul 22, 2023** ( _BCO_ 19-12) — Incomplete review of

**Response:** Concerning 2024-2,3,4,5, regarding the

**2024-05: Oct 21, 2023** ( _BCO_ 19-12) — Incomplete review of

<a id="ga52-p688"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=690 printed_page=688 -->

66. That the Minutes of **Platte Valley Presbytery:** _61-0-1_
**a.** Be approved without exception: **General 2024; Apr 20,**
**2024; Aug 10, 2024; Oct 26, 2024.**
**b.** Be approved with exception of form: **None.**
**c.** Be approved with exception of substance:
**2025-01: Nov 4, 2023** ( _BCO_ 21-4.f; _BCO_ 21-4.g) —
Presbytery appears to have incorrectly ruled the following
stated difference as merely semantic. Candidate appears to
deny the spiritual presence of Christ in the Lord’s Supper,
holding only to a memorialist view of the sacrament, which is
hostile to our system of doctrine. In candidate’s own words:
“I do affirm that by the Spirit, Christ is present in and at the
observance of the Supper. I believe that the Spirit does
confirm and encourage the faith of those who receive the
elements by faith in following Jesus in a unique way. We are
to remember with love for Christ and sorrow for our sins his

body and blood which were sacrificed for us on the cross. But
I cannot say that we feed on his spiritual blood or spiritual
body in some literal fashion. The act, both physically and
spiritually, occurred once and for all time at Golgotha. We
rightly counter Roman Catholics who seem repeatedly to
crucify Christ when practicing transubstantiation that turns
the bread and wine into the corporeal body and blood to feed
upon. I am uncomfortable with the Reformed rite if it turns
the bread and wine into his spiritual body and spiritual blood
upon which we literally feed. The RCC bodily crucifies Christ
again and again through the Mass—or so it appears to many
Protestants, myself included. I don’t wish to appear to crucify
Christ spiritually again and again through a Lord’s Supper that
has recipients eating the spiritual body and spiritual blood.
When we “feed on him in our hearts by faith with
thanksgiving,” I interpret this to mean that we pause to
remember and celebrate that at the cross it was finished both

in body and in spirit for our salvation—hallelujah and amen.
We figuratively feed upon the accomplished work of Christ at
the cross as we apply again and again its saving graces to our
daily lives through the agency of the supper when
accompanied by faith through the Holy Spirit. Again, I may
be misunderstanding what the article is saying, in which case,

<a id="ga52-p689"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=691 printed_page=689 -->

I welcome conversation, and this might not be an exception.
For more see the Larger Catechism 168-170 and the Shorter
Catechism 96.”.
**2025-02: Feb 3, 2024** ( _BCO_ 15-2) — The Presbytery
established a commission of one person.
**2025-03: May 11, 2024** ( _BCO_ 13-12) — Notice for a called
meeting not in order (number and names of elders requesting
the called meeting is not documented; purpose of the meeting
not stated and/or there was business conducted beyond the
purpose of the called meeting).

68. That the Minutes of **Providence Presbytery:** _57-0-0_
**a.** Be approved without exception: **Feb 6, 2024 (ES); May 7,**
**2024 (ES); Sep 17, 2024; Nov 5, 2024.**
**b.** Be approved with exception of form: **None.**
**c.** Be approved with exception of substance:
**2025-01: General 2024** ( _BCO_ 40, _BCO_ 5-3; Providence
Presbytery Bylaws) — Incomplete record of review of records
