<a id="ga52-p799"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=801 printed_page=799 -->

**REPORT OF**

**RIDGE HAVEN**

**BREVARD, NC – CONO, IA**

_See 52-27 (p. 53) for the report of the Committee of Commissioners_

_and the Assembly’s action_

Ridge Haven aims to be an indispensable resource to the PCA church,
remaining faithful to the fundamentals of the PCA and praying that all who
come through Ridge Haven would know Christ more when they leave campus
than when they arrived. Our hope for all staff and guests is that they can claim
along with Paul that “He has delivered us from the domain of darkness and
transferred us to the kingdom of his beloved Son, in whom we have
redemption, the forgiveness of sins” (Colossians 1:13).

The Lord has seen fit to bless His ministry at Ridge Haven once again in many
ways, notably through the difficult event of Hurricane Helene. By God’s grace,
all staff, their families, and homes, were safe. However, this still was an
immensely hard time for our staff who were without electricity and limited cell
service for 11 days. The devastation around us and the isolation was heavy,
yet the Lord strengthened the hearts and unity of our community to not only
immediately address our own needs but those of the surrounding area as well.
During this time, groups were unable to access our campus, however our staff
was remarkable in their efforts to clean up the main campus and repair
damages so that once power was restored, we could immediately host groups
again.

Despite this significant event that cancelled nearly 1,000 expected guests, we
were still able to host over 13,000 people and over 200 unique churches and
ministries this past year. We continue to marvel at how the Lord works in our
church to grow His number and build up the body for His glory. it is such a
joy to get a front row seat to see how the Lord is unifying the church and
raising up leaders for ministry. I am encouraged and want to ask us all to lift
our voices in praise for our victorious Savior who continues to prove He has
conquered all things.

<a id="ga52-p800"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=802 printed_page=800 -->

Ridge Haven provides camp, conference, retreat, and educational
opportunities for the church year-round. In Brevard we have facilities to host
over 800 individuals at a time and often accommodate up to seven unique
ministries on campus at once. We currently have a lake, two activity fields,
one open-air recreational center and one winterized year-round gymnasium,
meeting spaces that can hold up to 1,250 people, a dining facility that can serve
over 400 people in one seating, miles of hiking trails, a coffee shop, a soda
shoppe, three camp stores, and much more throughout our more than 700 acres
of property. At Cono, we offer year-round educational opportunities, youth
gatherings, retreat services and summer camps that have seen fit to host five
PCA Presbytery camps and numerous other ministries this past year. We hope
to finish our activity pool at Cono by this summer. In Brevard we have just
started four family cabins, and a chapel atop Keyes Summit. We will soon
break ground for a new lake that we hope to have completed by the end of the

year.

What appears to be a major time of disconnect in our society, with people
becoming more virtual and personal contact often limited to social media,
Ridge Haven has been able to provide an opportunity for our churches to
**reconnect** . It is our desire to provide the space and opportunity for families,
youth groups, churches, college ministries, and many others to reconnect with
one another, build up the family of believers, and spend extended time at the
foot of the cross together. As long as churches and ministries see fit to
maximize our facilities, we will seek to grow this ministry to accommodate
them. After a year in which over 200 ministries saw value in spending time
gathering at Ridge Haven, we look forward with eager expectation to what the
Lord has in store over the coming years. Though delayed by Helene, we hope
to make a major announcement about our new Capital Campaign this fall for
continued expansion to both of our campuses and ministries.

it is a privilege to minister alongside a staff that desires to be of service and a
resource to the church. This truly is a great joy and honor that we get to serve
others. Our hope and prayer are that our work is of great service to the church
and provides a respite for the body of Christ in hopes that they are better
equipped to re-engage with the work the Lord has called them to do. This
central element to our ministry, service, is one that our youth value greatly.
One former staff member had this to say when reflecting on how the Lord has
used the opportunity to serve at Ridge Haven in her life: “[Amidst] the sin and
the struggles it is hard to keep your eyes set on things above and on Christ and

<a id="ga52-p801"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=803 printed_page=801 -->

the beauty of the Cross. Being at Ridge Haven has shown me what it looks like
to live out the gospel every day and to pursue Christ in an intentional way so
that you can show Christ through your life.”

We are humbled to continue seeing an expansion in our summer staff and
Explorer’s Project (leadership development program for those in high school
up through college graduates) and are glad to provide opportunities for well
over 150 staff each year to participate in service and ministry to the church.
Our constant prayer and hope are that the Lord will continue to use Ridge
Haven to develop and grow leaders for the future of the church. We are thrilled
to see a high number of our staff that have gone on into ministry and have
taken on roles of service in the church. There is a heart and desire in our youth
to see the glory of God go forward and once they witness it here at camp it is
infectious, and they crave to see it in every aspect of their lives.

One individual, Isaac Hathaway, who first served with us during the summer
of 2022, joined our Gap-Year Program and this past year was promoted to
being our Summer Counselor Director. As Isaac has openly shared, he faced
some dark and difficult times in his life before reaching out to Ridge Haven
while he was at a loss for what to do. When asked about how the Lord turned

his life around, he said, “From where I was in utter darkness, far from the Lord,
not aware of my need for Him, to where i am now. I can truly say that Ridge
haven ha splayed an incredible part in guiding met o be ina place where i can
hear the Lord... and let the Lord guide me.” The community of believers and
chance to engage in gospel ministry set Isaac’s heart on fire for Christ. Isaac
is an incredible man and leader who returned to college and is now finishing
his undergraduate degree from Covenant College with hopes of pursuing a life
of ministry. Our hope is that he would return to Ridge Haven in a full-time
capacity, yet we are thrilled to know that wherever the Lord calls him his desire
is to serve and support the church.

Again, God is reminding us daily that He is victorious and has conquered all
things. This daily reminder is what drives us on to seek ways to better serve
and support the PCA and prayerfully be used by God to glorify His name and
witness to the ends of the earth. Please continue to pray that the Lord’s
protection would cover every facet of the ministry here. Please continue to
pray for the hearts and minds of our staff who are being tasked with intense
service and gospel evangelism. Please pray for wisdom and guidance from the

<a id="ga52-p802"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=804 printed_page=802 -->

Lord as we seek ways to continue growing alongside the PCA and desire for
more individuals to know the saving grace of our Savior.

**R** **ECOMMENDATIONS**

1. That the proposed budget for 2026 for Ridge Haven be approved.

2. That the minutes of the Board of Directors of Ridge Haven for March 11
13, 2024, and September 16-18, 2024, be approved without exceptions.

3. That February 15, 2026, be a day for our churches to pray for the ministries

of Ridge Haven.

4. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly

answer **Overture 9** from James River Presbytery, “Amend _BCO_ 14-1 to
Make AC Members Appointed by Committees and Agencies Non-voting
Advisory Members,” in the **NEGATIVE** .That the Overtures Committee
recommend that the General Assembly answer **Overture 9** from James
River Presbytery “Amend _BCO 14-1_ to Make AC Members Appointed by
Committees and Agencies Non-voting Advisory Members" in the
**NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

We believe that the overture fails to make its case that the

current membership of the AC undermines or is contrary to a
grassroots Presbyterianism. Every member of the AC, whether atlarge or appointed by the Committees and Agencies, has been
duly elected by the General Assembly.

We also fail to see how the disenfranchisement of nine

members of the AC, each of whom has been duly elected by the
General Assembly, better serves the corporate mission of our
beloved church. To the contrary, we believe this change fails to
take into account the vital importance of unity in our mission.

The unity of our mission governs the division of labor
among our committees and agencies. As _BCO_ 14-1.3 states, “The
work of the Church as set forth in the Great Commission is one

work, being implemented at the General Assembly level through
equally essential committees.” Dr. Morton Smith highlights the
priority of unity in his commentary on this section of the _BCO_
when he states,

In this chapter of the Book, which provides for
the erection of several different committees of the

<a id="ga52-p803"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=805 printed_page=803 -->

Assembly to assist it in carrying out the Great
Commission, the unity of the Church's work is
affirmed. This unity is at times overlooked as
individuals get involved with one or another
aspect of the Church's work. Realizing this
danger, this affirmation is made, before a division
of labor is made at the Assembly level. To
reinforce this concept of the unity of the work of
the Church, the last part of the sentence asserts
that the work of the Assembly is to be
implemented through equally essential
committees. This concept is all too often
neglected, as one committee is elevated above
others in the minds of the people. [1]

We believe that the unity of our mission is uniquely
expressed in the Administrative Committee in our current polity,
both in the scope of its work and in the makeup of its membership.
To remove the voting rights of those members appointed by the
Committees and Agencies, therefore, is to diminish the unity of
our mission. It also serves, contrary to the design of the Overture,
to practically elevate the AC even further above the other
committees and agencies, whose budgets the AC approves.

It should be noted that Dr. Smith, a champion of the
grassroots nature of the PCA, makes no critical comments in his
commentary regarding the makeup of the AC. While arguments
from silence do not carry much weight, Smith is explicit and firm
in his concerns elsewhere. This makes his silence regarding the
AC slightly more audible.

It also highlights what is of fundamental importance in our
polity with regard to committees and agencies. Among Dr.
Smith’s stated concerns in _BCO_ 14-1 is that the committees

understand and operate according to their relationship to the
General Assembly. He rightly states, with regard to _BCO_ 14-1.7,
that “a committee is always the servant of the appointing body,”

1
Morton H. Smith, _Commentary on the Book of Church Order of the Presbyterian_
_Church in America_, 6 [th] ed.,
(Taylors, SC: Presbyterian Press, 2007), 139.

<a id="ga52-p804"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=806 printed_page=804 -->

i.e., the General Assembly. The Overture implies that by allowing
appointed members both voice and vote, the AC is less directly
accountable to the General Assembly. To borrow Smith’s phrase,
it renders the AC less of a “servant of the appointing body.” But
every member of the AC—whether at-large or appointed—is
accountable to the General Assembly. And each appointed
member is additionally accountable to the Committee or Board
that appointed him. Thus, while we are sympathetic with the
concern for undue power and influence on any committee, we are
not convinced of the nature of the problem outlined in the
Overture, nor of the solution offered.

5. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly

answer **Overture 11** from South Texas Presbytery, “To Study
Incorporating Bitcoin into Session, Presbytery, and Committee & Agency
Balance Sheets” in the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

This overture is not necessary and seems to lie outside the scope
of the mission entrusted to us. Further, the overture’s focus on one
particular investment/asset is peculiarly narrow.

6. That the Overtures Committee recommend that the General Assembly

answer **Overture 27** from Northwest Georgia Presbytery, “Erect Ad
Interim Committee to Receive Suggestions for Permanent Committee and
Agency Operations, Resources, and Expenses” in the **NEGATIVE** .

Grounds:

Given that an Ad Interim Committee may only be erected to
“handle particular matters of business” ( _RAO_ 9-1); and, given that
PCA Committees and Agencies are already required to submit
their annual budgets to the Administrative Committee for
evaluation and recommendation to the General Assembly ( _RAO_
4-11); and, given that the description of the work of the proposed
committee is expressed in language which leaves many open
questions; Therefore, the Ridge Haven Board of Directors
recommends that Overture 27 from Northwest Georgia Presbytery
be answered in the NEGATIVE.

<a id="ga52-p805"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=807 printed_page=805 -->

Supplemental Report

**SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF**

**RIDGE HAVEN**

**Additional Recommendations for the 52** **[nd]** **General Assembly**

7. Regarding **Overture 49** from Pacific Northwest Presbytery “Erect an Ad

Interim Committee on AI,” we believe this is a worthy topic of study, but
have no specific advice to the OC as to whether or not the PCA should
establish a study committee on it.

<a id="ga52-p806"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=808 printed_page=806 -->

Second Supplemental Report

**SECOND SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF**

**RIDGE HAVEN**

**Additional Recommendations for the 52** **[nd]** **General Assembly**

8. The Ridge Haven Board of Directors recommends that the Overtures

Committee recommend that the General Assembly answer **Overture 30**
from Houston Metro Presbytery, “Amend _BCO_ 8-4, 20-1, 21-1, 21-12, and
23-1 re the Calling and Dissolution of TE Relationships for Needful
Works,” by **DEFERRING** it to the 53 [rd] General Assembly in 2026.

Grounds:

We believe this overture generally clarifies our current polity in a
helpful and needed way. However, since the Overture arrived after
our stated Board meeting, and the matter bears no apparent
urgency, we believe it best to defer to the 53 [rd] General Assembly.

<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=809 printed_page=807 -->

**III. REPORT OF THE CASES**

**CASE 2023-05**

**MS. EMILY HYLAND**

**v.**

**CHICAGO METRO PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON COMPLAINT**

**October 17, 2024**

**A minute explanatory** : The vote on the following decision was 12-12-0;
therefore, the decision of the Presbytery stands by virtue of an equally divided
Standing Judicial Commission.

**CASE SUMMARY**

Complainant was a church employee who was terminated by the Pastor of the
church. She subsequently began, but did not complete, the _BCO_ 43 complaint
process with her Session and filed employment discrimination and wage
claims with two agencies of the State and subsequently filed charges against
the Pastor and the Associate Pastor of the church. Presbytery received those
charges as accusations and investigated in accordance with _BCO_ 31-2,
declining to indict the accused after failing to raise a strong presumption of
guilt. Complainant then lodged a complaint against Presbytery for its decision
not to indict, which complaint Presbytery then denied for being out of order
due to lack of standing. Complainant then carried her complaint to the General
Assembly.

The case was received by the SJC and assigned to a Panel. That Panel
determined that the Complaint was Judicially Out of Order due to _OMSJC_ 10.5
defects, but at its March (2024) Stated Meeting the SJC appointed a new Panel
to further consider the matter ( _OMSJC_ 10.6). The second Panel conducted a
full hearing on April 5, 2024 and reached the same conclusion as the original
Panel. This Complaint is Judicially Out of Order.

**I.** **SUMMARY OF THE FACTS:**

03/19/21 Emily Hyland, a member of Naperville Presbyterian Church
(NPC) and an employee of the church since June 1, 2013, was

<a id="ga52-p811"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=813 printed_page=811 -->

terminated from her employment. At the time of her
termination, she had been serving NPC in the position of
Director of Operations.

04/09/21 NPC Session received notification of representation from a law
firm evaluating claims arising from the termination of Ms.
Hyland’s employment. The notification included a statement:
“we have concluded that the Church’s decision to terminate her

gives rise to serious claims of sex discrimination and
retaliation.” The attorney representing Ms. Hyland requested
copies of personnel documents relating to Ms. Hyland.

04/19/21 Ms. Hyland requested “a meeting to address the entire NPC
elderboard in person regarding the events leading up to [her]
firing on March 19th, 2021.” It was also requested that
“subsequent actions of the pastors and session members” be
addressed. Ms. Hyland further conditioned her request by
adding that she desired the meeting to be held away from the
church, and that her counselor and a third-party representative
be present.

05/07/21 The NPC Session acceded to Ms. Hyland’s April 19, 2021
request for a meeting, but stipulated its own conditions, which
were that Ms. Hyland sign a release of liability pertaining to
employment issues with the church, that ten minutes would be
permitted for her to make her statement to the session, and
confirmation that she considered herself a member of the

church.

05/19/21 Ms. Hyland submitted by email to the CMP Stated Clerk a
written Complaint against the NPC Session. The Clerk, on that
same day, confirmed receipt of the email.

05/25/21 The CMP Stated Clerk responded to Ms. Hyland regarding her
complaint against the NPC Session by giving his explanation of
complaint procedure under _BCO_ 43. He stated that “before
Chicago Metro Presbytery would be able to hear the case, she
would first have to file a written complaint with the Session of
Naperville Presbyterian Church.”

<a id="ga52-p812"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=814 printed_page=812 -->

11/15/21 Ms. Hyland submitted to CMP through its Stated Clerk specific
allegations regarding the two Teaching Elders serving at NPC –
TE Dane Ortlund and TE David Chu. In a cover email submitted

with the allegations, Ms. Hyland stated:

“After much thought and prayerful
consideration, I am submitting charges per _BCO_
32.2 against TE Ortlund and TE Chu. Attached
are the two charges. I’ve also attached a list of
individuals who were witness to some of the

events and have included their phone numbers
wherever possible.”

Her email also included questions regarding the possible
submission of affidavits and documentary evidence. She
inquired as to whether materials would be routed to the
Administrative Committee of the Presbytery for an initial
review.

11/15/21 In addition to the cover email listed in the immediately
preceding entry, Ms. Hyland submitted to the Stated Clerk and
the Shepherding Committee Chairman of CMP her list of
allegations against each Teaching Elder, citing many of the Ten
Commandments and specifying how she perceived each
Commandment had been violated by each Teaching Elder. In
regard to each Teaching Elder individually, she included in her
materials this statement:

“For these reasons I am making the following charges
against TE Paul David Chu per _BCO_ 32-2. I ask that
the Chicago Metro Presbytery investigate this report,
initiate appropriate disciplinary measures and require
TE Chu to make satisfactory explanations concerning
these charges against him.”

Per the requirements of the 9th commandment, to
hold my peace in suppression of iniquity would be a
sin. By the same commandment I am also required to

<a id="ga52-p813"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=815 printed_page=813 -->

care for my own good name. For these reasons I am
making the following charges against TE Dane
Ortlund of Naperville Presbyterian Church
(hereinafter NPC) per _BCO_ 32-2. I ask that the
Chicago Metro Presbytery investigate this report,
initiate appropriate disciplinary measures and require
TE Ortlund to make satisfactory explanations
concerning these charges against him.

11/18/21 The CMP Shepherding Committee chairman forwarded the
accusations to the accused parties as a first investigatory step.

01/24/22 TE Chu emailed TE Erik Oldfather of the CMP Shepherding
Committee on behalf of himself and TE Ortlund and requested
an investigation into the “allegations” made by Ms. Hyland,
pursuant to _BCO_ 31-2.

02/16/22 CMP formed an Investigative Committee [1] to investigate Ms.
Hyland’s accusations against TEs Ortlund and Chu Pursuant to
“ _BCO_ 31-2.”

02/24/22 The Investigative Committee of CMP met many times for differing
to 08/15/22 purposes, including to conduct interviews of the Accuser, those

Accused and selected others. At the Committee meeting of
August 15, a report was approved to be presented to CMP.

08/15/22 The Report approved by the Investigative Committee included
the following statement:

“The Hyland Reports are styled as charges
against TE Ortlund and TE Chu under _BCO_ 322. Notwithstanding this designation, the
Committee notes that these are not properly
charges under _BCO_ 32-2. Rather, the Presbytery
and the Committee have received these

documents as “reports” under _BCO_ 31-2, which

1 The Record of the Case refers to this committee by a number of various names:
e.g., Ad Hoc Investigation Committee, Investigative Committee, Judicial Committee,
Ad Hoc Committee, Ad Hoc Investigatory Committee, etc.

<a id="ga52-p814"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=816 printed_page=814 -->

gave rise to the formation of the Committee and
its charge to investigate.
As such, the Committee is not bound by the form
or structure of the “charges” laid out in the
Hyland Reports.”

ina footnote to the above, the Committee also reported:
“Formal process (including the filing of charges under _BCO_ 322) would commence only in the event that the Presbytery
determined there was a strong presumption of guilt of one or
both of the accused parties.”

08/17/22 The Investigative Committee presented its report in Executive
Session at the 69 [th] stated meeting of CMP. While in Executive
Session CMP took the following actions on the Committee’s
Recommendations:

(1) To find that the reports from Emily Hyland did “not

create a strong presumption of guilt against the
character of TE Ortlund or TE Chu under _BCO_ 31-2.”

(2) To determine that the matter be “concluded without

initiating formal process.”

CMP also acted in Executive Session to “instruct the

administrative committee [of CMP] to consult with legal
counsel and report back to the next stated meeting concerning
potential communications with Ms. Hyland regarding the
investigation.”

08/19/22 TE Ortlund and TE Chu submitted a list of perceived
inaccuracies or corrections in the report, requesting that the
Committee’s Report submitted to Presbytery and acted upon at
the August 17 [th], 2022 meeting be edited to include those
corrections.

09/29/22 The Investigative Committee presented its previously submitted
report at a called meeting of CMP of this date with the edits
requested by the two affected teaching elders. In Executive
Session, the CMP took the following actions:

<a id="ga52-p815"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=817 printed_page=815 -->

(1) “to receive the committee’s report as amended.”
(2) to keep the report within Executive Session, yet to

distribute it to “Emily Hyland and the NPC Session
and permit NPC’s Session and the Administrative
Committee to, upon their discretion, discuss elements
of the document with external parties.”
(3) to approve the report as amended, and
(4) to dismiss the committee with thanks.

11/29/22 Ms. Hyland filed her complaint against the disposition of her
matter before CMP with the Stated Clerk of CMP.

11/30/22 CMP wrote the Stated Clerk of the PCA seeking advice on the
standing of local church accusers to files complaints against acts
of Presbytery.

01/27/23 CMP received from the Office of the Stated Clerk of the PCA
the following advice in response to its query of November 27 [th],
2023:

“Your interpretation of _BCO_ 43-1 is correct and
consistent with how the Standing Judicial Commission
has adjudicated such issues in the past. A person may only
submit a complaint against a court to whose jurisdiction
he or she is subject. For a presbytery, this is only those
Teaching Elders who are members of that presbytery and
any ruling elder who was present a ta meeting and was a
commissioner to that meeting (but only to such meetings
that they were present and a commissioner). Complaints,
therefore, may only be made against a court of original
jurisdiction.”

02/15/23 The complaint of Ms. Hyland submitted to CMP was ruled out
of order on the grounds that Ms. Hyland was not under the
jurisdiction of Presbytery ( _BCO_ 43-1).

03/21/23 Ms. Hyland registered her complaint against CMP with the
office of the Stated Clerk of the PCA.

<a id="ga52-p816"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=818 printed_page=816 -->

07/24/23 The first SJC Panel determined the case to be Judicially Out of
Order on the grounds that it was an employment matter that had
been escalated to civil authorities and properly belonged to the
sphere and not the spiritual sphere of the church.

03/07/24 At its Stated meeting, the SJC appointed a second panel to
consider and hear the complaint.

04/05/24 The second SJC Panel conducted a full hearing of the matter.

**II.** **STATEMENT OF THE ISSUE**

Did Chicago Metro Presbytery err when it ruled Ms. Hyland’s Complaint
Judicially Out of Order for lack of standing?

**III.** **JUDGMENT**

No. The Complaint is Judicially Out of Order

**IV.** **REASONING**

Complainant does not have standing to file a _BCO_ 43-1 complaint with the
Presbytery.

**V.** **AMENDS**

None.

**VI.** **RECORDING IN MINUTES**

The SJC instructs the Presbytery to record this Decision in the minutes of the
next stated meeting following its receipt of the full Decision.

<a id="ga52-p817"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=819 printed_page=817 -->

A proposed decision was drafted by RE Dowling and adopted 2-1 by the Panel.
The SJC reviewed each part of the proposed decision, its final vote was **12-12-**
**0.**

<a id="ga52-p818"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=820 printed_page=818 -->

**CASE 2023-12**

**TE ANDREW FLATGARD**

**v.**

**METRO ATLANTA PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON APPEAL**

May 24, 2024

**I. SUMMARY OF FACTS**

05/08/21 TE Flatgard's wife asked him for a period of separation wherein
he would leave the house. At the time they had been married 14
years, had three children, and the Appellant had been Assistant
Pastor at Intown Community Church for five years.

05/13/21 Five days later, Appellant's wife filed a lawsuit for legal
separation and alimony. Appellant contended he returned to the
home on May 13, unaware of his wife's filing, and did so on advice
of his counselor. He was met by three members of the Intown
Session who discouraged him from returning to the home.

05/14/21 After spending one night in the home, Appellant departed after
hearing that his wife might file a temporary restraining order.
Appellant contended his lawyer advised him to file for divorce.

05/18/21 Appellant's wife filed an addendum to the lawsuit seeking to
restrict Appellant's time with their children to 6 hours per week,
spent exclusively in restaurants. Appellant contended his lawyer
advised him to oppose the lawsuit, warning a judge would make
this custody arrangement permanent if his wife filed for divorce.

05/30/21 Intown Session granted Appellant paid personal leave.

06/25/21 Appellant withdrew his request for paid mediation in response to
an Intown elder's request that he do so.

<a id="ga52-p819"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=821 printed_page=819 -->

07/21/21 A mediation meeting was held, compelled by Appellant's wife.
Intown's Senior Pastor advised Appellant to attend without a
lawyer, given the Appellant's concerns about family finances.

08/04/21 ina letter to the Session, Appellant requested it "investigate the
biblical nature of this marriage separation with the hope that your
inquiry and shepherding will lead to genuine reconciliation for our
family."

08/12/21 Appellant's wife legally compelled a $25,000 mediation despite
Appellant's withdrawing his request for such. Appellant attended
the mediation with his lawyer. The co-signed Consent Order
issued on this date stipulated: "The parties shall begin marital
counseling sessions no later than October 31, 2021, and they shall
continue in marital counseling thereafter at a frequency
recommended by the therapist. The parties shall cooperate with
one another to reach an agreement on who shall serve as their
marital therapist."

08/15/21 Session extended Appellant's paid personal leave another month,
through 09/24.

08/17/21 Appellant asked his wife to try to find work to pay for her
separation plan.

09/19/21 Appellant suggested names of five female marriage counselors to
his wife.

09/21/21 Metro Atlanta Presbytery ("Presbytery") formed a special
shepherding committee ("SC"), tasking as shown below.

1. Move that the moderator be given authority to appoint a

committee to shepherd TE Flatgard in hopes of restoring
his marriage. We would ask that 2 TE’s and 2 RE’s be
appointed along with at least one female advisor.
2. If his marriage cannot be restored, then that committee be

empowered to investigate and determine whether a
‘strong presumption of guilt’ is found against him,
thereby requiring us to institute process.

<a id="ga52-p820"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=822 printed_page=820 -->

3. We exhort Andrew that while he works on restoring his

marriage, that he step away from his official functions for
a time (Process has not been instituted and we cannot
force him to step away).”

TE Bob Brunson, TE Hace Cargo, RE Russell Berry, and RE Chet
Lilly were appointed to serve on the SC. TE Rob Rienstra and
Mrs. Anne Marie Dine agreed to serve as advisors to the
committee. RE Lilly was elected chairman.

09/23/21 Appellant and wife signed a Consent Temporary Order agreeing
to joint legal custody of their children.

09/27/21 Appellant's wife filed for no-fault divorce in the State of Georgia.
First meeting held between Appellant and the SC.

10/03/21 The Session decided to petition Presbytery to dissolve the
Appellant's pastoral call at the Church and provide severance
through 12/31/21, thus totaling seven months-worth of pay since
beginning on paid leave on 5/30/21.

10/14/21 Appellant met with Church's Shepherding Committee and two
members of the SC.

10/23/21 Appellant filed counterclaim for divorce.

10/22/21 A "Separation/Severance Agreement and Release" was signed by
the Appellant and the Senior Pastor of the Church (for the Church)
agreeing that employment termination would occur on October 3,
2021 with full pay continuing through December 31, 2021, along
with other standard items in such an agreement. Below is one
provision from the Agreement.

9. Non-disparagement. Employee and the Church shall refrain
from all conduct, verbal or otherwise, that disparages or
damages or could disparage or damage the reputation,
goodwill, or standing in the community of the other Party.
Accordingly, the Parties will not engage in the spreading of
rumors or innuendo, verbally or in writing that defame,

<a id="ga52-p821"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=823 printed_page=821 -->

disparage, or otherwise put each other in a negative light.
Employee agrees that a breach of this covenant entitles the
Church to a return all Severance Amount compensation paid
to Employee and an immediate termination of any obligation
by the Church to pay to Employee any unpaid Severance
Amount in addition to all other damages that may result from
the breach. If the Church is asked to provide an employment
reference for Employee, the Church will verify the position(s)
Employee held, his dates of employment, and salary
information. The Church may also decide, in its sole
discretion, to provide anyone making such inquiry with a copy
of communication to the congregation attached as Exhibit A

[which announced the termination of the call].
To provide clarity to both Parties regarding statements
regarding Employee's separation of employment from the
Church that may be made by the Church leadership to the
Church congregation, the Parties agree that the Church may
send the letter attached as Exhibit A hereto to the congregation
and communicate in person with members of the congregation
the contents of such letter.

10/29/21 Appellant contended his wife prohibited visitation of his children
indefinitely and restated this prohibition on 11/2/21.
11/01/21 Appellant's wife met with the SC.

11/04/21 The day after receiving an email from his wife indicating she does
"not agree to any overnight visits until the Court or Arbitrator
enters an Order requiring overnight visits," Appellant filed a
_Motion for an Emergency Hearing_ with DeKalb County Superior
Court so that his children could be with him. The Motion indicated

"Plaintiff has recently started moving her residence to Nashville
and has told Defendant [the Appellant] that she intends to take the
children with her, a violation of the automatic stay [which reads]
"Each party is hereby enjoined or restrained from causing the
minor children to be removed from the jurisdiction of this Court."

11/29/21 Superior Court issued a _Temporary Parenting Order_ that ensured
the children would be with the Appellant.

<a id="ga52-p822"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=824 printed_page=822 -->

03/14/21 Intown's Senior Pastor met a second time with the SC.

04/21/22 Appellant's divorce attorney sent letter to the Session Clerk. Here
was an excerpt:

"It is my understanding that elders from your church have
spoken with members of a Metro Atlanta Presbytery
committee regarding my client in a disparaging way. We
consider this a violation of the ... Agreement that you and your
elders signed on 10/22/2021. ... If your Church leadership
ignores this Agreement or intends to disparage my client in
any way, we will be forced to take legal action."

The Church's attorney replied, and the following are some
excerpts.

"The Church disagrees with the assertion in your letter that the
Church has violated the provisions of the Non-Disparagement
Agreement that is contained in the Separation and Severance
Agreement and Release between the Church and Mr. Flatgard.
... The meeting between the [Church's sr. pastor] and the

[Presbytery] Committee was a non-public and confidential
forum and the Church is a member of the Metro Atlanta

Presbytery, which is part of the larger Presbyterian Church in
America ("PCA"). In this regard, [the pastor's] meeting was
clearly part of the larger Presbyterian process of which your
client remains a participant (i.e., Mr. Flatgard continues to
participate in the Presbytery shepherding process).
Participation in a confidential, non-public Presbytery process
that relate to the Church and the PCA's theological beliefs is
not violative of the Non-Disparagement provision of the
Agreement. [The pastor] did not engage in spreading
falsehoods, rumors, or innuendo about Mr. Flatgard, nor will
hed o so in the future. ... I am not aware of any public
statements that have been made by the Church that might
disparage your client or violate this provision."

<a id="ga52-p823"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=825 printed_page=823 -->

05/27/22 An Arbitrator determined that the Appellant violated a court order
by withdrawing significant retirement funds without wife's

consent.

07/14/22 Divorce is finalized.

08/14/22 Intown's Senior Pastor signed a letter on Session letterhead to
Appellant's wife ... Appellant contended he was unaware of the
letter or its contents until the Prosecutor handed him a copy during
the trial over five months later.

09/13/22 Presbytery's SC chairman orally informed Appellant that the SC
had, by divided vote, concluded there was a strong presumption
of guilt.

09/20/22 Presbytery meeting. In executive session, the SC chairman
presented two-pages of its twelve-page report to Presbytery.
Below is an excerpt from the Findings and Recommendations"
section of the Committee report:

Therefore, while not unanimous, our committee, with
sadness but a sense of responsibility, also recommends
the court institute BCO 31-2 process largely in response
to actions, behaviors, and general conduct unbecoming
o fa minister of the gospel, on and after May 8 of 2021,
when Shelley sought a time of separation.

In short, the committee believes there is insufficient
evidence on which to base charges before May 8, 2021,
but TE Flatgard’s behavior after then “very well
contributed to divorce.”

As chargeable offenses must be against Scripture or our
standards, we offer the following for your
consideration: “Failure to maintain the qualifications of
an elder as set forth in 1 Timothy 3:1-7 and Titus 1:59, as well as failure to uphold ordination vows 6 & 7
under BCO 21-5.”

<a id="ga52-p824"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=826 printed_page=824 -->

The committee recommends that Presbytery institute
process, appoint a Prosecutor to prepare the indictment
and to conduct the case, which should be held before a
judicial commission appointed by the moderator of at
least 3 RE’s & 3 TE’s.

The Moderator appointed a Judicial Commission ("JC") that
included TE Matthew Armstrong, RE George Vaughn, TE
Brandon Dean, TE Jamie Lambert, and RE Jim Sheppard. At some
point, the Presbytery Shepherding Committee delivered the 12page Report to the Prosecutor, TE Drew Jones.

10/24/22 Appellant received indictment and citation for arraignment from
the JC. The charges are below:

In the name of the Presbyterian Church in America,
the Metro Atlanta Presbytery charges TE Andrew
Flatgard with the following violations of Scriptural
law and the constitution of the Presbyterian Church in
America during the time period starting May 8, 2021
and ending September 20, 2022:

1. Failure to maintain qualifications of an elder as set

forth in 1 Timothy 3:1-7 and Titus 1:5-9
2. Failure to love his wife as set forth in Ephesians

5:25-33.

3. Failure to uphold ordination vows six and seven

under The Book of Church Order (BCO) 21-5.

11/08/22 At the arraignment, Appellant pled not guilty to each of three
charges.

02/04/23 Two-day trial began on Saturday, three months after the
arraignment, reconvening and continuing a week later, on Sat.,
Feb. 11. The Prosecutor was TE Drew Jones.

02/15/23 JC adopted decision, finding Appellant guilty on each of the three
charges, adopted a censure recommendation of deposition, and
filed its Report with Presbytery.

<a id="ga52-p825"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=827 printed_page=825 -->

05/02/23 Three months later, Presbytery approved the judgment of the JC
and imposed the censure of deposition from office.

05/03/23 Appellant sent notice to the Presbytery Clerk of his intention to
appeal the decision to the SJC.

05/23/23 Appellant was officially notified of Presbytery's decision.

06/09/23 Appellant filed his Appeal with the PCA Stated Clerk's office.

07/20/23 PCA Stated Clerk's office received a Record of the Case.

08/19/23 Panel was drawn, which included RE S. Duncan (convener and
chair), RE Jay Neikirk, TE Guy Waters, and alternates TE Art
Sartorius and RE Howie Donahoe.

01/07/24 Panel Hearing on the dispute on the Record of the Case.

02/06/24 Panel Hearing on the Appeal. Both Parties had filed briefs
beforehand. Appellant was represented by TE Dominic Aquila
and Presbytery was represented by TE Matthew Armstrong (who
was the chairman of the Judicial Commission that conducted the

trial). TE Randy Schlichting (Presbytery's Clerk) was also present.

**II. STATEMENT OF THE ISSUES**

1. Did Appellant demonstrate any mistake or injustice in the judgment

and censure?

2. Did Appellant demonstrate any irregularity in the proceedings of the

lower court?

3. Did Appellant demonstrate any receiving of improper or declining

to receive proper evidence?

<a id="ga52-p826"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=828 printed_page=826 -->

**III. JUDGMENT**

1. Yes. The Record fails to provide adequate evidence to render a guilty

verdict. Therefore, the Appeal is sustained, and the verdicts and
censure are reversed in whole.

2. See Judgment 1.

3. See Judgment 1.

**IV. REASONING AND OPINION**

As in most appeals, the Appellant sought higher court review of several things,
including the nature of the charges and the weight and propriety of the alleged
evidence against him. Here is an opening statement in the Appeal: "Appellant
contends that MAP erred in its judgments and censures against me, and in
support of this Appeal offering the following." Below is a concluding
paragraph in his four-page Appeal, reformatted for emphasis.

Appellant maintains that MAP

[1] erred in charging him for failing to maintain qualifications of

an elder, failing to love his wife, and failing to uphold
ordination vows six and seven and

[2] then inflicting the censure of deposition from office, and

[3] that these alleged offenses took place between May 8, 2021 and

September 20, 2022. Further,

[4] MAP erred by taking testimony that fell outside of the sixteen
month period specified in the Indictment, and

[5] drawing conclusions of alleged events that had not occurred

within the time period MAP itself had determined. (emphasis,
and brackets with numbers, added)

In an appeal, if a single specification of error warrants sustaining that appeal,
there is little reason to address any others. In this Case, given the evidence and
testimony presented by the prosecution, and by the defense, it was
unreasonable to reach guilty verdicts and thus, an error to impose any censure.
Thus, the Appellant demonstrated a mistake and injustice in the judgment and
censure. Therefore, this Appeal is sustained on specifications of error 1 and 2
above, and the verdicts and censure are reversed in whole.

<a id="ga52-p827"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=829 printed_page=827 -->

The Panel's original proposed decision was amended by the SJC, and the
final decision was adopted by vote of 14-3, with 1 abstention, 4 absent, 1
disqualified, and 2 recused.

Bankson _Concur_ S. Duncan _Concur_ Maynard Absent
Bise Recused Eggert _Concur_ Neikirk _Dissent_
Carrell _Concur_ Evans _Dissent_ Pickering Absent
Coffin _Concur_ Garner Absent Sartorius _Concur_

Dodson _Concur_ Greco _Concur_ Ross _Abstain_

Donahoe _Concur_ Kooistra Recused Waters _Dissent_

Dowling _Concur_ Lee _Concur_ White Disqualified
M. Duncan _Concur_ Lucas _Concur_ Wilson _Concur_

re white was disqualified because he is a member o fa ch urchin metro
Atlanta Presbytery.

RE Bise indicated he was disqualified in this Case due to personal friendships
with Flatgard family members.

TE Kooistra indicated he recused for the following reason: "Earlier, I met with
TE Flatgard when he said he was concerned about his marriage. Then an elder
from Intown Church Atlanta contacted me suggesting we work together to try
to help TE Flatgard. we meta couple of times, but it was not fruitful. I remain
warmly sympathetic toward Andrew Flatgard."

**CONCURRING OPINION**

**Case 2023-12:**

_Appeal of TE Flatgard v. Metro Atlanta_
RE Howie Donahoe, joined by TE Coffin (in parts 1-3)

& RE Eggert (in parts 1-2)

June 9, 2024

This Opinion addresses four areas. The first is procedural, three and four
address matters in the Record, and the bulk of attention is given to the second.

1. Standards of Review 3. Non-Disparagement
2. Charges, Evidence, and Testimony 4. Two-Hatted Committees

<a id="ga52-p828"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=830 printed_page=828 -->

**1. Standards of Review** - _BCO_ Chapter 39 outlines standards of review for

higher courts. _BCO_ 39-3.2 uses a subjective adverb and two subjective
adjectives, italicized below.

3.2. A higher court should _ordinarily_ exhibit _great_ deference
to a lower court regarding those factual matters which the
lower court is more competent to determine, because of its
proximity to the events in question, and because of its
personal knowledge and observations of the parties and
witnesses involved. Therefore, a higher court should not
reverse a factual finding o fa lower court, unless there is _clear_
error on the part of the lower court. (emphasis added)

The SJC complied with that obligation. The _BCO_ does not define " _clear_ error"
or " _great_ deference." What is "clear" or "great" to one judge might not be to
another. In my 24 years on the SJC, I expect I've deferred to the lower courts
as often as any other member. But this Case did not rest on those adjectives,
nor did it rest on any "factual" finding of the lower court. The question is not
whether factual findings were clear errors. The question is whether any of the
factual findings demonstrated censurable sin.

There’s little debate on the following facts. The Appellant:

1. Did not share details of marriage challenges with church Session (his

employer).
2. Hesitated to leave the home on May 8, despite the wife's request and

elders' counsel.

3. Returned home on May 13 for one night, despite the wife's objection.
4. Entered a motion in County Court seeking sole custody of the

children.

5. Withdrew money from IRA without the wife's knowledge.
6. Approved an attorney sending a letter to the Session regarding an

alleged violation of the non-disparagement clause in the separation
agreement.

The SJC did not say there was "clear error" in those "factual findings." While
the SJC Decision does not explicitly identify what it deemed to be the primary
standard of review involved in this Case, I contend it was _BCO_ 39-3.3 rather
than 3.2.

<a id="ga52-p829"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=831 printed_page=829 -->

3.3 a higher court should ordinarily exhibit great deference to a
lower court regarding those matters of **discretion and judgment**
which can only be addressed by a court with familiar acquaintance
of the events and parties. Such matters of discretion and judgment
would include, but not be limited to: the moral character of
candidates for sacred office, the appropriate censure to impose
after a disciplinary trial, or judgment about the comparative
credibility of conflicting witnesses. Therefore, a higher court
should not reverses uc ha judgment by a lower court, unless there
is **clear** error on the part of the lower court. (emphasis added)

i concluded the lower court made a clearer r orin its decision ina matter of

discretion and judgment: _Did the facts rise to the level of something_
_warranting a guilty verdict and a formal censure?_ That is a fair question for
the appellate court to consider. And it does not directly involve a "factual
finding" ( _BCO_ 39-3.2) and is not a question of constitutional interpretation
( _BCO_ 39-3.4). So, according to _BCO_ 39-3.3, a higher court ordinarily cannot
reverse such a judgment of the lower court unless the higher court deems it to
be "clear" error. I voted to sustain the Appeal for that very reason.

**2. Charges, Evidence, and Testimony**

In the video recording of the Panel Hearing, there's a 10-minute exchange that
is instructive. The Panel Chairman first asked Presbytery's Representative,
who had also been the Moderator of the trial commission, this question: _"What_
_are your primary pieces of evidence during that particular 16-month time_
_period that justified the guilty verdicts?"_ The Representative's initial answer
was wavering. The Chairman reiterated, _"I'm not asking about testimony, I'm_
_asking for facts."_ And shortly after, for a third time, the Chairman asked, _"Mr._
_Armstrong, did you have any other facts or evidence you'd like to point out in_
_response to my prior question?"_

During this exchange, Presbytery's Representative/Trial Moderator referenced
five items:

1. the events of May 8 (when Appellant's wife asked him to leave

the home)

<a id="ga52-p830"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=832 printed_page=830 -->

2. the events of May 13 (when Appellant returned to the home on

advice of counsel)
3. the Appellant's October 2023 petition for custody of the children
4. the Appellant attorney's letter to Intown regarding an alleged

violation of the non-disparagement clause in the separation
agreement
5. the Appellant's withdrawal of some of his retirement funds

without his wife's knowledge

The Record contains explanations for actions related to those events that
demonstrate the Appellant's actions should _not_ be regarded as indictable or
censurable offenses. Below are the three charges and specifications from the
Indictment followed by a short assessment of problems with the wording of
each charge and then by a deeper assessment of the weakness of the evidence
for each charge.

**Charge 1** : Failure to maintain elder qualifications set forth in 1 Tim. 3:1-7 &

Titus 1:5-9.

An elder "must be above reproach" ( 1 Tim. 3:2; Titus 1:6-7). The two
Greek words used in these respective passages ... communicate the
underlying of idea of blamelessness. Furthermore, he "must manage
his own household well" (1 Tim. 3:4). The Greek phrase ...
communicates the idea of "exercising a position of leadership" while
meeting "high standards of excellence or expectation."

From May 8, 2021 through September 20, 2022, TE Flatgard
demonstrated a pattern of reproachable behavior towards his former
wife and his fellow teaching and ruling elders at Intown Community
Church that is inconsistent with Scriptural requirements of an elder.

[Charge 1] Representative incidents

1. On May 8, 2021, TE Flatgard's wife (Shelley Flatgard) requested a

time of temporary physical separation in order to begin healing
their marriage. This request is in itself evidence of a preexisting,
long-term, significantly unhealthy marriage relationship between
Andrew and Shelley Flatgard that was never disclosed to the
Session of Intown Community Church prior to May 8.

<a id="ga52-p831"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=833 printed_page=831 -->

2. On or about November 3, 2021, TE Flatgard entered a motion into

Dekalb County Superior Court seeking sole custody of the
children. Up to that point in their legal separation, he and his wife
had shared joint custody of the children.

"Failure to maintain qualifications o fan elder” is not a censurable "offense"
under our standards. Any men in a congregation who are not serving as officers
or who have been turned down for office have not by reason of their failure to
qualify committed a censurable offense. The qualifications for elder and
deacon set forth in Scripture are not, strictly speaking, commands for
obedience imposed upon individual church members as a disciplinary code of
conduct; they are conditions for _eligibility_ to serve in office. Hence any man's
failure to qualify for office by the Scriptural standards, whether in the absence
of his pursuit of the office, in his failure to qualify for office after attempting
to do so, or even the presence of what would be disqualifying conditions that
emerge after his qualification and ordination to office, does not thereby subject
him to disciplinary action by the Church.

**Charge 1 Incident 1** - A husband is under no biblical requirement to vacate
his home upon the request of his wife, even temporarily, even if encouraged
to do so by elders from the wife's church. Presbytery's Trial Commission
reported, "Things only became confrontational when the Accused _refused to_
_honor_ [his wife's] request that the Accused sleep outside the home that night
as a first step of an indefinite time of separation." (emphasis added, ROC 896,
first paragraph) The Commission reported, "as of May 8, 2021, [the wife] was
not seeking divorce." However, the wife filed for legal separation five days
later. [2]

In addition, there is no biblical requirement for a couple to disclose marital
struggles to the wife's Session or the minister's employer. The Trial
Commission Report repeatedly highlights this as if it were automatically
sinful. The Commission Report was mistaken to unequivocally maintain: "The
Scriptures charge elders to "manage their own households well" (1 Tim. 3:4),
_and part of that management is not concealing serious marriage problems._ "
(emphasis added)

2 In Georgia, legal separation determines all the same issues as a divorce (alimony,

custody, etc.,) but at the end the couple is still married. At the end of a legal
separation proceeding in GA, you may not re-marry. at thee n do fa divorce, you
may. https://family-law.fbalawfirm.com/legal-separation-in%20georgia/

<a id="ga52-p832"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=834 printed_page=832 -->

failure to maintain qualifications would ordinarily b ea sufficient reason fora
Session to encourage an assistant pastor to _resign_, or for a Session to petition
Presbytery to dissolve his call, and probably for him to consider demitting. But
it is not, on its face, a chargeable offense.

**Charge 1 Incident 2** - Regarding the November 3, 2021 custody motion, we
note the wife filed for no-fault divorce in the State of Georgia _five weeks_
_earlier_, on September 27, 2021. Appellant indicated that a week prior to his
sole custody motion, on October 29 2021, his wife prohibited visitation of his
children indefinitely and that she was making plans to move out of state and
take the children with her. Upon advice of counsel, the Appellant filed the
custody motion. Twenty-five days after that filing, the Superior Court issued
a _Temporary Parenting Order_ ensuring the three children would be with the
Appellant.

Sometimes in marriage dissolutions one party takes legal steps which, at first,
might appear to be sinful. But after discerning the motivation behind those
steps, they sometimes look less so. Consider the 2014 unanimous SJC
Decision in Case 2013-10: _Appeal of TE Latimer v. Chicago Metro._ Below is
an excerpt from the Reasoning.

Presbytery was focused on the following question, prior to
indictment and at trial: _“Did TE Latimer have biblical grounds_
_for divorce?”_ Presbytery rightly answered: No. But that was
not the most pertinent question. The most pertinent question,
and the one on which our judgment to sustain in part rests, is:
_Did TE Latimer sin on June 27, 2012 when he filed a divorce_
_petition with the State of Illinois?_

We do not find so and rule that Presbytery clearly erred in
judging that he sinned in doing so. There is no indication in the
record that TE Latimer ever had “grounds to divorce” his wife.
But whether his June 27, 2012, filing constituted sin turns not
on whether he had grounds to divorce, but on whether his
filing, combined with other evidence in the record, can
reasonably be read to indicate an intent on his part to divorce.
In other words, was his true objective to get divorced, or was
the divorce filing intended for other purposes entirely, such as

<a id="ga52-p833"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=835 printed_page=833 -->

the protection of his children, as he argued? It doesn’t matter
whether the filing was a wise or well-advised means to achieve
his objective, or whether the children needed protecting, none
of which the SJC can evaluate. What matters is whether TE

Latimer’s intentions in filing were sinful. We find no
conclusive evidence in the record that TE Latimer's intentions

were sinful, and Presbytery clearly erred in finding otherwise.
( _M43GA_, 2015, p. 578 ff.)

**Charge 2** : Failure to love his wife as set forth in Eph. 5:25-33

Ephesians 5:25 states, " Husbands, love your wives, as Christ loved
the church and gave himself up for her" (Eph. 5:25). The Greek word
... communicates the idea of handing over, turning over, or giving up
a person to authorities, and is used most frequently in the New
Testament to refer to Jesus being turned over to the Jewish religious
authorities immediately prior to his crucifixion. In Ephesians 5:25
then, Christ's love for the Church is supremely seen in his selfsacrifice in which he willingly surrendered his rights and subjected
himself to pain and even death for his bride, the Church.
From May 8, 2021 through September 20, 2022, TE Flatgard
demonstrated a pattern of aggressive, self-protective behaviors toward
his former wife during their separation and eventual divorce. Multiple
eyewitnesses during this period state that TE Flatgard's self-protective
behavior made reconciliation with Mrs. Flatgard practically
impossible. [3]

[Charge 2] Representative incidents:

1. Following his wife's request that he temporarily leave the home

on May 8, 2021, TE Flatgard returned to his family's home on
or about May 13, 2021 and refused to leave, compelling his
wife and their children to go to a hotel for multiple nights.
2. During their legal separation, TE Flatgard withdrew $95,350

from [his] IRA without his wife's knowledge or consent.

3 In its post-trial Report to Presbytery, the Judicial Commission adds the following to
Charge 2: "... specifically in a lack of willingness to self-sacrifice and surrender his
rights for his bride."]

<a id="ga52-p834"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=836 printed_page=834 -->

Failing to love your wife, taken in itself, is too broad a charge to be censurable.
Every husband fails to obey the command to love his wife as Christ loved the
Church. But not every breach of that command is subject to formal process
from the Church. In the absence of specifications that would justify censure,
this charge was not justiciable. A "failure to love" must be demonstrated ina
particular way, and the manner of the breach is the essence of the offense.

The claim that the Appellant “demonstrated a pattern of aggressive, selfprotective behaviors toward his former wife during their separation and
eventual divorce” is vague and overly inclusive. “Aggressive” as well as “selfprotective behaviors” might be entirely consistent with loving one’s wife and
justifiable in a separation and divorce, so these broad allegations, taken in
themselves, did not clarify what conduct was at issue that would amount to a
censurable sin. Likewise, the Trial Commission Report repeatedly used vague
phrasing, like "the accused failed to acknowledge or validate his wife's
experience of the marriage," which is not, per se, evidence of sin.

**Charge 2 Incident 1 -** Prosecution did not demonstrate how the Appellant’s
initial declination to leave his own home on May 8 by obeying the “command”
of his wife would be a sin.

And regarding the Appellant's May 13 attempt to return five days later, even
if some of the Church's Session members recommended the physical
separation, none of those members had jurisdiction or authority over the
Appellant. Yet the Trial Commission Report stated, "The Accused _refused to_
_take advice_ [of three Session members] and stayed in the house that night,
resulting in [the wife] and the children leaving the home." (emphasis added)
While the Appellant's wife may have felt compelled to leave that evening, the
Record does not indicate any allegations of physical or verbal abuse
warranting such.

A six-minute video of the May 13 interaction between the Appellant and his
wife and some of the Church's elders was entered into evidence at the trial,
and this interaction shows the Appellant was calm and non-threatening. [ 4] The

4 The Prosecutor entered this six-minute video as evidence at trial, marked as
Prosecution Exhibit 3. A transcript of the video appears in ROC 783-85. The video
was available to the Panel in the Record it reviewed. It was authenticated by the
Presbytery Clerk "as part of the Records of the Metro Atlanta Presbytery in this
matter."

<a id="ga52-p835"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=837 printed_page=835 -->

Appellant left the home again after just one night. We note that earlier in the
day on May 13 the wife filed for legal separation, and it was upon advice of
his counselor that the Appellant returned to the home that day, and this fact
was shared with the wife.

**Charge 2 Incident 2** - Regarding the withdrawal of retirement funds, the
Appellant claimed he withdrew the money to support his family and was
prevented from providing that evidence. That a husband may only withdraw
funds from his retirement account with his wife’s “knowledge and consent,”
as alleged and proven, is not a proposition that can be derived from the
scriptures as a violation o fa husband’s responsibility to love his wife for
which the Church may reasonably censure a man.

The Appellant's brother testified as follows at the trial:

He was charged in an order by the court to pay his mortgage,
and there wasn't any other -- they [wife and her attorney]
wouldn't agree to any kind of HELOC or debt, and there
wasn't any income coming in from the [wife's] family that
was living in his house to help pay for the utilities. So, I think
it was a loving thing that he, he drained his part of his
retirement to pay the mortgage as he was bound to by the
court order.

The Appellant maintained the withdrawal was necessary to cover extra living
expenses resulting from his exclusion from the home, as well as added legal
expenses being incurred by both the husband and the wife. For example, the
Record indicates that nine months prior to the withdrawal, the Appellant's wife
legally compelled a $25,000 mediation on August 12, 2021. On September 27,
2021, the wife filed for no-fault divorce in GA. The Appellant's pay ceased
from Intown Church on December 31, 2021. The Record doesn't indicate the
dates when other divorce-related fees were incurred, but the July 8, 2022 Final
Arbitration Award noted the attorney fees and litigation fees totaled $74,000
for the Appellant and $167,000 for the wife.

A civil court's opinion on the funds withdrawal does not settle the question of
whether it is an offense censurable by a church court. Unlike a church court,
the civil court does not ordinarily consider the motivation behind an act. The
Presbytery prosecutor did not offer evidence that the retirement funds were

<a id="ga52-p836"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=838 printed_page=836 -->

spent sinfully or that the Appellant failed to provide financially for his wife
and children during the separation.

Finally, it will often be difficult for a church court to accurately apportion
culpability in the demise of a marriage. For example, the Judicial/Trial
Commission Report had an excerpt indicated the Appellant stated that "his
wife had not touched him (assuming to be i nan intimate fashion) for over two
and a half years." At trial, the Intown shepherding elder assigned to the
Appellant's wife testified at trial that the Appellant and his wife "had not slept
in the same bed for many years. Or at least lengthy periods at a time." Those
would seem important statements to pursue, but the Record does not indicate
they ever were.

Regarding apportioning or determining culpability, there was an interesting
exchange when the Appellant cross-examined the Intown Senior Pastor during
the trial. The Appellant referred to an August 4, 2021 letter he had sent to the
Intown Session, i.e., his wife's Session. Here is an excerpt from the trial
transcript.

Appellant: "The sentence highlighted [in Appellant's letter to the

Session] states, "After much prayer and consultation with
PCA pastors, I'm writing to ask you to investigate the
biblical nature of this marriage separation with the hope
that your inquiry and shepherding will lead to genuine
reconciliation for our family." Did you - the Session
investigate the biblical nature of the marriage
separation?"
Witness: "We did not carry out a judicial investigation. In fact, you

[the Appellant] would have no grounds to request a
judicial investigation against a member of our church

[i.e., the Appellant's wife]. _That request would be out of_
_order._ " (emphasis added)

**Charge 3:** Failure to uphold ordination vows 6 and 7 under _BCO_ 21-5.

BCO 21-5, question 6: "Do you promise to be zealous and faithful in
maintaining the truths of the Gospel and the purity and peace and unity
of the Church, whatever persecution or opposition may arise unto you
on that account?"

<a id="ga52-p837"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=839 printed_page=837 -->

BCO 21-5, question 7: "Do you engage to be faithful and diligent in
the exercise o fall your duties as a christian and a minister of the
Gospel, whether personal or relational, private or public; and to
endeavor by the grace of God to adorn the profession of the Gospel in
your manner of life, and to walk with exemplary piety before the flock
of which God shall make you overseer?"
TE Flatgard's behavior towards his former wife and his fellow
teaching and ruling elders at Intown Community Church during the
period from May 8, 2021 through September 20, 2022 have broken
the purity, peace, and unity of the Church and failed to walk with
exemplary piety before the flock of God.

[Charge 3] Representative incidents:

1. On or about August 30, 2021, TE Flatgard stated that he preferred

not to meet further with TE Jimmy Agan unless compelled to do so
as part of the Intown Session's efforts to shepherd him and his wife
toward marital reconciliation.

2. On or about October 3, 2021, TE Flatgard told one of his children

that he had voluntary [sic] resigned from Intown

Charging officers with breaking their oath of office, without additional
specification, is just another way of accusing a man of “failing to maintain the
qualifications of office.” Breaking an oath is indeed an offense, but the naked
claim of breach without more falls short. An indictment, for example,
charging a man with “breaking his marriage vows” without specifying how he
did so is deficient. As mentioned above regarding failing to love one's wife as
Christ love the Church, avow must be broken ina particular way, and the
manner of the breach is the essence of the offense. The fact that the obligation
was sealed by a vow is important because it is an _aggravating_ consideration.
This language, taken in itself, is not sufficient to describe a censurable offense.
The first three sentences (in the expanded charge) simply reiterate the vows.
The fourth sentence identifies no particular conduct at all.

**Charge 3 Incident 1** rests on a false premise. The Appellant was not
accountable to the Intown Senior Pastor or the Session; he was accountable to
_Presbytery_ . Therefore, the Session had no _power of_ _compulsion._ The Appellant
was (and is) to be “shepherded” by his _Presbytery_, not the Session or its
Moderator. That principle is fundamental to our polity, particularly in a case

<a id="ga52-p838"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=840 printed_page=838 -->

involving formal discipline. The Trial Commission Report noted, "this
commission found the Accused's actions on May 13, based on both video
evidence (Prosecution Exhibit 3) and witness testimony, were to disregard the
counsel of fellow elders and pastors of Intown Church." But there is no sin in
declining to heed _counsel_ from fellow elders, even if you are under their direct
jurisdiction (which Appellant was not).

The Trial Commission Report frequently contends the Appellant's "posture"
was "defensive" and "quarrelsome" and "unresponsive to instruction" toward
the Intown Session, a Special Care Team created by Intown, and the
Presbytery's ad hoc Shepherding/Investigative Team. However, i ti snot a
censurable sin, and is probably understandable, to have such a "posture" in
such a situation.

**Charge 3 Incident 2** - What the Appellant may have said to his child is not
conduct that warrants censure as it does not rise to a level of materiality with
respect to his function as pastor as to justify formal process but could and
should be remedied _privately_ as between father and child.

**3. Non-Disparagement** - The Record prompted a question rightfully
unaddressed by the SJC, but it bears attention. The Appellant (while still
employed as an Assistant Pastor) signed a "Separation/Severance Agreement
and Release" with the Church that included a "non-disparagement" clause.
After the signing, the Senior Pastor was called to meet at least twice with
Presbytery's investigating committee, which later recommended an indictment
on three charges. The Record does not indicate what was said in those
meetings, but the Record indicates the Appellant's attorney later exchanged
letters with the church's attorney. The (Georgia) Church's attorney maintained
that the Senior Pastor's conversations with the investigating committee were
not addressed by the language in the non-disparagement clause.

While the letter was not mentioned in the Indictment, Presbytery's
Representative mentioned it in his Brief and at the Hearing as if it were
evidence of sin, and thus, the reason for comment here. Whether it was a
violation o fa legal contract is a question for georgia courts. This Opinion does
not express any position on that legal question, but simply highlights the
importance for both parties to ensure they're clear on what such a clause
prohibits, and what it permits, before signing. But the Appellant's sending of

<a id="ga52-p839"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=841 printed_page=839 -->

su chalet teri snot o nits face a violation of scripture and thus not a chargeable
offense, nor evidence of an offense.

**4. Wearing two Hats** - this was another instance o fa pres byte ry tasking a
committee to wear 2 hats - shepherd & investigator. While it looks like the
committee was to wear only one at a time, that can't realistically be done.
Presbytery adopted the two-part motion below.

1. Move that the moderator be given authority to appoint a

committee to _shepherd_ [the TE] in hopes of restoring his
marriage. We would ask that 2 TE’s and 2 RE’s be
appointed along with at least one female advisor. (emphasis
added)

2. If his marriage cannot be restored, then that committee be

empowered to _investigate and determine_ whether a ‘strong
presumption of guilt’ is found against him, thereby
requiring us to institute process. (emphasis added)

While such dual-hattedness is not unconstitutional, it usually proves unwise.
Perhaps it would be more prudent to use a pastoral care committee with which
a pastor could converse without fear that his conversation might result in an
indictment or adverse testimony at trial. (In the present Case, the chair of
Presbytery's Shepherding/Investigation Committee testified at length in the
trial as a prosecution witness.) In the non-church world, it's unlikely anyone
would voluntarily speak to a counselor/shepherd who was - at the same time
or could morph into - an on-duty police detective assigned to investigate him.
At present, our Constitution only explicitly exempts someone from testifying
against his or her spouse. Perhaps an amendment to _BCO_ 25-14 could provide
the same exemption for defense counsel or for a person to whom the accused
confides (akin to the clergy-penitent privilege in US law). That would give
PCA members the opportunity to speak freely with an advocate or counselor
without fear that those listeners would be cited to testify by the prosecution.

if that exemption existed and could be applied to a member o fa shepherding
committee, it might be prudent for that presbytery to also have a standing
investigative committee - to wear the other hat. In that scenario, if a minister

<a id="ga52-p840"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=842 printed_page=840 -->

got a call from the shepherding committee, he might be relieved, but if he got
a call from the investigative committee, he might think about Mirandizing. [5]

In 2015 and 2016, two Assemblies each _disapproved_ different overtures that
sought to revise _BCO_ 35-2 to delete an accused officer's right to decline to
testify. If a man cannot be compelled to testify at trial, presumably he cannot
be compelled to answer questions during an investigation that could result in
a trial. A two-hatted shepherding committee will, almost unavoidably, injure
the right protected by _BCO_ 35-2 (perhaps fatally).

/s/ RE Howie Donahoe

**CONCURRING OPINION**

**Case 2023-12:**

_Appeal of TE Flatgard v. Metro Atlanta_

RE Jim Eggert

June 6, 2024

I write to clarify my agreement with the Decision in this case and to clarify
and qualify my joinder in the fine Concurring Opinion of RE Howie Donahoe.

Regarding the “Standards of Review” section set out in RE Donahoe’s
Concurring Opinion, I wanted to explain that my own view departs somewhat
from TE Donahoe’s reasoning. I believe that the Decision in the case rightly
rested on _BCO_ 39-3.4 rather than _BCO_ 39-3.3. _BCO_ 39-3.4 provides:

The higher court does have the power and obligation of
judicial review, which cannot be satisfied by always
deferring to the findings of a lower court. Therefore, a
higher court should not consider itself obliged to exhibit
the same deference to a lower court when the issues being
reviewed involve the interpretation of the Constitution of
the Church. Regarding such issues, the higher court has the

5 Pacific NW Presbytery has a "Preliminary Investigative Committee," whose Rules

are in Presbytery's Standing Rules. Pacific NW also has PIC Guidelines that can be
revised at any meeting by simple majority.
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1lfLXObR01Gm9y5udV9Pu2duR1OfedmcJ/view?
usp=drive_link.

<a id="ga52-p841"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=843 printed_page=841 -->

duty and authority to interpret and apply the Constitution
of the Church according to its best abilities and
understanding, regardless of the opinion of the lower court.

I concluded that the charges together with the specifications -- the charging
document identified “representative incidents” -- considered in light of the
evidence adduced in the case as reflected in the Record failed to support the
conclusion that any censurable offense had been established, and therefore the
censure imposed against the Appellant in this case was not justified under our
Constitution.

in contrast tore Donahoe’s Concurring Opinion, which accedes deference to
the lower court regarding “matters of discretion and judgment which can only
be addressed by a court with familiar acquaintance of the events and parties,”
including “the appropriate censure to impose after a disciplinary trial” ( _BCO_
39-3.3), it ism y view that, in an appeal like this one, a higher court owes no
deference to a lower court regarding whether the record supported that a
censurable offense had been alleged and proven in the first place. “Great
deference” is owed to a court of original jurisdiction regarding “the
appropriate censure to impose after a disciplinary trial” only in reference to
that court’s choice among the censures authorized by _BCO_ Chapter 30, not as
to whether no censure at all may be imposed in a particular case. A court’s
decision as to whether an offense as charged and proven is a proper object of
formal censure presents a purely Constitutional question which the court of
original jurisdiction has no greater capacity to evaluate than a reviewing court.
The court of original jurisdiction’s “familiar acquaintance of the events and
parties” ( _BCO_ 39-3.3) gives that court no advantage in evaluating whether any
censurable offense had been established under our Constitution.

The reasons why no censurable offense was established on the Record in this
particular matter are ably described in RE Donahoe’s Concurring Opinion in
Section 2, “Charges, Evidence, and Testimony.”

out o fa sense of restraint, I refrain from joining with respect to Numbers 3
and 4 of RE Donahoe’s Concurring Opinion since the resolution of the
questions addressed in those paragraphs are not necessary to dispose of the
matters before the Commission.

<a id="ga52-p842"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=844 printed_page=842 -->

**DISSENTING OPINION**

**Case 2023-12**

_Appeal of TE Andrew Flatgard_

_v. Metro Atlanta Presbytery_
TE Brad Evans, RE Frederick Neikirk, TE Guy Waters

May 24, 2024

We respectfully dissent from the decision of the Standing Judicial
Commission in Case 2023-12. In so doing, we also want to emphasize what
we understand the Commission to have said and not said in this Case.

While concerns were raised by some members of the Commission
regarding the nature of the charges and regarding some of the procedures
followed by Presbytery’s judicial commission, it is our understanding that the
Standing Judicial Commission did not take any position on those matters. As
is reflected in the Judgment in the Case, the decision turned on whether the
Record provided adequate evidence to demonstrate the Appellant’s guilt. The
Commission concluded that there was not adequate evidence, and it, therefore,
sustained the Appeal.

Two of the signers of this dissent were members of the Panel that
heard this Appeal. We, too, were concerned with the level of evidence evinced
in the Record. Matters of evidence, however, fall under the categories of
“factual matters” and “matters of discretion and judgment which can only be
addressed by a court with familiar acquaintance of the events and parties.”
(See _BCO_ 39-3.2,3.) While we had concerns about the evidence shown in the
Record, we could not say that there was “clear error on the part of Presbytery”
in reaching their guilty verdict on the basis of the evidence presented in the
Record. As such, we believe we, and the Standing Judicial Commission, were
obliged to exhibit “great deference” to the findings of Presbytery. (See _ibid_ .)
This, we believe, the Commission failed to do. It is on the basis of that failure
to follow the Constitutional obligation to demonstrate “great deference” to the
lower court in matters of evidence that we dissent.

<a id="ga52-p843"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=845 printed_page=843 -->

**CASE 2023-13**

**PCA**

**v.**

**METROPOLITAN NEW YORK PRESBYTERY**

**RULING ON 40-5 MATTER**

**March 6, 2025**

This matter arises from the findings and citation of the 51 [st] General Assembly
regarding Presbytery’s actions and reports to the Committee for the Review of
Presbytery Records. The 51 [st] General Assembly found that “Presbytery’s
response to SJC Case No. 2023-13 indicates the Presbytery’s persistence in an
error pursuant to _RAO_ 16-10.c.” This Case concerned Presbytery’s handling
o fa teaching elder who permitted a woman to deliver a message from the
pulpit during a worship service. Subsequent to the finding of the 51 [st] General
Assembly, this Teaching Elder came before Presbytery under _BCO_ 38-1.
Presbytery thereupon rendered judgment without process and imposed a
censure upon the Teaching Elder.

The parties’ representatives submitted, and the Stated Clark collated,
documents bearing on this matter as the equivalent of a Record of the Case.
Presbytery and the representatives of the General Assembly submitted briefs,
including Presbytery’s report of its actions taken in response to the General
Assembly’s citation.

As Presbytery responded to the General Assembly citation by bringing
a _BCO_ 38-1 case without process against TE Higgins, and the case was
