resolved by a finding of guilt and a censure of admonition, the SJC finds that
these actions satisfy the deficiencies complained of in the citation. All
necessary remedial action with respect to this citation has been accomplished.
The SJC makes no judgment on Presbytery’s categorization of TE Higgins’
stated difference because this matter is not before the court, given that
Presbytery’s action occurred after the citation.

The SJC reviewed the proposed Ruling, and after several amendments,
approved the final version of the Ruling by a vote of **23-0-0, with 1 absent**,
thus concluding the matter.

<a id="ga52-p844"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=846 printed_page=844 -->

Bankson _Concur_ Eggert _Concur_ Maynard _Concur_
Bise _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Neikirk _Concur_

Bruce _Concur_ Garner _Concur_ Pickering _Concur_
Carrell _Concur_ Greco _Concur_ Sartorius _Concur_

Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall _Concur_
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Concur_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe _Concur_ Lee _Concur_ White Absent
Dowling _Concur_ Marcinak _Concur_ Wilson _Concur_

<a id="ga52-p845"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=847 printed_page=845 -->

**CASE 2023-16**

**TE BULKELEY & TE GILLIKIN**

**v.**

**HIGHLANDS PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON COMPLAINT**

**JULY 22, 2024**

**I. SUMMARY OF THE FACTS**

10/25/22 SJC ruled in case 2022-04 that HP erred in both matters brought
before it by Arden Presbyterian Church's ("APC") previous
pastor, stating, “Presbytery is debarred from prosecuting any of
the allegations embraced by the subject matter of this case.”

11/13/22 RE Brett Callaway wrote the APC Session expressing concern
over an APC Session proposal to send a letter to the Highlands
Presbytery to “affirm the Christian character of Kevin, Jill, and
Delaney Martin.”

11/17/22 Despite RE Callaway's concerns, APC Session approved the
sending of the letter to HP.

12/5/22 APC Session sent a letter to HP stating:

As part of our shepherding responsibility, we desire to affirm
the Christian character of Kevin, Jill, and Delaney Martin, and
further, affirm the position we took in the summer of 2020
when we unanimously referred their concerns to Highlands
Presbytery for Biblical resolution. As shepherds, our hearts
are distressed because of the pain and anguish the Martin
family is enduring due to the fact that their concerns were not
resolved, either by a court finding orb ya reconciliation. We
pray that ... God will ... be a source of healing for the
resolution they were denied.

<a id="ga52-p846"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=848 printed_page=846 -->

12/8/22 RE Brett Callaway of the APC Session filed a complaint to the
APC Session regarding the APC Session decision to send a letter
to HP to “affirm” the Martins’ “Christian character.”

1/19/23 APC Session denied RE Callaway’s complaint. The Session failed
to specify a reason for the denial.

1/25/23 RE Callaway filed a “Notice of Dissent Against an Action of
Arden Presbyterian Church Taken on January 19, 2023” with the
APC.

2/16/23 APC Session refused to record RE Callaway's Dissent and spread
it upon it minutes on the grounds that it “was found to include
intemperate language.” The Session failed to specify what
language in the Dissent it found intemperate.

2/25/23 At its stated meeting, HP received the letter from the APC Session
commending the Martins and received it into its record.

3/27/23 TEs Bulkeley and Gillikin filed a “Dissent Against An Action of
Highlands Presbytery (PCA) on February 25, 2023” (the
"Dissent") for receiving the APC Session letter into its record.

5/2/23 HP denied the Dissent on the grounds that it was found “to not be
couched in temperate language, per _BCO_ 45–5, and therefore not

[to] be recorded in the minutes of Highlands Presbytery.” Nothing
in the minutes indicates what language in the Dissent was
determined to be phrased in intemperate language. HP also
removed TE Gillikin’s name from the Dissent because he was not
present at the February 25 meeting of HP where the letter was
received, and therefore he was not able to join in the Dissent.

5/18/23 TE Bulkeley, TE Gillikin, RE Callaway, and RE Conley Brown
filed a complaint with HP for not recording the Dissent per _BCO_
45-5.

8/5/23 HP denied the Complaint stating:

<a id="ga52-p847"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=849 printed_page=847 -->

(1) No, Presbytery did not err in not recording the dissent
because it found the Dissent to “not be couched in temperate
language” ( _BCO_ 45–5). (2) No, Presbytery did not err in not
recording the reasons for finding the Dissent intemperate,
because (a) the _BCO_ nowhere requires such recording of
reasons, and (b) to record such reasons would amount to
recording the intemperate dissent in the minutes in violation
of _BCO_ 45–5.

HP also removed RE Brown from the Complaint since he was not
a commissioner at the meeting in which the action complained of
took place.

8/23/23 TEs Bulkeley and Gillikin and RE Callaway carried their
Complaint to the SJC.

3/29/24 The SJC Panel conducted a hearing via Zoom technology. RE
John Pickering chaired the hearing. TE Art Sartorius, RE Steve
Dowling, and TE Mike Ross served on the panel. RE Mel Duncan
was unable to participate. For the Complainants, TE Dominic
Acquila served as spokesman and presented their arguments. TE
Craig Bulkeley and TE Skip Gillikin were present. The
Respondents for HP were TE David Hina and TE Patrick Lafferty.
RE Brett Callaway and RE Conley Brown were also in attendance.

**II. STATEMENT OF THE ISSUE**

Did the parties comply with the SJC's October 2022 Decision in
Case 2022-04: _Complaint of TE Sheppard v. Highlands_ ?

**III. JUDGMENT**

No. Therefore, the SJC denies the Complaint, annuls
Presbytery's action of recording the Church's letter in its
minutes, and instructs the Letter be expunged from Presbytery
minutes. The question of the Dissent is thereby moot.

<a id="ga52-p848"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=850 printed_page=848 -->

**IV. REASONING AND OPINION**

Before addressing the question of whether Presbytery erred in disallowing the
Dissent, a more fundamental question is whether the parties sufficiently
complied with the SJC Decision in a prior Case related to this matter. Both the
Presbytery's public recording of the Church's Letter and the Dissent filed
thereafter violate _BCO_ 14-7:

_BCO_ 14-7. Judicial decisions shall be binding and conclusive
on the parties who are directly involved in the matter being
adjudicated.

Recording the Letter or the Dissent in public minutes is contrary to the SJC's
October 2022 Decision in _Sheppard_ . ( _M50GA_, 2023, p. 825) The SJC
Judgment in _Sheppard_ stipulated:

Presbytery is debarred from prosecuting any of the allegations
embraced by the subject matter of this case.

And the SJC's Reasoning concluded:

Highlands Presbytery is debarred from further prosecution of
the offense alleged by the [family] or of any other alleged
offense embraced by the subject matter of this Case.

In the past, the SJC has reached to constitutional issues not directly raised in a
complaint or an appeal. For example, the SJC has ruled complaints out of order
because they were not timely filed with a Session, even though the Session
considered the complaint, and then the Presbytery also considered it. (Case
2001-32: _Session of Christ Covenant v. Central Carolina_, _M31GA_, 2003, p.
107) If the SJC can do that, then it can address what appears to be the disregard
of an SJC decision (a constitutional violation of _BCO_ 14-7) especially since it
bears on whether this Dissent was permissible. If Highlands Presbytery had
declined to record the Church's Letter continuing indirect accusations against
the minister, there never would have been a Dissent defending the minister.

To allow the Church Letter and Dissent would be tantamount to interpreting
the Sheppard Decision as meaning: "As long as no _formal_ prosecution occurs,

<a id="ga52-p849"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=851 printed_page=849 -->

the parties are free to continue making public insinuations and defenses related
to the August 2020 allegation—of an offense alleged to have occurred _in the_
_fall of 2017_ —and have these recorded in Presbytery minutes." [6]

Consistency demands a coupling of an evaluation of the Bulkeley Dissent with
an evaluation of Presbytery’s action in recording the Church Letter in its
Minutes. The Church's Letter contained exculpatory statements about the
family with implicit inculpatory reference to the minister. The Dissent
contained exculpatory statements about the minister and inculpatory
statements about the family. Neither were appropriate, given the SJC Decision
in _Sheppard_ .

Six weeks after the Session was notified of the SJC's October 25, 2022
Decision in _Sheppard_, it found away to enter into pres byte ry records a defense

—
of the accusers and an indirect critique of the SJC Decision in _Sheppard_
despite a session member filing a formal complaint against the session doing
so and unsuccessfully attempting to file a dissent. And Presbytery (which was
overruled in the _Sheppard_ Decision) was willing to oblige and entered the
Letter into its records on February 27, 2023. Allowing this would, for example,
set an unacceptable precedent in appeals before the SJC. Were it allowed, and
if the SJC sustained a minister's appeal, then his accuser or the prosecutor
could ask Presbytery to record a letter essentially disagreeing with the SJC,
which would likely trigger a dissent defending the minister, both of which
would be in Presbytery minutes _after_ the SJC had sustained the appeal. It
should be obvious why this is contrary to _BCO_ 14-7.

The allowance of any dissent also depends on the subject matter of a dissent.
Even if all the words within the four corners of a dissent are deemed to be

temperate, if the subject matter is inappropriate, it should not be recorded. For
example, an SJC member could write a dissent using clearly temperate
language, but if it inappropriately divulged things outside of closed session,
the SJC would not allow it to be recorded. ( _OMSJC_ 18.13; _BCO_ 42-8, 43-9)
Nothing in the _BCO_ or SJC Manual explicitly stipulates that
"inappropriateness" could be areas onto disallow a dissent, but such a dissent
would be disallowed. The Bulkeley Dissent was inappropriate. And so was
Presbytery's recording of the Church's Letter. Both should be erased.

6 For date of alleged offense, see Summary of Facts at 06/08/20 in _Sheppard_
Decision. (M50GA, p. 827)

<a id="ga52-p850"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=852 printed_page=850 -->

Allowing the Church's Letter to be recorded in Presbytery Minutes would be
akin to the following example. The SJC sustains a minister's appeal and annuls
the verdict and censure, but soon thereafter, that Presbytery files an overture
with GA asking the Assembly to record in its minutes statements wherein the
Presbytery "vouches" for the Christian character of the original accuser. Such
would (hopefully) never be allowed. Regardless of how temperate the
language might be in the overture, it would be improper, if for no other reason
than the SJC sustained the minister's appeal.

Finally, according to Presbytery's May 2, 2023 minutes, the minister is
probably now a member of another PCA Presbytery. And those May minutes
indicate Highlands sent to that Presbytery, "the SJC record and report, the

[Highlands] Presbytery commission report, and the reconciliation report." If
Highlands Presbytery no longer has jurisdiction over that minister, then that is
all the more reason why his name and any reference to the August 2020
accusations should cease appearing in its minutes - regardless of the form.

**V. AMENDS**

At its stated meeting following the receipt of this Decision, Highlands
Presbytery shall record its action expunging the Church's Letter, so that the
2025 GA Committee on Review of Presbytery Records can confirm that this
is done. ( _OMSJC_ 17.1.e, f) Thus, neither the Church's Letter nor the Bulkeley
Dissent can appear in Presbytery's minutes.

______________________________________________________________

The Panel's proposed decision was amended by the SJC, and the final Decision
was adopted by vote of 15-4, with 4 absent and 1 recused.

Bankson Absent Eggert _Concur_ Maynard _Concur_
Bise _Concur_ Evans Absent Neikirk Absent*

Bruce _Concur_ Garner Recused Pickering _Dissent_
Carrell _Concur_ Greco Absent Sartorius _Dissent_
Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall _Dissent_
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Concur_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe _Concur_ Lee _Concur_ White _Concur_

Dowling _Concur_ Marcinak _Concur_ Wilson _Dissent_

<a id="ga52-p851"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=853 printed_page=851 -->

RE Neikirk was present for the discussion but absent from the final vote.

TE Garner recused himself on the case due to concern that his historic personal
connections will distort the perception of his objectivity in this case.

**CONCURRING OPINION**

_Case No. 2023-16: Bulkeley & Gillikin v. Highlands_

RE Howie Donahoe

joined by RE John Bise, RE Steve Dowling, TE David Coffin

and TE Paul Kooistra

August 12, 2024

I concurred with the Decision but believe further comments are warranted

regarding how the SJC could reach the issue of Presbytery's recording of the
Church's Letter, a reach which some might conclude was contrary to the
restriction in _BCO_ 39-3.1.

_BCO_ 39-3.1 A higher court, reviewing a lower court, should limit
itself to the issues raised by the parties to the case in the original
(lower) court. ...

There appears to be ample evidence for such a conclusion. Below is an excerpt
from the original one-page Complaint filed with Presbytery:

Complainants contend that Highlands Presbytery erred by failing
to receive and record a Dissent that was timely filed by not
providing any reasons to support the Presbytery’s contention that
the Dissent was not couched in temperate language.

Below are the issues as proposed by the Parties in their Preliminary Briefs to
the SJC:

Complainant: Did Highlands Presbytery err when it denied the
Complaint alleging that Presbytery failed to receive and record a
timely filed Dissent, without identifying any words or phrases in

<a id="ga52-p852"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=854 printed_page=852 -->

the Dissent that were intemperate, consequently resulting in the
court declaring the Dissent out of order under BCO 45-5, and
where that Dissent, just on its face, is couched in temperate
language?

Presbytery: The original Complaint to Highlands Presbytery (HP)
states the contention of the complainants that HP erred in its action
to not receive and record the Dissent “by not providing any reasons
to support the Presbytery's contention that the Dissent was not
couched in temperate language.” The central issue is whether or
not Highland’s Presbytery erred in denying this Complaint.

The SJC Panel proposed the following as being the two Issues in this Case:

A. Did HP err in denying the original Complaint of TEs Bulkeley

and Gillikin concerning HP’s refusal to receive and record their
Dissent because it was not couched in temperate language?

B. Did HP err in failing to specify the particular language in the

Dissent it deemed intemperate?

For many readers, it will appear that the temperance of the dissent was " _the_
issue raised by the parties in the case in the original court." And that the SJC
is therefore limited to reviewing that specific issue only. However, the SJC
judged otherwise, and framed the Issue as follows:

SJC: Did the parties comply with the SJC's October 2022 Decision

in Case 2022-04: _Complaint of TE Sheppard v. Highlands_ ?

i expects u ch framing might come as a surprise to the Parties. I expect they
might wonder, "How could the SJC reach an issue if it was not _the_ issue "raised
by the parties in the original court"? That's a legitimate question. Was it
constitutionally permissible for the SJC to reach the issue of Presbytery
recording the Church's Letter in its minutes? It was.

Reframing & Reaching Issues—The SJC Manual stipulates that a party's
Preliminary Brief "should include the party’s position with regard to ... a
statement of the issue." ( _OMSJC_ 8.1.b.3) However, the SJC is not bound by
the statement of the issue proposed by any party. The SJC occasionally frames

<a id="ga52-p853"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=855 printed_page=853 -->

the issue differently than what's proposed and often, more importantly for this
Case, sometimes reaches an issue not directly raised by the parties.

Here are three examples. a higher court can reach a jurisdictional issue even
if neither party raises it in the original court. The SJC has reviewed cases where
an refiled a complaint against an action taken a ta pres byte ry meeting at
which he was not a commissioner. And when those cases have come to the SJC

it has ruled them administratively out of order regardless of whether a party
"raised the issue." [7] In addition, if the SJC notices in the record that a complaint
was not timely filed with the original court, it doesn't matter whether parties
raise the issue. The SJC will rule it administratively out of order for the
untimeliness of the original complaint. [8] And the SJC has often, _sua sponte_,
declared a case mooted based on something not raised by either party, even
when one or both parties still desire for the matter to be adjudicated. [9] Granted,
these are jurisdictional issues, but they are unraised issues nonetheless, and a
constricted reading of _BCO_ 39-3.1 would seem to preclude reaching even
them.

7 See Decision in Case 2022-09 RE Snoke v. Pittsburgh (M50GA, 2023, p. 882).

Vote was 20-1. See also: Case 2021-07 RE Acree v. Tennessee Valley (SJC
Report to 49th GA, p. 2144)
Case 2020-13 Benyola v. Central Florida (M48GA, 2021, p. 817)
Case 2020-01 Benyola v. Central Florida (M48GA, 2021, p. 801)
Case 2012-08 RE Warren Jackson v. NW Georgia (M43GA, 2015, p. 568)
Case 2012-06 Deacon Don Bethel v. SE Alabama (M41GA, 2013, p. 614)
Case 92-9b Overman v. Eastern Carolina (M21GA, 1993, p. 223)
8 Case 2001-32: _Session of Christ Covenant v. Central Carolina_ (M31GA, 2003, p
107). SJC was unanimous.
9
_sua_ _sponte_ is Latin for _"of_ _one's_ _own_ _accord"_ https://www.law.cornell.edu/wex/sua_sponte
Below are the six most recent mooted Cases, and case numbers for six older ones.
2022-16 Michelson et. al. v. Northwest Georgia (M50GA, 2023, p. 924)
2022-05 Crouse et al. v. Northwest Georgia (M50GA, 2023, p. 851)
2021-11 Brian Park et al. v. Korean Central (M50GA, 2023, p.796)
2018-04 Mapes, et al. v. Metro New York (M47GA, 2019, p. 576, three members

dissented)
2016-13 Daniels v. Nashville (M46GA, 2018, p. 523; six SJC members dissented,

p. 524)
See also Cases 2015-03, 2008-13, 2007-01, 2005-09, 2004-11 and 2001-36.

<a id="ga52-p854"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=856 printed_page=854 -->

The exercise of this power is not unlike the ability of US Federal Courts of
Appeal to correct unpreserved errors if they: are plain errors, affecting
substantial rights, and seriously affect the fairness, integrity or public
reputation of judicial proceedings. [10]

Unraised Constitutional Violations—Notwithstanding the above, my support
for the SJC Decision is primarily based on my answer to this question: In any
case before a higher court, if that court discovers the lower court acted contrary
to a ruling of that higher court, can it reach that issue, even if it is not raised
by the parties as an issue in the immediate case? Yes, it can.

For example. if TE Bulkeley had complained about something entirely
unrelated to Presbytery's recording of the Letter and/or the temperance of his
dissent, but the SJC noticed in the Record that the Presbytery had acted
contrary to _Sheppard_, it would be permissible for the SJC to address that
matter. It might not be the only issue addressed in such a case, but it should be
one of them. And in the present Complaint, Presbytery's action in recording
the Letter was an action contrary to the _Sheppard_ Decision, and thus
reviewable and correctable. And since it was deemed to be contrary to _BCO_
14-7, it was annulled, which rendered the Dissent moot.

The SJC's Judgment in _Sheppard_ stated: "Presbytery is debarred from
prosecuting any of the allegations embraced by the subject matter of this case."
And the SJC's Reasoning concluded: "Thus, Highlands Presbytery is debarred
from further prosecution of the offense alleged by the [family] or of any other
alleged offense embraced by the subject matter of this case."

The words "prosecuting" and "prosecution" have technical and non-technical
definitions. In the technical sense, they mean the institution and conducting of
legal proceedings against someone in respect to a charge. But in the broader
sense, they mean the continuation of a course of action. Presbytery should have
understood it in both senses. For example, it should be clear that further
investigation of allegations was disbarred after _Sheppard,_ even though that's
not "prosecution" in the technical sense. Similarly, it was not reasonable to

10 _United States v. Olano_, 507 U.S. 725 (1993)

<a id="ga52-p855"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=857 printed_page=855 -->

read _Sheppard_ and conclude it was permissible to record a document in
Presbytery minutes that's an indirect continuation of the matter. [11]

And thus, it was contrary to the conclusiveness of SJC decisions stipulated in
_BCO_ 14-7 and the SJC has the "duty and authority" to reach and correct that
constitutional error. And because the SJC acts as the General Assembly with
regard to SJC decisions, _BCO_ 11-2 provides the SJC “all the administrative
authority necessary to give effect to” its decisions. If the SJC finds a breach,
the SJC has authority to act. With regard to compliance with the _Sheppard_
Decision, the SJC is the court of jurisdiction. it is important to
communicate that _BCO_ 14-7 is not a dead letter: "Judicial decisions _shall_ be

binding _and conclusive_ on the parties who are directly involved in the matter
..."

If the SJC declined to reach the Letter, it seems there would only have been
two other alternatives: (1) ignore the breach or (2) note the violation of _BCO_
14-7 and refer it to the GA Committee on Review of Presbytery Records, like
a _BCO_ 40-5 report. But that's unnecessary and unwise. The SJC is more
familiar with the _Sheppard_ Decision and the Record of that Case than RPR
members. In _Sheppard_, the Panel was required to read the entire 188-page
Record and hear oral arguments. When SJC members qualified to debate and
vote, they needed to have read the Panel's proposed decision, all the briefs of
the Parties, and enough of the Record to understand the issues in the Case.
There is no requirement for any RPR member to do so, nor would there be if
RPR was reviewing a _BCO_ 40-5 report.

Ensuring compliance with SJC Decisions was one reason why the SJC
proposed, and the 51 [st] GA adopted, a change to _OMSJC_ 17.1 adding two new
sections to the four already required in SJC decisions—i.e., in addition to (a)
summary of the facts, (b) statement of the issue, (c) judgment, and (d)
reasoning and opinion. These two additions address compliance and review
thereof.

11 I dissented in the _Sheppard_ Decision and believed the allegations could have been

adjudicated. I did not agree that the previous version of _BCO_ 32-20 stipulated a hard,
one-year statute of limitations on all allegations. But the SJC ruled otherwise.
Fortunately, _BCO_ 32-20 was revised in June 2022 to stipulate that a hard statute of
limitations does not exist.

<a id="ga52-p856"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=858 printed_page=856 -->

(e) Any Amends. (Directions as to what the lower court must

do, or undo, in light of the Judgment. Cf. _BCO_ 42-9; 4310; 40-5).

(f) A direction that the full decision ( _OMSJC_ 17.1(a-f)) shall

be recorded in the minutes of the lower court(s), as well
as a statement of how an affected lower court has

complied with any amends therein.

This new addition should enable RPR to read an SJC decision in a Presbytery's
minutes, which will now include a specific statement about what that
presbytery was required to do or cease doing, along with Presbytery's
statement about how it complied. But absent this new change, expecting RPR
to review Highlands' compliance with _Sheppard_ would have been
unreasonable.

And in the end, the Complainants in this Case prevailed, in a sense, because
the action of recording the Letter, against which the Dissent was filed, was
annulled.

/s/ RE Howie Donahoe [12]

**CONCURRING OPINION**
_Case No. 2023-16: Bulkeley & Gillikin v. Highlands_

RE Jim Eggert
August 9, 2024

I write to clarify my reasons for concurring in the Decision.

This is the second time this court has been called to address controversy arising
out of the underlying matters involved in the instant complaint. In our previous

12 As a historical note, _BCO_ 39-3 was added in 1996, entirely new, by the 24th GA in
Ft. Lauderdale, when that GA enacted a recommendation proposed in 1995 by the
Ad Interim Committee on Judicial Procedures. (M23GA, p. 86 & M24GA, p. 79)
TE David Coffin was chairman of the AICJP and even 30 years later the AICJP
should be commended for its excellent work. The AICJP membership included TEs
Morton Smith, P. Gilchrist, P. Fowler, Lee Ferguson and David Gordon, along with
REs Dale Peacock and Jack Williamson

<a id="ga52-p857"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=859 printed_page=857 -->

_Sheppard_ case the SJC debarred further action against TE Sheppard on the
ground that former _BCO_ 32-20 prohibited Presbytery from adjudicating claims
raised against TE Sheppard because Presbytery had waited too long (more than
one year) to initiate judicial process against TE Sheppard. The underlying
matter pertained to allegations against TE Sheppard made against him by
members of the church that he served, allegations that by virtue of our previous
decision can now never be adjudicated by our ecclesiastical courts.

_BCO_ 14-7 provides: “Judicial decisions shall be binding and conclusive on the
parties who are directly involved in the matter being adjudicated…”

“Binding” means imposing an obligation, and “conclusive” means putting an
end to debate or question.

This provision for finality memorializes the principle that ecclesiastical
disputes, for better or worse, must come to an end for the parties bound by the
decision of a higher court.

Presbytery being party to the _Sheppard_ case, the SJC’s decision has “debarred”
Presbytery from “prosecuting any of the allegations embraced by the subject
matter of this case.” That debarment was and continues to be binding and
conclusive not only upon Highlands Presbytery but also, by implication, on all
the member churches of Highlands Presbytery and its Sessions since “The
Presbytery consists of all the teaching elders _and churches_ within its bounds
that have been accepted by the Presbytery” ( _BCO_ 13-1) (emphasis added). The
Church courts being organized “in regular gradation” ( _BCO_ 10-1), the Session
represents the government of those churches that are members of Presbytery
and are in that measure _part_ of Presbytery in the sense that that they have “a
mutual relation” such that “every act of jurisdiction is the act of the whole
Church performed by it through the appropriate organ.” ( _BCO_ 11-4). When
the SJC decides a particular case as it did in _Sheppard_, it is an “act of
jurisdiction,” and therefore was an act of the “whole Church.”

i would add that i dissented in _Sheppard_, arguing that former _BCO_ 32-20
should not have been understood as a “statute of limitations” and that the

charges against TE Sheppard should have been permitted to proceed so that
the controversy could have been settled in a trial governed by our _Rules of_
_Discipline_ . As I stated then, reading _BCO_ 32-20 as a debarment permits the
scandal in such cases to continue unabated, only now with the added dynamic

<a id="ga52-p858"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=860 printed_page=858 -->

that the Church is rendered unable to substantively address the claims via
judicial process.

But regardless of any disagreement regarding the _Sheppard_ case, including my
own, the SJC’s decision was _final_, and therefore _BCO_ 14-7 binds the SJC to
hold that the allegations raised against TE Sheppard, having been debarred,
cannot be settled in any ecclesiastical proceeding and therefore the claims
touching on that case must be understood to be deferred indefinitely.

When a controversy is decided by an ecclesiastical court of last resort, the
Church should make every effort, as difficult as that may be, to lay aside
further controversy and scandal regarding the matter. Experience teaches us
that sometimes the parties to an ecclesiastical controversy are dissatisfied with
a court’s decision. Even so, the court’s decision stands.

Notably, our _Confession_ concedes that courts may err, and we are comforted
by our conviction that all matters will be resolved at the Last Judgment when
“all persons, that have lived upon earth, shall appear before the tribunal of
Christ, to give an account of their thoughts, words, and deeds'' ( _WCF_ 33.1) and
that Christ is both a gracious and perfect Judge and King. This matter having
been debarred by our previous action, we must defer further judicial
controversy arising out of the _Sheppard_ case until the Last Day. Until then,
_BCO_ 14-7 directs that the decision “shall be binding and conclusive on the
parties who are directly involved in the matter being adjudicated,” meaning
that any disappointed parties must patiently entrust themselves to “Him who
judges justly.” (1 Peter 2:23).

i concurred in the decision today because i was convinced that t hes j cis
bound by the finality demanded in _BCO_ 14-7 to seek to quell the instant
controversy over the _Sheppard_ matter as presented to us in this case.

In an immediate sense, the matter presented to the SJC was whether the dissent
filed by the complainants was wrongfully refused by the Presbytery, and it
might have been easy enough for the SJC to consider the simple question of
whether or not the dissent was “couched in temperate language” ( _BCO_ 45-5).

But the more I considered the record and whether the dissent was “couched in

temperate language” or not, the more I realized that the record revealed that
the dissent was itself offered in a wider context of continuing contention in the

<a id="ga52-p859"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=861 printed_page=859 -->

Presbytery about the _Sheppard_ matter, and I became convinced that the finality
mandate of _BCO_ 14-7 imposed an overriding obligation not only over the
dissenters but over the whole Presbytery.

I similarly became convinced that _BCO_ 14-7 placed an overriding obligation
upon _me,_ as a member of the SJC _,_ to try to realize and apply the finality that
_BCO_ 14-7 directs. After all, I have sworn an oath to "judge according to the
Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America, through my best efforts
applied to nothing other than the record of the case and other documents
properly before me.”

Based on this, I concluded that limiting the ruling to whether the dissent was
“couched in temperate language” or not, as the simple framing of the
complaint would have proposed, would undermine my wider obligation to
uphold the mandate of finality imposed by _BCO_ 14-7.

Our form of government is designed to assure that our courts restrain the
exercise of their jurisdiction and show proper deference to lower courts. In
today's decision, the SJC directs the Presbytery to remove from its records a
letter it received and recorded in its minutes from one of its member Sessions

about the _Sheppard_ matter, an action that was not immediately requested in
the case by either party. Our so directing the Presbytery is an unusual exercise
of ecclesiastical power and deserves further explanation.

_BCO_ 39-3.4 provides:

The higher court does have the power and obligation of
judicial review, which cannot be satisfied by always deferring
to the findings of a lower court. Therefore, a higher court
should not consider itself obliged to exhibit the same
deference to a lower court when the issues being reviewed
involve the interpretation of the Constitution of the Church.
Regarding such issues, the higher court has the duty and
authority to interpret and apply the Constitution of the Church
according to its best abilities and understanding, regardless of
the opinion of the lower court.

This provision is an express grant of power of “judicial review” regardless of
the findings or machinations of a lower court, such that there is no deference

<a id="ga52-p860"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=862 printed_page=860 -->

owed to the lower court “when the issues being reviewed involve the
interpretation of the Constitution of the Church.”

The phrase “the issues being reviewed” in this context includes any issue that
is properly before the SJC. Certainly, the issue being reviewed in this case, as
putt o usb y the parties, was whether Presbytery should have received the
dissent.

But the SJC’s analysis of whether the Presbytery properly declined to record
the dissent necessarily required that the SJC consider the Constitutional issue
of finality under _BCO_ 14-7. But the SJC could not act consistently with _BCO_
14-7 unless it also took into account the Presbytery’s receipt of the Session’s
letter, the very subject of the dissent.

If the SJC sustained the complaint and annulled the Presbytery’s refusal to
receive the dissent, thereby requiring the Presbytery to receive it, the SJC
would have permitted the Presbytery to continue to contend about the
_Sheppard_ case in contravention of _BCO_ 14-7.

On the other hand, if the SJC had denied the complaint without also directing
that the Session’s letter be expunged, the dissent would have been excluded
from the Presbytery’s records, but the letter from the Session expressing its
contentions about the _Sheppard_ matter would remain, contrary to the finality
mandated by _BCO_ 14-7.

Therefore, to vindicate the finality of _BCO_ 14-7, I found it necessary to not
only deny the complaint, but to require that the Session’s letter be expunged.
I could not in good conscience defer to the lower court’s continued contentions
about the _Sheppard_ matter represented by the Session’s letter and the
complainants’ dissent.

**DISSENTING OPINION**

_Case No. 2023-16: Bulkeley & Gillikin v. Highlands_

RE John Pickering

August 10, 2024

The SJC looks with disfavor on Highlands Presbytery’s recording of the letter
from Arden Presbyterian’s Session, a letter which loudly takes aside ina

<a id="ga52-p861"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=863 printed_page=861 -->

dispute the SJC previously judged nonresolvable through judicial process due
to the same Presbytery’s previous mishandling of it. The SJC holds that
Highlands Presbytery should never have recorded the letter in the first place,
and the SJC may be right. However, despite the strong feelings in the
Presbytery surrounding this matter that are evident from the record, not a
single member of the Presbytery complained against the recording of the letter.
Nevertheless, the SJC has decided to reach outside of the case before it to right
the wrong that took place when the letter was recorded, doing so in
contravention of the _BCO_, as ably expressed by the other dissenters, with
whom I join. Highlands Presbytery erred in recording the letter, and now the
SJC errs in undoing that action without jurisdiction. As countless children have
been counseled, “Two wrongs don’t make a right.”

As support for its decision, the SJC and the concurring opinion of RE Howie
Donahoe cite examples of cases purportedly decided on the basis of an issue
or argument not raised, briefed, or argued by the parties. But in each example
given, the SJC threw out the complaint because of jurisdictional defects, and
therefore did not rule on the challenged lower court actions, much less actions
about which the complainants did not complain. In the examples given, the
complaints were out of order because the complainant lacked standing, the
complaint was not timely filed, or the complaint had been rendered moot by
subsequent events. The proposition actually supported by these examples is
not that the SJC has the power to reach issues not presented, but just the
opposite—that the SJC lacks the power to reach issues not properly before it.
For these reasons, I dissent.

**DISSENTING OPINION**

**Case 2023-16**

TE Art Sartorius

August 12, 2022

The case of Bulkeley v. Highlands Presbytery (SJC Case # 2023-16) came
before the SJC by way of Complaint. This Complaint was initiated when the
Presbytery refused to record a written dissent ( _BCO_ 45) submitted by members
of the Presbytery on the grounds that the dissent was found “to not be couched
in temperate language,” per _BCO_ 45–5. That particular dissent was being
offered against an action taken by the Presbytery to place in its records a letter
from the session of Arden Presbyterian Church.

<a id="ga52-p862"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=864 printed_page=862 -->

Once Highlands Presbytery acted to record the letter from the Arden
Presbyterian Session, no Complaint against the action of recording the letter
was taken. There was only the offering of the aforementioned dissent. Further,
when the Complaint was made against the refusal to record the dissent, the
requested amends were only that the Compliant be sustained and that
Highlands Presbytery act on the requirement of _BCO_ 45-5 to record the
dissent.

I dissent from the Standing Judicial Commission’s Decision. The SJC should
not reach out to, in effect, take jurisdiction of a question not before it. No
complaint was filed against the recording by Highlands Presbytery of the letter
from the session of Arden Presbyterian Church, so the SJC should not be ruling
on whether the Presbytery should have recorded the letter.

_BCO_ 15-5.a establishes that “the Standing Judicial Commission shall have the
judicial powers and be governed by the judicial procedures of the General
Assembly.”

Procedurally, in regard to complaints, _BCO_ 43-10 speaks to the power of the
higher court in dealing with complaints brought before it. “The higher court
has power, in its discretion, to annul the whole or any part of _the action of a_
_lower court against which complaint has been made_, or to send the matter back
to the lower court with instructions for a new hearing.” (Emphasis added). This
_BCO_ provision does not envision the SJC arrogating to itself matters not raised
_before_ it when a complaint is lodged.

Further, and more specifically in regard to judicial procedures, _BCO_ 39-3 deals
with principles which guide a higher court when reviewing lower court judicial
proceedings. The first of the enumerated principles states that “[a] higher
court, reviewing a lower court, should limit itself to the issues raised by the
parties to the case in the original (lower) court.”

The decision of the SJC in this matter reaches what seems to be a reasonable

conclusion. It could be said that it would have been wise for the lower court in

this matter to never have recorded the Arden session’s letter so as not to prompt
an offer o fa written dissent. Yet, that is not how this matter arose, and the SJC
should not _sua sponte_ rule on a matter regarding the letter which is not before
it, thus circumventing our _BCO_ judicial procedures. To do so, not only rules

<a id="ga52-p863"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=865 printed_page=863 -->

on a matter not before the SJC, but it also precludes the lower court or any
member of the lower court, from presenting argument for or against the SJC
ruling, which it could not have anticipated.

The SJC decision expresses a preference for the approach the Commission
takes a sit is helpful inputting an end to “parties making public insinuations
and defenses” related to allegations which have been the subject of a concluded
case. However, there should be some freedom for those in the Church to
disagree with decisions made by fallible decision makers. That freedom should
also allow those different opinions to be made either privately or publicly. If
that freedom of disagreement is ever abused, courts are also not without
recourse. As already mentioned, the act of recording a public letter is subject
to complaint. Furthermore, those making public statements can be opening
themselves up to charges of slander or defamation in church courts if they are
overstepping. Remedies, quite simply, are available without the SJC policing
its own prior decisions.

In summary, it is the view expressed in this dissent that the SJC should have
limited its decision to the specific matters raised in the Complaint. Doing so
would have precluded the SJC from acting beyond the Complaint to direct the
expungement of the letter from the session of Arden Presbyterian Church from
the records of the Highlands Presbytery. This dissent sees no need at this point
to address how the Complaint should have ultimately been dealt with or what
standard of review should have governed the handling of the Complaint. It
suffices merely to express that the SJC approach goes beyond BCO procedural
limitations.

**DISSENTING OPINION**

2023-16 _Bulkeley et al. v. Highlands Presbytery_

RE Jack Wilson, joined by RE Caleb Stegall

August 6, 2024

The first enumerated principle of judicial review in our Constitution provides
that a higher court should limit itself to the issued raised by the parties in the
original court. _BCO_ 39-3(1). The Complaints in the lower courts concerning
the Church’s submission and Presbytery’s reception of the Letter were not
elevated to the General Assembly. Neither the contents of the Letter, nor its
reception by Presbytery was the subject matter of the Complaint in this case.

<a id="ga52-p864"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=866 printed_page=864 -->

Since the parties in this case did not raise issues with respect to Letter, under
our first principle of review, the Commission should not have addressed issues
related to the Letter. Moreover, I do not believe Presbytery’s reception of the
Letter offended the prohibition against further prosecution articulated in Case
No. 2022-04 Sheppard v. Highlands Presbytery.

Presbytery rejected and declined to record a Dissent proffered by the
Complainants. The Dissent was regularly and properly submitted in
accordance with _BCO_ 45-1. It expressed a different opinion from Presbytery’s
in accordance with _BCO_ 45-2. It provided reasons for a belief that Presbytery’s
action was in error pursuant to _BCO_ 45-3. While expressing strong
disagreement with Presbytery’s action, it was couched in temperate language.
I believe Presbytery clearly erred _(BCO_ 39-3(2) and (3)) when it declined to
record the Dissent which conformed to _BCO_ 45. The Complaint should have
been sustained on the Record presented in this matter. For these reasons, I
respectfully dissent.

<a id="ga52-p865"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=867 printed_page=865 -->

**CASE 2023-18**

**TE IAN SEARS**

**v.**

**NASHVILLE PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON APPEAL**

**October 17, 2024**

**CASE SUMMARY**

This case came before the SJC on the Appeal of TE Ian Sears, then Pastor of
The Church of Grace Village in Nashville, Tennessee, within the Nashville
Presbytery (“NP”). The Appeal arose from the censure of deposition imposed
by ann p commission under _BCO_ 38-1, the case having been handled as a case
without process. The censure was in response to two incidents, in 2010 and
2011, involving TE Sears and a woman then in his congregation (referred to
herein as “Ms. W.”).

The Panel heard the case on August 5, 2024, by means of a GoToMeeting web
conference. Appellant, TE Sears, appeared at the meeting with his
representative, TE Dominic Aquila. NP, the Appellee, was represented by two
of its Ruling Elders, William Ayers and Bryce Sullivan, both from Nashville.

The Panel was composed of TE Fred Greco (Chairman), TE Paul Kooistra,
and RE Dan Carrell, along with Alternates TE Guy Waters and RE Jack
Wilson (Secretary). Having heard the matter for about two and a half hours,
and upon their close review of the Record and a series of deliberations, they
have unanimously determined to deny the Appeal.

**I.** **SUMMARY OF THE FACTS**

2019 TE John Barrett, of another PCA Presbytery, contacted TE
Richard Schwartz, a member of NP’s Shepherding Committee
(“SC”), with allegations of sin against TE Sears, dating from 2010
and 2011 and involving Ms. W, who by then had become a
member of the congregation that TE Barrett served as a Pastor.

<a id="ga52-p866"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=868 printed_page=866 -->

2019-20 Several members of SC met with TE Sears regarding the
allegations raised by TE Barrett.

09/11/20 a portion oft hes c “accepted the recommendation” of its Ad Hoc
Committee that “no further action be taken concerning” a report
regarding TE Sears and events that “occurred over eight years
ago” “except to continue to shepherd and support TE Sears and
his family.”

02/02/23 NP, through its Stated Clerk, received a letter from TE Barrett and
Ms. W repeating their previous allegations of sin against TE Ian
Sears and urging that TE Sears “be investigated by his presbytery”
“according to the _Book of Church Order_ 31-2.” The letter
(hereafter the “Feb. 2 letter”) stated that TE Barrett “had
previously reported this incident to the Shepherding Committee of
Nashville Presbytery in early 2020, but the committee declined to
act and did not report it to the Presbytery, to our knowledge.”

02/10/23 The Stated Clerk of NP offered three procedural “options” to TE
Sears in a “confidential” e-mail – “allow a _BCO_ 31-2 investigation
to commence as a result of the letter,” “ask for a _BCO_ 31-2
investigation yourself if the accusations made in the letter are
entirely false,” [or] “come forward to confess your sins with
regards to this situation (not necessarily agreeing with all the
accusations) before a judicial commission.”

02/12/23 TE Sears submitted to the Stated Clerk and three other NP elders

a “rough draft of a preliminary response” to the Feb. 2 letter. In
this draft TE Sears “own[ed] the truth of both allegations,”
provided factual context, and described “My Repentance.”

02/14/23 At its Stated Meeting, NP received a report of the Feb. 2 letter and
thereupon adopted the following motion: “To empower the
Moderator to appoint a _BCO_ 15-1 Commission composed of three
teaching elders and three ruling elders with a quorum of four
members to conduct a _BCO_ 38-1 case without process pertaining
to the confession of TE Sears and that the commission be

empowered to approve TE Sears’ written confession and proceed
to a judgment, including imposing any censure under _BCO_ 36.

<a id="ga52-p867"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=869 printed_page=867 -->

The commission is instructed to determine whether it is in fact a

case without process, and, if it is not, to refer it back to the
presbytery.”

03/09/23 NP’s 15-1 Commission (15-1 Commission) met via video
conference with TE Barrett and Ms. W.

03/10/23 TE Sears emailed to the NP Stated Clerk his “response to the [Feb.
2] letter” for the 15-1 Commission.

03/23/23 The 15-1 Commission again met via video conference with TE
Barrett and Ms. W.

05/18/23 The 15-1 Commission “reviewed the initial draft statement of
facts and confession of guilt prepared by [a member of the 15-1
Commission] and discussed certain additions and revisions.”

05/29/23 The 15-1 Commission “drafted a statement of facts and confession
of guilt and sent it to TE Sears.”

06/21/23 The 15-1 Commission met with TE Sears in order “to discuss the

draft statement of facts and confession of guilt previously
provided to TE Sears and to answer any pertinent questions about
the case.” TE Sears raised questions and concerns about
“language” within the draft and “also challenged the admission in
the draft statement that he was guilty of the sin of adultery.”

06/24/23 TE Sears offered to submit to the 15-1 Commission’s Chair “a

statement of facts and confession of guilt that I’m ready to sign
and for the approval of the Commission.”

06/29/23 The 15-1 Commission “received and approved editorial changes
made to the statement of facts and confession of guilt following
the June 21 meeting with TE Sears.”

07/01/23 The 15-1 Commission’s Chair told TE Sears that the 15-1

Commission “edited the statement of facts a bit after our meeting,”
and informed him that he was “welcome to submit anything else

<a id="ga52-p868"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=870 printed_page=868 -->

he would like but at this point it will not necessarily help the
process to submit anything other than what you already did.”

07/07/23 The 15-1 Commission Chair sent to TE Sears a “finalized

statement of facts.”

07/14/23 TE Sears sent to the 15-1 Commission an e-mail “request[ing] the
Commission’s consideration to include additional Statements of

Fact.”

07/18/23 In an e-mail to TE Sears, The 15-1 Commission’s Chair informed
TE Sears that Sears’s own “revised statement included several

facts that would be disputed, as well as a number of statements
that would be relevant to potential censure. So we’d like to invite
you again to agree to the statement of facts that we provided after
meeting with you.”

07/20/23 The 15-1 Commission later reported that TE Sears had requested
by e-mail of July 20 that the 15-1 Commission “take into account
his February 13, 2023 letter, as well as statements submitted by
TEs Richard Schwartz and Brandon Eggar who were members of
the Nashville Presbytery’s Shepherding Committee in 2020.” The
15-1 Commission also reported that it was agreeable to this
request.

07/26/23 TE Sears signed a “Statement of Facts and Confession of Guilt,”
(sometimes hereafter the “Confession”).

08/02/23 The 15-1 Commission “approved the statement and confession as
signed” and began to discuss “an appropriate censure” for TE
Sears.

08/08/23 The 15-1 Commission “approve[d] the censure of TE Sears by
deposition from office pursuant to _BCO_ 30-5.”

08/16/23 TE Sears emailed TE Schwartz and several other TEs a draft
statement for the 15-1 Commission in which he would say he
“wanted to inform you of my decision to withdraw my confession
and remand this case back to the Presbytery.” In the email, he

<a id="ga52-p869"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=871 printed_page=869 -->

asked “Can you let me know what your thoughts [ _sic_ ]?” Just over
an hour later TE Schwartz responded with a few questions.

08/16/23 Meeting that evening, the 15-1 Commission “affirmed … that it
approved the censure of deposition from office ( _BCO_ 30-5) based
solely upon TE Sears’s signed statement of facts and confession
of guilt and the written response provided to the 15-1 Commission
byte sears by e-mail dated March 10, 2023, and the statements
of TEs Richard Schwartz and Brandon Eggar (see attached),”
statements that TE Sears had requested on July 20 for the 15-1
Commission’s consideration.

09/05/23 TE Sears sent to TE Schwartz and several other TEs “Anne

Sears[’s] statement intended for the Judicial Commission.”

09/05/23 TE Sears sent to the several TEs a “personal statement” that was
“much revised [from the one] I sent you yesterday.”

09/06/23 TE Sears wrote the 15-1 Commission “retracting my Statement
and Confession of Guilt **.** ”

09/06/23 15-1 Commission’s Chair informed TE Sears that the 15-1
Commission “has judged [your retraction] out of order” and that
once “the commission [had] rendered its judgment and the
censure, … [the] business of the case was concluded.”

09/07/23 TE Sears sent to the 15-1 Commission a “memo to the Judicial
Commission with a request to rescind its prior action in my case.”

09/07/23 The 15-1 Commission “inflicts the censure of deposition from
office upon TE Sears _in absentia_ by reading the censure aloud to
those present.”

09/08/23 The 15-1 Commission’s Chair informed TE Sears, by e-mail, of
the 09/07/23 infliction of the censure of deposition. He reported
“there will be a called meeting of the Nashville presbytery on
Tuesday, September 26, 2023, during which the moderator will
inform the presbytery of this judgment.”

<a id="ga52-p870"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=872 printed_page=870 -->

09/08/23 TE Sears notified the PCA Stated Clerk of his appeal from the
judgment of NP.

09/10/23 The 15-1 Commission, upon request from a member of the
Session of The Church of Grace Village **,** “suspend[ed] TE Sears
from office administratively pursuant to _BCO_ 42-6.” The 15-1
Commission’s Chair informed TE Sears, by e-mail, of that action.

09/25/23 TE Sears filed his appeal, dated September 22, 2023, “from the
judgment and censure of Nashville Presbytery, taken on
September 7, 2023.”

09/26/23 a ta called meeting of NP, The 15-1 Commission Chair
“reviewed the _BCO_ 31-2 report concerning TE Ian Sears, the work
of the Commission and the Nashville Presbytery’s censure of TE
Sears through the commission.”

NP “receive[d] the full report of the proceedings from the _BCO_
15-1 Commission created at the February 14, 2023 Stated Meeting
to conduct a _BCO_ 38-1 case without process pertaining to the
confession of TE Ian Sears and to take up any business arising
from or related to the commission’s report.”

NP acted “to direct the Stated Clerk to report NP actions
pertaining to TE Ian Sears to TE John Barrett … and to inquire
whether there is further assistance for the victim which NP may
provide.”

10/09/23 NP’s 15-1 Commission “approved for inclusion in the Record of
the Case and for reporting to the Nashville Presbytery at the
November stated meeting” a document, “Rationale of the
Nashville Presbytery 15-1 Commission Regarding the Judgment
and Censure Against TE Ian Sears.”

The document concluded: “In light of the undisputed facts agreed
to by TE Sears and the statements he approved as part of the
record, the commission concluded that TE Sears has not repented
of the grievous sins he confessed to in the statement of facts and
confession of guilt that he signed on July 26, 2023. Therefore, the

<a id="ga52-p871"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=873 printed_page=871 -->

commission determined that deposition was the appropriate
censure.”

**II.** **STATEMENT OF THE ISSUES**

1. Shall this specification of error be sustained: NP erred by

appointing a _BCO_ 38-1 commission as opposed to a _BCO_ 31-2
Investigative Commission?

2. Shall this specification of error be sustained: NP erred when the

15-1 Commission failed to hear Appellant’s statements first
before receiving any information adverse to the accused?

3. Shall this specification of error be sustained: NP erred when the

15-1 Commission drafted the statement of facts and confession of

guilt without the consent and collaboration of the accused?

4. Shall this specification of error be sustained: NP erred when the

15-1 Commission failed to examine the 2020 SC investigation,
and therefore failed to assess properly the veracity of its report,
and the work of the Spirit in the life of repentance?

5. Shall this specification of error be sustained: NP erred by not

allowing Appellant the right to withdraw his approved and cosigned _BCO_ 38-1 Confession?

**III.** **JUDGMENTS**

1. No.

2. No.

3. No.

4. No.

5. No.

<a id="ga52-p872"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=874 printed_page=872 -->

**IV.** **REASONING AND OPINION**

We find that each of the specifications of error raised by Appellant is
insufficient to overturn NP's judgment. As a result, we affirm the judgment of
the lower court in whole ( _BCO_ 42-9). as to each of the specifications, we
conclude:

**Specification 1**

Appellant alleges that NP erred by appointing a _BCO_ 38-1
commission as opposed to a _BCO_ 31-2 Investigative Commission. This
specification of error is not sustained.

Central to Appellant’s contention is an extra-Constitutional view that
there is a requirement that the accused must come forward to the court with a
confession of guilt before the court can take any steps toward a _BCO_ 38-1 case
without process. Appellant claims that when NP passed a motion to form the
15-1 Commission “to conduct a _BCO_ 38-1 case without process pertaining to
the confession of TE Sears and that the commission be empowered to approve
TE Sears’ written confession and proceed to a judgment, including imposing
any censure under _BCO_ 36,” it acted unconstitutionally and committed clear
error. In Appellant’s eyes, NP could only have formed a commission to
conduct a _BCO_ 38-1 case if Appellant had initiated such with NP. _BCO_ 38-1,
however, contains no such limitation. A commission is, by definition, a group
authorized by the court “to deliberate upon and conclude the business referred
to it” ( _BCO_ 15-1). In that sense, a commission has the same authority and
ability as the full court within the parameters of the business assigned to it.
_BCO_ 38-1 does not require a court to get the advanced permission of an
accused in order to hear a potential confession, arrange meetings related to a
potential confession, or make an initial determination as to whether an accused
is making a confession. All of these powers are inherent to the court. What
_BCO_ 38-1 requires is that the accused actually make a confession and give
evidence of the same. The text of _BCO_ 38-1 has been made explicit as to how
the court can determine that a confession is intended:

In the event a confession is intended, a written Confession
(i.e., a sufficient summary of the facts, the person’s specific
confession, and any expression or evidence of repentance)

<a id="ga52-p873"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=875 printed_page=873 -->

must be approved by the accused, and by the court, before the
court proceeds to a judgment, and the co-signed document
shall be appended to the minutes (regular or executive
session).

The Constitution clearly states that the accused intends to confess
when a written statement of the facts _**is signed by the accused**_ and the court.
Appellate courts do not need to look for additional evidence of intentionality—
the signature of the accused is dispositive evidence of his confession.

If a court may, therefore, take steps and actions prior to the co-signing
of a written Confession, such as speaking with the accused, confirming the
facts by speaking with potential witnesses, and even editing drafts of a written
Confession, it is perfectly consistent with our Constitution to vest those powers
in a _BCO_ 15-1 commission. When NP formed the 15-1 Commission, it was
not concluding a _BCO_ 38-1 case without process; it was empowering a group
of elders (as a commission) from NP to determine whether, in fact, there was
such a case without process, to review any written Confession, and to co-sign
the written Confession on behalf of NP. Appellant acknowledges that the
amended motion passed by NP included language specifically on this point:
“determine whether it is a case without process, and i fit is not, to refer it back
to the presbytery.” It certainly might have been the case that no co-signed
written Confession could be agreed upon. In the absence of such, the powers
granted to the 15-1 Commission did not include drawing up an indictment and
conducting a trial. The matter would have to have been referred back to NP to
determine the next steps. Appellant was under no obligation to testify against
himself or sign the written Confession. Had he failed to sign, the matter would
have been referred back to NP. The 15-1 Commission did not have the

authority to find Appellant contumacious, so there could be no possible threat
of contumacy for failure to sign the written Confession.

Appellant further contends that any report that comes to a court
requires that court to conduct a _BCO_ 31-2 investigation. However, such an
investigation is certainly not required when the accused confesses to sin, and
in this case Appellant proved his confession by signing the written Confession.
Appellant could have asked NP to conduct a _BCO_ 31-2 investigation. He did
not do so, but chose instead to sign a written Confession under _BCO_ 38-1.
Contrary to Appellant’s contention that he was coerced into a _BCO_ 38-1 case
without process, Appellant worked over a period of several months with the

<a id="ga52-p874"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=876 printed_page=874 -->

15-1 Commission to draft, revise, and finalize the written Confession. The 151 Commission would have had no indication from Appellant that a _BCO_ 31-2
investigation was necessary while it was collaborating with Appellant drafting
a written Confession.

**Specification 2**

Appellant alleges that NP erred when the 15-1 Commission failed to
hear Appellant’s statements first before receiving any information adverse to
the accused. This specification of error is not sustained.

The appellant bases this specification of error on an erroneous
interpretation of a previous case, SJC 2020-07 ( _TE Wilbourne v. Pacific_
_Presbytery_ ). Quoting Wilbourne, Appellant argues that a portion of the
decision applies in the instant case: “Hearing a negative report before hearing
a man’s confession is more prejudicial than after, given the importance of first
impressions.” From this statement, Appellant concludes that any _BCO_ 38-1
matter, whether conducted by a court ora commission, must begin with
meeting and receiving information from the accused. But this is simply not the
case, neither with respect to the Constitutional requirements of _BCO_ 38-1, nor
the holding of _Wilbourne_ . In _Wilbourne_, the court was dealing with
information that was presented to the court after a _BCO_ 38-1 confession had
been drafted and agreed to but before the court had heard the confession, not
at the beginning of the _BCO_ 38-1 process. Wilbourne was distinguishing the
facts from another case that Appellant cites, SJC 2019-10 ( _TE Evans v. Arizona_
_Presbytery_ ), in which additional information was provided to the court _**after**_ it
had heard the _BCO_ 38-1 confession. In both _Wilbourne_ and _Evans_, the issue
was the provision of information in addition to the confession agreed upon by
both the accused and the court. The Constitutional error of doing so has been
made even clearer by subsequent amendments to _BCO_ 38-1:

In the event a confession is intended, a written Confession
(i.e., a sufficient summary of the facts, the person’s specific
confession, and any expression or evidence of repentance)
must be approved by the accused, and by the court, before the
court proceeds to a judgment, and the co-signed document
shall be appended to the minutes (regular or executive
session). No other information may be presented without
written consent from the accused and the court, and this

<a id="ga52-p875"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=877 printed_page=875 -->

prohibition includes individuals, prosecutors, committees,
and commissions.

_BCO_ 38-1 expressly requires a _**written**_ Confession that is _**co-signed**_ to
avoid any confusion or ambiguity about the basis upon which the court
subsequent inflicts censure. It is only that written Confession and any other
information specifically consented to by the accused and the court that the
court may consider in its determination of a censure.

Appellant’s argument proves too much—according to Appellant, a
court may not even begin a _BCO_ 38-1 process without first receiving
information from the accused. But the _BCO_ 38-1 prohibition applies to the
period of time after the confession is finalized by the court and the accused,
not to the period of time leading up to its finalization. _BCO_ 38-1 gives no
requirement or guidelines for whom a court may meet with, discuss, or receive
information before a written Confession is agreed to. _BCO_ 38-1 now makes
explicit what both _Wilbourne_ and _Evans_ stand for, that is, that no additional
information beyond the written Confession and any other information that is
mutually agreed to in writing may be considered by the court in rendering
judgment.

**Specification 3**

Appellant alleges that the 15-1 Commission drafted the statement of
facts and confession of guilt without the consent and collaboration of the
Accused. This specification of error is not sustained.

Consider the extensive and collaborative interactions between the
accused and the 15-1 Commission via e-mail regarding the contents and
language of the proposed Confession. The 15-1 Commission was appointed by
NP in February 2023. Appellant submitted a written response to the allegations
in March 2023, in which he wrote, “The specifics of both allegations are
true…”. Appellant provided background information regarding his and his
family’s interaction with the person bringing the allegations. In May 2023, the
15-1 Commission drafted a proposed statement of facts and confession of guilt
and provided it to the Accused. Appellant and the Chairman of the 15-1
Commission exchanged a series of e-mails regarding the statement and
confession. On June 21, 2023, Appellant met with the 15-1 Commission to
review the statement, and Appellant objected to parts of the proposed

<a id="ga52-p876"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=878 printed_page=876 -->

statement. The 15-1 Commission agreed to revise the statement and confession
in response to Appellant’s requests. On June 29, 2023, the 15-1 Commission
approved changes to the statement and confession from its June 21 meeting
with Appellant. The 15-1 Commission forwarded the changes to Appellant
and offered to permit him to submit “anything else you would like …”. On
July 7, the 15-1 Commission Chairman sent Appellant a “finalized” statement
and confession. On July 14, Appellant requested that the 15-1 Commission
consider additions to the statement and confession. On July 18, the 15-1
Commission responded, indicating reasons it would not agree to Appellant’s
latest proposed revisions. On July 20, Appellant requested the 15-1
Commission “take into account” a February 13, 2023 letter from Appellant
and statements from two Teaching Elders in NP. The 15-1 Commission agreed
to this request. With these additions for the 15-1 Commission’s consideration,
Appellant e-mailed the 15-1 Commission indicating his intention to sign the
statement and confession. On July 26, Appellant signed the statement of facts
and confession of guilt. On August 2, the 15-1 Commission approved the
statement and confession as signed. On August 8, the 15-1 Commission
approved its censure.

Thus, the Record plainly demonstrates that the 15-1 Commission
offered Appellant numerous opportunities for dialogue and interaction in the
process of preparing the statement of facts and confession, and Appellant
freely participated in drafting the statement and confession. Appellant
suggested some revisions which the 15-1 Commission accepted and
incorporated in the later drafts. The 15-1 Commission rejected some revisions
proffered by Appellant and communicated its reasons for doing so. At the end
of the process, Appellant specifically requested that the 15-1 Commission
consider his February 13 letter and statements from two Teaching Elders. NP
acceded to this request of Appellant. The inclusion of these materials
(submitted by Appellant) with his consent is expressly authorized by _BCO_ 381. At the end of this process, which stretched over nearly six months, Appellant
voluntarily signed the statement and confession. Nothing in the Record
indicates that he was under any compulsion or duress in signing it. Appellant
attempted to rescind his action eight days after he signed. By that point, the
15-1 Commission had reached a decision on censure. Appellant’s reliance on
_Wilbourne_ to urge error in the consideration of material in addition to the
signed statement is misplaced. In _Wilbourne_, the lower court erred by
considering additional material without the consent of the Accused. Here,
Appellant specifically proffered, and the 15-1 Commission agreed to consider

<a id="ga52-p877"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=879 printed_page=877 -->

his additional materials according to _BCO_ 38-1. The Record does not reveal
any error by NP; therefore, this specification of error is not sustained.

**Specification 4**

Appellant alleges that the 15-1 Commission failed to examine the
2020 SC Investigation and therefore failed to assess properly 1) the veracity
of the report, and 2) the work of the Spirit in the life of repentance. This
specification of error is not sustained.

_BCO_ 38-1 does not require the court to delve into past investigations
when conducting a case without process. The court's sole function is to be
convinced of and then approve "a sufficient summary of the facts, the person's
specific confession, and any expression of evidence of repentance." Should the
person who is making the confession of guilt feel that the required statement
is not complete, does not accurately reflect his confession, or does not
accurately reflect his repentance he has full freedom to refuse to sign the
document, and thus to allow the court to proceed to process. But what he
cannot do is demand that the court conduct any sort of independent
investigation, much less one directed by the individual.

Nonetheless, we understand Appellant was very concerned that the
Commission did not examine the 2020 SC investigation. We, therefore, offer
the following observations. Allegations regarding Appellant’s conduct were
brought tot hes c in 2019. The Record indicates that members of SC met with
Appellant in 2019 and 2020 regarding those allegations. A four member “Ad
Hoc Committee” of SC deliberated concerning a July 2020 document that had
been filed with SC regarding Appellant’s conduct. The Ad Hoc Committee
concluded in a September 10, 2020 report that Appellant had “repented
immediately, has greater godly discernment from the experience, continues to
be held accountable for his actions, and has responded with a teachable spirit.”
it recommended to sc that “no further action be taken concerning this report,
except to continue to shepherd and support TE Sears and his family.” The
September 10, 2020 report then reads “MSP: The Shepherding Committee …
accepted the recommendation of the Ad Hoc Committee.”

But, in fact, the Record shows that SC lacked a quorum at the
September 10, 2020 meeting. SC therefore took no action on September 10,
2020. Furthermore, the Record gives no indication that SC subsequently

<a id="ga52-p878"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=880 printed_page=878 -->

notified NP of the document that had been brought against Appellant. Nor is
there indication in the Record that SC informed NP of its deliberations on
September 10, 2020. The 15-1 Commission cannot be found to be in error for
having failed to consider a report 1) that did not carry the recommendation of
SC and, more importantly, 2) that SC never carried to NP. The 15-1
Commission could not deliberate or take action upon a report of which it was
officially unaware.

Furthermore, it is important to note that the Record gives no indication
that Appellant disclosed the document or its contents to the congregation of
which he was pastor at the time, or even to the church’s Session, or to NP. The
document does not appear to have made its way beyond a handful of members
of SC. These facts at the very least call into question the handful of SC
members’ assessment that Appellant had adequately demonstrated repentance
with respect to the matters raised in the document.

**Specification 5**

Appellant alleges that NP erred by not allowing Appellant the right to
withdraw his approved and co-signed BCO 38-1 confession. This specification
of error is not sustained.

Appellant’s contention is that after he agreed to a _BCO_ 38-1 written
Confession and the infliction of a censure by JC, he should have been
permitted to withdraw his Confession. Again, the Appellant cites _Wilbourne_
for the principle that an accused may make such a withdrawal. But in
_Wilbourne_ the issue was whether Wilbourne had the right to withdraw his
confession _**after the SJC had annulled the censure**_ by sustaining his
complaint. There is no principle that permits an accused to unilaterally
withdraw a mutually agreed written Confession after censure has been
announced. To allow Appellant to do so could well change the nature of _BCO_
38-1; it could permit a provision designed to allow the confession o fa person
convinced of his guilt to metamorphose into a plea-bargain process in which
an accused could withdraw his confession because he disagreed with the
censure inflicted.

The Presbytery’s judgment and censure are affirmed.

<a id="ga52-p879"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=881 printed_page=879 -->

**VI. AMENDS**

None.

**VII. RECORDING IN MINUTES**

The SJC instructs the Presbytery to record this Decision, and any
Concurring and Dissenting Opinions, in the minutes of the next stated meeting
following its receipt of the full Decision.

The proposed decision was drafted by TE Fred Greco, TE Guy Waters, and
RE Jack Wilson and approved unanimously by the panel. The SJC reviewed
each part of the proposed decision and approved the final decision by vote of
24-0.

Bankson _Concur_ Eggert _Concur_ Maynard _Concur_
Bise _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Neikirk _Concur_

Bruce _Concur_ Garner _Concur_ Pickering _Concur_
Carrell _Concur_ Greco _Concur_ Sartorius _Concur_

Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall _Concur_
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Concur_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe _Concur_ Lee _Concur_ White _Concur_

Dowling Concur Marcinak _Concur_ Wilson _Concur_

**CONCURRING OPINION**

Case No. 2023-18: _Appeal of TE Sears v. Nashville_

RE Howie Donahoe

November 10, 2024

_BCO_ 38-1 and the procedures for a Case Without Process ("CWOP") have
been improved over the last few years, but this Case demonstrates there might
still be room for improvement—or at least a warning. In this Case it seems the
censure of deposition was unexpected by the Appellant. While _BCO_ 38-1
doesn't require an investigative committee or a commission to inform the

<a id="ga52-p880"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=882 printed_page=880 -->

confessor what censure the committee will recommend or the commission will

impose, it might be fair and wise in many instances.

It's my understanding that in US criminal courts an accused person sometimes
offers to plead or is invited by the prosecutor to plead guilty to a lesser charge
rather than going to trial on a greater charge. But in the PCA, we don't have
statutory minimum and maximum sentences specified for offenses. Nor do we
have defined 1st, 2nd, and 3rd degree offenses. Nevertheless, nothing
precludes a Session or Presbytery (or one of its committees or commissions)
from informing a prospective confessor what _BCO_ censure it will recommend
or impose. And a confessor is free to decline to co-sign the _BCO_ 38-1
document unless he is apprised of the recommended or intended censure.
Granted, that could result in a trial instead, but it could also result in a
satisfactory resolution of the matter outside of a trial.

Ordinarily, if i were advising a minister considering a CWOP, and the
proposed document didn't mention what censure would be recommended or
imposed, I might encourage him to consider not signing unless he was willing
to accept the severest censure. And if the confessor was not willing to sign
without knowing the recommended or intended censure, the committee or
commission would need to discuss whether to (1) proceed to formal judicial
process (a cumbersome and often divisive path), or (2) collaborate with the
confessor or his counsel to get to a point at which he could sign. That might
not sound very ecclesiastical, but it's sensible.

Some might argue that _BCO_ 38-1 only envisions a "real" confession, where a
"real" confession is purported to be one in which the confessor is willing for
the court to impose whatever censure the court deems appropriate. I'm
unpersuaded. There are different kinds of legitimate and genuine confessions,
and not all arise from the same degree of heart repentance. Some confessions
come out of the blue, from a man or woman whose sin will probably not be
discovered. But those are rare and probably will always be—especially from
men whose credentials, public reputation, and continued employment would
be at risk. Many _BCO_ 38-1 cases arise when someone else becomes aware of
another person's sin. And many arise during the course of a _BCO_ 31-2
investigation, when a trial appears t ob ea likely alternative, conducted by men
who've never conducted a trial. So, it's hard to craft a "one size fits all"
procedure, which is why we must rightfully trust Session members and
presbyters to use their wisdom and good judgment.

<a id="ga52-p881"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=883 printed_page=881 -->

Generally speaking, evaluating repentance is not as simple as the _BCO_ might
seem to imply. _BCO_ 30-1 uses the phrase "remains impenitent" but doesn't
suggest a definition. I've read that Spurgeon once said: "Remember that the
man who truly repents is never satisfied with his own repentance. We can no
more repent perfectly than we can live perfectly. However pure our tears, there
will always be some dirt in them; there will always be something to be repented
of even in our best repentance." Most of us have undisclosed sins of thought
which, if they were put in a signed document and distributed to our peers,
would probably result in considerable loss respect for us. Is _our_ repentance
"real" if we don't confess _them_ openly? Why don't we lay ourselves at the
mercy of our courts for our private sins? Put another way, why would a man's
confession in a CWOP only be considered "real" if he doesn't care what
censure is imposed, but we regard _our_ repentance as real despite being content
to keep our private thoughts just between ourselves and God?

Even a truly repentant man can legitimately have reticence about participating
in a CWOP because, as our Standards teach, "all courts may err; and many
have erred." ( _WCF_ 31.4) For example, it's possible in the discussion of a
censure, that a speech might be made that presents an inaccurate interpretation
of the _Westminster Standards_ or the _BCO_, and nobody corrects it, and an
overly harsh censure is imposed. Laying oneself "at the mercy of the court" is
not always a prudent decision, even in a "real" confession. [13]

Using a CWOP, and thereby avoiding formal judicial process, is often the
wisest course—for several reasons. Hopefully, Presbyteries will consider
assigning any CWOP to a commission with authority to fully adjudicate it,
including deciding on any censure, rather than relying on a committee to bring
a recommendation to a meeting of the full court. [14]

Finally, let me offer a word of warning. One of our Presbyteries recently
handled a CWOP in which an experienced investigative committee comprised

13 In its Report to the 51 st gain richmond (2024), the SJC announced decisions in 10
Cases and found that a lower court erred or partially erred in _seven_ of those Cases.
(This did not include any _BCO_ 38-1 cases, or the four Cases deemed out of order or
procedurally remanded.)
14 In 2023, _BCO_ 15-1 and 15-3 were revised and no wallow a commission to handle a

38-1 case to completion.

<a id="ga52-p882"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=884 printed_page=882 -->

of long-serving ministers and elders unanimously recommended indefinite
suspension from office, and that recommendation was in the document the
confessing minister co-signed. But the Presbytery, by a slim margin, declined
to adopt the committee's recommendation and voted to depose instead.
Ministers need to be aware such a thing can happen. Unfortunately, examples
