like that will probably give the next confessing minister pause before
participating in a CWOP, unless he is the second signature on a _commission_
document that specifies the particular censure that will be imposed.

RE Howie Donahoe

<a id="ga52-p883"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=885 printed_page=883 -->

**CASE 2023-20**

**RE JOHN MARTINEZ**

**v.**

**PACIFIC PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON COMPLAINT**

May 24, 2024

**CASE SUMMARY**

by a vote of 5-3, a session adopted a policy that worship services should
close with a benediction and not a doxology. Complainant RE Martinez voted
in favor. The long-time Senior Pastor did not agree with that decision, and two
Session members filed a complaint with the Session, which was denied. Then,
over the course of several meetings, Presbytery alternately sustained, denied,
and sustained complaints related to this Session decision, with the final
Presbytery decision being that the Session erred in its decision.
The SJC concludes that the Session had the authority to make that
decision ( _BCO_ 39-3.4), and that the liturgical decision was not a clear error of
judgment or discretion ( _BCO_ 39-3.3), and therefore the Complaint is sustained,
and Presbytery's action is annulled in whole.

**I. SUMMARY OF THE FACTS**

07/11/22 Stated meeting of the Session of Valley Presbyterian Church
(hereafter "VPC") at which by vote of 5-3, the Session, approved
a motion "to have God's blessings as formal benedictions to
conclude the service and not doxologies."

08/09/22 Session members RE Riedinger and Shaw and filed a timely _BCO_
43-1 Complaint with the Session regarding that decision. [.15]

09/08/22 Session denied the Reidinger/Shaw Complaint.

10/05/22 REs Reidinger and Shaw took that Complaint to Presbytery.

15 Sr. Pastor TE Svendsen "joined" that Complaint, but because a pastor is not
a member of the church, a pastor cannot file abc o 43-1 complaint with the session.
However, as a voting member of the session, a pastor can ordinarily make a motion
to rescind or amend something previously adopted.

<a id="ga52-p884"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=886 printed_page=884 -->

01/28/23 Pacific Presbytery stated meeting in Santa Barbara, at which it
sustained the Shaw/Riedinger Complaint and annulled the
decision of the VPC Session. REs Martinez and Jesse Cook were

VPC commissioners to this meeting.

03/12/23 REs Martinez and Cook filed a _BCO_ 43-1 Complaint with
Presbytery against its 1/28/23 action in Santa Barbara.

05/02/23 Presbytery stated meeting in Las Vegas, at which it sustained the
Martinez/Cook Complaint, thus rescinding/reversing its prior
decision of 01/28/23. The original Session decision was
reinstated.

07/01/23 TE Svendsen, VPC Sr. Pastor, filed a timely _BCO_ 43-1 Complaint
with Presbytery against its 5/02/23 action that sustained
Martinez/Cook Complaint in Las Vegas.

10/03/23 Presbytery stated meeting in Calabasas, CA (45 minutes NW of
Los Angeles). Presbytery sustained the Svendsen Complaint, thus
returning to, and reaffirming, its decision eight months prior on
01/28/23 in Santa Barbara wherein it annulled the Session
decision. [16] No rationale was adopted.

10/30/23 RE Martinez, having been a VPC commissioner to the 10/03
Presbytery meeting in Calabasas, carried his Complaint to the SJC
via _BCO_ 43-3. RE Cook was not a VPC commissioner to the 10/03

Presbytery meeting.

11/30/23 The Presbytery Clerk filed the 51-page Record of the Case.

02/09/24 The Case was assigned to the same Panel that previously heard
2023-10 _Complaint of REs Martinez and Cook v. Pacific,_ which
was eventually decided by the SJC on 3/8/24. The Panel included
TE Paul Bankson (chairman), RE Dan Carrell and RE Howie

16 VPC REs Shaw, Riedinger, and Mateko also signed the Svendsen Complaint,
but because they were not VPC commissioners to the May Presbytery meeting, they
did not have standing to file a _BCO_ 43-1 Complaint.

<a id="ga52-p885"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=887 printed_page=885 -->

Donahoe, with alternates TE Paul Kooistra and RE John
Pickering.

02/15/24 Panel held its Convening Meeting.

02/29/24 The Record of the Case was revised, after eight pages were added
at the Complainant's request, to which the Respondent agreed.

03/07/24 The Panel met and judged the Record complete and the Case
judicially in order. Thereafter, Parties filed Briefs (Complainant
on 3/28, Respondent on 4/10).

04/22/24 Panel held the Hearing. Complainant RE Martinez was assisted by
fellow VPC RE Jesse Cook. Presbytery was represented by TE
Jonathan Key and RE Bruce Brady (both at Providence Reformed
Church of Bakersfield).

04/26/24 Panel adopted a proposed decision, filed it with SJC, and emailed
it to the Parties.

**II. STATEMENT OF THE ISSUES**

1. as a matter o flaw ( _BCO_ 39-3.4) did the Session have constitutional

authority to instruct the minister to close worship with benedictions
instead of doxologies?

2. In exercising that authority, was there clear error in the Session's

discretion and judgment in deciding to close worship in that manner?
( _BCO_ 39.3.3)

**III. JUDGMENT**

1. Yes

2. No. Therefore, the Complaint is sustained. Presbytery's October 3,

2023 action in sustaining the Svendsen Complaint is annulled. This
returns the matter to where it stood after Presbytery sustained the

<a id="ga52-p886"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=888 printed_page=886 -->

Martinez Complaint on May 2, 2023, meaning the Session's July 11,
2022 decision regarding benedictions and doxologies has not been
annulled.

**IV. REASONING AND OPINION**

The Presbytery erred when it sustained the Svendsen Complaint on October 3,
2023 at its stated meeting in Calabasas, CA. We note that Presbytery did not
adopt a statement of the issue or adopt any reasoning for why it sustained the
Svendsen Complaint and annulled the Session decision. While _BCO_ 43 does
not require such, it would have been helpful to both the congregation and
Session of Valley Presbyterian Church to know the reasons why Presbytery
was annulling an action of the Session.

In this Complaint, RE Martinez identified what amounted to two alleged
errors. One questioned the timeliness of the filing of the Svendsen Complaint
on July 1, 2023, but this was sufficiently addressed and explained by
Presbytery. It was timely because it was a _BCO_ 43-1 filing (60 days to the
original court) and not a _BCO_ 43-3 filing (30 days to the higher court). The
main error that was alleged is as follows.

"This act [of Presbytery sustaining the Svendsen Complaint

and annulling the Session decision] infringes upon the
jurisdiction of VPC Session's duties and charge in
maintaining the spiritual governing of the church ( _BCO_ 125.e; 11-2; 3-2; 4-3; 39-3)." ... "The Session is in fact charged
with overseeing the whole service, with all of its elements.
That is from the Call to the Benediction ( _BCO_ 12.5.e)."

In his Brief, Presbytery's Representative framed the issues as follows:

1. "The complainant [RE Martinez] claims that [Pacific

Presbytery] infringed upon the rights and duties of VPC
Session in regard to its government of public worship."

[Brief p. 2, items (c) and (d)]

<a id="ga52-p887"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=889 printed_page=887 -->

2. "The complainant cites _BCO_ 4-3 and 12-5.e to defend

[VPC Session's] authority to restrict the closing of
worship at VPC only with (sic) formal benedictions."

In this SJC Decision, the authority issue is addressed in Issue 1. But because
we find that the Session had the authority to render its decision, a second issue
needs to be addressed regarding the exercise of judgment and discretion. This
Case involves both a matter of law (i.e., differing constitutional
interpretations) and a matter of discretion and judgment.

Part of the argumentation in the first Complaint against the Session's
benediction decision, sustained by Presbytery at its January 2023 meeting in
Santa Barbara, included quotes from _BCO_ 50, 51, and 52 regarding the
minister's discretion and responsibilities in worship. However, those chapters
are not part of our Constitution. The second Complaint against the same
Session decision, which was sustained by Presbytery at its October 2023
meeting in Calabasas, argued primarily that the Session's decision bound the
conscience of the minister. The issue of conscience is addressed in the

Reasoning below on Issue 2.

Regarding Issue 1 and Session authority, the Parties disagreed primarily on the
interpretation of _BCO_ 12-5.e and on how extensive a Session's authority is
regarding worship services. Complainant contends a Session has more
authority than what Presbytery contends. The disputed section is below, with
enumeration added for clarity.

_BCO_ 12-5. The church Session is charged with maintaining the spiritual
government of the church, for which purpose it has power:

e. To exercise, in accordance with the Directory for Worship,

1. authority over the time and place of the preaching of the
Word

2. and the administration of the Sacraments,
3. over all other religious services,
4. over the music in the services, and
5. over the uses to which the church building and associated
properties may be put;
6. to take the oversight of the singing in the public worship of
God;

<a id="ga52-p888"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=890 printed_page=888 -->

7. to ensure that the Word of God is preached only by such men
as are sufficiently qualified ( _BCO_ 4-4, 53-2, 1 Timothy 2:1112);
8. to assemble the people for worship when there is no minister;
9. _to determine the best measures for promoting the spiritual_
_interests of the church and congregation;_ (emphasis added)

Considering _BCO_ 12.5.e, and particularly the final clause, the SJC finds that
the VPC Session had the constitutional authority to make the decision it made,
i.e., to have worship services closed with benedictions and not with
doxologies.

To cite a constitutional example, _BCO_ 58-1 explicitly grants a Session the
authority to determine _how often_ a congregation observes the Lord's Supper.
If a Session has authority over that extremely significant matter, then it's
reasonable to conclude a Session has authority over whether worship will close
with a benediction. [17]

Issue 2 questions whether this Session's constitutionally permitted exercise of
discretion and judgment was a clear error of discretion and judgment. ( _BCO_
39.3.3) We note first, regarding worship, both a Session and Presbytery have
authority and duties under _BCO_ 11-2.

_BCO_ 11-2. Secondly, they [church courts] have power to
establish rules for the government, discipline, _worship_, and
extension of the Church, which must be agreeable to the
doctrines relating thereto contained in the Scriptures, the
circumstantial details only of these matters being left to the
Christian prudence and wisdom of Church officers and courts.

There are probably many churches that exclusively use benedictions to close
worship, so it would seem difficult to demonstrate this injures the Church. In
the 1645 _Westminster Directory for the Public Worship of God_, in the section
titled "Of Prayer After Sermon," the final paragraph says:

17 _BCO_ 58-1. "The Communion, or Supper of the Lord, is to be observed
frequently; the stated times to be determined by the Session of each congregation,
as it may judge most for edification."

<a id="ga52-p889"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=891 printed_page=889 -->

The prayer ended, let a psalm be sung, if with convenience it may
be done. After which (unless some other ordinance of Christ, that
concerns the congregation at that time, be to follow) let the
minister dismiss the congregation _with a solemn blessing_

[emphasis added].

In the 1645 Directory section titled, "Sanctification of the Lord's Day,"
we read:

That all the people meet so timely for public worship, that the
whole congregation may be present at the beginning, and with
one heart solemnly join together in all parts of the public worship,
and not depart until _after the blessing_ [emphasis added].

The 1879 PCUS _BCO_ contained the following (in what is now _BCO_
8-5):

when a minister is called to labor as a Pastor, it belongs to his
office to pray for and with his flock, as the mouth of the people
unto God; to feed the flock by reading, expounding, and
preaching the Word; to direct the congregation in singing the
praises of God; to administer the sacraments; _to bless the_
_people from God; ..._ [emphasis added].

In his 1898 Exposition on this section of the 1879 PCUS Book, F.P. Ramsay

wrote:

It belongs to the Pastor _to bless the people from God_ ;
wherefore the benediction is by him pronounced as an official
declaration of the divine mind [emphasis added].

In light of these considerations, we cannot say that the Session's decision in
this case that a benediction rather than a doxology is to be used at thee nd of
the worship service was a clear error of liturgical judgment under the standard
of _BCO_ 39-3.3, despite the fact that its longtime Senior Pastor disagreed. The
Session has the power to establish rules for worship, and with that power came
the discretion to set the policy about benedictions, a practice supported by the
historic authorities cited above. Decisions about the rules for worship remain
within the discretionary authority of a Session, even if Presbytery or other

<a id="ga52-p890"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=892 printed_page=890 -->

Sessions or its own minister might have a different opinion, as long as such
decisions are not "clear error" (BCO 39-3.3), do not violate the Constitution
( _BCO_ 39-3.4), and are not contrary to the Scriptures.

Regarding the assertion about conscience made in TE Svendsen's Complaint,
sustained by Presbytery in May 2023, we are not persuaded the VPC Session
preference for benedictions over doxologies would reasonably result in a
violation of a minister's conscience. In his Complaint sustained at the May
2023 meeting, TE Svendsen contended that when Presbytery allowed the VPC
Session decision to stand, the Presbytery had "bound the conscience of a
minister by requiring him to follow his session's motion." He contended, "If it
is not scripturally required, then it is a commandment of men o vera matter of
worship and cannot be anything less that the binding of the conscience."
Similar arguments were made by Presbytery in its Brief in this present Case. [18]

However, we are not persuaded the Session's July 2022 decision involved a
matter like what is referenced in _Westminster Confession of Faith_ 20.2:

"God alone is Lord of the conscience, and hath left it free

from the doctrines and commandments of men, which are,
in anything, contrary to his Word; or beside it, if matters of
faith, or worship. So that, to believe such doctrines, or to
obey such commands, out of conscience, is to betray true
liberty of conscience: and the requiring of an implicit faith,
and an absolute and blind obedience, is to destroy liberty of
conscience, and reason also." [19]

18 _BCO_ Preliminary Principle 1: "God alone is Lord of the conscience and has
left it free from any doctrines or commandments of men (a) which are in any respect
contrary to the Word of God, or (b) which, in regard to matters of faith and worship,
are not governed by the Word of God."
_BCO_ 11-2: First, [church courts] can make no laws binding the conscience; but
may frame symbols of faith, bear testimony against error in doctrine and immorality
in practice, within or without the Church, and decide cases of conscience. Secondly,
they have power to establish rules for the government, discipline, worship, and
extension of the Church, which must be agreeable to the doctrines relating thereto
contained in the Scriptures, the circumstantial details only of these matters being left
to the Christian prudence and wisdom of Church officers and courts.
19 See also _WCF_ 17.3, 18.1, 18.4, 23.4 & _Larger Catechism_ questions 28, 36,
55, 80, 83, 89, 96, 105, 151 & 175.

<a id="ga52-p891"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=893 printed_page=891 -->

While the minister believed he should have more freedom in how he closed

worship services, it is not reasonable to see how the Session's restriction would
have caused the minister to sin. And if the minister's conscience deemed it as

sin, he was free to ask (and should ask) an eligible elder to issue the
benediction instead.

Finally, an ongoing liturgical dispute between a long-time pastor and a
majority of elders on a Session might be something reasonably expected to
harm the peace of the Church, or something unsuited to promote the welfare
of the Church. It might also be reasonable to expect this given there have been
three complaints to Presbytery from the Pastor and members of the VPC
Session on this matter, and now this present one to the SJC. One cannot read
the Record of this Case without wondering how the ordination promise of
"subjection to your brethren in the Lord" was applied. ( _BCO_ 21-5, vow 4 and
_BCO_ 24-6, vow 5) However, the prior Martinez/Cook Complaint contended
this matter had not disrupted the collegiality of the Session, and that contention
was not disputed in the adjudication of that Complaint or in this present one.
Regardless, the effect of this disagreement on the Session, or on the Pastor, or
on Valley Presbyterian Church was not an issue raised in the Svendsen
Complaint before Presbytery in October 2023, or in this present one.

_______________

The Panel's proposed decision was initially drafted by RE Donahoe, and after
amendments, the Panel adopted it by vote of 3-0. The SJC reviewed each part
of the proposed decision and approved the final version of the Decision by
vote of 19-1, with 4 absent.

Bankson _Concur_ S. Duncan _Concur_ Maynard Absent
Bise _Concur_ Eggert _Concur_ Neikirk _Concur_
Carrell _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Pickering Absent
Coffin _Dissent_ Garner Absent Sartorius _Concur_

Dodson _Concur_ Greco _Concur_ Ross _Concur_

Donahoe _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Waters _Concur_

Dowling _Concur_ Lee _Concur_ White _Concur_
M. Duncan _Concur_ Lucas Absent Wilson _Concur_

<a id="ga52-p892"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=894 printed_page=892 -->

**CONCURRING OPINION**

Case 2023-20: _RE Martinez v. Pacific_
RE Howie Donahoe, joined by RE Steve Dowling

June 9, 2024

I concurred but believe additional attention should be given to defining the
issues in this Case, to avoid being distracted by other issues.

_BCO_ 11-2 provides:

[Church courts] have power to establish rules for the
government, discipline, worship, and extension of the
Church, which must be agreeable to the doctrines relating
thereto contained in the Scriptures, the _circumstantial details_
only of these matters being left to the Christian prudence and
wisdom of Church officers and courts. (emphasis added)

Both parties made this more complicated than necessary. In the end, the matter
was simple.

A worship service must end.
- Someone must decide how it ends.

So, who ultimately decides? Presumably, absent some biblical mandate, it's
either the minister or the Session. In _Martinez_, the SJC held the _BCO_ grants
such authority to a Session. (Issue 1; _BCO_ 39-3.4) And a Session's exercise of
its authority prevails in that matter of discretion unless it is judged to be a clear
error of judgment. (Issue 2; _BCO_ 39-3.3)

And it matters little whether this Complainant or his Session believed the
Scriptures _require_ a benediction to be pronounced at the close of worship. For
example, i could vote in favor o fa motion even though i disagree with the
reasons given by the maker. The action proposed could still make sense. While
a closing benediction is _not_ a Scriptural requirement, that Session's authority
to choose that option still prevails, unless it's demonstrated to violate Scripture
or is a clearer r or of judgment—neither of which was demonstrable. _BCO_ 587 ends this way: "[after the Lord's Supper] ... Now let a psalm or hymn be sung,
and the congregation dismissed, with the following or some other Gospel
_benediction_ ." (emphasis added)

<a id="ga52-p893"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=895 printed_page=893 -->

There are numerous ways a worship service could "end"—some wise; some
not so. A Session might decide that every worship service will close with:

1. whatever the minister chooses

2. whatever benediction the minister chooses

3. the minister's reading of Numbers 6:24-26
4. the minister's choice of one of 12 Scripture passages in a Session
supplied list
5. an offering received for the Deacon's Fund
6. singing the final stanza of Trinity 385—God Be with You Till We

Meet Again
7. singing all the stanzas of Trinity 385, twice
8. 60 seconds of silent prayer
9. 60 seconds of unison shouting of individual prayers
10. a hymn chosen by the minister
11. a hymn chosen by the music director
12. a hymn chosen by the tallest 3rd grader
13. open-mic prayer requests with no time limit
14. the minister's blessing of one pet each week
15. singing the National Anthem
16. a conga line dance around the edge of the sanctuary
17. popping champagne bottles like a winning sports team in the locker

room

I favor the first option (whatever the minister chooses). And most of us would
regard some of the above choices to be clear and reversible errors of judgment,
and some perhaps even matters involving a minister's conscience.

In _Martinez_, if Presbytery had demonstrated that the regulative principle
requires doxologies at the close of worship, or that Scripture disallows
benedictions, or that the particular benedictions selected by this Session were
clear errors of judgment, the Complaint would have failed. But that was not
demonstrable. The issue was not the application or interpretation of the
regulative principle, nor a legitimate matter of conscience. The issue was
whether the _BCO_ gives final authority to the minister or the Session in matters
of discretion related to the circumstances of a worship service.

<a id="ga52-p894"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=896 printed_page=894 -->

Who decides what English translation of the Bible is used in worship? The
Session does. Who decides whether any, or which, hymnal is used in worship?
Same answer. Who decides whether an offering is taken physically or a QR
code is just included in the folder? Same answer (presumably giving great
deference to the minister's opinion in each of those decisions). And a Session
does not need to defend its decision. The burden is on others to demonstrate

the decision: (1) violates the Westminster Standards, (2) is unbiblical, (3) is
not something under session authority, or (4) is a clearer r or of judgment.
While I personally favor the ESV and the red Trinity, it would _not_ be a clear
error of judgment to use the NASB and the "Sing to the Lord" hymnal
published by the Reformed Churches of New Zealand. In the end, the Session
renders the final decision.

A closing benediction is simply reading/pronouncing a _particular kind_ of
Scripture, and in that sense it is, generally, one of the several elements of
worship listed in _WCF_ 25-5. And the decision on whether a worship service
ends with that particular kind of scripture is a judgment made ina matter of
discretion, over which the Session has authority.

/s/ RE Howie Donahoe

**CONCURRING OPINION**

Case 2023-20 _RE_ _Martinez v. Pacific Presbytery_

RE Jack Wilson, joined by RE John Bise

June 10, 2024

I concur in the judgment of the Commission. I write separately to
outline some of the positions and arguments that were considered in the course
of the Commission’s proceedings ( _OMSJC_ 18.12(b)(2)) and my understanding
of the limited scope of this decision.

This case arises from a Session’s disagreement about structuring
sanctioned Biblical elements of worship in a church’s regular worship
services. The primary issue presented concerns which party (the majority of
the Session or a minority of the Session, including the Pastor) controls the
inclusion and sequencing of legitimate elements of a worship service. _BCO_
12-5 addresses the question presented in this case with clarity as it provides in
relevant part:

<a id="ga52-p895"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=897 printed_page=895 -->

12-5. The church Session is charged with maintaining the spiritual
government of the church, for which purpose it has power:

…e. _**To exercise, in accordance with the Directory for Worship,**_
_**authority**_ over the time and place of the preaching of the Word and
the administration of the Sacraments, _**over all other religious services,**_
_**over the music in the services**_, and over the uses to which the church
building and associated properties may be put; to take the oversight of
the singing in the public worship of God; to ensure that the Word of
God is preached only by such men as are sufficiently qualified ( _BCO_
4-4, 53-2, 1 Timothy 2:11-12); to assemble the people for worship
when there is no minister; _**to determine the best measures for**_
_**promoting the spiritual interests of the church and congregation**_ ; …
(emphasis supplied).

Here the Constitution assigns the Session the responsibility to exercise
authority over worship services. Such authority certainly includes decision
making related to the order, sequence and inclusion of proper elements,
consistent with the Directory for Worship.

In this case, the Session did not request performance of any action
contrary to Scripture, the Constitution, or the Directory for Worship. The
Session did not ask the Pastor to include any extra-Biblical element or activity
in the worship service, and questions related to such elements were not
presented or decided here. The Session did not attempt to direct the Pastor
what or how to preach. The decision does not reach questions related to
whether benedictions are “required” in a worship service. The outcome did not
turn on, approve, or disapprove any party’s proffered definition of the elements
at issue. in the face o fa narrow, but reasonable difference of opinion and
emphasis, the question presented and decided was which party determines
which Biblical elements appear in a worship service and in which order. The
Commission’s decision answers that question according to _BCO_ 12-5(e).

<a id="ga52-p896"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=898 printed_page=896 -->

**DISSENTING OPINION**

Case 2023-20 _RE John Martinez v. Pacific Presbytery_

TE David F. Coffin, Jr.

June 10, 2024

I dissent from the Decision of the Standing Judicial Commission (SJC) in this
case. I believe that the SJC has failed to rule according to the most basic
principles of Biblical and Presbyterian polity.

First, consider the authorities I will assert in favor of my understanding of our
wholesome tradition, included in an Appendix to this dissent. I trust that the
reader will reflect upon _these principles_, as they are the _foundation_ of all that
I _will argue_ in favor of the view I represent in this dissent. i ti sin the context
of these authorities that I ask, what is the issue before us? it is claimed in the
Commission’s decision that the _BCO_ recognizes the Session’s authority in
these matters, and that settles the question. This conclusion is founded upon
_BCO_ 12-5.e.

The church Session is charged with maintaining the spiritual
government of the church, for which purpose it has
power. . . .To exercise, in accordance with the Directory for
Worship, [20] _authority over the time and place of the preaching_
_of the Word and the administration of the Sacraments_ . . . _to_
_determine the best measures for promoting the spiritual_
_interests of the church and congregation_ . . . . [emphasis added]

However, before one can ask, what are the powers of the Session with respect
to public worship, it must be determined what are the powers of the church, as
such, in the matter. a session is but a court of the church that i soft he same
nature, constituted of the same elements, possessed inherently of the same
kinds of rights and powers, as any other court of the church, differing only as
the Constitution may provide. But what of the church?

With respect to this broader question, there are two categories before the
government of the church with respect to the governing of the worship service:
things necessary to the substance of the life of the church as revealed in the

20 From the outset please notice the problem here: _The PCA does not have a_
_Constitutional_ Directory of Worship _, but it does have a Constitutional provision that_
_requires the Session to act according to the Directory of Worship!_

<a id="ga52-p897"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=899 printed_page=897 -->

Scriptures, and things circumstantial. In things necessary to life, they must be
derived only from express statements from the Word of God, or by good and
necessary consequence deduced from Scripture. In things circumstantial, in
worship and government—in matters common to human actions and
societies—they are to be ordered by the light of nature, and Christian prudence,
according to the general rules of the Word.

That is to say, the terms “necessary” and “circumstantial” as they have been
historically employed in questions about worship are as follows. Something
_necessary_ is something required by Scripture. Something _circumstantial_ is
something not addressed by Scripture, but _is necessary because of the_
_circumstances_ .

Concerning things necessary, the government of the church has no discretion,
it cannot add, nor take away. Concerning things circumstantial, the church has
discretion and is called to act with prudence. And, concerning circumstantials,
the Lord of the conscience has left it free from the doctrines and

commandments of men.

Thus, a simple question: is the matter o fa benediction ina worship service a
thing necessary, or a thing circumstantial? i fit is a circumstantial, it is liable
to the government’s discretion, and also, it cannot be imposed as a
conscientious obligation (certainly no ton the grounds of avow to be in
submission to the brethren, as that vow can only apply to one’s prudential
judgments, not one’s conscientious convictions.). i fit is a necessity, it must be
shown to be so from Scripture (by express or implied warrant), and it is amoral
obligation _for all_ .

In this case, it could not matter more what arguments the Session believed
were in favor of its decision. The arguments employed essentially betray the
ground upon which the action is justified. If the argument is that the Scripture
requires the action, it is no longer a circumstantial, it is a scriptural necessity.
If the argument is that merely the circumstances make necessary the action,
then it is circumstantial.

In this case, the burden of proof is on the proposer, a fundamental principle of
logic. The Session had proposed a rule; the burden was on the Session to
vindicate its action as either a scriptural necessity ora circumstantial
necessity. The Session mixed the two, and succeeded in neither.

<a id="ga52-p898"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=900 printed_page=898 -->

The historic Reformed doctrine is not that the Session must deduce from

Scripture the way to end a service. If you could deduce that from Scripture it
is a thing necessary according Confession of Faith 1.6. It is things that are
neither expressly set down, nor are implied by Scripture, that constitute
circumstancials.

Further, if the close of the worship service is a part of the ministry of the Word,
the minister decides as to his service. If the close has only to do with the
circumstance that there must be some sort of closing, the Session may decide,
but it may not bind the minister in the matter.

The General Assembly, in its responsibility to oversee the Constitution of the
church, and itself having inherently all the powers of any counsel of Elders,
has never proposed, and the Directory for Worship does not include,
benedictions as a Scriptural element of worship.

If this true for the church, as such, it must be true for any particular expression
of the church, such as the Session. Furthermore, with respect to the Session
and the Presbytery, their judgments in each case, according to its kind, are
subject to the review and correction by the court next higher. That is to say,
the judgment as to whether a matter is necessary or circumstantial, as well as
the judgment of the matter materially within whatever framework is adopted,
is properly subject to review and correction.

In this case, complainants from the Session, from their argumentation, clearly
do not believe the benediction is a circumstantial, but in their Complaint, and
according to the Commission’s decision, they would have the Presbytery, and
the Assembly, treat it as such. On the other hand, if they believe, as they must,
according to their argumentation, that the benediction is a thing necessary, they
have clearly gone beyond the Directory for Worship, and the Scripture, and
furthermore, have failed to meet the burden of proof. According to
Complainants, from the point of view of the _BCO_, either the listing of elements
of worship i sin error, or the benediction must be, at best, a circumstantial. To
_require_ an element beyond this, as necessary, denies this stance, and illicitly
adds to the Directory, and thus is forbidden. Recall _BCO_ 12-5.e., “To exercise,
in accordance with the Directory for Worship. . . .”

<a id="ga52-p899"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=901 printed_page=899 -->

With respect to this point, the Respondent’s arguments set forth in their Brief
are, in my judgment, dispositive. [21] Here are displayed the arguments that
should have informed the Commission’s decision.

**Regarding the allegation that PP infringed upon the rights and**
**duties of VPC Session:**

As we see it, the question hinges upon three issues: 1) is REs Martinez
and Cook’s definition of benediction correct? 2) i sit scriptural or
Constitutional to require that worship must be concluded with a
“formal benediction” as defined by REs Martinez and Cook? and 3)
Does the ruling of VPC session go beyond the authority granted a
session by the _BCO_, potentially endangering the freedom of a TE’s
conscience?

Firstly, in their March 23 complaint to PP, REs Martinez and Cook
defined a benediction as “…not prayers, or praises to God, but
pronouncements of God's blessings upon his covenant people.” [22]
They go on to distinguish benediction especially from doxologies
based on the “movement” of the two. “[A] key difference between a
doxology and a benediction is that doxologies are praises directed to
God from God's people and benedictions are the blessing of God
directed to God's people from God himself,” [23] eventually concluding
that “doxologies are not benedictions.” [24] However, is this rigid
definition of benediction warranted?

The English word “benediction” is derived from the Latin language.
In Latin, we find the roots _bene,_ which means “well, rightly, kindly,
prosperously,” [25] and _dictio,_ “a setting forth in words, stating,
declaring.” [26] Together, _benedictus_ means “a good word,” without any
apparent regard to whom the word is spoken to or by.

21 “Preliminary Brief on behalf of Respondents TE Jonathan Key and RE Bruce
Brady,” 4/10/24.
22 ROC p.34, lines 30-31
23 ROC p. 35, lines 35-36
24 ROC p. 35, line 44
25 Chambers-Murray Latin-English Dictionary, s.v. “bene.”
26 Chambers-Murray, s.v. “diction.”

<a id="ga52-p900"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=902 printed_page=900 -->

When applied to the Bible, _benedictus_ and the cognate _benedixit_
appear in the Latin Vulgate in reference to a blessing spoken by one
party to another. Moreover, the subject and recipient of the blessing
may be “God to man” or “man to God,” and the same words would be
used.

Numbers 4:22-24 (ESV): The LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
“Speak to Aaron and his sons, saying, Thus you shall **bless**
the people of Israel: you shall say to them, The LORD **bless**
you and keep you…”. . . .

Daniel 2:19-20 (ESV) Then the mystery was revealed to
Daniel in a vision of the night. Then Daniel **blessed** the God
of heaven. Daniel answered and said: “ **Blessed** be the name

of God forever and ever, to whom belong wisdom and
might.”. . . .

Ephesians 1:3 (ESV) **Blessed** be the God and Father of our
Lord Jesus Christ, who has **blessed** us in Christ with every
spiritual **blessing** in the heavenly places…”. . . .

When we consider the place of a benediction in the liturgy of the
church, we can turn to WCF 25.5, which lists those things the divines
considered “parts of the ordinary religious worship of God.”
Benediction and doxology are not mentioned or defined among them,
neither is anything mandated on how to close worship. This implies
that the divines did not think it necessary to specify such a thing.

In our _BCO_, at no point is the term “benediction” defined or its
function in our liturgy explained. In several places, such as 58-7, the
word is used, but never is it explained how exactly it is defined,
especially in distinction from a doxology.

Taken together, the etymological, constitutional, and historical
evidence that we found has not led us to a definition of benediction

that explicitly or even implicitly excludes any consideration of
doxology from the definition of benediction.

<a id="ga52-p901"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=903 printed_page=901 -->

Secondly, REs Martinez and Cook argue that “It is biblical and
Constitutional to conclude public worship with a benediction, where a
benediction is defined as a blessing from God, and not a doxology.” [27]
We do not deny that to quote a passage of divine blessing in closing
public worship is indeed a help and encouragement to the saints.
However, we do disagree with the idea that to close worship with a
doxological passage is somehow contrary to Scripture or perhaps “less
biblical” than to use a word of formal benediction instead.

Nowhere in Scripture is it written that worship must be concluded with
a word of formal benediction, much less are doxologies forbidden in
the same portion of worship. Therefore, then, the burden of proof
would seem to fall upon the complainants to prove that doxologies, if
not unbiblical, are at least a “less biblical” end to worship than formal
benedictions. We do not think they have done so nor are they able to.

One of the passages cited by the complainants as a benediction [28] is
Heb. 13:20-21:

Now may the God of peace who brought again from the dead
our Lord Jesus, the great shepherd of the sheep, by the blood
of the eternal covenant, equip you with everything good that
you may do his will, working in us that which is pleasing in
his sight, through Jesus Christ, **to whom be glory forever and**
**ever** . Amen.

Note that the bolded section above is plainly an ascription of glory to
Jesus Christ by the author of Hebrews. To use the terminology of the
complainants, this is a movement of praise to Jesus, the God-man,
from the lips of God’s people. Far from separating blessing and praise
from one another, it seems to us that the Holy Spirit sovereignly led
the author of Hebrews to unite these concepts together so that blessing
and praise, benediction and doxology, might be read together in the
same breath.

27 ROC p. 34, lines 28-29
28 ROC p. 35 line 41

<a id="ga52-p902"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=904 printed_page=902 -->

Citing an example of a doxology that ought not be used for the closing
of public worship, the complainants note that Jude 24-25 is considered
by most English translators of the Bible to be a doxology. [29] However,
we believe that Jude 24-25 is very appropriate for the encouragement
and blessing of God’s people.

Jude’s epistle was written to people who had been secretly invaded by
false Christians causing trouble among the genuine believers. It is
notable then that Jude closes his epistle by announcing that God “is
able to keep [them] from stumbling and to present [them] blameless
before the presence of his glory with great joy…” A greater blessing
or word of encouragement than this can scarcely be imagined.
Whether the direction of this word is primarily “God-ward” or “manward,” these verses seem as though they would be a welcome
affirmation of Christ’s benefits in the ears of believers going out into
a world that challenges their faith.

Additionally, under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit, several epistles
end on notes of praise rather than explicit blessing, such as Jude 2425, 1 Peter 5:11, and 2 Peter 3:18. As seen above, Heb. 13:20-21
features both blessing and praise. If the Holy Spirit was pleased to
have multiple epistles end on notes of praise to our God in addition to
the multitude of benedictory endings, it seems perfectly Biblical to
close public worship in this way too.

REs Martinez and Cook have argued that VPC Session believes that
“Since God gets the first word when he formally summons his
covenant people into his presence by his call to worship, God should
also give, through the office of the minister, the last word in the form
o fa benediction as a formal blessing of god upon his people.” [30]
However, we note 2 Timothy 3:16 says that “All Scripture is breathed
out by God.” If a passage of Scripture is being read in worship, surely
God is speaking to His people.

Regarding the Constitution of the PCA, as noted above, the
Westminster Standards mention neither benediction or doxology as

29 ROC p. 35 lines 45-47
30 ROC p. 39 lines 181-184

<a id="ga52-p903"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=905 printed_page=903 -->

elements of worship. WCF 21:5 commends “The reading of the
Scriptures with godly fear,” but it does not instruct which passages of
Scripture are to be read at which points of the liturgy. It would seem,
then, that our Standards allow freedom in the selection of Scripture
that is read in worship.

Likewise, in _BCO_ 4-4, we have the ordinances of worship listed for

us:

. . . prayer; singing praises; reading, expounding and
preaching the Word of God; administering the Sacraments of
Baptism and the Lord's Supper; public solemn fasting and
thanksgiving; catechizing; making offerings for the relief of
the poor and for other pious uses; and exercising discipline;
the taking of solemn vows, and the ordination to sacred office.

But as with the Westminster standards, blessing/benediction is not one
of those ordinances. And again, nowhere in the _BCO_ is “benediction”
ever defined. That such relatively little attention is paid to the
benediction in liturgy suggests that it is not constitutionally bound by
such a rigid definition as what the complainants insist must be the case

_BCO_ 58-7 does speak to the use of a benediction in worship,
specifically in the context of a day when the Lord’s Supper is being
celebrated. After the sacrament has been observed, 58-7 instructs:

Now let a psalm or hymn be sung, and the congregation
dismissed, with the following or some other Gospel
benediction:

**Now the God of peace, that brought again from the dead**
**our Lord Jesus, that great Shepherd of the sheep, through**
**the blood of the everlasting covenant, make you perfect in**
**every good work to do His will, working in you that which**
**is well pleasing in His sight, through Jesus Christ; to**
**whom be glory for ever and ever. Amen.** (Bolded original)

This passage, Hebrews 13:20-21, is the only one explicitly
recommended for use in public worship by the _BCO_ . However, “some

<a id="ga52-p904"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=906 printed_page=904 -->

other” implies that other passages may be legitimately described as
“gospel benedictions” and be substituted for Heb.13:20-21. But at no
point here or elsewhere does the _BCO_ present a formal list of
appropriate substitutes, apparently leaving the choice of passage to the
discretion of the minister who will pronounce the benediction.

We also think it important that, of all the passages traditionally
associated with liturgical benediction, it was Heb. 13:20-21 that was
chosen as the model Gospel benediction in the _BCO_ . As noted above,
this passage features elements of both blessing and praise, benediction
and doxology. Despite the clear “God-ward” language of praise in the
last clause of Heb. 13:21, the _BCO_ thinks this a perfectly
commendable way to close public worship. How then can it be
unconstitutional to close worship with a word of praise?

In sum, we do not think that REs Martinez and Cook have proven that
a “formal benediction” as they defined it is required either by Scripture
or by our Constitution to close public worship. We agree with the
complainants that it is indeed biblical and constitutional to end public
worship with a benediction. However, to say that a doxology is less
biblical or Constitutional goes beyond the evidence we find in either
the Bible or our Constitution.

Thirdly, the complainants argue that, according to _BCO_ 4-3, the
session o fa particular congregation acts as a joint power and, in accord
with _BCO_ 12-5e, the session has the power “to determine the best
measures for promoting the spiritual interests of the church and
congregation.” Hence, they argue that broad discretionary power is
granted to VPC session to set up their liturgy in the way that they
believe is best for the members of Christ’s flock whom they shepherd.
We do not disagree with the goals of the complainants in principle,
nor with the ideas they cite from the _BCO_ . However, it is our opinion
that VPC Session inadvertently grant themselves authority which
neither the _BCO_ nor DFW do.

The very first preliminary principle of the _BCO_ states that:

God alone is Lord of the conscience and **has left it free from**

**any doctrines or commandments of men…which, in**

<a id="ga52-p905"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=907 printed_page=905 -->

**regard to matters of faith and worship, are not governed**
**by the Word of God.** Therefore, the rights of private
judgment in all matters that respect religion are universal and
inalienable. (Emphasis added)

As noted above, Scripture neither mandates that public worship be
ended with “formal benediction,” nor does it forbid the use of
doxological praise in the same. If VPC Session is commanding a form
of worship be followed that Scripture has not commanded that the
Church Catholic is to abide by, then does not the complainants’
interpretation and application of _BCO_ 4- 3 and 12-5e risk binding of
the conscience of TE Svendsen?

Further, _BCO_ 12-5e does not grant to a session the powers which the
complainants ascribe to themselves. Dr. Morton Smith notes that 125e is “a broad statement concerning the responsibility of the Session
regarding worship services…” However, he also notes that in terms of
specific areas of oversight, the session does not have the authority to
command a minister what he is to preach. [31] Although the Session does
have general authority over the worship services in their particular
congregation, it doesn’t seem likely that the _BCO_ grants the session
authority over the minister’s usage of Scripture at other points during
worship. Even though the Session can and ought “to consider all
possible ways it can improve the spiritual life of the congregation” [32]
under 12-5e, forbidding the use of certain Scripture passages based on
a definition of “benediction” not contained in our Constitution seems

not to fall under this responsibility.

Though lacking constitutional authority, _BCO_ 52-4 reminds us that
“Ministers are not to be confined to fixed forms of prayer for public
worship…” If TE Svendsen ought not to be confined to a particular
from of prayer, why may he be confined to a particular use of Scripture
when such is not required by Scripture nor our Constitution, especially
when it relies upon a definition of benediction not used by the _BCO_ ?

31 Morton H. Smith, _Commentary on the PCA Book of Church Order_ . Fourth ed.
(Taylors, SC: Southern Presbyterian Press, 2000) p. 104.
32 Smith, _Commentary_ _on the PCA Book of Church Order._ p. 105.

<a id="ga52-p906"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=908 printed_page=906 -->

In VPC session’s response to the August 9 complaint of TE Svendsen
et al, they find that their ruling to only have formal benedictions is a
reformation of liturgy, one that is “in accordance with a good and
necessary consequence of Scriptural interpretation.” [33] But is it truly
_necessary_ ? Is this how worship in the church _must_ be conducted? The
complainants admit as much that it is not, in that they claim that they
do not seek to bind any other congregations by their decision. [34] But if
it is not something that is necessary for the church at large, why may
VPC Session restrict the freedom of TE Svendsen to this particular
pattern?

In their complaint that was received at the Oct 3, 2023 meeting of PP,
TE Svendsen, et al. expressed concern that PP’s actions at the May 5,
2023 meeting were in violation of the first preliminary principle of the
_BCO_ . Since neither Scripture nor the _BCO_ forbids God being
addressed in prayer or praise in the close of public worship, they
contend that PP’s ruling bound the conscience of TE Svendsen to the
motion of VPC session in a matter of worship. [35]

In their response to the complaint of TE Svendsen et al. at the Oct 3,
2023 meeting, the complainants seem to argue that based on the
second preliminary principle, PP did not violate the first preliminary
principle at the May 2, 2023 meeting. Rather, it merely upheld the rule
that VPC session made. [36] However, they may still be open to the claim
that they made “improper use of [their] own [authority],” as the second
principle states.

In his discussion of the first preliminary principle, Dr. Smith notes:

Though the Presbyterian view of the Church is a high view…it
does not maintain that private Christians are bound to submit
without hesitation to all decisions of church officials. Rather,
the Presbyterian understanding of human rights and
responsibilities sustain the rights of private judgment. [37]

33 ROC p. 58 lines 6-7.
34 Cf. ROC p. 37 lines 101-104.
35 ROC p. 43.
36 Smith, _Commentary on the PCA Book of Church Order._ p. 21.
37 Ibid, p. 20.

<a id="ga52-p907"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=909 printed_page=907 -->

In the matter before us, it was the private judgment of TE Svendsen
that doxological passages of Scripture could be used appropriately to
end public worship. In the view of VPC session, it was better for the
spiritual health of VPC to close with a formal benediction exclusively.
However, neither Scripture nor the _BCO_ approves this limiting of
usage of Scripture in public worship in the way they have ruled. As
such, we believe that VPC session made improper use of their rights
at their July 11, 2022 stated meeting. Likewise, PP made improper use
of its own rights at its May 2, 2023 stated meeting and corrected that
mistake at its Oct. 3, 2023 stated meeting.

On the other hand, if VPC’s definition and requirement of a formal
benediction is indeed good and necessary, as they claim, and this
element of worship needs reformation, it still seems to us that proper
procedure wasn’t followed. Suppose the session of a particular church
discerns that there has been serious laxity in the worship of the Lord.
Further suppose that this laxity is widely practiced by the PCA and
considered to be in conformity with the Bible and our Constitution.
Shouldn’t that session first petition its presbytery to submit an overture
to the General Assembly asking for a change to be made to the _BCO_
(RAO 11-5)? It seems improper, then, that a session may essentially
“jump the gun” and apply a ruling to its t es about apart of worship
when our Constitution has not yet been amended to require it.

The complainants argue that the rule they set forth at the July 2022
meeting has no effect on any church other than their own, and that no
other local church is constitutionally bound to abide by this practice. [38]
However, in their deliberations, VPC session made theological,
biblical, and constitutional arguments defending the practice of
limiting the TE in the pulpit to select passages of Scripture. In effect,
VPC Session has introduced a new requirement upon any who would
be called a sate in their church, namely, that he must submit to their
definition of a benediction and utilize only those Scripture verses
which conform to their definition. In seeking to exercise their rights
and duties as the session, VPC’s session risks binding the conscience
of TE Svendsen and any other TE they may call in the future to a

38 Complainants’ Brief p. 8 lines 18-22

<a id="ga52-p908"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=910 printed_page=908 -->

principle and form of liturgy which TEs are not required to agree to
by Scripture or our Constitution.

VPC Session has explained that their intention in mandating a formal
benediction was in order that God should have the last word of the
service. [39] But we believe that they have gone beyond what Scripture
requires of the Church when she worships the Lord. That the last word
of worship must be a formal benediction is not required by Scripture,
nor is it a rule of the Constitution which all have agreed to be governed
by in the PCA. As such, however laudable the intentions, we cannot
affirm VPC session’s desire as more than a self-made practice. In his
comments on WCF 21-1, Chad van Dixhoorn explains:

The call to do in worship what is prescribed in the Bible,
rather than merely to avoid what is proscribed in the Bible, is
an important regulating principle for worship…As a principle,
this attention to biblical prescription usefully keeps us from
promoting in our churches any activity that sounds practical,
appears beautiful, smells wonderful, feels comfortable or
tastes vaguely theological, but that is not prescribed in the
Bible. [40]

We admire and commend our brothers at VPC for their attention to the

proper worship of the Lord our God and for their concern for the
spiritual nourishment of the people of Christ. However, we believe
that by ruling that public worship must be closed with a formal
benediction, something not required by Scripture or our Constitution,
they have overstepped the authority they may legitimately wield as a
session under _BCO_ 12-5e. . . .

The Respondents, in my judgment, served the SJC, the PCA, and our Lord,
with thoughtful, careful, charitable, and Scriptural arguments that should have
prevailed with the Commission.

I know, from long experience, that all of my brothers on the Commission labor
mightily for the good of the church, in faithfulness to our Lord. My dissent in

39 ROC p. 39, lines 181-184.
40 Chad van Dixhoorn, _Confessing the Truth_ (East Peoria, IL: Versa Press inc. 2014),
p. 277.

<a id="ga52-p909"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=911 printed_page=909 -->

no way questions that conviction. My testimony is only that we have failed in
judging righteous judgment in this case.

APPENDIX: CONSTITUTIONAL AUTHORITIES

_BCO_ Preface. The King and Head of the Church. . . . Christ, as King, has given
to His Church officers, oracles and ordinances; and especially has He ordained
therein His system of doctrine, government, discipline and worship, all of
which are either expressly set down in Scripture, or by good and necessary
inference may be deduced therefrom; and to which things He commands that
nothing be added, and that from them naught be taken away.

PP 1. God alone is Lord of the conscience and has left it free from any doctrines
or commandments of men (a) which are in any respect contrary to the Word
of God, or (b) which, in regard to matters of faith and worship, are not
governed by the Word of God. Therefore, the rights of private judgment in all
matters that respect religion are universal and inalienable. . . .”

PP. 7. All church power, whether exercised by the body in general, or by
representation, is only ministerial and declarative since the Holy Scriptures are
the only rule of faith and practice. No church judicatory may make laws to
bind the conscience. All church courts may err through human frailty, yet it
rests upon them to uphold the laws of Scripture though this obligation be
lodged with fallible men.

CF 1. 6. The whole counsel of God concerning all things necessary for his own
glory, man’s salvation, faith and life, is either expressly set down in Scripture,
or by good and necessary consequence may be deduced from Scripture: unto
which nothing at any time is to be added, whether by new revelations of the
Spirit, or traditions of men. . . . Nevertheless, we acknowledge . . . that there
are some circumstances concerning the worship of God, and government of
the church, common to human actions and societies, which are to be ordered
by the light of nature, and Christian prudence, according to the general rules
of the Word, which are always to be observed.

CF 21. 1. The light of nature showeth that there is a God, who hath lordship
and sovereignty over all. . . . But the acceptable way of worshipping the true
God is instituted by himself, and so limited by his own revealed will, that he
may not be worshipped according to the imaginations and devices of men, or

<a id="ga52-p910"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=912 printed_page=910 -->

the suggestions of Satan, under any visible representation, or any other way
not prescribed in the holy Scripture.

CF 25-5. The reading of the Scriptures with godly fear, the sound preaching and
conscionable hearing of the Word, in obedience unto God, with understanding,
faith, and reverence, singing of psalms with grace in the heart; as also, the due
administration and worthy receiving of the sacraments instituted by Christ, are
all parts of the ordinary religious worship of God: beside religious oaths, vows,
solemn fastings, and thanksgivings upon special occasions, which are, in their
several times and seasons, to be used in an holy and religious manner.

_BCO_ 47-1. Since the Holy Scriptures are the only infallible rule of faith and
practice, the principles of public worship must be derived from the Bible, and
from no other source. . . .

_BCO_ 47-6. The Lord Jesus Christ has prescribed no fixed forms for public
worship but, in the interest of life and power in worship, has given His Church
a large measure of liberty in this matter. It may not be forgotten, however, that
there is true liberty only where the rules of God’s Word are observed and the
Spirit of the Lord is, that all things must be done decently and in order, and
that God’s people should serve Him with reverence and in the beauty of
holiness. From its beginning to its end a service of public worship should be
characterized by that simplicity which is an evidence of sincerity and by that
beauty and dignity which are a manifestation of holiness.

_BCO_ 47-9. The Bible teaches that the following are proper elements of worship
service: reading of Holy Scripture, singing of psalms and hymns, the offering
of prayer, the preaching of the Word, the presentation of offerings, confessing
the faith and observing the Sacraments; and on special occasions taking oaths.

_BCO_ 52-4. Ministers are not to be confined to fixed forms of prayer for public
worship, yet it is the duty of the minister, previous to entering upon his office,
to prepare and qualify himself for this part of his work, as well as for preaching.
He should, by a thorough acquaintance with the Holy Scriptures, by the study
of the best writers on prayer, by meditation, and by a life of communion with
God, endeavor to acquire both the spirit and the gift of prayer. Moreover, when
he is to offer prayer in public worship, he should compose his spirit, and so
order his thoughts, that he may perform this duty with dignity and propriety,

<a id="ga52-p911"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=913 printed_page=911 -->

and with profit to the worshippers, lest he disgrace this important service by
coarse, undignified, careless, irregular or extravagant expressions.

_BCO_ 11-3. All Church courts are one in nature, constituted of the same
elements, possessed inherently of the same kinds of rights and powers, and
differing only as the Constitution may provide. When, however, according to
Scriptural example, and needful to the purity and harmony of the whole
Church, disputed matters of doctrine and order arising in the lower courts are
referred to the higher courts for decision, such referral shall not be so exercised
as to impinge upon the authority of the lower court.

_BCO_ 11-4. For the orderly and efficient dispatch of ecclesiastical business, it
is necessary that the sphere of action of each court should be distinctly defined.
The Session exercises jurisdiction over a single church, the Presbytery over
what is common to the ministers, Sessions, and churches within a prescribed
district, and the General Assembly over such matters as concern the whole
Church. The jurisdiction of these courts is limited by the express provisions of
the Constitution.

Every court has the right to resolve questions of doctrine and
discipline seriously and reasonably proposed, and in general to maintain truth
and righteousness, condemning erroneous opinions and practices which tend
to the injury of the peace, purity, or progress of the Church. Although each
court exercises exclusive original jurisdiction over all matters especially
belonging to it, the lower courts are subject to the review and control of the
higher courts, in regular gradation. These courts are not separate and
independent tribunals, but they have a mutual relation, and every act of
jurisdiction is the act of the whole Church performed by it through the
appropriate organ.

<a id="ga52-p912"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=914 printed_page=912 -->

**CASE 2023-21**

**TE SAMUEL KANG, ET AL.**

**v.**

**KOREAN CENTRAL PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON COMPLAINT**

**October 17, 2024**

**SUMMARY OF THE CASE**

This Complaint was filed by three Teaching Elders of the Korean Central
Presbytery (“KCP”). The case involves some of the same parties and issues as
SJC Case No. 2021-11. Presbytery appointed an investigative committee to
examine allegations of misconduct by a Teaching Elder. The TE was laboring
out of bounds as the senior pastor of an independent church, Covenant
Fellowship Church (“CFC”) in Champaign-Urbana, Illinois. After reviewing
the alleged offenses and meeting with the Accused, Presbytery determined not
to indict the Teaching Elder and to take no further action. The Complainants
timely filed to complain against that decision.

**I.** **SUMMARY OF THE FACTS**

06/02/22 The SJC issued its decision in Case No. 2021-11 sustaining
the Complaint and determining that further action was moot
because the primary alleged victim of the offense declined to
participate in Presbytery’s investigation or actions regarding
the Accused.

9/22 Several former members and former staff of CFC wrote to
KCP presenting additional written allegations against the
Accused. The new allegations include claims of financial
impropriety; “spiritual abuse” in the treatment of staff and
congregants in rebuking and shaming them; and physical
abuse.

11/11/22 KCP formed an “Investigation Committee” to review the
allegations against the Accused and directed it to report at the
next meeting of Presbytery. KCP further directed the “The

<a id="ga52-p913"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=915 printed_page=913 -->

report will include the decision whether to indict or dismiss
the case…”.

11/8/22 to

4/11/23 The Investigation Committee met six times. It reviewed the
written allegations presented by former CFC staff and
members, and it interviewed the Accused.

4/18/23 The Investigation Committee issued a report summarizing the
allegations and its interview with the Accused and
recommended that KCP decline to indict the Accused and

therefore “dismiss” the allegations against him. KCP voted to
receive the Committee’s report “as it is.”

5/15/23 The Complainants filed their Complaint with KCP against the
decision not to indict alleging errors by the Investigation
Committee in its work. The Committee provided KCP a
written response to the Complaint.

10/10/23 KCP heard the Complaint. The meeting was conducted in
Korean, with English translation provide for non-Korean
speakers. The minutes of the meeting were produced in
Korean. The initial translation of the minutes provided in the
Record of the Case reads as follows: “The complaints are
sustained. However, due to the age of the matter, difficulties
have arisen in the investigation. Therefore, the Presbytery has
decided to dismiss the complaints.” In its brief, KCP contends
that the translation of “complaints are sustained” from the
original Korean to English was inaccurate, and that the proper
translation should have been “Although the Complaint is
valid…”

11/5/23 The Complainants carried the Complaint to the General
Assembly.

3/18/2024 After conferring and amending by consent, the parties agreed
upon the final Record of the Case.

<a id="ga52-p914"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=916 printed_page=914 -->

06/27/24 The Panel conducted the hearing with members RE Jack
Wilson, (Chairman), TE David Garner (Secretary) and RE
John Maynard in attendance. TE Fred Greco (alternate) was
also present. TEs Samuel Kang and Brian Park presented for
the Complainants, and TE Kukhun Lee represented KCP.

**II.** **STATEMENT OF THE ISSUE**

Did Korean Central Presbytery clearly err ( _BCO_ 39-3.2 and .3) in its _BCO_ 312 investigation and determination not to indict and to take no further action on
the allegations presented against the Accused?

**III.** **JUDGMENT**

No.

**IV.** **REASONING AND OPINION**

The Record shows that when Presbytery dealt with this Complaint they took
the following action: "The complaints are sustained. However, due to the age
of the matter, difficulties have arisen in the investigation. Therefore,
Presbytery has decided to dismiss the complaints." In its hearing brief
Respondents argued that the Korean word translated "sustained" was intended
by Presbytery not to indicate a formal judicial action, but only to indicate that
the Complaint had some degree of merit. It is unfortunate that an apparent error
in the translation of a Korean word created confusion about whether KCP

intended to “sustain” the Complaint judicially or merely to note that it raised
valid concerns. [41] While this clarifies a seeming inconsistency in Presbytery's

41 At the Panel hearing, Complainants acknowledged that their primary language was
English. Presbytery’s meetings and work are conducted in the Korean language.
Presbytery’s meetings are conducted with “live” translation from Korean to English
through listening devices. The Complainants indicated that they were understandably
confused by the English translation of Presbytery’s action indicating the Complaint
was “sustained” but then dismissed. The Complainants indicated that they did not
understand until they read the Presbytery’s hearing brief that the Korean word

<a id="ga52-p915"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=917 printed_page=915 -->

action, it still had the effect of denying the Complaint; thus this matter is
properly before us.

The reports of misconduct underlying this case include the original allegations
adjudicated by the SJC in Case No. 2021-11. This case also presents additional
allegations of misconduct. This case is similar to the former case in that many
of those allegations occurred in the distant past. The allegations of financial
misconduct came from a church treasurer who served from 1997 to 2007. She
left CFC in 2008. Another former staff member alleged manipulative and
abusive conduct from 2018 and 2019. a church member alleged that a fellow
member had been disciplined too lightly, or not at all, for an alleged incident
of sexual assault that occurred in 2000. Another member, the wife of a former
staff member, relayed several actions of the Accused in a timeline from 1990
to 2009.

KCP’s Investigation Committee reviewed these written statements and
determined that the Accused “did not correctly handle” one incident of sexual
misconduct by a church member in 2000. The Committee further reported that
the Accused “lacked wisdom” in many of his words and actions, but that it
would be difficult to prove that those bad acts constituted spiritual abuse. The
Committee indicated that the Accused disputed some of the claims. In
summary, the Committee concluded that it could not recommend that KCP
indict the Accused based on the allegations presented. We note that KCP
specifically evaluated the age of these allegations (and presumably the passage
of time) as one reason for not proceeding.

On the Record presented, we cannot say that KCP _clearly erred_ in reaching
this conclusion. Our Constitution requires a higher court to exhibit “great
deference” to lower courts regarding factual matters ( _BCO_ 39-3(2)) and
matters of discretion and judgment ( _BCO_ 39-2(3)). Judgments and decisions
based on such fact finding or the exercise of discretion and judgment may not
be reversed unless there is clear error on the part of the lower court. While the
Complainants allege that the Investigative Committee erred in failing to be
diligent or thorough enough in its work, we cannot say that KCP clearly erred
in acting on its Committee’s recommendations. KCP and its Committee faced
daunting questions arising from serious allegations of misconduct over a

translated “sustained” was intended by Presbytery not to indicate a formal judicial
action, but only to indicate that the Complaint had some degree of merit.

<a id="ga52-p916"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=918 printed_page=916 -->

lengthy period of time. Proceeding to trial on these allegations was doubtless
complicated by the passage of time since many of the identified events
occurred. It was within Presbytery’s judgment and discretion to determine, as
the SJC did in Case No. 2021-11, that the passage of time made prosecution
unproductive.

While the circumstances and allegations presented here are serious,
our decision is controlled by the standards from _BCO_ 39-3(2) and 39-3(3).
By virtue of KCP’s closer proximity to the facts and circumstances, and in
view of the investigation summarized in the Record, we cannot say
Presbytery clearly erred in its decision not to indict. For these reasons, the
Complaint is not sustained.

**VI. AMENDS**

None.

**VII. RECORDING IN MINUTES**

The SJC instructs the Session and Presbytery to record this Decision, and any
Concurring and Dissenting Opinions, in the minutes of the next stated
meeting following its receipt of the full Decision.

The decision was drafted by RE Jack Wilson and the SJC approved the final
decision as amended by a vote of **15-8-1.**

Bankson _Concur_ Eggert _Concur_ Maynard _Dissent_
Bise _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Neikirk _Concur_

Bruce _Dissent_ Garner _Dissent_ Pickering _Dissent_
Carrell _Dissent_ Greco _Concur_ Sartorius _Dissent_

Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall _Dissent_
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Dissent_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe _Concur_ Lee _Abstain_ White _Concur_

Dowling _Concur_ Marcinak _Concur_ Wilson _Concur_

<a id="ga52-p917"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=919 printed_page=917 -->

**DISSENTING OPINION ON COMPLAINT IN CASE 2023-21**

**By RE Caleb Stegall, TE Art Sartorius, RE Dan Carrell,**

**TE Eric Landry, RE John Pickering, TE David Garner,**

**RE John Maynard, and TE Jay Bruce**

**NOVEMBER 5, 2024**

The SJC decision frames this case as a question of whether the Korean
Central Presbytery (KCP) clearly erred in its _BCO_ 31-2 investigation—and
answers that question in the negative. Contrary to the SJC Decision, however,
this does not end the matter. Indeed, it might be fair to say that this misses the
Complainants’ primary point.

The heart of the Complaint, as we read it, is not necessarily that KCP
got it wrong with respect to some factual finding or discretionary decision, but
rather that KCP never gave itself the opportunity to make any reasoned
judgment at all because it did not adequately investigate the allegations. To put
this in _BCO_ terms, the Complainants suggest that KCP’s _BCO_ 31-2
investigation was constitutionally deficient because it failed to comply with
that provision’s “due diligence” requirement. See _BCO_ 31-2.

Whether a lower court’s _BCO_ 31-2 investigation is constitutionally
sound in its process (as opposed to its factual findings or discretionary
judgments) is not a question on which the SJC owes any deference to the lower
courts. Determining the meaning and scope of the constitutional demand for
“due diligence” in a _BCO_ 31-2 investigation involves the “interpretation of the
Constitution of the Church” and “[r]egarding such issues, the higher court has
the duty and authority to interpret and apply the Constitution of the Church
according to its best abilities and understanding, regardless of the opinion of
the lower court.” _BCO_ 31-3.4.

We must, therefore, perform the analysis the majority opinion lacks.
Namely, was the investigation at issue in this case constitutionally sufficient—
or did it fall short of the “due diligence” required by _BCO_ 31-2? The case arose
in September 2022 after six former members and former staff of Covenant
Fellowship Church (CFC) in Champaign, Illinois wrote to KCP presenting
written allegations against the Accused. These accusations were treated as

<a id="ga52-p918"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=920 printed_page=918 -->

“reports” at the Presbytery meeting of October 11-12, 2022, and assigned to
an investigative committee to conduct a _BCO_ 31-2 investigation. The
committee was instructed to report back to the Presbytery by the next
Presbytery meeting, and to include in its report the committee’s decision on
whether to indict or dismiss.

Prior to the next Presbytery meeting of April 18-19, 2023, the
investigative committee met six times, but did not hold any interviews or pose
questions to the Accusers. Regarding the Accusers, the committee only
reviewed the written reports. The sole person questioned by the investigative
committee was the Accused himself. He was interviewed on April 11, 2023,
but the Record of the Case only records a broad summary of his statement.

Nonetheless, it is clear from the written report submitted by the
investigative committee that some of the reported accusations may have been
admitted to by the Accused. The committee summarized the Accused’s
statements as follows:

- The Accused was operating in the “context of Korean culture and
church ministry when he planted CFC.”

- The Accused did use “strong language when rebuking or correcting
people.”

- The Accused “did not remember the exact words that he said to [one
of his Accusers] in times of prophetic rebuking and prayer, but he
admitted that his words could have been errant.”

- The Accused “gave his side of the story that differed from that of the
allegations.”

Furthermore, when stating its conclusions, the investigative
committee went on to add that “the committee believes that [the Accused]
lacked wisdom in many of his words and deeds at CFC. He mishandled cases
of sexual misconduct. He spoke wrongly in moments of rebuke or counsel. He
did not uphold financial accountability and transparency. His ways of
leadership led to many damaged relationships.”

<a id="ga52-p919"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=921 printed_page=919 -->

It would not be wise to attempt to set forth precisely what is required
during the course of _BCO_ 31-2 investigations. The _BCO_ itself does not do this
beyond the requirement for due diligence. But the concept of “due diligence”
is not foreign to any of us, and it must be given adequate constitutional teeth.
“Due diligence” is well understood with a commonly accepted definition.
_Black’s Law Dictionary_ defines due diligence as: “Such a measure of
prudence, activity, or assiduity, a sis properly to be expected from, and
ordinarily exercised by, a reasonable prudent man under the particular
circumstances; not measured by any absolute standard, but depending on the
relative facts of the special case.” We agree with this definition, and see it as
an accurate expression of what should be required from lower courts
endeavoring to comply with _BCO_ 31-2.

As such, due diligence is inherently definable only in relation to the
nearly infinite varieties of specific circumstances that may arise in the course
of an investigation. We cannot, then, say what all future _BCO_ 31-2
investigations _must_ look like. But we can make the more limited judgment as
to what this particular _BCO_ 31-2 investigation _should have_ looked like.

And in our judgment, KCP was obligated by the constitutional
responsibility of due diligence in this instance to do more. Given the nature of
the allegations (including allegations of hiding cases of sexual misconduct and
physically abusing some persons within the church); the statements of the
Accused admitting at least some guilt; and the conclusions of the investigative
committee finding serious pastoral deficits, due diligence required the
investigative committee to conduct at least some follow-up investigation.

We do not reach such a conclusion lightly. We acknowledge that a fair
measure of leeway must be accorded lower courts to fashion their own
investigations. We also affirm that due diligence does not require lower courts
to act as we would have acted. It only requires such “prudence ... as is properly
to be expected from ... a reasonable prudent man under the particular
circumstances.” This standard must, however, take into account the overriding
principle that such investigations exist in order that lower courts may “exercise
care over those subject to their authority” to the end that the “peace, unity and
purity of the Church, and the honor and majesty of the Lord Jesus Christ, as
the King and Head thereof” might be preserved. See _BCO_ 31-2 and _BCO_ 314.

<a id="ga52-p920"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=922 printed_page=920 -->

When allegations of serious wrongs—which we believe all SJC
members would readily concede do great harm to the peace, unity and purity
of the Church—have been found to have at least some measure of evidentiary
support, the reasonably prudent course of action demands that the investigation
not simply end. Care for both the Accused as well as those Church members
under his authority demands this. Such further investigation would serve to
either vindicate the Accused (thus caring for and preserving his future
ministry) or to vindicate his Accusers (thus caring for them by according
justice and preventing future abuses). As it is, however, here neither outcome
was reached. By prematurely ending its _BCO_ 31-2 investigation, KCP was
constitutionally deficient leaving no party satisfied and leaving the “peace,
unity and purity of the Church” in doubt.

The proper outcome here, in our judgment, is to remand this matter
back to the KCP to complete its investigation in a constitutionally sufficient
manner. Because the majority fails to even consider this question, and leaves
in place a deficient _BCO_ 31-2 investigation, we dissent.

<a id="ga52-p921"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=923 printed_page=921 -->

**CASE 2023-22**

**TE** **SAM JUNG SUH**

**v.**

**KOREAN SOUTHEASTERN PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON APPEAL**

**March 6, 2025**

**CASE SUMMARY**

This case came before the SJC on the Appeal of TE Sam Jung Suh. The Appeal
arose from the censure of indefinite suspension imposed by Korean
Southeastern Presbytery (KSEP) after trial proceedings.

The Panel heard the Appeal in person on October 16, 2024, at the Westin
Atlanta Airport. Appellant, TE Sam Jung Suh, appeared at the meeting with
his representative, TE Mike Suh. KSEP, the Appellee, was represented by TE
Eddie Lim.

The Panel was composed of TE David Coffin (Chairman), RE Jim Eggert
(Secretary), TE Paul Bankson, John Bise (alternate), and TE Paul Lee
