(alternate). Having heard the matter for about two and a half hours, and upon
their close review of the Record, and a series of meetings for deliberation, the
Panel have decided to recommend that the decision of KSEP be reversed in

whole.

**I.** **SUMMARY OF THE FACTS**

09/26/22 Three Teaching Elders signed a “Formal Petition” to Korean
Southeastern Presbytery (“KSEP”) alleging a “formal charge
of financial misconduct” against TE Suh, the pastor of Korean
First Presbyterian Church (“KFPC”). The document claimed
that TE Suh had “publicly admitted his wife deposited church
funds from the sale of church property into her and his
personal bank accounts.”

10/04/22 At its stated meeting, KSEP considered what it called a
“petition for KSEP to investigate allegations of financial

<a id="ga52-p922"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=924 printed_page=922 -->

misconduct” against TE Suh and formed an investigative
committee to investigate “the nature of these allegations”
(“the IC”)

10/05/22 The IC could not initially come to an agreement regarding the
scope of the investigation. Some members believed the scope
should be limited to the video of TE Suh’s apology on July 3,
2022, while others believed the investigation “should include
TE Suh’s alleged financial misconduct as senior pastor.”

10/31/22 At a called meeting, KSEP instructed its committee to
determine the scope of the investigation.

11/04/22 The IC decided to “investigate allegations of financial
misconduct as reported by KFPC’s transition committee.”

11/11/22 The IC decided to request documents from the church's
transition committee that supported “their claims of TE Suh's
alleged embezzlement and money laundering.”

11/17/22 The IC met with the Transition Committee for about 3.5

hours.

12/18/22 The IC met with the KFPC Session for approaching 3.5 hours.

12/19/22 The IC met with TE Suh and his wife for a little over 3 hours.

02/08/2023 The IC completed its report stating it believed there was a
strong presumption of guilt against TE Suh. The IC reported
that it held 23 meetings lasting about 33 hours. The Report
stated, “The investigation focused on the video footage of
Pastor Sam Jung Suh submitted as evidence by the accusers,
the ‘Financial Story’ of the senior pastor and his wife and the
suspicions mentioned in the report of the transition committee
formed by the session of Korean First Presbyterian Church.”
The IC also “investigated additional suspicions.” The Report
noted that it requested “additional evidentiary material” from
TE Suh and his wife but “did not receive everything we asked
for.” The Report “confirmed that there was numerous

<a id="ga52-p923"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=925 printed_page=923 -->

financial misconduct [ _sic_ ].” Noting that the IC “believes there
is a strong presumption of guilt,” it recommended Presbytery
institute four charges (including factual specifications)
against TE Suh.

02/13/2023 At a called meeting, KSEP received the report of the IC and
concluded: “As the IC reported a strong presumption of guilt,
KSEP will institute process according to the _BCO_
(determining whether the trial will be conducted as a whole or
by a judicial commission and the procedure of indictment and
entering a plea thereafter) at the upcoming spring stated
meeting.”

04/10/2023 After allowing TE Suh to address the stated meeting of
Presbytery, KSEP voted to indict TE Suh (the vote was 3217-1), formed a judicial commission to try the case, and
appointed a prosecutor. The Presbytery set the judicial
commission at five members, inclusive of two alternates (3
TEs and 2 REs). The Presbytery elected two “Koreanspeaking commissioners” and one “English-speaking
commissioner.”

05/01/23 The Moderator and Stated Clerk signed the First version of
the “Charges and Specifications against TE Suh (“the Original
Indictment”).

06/13/23 The General Assembly adopted _BCO_ 15-1 and 15-3, stating
that the commission rather than the Presbytery is to decide the
judgment in cases delegated to a judicial commission. Judicial
matters committed to a commission would no longer return to
the commissioning Presbytery for vote: “The commission
shall try the case in the manner presented by the Rules of
Discipline and shall submit to the Presbytery a full statement
of the case and the judgment rendered. The judgment of the
commission shall be the decision of the Presbytery, and the
statement of the case and judgment printed in its minutes.”

07/24/23 A Korean translation of the Original Indictment (originally
written in English) was delivered via certified mail to TE

<a id="ga52-p924"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=926 printed_page=924 -->

Suh’s residence. The Original Indictment contained three
charges, claiming that TE Suh (1) violated the Ninth
Commandment, (2) violated the Eighth Commandment, and
(3) failed to uphold ordination vows three, four, and seven as
senior pastor and moderator of the Session, breaking the Fifth
Commandment.

08/07/23 The arraignment occurred, and TE Suh pled “not guilty to all
charges.”

08/25/23 The Prosecutor presented and the Moderator, Stated Clerk and
the Prosecutor executed a new version of the Original
Indictment (“the Amended Indictment”).

The Amended Indictment struck the first specification of
Charge 1, which had stated that TE Suh falsely informed the
church Session that $150,000.00 was owed on a church loan,
even though the lender had forgiven the loan.

In place of the former specification, the Amended Indictment
added a “representative incident” that TE Suh falsely
represented to the congregation that his wife had negotiated a
reduction in the outstanding church loan in a lesser amount
than she actually had, so as to make it appear that the church
still owed $250,000.00 when the church only owed
$200,000.00.

08/26/23 TE Suh received the Amended Indictment from the

Prosecutor. The Chairman of the judicial commission emailed
TE Suh (in Korean) notifying him that the Prosecutor had
provided a “revised indictment,” and represented that the
amendment made “no change in the 3 main subjects” of the
original. The email added: “There are some revised
specifications in those subjects, noting the “removed
specification has a line through it and the added one is
underlined.” The Chairman concluded, “I believe you
understand English, so I have not translated it and sent the
prosecutor’s original.”

<a id="ga52-p925"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=927 printed_page=925 -->

09/01/23 The trial was conducted over the course of approximately nine
hours. The Prosecutor presented two witnesses as well as
corroborating evidence (13 documentary exhibits, 1 video,
and 2 audio files). TE Suh presented ten witnesses.

09/06/23 The Judicial Commission rendered its decision in the case
finding TE Suh guilty on all three counts and recommended
alternate censure depending on whether TE Suh was deemed
to be repentant.

09/30/23 TE Suh received the verdicts.

10/02/23 KSEP met to consider the report of the judicial commission
recommending alternate censure. The commission reported its
guilty verdict on all three counts and “recommended” censure
in the alternate, dependent upon the Presbytery's finding of TE
Suh’s repentance:

- “If TE Suh is repentant and satisfies the court as to his

repentance” then “definite suspension from the
sacraments and from office be imposed for a period of one
year.”

- “if TE Suh is not repentant” then “indefinite suspension

from the sacraments and office and to form a shepherding
committee to shepherd TE Suh toward a sense of his sin
and to repent.”

TE Suh was permitted to address the judgment and indicated
that he believed the one-year suspension was “excessive,” but
he “no longer wants to make this a matter of controversy to
the Presbytery and does not intend to complain to the General
Assembly.” He added that he had “decided to accept the
Presbytery’s ruling as is but does not admit to any wrong
doing. [ _sic_ ]” TE Suh then “excused himself from Presbytery,
without seeking permission, stating fatigue and his need to
rest,” having offered no “written statement of confession or
repentance.” KSEP found him “impenitent as his address did
not satisfy the court as to his repentance,” and censured him
with indefinite suspension from the sacraments and the

<a id="ga52-p926"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=928 printed_page=926 -->

exercise of his office as a teaching elder” until he gives
satisfactory evidence of repentance. The judicial commission
was then dissolved.

10/7/23 TE Suh was emailed the decision and censure.

11/06/23 TE Suh filed a timely appeal from the judgment.

12/13/23 The SJC Officers appointed a panel consisting of TE David
Coffin, RE Sam Duncan, and TE Paul Bankson, with RE Jim
Eggert and TE Paul Lee as alternates.

09/06/24 After finalizing the Record of the Case, the Panel found the
case to be judicially in order and ready to proceed to final
hearing. (When RE Sam Duncan rolled off the SJC in June
2024, the SJC Officers appointed RE John Bise as Alternate
and RE Jim Eggert (original Alternate) assumed RE Sam
Duncan’s original position as Principal.)

10/16/24 The Panel heard oral arguments of the parties. Appellant was
represented by TE Mike Suh and Appellee was represented by
TE Eddie Lim.

**II.** **STATEMENT OF THE ISSUE**

Did Korean Southeastern Presbytery (KSEP) fail in its constitutional
obligations when it initiated judicial process against TE Sam Jung
Suh?

**III.** **JUDGMENT**

Yes. The decision of KSEP finding a strong presumption of guilt on
the part of TE Suh, and thus to institute process, appoint a prosecutor
to prepare an indictment and to conduct the case, the work of the
Judicial Commission (JC) and its judgment, and the censure imposed,
is reversed in whole. TE Suh is herein restored to his standing in KSEP
as it was before Presbytery entertained the “charges,” and no judicial

<a id="ga52-p927"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=929 printed_page=927 -->

process, with respect to any matter in the proceedings reversed, is
permitted hereafter.

**IV.** **REASONING & OPINION**

In his Appeal TE Suh set forth specifications of error with respect to KSEP’s
actions: (1) in the pre-investigation; (2) in the _BCO_ 31-2 investigation and the
finding of a strong presumption of guilt; (3) in the indictment, trial proceedings
and judgment; and (4) in the censure.

This Court finds that the pre-investigative phase, the _BCO_ 31-2 investigation,
and the commencement of process, were marred by a number of grave
constitutional errors. This finding fully justifies the vindication of the
Appellant, and a decision to reverse in whole.

Thus, although TE Suh, in his Appeal, brought many other specifications of
error with respect to these proceedings—specifications of varying merit, and
some of which, of themselves, might have warranted this judgment—these
need not be taken up in this Decision, as the whole proceeding is reversed on
the ground of the unconstitutional initiation of judicial process.

THE FIRST specification of error alleges that KSEP failed to require that those
bringing charges do so in compliance with _BCO_ 27-5. Without speaking to TE
Suh, three Teaching Elders of the Presbytery brought charges against him in a
Petition to the Presbytery (September 26, 2022). They did so based on a
YouTube video (July 3, 2022) wherein TE Suh addresses the congregation of
Korean First Presbyterian Church (KFPC). TE Suh was first informed about
the charges by receipt of the agenda three days prior to the Presbytery meeting
at which the charges were to be considered (October 4, 2022).

This specification of error is sustained.

A Minute Explanatory. _BCO_ 27-5 identifies as crucial to Scriptural discipline
“Individual’s responsibility to admonish one another,” citing both Matthew
18:15 and Galatians 6:1. The latter directs that “if anyone is caught in any
transgression, you who are spiritual should restore him in a spirit of
gentleness.” The alleged transgression in view in this case appears to be a
general ( _BCO_ 29-3), public ( _BCO_ 29-4) offense. The Rules of Discipline set

<a id="ga52-p928"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=930 printed_page=928 -->

forth distinct procedures to follow with respect to the kind of offense in
question. _BCO_ 31-5 insists that “An injured party shall not become a
prosecutor of personal offenses without having tried the means of
reconciliation and of reclaiming the offender, required by Christ,” citing Mat.
18. However, _BCO_ 31-7 grants that “When the prosecution is instituted by the
court, the previous steps required by our Lord in the case of personal offenses
are not necessary.”

Clearly the three charging Teaching Elders were not pursuing personal
offenses, but rather were charging a public, general offense, with respect to an
act they specifically identify. Consider:

“Formal Petition Submitted to the Korean Southeastern Presbytery of
The Presbyterian Church In America,” September 26, 2022 (ROC 23,
emphasis added)

. . . It is with humble and sad hearts that we bring _a formal charge_
against TE Sam Jung Suh regarding financial misconduct with church
funds during his time as Senior Pastor of Korean First Presbyterian
Church.

In a publicly broadcast and recorded speech
(https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=s6LHr-nngDg, dated July 3,
2022), _TE Sam Jung Suh publicly admitted that his wife deposited_
_church funds from the sale of church property into her and his_
_personal bank accounts_ . This action is _financial misconduct_ with
church funds and is a chargeable offense. . . .

However, just as clearly, the court itself was not instituting process; on the
contrary, it was responding to charges laid. Consider:

October 4, 2022, KSEP Meeting Minutes (ROC 31, emphasis added)

74-14. New Business

1. Petition for KSEP to investigate allegations of financial misconduct
against TE Sam Suh.
_**MSC**_ _to vote by secret ballot to determine if KSEP will consider_
_allegations of financial misconduct against TE Sam Suh. 24-8-1_

<a id="ga52-p929"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=931 printed_page=929 -->

_**MSC**_ _to form a committee to investigate the nature of these allegations_
_and to report to Presbytery. TEs Thomas Oh. Jongsu Hong, Sang Joon_
_Kim and RE Yoonho Kang shall serve as committee members. 19-5-1_

Note that the motion employs the language of the Petition.

Thus, is Mat. 18 compliance required of the chargers, or no? It is instructive
to note that _BCO_ 31-7 goes on to say that “There are many cases, however, in
which it will promote the interests of religion to send a committee to converse
in a private manner with the offender, and endeavor to bring him to a sense of
his guilt, before instituting actual process.” Thus, a form of Mat. 18
compliance is urged as a course of wisdom, at the least, even when not
required.

beth a ta sit may, under “General Provisions” the Rules return to Galatians 6:

_BCO_ 32-1, “Brethren, if a man is overtaken in any trespass, you who
are spiritual restore such a one in the spirit of gentleness, considering
yourself lest you also be tempted.”

The goal is to restore, the manner is with gentleness. _BCO_ 32-2 sets forth two
different paths possible for a case of process: “some person or persons
undertake to make out the charge”; or “the court finds it necessary, for the
honor of religion, itself to take the step provided for in _BCO_ 31-2.”

In this case some persons _have_ undertaken to make out a charge, and, at the
very least, they had the duty under Galatians 6 to do what they could to restore
with gentleness and wisdom. Had KSEP required evidence of such effort, the
accusing Teaching Elders, having inadvertently misrepresented TE Suh’s
comments—i.e., TE Suh apologizing for taking money from the sale of church
property—could have been easily corrected, as restoration, wisdom and
gentleness required. The undisputed claim of the Appellant is that the charging
TEs were completely removed from the situation and had no context for
understanding what they saw and heard on the video. Further, the person they
were accusing had faithfully served at his church for 46 years without scandal
and certainly was due the judgment of charity. He was a founding member and
father of the Presbytery and a distinguished member of it until the accusations
were brought. The Presbytery honored him upon his retirement in May of
2022. And yet, in this instance, instead of brothers coming to TE Suh, to restore

<a id="ga52-p930"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=932 printed_page=930 -->

him ina spirit of gentleness, TE Suh first learned of the charges through an
agenda emailed a few days before the October 2022 presbytery meeting that
was to act on the charging Petition.

The misrepresentation of the charge _was_ finally corrected, in fact the original
charge was not included in the Indictment. Further, at the Hearing on the
Appeal, Presbytery generously acknowledged that the initial charge was
without merit:

Presbytery accedes to the Appellant’s claim that the allegations were
indeed a misunderstanding of the Appellant’s public confession on the
YouTube video. Absent from the indictment is any reference to a
charge or specification related to _the sale of church property_ . The
investigative committee looked into financial aspects regarding the
sale of a piece of property owned by the church but was unable to
proceed further due to lack of documentation (ROC 51, lines. 16-24,
emphasis added).

Yet that admission was too late: Grievous harm had been done, as scandalous
charges were received against TE Suh—not on slight grounds—but on no
grounds at all (cf. _BCO_ 34-2). And that groundless charge became, in the work
of the Investigative Committee (IC), an unlimited license for intrusion into the
life of TE Suh and the Session of KFPC.

THE SECOND specification of error alleges that KSEP failed to correct its
Investigative Committee (IC) for violations of constitutional responsibility,
i.e., _BCO_ 31-8 requires that

Great caution ought to be exercised in receiving accusations from any
person who is known to indulge a malignant spirit towards the
accused; who is not of good character; who is himself under censure
or process; who is deeply interested in any respect in the conviction of
the accused; or who is known to be litigious, rash or highly imprudent.

This specification of error is sustained.

A Minute Explanatory. IC relied upon testimony of 3 people who had recently
departed from the church and relied on the testimony of one witness that the
Record of the Case (ROC) clearly shows should have been treated with “great

<a id="ga52-p931"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=933 printed_page=931 -->

caution”. Yet there is no evidence of the IC acting with “great caution”. On the
contrary, the IC gave greater weight to these sources than it did to TE Suh, a
minister of 46 years of unblemished reputation, and to the very Session of the
Church he served, the Session that would have been harmed by his dereliction.

THE THIRD specification of error alleges that at its meetings on February 13
and April 10-11, 2023, KSEP failed to comply with _BCO_ 31-2 in approving
the Report of its IC, finding “a strong presumption of guilt” with respect to TE
Suh. IC’s _BCO_ 31-2 investigation was constitutionally deficient because it
failed to comply with the provisions of _BCO_ 31-2. [42]

This specification of error is sustained.

A Minute Explanatory.

a. KSEP did not have before it “reports,” (i.e., “common fame”

information deleterious to reputation); it had “charges” (see
specification 1).

b. KSEP, treating “charges” as “reports,” failed to comply with the

leading direction of the _BCO_ 31-2: To “demand” _from TE Suh_
satisfactory explanations of the report (i.e., the charge of the Petition.).

c. KSEP allowed an investigation of TE Suh that, having abandoned the

original charge as untrue, then without warrant, expanded to matters
having nothing to do with the specificity of original report.

d. KSEP’s _BCO_ 31-2 “investigation was constitutionally deficient

because it failed to comply with that provision’s ‘due diligence’
requirement.” Here it is critical to understand that “[d]etermining the
meaning and scope of the constitutional demand for ‘due diligence’ in
a _BCO_ 31-2 investigation involves the ‘interpretation of the
Constitution of the Church’ and ‘[r]egarding such issues, the higher
court has the duty and authority to interpret and apply the Constitution
of the Church according to its best abilities and understanding,

42 Here is it important to observe that KSEP, in its minutes, never formally stated that
the IC was governed by _BCO_ 31-2. Thus, it would be of no surprise that Presbytery
was not careful to comply with the cited provisions.

<a id="ga52-p932"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=934 printed_page=932 -->

regardless of the opinion of the lower court.’ _BCO_ 31-3.4.” (see SJC
Case 2023-21 “Dissenting Opinion on Complaint”).

i. The IC did not meet with TE Suh until late in its investigation

rather than as the focus of it as required by _BCO_ 31-2.

ii. The IC, in preparation for the interview with TE Suh, had

compiled questions to present to him, but failed to provide
them to TE Suh prior to the meeting, though that would have
enabled TE Suh to prepare “satisfactory explanations” in
relation to the extensive matters under consideration. (ROC
42, Session 11)

iii. The IC only met with TE Suh once, though the matters under

discussion were complex and extensive.
iv. The IC’s Report contained accusations never raised with TE

Suh, e.g., 1.c., ROC 49, lines 32-38; 2.b., ROC 50, lines 8-10;
4.a., RC 50, lines 45-46.

THE FOURTH specification of error alleges, by implication, that since the
_BCO_ 31-2 investigation of the “report” had abandoned the original report as
untrue, there was no possibility of a strong presumption of guilt, and thus no
antecedent condition necessary to institute process.

This specification of error is sustained, as evident _prima facie_ in the ROC.

THE FIFTH specification of error alleges that KSEP failed to require that the
IC set before it what explanations TE Suh had offered as satisfactory
explanations concerning the Petition’s report, and why the IC found those
explanations unsatisfactory.

This specification of error is sustained, as evident _prima facie_ in the ROC.

A FINAL WORD. This decision notwithstanding, the SJC commends KSEP
for its diligence in this first attempt of the Presbytery at the very difficult work
of formal church discipline. The Court is confident that the errors herein
identified did not arise from ill will, but rather a desire to serve the Lord albeit
in a work with respect to which they had no experience. Yet it must be said
that this decision does not in any sense vindicate all of the financial practices

<a id="ga52-p933"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=935 printed_page=933 -->

that had come to be customary at KFPC. Cultural differences ought to be
accommodated in different cultures, but in the culture of the PCA, if one is to
participate according to that culture, some of the practices of KFPC must
change.

So too the SJC commends the godly manner in which Appellee and Appellant
conducted themselves throughout this ordeal, in seeking to be faithful to their
causes, and at the same time endure with civility and fair-mindedness the
complex regulations of the appellate process. We trust the Lord has been
honored in the Christian spirit evident in the manner of our proceedings, even
though some may not have that hope for the matter in the outcome.

Overall, the SJC encourages our courts, when undertaking a _BCO_ 31-2
investigation, to have a care for precisely what the provision requires, and to
consider those requirements, in light of the principles of justice embodied in
the demand for probable cause required of all sound governments in the
exercise of their governing authority.

The proposed decision was written by TE David Coffin, as to the Issue,
Judgment and Reasoning & Opinion and RE James Eggert, as to the Summary
of the Facts, all as amended by the Panel. It was approved by Panel members
TE David Coffin and TE Paul Bankson, supported by RE John Bise (alternate)
and TE Paul Lee (alternate). RE James Eggert dissented.

**V.** **AMENDS**

TE Suh is herein restored to his standing in KSEP as it was before Presbytery
entertained the “charges,” and no judicial process, with respect to any matter
in the proceedings reversed, is permitted hereafter.

**VI.** **RECORDING IN MINUTES**

The SJC instructs KSEP to record this Decision and any Concurring and
Dissenting Opinions, as well as a statement of how KSEP has complied with
the amends herein, in the minutes of the next stated meeting following its
receipt of the full Decision.

<a id="ga52-p934"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=936 printed_page=934 -->

The SJC reviewed each part of the proposed decision and after the approval of
various amendments the vote on the decision was **17-6-0**, **with 1 absent,** thus
sustaining the Appeal.

_It was moved, seconded, and carried to announce the decision immediately to_
_the parties (OMSJC 17.2)._

Bankson _Concur_ Eggert _Dissent_ Maynard _Dissent_
Bise _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Neikirk _Concur_

Bruce _Concur_ Garner _Dissent_ Pickering _Concur_
Carrell _Concur_ Greco _Concur_ Sartorius _Dissent_

Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall _Dissent_
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Concur_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe _Concur_ Lee _Concur_ White Absent

Dowling _Concur_ Marcinak _Dissent_ Wilson _Concur_

**DISSENTING OPINION**

**CASE 2023-22**

**March 24, 2025**
**By RE Jim Eggert, and joined by RE Caleb Stegall, RE Fred Marcinak,**

**RE John Maynard, and TE Dave Garner**

The higher courts owe great deference to the lower courts regarding “factual
matters which the lower court is more competent to determine, because of its
proximity to the events in question, and because of its personal knowledge and
observations of the parties and witnesses involved” as well as “matters of
discretion and judgment which can only be addressed by a court with familiar
acquaintance of the events and parties.” ( _BCO_ 39-3.2 and 39-3.3). A
Presbytery’s conclusions at a trial about the credibility of witnesses, the
personal observation of the parties involved, and the censure imposed
represent exactly the kind of judgment and discretion that higher courts should
be hesitant to overturn.

<a id="ga52-p935"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=937 printed_page=935 -->

In this case, Presbytery brought an indictment and, having afforded TE Suh
the opportunity to defend himself, conducted a full trial in which he was found
guilty.

The guilty verdict is significant. If the trial itself was unfair, then the right of
appeal permitted this court to remedy any such wrong. But the Decision of the
SJC, reversing as it does the “strong presumption of guilt” per _BCO_ 31-2, sets
aside all the lower court’s subsequent proceedings, including Presbytery’s
judgment, and therefore never reaches the merits of the evidence presented at
the trial and the conclusion reached by the lower court based on that evidence.

But it is difficult to conclude that Presbytery had no legitimate ground for
finding a “strong presumption of guilt” per _BCO_ 31-2 when Presbytery had
found _actual guilt_ after a full trial.

Actual guilt after indictment and trial is the product of higher scrutiny than the
finding of a mere “strong presumption of guilt” under _BCO_ 31-2. The finding
of actual guilt after a full adjudication and opportunity for both sides to be
heard should supersede the Decision’s concerns about Appellant’s alleged lack
of opportunity to provide “satisfactory explanations” about a “report affecting
his Christian character” as well as Presbytery finding a “strong presumption of
guilt.” are wet o suppose in retrospect that a pres byte ry that has found a man
guilty after a full trial would have found no “strong presumption of guilt” if
only it had conducted its proceedings antecedent to the trial differently, even
though such antecedent proceedings are subject to _lower_ standards than a trial?
It seems that any errors of an antecedent investigation, even significant errors,
should be sufficiently mitigated by the protections afforded by a trial, and that
any errors that might occur in a trial would appear to be sufficiently mitigated
by the fact that trials are subject to review of the higher courts via appeal. Yet
the Decision “reverses [the judgment and censure] in whole” based on the
claimed errors it identifies in the _antecedent proceedings_ rather than the trial
where the facts were more fully explored with the accused being given a fuller
opportunity to test the evidence and be heard.

While we concede at least the possibility that antecedent investigatory
proceedings might be so infected by errors as to render a resulting conviction
and censure a reversible travesty, such a finding should be vanishingly rare
where a trial has been conducted and, for the reasons set out herein, this is not
an exceptional case.

<a id="ga52-p936"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=938 printed_page=936 -->

**I.** **PRESBYTERY COMMITTED NO ERROR IN ITS PRE-**

**INVESTIGATION**

We disagree with the Decision’s claims that Presbytery erred in its preliminary
investigation by (1) transgressing Matthew 18 and _BCO_ 27-5 and (2) deeming
the Petition as a “report” rather than a “charge.”

**A.** **Matthew** **18** **and** _**BCO**_ **27-5** **Were** **Not**

**Transgressed.**

This matter began when three Teaching Elders brought what was styled a
“Formal Petition” to the Presbytery (“the Petition”). The subject line of the
Petition was identified as: “RE: A formal charge of financial misconduct
against TE Sam Suh.” The first sentence of the Petition began, “It is with
humble and sad hearts that we bring a formal charge against TE Sam Jung Suh
regarding financial misconduct with church funds during his time as Senior
Pastor of Korean First Presbyterian Church,” referencing a “publicly broadcast
and recorded speech, dated July 3, 2022” in which TE Suh “publicly admitted
that his wife deposited church funds from the sale of church property into her
and his personal bank accounts.” TE Suh’s YouTube apology video was
replayed at the trial, where he is recorded saying, “I made a very big, we made
a mistake, and we did wrong … I admitted my wrongdoing and respectfully
expressed my regret … That still doesn't excuse me from depositing the check
into my personal account without permission from the church. I really did
wrong. I'm sorry.” The Petition declares that TE Suh’s action was “financial
misconduct with church funds and is a chargeable offense as behavior
unbecoming of a minister of the Gospel.” The Petition continued, “We are
grieved to have to call for accountability in this matter, but we pray for a
genuine confession from Pastor Suh with an appropriate response from our
Presbytery.”

The Decision holds that Presbytery erred when it “failed to require that those
bringing charges do so in compliance with _BCO_ 27-5” and because Presbytery
“abandoned the original charge as untrue, then without warrant, expanded to
matters having nothing to do with the specificity of original report.” We
disagree.

<a id="ga52-p937"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=939 printed_page=937 -->

The Decision maintains that the Petitioners transgressed _BCO_ 27-5 and
Matthew 18 because, having “no context for what they saw and heard on the
YouTube video,” had “inadvertently misrepresented TE Suh’s comments” as
TE Suh apologizing for taking money from “the sale of church property” rather
than more accurately referring to “the check” TE Suh said he had “deposit[ed]
… into my personal account without permission from the church.” The
Petitioners’ “misrepresentation” was compounded, the Decision supposes, by
their failure to personally approach TE Suh about his confession since the
matter “could have been easily corrected” if only the Petitioners had inquired
of TE Suh.

The progressive personal confrontation required by Matthew 18 and _BCO_ 275 is only prescribed in cases of _personal offenses_, but the Record in this case
does not support the conclusion that the three TEs advanced the matter as if it
were a “charge” arising out of a _personal_ offense against them. Indeed, the
Decision concedes that the offenses were not personal but general offenses,
stating: “Clearly the three charging Teaching Elders were not pursuing
personal offenses, but rather were charging a public, general offense with
respect to an act they specifically identify.” “General offenses” include
“wrongs or injuries to particular individuals … considered apart” from those
individuals ( _BCO_ 29-3). Serious financial misconduct by a minister confessed
publicly to his congregation and posted to a YouTube channel would certainly
fall into that category. Nevertheless, the Decision, citing _BCO_ 31-7 and
Galatians 6, insists that the Petitioners had a duty to “restore TE Suh with
gentleness and wisdom” before advancing the “charges.” Because the Petition
presented a _general offense_, the Decision effectively contradicts _BCO_ 31-7:
“When the prosecution is instituted by the court, the previous steps required
by our Lord in the case of personal offenses are not necessary” ( _BCO_ 31-7).

Further, the Decision’s claim that the “misrepresentation of the charge was
finally corrected” after the investigation is not supported by the Record. The
Petition was not a misrepresentation; it alluded to the YouTube video, and
anyone who viewed the video would have naturally and justifiably engaged in
further inquiry into the conduct that lay beneath TE Suh’s apology. i ti snot a
higher court’s place to speculate whether the Petitioners would have been
satisfied with his explanation had they approached TE Suh personally,
particularly where the committee of Presbytery which made the same inquiry
was manifestly _not_ satisfied.

<a id="ga52-p938"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=940 printed_page=938 -->

As a public and general offense, Matthew 18 and _BCO_ 27-5 did not apply
under these circumstances, the Petitioners were free to bring the report of the
same to the attention of Presbytery and were not _required_ to engage ina
progressive personal confrontation of TE Suh as supposed by the Decision.

**B.** **Presbytery Properly Deemed the Petition a “Report”**
**Rather Than a “Charge.”**

The Decision holds that (1) Presbytery incorrectly treated “charges” as
“reports,” (2) because it “abandoned the original charge as untrue, then without
warrant, expanded to matters having nothing to do with the specificity of the
original report,” and (3) because when it treated the “charges” as “reports” it
“failed to comply with the leading direction of the _BCO_ 31-2: “To ‘demand’
from TE Suh satisfactory explanations of the report (i.e., the charge of the
Petition).” We disagree.

Notably _, BCO_ 31-2 prescribes no particular form that a “report” must take,
whether oral or written, named or anonymous. Therefore, there was no
constitutional reason prohibiting Presbytery from treating the Petition as a
“report” rather than a “charge,” even though the Petition contained the phrase
“formal charge.” After all, a “report” can always be characterized as a
“charge,” and distinguishing a “report” from a “charge” is a question of (1) the
degree of its detail and formality, (2) the intention of the person(s) bringing
the matter to the attention of the court, and (3) whether it should be deemed as
credible enough and in a sufficient form to prosecute without further
investigation.

whether a matter is sufficiently framed to justify prosecution is a “factual
finding” a swell as a “matter of discretion and judgment which can only be
addressed by a court with familiar acquaintance of the events and parties,”
decisions to which the higher courts owe “great deference” to the lower. ( _BCO_
39-3).

Indeed, our cases illustrate the repeated embarrassment involved in
categorically distinguishing “reports” and “charges.” A review of our cases
shows that allegations of varying detail, even some that have been _very_
detailed, have nevertheless been treated as mere “reports” to be investigated,
not “charges” to be tried. See e.g., Case 91-6: _Mrs. Sandra Lovelace vs._
_Northeast Presbytery_, _M21GA_, 1993, p. 185 (where the Presbytery “received

<a id="ga52-p939"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=941 printed_page=939 -->

charges” from the complainant against two TEs and two REs yet nevertheless
“investigated” them); Case 92-7: _William A. Conrad v. Central Carolina_
_Presbytery_, _M21GA_, 1993, pp. 218, 219 (where a “request” came to the
Presbytery “to establish an Investigative Committee to look into charges”
against a TE, yet the Presbytery “accepted the findings and recommendation
of the committee that there was no constitutional ground for the Presbytery to
initiate any disciplinary action” against him); Case 97-9: _Robert M. Shive vs._
_Central Carolina Presbytery_, _M26GA_, 1998, pp. 134-135 (where two women
church members “reported being greatly traumatized” by the accused, and the
Session, rather than treating the matter as a “charge,” appointed a “committee
to investigate the matter”); Case 98-2: _Complaint of St. Paul Session, et al., vs._
_Central Florida Presbytery_, _M26GA_, 1999, p. 79 (where four Ruling Elders
“brought charges” against a Teaching Elder “detailing two charges and 45
specific evidences or examples of those charges, with specific dates, times,
and places,” but the Presbytery, rather than conduct a trial of the “charges,”
decided to conduct a _BCO_ 31-2 investigation and concluded that there was no
strong presumption of guilt); Case 2009-28: _Complaint of Matt Ruff v._
_Nashville Presbytery_, _M39GA_, 2011, pp. 567-568 (where a “letter and
supporting materials exceeded 45 pages” addressing a “series of conflicts”
between a minister of the Presbytery and the complainant as well as others and
the Presbytery’s Shepherding Committee—which could easily be interpreted
as a lengthy “charge”—resulted in Presbytery’s Shepherding Committee
investigating the matter and finding “no chargeable offenses” against the
minister). As these cases illustrate, this court has yet to discover an easy bright
line test or uniformly applied standard distinguishing a “report” from a
“charge.”

The “report” in this case is less detailed than many of the precedents cited
above. It expended all of one sentence to a description of TE Suh’s
objectionable conduct, alluding to a YouTube video that would presumably
give a fuller picture of the issue. Presbytery did not err in interpreting the
Petition as a “report” requiring further investigation rather than as a “charge”
ready for prosecution.

A “report” can variously mean (1) _common talk or an account spread by_
_common talk and can include rumor_ or (2) _an account or statement (usually,_
_but not always, detailed)_ . The former version of this _BCO_ provision used the
phrase “common fame” rather than “report.” The phrase “common fame,” now
antiquated, is essentially a synonym for _scandal_, and referred to something

<a id="ga52-p940"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=942 printed_page=940 -->

that is a “matter of public talk or knowledge” or that which is “generally
known.” (“Fame, note 7,” _Oxford English Dictionary_, 1971). Charles Hodge
explained that common fame “is not any and every vague rumour,” but “must
be specific, serious, notorious, and generally believed.” _The Revised Book of_
_Discipline_, _A Commentary_, Charles Hodge (1858), page 700.

All of Hodge’s factors were met by the Petition in this case. Presbytery may
have reasonably concluded it was “generally believed” that TE Suh had
committed an offense when he had publicly apologized to his congregation for
financial “wrongdoing” that had demonstrably scandalized his congregation,
where the apology was not some “vague rumour” but was publicly available
on YouTube, where the matter had become so openly scandalous that no less
than three Teaching Elders of the Presbytery who “were completely removed
from the situation and had no context for what they saw and heard on the
video” were given to understand that TE Suh had engaged in financial
misconduct, and where the scandal promised to continue spreading to the
broader public.

Even if we assume that _BCO_ 32-2 is rightly understood to prohibit a _BCO_ 312 investigation where an individual or individuals have “laid a charge,” it was
reasonable for Presbytery to have understood the Petition in this case as a
“report” of a _general_ and _public_ offense ( _BCO_ 29-4) appropriate for
consideration under the Presbytery’s “oversight, or jurisdictional” authority
per _BCO_ 31-2 in which “the elders take the initiative according to their own
Christ-given authority for the care of believers” and over which they have a
“positive duty to exercise oversight and that regardless of how a matter of
concern arises.” Case 2008-14: _Complaint of TE Wes White v. Siouxlands_
_Presbytery_, _M38GA_, 2010, p. 135.

The circumstances of this case presented to Presbytery as a scandal. TE Suh’s
church was, by his own description, “thrown into chaos” over the events
leading up to his apology. His Clerk of Session testified that the church was in
“a very noisy and chaotic state” up until the middle of September—the Petition
is dated September 29—because there were “a number of different issues that
initially triggered the various problems” at the church in part because rumors
were passing through the congregation that Pastor Suh allegedly
“misappropriated the downpayment from the sale of Korean First Presbyterian
Church’s Suwanee property, and all the members of the church knew about
it.” That “number of different issues” included the work of a transition

<a id="ga52-p941"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=943 printed_page=941 -->

committee appointed at the time of TE Suh’s recent retirement whose “main
purpose was to investigate the previous 7 years of TE Suh’s finances.” TE Suh
admitted that “The combination of [his] successor’s resignation and the rumors
about [his] and his wife’s alleged financial improprieties caused great
confusion and turmoil in the church.”

The Decision holds that “since the _BCO_ 31-2 investigation of the ‘report’ had
abandoned the original report as untrue, there was no possibility of a strong
presumption of guilt, and thus no antecedent condition necessary to institute
process.” But this overlooks that the Petition referred to the confession in the
YouTube video that suggested the necessity of further investigation.

Under these circumstances the Presbytery committed no “clear error” when it
treated the Petition as a “report,” rather than as a “charge,” and appointed a
committee to investigate the nature and scope of the scandal that had given rise
to the Petition before deciding whether there was a “strong presumption of
guilt” justifying formal charges. ( _BCO_ 31-2).

**II.** **PRESBYTERY COMMITTED NO CLEAR ERROR IN**

**ITS INVESTIGATION**

The Decision maintains that Presbytery failed to carry out its _BCO_ 31-2
investigation with “due diligence.” We disagree.

**A.** **Presbytery did not err in its investigation with**
**respect to** _**BCO**_ **31-8**

The decision holds that Presbytery “failed to correct its Investigative
Committee (IC) for violations of … _BCO_ 31-8.” That provisions states:

Great caution ought to be exercised in receiving accusations
from any person who is known to indulge a malignant spirit
towards the accused; who is not of good character; who is
himself under censure or process; who is deeply interested in
any respect in the conviction of the accused; or who is known
to be litigious, rash or highly imprudent.

The Record does not demonstrate that the IC violated this provision.

<a id="ga52-p942"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=944 printed_page=942 -->

The IC minutes show that the IC met with four members of KFPC’s transition

committee, including Seok Joo Lee. Far from showing the characteristics of a
_BCO_ 31-8 accuser, Seok Joo Lee in his testimony later given to the judicial
commission at trial insisted on reading a written petition for the judicial
commission asking for leniency for TE Suh, conduct inconsistent with the
characteristics of a _BCO_ 31-8 witness.

Besides this, TE Suh’s claim that these men had “recently departed the
church,” does not obviously identify any particular _BCO_ 31-8 factor.

Further, the Decision does not demonstrate from the Record that the IC did _not_
exercise “great caution in receiving accusations” from them unless it simply
means that _BCO_ 31-8 flatly prohibited the IC from interviewing these
individuals at all, a blanket preclusion that cannot be derived from the text of
_BCO_ 31-8 itself. The IC minutes show that the IC not only spent time
interviewing four members of KFPC’s transition committee, but also spent
considerable time interviewing the Session, reviewing documents it received,
as well as interviewing TE Suh and his wife, all of which were evidence of and
consistent with the exercise of “great caution.”

Among the four Ruling Elders identified, the only one the Record reveals a
colorable case for having _BCO_ 31-8 characteristics was a Ruling Elder, who
not only met with the IC but also testified at the trial. But the Record fails to
demonstrate that the Appellant brought _BCO_ 31-8 information about this
Ruling Elder to the attention of the IC Committee during its investigation, so
the IC cannot be faulted for failing to implement _BCO_ 31-8 considerations
toward that witness since the IC could not exercise “great caution” if the
Record does not show that Appellant timely advised the IC that he allegedly
had _BCO_ 31-8 characteristics. The Record shows that TE Suh’s warnings only
came later when the matter was pending before the _judicial commission_ some
six months _after_ the IC report was received by the Presbytery. Therefore, the
Record does not support that Presbytery compromised the _preliminary_
_investigation_ by entertaining known _BCO_ 31-8 witnesses as supposed by the
Decision.

**B.** **The IC did not clearly err by failing to meet with**
**Appellant or his wife until late in its investigation.**

<a id="ga52-p943"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=945 printed_page=943 -->

Another asserted error of the Decision: “The IC did not meet with TE Suh until

late in its investigation rather than as the focus of it as required by _BCO_ 31-2.”
This alleged error is not supported by our Constitution.

Since _BCO_ 31-2 does not prescribe what order the investigation must follow,
“great deference” is owed to the Presbytery in the order of investigation that it
chooses and is reversible only upon a showing of “clear error” ( _BCO_ 39-3). In
this case, the IC made no “clear error” investigating TE Suh’s July 3 apology
and his alleged “financial misconduct” arising out of the “Formal Petition”
before meeting with the Appellant. Although _BCO_ 31-2 requires that the court
“demand satisfactory explanations” from the subject of the report, the “focus
required” does not bestow any right to chronological primacy in favor of
interviewing the accused first in a committee’s investigatory process.

_BCO_ 31-2 investigations require a twin “focus” on both a “report” and a
“satisfactory explanation.” The Decision seems to emphasize the latter to the
detriment of the former. An investigating court will be wise to first “focus” on
a “report,” which, depending on the circumstances, may prioritize it hearing
statements made by others or reviewing other pertinent information to better
understand the nature of the report so that when it meets with the subject of
the report it can then “focus” on whether he is providing a “satisfactory
explanation” about the report. For such reasons, and depending on the
circumstances, it may be best for the investigating court to meet with the
subject of a report “late in its investigation.” At what point and under what
circumstances the “focus” should be on the “report” as opposed to the
“satisfactory explanation” is a matter of discretion and judgment to which the
higher court’s owe great deference to the lower courts. Moreover, when the
matter finally returned to the Presbytery via the IC’s report, the Presbytery
gave TE Suh an opportunity to address the Presbytery about the report.
Presbytery committed no “clear error” in this case regarding the ordering of its
investigation. ( _BCO_ 39-3).

**C.** **The IC did not clearly err in the meeting that it did**
**eventually have with TE Suh and his wife.**

The Decision also finds error in the IC’s failure to provide TE Suh its
“compiled questions” to TE Suh in advance of its meeting with him so that he
could provide “satisfactory explanations.” But nothing in _BCO_ 31-2 strictly
requires that a committee must provides u ch information to a person

<a id="ga52-p944"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=946 printed_page=944 -->

investigated before such a meeting, and such organizational issues regarding
the conduct of the investigation are matters of discretion to which great
deference is owed to the Presbytery. The Record recounts that at that meeting
the IC “presented initial findings regarding the allegations against him and
asked for TE Suh’s explanation as to each item,” a course that is on its face
consistent with the requirements of _BCO_ 31-2.

The Decision also holds that Presbytery erred by meeting with TE Suh “only
once,” even “though the matters under discussion were complex and
extensive.” The number of times that a court meets with the subject of an
investigation is a matter of discretion and judgment which can only be reversed
upon a showing of “clear error,” since the lower court is in “proximity to the
events in question, and because of its personal knowledge and observations of
the parties and witnesses involved” and “can only be addressed by a court with
familiar acquaintance of the events and parties” ( _BCO_ 39-3.2-3). Because of
these considerations, the lower court is in the best position to judge whether a
single or multiple meetings with the subject of the report would be productive.

The Record shows that the IC met with TE Suh and his wife for three hours

and eleven minutes. The Record also reflects that Presbytery listened to TE
Suh at the Presbytery meeting where the IC report was delivered. These facts
do not support a finding of “clear error” in this case.

More fundamentally, the Decision mistakenly treats these alleged errors of
Presbytery as though they are governed by _BCO_ 39-3.4, Constitutional errors
for which no deference is owed to the discretion of the lower court. That is

incorrect. _BCO_ 31-2 does not prescribe precisely how a _BCO_ 31-2
investigation must be conducted, and therefore the conduct of such
investigations are matters of “discretion and judgment” and Presbyteries have
great discretion regarding them, subject to reversal only upon finding “clear
error” as set out in _BCO_ 39-3.3. Furthermore, if the court commits something
less than a “clear error” in the antecedent proceedings, the risk of harm is
mitigated by the accused’s opportunity to be fully heard and rebut any
evidence adduced against him at a trial.

**D.** **The Record does not show that Presbytery**
**indicted TE Suh regarding matters that it did not raise**
**with him.**

<a id="ga52-p945"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=947 printed_page=945 -->

The Decision holds that the IC Report “contained accusations never raised
with TE Suh,” but this is not supported by the Record. The IC Report states
that it reviewed “eleven separate suspicions” with TE Suh. These suspicions
appear to include conduct bearing on the charges that were ultimately brought
against him.

**E.** **Presbytery did not clearly err by failing to require**
**its IC to include what explanations TE Suh had offered**
**as satisfactory explanations, and why the IC found those**
**explanations to be unsatisfactory.**

The Decision holds that Presbytery erred by failing to “require that the IC set
before it what explanations TE Suh had offered as satisfactory explanations
concerning the Petition’s report, and why the IC found those explanations
unsatisfactory.”

_BCO_ 31-2 does not prescribe the form that an investigative committee’s report
must take. Nothing in _BCO_ 31-2 required that the IC report “include TE Suh’s
explanations,” and the IC was not required to generate a document satisfying
TE Suh. Presumably any _BCO_ 31-2 investigatory report recommending a
“strong presumption of guilt” will in some way be deemed deficient by any
accused person who believes that he or she has provided or can provide a
“satisfactory explanation.” The minutes of the meeting where the IC report
was delivered show that TE Suh was afforded an opportunity to respond to the
Presbytery and therefore did have an opportunity to present a “satisfactory
explanation.” TE Suh even admits that he circulated his own written response
to the report both before and at the meeting. the record doe snot a support a
finding of “clear error” in this regard.

_**Conclusion**_

The Decision reverses the Presbytery’s judgment after a full trial, but it does
so based solely on objections to the Presbytery’s management of its
preliminary handling of the alleged “charge” and its antecedent investigation
of the same. Although other Presbyteries may have managed the matter
differently, Presbytery’s chosen course did not rise to the level of “clear error,”
the standard applicable to this case. Moreover, the Decision’s focus on claimed
deficiencies in the antecedent investigation overlooks that the trial afforded a
full opportunity for both sides to be heard. The trial resulted in a finding of TE

<a id="ga52-p946"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=948 printed_page=946 -->

Suh’s guilt, apparently vindicating Presbytery’s antecedent finding of a strong
presumption of guilt after its preliminary investigation.

For the reasons set out above, it is our view that the Decision fails to afford
Presbytery the deference rightly due to its judgment regarding this contested
matter. Another court, had it sat as judge in the original trial of this case, may
have reached a different conclusion about the guilt of TE Suh and, if guilt was
found, the appropriate censure. But as a reviewing court, the SJC is limited in
that it may only reverse errors arising out of “factual matters” and “matters of
discretion and judgment” when such can be demonstrated to be “clear errors,”
a standard that was not met in this case. In the end, this case would have been
better disposed of based on the merits of the charges considered at the trial,
and any specifications of error relating to them, but today’s Decision has cut
short such considerations.

The Decision holds that “the pre-investigative phase, the _BCO_ 31-2
investigation, and the commencement of process were marred by a number of
grave Constitutional errors,” adding that this finding “fully justifies the
vindication of the Appellant, and a decision to reverse in whole.” We would
have preferred that the SJC had also considered the alleged errors in the trial,
where TE Suh vigorously contested the charges against him.

This Dissent only maintains that the SJC should have afforded deference to the
Presbytery’s decisions and judgment about its pre-investigative phase as well
as its antecedent investigation. It is not offered as an opinion about the merits
of the case made against TE Suh at trial While Presbytery’s verdict at trial
affords retrospective support for Presbytery’s having found a “strong
presumption of guilt” that justified it bringing a case of process, it does not
necessarily follow that the evidence adduced at trial was sufficient to establish
TE Suh’s actual guilt or to justify the censure imposed. Because the Decision
has “restored [TE Suh] to his standing in KSEP as it was before Presbytery
entertained the ‘charges,’” and because “no judicial process, with respect to
any matter in the proceedings reversed, is permitted hereafter,” it is now
impossible for any court of the Church to conduct a case of process and
formally adjudicate the merits of the charges for which TE Suh stood trial. For
that reason, this Dissent takes no position about TE Suh’s guilt or the propriety
of Presbytery’s chosen censure against him.

<a id="ga52-p947"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=949 printed_page=947 -->

**DISSENT**

**CASE 2023-22**

**TE SAM JUNG SUH V. KOREAN SOUTHEASTERN PRESBYTERY**

**March 24, 2025**
**TE Art Sartorius**

i submit this dissent noting first that i agree with the dissent filed by re jim
Eggert and others (Eggert Dissent). The reason, however, for not joining that
Dissent and filing separately, is to speak directly to one particular matter
peculiar to this case.

There is no dispute that this case was initiated when three Teaching Elders
submitted to the Presbytery what was styled a “Formal Petition.” The subject
line of that document further identified what was being submitted as a “formal
charge of financial misconduct against TE Sam Suh.”

The SJC Decision holds, in part, that the Presbytery incorrectly treated what
were “charges” as “reports.” The Eggert Dissent suggests, alternatively, that
there “was no constitutional reason prohibiting Presbytery from treating the
Petition as a ‘report’ rather than a ‘charge.’” While I agree with the Eggert
Dissent, I do so only based upon the specific facts of this case.

This separate Dissent is written so as to emphasize, that as a general rule, a
Presbytery would have no discretion to convert what are truly “charges” and
which should be tried into “reports” regarding one’s Christian character ( _BCO_
31-2), but even so, though the Petition when filed used the word “charge” what
was contained in the body of the Petition was not a charge at all. It was a
presentation of what were commonly and publicly known matters—reports.

_BCO_ 32-2, states:

32-2. Process against an offender shall not be commenced
unless some person or persons undertake to make out the
charge; or unless the court finds it necessary, for the honor of
religion, itself to take the step provided for in _BCO_ 31-2.

In my view, this provision presents two separate, distinct, and intentionally
bifurcated avenues of commencing judicial process. The first avenue is for
“some person or persons to undertake to make out the charge” (the first clause

<a id="ga52-p948"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=950 printed_page=948 -->

of _BCO_ 32-2). The second avenue, (which is again separate and distinct from
the first) is for the Court of original jurisdiction over a particular matter to
itself “take the step provided for in _BCO_ 31-2.” To that extent I would agree
with the SJC analysis.

Yet, the Eggert Dissent has also described some of the difficulties which have
existed when church courts try to distinguish whether a charge is being laid or
a report is being presented; but more importantly, in the body of the Petition
submitted—which in this case led to the Presbytery initiating a _BCO_ 31-2
investigation—was the primary reference to an internet “link” of “a publicly
broadcast and recorded speech” by the man whose Christian character was in
question. In that speech the man publicly apologized to his congregation for
financial “wrongdoing.”

This seems to definitionally be the very type report of what was once referred
to as “a common fame report” envisioned in _BCO_ 31-2.

Again—the chief purpose of this Dissent is to emphasis that while normally, a
Presbytery should have no true discretion to convert what are truly “charges”
( _BCO_ 32-2) into “reports” ( _BCO_ 31-2) the facts of the case show why it is that
it deviates from that norm.

<a id="ga52-p949"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=951 printed_page=949 -->

**CASE 2023-23**

**COMPLAINT OF MUELLER V. SOUTH FLORIDA PRESBYTERY**

On December 21, 2023 the SJC received a letter signed by the Complainant,
the Presbytery Clerk, and the Chairman of Presbytery's Judicial Commission
indicating the Complaint is withdrawn by agreement of the parties and will be
heard by Presbytery. Without objection, the SJC approved the request, and the
matter has been remanded without instructions and without prejudice.

**CASE 2024-01**

**TE ANDY MOEHN**

**v.**

**WESTMINSTER PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON COMPLAINT**

**March 6, 2025**

**I.** **SUMMARY OF THE FACTS**

Fall 2022 The Session of Walnut Hill Presbyterian Church (WHPC) had
discussions with TE Andy Moehn (pastor of WHPC)
regarding his concerns about the state of his marriage. Equine
couples therapy was begun.

02/16/23 The Session of WHPC met with TE and Mrs. Moehn to

discuss the state of their marriage and correspondence each
had sent to Session. The ruling elders of WHPC had several
other meetings in February to consider the state of the
marriage.

03/06/23 TE Moehn wrote to the Session of WHPC to follow up on a
discussion at a Session meeting, apparently held on March 2.
In that letter he shared thoughts on the state of his marriage
and his reflections on the question of whether his wife was
guilty of willfully deserting him.

<a id="ga52-p950"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=952 printed_page=950 -->

03/09/23 WHPC Session meeting was summarized as “Firm discussion
of the RE Session’s [ _sic_ ] letter to TE Andy Moehn concerning
mediation and potential marital reconciliation options.”

Apr-Jun 23 WHPC ruling elders had at least eight meetings dealing with
the Moehn matter. Two of those meetings included TE and
Mrs. Moehn, one included the Moehn children, and one
included a couple who had been functioning as “informal
marital counsellors.” Also included is a discussion of “the

therapy process with Dr. Arnold.”

06/26/23 The WHPC ruling elders met with TE and Mrs. Moehn, and
one of the “informal counsellors.” The meeting summary
records “Significant discussion of the marital situation with
all parties. Presentation of the Session’s findings and a
discussion of potential options moving forward.”

07/03/23 TE Moehn wrote to the Session of WHPC to inform them he

believed he had biblical grounds for divorce from his wife,
Kacey Moehn. He alleged “prolonged emotional and
relational desertion and neglect by my wife” and he requested
“an investigation into the matter, per _BCO_ 31-2.”

07/03/23 The Clerk of Session of WHPC forwarded TE Moehn’s

request to the Clerk of Westminster Presbytery (WP). The
Clerk of Session stated that it was the understanding of
Session that the question of whether TE Moehn had biblical
grounds for divorce was a matter for Presbytery. He further
stated that Session understood it had jurisdiction over
questions of unrepentant sin in the life of Mrs. Moehn. He
went on, however, to state “due to the session’s closeness to
this matter and the role we have taken over the last several

months to counsel and shepherd TE Moehn and his wife and
their children, we as a session would like to ask the
Shepherding Committee and Presbytery to take original
jurisdiction over these matters as a whole.”

08/01/23 WP held a called meeting to consider the communication from
TE Moehn and the request from the WHPC Session.

<a id="ga52-p951"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=953 printed_page=951 -->

Presbytery voted to erect an eight-member committee, to be
appointed by the Moderator, to investigate the matter and
report back to Presbytery. The minutes also record that the
Moderator appointed the eight men after the meeting.

08/08/23 - The “Moehn Committee” (MC) met ten times. It conducted
12/16/23 interviews with the WHPC Session, TE Moehn, Mrs. Moehn,
the four Moehn children (together), a therapist who worked
with Mrs. Moehn, a therapist who worked with TE Moehn,
and an equine therapist who had worked with TE and Mrs.
Moehn together, as well as, more recently, Mrs. Moehn
separately.

MC also considered an undated document titled “Findings of
the Walnut Hill Session,” wherein the Session concluded both
TE Moehn and Mrs. Moehn were “professing believers;” the
“marital relationship is dysfunctional;” and “as sinners saved
by grace, you both have contributed to this dysfunctional
marital relationship.” The document went on to set forth
Session’s understanding of the specific failures of each
partner. It then quoted the following from “The PCA
Guidelines on Divorce and Remarriage,” “The elder should
instruct the couple that, excepting sexual immorality, divorce
is not an option for two people professing Christ.” In light of
the above, the document advised the couple that their options
were to “seek and pursue reconciliation” or “file charges
against your spouse.” With regard to the latter, the document
concluded, “In the event allegations of abuse or desertion are
brought pursuant to I Corinthians 7, and the appropriate
grounds are found to support the allegation, church discipline
will be exercised. If the guilty spouse remains unrepentant,
eventual excommunication of your spouse will be necessary
before you would biblically be free to divorce pursuant to I
Corinthians 7:15.” It is not clear if this document is a

reflection of the 03/09/23 Session meeting, the 06/26/23
meeting of the ruling elders, or some other meeting.

As part of their work, MC also reviewed the report of the
PCA’s _Ad Interim_ Committee on Divorce and Remarriage,

<a id="ga52-p952"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=954 printed_page=952 -->

and relevant portions of the report of the _Ad Interim_
Committee on Domestic Abuse and Sexual Assault. The MC

shared progress reports at the WP stated meetings of August
12 and November 11.

11/11/23 a commission of wp dealing with a matter involving another
teaching elder reported it had “approved a definition for
Biblical divorce based on WCF 24.6.” Part of that

“definition” included the statement “Willful Desertion: This

refers to a situation where an unbelieving spouse willingly
abandons the marriage and reconciliation is impossible.”

02/13/24 MC reported at a called meeting of WP. The MC report
recognized the extreme difficulties in the marriage, at least
some of which were, in the opinion of the MC, attributable to
a mental health issue with which they concluded Mrs. Moehn
had been diagnosed. The MC also concluded that, while TE
Moehn was not blameless, he and the children had had to
endure much over the course of the marriage.

MC then shared four recommendations, each of which
Presbytery adopted, albeit after amending recommendations
1, 2, and 4. The final language of the two recommendations at
issue in this Complaint, as adopted by WP, was as follows:

Recommendation 1: “As a result of our investigation
into the above accusations made by Andy Moehn
towards Kacey Moehn, we, the Moehn committee, do
not believe there to be a strong presumption of guilt.”
The vote on the amended recommendation was 13
11.

Recommendation 3: “Because we do not find a strong
presumption of guilt, it renders the question of the
biblical grounds of the divorce moot. Therefore, we
recommend that this concludes the investigation into
Kasey [ _sic_ ] Moehn as referred to Westminster
Presbytery from Walnut Hill.” No counted vote on
this recommendation was recorded.

<a id="ga52-p953"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=955 printed_page=953 -->

WP also voted to “receive the report [of the MC] as
a whole.”

02/28/04 TE Moehn and others complained against the above actions
of WP, alleging that it erred in adopting recommendations 1
and 3, and setting forth 10 supporting reasons for the
Complaint.

03/09/24 WP considered the Complaint. During the discussion of the
Complaint Mrs. Moehn was recognized. With no one voicing
objection, Mrs. Moehn was allowed to respond to questions
that were addressed to her. The Complaint was denied by a
vote of 16-9.

03/19/24 The Session of WHPC held a called meeting that included
the following:

1) Request from Kacey Moehn to withdraw membership from
Walnut Hill and request from TE Moehn regarding trial
a. The Session has scheduled a meeting with GA

Moderator Fred Greco on Thursday, 3/21 to
discuss the potential legalities of Kacey’s request.
This will be a called meeting and the Session will
make a final decision after the conversation with

the Moderator.

b. Andy Moehn is suspending his request for a trial

pending the decision of the session concerning
Kacey’s membership status.

03/22/24 The Session of WHPC held a called meeting [although it does
not appear that a TE was present ( _BCO_ 12-2)]. The following
action was recorded:

2) The Session has accepted Kacey Moehn’s request to
withdraw her membership from Walnut Hill Presbyterian
Church effective February 15, 2024 per _BCO_ 38-3(a) noting
the following irregularities:

<a id="ga52-p954"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=956 printed_page=954 -->

a. Kacey Moehn has joined Celebration Church, a
nondenominational body that does not recognize
normal transfer of membership between evangelical
churches.

b. Kacey Moehn is the wife of a teaching elder in the
Presbyterian Church in America.
c. Kacey Moehn testified publicly at the March 9,
2024 stated meeting of Westminster Presbytery her
intent to not return to Walnut Hill Presbyterian
Church.

03/27/24 TE Moehn, _et al_, elevated their Complaint to General
Assembly.

04/30/24 WP held a called meeting at which it appointed Respondents
and adopted reasoning in support of the actions taken by
Presbytery on 02/13/24.

06/26/24 The officers of the SJC determined the Complaint to be
administratively in order and assigned it to a Panel consisting
of TEs Evans, Landry, and Dodson (alt) and REs Neikirk and
White (alt.)

07/25/24 The Panel held its third meeting and voted to finalize the
Record of the Case. As part of this decision the Panel added
to the Record of the Case (ROC) 16 documents requested by
Complainant. Respondent had no objection to these
documents being added. It was also reported that both parties
had agreed to an expedited hearing date. Subsequent to this
meeting, and within the time limits established by the
expedited hearing schedule, both parties filed briefs.

09/09/24 The hearing was held via Zoom. TE Moehn represented
Complainants, and Complainant TE Will Barbour was also
present. TEs Andrew Wyatt and Richard Steele represented
Respondent Presbytery.

<a id="ga52-p955"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=957 printed_page=955 -->

09/13/24 The Panel voted to sustain the Complaint in part and to direct
the Presbytery to conduct a new investigation based on new
evidence that was presented.

09/30/24 Presbytery’s Representatives filed a Supplemental Brief
asking for a rehearing of the matter, arguing the Panel’s
proposed decision was based on incorrect information or a
misunderstanding of Presbytery’s actions.

10/17-18/24 The full SJC denied the request for rehearing and considered
the Panel’s proposed decision on this case. The SJC voted to
remand the case to the Panel for a new hearing that would
focus on the following questions:

1. Presbytery’s understanding of 1 Corinthians 7
and _WCF_ 24.6.

2. Whether TE Moehn disagrees with Presbytery’s
understanding of 1 Corinthians 7 and _WCF_ 24.6.
3. Having the parties present and defend their
positions on the meaning and application of 1
Corinthians 7 and _WCF_ 24.6.

4. How Presbytery’s understanding of 1 Corinthians
7 and _WCF_ 24.6 impacted its actions on the
Moehn Committee Report; and
5. Whether any portion of the Complaint is now
moot given Mrs. Moehn’s departure from the
PCA, or for any other reason.

11/11/24 The Chairman of the Panel notified the parties of the decision
of the SJC. He also notified the parties of their right to propose
additional material for the ROC if they deemed that necessary,
and of their right to file additional briefs discussing the
questions posed by the SJC.

01/09/25 The Panel, having ascertained that neither party believed any
additions to the Record were necessary, and having received
briefs from both parties, held the second hearing on the Case
via Zoom. TE Andrew Moehn represented Complainants. TEs
Andrew Wyatt and Richard Steele represented Respondent
Presbytery.

<a id="ga52-p956"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=958 printed_page=956 -->

**II.** **STATEMENT OF THE ISSUES**

1. Did WP err in its _BCO_ 31-2 investigation of Mrs. Moehn?
2. Did WP err in determining that the results of the _BCO_ 31-2
investigation of Mrs. Moehn render moot the question of
whether TE Moehn had biblical grounds for
divorce?

3. Would a new _BCO_ 31-2 investigation of Mrs. Moehn be in
order?

**III.** **JUDGMENT**

1. Yes.

2. Yes.

3. No.

**IV.** **REASONING AND OPINION**

Judgment 1
The Panel finds that Westminster Presbytery (WP) erred in four separate ways
in conducting the _BCO_ 31-2 investigation of Kacey Moehn.

First, WP erred by hampering their own investigation with an extra
confessional understanding of _WCF_ 24.6.

Although the corruption of man be such as is apt to study arguments
unduly to put asunder those whom God hath joined together in
marriage: yet, nothing but adultery, or such willful desertion as can no
way be remedied by the church, or civil magistrate, is cause sufficient
of dissolving the bond of marriage: wherein, a public and orderly
course of proceeding is to be observed; and the persons concerned in
it not left to their own wills, and discretion, in their own case.

The Presbytery does not believe the desertion clause applies to a professing
Christian. They believe that in order for desertion by a Christian spouse to be
a valid reason for divorce, such a person would have to be excommunicated

<a id="ga52-p957"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=959 printed_page=957 -->

by the church prior to a divorce. The Confession, however, simply refers to the
act of desertion and not the spiritual state of the one who has deserted his
spouse (in a similar fashion, the Confession also speaks simply of the act of
adultery and not the spiritual state of the one who commits adultery). This extra
confessional understanding created a standard of guilt for the Moehn
Committee that Mrs. Moehn would never meet so long as she continued to
hold what they perceived to be a credible profession of faith regardless of the
choices she made.

Second, WP erred by dismissing Mrs. Moehn’s physical separation from TE
Moehn as evidence of desertion. The Moehn Committee was wrong when it
reported to the Presbytery that the fact of Mrs. Moehn moving out of the family
home was “outside of the scope of the accusations and the investigation.”
Clearly, a spouse moving out of the family home and into anew home, not to
mention accompanying that move with allegedly inequitable financial and
child-care responsibilities, must at least be considered in determining whether
that spouse may be guilty of desertion.

Third, Presbytery erred in not having before it all relevant evidence that would
bear on the accuracy of the conclusions reached through the _BCO_ 31-2
investigation. In particular, the Moehn Committee chose not to interview Dr.
Carolyn Arnold, the therapist who directed the Intensive Marriage therapy for
the Moehns in April 2023. She was the last counselor who worked with the
Moehns together. Surely, then, her conclusions regarding the Moehns’
relationship and Mrs. Moehn’s commitment to the marriage would have been
critical to ascertaining whether Mrs. Moehn might be guilty of desertion and
whether TE Moehn might have biblical grounds for divorce.

Fourth, WP erred in its 31-2 investigation of Mrs. Moehn by increasingly
turning its focus to TE Moehn, ultimately placing a significant portion of the
blame on him and his actions, which they believe led to the dissolution of his
marriage. Along with the Session of Walnut Hill and WP, the Panel recognizes
generally that both spouses share some blame when a marriage deteriorates.
The result of the investigation of Mrs. Moehn, however, led WP to adjudicate
informally TE Moehn’s role in the marriage’s demise without his having the
benefit of the protections offered by formal process or even a _BCO_ 31-2
investigation. Several examples should suffice:

<a id="ga52-p958"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=960 printed_page=958 -->

- The Moehn Committee believes that if TE Moehn had pursued Mrs.
Moehn, neither the Moehns nor the Presbytery would be in this
situation today.

- WP blames TE Moehn for desiring to end the marriage, despite the
ROC indicating that both TE Moehn and Mrs. Moehn had
communicated to Walnut Hill’s Session their desire to seek a divorce

- WP finds it difficult to fault Mrs. Moehn for moving out of the family
home since TE Moehn slept in a different bedroom and asked for the
investigation.

- WP asserts that TE Moehn created an intolerable situation for Mrs.

Moehn at Walnut Hill PCA.

WP could have concluded their investigation of Mrs. Moehn and then
recommended that WP begin a new investigation of TE Moehn, but they did
not do so despite the many accusations made against him and his Christian
character that are now public.

Judgment 2
TE Moehn’s original request to the Session of Walnut Hill PCA included an
inquiry into whether or not he would have grounds for divorce. WP, having
consented to Session’s request that Presbytery take jurisdiction over these
matters, and having determined there was no strong presumption of guilt with
regard to Mrs. Moehn, concluded “Because we do not find a strong
presumption of guilt, it renders the question of the biblical grounds of the
divorce moot.”

While the word “moot” can have various meanings, it appears from the context
and by the arguments put forth by Presbytery’s representatives that
Presbytery’s conclusion was “there is no need to discuss whether TE Moehn
has grounds for divorce because Mrs. Moehn continues to be a professing
Christian and our _BCO_ 31-2 investigation gives us no reason to question the
credibility of that profession.” But, by extension, that means Presbytery’s
advice, albeit implicit, was that TE Moehn does not have biblical grounds for
divorce. In their briefs and arguments, both parties recognized this conclusion.
That determination, in turn, rests on Presbytery’s extra-confessional reading
of _WCF_ 24.6. Further, even if Presbytery’s understanding of _WCF_ 24.6 were
correct, the other errors in the conduct of Presbytery’s investigation would
render this conclusion suspect.

<a id="ga52-p959"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=961 printed_page=959 -->

Finally, all parties agree that a determinative finding with regard to whether
TE Moehn has biblical grounds for divorce could only be arrived at after an
ecclesiastical trial, not simply as a result of an investigation. This is not to say,
however, that WP did not have the right to render advice to TE Moehn. But
that advice, when taken as a formal action of Presbytery, must be grounded in
a proper understanding of Scripture and our Constitutional Standards, and on
a proper investigation. Given the errors noted above, WP erred in stating that
lack of a strong presumption of guilt with regard to Mrs. Moehn rendered the
question of whether TE Moehn had biblical grounds for divorce moot,
particularly when the thrust of that conclusion clearly indicated that the
meaning was that TE Moehn did not have biblical grounds for divorce. This
error was compounded by the fact that so much of the Moehn Committee’s
report dealt with TE Moehn, who was not the subject of any formal
investigation, and by the fact that the results were reported and discussed in
open session, which could only harm the reputation of both parties.

Judgment 3
The Session of Walnut Hill PCA allowed Mrs. Moehn to remove her

membership and join a nondenominational church. WP no longer has any
jurisdiction over Mrs. Moehn, and it would be improper for WP formally to
conduct a new investigation into her Christian character.

A Final Note on the Judgment in this Case
