Nothing in this decision can or does render a judgment as to whether Mrs.
Moehn was or is guilty of desertion or whether TE Moehn has biblical grounds
for divorce. Our ruling asserts only that the procedures and the confessional
understanding used by Presbytery were clearly deficient, and, therefore, the
conclusions reached by Presbytery cannot stand.

**V.** **AMENDS**

The following recommendations adopted by WP on February 13, 2024 are
annulled ( _BCO_ 43-10):

Recommendation 1: “As a result of our investigation into the above
accusations made by Andy Moehn towards Kacey Moehn, we, the
Moehn committee, do not believe there to be a strong presumption of
guilt.”

<a id="ga52-p960"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=962 printed_page=960 -->

Recommendation 3: “Because we do not find a strong presumption of
guilt, it renders the question of the biblical grounds of the divorce
moot.”

**V.** **RECORDING IN MINUTES**

In accordance with _OMSJC_ 17.4(f), the full decision in this matter, as adopted
by the Standing Judicial Commission, shall be recorded in the minutes of
Presbytery, as well as a statement of how Presbytery has complied with the
amends set forth above.

The Summary of the Facts was written by RE Neikirk. The Statement of the
Issues, Judgments, and Reasoning and Opinion were written by TE Landry and
RE Neikirk. The Panel adopted the Proposed Decision by a vote of 3-0, with
both alternates stating their concurrence.

The SJC reviewed each part of the proposed Decision, and after the approval
of various amendments, the vote on the Decision was **21-1-0, with 1 absent**
**and 1 recused,** thus sustaining the Complaint.

Bankson _Concur_ Eggert _Concur_ Maynard _Concur_
Bise _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Neikirk _Concur_
Bruce _Concur_ Garner _Concur_ Pickering _Concur_
Carrell _Concur_ Greco Recused Sartorius _Concur_

Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall _Dissent_
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Concur_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe _Concur_ Lee _Concur_ White Absent
Dowling _Concur_ Marcinak _Concur_ Wilson _Concur_

**Concurring Opinion**

Case No. 2024-01

_TE Moehn v. Westminster Presbytery_

RE Howie Donahoe

March 24, 2025

I concur with the Decision but believe additional rationale could be helpful.

<a id="ga52-p961"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=963 printed_page=961 -->

1. Standard of Review—The issue under review involved the interpretation

of _WCF_ 24.6, and thus the pertinent standard of review is in _BCO_ 39-3.4.
Thus, the SJC is not obliged to exhibit any deference to the lower court's
interpretation of the Constitution. On the question of whether the husband
had grounds for divorce, Presbytery ruled the question was moot on the
sole reason that the deserting wife was a professing believer (though no
longer under PCA jurisdiction). Apparently, case closed. But the
interpretation of _WCF_ 24.6 was critical to the mootness question, and thus,
was not a matter raised sua sponte by the SJC.

2. _Westminster Confession of Faith_ 24.6 does not explicitly restrict the

desertion clause to instances where the deserting spouse is an unbeliever.

_WCF_ 24.6. Although the corruption of man be such as is apt to
study arguments unduly to put asunder those whom God hath
joined together in marriage: yet, nothing but adultery, or such
willful desertion as can no way be remedied by the Church, or
civil magistrate, is cause sufficient of dissolving the bond of
marriage: wherein, a public and orderly course of proceeding
is to be observed; and the persons concerned in it not left to
their own wills, and discretion, in their own case.

In his commentary on _WCF_ 24.6, OPC minister Dr. Chad Van Dixhoorn
observes:

Is there a particular case in Corinth [Paul] has in view [in 1 Cor
7:15-16]? Or, a likely scenario that he envisaged or frequently
encountered? o ri she limiting the 'desertion grounds' for
divorce to desertion of an unbelieving spouse only? The latter
option is difficult to accept simply because it is hard to
imagine, in a parallel case, that the Scripture would limit the
'adultery grounds' for divorce to adultery by an unbelieving
spouse only. [43]

43 _Confessing the Faith: A Reader's Guide to the Westminster Confession of Faith_

(Banner of Truth, 2014).

<a id="ga52-p962"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=964 printed_page=962 -->

In his 1961 book, _Divorce_, longtime Westminster Theological Seminary
Professor John Murray ended his analysis of the Confession's statement on
desertion with his opinion that _WCF_ 24.6 was broader than the specific
example Paul gave in 1 Corinthians 7:15. [ 44]

3. Some might contend that a contrary interpretation was adopted by the 20 [th]

General Assembly in 1992 when the Ad Interim Committee on Divorce and
Remarriage [45] ("AIC") reported. But that would not be correct.

We note first that the AIC Report was _not_ adopted by the GA. The Report
is not a "position paper" of the PCA, or even of the 20 [th] GA. The Roanoke
GA was only asked to vote on specific recommendations within the Report.
Perhaps some confusion comes from GA's adoption of AIC
Recommendation 1, which proposed: "That the General Assembly receive
the report of the Ad-Interim Committee on Divorce and Remarriage." But
Robert's Rules indicates such a motion is unnecessary. A report is
"received" when it is set before the body. AIC Recommendation 1 did not
propose the GA adopt the entire Report.

AIC Recommendation 2 proposed the Assembly adopt nine "conclusions,"
each separately, and GA did. Recommendations 2.f. and g. are most
pertinent to the desertion discussion.

f. That when an unbeliever separates from the marriage

relationship with a believer, the believer is free from that
marriage and free to remarry but only in the Lord (1 Cor.
7:15, 39).
g. That under extreme circumstances, a Session may properly

judge that such desertion (separation) has occurred, even
though the deserting spouse is still physically present in the
home ("desertion" being viewed in the sense understood in
the Committee report, Chapter 2, Section II.E.4.). [46]

Conclusion 2.f. only directly addressed one situation—that mentioned in 1
Cor. 7:15. Conclusion 2.g. contained no clause restricting desertion to
unbelieving deserters. If Presbytery's interpretation in the present Case

44 John Murray, _Divorce_ (Puritan & Reformed Books, 1961, p. 76-77)
45 _M20GA_, 1992, Roanoke, pp. 513-605
46 _M20GA_, p. 59-61; 635

<a id="ga52-p963"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=965 printed_page=963 -->

were correct, one would expect AIC Recommendation 2.g. to have said: "...
the _unbelieving_ deserting spouse. ..."

The AIC Report section referenced in 2.g is titled _"Applying Paul's_
_Instructions about Desertion Today."_ [47] That is most extended treatment on
desertion in the 94-page Report, and it doesn't express the interpretation
held by Presbytery in this present Case. If there were an obvious place for
the AIC to have driven home the point that grounds cannot exist if the
deserter is a professing believer, that would certainly have been the place
to do it.

Finally, Recommendation 3 from the AIC proposed amending _WCF_ 24.6
to codify a desertion interpretation similar to Westminster Presbytery's. But
it was _not_ adopted.

3. That the General Assembly propose the revision of 24.6 of

the Confession ... for clarity's sake to provide a more
explicit statement of the Pauline teaching by means of the
following amendment:

a. Add, after the word "desertion" the words

"of a believer by an unbeliever,"
b. Add as the concluding words of the section

the sentence: "Believers who separate for
reasons other than adultery must remain
unmarried, or else be reconciled." [48]

The Assembly Commissioners in Roanoke may well have had a variety for
reason for opposing the adoption of AIC Recommendation 3. But it's clear
that in 1992, the 20 [th] General Assembly declined to codify the interpretation
of _WCF_ 24.6 held by Westminster Presbytery in this present Case. The fact
that a deserting spouse is still a member o fa christian church doe snot
_automatically_ render moot the question of whether the deserted spouse has
"cause sufficient of dissolving the bond of marriage" ( _WCF_ 24.6).

/s/ RE Howie Donahoe

47 _M20GA_, 558-65
48 _M20GA_, p. 635

<a id="ga52-p964"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=966 printed_page=964 -->

**CASE 2024-01**

**TE ANDY MOEHN**

**VS.**

**WESTMINSTER PRESBYTERY**

**DISSENT**
**RE Caleb Stegall**

**March 10, 2025**

May a marriage between Christian spouses be biblically dissolved on
the grounds of desertion? This question has vexed and divided scholars,
theologians, and elders within the reformed faith for centuries. The Confession
states: “nothing but adultery, or such willful desertion as can no way be
remedied by the church, or civil magistrate, is cause sufficient of dissolving
the bond of marriage.” _WCF_ 24.6. Whether the clause “as can no way be
remedied by the church” renders the grounds of desertion lawfully unavailable
to believing spouses ina christian marriage has remained a matter of some
debate and ambiguity.

This dissent is not the place to recount the lengthy scholarship,
exegesis, or historical record surrounding the debate. Indeed, the PCA has
largely completed and documented that work. The 16 [th] General Assembly of
the PCA appointed an ad-interim committee to study this question, asking it in
part “to consider whether there could be other legitimate grounds for divorce
besides ‘desertion by an unbelieving spouse’ or ‘adultery’.” Report of the AdInterim Committee on Divorce and Remarriage to the Twentieth General
Assembly at 183 (adopted as a Position Paper of the PCA by the 20 [th] General
Assembly and hereinafter referred to as the Report).

The Report acknowledges as a starting point that:

“the general consensus among Reformed believers is the view
that the Bible neither condones nor commands divorce, but
rather permits and regulates divorce due to sin. However, a
person can divorce only for adultery and _separation of an_
_unbelieving spouse_ . In the words of the Westminster
Confession of Faith: ‘...nothing but adultery, or such wilful
desertion as can no way be remedied by the church or civil

<a id="ga52-p965"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=967 printed_page=965 -->

magistrate, is cause sufficient of dissolving the bond of
marriage.’ ( _WCF_ 24:6). John Stott and John Murray are
among those who insist that such permission is given only in
negative and reluctant terms. According to Stott: ‘Only if a
person divorces his partner on the ground of marital
unfaithfulness is his remarriage not adulterous. Only if the
unbeliever insists on leaving is the believer not bound.’”
Report at 204 (emphasis added).

Thus, the Report takes the rather unambiguous position that the natural
and historically accepted reading of _WCF_ 24.6 is that it does _not_ permit
divorce between Christian spouses on the grounds of desertion. The Report
does, however, give ample attention to the opposing view that neither the text
of _WCF_ 24.6 nor Paul’s scriptural injunctions (particularly those found in I
Corinthians 7) limit the lawful grounds for divorce between Christian spouses
to adultery. And specifically, that desertion must be understood as a lawful
ground of divorce even between believing spouses.

Perhaps the closest the authors of the Report take to committing to a
position is found in their recommendations (f) and (g), both of which were
adopted by the 20 [th] General Assembly. Recommendation (f) states: “That when
believers divorce for other than Biblical grounds, they should remain
unmarried or else be reconciled (1 Cor. 7:11).” Report at 293. And
recommendation (g) states: “That when an unbeliever separates from the
marriage relationship with a believer, the believer is free from that marriage
and free to remarry but only in the Lord (1 Cor. 7:15, 39).” Report at 293.
Read together, these recommendations at least create the reasonable inference
that if the “separation” is between two believing spouses, a subsequent divorce
is unbiblical and remarriage is not permitted as a consequence. Though the
inference is not made explicit.

This silence is consistent with the tenor of the Report as a whole which
throughout takes great care to render a good faith and fair recitation of each of
these opposing views, recognizing and taking into account all of the exegetical
complications, pastoral concerns for abused and victimized spouses, and
historical and cultural contingencies represented by both the scriptural and
confessional language. And in the end, given the difficulties and sensitivities
of the question, the Report does not actually answer the question it was charged
to answer. It does not directly declare a position for the PCA either way. In

<a id="ga52-p966"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=968 printed_page=966 -->

other words, it does not affirm that biblically permissible divorce is limited to
only circumstances of “adultery” or “desertion by an unbelieving
spouse.” But neither does it reject that position as too limiting.

Instead, it commends the exhaustive nature of the Report and the
pastoral advice it contains to the courts of the church for their sound and
careful consideration as they are required to deal with factually unique and
complex questions concerning individual marriages within the church. In this,
the Report is assiduous to honor the principle that in “unclear cases …
judgment will have to rest with the Session in their application of biblical
principles” and that we “should allow Sessions the liberty to discern with much
prayer what would be the proper response in particular
circumstance[s].” Report at 223 & 227.

In my view, this approach is both wise and prudent—and it forms the
basis of my dissent. For the SJC decision in this case authoritatively declares
(at least for the parties and, practically speaking, for the PCA as a whole) that
_WCF_ 24.6 expressly permits divorce between Christian spouses on the grounds
of desertion. And it does so with almost no discussion.

The decision simply recites that Westminster Presbytery “does not
believe the desertion clause applies to a professing Christian” and then
declares that the “Confession, however, simply refers to the act of desertion
and not the spiritual state of the one who has deserted his spouse (in a similar
fashion, the Confession also speaks simply of the act of adultery and not the
spiritual state of the one who commits adultery).” The SJC describes the view
held by Westminster Presbytery (that the “desertion clause” does not apply to
“a professing Christian”) as an “extra confessional understanding” which
“created a standard of guilt”—that is, an evidentiary threshold for a finding of
biblical grounds for dissolving the Moehn marriage—that could never be met
“so long as [Mrs. Moehn] continued to hold what [Presbytery] perceived to be
a credible profession of faith regardless of the choices she made.” Decision at
6.10-20.

This outcome is made more surprising by the fact that _both_ of the
parties below hold the view that is condemned by today’s decision.
Westminster Presbytery clearly takes the view that desertion is only grounds
for a biblically sanctioned divorce if the deserting party is found to be an
unbeliever. But so too does TE Moehn. At the hearing on this matter, a panel

<a id="ga52-p967"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=969 printed_page=967 -->

member asked TE Moehn, “Am I hearing you correctly, when you said you
agree that desertion has to be by a non-Christian spouse you mean by that one
who … either has no profession or who is behaving in a way that is inconsistent
with a profession?” TE Moehn answered, “Correct.” Hearing at 2:07:302:08:05.

It is clear the point of contention between the parties is _not_ whether
desertion o fa believing spouse creates grounds fora biblical ly sanctioned
divorce (the parties agreed it does not). They disagree instead over what
evidence may suffice for a court to declare that the deserting spouse is in fact
an unbeliever. Westminster Presbytery takes the seemingly untenable position
that excommunication is required, while TE Moehn takes the more reasonable
view that where excommunication is impossible (due to the deserting spouse
having left the PCA), a court may weigh the deserting spouse’s profession of
faith against that spouse’s actions and find it to be not credible. The SJC could
have resolved this matter on those grounds but chose not to.

Given the extraordinary pedigree of this debate within the reformed
faith broadly, and within the PCA specifically, it is remarkable that the SJC has
purported to resolve it in one scant paragraph. To be clear, today the SJC holds
that it is unconstitutional for a court of the PCA to consider the “spiritual state”
of the deserting party within a marriage when weighing questions of whether
a divorce may be lawfully permitted under our standards. For all practical
purposes, this amounts to a total rejection of what the Report described as “the
general consensus among Reformed believers” that the Bible and the
Confession permit divorce “only for adultery and _s_ eparation of an unbelieving
spouse.” Report at 204.

This dissent, like the Report itself, takes no position on the question. I
agree with today’s decision that the question is a constitutional one on which
the SJC owes no deference to the lower courts. _BCO_ 39-3.4. My position is
not precisely one of deference. Instead, I would find that on questions such as
this one—which have admitted to alternative interpretive stances across the
church, confirmed by long-standing practice, tradition, and authoritative
guidance—the SJC ought not find error when a lower court consistently
applies one of the acceptable interpretations. i would therefore commend a
resolution of the question to the various judgments of individual Sessions and
Presbyteries as they weigh the individual cases that come before them.

<a id="ga52-p968"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=970 printed_page=968 -->

Thus, in light of the vexing nature of the question at hand, its historical
development, varying practice across the PCA, and the Report adopted as the
position of the PCA on these matters by the 20 [th] General Assembly, I would
not find error in Westminster Presbytery’s view of _WCF_ 24.6.

<a id="ga52-p969"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=971 printed_page=969 -->

**CASE 2024-02**

**MR. GREG GLEASON**

**v.**

**HOUSTON METRO PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON COMPLAINT**

**October 17, 2024**

**CASE SUMMARY**

Mr. Gleason filed a _BCO_ 43-1 Complaint with his Session alleging the Session
violated the Ninth Commandment. He contended the offense was the Session's

purported approval of the contents of a Personnel Committee Statement that
was later read to the congregation by the Chairman of that Committee in a
meeting to consider voting on the dissolution of an Associate Pastor's call. The
Session ruled the Complaint out of order, and Mr. Gleason carried it to the
Presbytery, which also ruled it out of order. he carrie ditto the SJC.

**RULING**

This matter is judicially out of order and cannot be put in order. _BCO_ 43 is not
a constitutionally permissible avenue for processing accusations of offenses.

**REASONING AND OPINION**

In general, while a speaker a ta congregational meeting might be liable to
church discipline for violating the Ninth Commandant, such a matter would
involve an “offense.” an offense is defined as “the proper object of judicial
process,” being “anything in the doctrines or practice of a Church member
professing faith in Christ which is contrary to the Word of God” ( _BCO_ 29-1).
But such cases of process are not the proper subject of _BCO_ 43 proceedings,
which is the mode in which the instant matter is presented to us.

Indeed, the Complaint in this Case alleges an offense for which it seeks
redress. The Complaint suggests the Session ostensibly sinned, by violating
the Ninth Commandment when the Personnel Committee Statement ("PCS")
was read to the congregation. More fundamentally, however, the gravamen of

<a id="ga52-p970"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=972 printed_page=970 -->

the Complaint is that individuals in the leadership allegedly committed sins
against the Associate Pastor before the congregational meeting such that the
PCS was “misrepresenting the history” of supposed offenses by the leadership.
The “Preamble” to the Complaint said, “[I]t is my sincere hope and prayer that
the following complaint is taken in such a way as to bring repentance where it
is needed, and thereby healing to the Lord’s church,” adding, “[w]hile the
Session has erred in this matter, the road to repentance and forgiveness is wide
open to all who repent and believe.” The Complaint’s concluding section is
titled, “Sin, Confession, and Repentance,” wherein the Session is called to
“confess the truth,” revise and republish the PCS, and “ask for the forgiveness”
of both the Associate Pastor and the congregation.

The grievances that the Complaint seeks to adjudicate cannot be addressed via
the instant “mode of review.” For the reasons detailed below, a complaint like
this one commits a category error in seeking a ruling that either a session has
sinned or individuals have sinned and requesting that a higher court require
such to “confess” and “repent.”

individuals accused of sinning in such a manner are subject to protections ina
“case of process,” after the preliminary exhaustion of progressive
confrontation required by Matthew 18 or in such cases that “the previous steps
required by our Lord in the case of personal offenses are not necessary”
because “prosecution is instituted by the court” rather than by an individual
accuser ( _BCO_ 31-7). _BCO_ 43, being a “mode of review” to challenge the acts
or decisions of a court of the Church, certainly cannot redress the alleged sins
of individuals.

The idea that _BCO_ 43 might permit a member o fa congregation to redress his
or her sense that a Session, rather than an individual, has sinned is even more
problematic. Contending that sin as it relates to a court of the Church can be
treated like the sin o fan individual is a category error ak into supposing that a
Church court can be effectually called, justified, adopted, sanctified, have
saving faith, repent unto life, persevere to the end, do good works, and have
assurance of salvation. Certainly, a court’s individual members can and do
experience all these things, but just as there is no coherent principle that makes
a court of the Church as such either an object of salvation or of damnation in
God’s salvation economy, so also our polity does not treat a court of the
church as a subject o fa “case of process” in the same way it treats an
individual.

<a id="ga52-p971"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=973 printed_page=971 -->

Thus, at least with respect to _BCO_ 43 complaint proceedings, we do not
evaluate whether a court of the Church has sinned so much as whether it has

erred. To be sure, when courts err, they show the effects of sin in the Church,
yet the errors of Church courts do not subject them to church discipline via a
case of process in the same way that sin does to individuals. Because that mode
of review we call a complaint is a “written representation made against some
act or decision of a court of the Church” ( _BCO_ 43-1) that remedy is only
available against a court’s joint action, or what _BCO_ 3-2 calls the exercise of
the power of “jurisdiction.” Complaints cannot redress alleged sinful actions
by individual church officers because complaints only reach the “acts or
decisions” of a court.

_BCO_ 39-1 makes it clear that the “acts and decisions of a lower court are
brought under the supervision of a higher court” only “in one or another of the
following modes: (1) Review and Control, (2) Reference, (3) Appeal, and (4)
Complaint.” Our Rules of Discipline provide no procedure to treat corporate
sin of church courts as ecclesiastical discipline in the same manner as an
individual. The closest analogy is _BCO_ 40-5, which provides that a higher
court may, among other things, “censure” a “delinquent court,” but even then,
it is only f oran “important delinquency or grossly unconstitutional
proceeding,” not for a _BCO_ 29-1 “offense.” Regardless, this Case has not come
before us through the procedures prescribed by _BCO_ 40-5, so that is not before

us.

A complaint under _BCO_ 43, as filed in this Case, cannot redress alleged sin in
local church leadership as the Complaint seeks. In complaint proceedings, the
higher court has power at most only to “annul the whole or any part of the
action of a lower court against which complaint has been made, or to send the
matter back to the lower court with instructions for a new hearing” ( _BCO_ 4310). The higher courts must avoid adjudicating allegations of sin and
grievances within a congregation or its leadership that can only be fairly
adjudicated with the special protections afforded in a case of process. In _BCO_
43 proceedings, the higher court has no power at all to investigate, much less
to censure, a lower court, nor may it assume jurisdiction to decide whether
particular individuals have sinned and whether a lower court needs to “confess,
repent, and ask forgiveness.” Adjudicating the allegations in this Complaint
would deny alleged offenders the protections of a case of process under our
_Rules of Discipline_ .

<a id="ga52-p972"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=974 printed_page=972 -->

A preliminary ruling was sent to the Complainant, and he filed a two-page
Review Brief. However, he declined to address the concerns in that
preliminary ruling regarding accusations of sin and _BCO_ 43 complaints. The
_BCO_ provides other avenues for persons who feel aggrieved by sin, but _BCO_
43 is not one of them.

**AMENDS** - None

**RECORDING IN MINUTES**

The SJC instructs the Session and Presbytery to record this Decision, and any
Concurring and Dissenting Opinions, in the minutes of the next stated meeting
following its receipt of the full Decision.

The Panel members were RE Eggert, TE Bruce and RE Donahoe, with
alternates TE Kooistra and RE Stegall.

The decision was drafted by RE Eggert and the SJC approved the final decision
as amended by vote of 21-0, with 1 absent, 1 disqualified, and 1 recused.

Bankson _Concur_ Eggert _Concur_ Maynard Absent
Bise _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Neikirk _Concur_

Bruce _Concur_ Garner Recused Pickering _Concur_
Carrell _Concur_ Greco Disqualified Sartorius _Concur_
Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall _Concur_
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Concur_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe _Concur_ Lee _Concur_ White _Concur_

Dowling _Concur_ Marcinak _Concur_ Wilson _Concur_

TE Greco was disqualified because he is a member of this Presbytery.

TE Garner indicated he recused himself “due to his professional relationship
with a member of the Houston Metro Presbytery who features prominently in
these cases, and in accordance with the stipulations of _OMSJC_ 2.10.d, which
assert the necessity of SJC member disqualification when "impartiality might
reasonably be questioned.”

<a id="ga52-p973"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=975 printed_page=973 -->

**CONCURRING OPINION**

**2024-02** _**COMPLAINT OF GLEASON**_ _**v.**_ _**HOUSTON METRO**_

**RE Howie Donahoe**

**November 10, 2024**

I was on the Panel for this Case and agreed with the SJC's ruling. But it was
more than just procedurally out of order. It was highly improper for someone
to publicly accuse church officers of sin in a _BCO_ 43 complaint. Accused
persons have no voice in _BCO_ 43 proceedings and no opportunity to defend
themselves.

But this Case also involved, at least indirectly, the cessation of a minister's
employment. And even if this Case didn't involve accusations of sin, it's my
opinion that _employment_ decisions should not be reviewable under _BCO_ Rules
of Discipline ( _BCO_ 39, 40, 43, etc.). The Evangelical Presbyterian Church has
a wise provision in Chapter 1 of its Rules of Discipline:

EPC ROD 1-3. Church Members Who Are Also Employees

The employer/employee relationship is not within the
scope of the Book of Discipline. Church members who are
also employees of the church shall be subject to discipline
as an employee under whatever procedures may be
established by the church for employees. They may also be
subject to discipline as a member under the Book of
Discipline. [49]

**Employment Decisions and** _**BCO**_ **43** —While it might be helpful to have such
an explicit provision in our _BCO_, I believe it's implied. Consider two
examples. a session decides to end the employment o fa non-ordained
employee for budgetary reasons alone. That certainly seems to be a _business_
decision rather than an _ecclesiastical_ one _._ Or, a Session decides an employee
(and church member) is not doing adequate work, and the employer believes
a new hire is warranted. Should the employees in either example have redress
under _BCO_ 43? I don't think so. Those employment decisions don't seem to be

49 https://epc.org/wp-content/uploads/Files/4-Resources/5-Downloadable
EPC-Resources/A-ConstitutionDoctrine/BookOfOrder2022-2023.pdf

<a id="ga52-p974"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=976 printed_page=974 -->

actions of a _church court_ . ( _BCO_ 43-1) _BCO_ 11-2 says: "The _jurisdiction_ of
Church courts is only ministerial and declarative, and relates to the doctrines
and precepts of Christ, to the order of the Church, and to the exercise of
discipline" (emphasis added). An employment decision doesn't seem to fall
into the category of something that's only "ministerial" or "declarative." [50]

If employment decisions are reviewable by higher courts, a church member in
good standing could file a _BCO_ 43-1 complaint against the hiring of any
employee, even a non-ordained one. And any dismissed church employee who
is a PCA member in good standing could seek higher court review of his
dismissal. But it would be irrational to allow such.

**Ministerial Calls** —So, how does that relate to ministerial calls and TE
employment? The approval of a ministerial "call" and the dissolution of a call
are not necessarily two sides of the same coin. installation o fate ina pastoral
call in a PCA church requires consent from _three_ parties—the congregation
(or Session for assistant pastors), the man, and the Presbytery. However, it's
not the same for dissolution. A pastor's employment can end by his resignation,
by congregational/Session vote, orb ya pres byte ry censure.

There's a difference between _credentialling_ jurisdiction and _employment_
jurisdiction. I have no expertise in US labor law or the FLSA, but I don't think
a pres byte ry is considered to bean employer unless it has a direct employment
relationship with someone, even if the Presbytery is the entity that (1) decides
whether the employment is regarded as a valid ministerial call and (2) annually
approves a man's housing allowance amount. [51]

50 PCA Corporation Bylaws—Article III. Board of Directors. Section 1. The
_business affairs_ of the Corporation, _as distinguished from the ecclesiastical matters_,
shall be managed by the Board of Directors [the 20-member GA Admin
Committee], which shall have such powers and duties as are set forth in the charter
of the Corporation and the Bylaws. (emphasis added)

51 "The Supreme Court has said that there is no definition that solves all problems
relating to the employer-employee relationship under the Fair Labor Standards Act
(FLSA). The Court has also said that determination of the relation cannot be based
on isolated factors or upon a single characteristic, but depends upon the
circumstances of the whole activity. The goal of the analysis is to determine the
underlying economic reality of the situation and whether the individual is

<a id="ga52-p975"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=977 printed_page=975 -->

Ministers are credentialed like many other professions. To practice law or
medicine, a person needs t ob ea credential ed by th estate bar association or
the state medical board. t ob ea minister i nap ca church, a man must bean
ordained TE and approved by the Presbytery that has jurisdiction of the church
into which he will be installed. To work as a commercial airline pilot, a person
must be credentialed by the FAA. While most analogies have shortcomings,
let me offer this comparison.

Credentialling entity = _FAA_ _Presbytery_
Credential = student pilot license candidacy
private pilot license licensure
instrument rating seminary degree
multi-engine rating completed internship
commercial pilot rating ordination
airline transport rating solo/sr. pastor installation

Employer = commercial airline PCA church

Three parties must agree for someone to fly for an airline—the pilot, the FAA,
and the airline (the employer). Likewise, three parties need to agree for a man
to be installed asap astor i nap ca church—the man, the Presbytery, and the
employer (i.e., the church or Session). But it only takes _one_ party to end the
employment. A pilot can resign, an airline can fire a pilot without needing
FAA permission, and the FAA can rescind or suspend a pilot's license without
the airline's permission (e.g., for rule violations or health reasons). The airline
and the FAA exercise different jurisdictions—one over the pilot's employment
and one over his license.

Similarly, for all intents and purposes, it only takes one party to end the church
employment of a PCA minister. He can resign, the church or Session can
dismiss him (with or without cause), or the Presbytery can suspend or remove
his credentials for cause. The church/Session and the Presbytery exercise
different jurisdictions—one over the minister's continued employment and one

economically dependent on the supposed employer. In general, an employee, as
distinguished from an independent contractor who is engaged in a business of his
own, is one who "follows the usual path of an employee" and is dependent on the
business that he serves." https://webapps.dol.gov/elaws/whd/flsa/scope/er14.asp.
Accessed 10/28/24.

<a id="ga52-p976"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=978 printed_page=976 -->

over his license/ordination. A church/Session (the employer) can end a pastor's
employment without Presbytery "permission." Granted, after such an
employment decision the _BCO_ requires the Presbytery to vote on whether to
"grant" the "request" for the "dissolution" regardless of whether it comes from
the minister, the Session, or the congregation.

_BCO_ 23-1. When any minister shall tender the resignation of
his pastoral charge to his Presbytery, the Presbytery shall cite
the church to appear by its commissioners, to show cause why
the Presbytery should or should not accept the resignation. If
the church fails to appear, or if its reasons for retaining its
pastor be deemed insufficient, his resignation shall be accepted
and the pastoral relation dissolved. If any church desires to be
relieved of its pastor, a similar procedure shall be observed.

This is essentially the same wording as in the PCUS Book of 1879, and it's a
fairly toothless provision. If a PCA minister resigns from his employment
today, nobody can force him to return to work. and i fa church dismisses a
minister from employment, the Presbytery cannot force it to keep paying him.
While a Presbytery might delay official dissolution if it deemed some kind of
inquiry was warranted, a Presbytery can't force a minister to show up for work
and cannot force a church to continue paying any employee it dismisses, even
if it's a minister. Granted, as a last resort, a Presbytery could eventually dismiss
such a church from its membership, thereby dissolving that church's
relationship with the PCA. But that would be a rare occurrence.

I’ll offer another example. _BCO_ 31-10 stipulates: "When a member o fa church
court is under process, all his official functions may be suspended at the court’s
discretion; but this shall never be done in the way of censure, and this requires
a two-thirds (2/3) majority." That's an example o fa Presbytery's credentialling
jurisdiction. But if a pastor is accused of something the Session judges as
warranting immediate administrative suspension, the Session (representing the
church) has the employment jurisdiction to place him on administrative leave
until the matter is resolved. And if the alleged offense is significant enough
and the preliminary evidence is strong enough, I don't believe the church is
required to pay him during that leave. The Session has the discretion to
exercise its judgment on that matter, because the church has _employment_
jurisdiction. And considering the time ordinarily required for a sufficient
Presbytery investigation, a trial, and an appeal, the final resolution of the

<a id="ga52-p977"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=979 printed_page=977 -->

allegations could take a year from when the session learns of the allegations.
Granted, the Session's decision might be a tragically bad mistake (which its
D&O or Leadership Liability insurance might need to address later), but it
would be an employment decision, not a credentialing one.

We're all familiar with _BCO_ Preliminary Principle 6, which states: "the power
to elect persons to the exercise of authority in any particular society resides in
that society." Thus, no Presbytery can install a minister who is unacceptable
to a church, and likewise, no Presbytery can require a church to continue to
employ a minister it no longer finds acceptable. Neither _BCO_ 23-1 nor 24-7
require a congregation to adopt a reason when it votes to be "relieved of its
pastor" or to "dissolve the official relationship between the church and [a
ruling elder] without censure." [52]

Similarly, no Presbytery can require a PCA agency or school to continue
employing a minister it no longer finds acceptable, and no such agency or
school needs a Presbytery's permission to dismiss such an employee. Granted,
the minister or his employer should promptly notify the credentialling entity
(Presbytery) if he is suspended or dismissed from employment, especially if
allegations are involved. But that would simply be akin to an airline notifying
the FAA if it dismissed a pilot from employment. It wouldn't need the FAA's
permission to dismiss him.

For example, a PCA military chaplain is employed by the DOD. A minister
teaching in a Christian high school, college, or seminary is employed by the
school. ate missionary is employed by MTW, Inc. A campus minister is
employed by RUF, Inc. and any TEs on the staff of Geneva Benefits Group or

52 _BCO_ 24-7: "The ruling elder or deacon, though chargeable with neither heresy nor

immorality, may become unacceptable in his official capacity to a majority of the
church which he serves. in such a case the church may take the initiative by a
majority vote at a regularly called congregational meeting and request the Session
to dissolve the official relationship between the church and the officer without
censure. The Session, after conference with the ruling elder or deacon, and after
careful consideration, _may use its discretion_ as to dissolving the official
relationship." (emphasis added) However, it would be very disruptive to retain such
a ruling elder if the majority of his church voted otherwise, and contrary to
Preliminary Principle 6.

<a id="ga52-p978"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=980 printed_page=978 -->

Ridge Haven are employed by those corporations. [53] ate laboring out of
bounds in a non-PCA church is employed by that church—an entity over
which the Presbytery has no jurisdiction. The Presbyteries with credentialling
jurisdiction over such ministers cannot force those entities to continue
employing the minister, and those entities don't need any Presbytery's approval
to dismiss such an employee.

At the same time, it's the Presbytery and not those various employers that
determines whether the minister is with or without call in any given job. [54] But
that's a credentialing matter, not an employment matter. Presbytery cannot
prevent a minister who is without call from accepting a job that pres byte ry
does not regard as being a ministerial call. A man must provide for his family.
Granted, if that minister "habitually fails to be engaged in the regular discharge
of his official functions" (i.e., remains without call for an extended period),
Presbytery can divest him without censure following the procedure in _BCO_
34-10. But that's a credential ing matter ak into the faa rescinding a pilot's
license for failure to maintain currency.

**Familiar Situation** —Shifting gears somewhat, the Record in the present Case
described a circumstance that's unfortunately familiar. Occasionally, the
working relationship between a senior pastor and an associate or assistant
pastor becomes ineffective. While there are no allegations of sin warranting
investigation, the Session deems it wise (as an employer) to effectuate a
Paul/Barnabas type separation, with the senior pastor remaining. (Acts 15:39;
"a sharp disagreement" in ESV) This is simpler with an assistant pastor, but
more challenging with an associate because the congregation will need to vote.
So, the Session will sometimes encourage the associate (or assistant) to
consider asking Presbytery to dissolve his call and will encourage him to avoid
any unflattering speech or writing related to the senior pastor or the Session.
The Session financially compensates him for his willingness to be careful to
avoid such speech, and in exchange for that financial consideration, he

53 RAO 3-1 ... The Stated Clerk shall be authorized to _employ_, with the approval
of the Administrative Committee of General Assembly, a Business Administrator.
All other staff personnel for said Committee shall then be _employed_ by the Business
Administrator with the approval and consent of the Stated Clerk. ...
RAO 4-7. _Employees_ of the Assembly’s Committees and Agencies are not eligible

for office on an Assembly’s Committee or Agency which administers matters
directly related to their area of _employment_ .
54 _BCO_ 8-7, 20-1, and 21-1.

<a id="ga52-p979"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=981 printed_page=979 -->

voluntarily relinquishes his right to criticize the senior pastor and the
Session. This would _not_ be a financial agreement to cover up sin. It's an
agreement to voluntarily guard the tongue more carefully than what's already
required by the Ninth Commandment.

Eventually, a congregational meeting is held to vote o na motion to
communicate to Presbytery the congregation's position on whether Presbytery
should approve the associate pastor's request that his call be dissolved. ( _BCO_
22-2 & 4) That meeting is awkward, because the associate is not immediately
going to a new call. People wonder whether the associate truly initiated the
resignation or was "asked" to resign. At the meeting, the moderator or whoever
is presenting the report wants to speak well of the departing associate,
regardless of the details, and there's little wisdom in airing details to the
congregation. So, any mention of the Paul/Barnabas thing is prudently
avoided. The exercise of that discretion is not contrary to Westminster LC 144
on the Ninth Commandment. It is, instead, adherence to it.

Later, someone circulates a report, or a document indicating the associate's
departure wasn't 100% on his initiative and provides bits of incomplete
information that puts the Session and/or ate staff member ina bad light.
Anyone who does this should lament that behavior, especially if he's aware of
the agreement between the Session and the associate. And anyone advising
that person to follow such a course should likewise lament that behavior.

Such conduct is unacceptable from anyone who promised to submit to the
government of the Church and promised to study [ _strive for_ ] its purity and
peace. It's often the worst kind of peace-disrupting behavior in our churches.
And it might, understandably, lead some churches to decide to never again call
an _associate_ pastor (because no congregational meeting is needed if the
Session deems the call of an assistant pastor needs to be dissolved.) Or it might
lead churches to only hire an associate pastor for a set term, wherein
employment ends on a specific date unless renewed by the congregation. If a
Presbytery approved such a call up front, there would be no need for
Presbytery approval for the dissolution at the end of the term.

/s/ RE Howie Donahoe

<a id="ga52-p980"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=982 printed_page=980 -->

**CASE 2024-04**

**PAUL GOZÉ**

**v.**

**HILLS AND PLAINS PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON COMPLAINT**

**October 17, 2024**

The SJC reviewed the Complaint and found the case Administratively Out of
Order. The SJC determined that the case cannot be put in order _(OMSJC_ 9.2),
because i ti snot a complaint against ana c to fa court (the Session).

**AMENDS** - None

**RECORDING IN MINUTES**

The SJC instructs the Session and Presbytery to record this Decision, and any
Concurring and Dissenting Opinions, in the minutes of the next stated meeting
following its receipt of the full Decision.

The SJC Officers drafted the decision, which was adopted as the SJC's final
decision by vote of **22-0-0, with 1 absent, and 1 recused.**
