**CASE 2024-05**

**TE KEVIN BIGELOW**

**v.**

**NORTH FLORIDA PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON COMPLAINT**

**March 6, 2025**

**I.** **SUMMARY OF FACTS**

02/06/18 The Session of Christ Church East (CCE) discussed “Kevin’s
compensation package and his call letter.” Kevin Bigelow was
present as a ruling elder member of Session.

04/30/19 The Session of CCE approved Mr. Bigelow’s housing allowance
through the end of the 2019 calendar year.

05/03/19 The Stated Clerk of North Florida Presbytery (NFP) emailed the
pastor of CCE and relevant leaders of Presbytery stating, “Good
to talk with you today and to hear you all are okay with the
suggested procedure to call Kevin as an assistant pastor for the
Beaches church plant as a daughter church plant and to specify
housing allowance.”

05/09/19 NFP examined Mr. Bigelow for ordination. The ordination
examination was sustained. NFP’s minutes then record, “TE
Abney presented the terms of call from Christ Church Intown ( _sic_ )
as Assistant Pastor with the understanding that Mr. Bigelow’s
ministry would consist in church planting in the Beaches area of
Jacksonville. Presbytery approved the call. Mr. Bigelow accepted
the call and it was placed in his hands.” The Record of the Case
(ROC) includes an undated letter of call that begins “Christ
Church East (PCA) calls you, Kevin Bigelow, to the position of
Planting Pastor of Christ Church Beaches, promising and obliging
ourselves to pay you the following salary, benefits, allowances,
and vacation.”

<a id="ga52-p982"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=984 printed_page=982 -->

05/19/19 TE Bigelow was ordained and installed. The report of the
Commission to ordain and install begins, “Report of the
Commission to ordain and install Rev. Kevin Bigelow as Assistant
Pastor of Christ Church East, Jacksonville, Florida, with the
specific assignment of church planting in the Beaches area of
Jacksonville.” The minutes also state, “The Chairman then
propounded the constitutional questions to ( _sic_ ) Congregation
( _BCO_ 21.6).” (emphasis added) The minutes continue, “The
Chairman then declared that Rev. Kevin Bigelow had been
regularly elected, ordained, and installed as Assistant Pastor of the
Church.” The order of service refers to “Questions to the
Congregation,” but the order of service also deletes the clauses
_BCO_ 21-6 says are to be deleted for an assistant minister. The
personal notes of the Presiding Minister state the vows are to be
propounded to the elders.

05/19/19 – The Christ Church Beaches (CCB) church plant was governed by
08/26/23 the Session of CCE, with TE Bigelow apparently participating and
voting in CCE Session meetings when matters concerning CCB
were before Session. TE Bigelow carried out the pastoral ministry
at CCB during this period. NFP Directories from this period listed
TE Bigelow both as Assistant Pastor at CCE and as “Planting
Pastor” at CCB. The rolls for Presbytery meetings listed TE
Bigelow as “Organizing Pastor” for CCB.

Spring- Some members of CCB raised concerns about TE Bigelow. The
Sum 23 Session of CCE became involved in the matter. As conflict

between TE Bigelow and some members of the CCE Session
grew, the Shepherding Committee of NFP was invited to provide
assistance.

06/18/23 A meeting of the CCB Congregation was held “to address the
gossip and dissension within the church.” Members of the CCE
Session and the NFP Shepherding Committee participated in the
meeting.

07/21/23 TE and Mrs. Bigelow had a telephone conversation with some of
the elders from CCE.

<a id="ga52-p983"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=985 printed_page=983 -->

08/01/23 The Session of CCE met with TE Bigelow to share concerns that
at least some members of Session had about him, both relationally
and ministerially. As part of this discussion, members of Session
shared with TE Bigelow a letter alleging various sins on his part.
TE Bigelow apologized for hurting some of the elders. In a
separate item of business, the RE member of the CCE Session who
was most involved at CCB shared a plan for particularizing CCB
in the fall of 2023. Among other things, this plan would constitute
TE Bigelow and this RE as a commission, effective August 2,
2023, on which commission “would be conferred all the authority
granted to a temporary form of government as specified in the
_BCO_ .” The motion to institute this plan failed by a vote of 1-5.

08/07/23 The Session of CCE held a called meeting to continue the
discussion of its relationship to TE Bigelow. The minutes record
that TE Bigelow agreed with and owned stated sins in regard to
his relation to CCE Session but did not agree with those sins in
relationship to his leadership at CCB. The CCE minutes then
record the following:

3. Communication to Kevin that we are placing

him on an immediate break from his pastoral
responsibilities at CC Beaches.
a. Don Roberts made a motion for Kevin to

take an indefinite break from preaching
responsibilities.
i. Motion was seconded by Ed Morales.
ii. Discussion was had

1. Kevin protested decision to have

him removed from preaching
responsibilities based on council
( _sic_ ) of his therapist, coach, and his
personal opinion.
2. Doug Hayman read a written

complaint against the CCE
session. A written copy of the
complaint was given to the acting
Clerk of the Session.

iii. Motion was passed by a vote of 4-1

<a id="ga52-p984"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=986 printed_page=984 -->

1. Doug Hayman requested his “No”

vote to be recorded.

08/10/23 The CCE Session sent a letter to the Congregation of CCB. The
letter stated “…the Christ Church East Session, which serves as
the temporary governing body of Christ Church Beaches, has
decided to remove Kevin Bigelow from preaching duties on
Sunday mornings so that he can seek healing and restoration.”

08/11/23 TE Chuck Colson wrote to the Clerk of Presbytery expressing
concern about Session’s action in removing TE Bigelow from
preaching duties at CCB. TE Colson questioned the constitutional
basis for that action and the Session’s claim that removing TE
Bigelow from preaching was not a censure. He suggested that
Presbytery needed to investigate the matter and asked for a called
meeting of Presbytery “to determine a wise path to address these
serious concerns.”

08/26/23 NFP held a called meeting to “discuss the relationship between
TE Kevin Bigelow and the Christ Church East Session and to
receive recommendations from the Administrative Committee

regarding these matters.” TE Keith Dickerson, Pastor of CCE,
presented a communication on behalf of Session wherein he laid
out some of Session’s concerns regarding TE Bigelow. He also
stated that, based on a conversation with the Stated Clerk _emeritus_
of the General Assembly, Session was of the opinion that it could
either deal with its concerns regarding TE Bigelow via formal
process or “according to _BCO_ 12-5-e, [it] could use the authority
granted to a session to remove a teaching elder from the pulpit.”

The Administrative Committee of NFP presented three
recommendations:

1. “To cite Christ Church East Session to appear
before Presbytery to answer credible reports concerning
unconstitutional proceedings by the Session of Christ Church East
in indefinitely removing TE Kevin Bigelow from his preaching
responsibilities without filing charges against him before the
Presbytery.”

<a id="ga52-p985"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=987 printed_page=985 -->

2. “To form a committee, in accordance with _BCO_ 312, to investigate the reports concerning the character TE Kevin
Bigelow and to investigate the credibility of other reports of the
proceedings of the Session of Christ Church East surrounding the
removal of TE Bigelow from his preaching responsibilities at
Christ Church Beaches.”

3. To transfer the temporary governance of CCB from
the Session of CCE to a commission of Presbytery, and to
empower the MNA Committee of Presbytery to appoint the
temporary commission.

In support of the first recommendation, the Administrative
Committee argued the fact that the Session of CCE cited moral
grounds for removing TE Bigelow from the pulpit gave the action
a disciplinary character. They argued this is outside the powers of
Session and that Session should have dealt with their concerns by
filing charges before Presbytery.

Presbytery adopted the first recommendation. Then, because
representatives from the CCE Session were present and had
explained Session’s action, the Chairman of the Administrative
Committee moved an additional action which had been previously
approved by the Committee: “to rule the action of Christ Church
East Session to remove TE Kevin Bigelow indefinitely from his
preaching responsibilities without filing charges against him out
of order.” The motion carried.

Presbytery adopted the second motion and then appointed the
members of the resulting Investigative Committee. Presbytery
adopted the third motion.

08/26/23 - CCB was governed by a “provisional session” of which TE
02/08/24 Bigelow was a member.

10/18/23 The Session of CCE filed charges against TE Bigelow.

11/09/23 The Investigative Committee reported on their investigation
regarding the character of TE Bigelow. They concluded “the

<a id="ga52-p986"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=988 printed_page=986 -->

dispute does _**not**_ rise to a judicial level. We believe there are issues
with how this has been handled by the CCE session and by TE
Bigelow, much of which is relational in nature” (emphasis in
original). The Committee’s recommendation was “If the
Presbytery decides to continue the relationship between CCE and
Bigelow, we recommend the Presbytery appoint a ‘ _**mediation**_
_**commission.’**_ We believe there are issues here regarding both
parties that need to be addressed. We encourage the leadership of
both churches to pursue a process of mediation for the purpose of
reconciliation” (emphasis in the original). The Committee
suggested that a mediator from Peacemaker Ministries be brought
in.

The first sentence of the recommendation was apparently not
made part of the Committee’s motion to Presbytery. The second
sentence of the recommendation was struck via amendment on the

floor of Presbytery. Presbytery then adopted the following: “The
investigative committee strongly encourages the leadership of
both churches to pursue a process of mediation for the purpose of
reconciliation. Our suggestion: Support the reconciliation process
and the mediation commission by bringing in a mediator from the
“Peacemaker Ministries” of Ken Sande.” Presbytery then
empowered the Administration Committee of Presbytery to
appoint the Mediation Commission. Finally, Presbytery referred
to the Mediation Commission recommendations from the

Investigative Committee concerning support from CCE to CCB
and how to deal with disaffected members at CCB.

12/13/23 a member of the provisional session made a recommendation that
TE Bigelow “resign as pastor of Christ Church Beaches.” This
recommendation was apparently supported by four of the six
members present. Reasons mentioned included health concerns
and a concern that TE Bigelow’s continued presence would make
the “replanting” of CCB more difficult. A final vote was not taken
so as to allow TE Bigelow to discuss the recommendation with his
wife.

<a id="ga52-p987"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=989 printed_page=987 -->

Jan 2024 The Mediation Commission conducted meetings with TE
Bigelow, with TE Dickerson, and with various ruling elders from
CCB.

01/23/24 The minutes of the provisional Session for CCB record “Kevin
made five requests of the provisional Session in a letter,” one of
which was “Support my petition to MNA to transfer my call to the
NFP. It’s hard to imagine going through mediation with integrity
with my termination by East being held over my head.” The
provisional Session did not vote to support this request and TE
Bigelow withdrew it.

02/02/24 The Mediation Commission developed a report summarizing their
work. They stated they had interviewed the parties separately and
then they met with TEs Bigelow and Dickerson together. As a
result of those meetings the Commission concluded that
reconciliation “was not possible at the current time” and that the
parties were at an impasse. The Commission report concluded that
the Commission was going to start meeting with concerned
members from CCB.

02/02/24 TE Bigelow communicated to TE Dickerson and the Mediation
Commission that he had grounds for filing charges against TE
Dickerson.

02/04 – Members of the Reconciliation Commission met in teams of two

03/01/24 with nine concerned members of CCB over five different

meetings.

02/04/24 The Session of CCE voted to dissolve the call of TE Kevin

Bigelow as Assistant Pastor. Session gave two reasons for their
action: 1) TE Bigelow was no longer under their jurisdiction
because of Presbytery’s action of August 26, 2023, to move the
governance of CCB to a commission of Presbytery and 2) “it has
become clear that our relationship with TE Bigelow cannot be
reconciled at this time.” Session further voted to offer TE Bigelow
six months of severance which was apparently conditioned on his
signing a nondisclosure agreement.

<a id="ga52-p988"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=990 printed_page=988 -->

02/08/24 Presbytery, at its stated meeting, was presented with a report from
the Mediation Commission that concluded the relationship
between TE Bigelow, TE Dickerson, and the Session of CCE “are
at an impasse, and we see no evidence of that situation
improving.” The letter from the Session of CCE seeking the
dissolution of the call to TE Bigelow was read. The Moderator of
the provisional Session for CCB and the Chairman of NFP’s MNA
Committee presented three recommendations that originated with
the CCB provisional Session and were supported by the MNA
Committee. Presbytery adopted the three recommendations with
the addition of the last clause of the second recommendation.

1. “That the Presbytery approve [Clerk’s note:
support, as explained in the **MNA** Committee’s report] Christ
Church East Session’ ( _sic_ ) dissolution of TE Bigelow’s call
with Christ Church East as Assistant Pastor/Beaches Church

Planter ( _BCO_ 22-4, Cf. 23-2).”

2. “That after the dissolution of TE Bigelow’s call,
MNA will not pick up his call, and to change TE Bigelow’s
status to be Without Call.”

3. “That the Presbytery transfer oversight of “Christ
Church Beaches” from the Presbytery established
“ _provisional session_ ” to the _MNA Committee_ ( _BCO_ 5-3c),
effective, February 12, 2024, to allow the committee to
undertake an assessment of the congregation’s overall
spiritual health, determining if a viable church planting work
exists in Jacksonville Beach ( _BCO_ 5-2).”

The CCE Session was asked if severance would be provided to TE
Bigelow. “The Session responded with its plan to provide 6
months of severance salary to TE Kevin Bigelow.” Presbytery, on
motion, voted to encourage the CCE Session to include health
insurance payments in that package.

The referenced “MNA Committee report” is written in the first
person. It begins “As Chairman of the MNA committee I am
asking that we fully support these following recommendations,
and support Christ Church East session (TE Keith Dickerson), and
the Provisional Session **in their decision to dissolve the pastoral**
**relationship of TE Kevin Bigelow’s call with Christ Church**

<a id="ga52-p989"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=991 printed_page=989 -->

**East as Assistant Pastor/Beaches Church Planter** ” (emphasis
in the original). After repeating the recommendations and offering
some rationale for each, the “Committee report” continues: “We’ll
need to think through this carefully, and pray for wisdom and
discernment on the “What If” possibilities . . . “If TE Bigelow
refuses to repent” and does not obey or comply with the
presbytery’s process, he shall be dealt with for his contumacy
( _BCO_ 32-6; cf. _BCO_ 33-2; 34-4) . . . [contumacy = stubbornness;
unyielding SJC 2024-05 November 26, 2024 obstinacy;
haughtiness; rude and sarcastic; reproachful; unrepentant;
inflexible; willful contempt and disobedience to lawful summons
of presbytery]; using _BCO_ language here . . .” it then goes onto
discuss how Presbytery should deal with one who persists in
contumacy. The minutes of NFP do not record whether this part
of the report was ever discussed by Presbytery. There was no
evidence that any action was taken on it.

02/11/24 NFP’s MNA Committee held an informational meeting at CCB
following Worship. Representatives from MNA explained the
actions of Presbytery and responded to questions from some in
attendance who said Presbytery’s actions sounded disciplinary in
nature. The representatives responded that the actions were not
disciplinary in that TE Bigelow was not suspended from office or
defrocked. At the same time, one of the representatives did say
“Yes it is to a degree. It’s not stated that way in judicial action but
it is stated that way in the sense that the relationship has been
dissolved. So it can feel that way look that way, and so to some
degree it is….”

02/14/25 The Reconciliation Commission met in response to concerns TE
Bigelow raised about the NDA proposed by the Session of CCE.
The Commission adopted the following statement that was
prefaced by quoting Colossians 3:14-17. “We continue to seek
reconciliation between parles ( _sic_ ). The separation agreement and
its language has been of concern to some of the parties. The
agreement does have a distinctly legal and corporate tone. For the
peace, purity, and unity of the body of Christ, we prefer the
separation agreement to be rewrite ( _sic_ ) with a more pastoral
approach and tone.”

<a id="ga52-p990"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=992 printed_page=990 -->

02/22/24 TE Bigelow emailed the Reconciliation Commission expressing
concerns about the second version of the NDA.

04/01/24 TE Bigelow filed Complaints against various actions of NFP.

04/06/24 TE Bigelow withdrew the Complaints filed on April 1 and
submitted five revised Complaints. Those five Complaints,
comprising 123 pages, alleged 1) there were various out of order
actions in the dissolution of TE Bigelow’s call; 2) there were
various unconstitutional actions in the dissolution of the pastoral
relationship between TE Bigelow and CCB; 3) that TE Bigelow
was disciplined without process; 4) that Presbytery erred in
various aspects of the mediation process; and 5) that there was
partiality in the mediation process and that Presbytery erred in
entering the mediation process without a third party. Each of these
Complaints alleged multiple underlying errors.

05/09/24 NFP took up TE Bigelow’s Complaints. At the recommendation
of the Administrative Committee of NFP, Presbytery voted to find
the first Complaint in order and that it would be taken up at that
meeting. Presbytery also voted to find the second Complaint to be
administratively out of order, and to find the third, fourth, and fifth
Complaints to be judicially out of order.

The rationale offered by the Administrative Committee for finding
the second Complaint to be administratively out of order was
“since TE Kevin Bigelow was ordained as an assistant pastor at
Christ Church East, Jacksonville, FL, and that the Christ Church
East Session, not the North Florida Presbytery, lawfully dissolved
the pasteral ( _sic_ ) relationship between Christ Church East and TE
Keving ( _sic_ ) Bigelow.”

The rationale offered by the Administrative Committee for
declaring the third, fourth, and fifth Complaints out of order was
that they did not involve acts or decisions of Presbytery. ( _BCO_ 431)

NFP held the hearing on the first Complaint following the
procedures of _BCO_ 43-9. The Administrative Committee moved

<a id="ga52-p991"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=993 printed_page=991 -->

the Complaint be denied. The Committee offered as rationale its
understanding that TE Bigelow had been called as an Assistant
Pastor by CCE, that his call was never changed, that his position
was as Planting Pastor at CCB but that did not change the fact that
his call was as Assistant Pastor at CCE, and that providing pastoral
ministry and governance to CCB through the Session of CCE and
through CCE’s Assistant Pastor did not contravene _BCO_ 5.

06/11/24 TE Bigelow carried his Complaint to General Assembly.

10/14/24 The Chairman of the Panel had previously reported to the Panel
that both parties sought additions and deletions to the Record of
the Case as prepared by the Clerk of NFP, and that each party
objected to at least some of the changes sought by the other party.
The Chairman had worked with the parties to seek agreement, but
some disagreements remained. The Panel, consisting of RE
Frederick (Jay) Neikirk (Chairman), TE Paul Lee (Secretary), TE
Jay Bruce, TE Brad Evans (alt.) and RE John Pickering (alt.)
therefore, held a hearing on the Record of the Case under _OMSJC_
7.4.e. Both parties had submitted briefs that were timely filed.
Complainant Bigelow represented himself. Respondent
Presbytery was represented by TE Tommy Park, who was assisted
by TE David Burke, Stated Clerk of NFP. After the hearing the
Panel ruled on the contested documents and then declared the

revised ROC to be final. In its ruling the Panel accepted any
contested document that was created contemporaneously with the
events to which they relate, but it did not accept contested
documents that were created by one of the parties after
Presbytery’s consideration of the Complaint.

01/09/25 The Panel held the hearing on the Complaint. Both parties had
submitted briefs that were timely filed. Complainant Bigelow
represented himself. Respondent Presbytery was represented by
TE Park.

<a id="ga52-p992"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=994 printed_page=992 -->

**II. STATEMENT OF THE ISSUES**

1. Did NFP err in dissolving the call of TE Bigelow at the request of the

CCE Session?
2. Did NFP err in declaring Complaints 2-5 out of order?
3. Did NFP err in dissolving the call of TE Bigelow without providing

the Congregation of CCB the opportunity to vote on that proposed
dissolution?

4. Did NFP err in dissolving TE Bigelow’s call as an act of discipline

without process?
5. Did the Mediation Commission of NFP err in how it conducted its

mediation efforts?

6. Did NFP demonstrate unbiblical and unconstitutional partiality in its

mediation efforts regarding TE Bigelow and the Session of CCE?

**III. JUDGMENT**

1. No

2. Yes

3. No

4. No

5. No

6. No

**IV. REASONING AND OPINION**

This Complaint involves five separate but related Complaints, each of which
has multiple underlying allegations. In judging these Complaints, we were
obliged to apply different review standards to the individual Complaints. We
find that Complaints 1, 3, 4, and 5 (Issues 1, 4, 5, and 6) are primarily matters
of discretion and judgment for which we must “ordinarily exhibit great
deference to [the] lower court” ( _BCO_ 39-3.3). The Presbytery’s out of order
rulings on Complaints 2-5 and Complaint 2 (Issues 2 and 3) are primarily
matters of constitutional interpretation for which we “are not obliged to give
the same deference to [the] lower court” ( _BCO_ 39-3.4).

<a id="ga52-p993"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=995 printed_page=993 -->

**Reasoning for Issue 1 (Complaint 1)**

Complaint 1 alleges 11 actions on which NFP erred. The core of this
Complaint, however, is the allegation that “NFP erred in approving the
dissolution of the call of ‘Assistant Pastor of Christ Church East’ while the call

on file was that of ‘Planting Pastor for Christ Church Beaches’.”

Complainant argued that, while the original call may have, in effect, been as
Assistant Pastor at CCE, the intent was always that he would be “Planting
Pastor” at CCB, and that was the call that was before NFP at its stated meeting
of May 9, 2019. Complainant stated that he never understood the call that was
approved by Presbytery to be for an Assistant Pastor, and he stated he never
signed a call to be an Assistant Pastor. In support of his allegation that he was
called as “Planting Pastor” he cited the fact that the words “Assistant Pastor”
do not appear in the call, that the Order of Service for his ordination and the
minutes of the Commission that conducted the Service both state that the

constitutional questions were propounded to the Congregation of CCB rather
than the Session of CCE, that the Directories of NFP list him as “Planting
Pastor” of CCB, that the minutes of NFP list him only as “Organizing Pastor”
of NFP, that he was able to vote in CCE Session meetings only on matters
involving CCB, that he functioned as the Pastor of CCB for four years, and
that _BCO_ 5-4 does not allow for an Assistant Pastor to provide pastoral
ministry for a mission church.

We grant that some of the documents are not as clear as they should have been.
This is particularly true with regard to the minutes of the Session of CCE
surrounding their approval of TE Bigelow’s call. But one salient fact
remains—there is only one call document in the ROC. That call clearly came
from the action of the Session of CCE in the spring of 2019. The document,
which Presbytery’s minutes clearly label as a call to an Assistant Pastor,
contains all required terms of call and was the basis of TE Bigelow’s
ordination ( _BCO_ 20-1). There is no evidence in the ROC that this call from the
Session of CCE was ever dissolved prior to February 8, 2024, or that it was
ever transformed into a different call from some different calling body,
whether i tb ea pres byte ry ora congregation.

Further, there is evidence throughout the ROC that indicates NFP understood
TE Bigelow’s call to be an Assistant Pastor at CCE. That evidence includes
the clear language of the minutes of the NFP meeting of May 9, 2019, which

<a id="ga52-p994"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=996 printed_page=994 -->

repeatedly refers to the call as that of an Assistant Pastor, the minutes of the
Ordination Commission which show that the questions to the “Congregation”
were in the form to be posed in the installation of an Assistant Pastor, the notes
of the Chairman of the Ordination Commission that show he understood the

installation was of an Assistant Pastor and that the questions should be directed
toward the elders, and the fact that NFP Directories consistently list TE
Bigelow as Assistant Pastor of CCE and “Planting Pastor” for CCB.

In addition, if TE Bigelow thought the May 9, 2019, NFP minutes incorrectly
recorded his call or that several years of Presbytery Directories were incorrect
in listing him as Assistant Pastor of CCE, he had plenty of opportunity to seek
corrections of those records. There is no evidence that he did so.

Finally, even Complainant’s own statement indicates his call was as Assistant
Pastor at CCE. On January 23, 2024, he wrote to the Provisional Session of
CCB asking that body to “Support my petition to MNA to transfer my call to
the NFP. It’s hard to imagine going through mediation with integrity with my
termination by East being held over my head.” This indicates that even in
January of 2024, Complainant understood his call was from CCE. Since there
is no evidence that the Congregation of CCE ever voted on any call to TE
Bigelow, such a call could only have been as an Assistant Pastor.

The procedure for the dissolution of a pastoral relationship is clear in _BCO_ 231. When either the pastor or the congregation (the calling body) desires to
terminate the pastoral relationship, the Presbytery shall hear from both parties
and then determine if the pastoral relation should be dissolved. In this case, the
Record makes clear, in spite of some confusion, that the call to TE Bigelow
was as Assistant Pastor of CCE and that the calling body was the Session of
CCE. The fact that TE Bigelow’s primary assignment by Session, and with the
concurrence of Presbytery, was to seek to plant a church in the Beaches area
of Jacksonville does not change the fact that the call was as Assistant Pastor.
As such, Session had every right, for whatever reasons seemed prudent to it,
to ask Presbytery to dissolve the call. It was incumbent on Presbytery, in turn,
to decide whether there was sufficient reason for the requested dissolution.
That decision is a matter of discretion and judgment, and we see no clear error
that would require the reversal of Presbytery’s action.

<a id="ga52-p995"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=997 printed_page=995 -->

The other 10 actions alleged to be in error in Issue 1 (Complaint 1) were either
part of the action of dissolving the call or grew out of that dissolution. Thus,
they stand or fall with the validity of the action of NFP to dissolve the call.

For these reasons we find no clear error on the part of NFP in their decision to
dissolve TE Bigelow’s call and Complaint 1 is denied.

**Issue 2 (NFP’s Out of Order Rulings for Complaints 2-5 (Issues 3-6))**

At its meeting of May 9, 2024, NFP ruled out of order TE Bigelow’s
Complaints 2, 3, 4, and 5. Complaint 2 was ruled administratively out of order
on the grounds that, “since TE Kevin Bigelow was ordained as an assistant
pastor at Christ Church East, Jacksonville, FL, and that the Christ Church East
Session, not the North Florida Presbytery, lawfully dissolved the pastoral
relationship between Christ Church East and TE Kevin Bigelow.” Complaints
3-5 were ruled judicially out of order on the grounds that the actions
complained against were not the acts or decisions of Presbytery. On each of
these rulings Presbytery erred.

As was discussed above, it is true that the Session of CCE was the calling body
for TE Bigelow. It is not correct, however, that this fact means that the Session
of CCE could or did dissolve the Assistant Pastoral relationship between itself
and TE Bigelow. _BCO_ 23-1 is clear that it is Presbytery that makes the final
determination on the dissolution of a pastoral call. Presbytery’s understanding
of that point was evidenced by the fact that they acted at their meeting of
February 8, 2024, to “approve [Clerk’s note: support, as explained in the MNA
Committee’s report] Christ Church East Session’ ( _sic_ ) dissolution of TE
Bigelow’s call with Christ Church East as Assistant Pastor/Beaches Church
Planter ( _BCO_ 22-4, Cf.23-2).” Since it was the action of Presbytery that
ultimately determined that TE Bigelow’s call would be dissolved, he had every
right to complain that this action was not rightly brought before Presbytery.

Complaint 3 raised concerns about whether the dissolution of the pastoral
relationship was brought about as a form of church discipline. Since Presbytery
is the final arbiter with regard to whether a call should be dissolved, TE
Bigelow was within his rights to complain to Presbytery alleging they
dissolved his call as a form of discipline.

<a id="ga52-p996"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=998 printed_page=996 -->

Complaints 4 and 5 raised concerns about the work of Presbytery’s Mediation
Commission and Presbytery’s actions with regard to mediation efforts. The
work of a Commission is the action of Presbytery. Further, in this case, part of
the complaints, in essence, raised concerns about the instructions and powers
Presbytery granted to the Mediation Commission. Thus, TE Bigelow was
within his rights to complain about the mediation efforts of Presbytery.

Normally, Presbytery’s error in ruling these Complaints out of order would
have resulted in us sending the matter(s) back to the lower court with
instruction that they conduct a new hearing. In this case, however, Presbytery’s
representative said during the hearing that if the SJC found Presbytery erred in
their out of order rulings he would prefer that the SJC immediately rule on the
substance of the Complaints. For that reason, the parties, in their briefs and
oral arguments, focused on the substance of Complaints 2-5. As a result, those
Complaints remain before the SJC for adjudication.

**Issue 3 (Complaint 2)**

Complaint 2 alleges 14 actions whereby Presbytery erred. The core issue in
Complaint 2 is Complainant’s allegation that NFP erred “in dissolving Christ
Church Beaches ( _sic_ ) relation with her pastor, TE Bigelow, ‘Planting Pastor of
Christ Church Beaches’, without holding a congregational vote to ascertain her
desire to be relieved of her pastor, without the tendered resignation of TE
Bigelow, and without TE Bigelow being under any form of formal discipline
or process.” Complainant further noticed that under _BCO_ 5-5 the members of
CCB “shall be understood to assent to the call of that minister and to affirm

the promises made to the pastor in _BCO_ 21-10.” The Complaint argues NFP
erred “in not recognizing that the receiving of members according to _BCO_ 55 into Christ Church Beaches established the pastoral relation directly between
Christ Church Beaches and her pastor, TE Bigelow, not TE Bigelow and any
Session.” Thus, according to Complainant, “The NFP erred in depriving Christ
Church Beaches of the pastoral care of her pastor when the pastoral relation
between Christ Church Beaches and TE Bigelow had never been
constitutionally dissolved.”

Part of Complaint 2 turns on the question of whether TE Bigelow’s call was
as an Assistant Pastor at CCE or the “Planting Pastor” at CCB. We dealt with
that question under Issue 1. There is a larger constitutional question in Issue 3
(Complaint 2). That is the question of the rights of members of a mission

<a id="ga52-p997"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=999 printed_page=997 -->

church with regard to the dissolution of the pastoral relationship with the man
who has been pastoring them.

Complainant argued that the provisions of _BCO_ 5, especially 5-5, read in light
of Preliminary Principle 6, mean that members of a mission church have the
same relationship to their pastor, and, thus, the same rights in the case of an
attempt to dissolve that pastoral relationship, as do members o fan organized
church. If that assertion is correct, it would mean that, regardless of how a man
was originally engaged to pastor at a mission work, once members were
received the pastor could not be removed from the mission work absent a vote
of the congregation of the mission work or some act of discipline.

Much of Complainant’s argument is embodied in the following quote:
“consistent with the SJC Harrel ( _sic_ ) et al ruling, _BCO_ 5-2 mission churches
are identical in every way to particular churches save for their need for
temporary governance ( _BCO_ 4-1) while the ‘polity governance between
members of the mission church and its temporary government is the same as
between any congregation and its officers, but only during the time from the
mission church’s inauguration to the time of its particularization.’” This must
mean, according to Complainant, that pastors of mission works can be
removed only in the way that a pastor o fa particular church can be removed.

There are several problems with Complainant’s use of the Harrell decision.
First, the language quoted and the reasoning surrounding that language comes
not from the Harrell decision but from a concurring opinion signed by only
one judge. Thus, this cannot be said to be the ruling of the SJC. Further, the
point being made in the concurring opinion at the point cited by Complainant
is not about how pastoral relationships are dissolved in mission works or the
rights of members in that process. Rather, the concurring opinion is focusing
on the rights of members o fa mission work to call a pastor at the time of
particularization. While Complainant argues that CCB was on a path to
particularization at the point his call was terminated, the fact remains that the
case before us is about the dissolution of a call, not about the rights of a mission
church when they seek to call a pastor at particularization.

Of course, it is true that _BCO_ 5-5 says that members received in a mission
church “shall be understood to assent to the call of that minister and to affirm

the promises made to the pastor in _BCO_ 21-10.” But that language exists to
make clear that the members of the mission work have voluntarily agreed to

<a id="ga52-p998"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1000 printed_page=998 -->

place themselves under the proper authority of a minister properly charged to
labor at the mission work, thus satisfying the principle that no man can be
placed over a congregation without their consent. That says nothing about how
a man can be removed from pa storing a mission work. By definition, a mission
work has a temporary form of government, and that government is provided
under arrangements determined by Presbytery, not by the congregation (see
_BCO_ 5-3). Until the point of particularization, the members of a mission work
have no right to determine the governing structure that will operate in the
mission work, no right to elect the men who will govern then, and no mandate
to consent to the dissolution of a man who is governing them, whether that
man beat e oran RE.

Complainant also argues that there must have been a pastoral relationship
between himself and CCB, which could be dissolved only with the consent of
the Congregation of CCB because the governing mechanisms set forth in _BCO_
5-4 do not make provision for an Assistant Pastor to function under any of the
arrangements. Presbytery argues that _BCO_ 5-4 i snot intended to be exhaustive,
hence the language of “may be provided,” so that Presbytery was within its
rights to assign an Assistant Pastor to work at a mission church. That may be
true, but the fact remains that even if it could be shown that Presbytery had
erred in assigning TE Bigelow, whose call was as an Assistant Pastor, to pastor
at CCB, that did not create a formal pastoral call between TE Bigelow and the
CCB congregation. Such a call could be established only at the time of the
particularization of CCB and only by a vote of the CCB congregation (and the
affirmation of Presbytery). None of those conditions were met in this case.

Of course, nothing in this analysis means that a Presbytery could not consult
with the congregation of a mission work before determining what governing
arrangement would be established, what men would be placed in that
government, or when a decision to remove a man from that government is
being considered. Indeed, such a course will often be wise. But saying that
Presbytery may, if it chooses, seek the advice of the congregation of a mission
work prior to removing a man from pastoring that work, is very different from
saying the congregation of the mission work has the right to vote on such a
removal. Nothing in our Constitution provides that right to the congregation
of a mission church. Thus Complaint 2 is denied.

<a id="ga52-p999"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1001 printed_page=999 -->

**Issue 4 (Complaint 3)**

Complaint 3 alleges three actions whereby Presbytery erred. At the heart of
this Complaint is the allegation that Presbytery unconstitutionally used the
removal of TE Bigelow from pastoring CCB as an act of church discipline,
particularly when the removal was justified with the CCE Session, before
Presbytery, and to the CCB Congregation in terms of “the substantial conflict
surrounding Kevin.”

Putting the Complaint and Complainant’s brief together makes clear that there
are three prongs to this argument. The first prong is the argument that unless
there is a disciplinary process against the pastor, the only ways a pastoral
relationship can be dissolved are those set forth in _BCO_ 23-1—either the pastor
must tender his resignation or the Congregation must vote to ask to be relieved
of their pastor. Since TE Bigelow, the planting pastor of CCB, did not tender
his resignation, and since the Congregation of CCB did not vote to ask
Presbytery to dissolve the call, the only basis for the dissolution of the
relationship would have to be by discipline. There was clearly no formal
process in this matter, so it must have been that the dismissal constituted
discipline without process. As we discuss above, however, while TE Bigelow
was charged to work as a church planter at CCB, the only call in the ROC is
the call of CCE to TE Bigelow to be their Assistant Pastor. In that case the
Session of CCE is the calling body ( _BCO_ 22-3) and thus the Session of CCE
is the body that had the right to petition Presbytery to dissolve the relationship
( _BCO_ 22-4).

The second prong is that the rationale of the CCE Session for asking for the
dissolution, the rationale offered by Presbytery for approving the dissolution,
and the explanation of the dissolution to the CCB Congregation all focus on
things that TE Bigelow was perceived to have done wrong and the conflict
surrounding him. It is a sad reality that many pastoral relationships end
because of broken relationships, often accompanied by accusations against the
pastor. It is not true, however, that when a calling body seeks to have the
pastoral relationship dissolved due to unhappiness with the pastor or broken
relationships that Presbytery has exercised formal discipline that requires
process when it approves that dissolution. It is important to remember that
nothing in _BCO_ 23-1 (para 2) sets any limits on the reasons a calling body may
have for desiring to have the pastoral relationship ended, nor does 23-1 specify
what factors the Presbytery may or may not consider in determining whether

<a id="ga52-p1000"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1002 printed_page=1000 -->

there is sufficient reason to dissolve the relationship. Thus, the Session of CCE
was entirely within its rights in this case to ask Presbytery to dissolve Session’s
relationship with TE Bigelow, and Presbytery was within its rights to grant
that request. Nothing in our Constitution requires that a pastor must be found
guilty of some offense before Presbytery can accede to the calling body’s
request that the pastoral relationship be dissolved. Additionally, no censure
was imposed in this matter. Nothing in Presbytery’s action prevents TE
Bigelow from seeking another call. Indeed, if or when CCB comes to organize,
nothing would prevent CCB from seeking to call TE Bigelow as their pastor.
Presbytery would then have to determine whether agreeing to that call would
be in the interest of religion, but that is a very different matter from the exercise
of formal church discipline.

The third prong focuses on statements that said either these actions were a
“form of discipline” or that discussed specific (formal) disciplinary measures
that one author thought the Presbytery might choose to take with regard to TE
Bigelow. With regard to the first of those concerns, it is important to remember
that _BCO_ 27-1 sets forth two aspects of church discipline. One refers to the
“whole government, inspection, training, guardianship, and control which the
church maintains over its members, officers, and courts.” The other has “a
more restricted and technical sense, signifying judicial process.” If a calling
body raises questions about its relationship with its pastor and if Presbytery
determines the relationship is so broken as to necessitate the dissolution of the
call, then Presbytery has exercised its disciplinary powers under the first
definition of _BCO_ 27-1, but it has not used the second definition. We agree
with Presbytery’s representatives that the word “discipline” or the phrase “in
some degree it is [discipline],” when used in the explanations of Presbytery’s
actions, was reflecting the fact that Presbytery had responded to the conflicts
surrounding TE Bigelow as allowed by the first use of the word “discipline”
in _BCO_ 27-1. We further note that the document that does suggest that formal
church discipline might be necessary is labeled as being part of a report from
a committee, and there is evidence that it may be the work of only one
individual. In either case, Presbytery did not take any action on that part of the
committee’s report, so it cannot be said to represent the action, or even the
opinion, of Presbytery.

Complaint 3 is, therefore, denied. There is not evidence that the action of the
CCE Session to seek the dissolution of the call was beyond their bounds, or
that the action of Presbytery in approving the dissolution was beyond their

<a id="ga52-p1001"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1003 printed_page=1001 -->

proper authority. Further, while there is plenty of evidence that these actions
were taken to deal with a situation in which conflict had become problematic,
there is no evidence that any _BCO_ 30 censure was imposed. TE Bigelow was
and is still free to take the Lord’s Supper, to preach, and to seek another call.

**Issue 5 (Complaint 4)**

Complaint 4 alleges seven actions where the Mediation Commission of NFP
erred. Among the issues alleged are that the Mediation Commission did not
use a third party mediator, that it ended its efforts too soon, that it did not
require certain individuals to meet with each other, that it did not require
individuals to “own their sins” or to name the sins they believed others had
committed, and by not “engaging in ‘instruction in the word’ and admonishing
one another, the first steps in biblical discipline and reconciliation.”

While a formal mediation effort may often be useful in situations where there
is conflict, nothing in our Constitution requires mediation in the formal sense
discussed here, nor does our Constitution mandate what should be involved in
such mediation efforts. Thus, this Complaint involves a matter in which we
must grant great deference to the lower court. Presbytery erected and
empowered the Commission, and Presbytery gave the Commission the
suggestion of using a third-party mediator. Presbytery did not mandate the use
of a third party nor did Presbytery mandate that any set of individuals meet
with each other or the Mediation Commission (assuming Presbytery even has
that power). The Mediation Commission did the work it felt it needed to do.
That work may not have satisfied all parties, but that does not mean it was
unconstitutional, or even unwise. Further, there is no evidence that Presbytery
was dissatisfied with the work of the Commission.

In view of the above considerations, and given that this is an issue on which
we must give great deference to the lower court ( _BCO_ 39-3.2,3), we find no
clear error that would require us to sustain this Complaint. Complaint 4 is,
therefore, denied.

**Issue 6 (Complaint 5)**

Complaint 5 alleges two errors with regard to the mediation efforts of NFP.
One alleged error was that the process demonstrated partiality in that the
Administrative Committee, empowered as a commission to establish the

<a id="ga52-p1002"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1004 printed_page=1002 -->

Mediation Commission, allowed itself to be “circularized” by meeting with
the Session of CCE and hearing additional concerns about TE Bigelow. This,
in Complainant’s view, led some members of the soon-to-be-appointed
Mediation Commission to call for TE Bigelow’s resignation. In Complainant’s
view, this partiality also went to failures on the part of the Mediation
Commission to appropriately seek truth from ones with whom they were
involved, and to give “a substantially incomplete summary report” to NFP.
The second errant action was in ending the mediation effort without the
involvement of a third party.

We have already discussed the latitude a Presbytery has in determining
whether to establish a mediation process and in how that process is to be
conducted. The Mediation Commission, acting for Presbytery, had that same
latitude, so long as they operated within the parameters set by Presbytery when
the Commission was established. While Complainant may have wished the
Commission would have acted in different ways, there is no evidence that there
was any constitutional violation. Further, the requirement that one not
“circularize the court” is used in the _BCO_ only in the context of formal process
(see 42-4 and 43-2). As there was no formal process in view here, there was
no bar to anyone talking to either Commission or to whom either Commission
could speak. Finally, while Complainant may not have gotten the result he
wanted from the Mediation Commission, there is no evidence that any action
was taken against him by the Commission. Certainly it is true that Complainant
argues that sins against himself and his family were left unredressed, but the
SJC has no ability to assess or redress that based on a Complaint about the
work of the Mediation Commission. Therefore, Complaint 5 is denied.

**Final Comments**

We are certainly saddened to see this evidence of conflict in NFP. We do not,
however, have the authority or the information to seek to redress, based on
these Complaints, whatever wrongs were committed that originated or
prolonged the conflict that led to this Case. As such, this Decision makes no
comment on allegations raised by or against any of the parties involved, nor
do we reach any conclusion as to whether the various decisions of NFP were
wise. Our decision is limited to an assessment of the constitutional issues

raised by Complainant and the question of whether Presbytery committed
“clear error” in any of its decisions. We conclude that Complainant’s argument
about the relationship established between a mission work and the church

<a id="ga52-p1003"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1005 printed_page=1003 -->

planting pastor does not withstand constitutional scrutiny. We also find that
NFP erred constitutionally in declaring four of the Complaints to be out of
order, but such error was harmless in this case in that the merits of the
Complaints were argued before and decided on by this body. Further, we
conclude that on the other issues either the facts favored the position of
Respondent Presbytery or there was no evidence to indicate Presbytery had
committed clear error.

Finally, we do note that had Session done a better job of documenting its call
process in its minutes, had some documents been more clearly written and
labeled, and had some chosen words more carefully, some of these issues
might not have had to come before us. Thus, we urge all courts of the PCA to
take more care to be sure their records are clear and that they include all the
information that might be necessary in case a Complaint should arise against
them.

**V. AMENDS**

There are no amends required in this matter.

**VI. RECORDING IN MINUTES**

The SJC instructs NFP to record this Decision, and any Concurring and
Dissenting Opinions, in the minutes of the next stated meeting following its
receipt of the final Decision of the full SJC.

The proposed decision was written by RE Neikirk and was amended by the
Panel. It was approved by a vote of 3-0 with both alternates expressing their
concurrence with the decision.

The SJC reviewed each part of the proposed decision and after the approval of
various amendments, the vote on the decision was **22-0-0**, **with 1 not**
**qualified, and 1 absent.**

Bankson _Concur_ Eggert _Concur_ Maynard _Concur_
Bise _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Neikirk _Concur_

<a id="ga52-p1004"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1006 printed_page=1004 -->

Bruce _Concur_ Garner _Concur_ Pickering _Concur_
Carrell _Concur_ Greco _Concur_ Sartorius _Concur_
Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall _Concur_
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Concur_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe _Not Qualified_ Lee _Concur_ White _Absent_
Dowling _Concur_ Marcinak _Concur_ Wilson _Concur_

RE Donahoe indicated he was disqualified because he was unable to
complete the required reading.

**CASE 2024-05**

**TE KEVIN BIGELOW v.**

**NORTH FLORIDA PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON COMPLAINT**

**Concurring Opinion**
TE David F. Coffin, Jr., joined by RE Steve Dowling

March 24, 2025

I readily concurred with the Statement of the Issues, the Judgment, and
the Reasoning and Opinion in the above-named decision. However, in the
Summary of Facts, at “08/26/23,” one finds:

NFP held a called meeting to “discuss the relationship between TE
Kevin Bigelow and the Christ Church East Session and to receive
recommendations from the Administrative Committee regarding these
matters.” TE Keith Dickerson, Pastor of CCE, presented a
communication on behalf of Session wherein he laid out some of

Session’s concerns regarding TE Bigelow. He also stated that, based
on a conversation with [an authority], . . . Session was of the opinion
that it could either deal with its concerns regarding TE Bigelow via
formal process or “according to BCO 12-5-e, [it] could use the
authority granted to a session to remove a teaching elder from the
pulpit.” (SJC 2024-05 Bigelow v. North Florida Presbytery, Decision
on Complaint, p. 3.)

Although according to the Record of the Case, Presbytery, rightly, _did not_
_accede_ to this claim of authority, Presbytery did so on other grounds, and did

<a id="ga52-p1005"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1007 printed_page=1005 -->

not need to address the interpretation alleged. That notwithstanding, it is of
some importance to point out the error of the Session’s interpretation of _BCO_
12-5.e.:

. . . to ensure that the Word of God is preached only by such men as
are sufficiently qualified ( _BCO_ 4-4, 53-2, 1 Timothy 2:11-12). . . .

Some, apparently even some with standing in such matters, have supposed that
this provision gives the Session the right, at any time, to inspect the
qualifications of the installed minister of the church, and if he is found
wanting, the Session, by majority vote, may remove him from the pulpit. This
is a serious mistake, and I would not have my concurrence be understood to
give it credence. such a view is a profound threat to our scriptural polity, and
to the purity and peace of the church.
First, as a matter of our polity overall, this _is not_, and _cannot_ be, the
meaning of this provision. Under our polity the Session has nothing
whatsoever to do with the qualifications or fitness of the minister. In most
cases, the Congregation, exclusively, calls the minister to the pulpit, and
Presbytery, exclusively, approves his qualifications and installs the minister.
The minister may not, as a censure, be removed against his will from that work
apart from due process exclusively before the Presbytery ( _BCO_ 34-1; 13-9.a;
13-9.c.; 13-9.d.). He may be removed from the work without censure, but only
at his own request, or at the request of the Congregation, and, in either case,
the concurrence of the Presbytery is necessary ( _BCO_ 23-1). The only exception
to this analysis is the case of an Assistant Pastor. Here the Session calls the
minister and Presbytery approves the qualifications and installs the minister.
Yet here too, the minister may not, as a censure, be removed against his will
from that work apart from due process exclusively before the Presbytery. He
may be removed from the work without censure, but only at his own request,
or at the request of the Session, and, in either case, the concurrence of the
Presbytery is necessary.
Second, to understand what _BCO_ 12-5.e. _does_ provide for, one must
understand something of its historical context, as it is relatively new to the
_BCO_ . The language of _BCO_ 12-5.e., “. . . to ensure that the Word of God is
preached only by such men as are sufficiently qualified ( _BCO_ 4-4, 53-2, 1
Timothy 2:11-12). . . .” was added to chapter 12 at the 30 [th] General Assembly
in 2002 (cf. _M30GA_, 30-10, Item 6, p. 71-72). The addition was proposed to
remedy the problem that some ministers and Sessions in the PCA apparently
believed that so long as they agreed not to have women _in office_, women could,

<a id="ga52-p1006"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1008 printed_page=1006 -->

nonetheless, be allowed to preach or teach in services of the church that include
the men and women of the church. The proposal in question came only two
years after an infamous case that brought such a view before the Assembly
judicially, causing confusion and controversy in its wake.
An overture was proposed to the 29 [th] GA in 2001 as Overture 27, from
the Presbytery of Philadelphia to “Amend _BCO_ 12-5e to Prohibit Women from
Preaching” (See _M29GA_, 29-44, III, 18, p. 223). The Overture was answered
in the affirmative, as amended by a Minority Report of the Bills and Overtures
Committee, and sent down to the presbyteries. The requisite number of
presbyteries approved.
The rationale for the amendment was set forth as follows:

GROUNDS:

1. It is absolutely clear that Scripture prohibits women preaching;
2. Some within the PCA have questioned whether our standards

prohibit women from preaching.
3. This amendment places responsibility for ensuring that women do

not preach at the feet of the session where it belongs; and
4. The amendment properly belongs in the section of the _BCO_ which

discusses the duties of the session.

One can clearly see from this account, as the amendment was put
before the Assembly and the Presbyteries, this provision has nothing to do with
the Session inspecting the qualifications of the _installed_ minister of the church,
but only has to do with making sure that only qualified _men_ fill the pulpit in
the minister’s absence (cf. _BCO_ 53-6, “No person should be invited to preach
in any of the churches under our care without the consent of the Session.”).

<a id="ga52-p1007"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1009 printed_page=1007 -->

**CASE 2024-06**

**PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

**v.**

**SOUTH COAST PRESBYTERY**

**RULING ON CASE**

**February 19, 2025**

This case came before the SJC at the direction of the 51 [st] General Assembly
based upon recommendations of the Committee on Review of Presbytery
Records (CRPR). By Citation of August 12, 2024, South Coast Presbytery
(SCP) was directed to respond to multiple exceptions. The Citation stemmed
from a pattern of SCP noncompliance, a continuing failure to respond to noted
exceptions to its minutes.

A Panel was appointed, and organized with RE Dan Carrell serving as
Chairman, RE Jim Eggert as Secretary, and TE Paul Kooistra as the other
principal member. TE Paul Bankson and RE Caleb Stegall served as
Alternates. The Panel was informed that the noncompliance was due to the
failure of the then previous stated clerk of SCP to carry out his duties. He was
replaced by one who diligently prepared and filed the requested responses with
the CRPR representative handling this matter, which had been approved by
Presbytery in two parts, the latter in January 2025.

From the outset the parties (through the then SCP stated clerk and the CRPR
representative) communicated with each other and agreed to submit the matter
to the SJC in writing in lieu of a hearing. Their having since agreed that the
requested responses had been received, the SJC has unanimously concluded
that SCP has fully complied with the Citation of August 12, 2024. The SJC,
however, takes no position on the contents of those responses, for those
contents are beyond the scope of the Citation and have yet to be approved by
CRPR.

SJC reviewed the proposed ruling and approved the final version of the Ruling
by vote of **19-0-1, with 3 absent and 1 recused** .

Bankson _Concur_ Eggert _Concur_ Maynard _Concur_
Bise _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Neikirk Recused

<a id="ga52-p1008"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1010 printed_page=1008 -->

Bruce _Concur_ Garner Absent Pickering _Concur_
Carrell _Concur_ Greco _Concur_ Sartorius _Concur_
Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall Absent
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Concur_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe Absent Lee _Abstain_ White _Concur_

Dowling _Concur_ Marcinak _Concur_ Wilson _Concur_

RE Neikirk recused himself in this Case because he was a member of the

Review of Presbytery Records Read Team that identified the exceptions that
led to the Citation.

<a id="ga52-p1009"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1011 printed_page=1009 -->

**CASE 2024-08**

**MS. JENNIFER BIGELOW**

**v.**

**NORTH FLORIDA PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON COMPLAINT**

**October 17, 2024**

The Complaint is Out of Order and cannot be put in order _(OMSJC_ 9.2)
because the Complainant is not a member of the Presbytery against whose
action she has complained. Therefore, the SJC rules the Complainant did not
have standing to file a _BCO_ 43-1 complaint with Presbytery. Presbytery
correctly found the Complaint out of order.

See similar SJC rulings on standing in:

Case 2023-01: _Hann v. Pee Dee Presbytery_ ( _M51GA_, 2024, p. 2024),
Case 2020-13: _Benyola v. Central Florida_ ( _M48GA_, 2021, p. 817),
Case 2020-01: _Benyola v. Central Florida_ ( _M48GA_, 2021, p. 801),
Case 2012-08: _RE Warren Jackson v. Northwest Georgia_ ( _M43GA_,

2015, p. 568),
Case 2012-06: _Deacon Don Bethel v. Southeast Alabama_ ( _M41GA_,

2013, p. 614), and
Case 92-9b: _Mr. Overman v. Eastern Carolina_ ( _M21GA_,1993, p.

223).

**AMENDS** - None

**RECORDING IN MINUTES**

The SJC instructs the Presbytery to record this Decision in the minutes of the
next stated meeting following its receipt of the full Decision.

The SJC Officers drafted the decision, which was adopted as the SJC's final
decision by vote of **23-0-0, with 1 absent.**

**CASE 2024-10**
**MR. PAUL GOZÉ**

**v.**

**HILLS AND PLAINS PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON APPEAL**

**February 19, 2025**

This case is judicially out of order and cannot be put in order because it was
not timely filed. On January 10, 2024, in the course o fa trial, the trial court
determined the Appellant to be contumacious, and it suspended him from the
sacraments. The Appellant appealed that decision to Presbytery on May 8,
2024, more than thirty (30) days after notification of the decision of the lower
court. As the appeal was not timely filed as required by _BCO_ 42-4, it is
judicially out of order ( _OMSJC_ 10.5(a)) and must be dismissed.

This decision does not reach or address any substantive issue or allegation in
the case.

SJC reviewed the proposed decision and approved the final version of the
Ruling by vote of **19-1-0, 3 absent and 1 recused** .

Bankson _Concur_ Eggert _Dissent_ Maynard _Concur_
Bise _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Neikirk _Concur_
Bruce _Recused_ Garner _Absent_ Pickering _Concur_
Carrell _Concur_ Greco _Concur_ Sartorius _Concur_

Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall Absent
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Concur_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe Absent Lee _Concur_ White _Concur_
Dowling _Concur_ Marcinak _Concur_ Wilson _Concur_

the dispute are dear friends.”

<a id="ga52-p1012"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1014 printed_page=1012 -->

**CASE 2024-10**
**MR. PAUL GOZÉ**

**VS.**

**HILLS AND PLAINS PRESBYTERY**

**DISSENT**

RE Jim Eggert

March 10, 2025

The instant appeal was found “judicially out of order” because it was untimely.
“Judicially out of order” cases often result in a decision providing limited
information. Because the particular facts and circumstances underlying the
SJC’s decision are invisible to the reader, I offer this dissent in hopes that it
increases understanding of the rules governing the timeliness of appeals.

While technical, timeliness is a matter of real consequence (1) to censured
members who wish to secure higher court review of a judgment against, (2) to
courts of original jurisdiction that seek to achieve finality of their decisions in
the exercise of their rightful powers and (3) to the denomination whose polity
is strengthened by higher court review.

In general, a person censured b yo neo four courts enjoys aright of appeal,
making such censure “subject to the review and control of the higher courts,
in regular gradation.” ( _BCO_ 11-4). The right of appeal, however, is not
absolute, being circumscribed by the prescriptions of _BCO_ Chapter 42,
including the thirty-day time limit for filing an appeal set out in _BCO_ 42-4.

It is my view that the time limit imposed by _BCO_ 42-4 should be strictly
construed because it removes the rights of censured individuals and cuts off
higher court review, an interpretive approach favoring the preservation of both
the rights of individuals as well as the realization of the “mutual relation”
( _BCO_ 11-4) of our courts inherent to our presbyterian form of government.

_**A Quick Look at the Circumstances of This Case**_

In this case, the session conducted a trial of appellant o na four-specification
indictment. During his trial, the Moderator gave the Appellant “four warnings
for his behavior” in the trial proceedings and eventually determined that the

<a id="ga52-p1013"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1015 printed_page=1013 -->

Appellant was contumacious pursuant to _BCO_ 32-6, which provides, “when
an accused person shall appear and refuse to cooperate with lawful
proceedings,” he shall be dealt with for his contumacy. _BCO_ 33-2 provides,
“When an accused person is found contumacious (cf. 32-6), he shall be
immediately suspended from the sacraments (and if an officer from his office)
for his contumacy.”

Having determined that Appellant had transgressed _BCO_ 32-6, the Session
suspended the trial, leaving the underlying indictment unadjudicated, and the
Moderator declared the censure of suspension from the Sacraments in the
Appellant’s presence.

The Appellant appealed the Session’s decision to the Presbytery some months
after the Session’s oral pronouncement. The Presbytery found that the appeal
was “not in order and thus should not be entertained” because Appellant “did
not meet the requirements of _BCO_ 42-2, nor was the appeal filed in a timely
manner in accordance with _BCO_ 42-4.” _BCO_ 42-2 provides: “The only parties
entitled to an appeal are those who have submitted to a regular trial…” _BCO_
42-4 provides: “Notice of appeal … shall be filed by the appellant with both
the clerk of the lower court and the clerk of the higher court, within thirty (30)
days of notification of the last court’s decision.”

Because the SJC’s decision cites only _BCO_ 42-4 relating to the _timeliness_ of
the appeal as the rationale for its decision, I will not discuss any implications
of _BCO_ 42-2 which would address whether Appellant was entitled to file an
appeal at all.

_**A Detailed Explanation of my Interpretation of BCO 42-4**_

I believe that the appeal was timely. The thirty-day period prescribed by _BCO_
42-4 never began to run because the Session never reduced its decision to
writing and never delivered the same to the Appellant in the manner prescribed
by _BCO_ 42-4.

_BCO_ 42-4 provides:

Notice of appeal may be given the court before its
adjournment. Written notice of appeal, with supporting
reasons, shall be filed by the appellant with both the clerk of

<a id="ga52-p1014"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1016 printed_page=1014 -->

the lower court and the clerk of the higher court, within thirty
(30) days of notification of the last court’s decision.

Therefore, strictly speaking, any notice of appeal must be filed “within thirty
(30) days of _notification_ of the last court’s decision” (emphasis added).

To faithfully apply this rule, we must settle on a satisfactory definition of
“notification.”

I interpret _BCO_ 42-4 to mean that _notification_ refers to a _written notification_
from the court, meaning that a court’s in-person pronouncement of censure
does not start the thirty-day period.

The history of the development of _BCO_ 42-4 informs my interpretive
understanding.

At the commencement of the PCA in 1973 our polity did not calculate the time
for filing an appeal from “notification” of the court’s decision but measured
the appeal period from the date of the court’s “rising,” which is another way
of referring to the conclusion of the meeting of the court. _Rules of Discipline_
16-4 (M1GA 1973, page 153). In 1984 the General Assembly increased the
time for filing an appeal to fifteen days from the “meeting of the court.”
(M12GA 1984, page 88). Three years later the General Assembly increased
the time to thirty days from the same starting point. (M15GA 1987, page 95).
In the case of censures imposed _in absentia_, this rubric made it possible for the
appeal period to commence regardless of when the censured individual learned
of the censure against him.

In 2013 the General Assembly abandoned the “meeting of the court” rubric,
marking a sea change in _BCO_ 42-2. The preceding year Pacific Northwest
Presbytery had offered an Overture proposing that “the date the person
receives a copy of the court’s decision,” rather than the date of the “meeting
of the court,” should commence the appeal period. (M40GA 2012, page 697).
The rationale offered by the Overture stated:

The 30-day appeal clock should not start until the guilty
person receives a copy of the court's decision. For example if
a session judges a church member guilty and imposes a
censure, his 30-day clock should begin the day he receives a

<a id="ga52-p1015"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1017 printed_page=1015 -->

copy of the session decision, rather than beginning on the date
of the session meeting. In addition, if he appeals to pres byte ry
and it is denied, his 30-day clock should begin the day he
receives a copy of the Presbytery decision on his appeal,
rather than the date of the Presbytery meeting. Presumably,
the decision would be delivered in person, by certified mail,
or in some other manner that ensures verification of the date

of receipt (similar to _BCO_ 32-4 regarding citations).

( _M40GA_ 2012, page 698). Notably, this proposal seemed to suggest that the
rule should be changed to require that the court reduce its decision to writing,
since the thirty-day appeal period would not begin to run until the censured
individual “received a copy” of the same. Obviously, an in-person censure
does not entail the “receipt of a copy” of anything.

Although the 40 [th] General Assembly (on the recommendation of the Overtures
Committee) did not adopt the Overture in exactly the form proposed, it adopted
its essential innovation that the time for counting the thirty day appeal period
would no longer be measured from the date of the “meeting of the court,” but
from the censured person’s “notification of the last court’s decision” and added
a paragraph describing how “notification shall be deemed to have occurred.”
Here is the change that was adopted:

Notice of appeal may be given the court before its
adjournment. Written notice of appeal, with supporting
reasons, shall be filed by the appellant with both the clerk of
the lower court and the clerk of the higher court, within thirty
(30) days following the meeting of the court of notification of
the last Court's decision. Notification shall be deemed to have

occurred on the day of mailing (if certified, registered or
express mail of a national postal service or any private service
where verifying receipt is utilized), the day of hand delivery,
or the day of confirmed receipt in the case of e-mail or
facsimile. Furthermore, compliance with such requirements
shall be deemed to have been fulfilled if a party cannot be
located after diligent inquiry or if a party refuses to accept
delivery. No attempt should be made to circularize the courts
to which appeal is being made by either party before the case
is heard.

<a id="ga52-p1016"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1018 printed_page=1016 -->

(M40GA 2012, page 73). The rationale offered by the Overtures Committee
for the amendment was as follows:

The change provides a bright line for when the clock begins
for the filing of an appeal. Rather than having the clock begin
potentially before notification of a court’s decision becomes
available to an appellant, the clock begins in relation to
notification of the decision. Provision is made for various

forms of proof of notification, with language adapted from
_OMSJC_ 18.10.

(M40GA 2012, page 73). The following year, the General Assembly adopted
the change with the requisite Presbytery approval. (M41GA 2013, page 110).
_BCO_ 42-4 was amended again in 2021 when the General Assembly brought
the first two sentences of _BCO_ 42-2 into conformity with one another so that
the same phrase -- “notification of the last Court’s decision” -- is used in each.
(M48GA 2021, page 145-146). The relevant section now reads:

Notice of appeal may be given the court before its
adjournment. Written notice of appeal, with supporting
reasons, shall be filed by the appellant with both the clerk of
the lower court and the clerk of the higher court, within thirty
(30) days of notification of the last court’s decision.
Notification of the last court’s decision shall be deemed to

have occurred on the day of mailing (if certified, registered or
express mail of a national postal service or any private service
where verifying receipt is utilized), the day of hand delivery,
or the day of confirmed receipt in the case of e-mail or
facsimile. Furthermore, compliance with such requirements
shall be deemed to have been fulfilled if a party cannot be
located after diligent inquiry or if a party refuses to accept
delivery...

Some understand that the 2013 and 2021 revisions of _BCO_ 42-4 hold that the
court’s in-person pronouncement of censure is a qualifying “notification”
triggering the thirty-day appeal period. According to this view, the “deeming”
clause added to the second sentence of _BCO_ 42-4 simply afforded _additional_
methods of “notification” besides the in-person pronouncement. For example,

<a id="ga52-p1017"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1019 printed_page=1017 -->

if a censured individual fails or refuses to personally appear for the declaration
of his censure, the “deeming” clause gives the court a means to institute the
thirty-day appeal clock. Similarly, the new provision afforded a means to start
the clock in cases where the convicted individual either cannot be found or

refuses to accept delivery. But, on this view, regardless of these additional
methods, “notification” would nevertheless be satisfied by a court’s in-person
announcement of censure, apparently rendering the express list of notification
methods either redundant or unnecessary where an in-person censure has been
imposed.

I am not persuaded of that interpretation of _BCO_ 42-4.

_**Considerations of Interpretation**_

While others read the phrase _notification shall be deemed to have occurred on_
_…_ to leave open other methods not in the list following the clause, I interpret
the mandatory verb “shall” to exclude any of the notification methods not
appearing in the list, such as an in-person pronouncement of censure. The
auxiliary verb “shall” ina law or rule usually signifies a mandatory obligation
or requirement and is often used to express that something must be done,
indicating a strong sense of compulsion or duty. _Shall_ is often contrasted with
_may_, where shall is mandatory and _may_ is permissive. Thus, we might read
_shall_ as _has a duty_ or _is required to_ . I propose that when the word “shall” can
reasonably be read as mandatory, it ought to be so read.

_BCO_ 42-4 begins, “Notice of appeal may be given the court before its
adjournment.” Here we find the use of the permissive _may_ . Thus, a person
dissatisfied with the court’s ruling _may_ give a “Notice of Appeal” at the
meeting of the court where the censure is imposed. This sentence does not
specify whether the “notice” must be in writing or may be spoken, but
presumably it could be either. Thus, we must at least consider the possibility
that “notice” might encompass an in-person statement for purposes of _BCO_
42-4, although this consideration is diminished by the fact that _BCO_ 42-4 uses
the word “notification” rather than “notice” in the parts of the text that are
dispositive of this matter.

The section continues, “Written notice of appeal”—the same phrase is used as
in the first sentence—”with supporting reasons, shall be filed by the appellant
with both the clerk of the lower court and the clerk of the higher court, within

<a id="ga52-p1018"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1020 printed_page=1018 -->

thirty (30) days of notification of the last court’s decision.” Obviously, a
“Written notice of appeal” must be in writing. Therefore, in this instance _notice_
means notice reduced to writing rather than oral notice. The following phrase,
s _hall be filed_ means that the written notice of appeal _is required_ to be filed
“within thirty (30) days of notification of the last court’s decision.” But does
the word _notification_ in that last phrase refer to delivery of a written document,
delivery of an oral pronouncement, or either mode? This is the critical
question.

We may note in passing that to secure his appeal, an appellant must file a
_written_ notice with supporting reasons. Is it not intuitive that the court
imposing the censure should similarly reduce its decision to writing in
connection with providing “notification” of the same to an appellant? This
interpretation would assign a fitting parity between the written “Notice of
appeal” required of the appellant and the “notification of the last court’s
decision” required by the court imposing censure. The expectation of parity in
the form of a written decision is not proven by the text so far, but this
conclusion becomes clearer as we read forward.

Thus, we continue to the next sentence of _BCO_ 42-4: “Notification of the last
court’s decision shall be deemed to have occurred on the day of mailing (if
certified, registered or express mail of a national postal service or any private
service where verifying receipt is utilized), the day of hand delivery, or the day
of confirmed receipt in the case of e-mail or facsimile.”

The passive voice, _shall be deemed_, makes the interpretation of the full phrase
“notification of the last court’s decision shall be deemed to have occurred”

somewhat awkward. On the one hand, the section does not say, “the court shall
provide written notification in the following manner…” That would have made
it easy to understand that a censuring court ha san obligation to provide a
written notification in at least one of the prescribed ways.

Instead of this clearer formulation, _BCO_ 42-4 uses the phrase _shall be deemed_
_to have occurred_, using the auxiliary verb “shall” to modify the passive verb
phrase _be_ _deemed_ . We can fairly take this to mean that the court _has a duty to_
_deem_ or _is required_ to _deem_ the “notification of the last court’s decision” to
have occurred on the date prescribed according to the listed modes of delivery.
To “deem” means _to regard or consider in a specified way; to come to think_
_or judge; to hold_ . Therefore, for example, this phrase can be fairly reiterated

<a id="ga52-p1019"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1021 printed_page=1019 -->

as “notification of the last court’s decision shall be _judged_ to have occurred on
the day of mailing…” I read the mandatory verb phrase “ _shall_ be deemed” to
create both a clarification of the preceding sentence of _BCO_ 42-4 and a
prescription for a written notification of the last court’s decision to the
censured person in one of the prescribed modes. The contrary interpretation
effectively, and I think mistakenly, supposes that a court “ _may_ ”—not “ _shall_ ”
as I understand the provision to mandate— _deem_, _judge_ or _regard_ notification
as occurring in some other way than the modes listed in _BCO_ 42-4 (such as,
for example, via the in-person imposition of censure).

I believe my conclusion is supported by the fair use and understanding of the
word “notification.” Notification usually means (1) _the act or an instance of_
_notifying_ or (2) _a written or printed matter that gives notice_ . ( _Webster's Ninth_
_Collegiate Dictionar_ y, 1991). Since the latter definition obviously mandates
delivery of a _written_ form of the last court’s decision, I will discuss only the
former sense which, for the reasons set out below, supports the same result.

Applying the former definition— _the_ _act oran instance of notifying_ —as
