informing the word “notification” in the phrase “notification of the last court’s
decision” would mean that an appeal shall be filed within thirty days of _the act_
_or an instance of notifying the appellant_ of the last court's decision _._ To _notify_
may mean alternately (1) _to give notice of or report the occurrence of_ or (2) _to_
_give formal notice_ ( _Webster's Ninth Collegiate Dictionar_ y, 1991).

Taking the first definition of “notify” would mean that an appeal shall be filed
within thirty days of _the act or an instance of giving notice of_ the court’s
decision. But what does “giving notice” refer to? A dictionary analysis reveals
that _notice_ (which must be “given” _)_ bears a range of meaning. The “act” or
“instance” of “giving notice” might mean variously an _announcement_, _the_
_condition of being warned or notified_ (usually expressed by the phrase “on
notice”), _information_, or _a_ _written or printed announcement_ . This definition is
ambiguous because, considering the possible range of meaning described
above, “giving notice” might be plausibly satisfied either by the court’s _in-_
_person pronouncement_ of censure on the accused orb y delivery o fa _written_
_announcement_ of the same.

In my view this ambiguity should be settled in favor of a stricter reading of
_BCO_ 42-4 requiring a written decision. This is because (1) because cutting off
the right to an appeal is in derogation of the rights of individuals who have

<a id="ga52-p1020"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1022 printed_page=1020 -->

been censured and (2) because cutting off the “review and control of the higher
courts” i sin derogation of the “regular gradation” inherent to our system of
polity. ( _BCO_ 11-4).

Having disposed of the first use of “notify,” we turn to the next. This would
mean “notification of the last court’s decision” would require that an appeal
shall be filed within thirty days of _reporting the occurrence of_ the court’s
decision _._ This definition would seem to require something more than the
occurrence of an in-person pronouncement of censure because certainly the
occurrence of the censure is a different thing than the _reporting of the_
_occurrence_ of that censure.

Similarly, if we assume that “notification of the last court’s decision” means
that an appeal shall be filed within thirty days of _giving formal notice_ to the
appellant of the court’s decision, we conclude yet again that written notice
would be required _. F_ o _rmal notice_ is commonly understood as official
information provided in writing advising someone of a specific action he needs
to take ora situation he needs to be aware of, usually with a clear deadline and
potential consequences if not followed. That definition would not be satisfied
by an unwritten in-person censure.

For all these reasons, I believe that the best interpretation of the express words
“notification of the last court’s decision” is that the thirty-day appeal period
only begins after a _written notification_ of the court’s decision is delivered to a
censured person.

_**Policy Considerations**_

Not only do the express words of _BCO_ 42-4 support my proposed
interpretation, but there are also good policy reasons for interpreting _BCO_ 424 as I do.

atria land resulting censure can b ea confusing and overwhelming experience
for an accused, and it would be neither just nor wise to permit an accused’s
thirty day appeal period to commence (and expire) based solely on the inperson censure of a court, thus forcing a censured individual to act based on
his perception of the court’s in-person pronouncement, subject a sit is to the
frailties and errors that may be the fruit of his own misapprehension,
confusion, or errors. Additionally, a court’s failure or refusal to memorialize

<a id="ga52-p1021"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1023 printed_page=1021 -->

its decision in a writing delivered to the censured individual increases the risk
of miscommunication or misunderstanding about the court’s decision.

Further, the burden of providing written notification strictly in accordance with
one of the modes prescribed in the second sentence of _BCO_ 42-4 is rightly
placed upon the court imposing censure since its censure removes “rights and
privileges” that the accused would otherwise enjoy in Christ’s Church. ( _BCO_
6-4). In the absence of a conforming “notification” and proof of one of the
approved modes of delivery, I believe that the accused may file his appeal at
any time since the thirty days cannot commence until the censuring court’s
strict compliance with _BCO_ 42-4 has been established.

Also, the 2012 Overture’s Committee’s rationale that the General Assembly
create a new “bright line” for commencing the thirty-day appeal period
oriented around “notification” rather than the “meeting of the court,” is best
understood as preferring _written notification_ . The 2012 Overtures Committee
expressed its concern that, prior to the amendment, the thirty-day clock might
have been understood to begin to run “before notification of a court’s decision

[became] available to an appellant.” But, consistent with the Overture from
Pacific Northwest Presbytery, the Overtures Committee’s recommendation
rooted “notification” in the decision’s “becoming available to an appellant,”
which seems to point away from the idea that the in-person imposition of
censure should serve as the triggering mechanism, suggesting instead that the
delivery of a _written memorialization_ of the decision in one of the prescribed
modes is required to commence the thirty-day period. In common parlance,
we would not describe a court’s pronouncement of censure in person as a
court’s decision “becoming available” to an individual. We would much more
easily understand the phrase “become available” as referring to the delivery of
a written decision.

Further, the proposed “bright line for when the clock begins for the filing of
an appeal” that commended the adoption of the 2013 amendment seems to wax
dim if we suppose that an in-person pronouncement of censure counts as
“notification” to trigger the thirty-day appeal period. For example, if a court
reduces its verdict to a written decision and delivers the same to the accused,
but is delayed in imposing the censure in person, are we to understand that the
thirty-day clock starts anew on the day of the later in-person pronouncement?
Which “notification” counts? Where is the “bright line?” Or suppose the
matter is the other way around and the court imposes an in-person censure at

<a id="ga52-p1022"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1024 printed_page=1022 -->

the conclusion of the trial but also later reduces its verdict and censure to

writing. In such a case will the thirty-day clock run from the date of the inperson pronouncement, or will it run from the date of notification provided in
accordance with the modes in the second sentence of _BCO_ 42-4? Again, unless
we interpret _BCO_ 42-4 to provide a unitary mode of written notification the
promised “bright line” of the 2013 amendment seems to grow obscure.

_**Concluding Thoughts**_

In my view, reading _BCO_ 42-4 to require a written memorialization of the
court’s decision delivered in the prescribed fashion, best implements the very
words of _BCO_ 42-4, vindicates the rights of individuals, advances the system
of our graded review, and fulfills the assured “bright line” test for determining
when the right of appeal will be extinguished by the mere lapse of time. It also
rightly imposes the burden on the courts of the church, who are removing
rights, to reduce their decision to writing and deliver it to a censured person in
one of the prescribed modes, thereby avoiding confusion and reducing the risk
of the loss of the right of appeal by misunderstanding or mistake.

I will add in passing that, while in this case the Session did not reduce its
decision to writing until after the Appellant filed his appeal, the Record seemed
to show that the Appellant at some point received a transcript of the
proceedings, which raised the prospect that perhaps a written transcript could
satisfy the written “notification” requirements of _BCO_ 42-4. Rather than
taking the time here to set out my analysis of that aspect of the case, I will
simply say that I was not persuaded that the date and mode of the written
transcript’s delivery satisfied the strict requirements of _BCO_ 42-4 to
commence the thirty-day period. I should add that, while I cannot say for sure,
it is at least theoretically possible that others on the SJC may have agreed with
my interpretation of _BCO_ 42-4 set out above yet disagreed with this portion of
my analysis. Since the decision lacks any detailed rationale, I leave open the
possibility that it may conceal disagreement on only this more limited point.

In the end, I learned in this case that others interpret _BCO_ 42-4 differently than
I do. Yet I wonder whether some reading this dissent might agree with my
conception. If so, I thought it would be helpful to share that the interpretation
I have described herein was, at least in this case, put into question. Thus, this
dissent is offered as a caution to both courts and our members to carefully
consider how the thirty-day rule might apply to a case in which they have (or

<a id="ga52-p1023"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1025 printed_page=1023 -->

will have) an interest, and such individuals and courts should proceed with due

care.

Lastly, i respect t ha treasonable minds might disagree about whether ani n person pronouncement of censure should b ea sufficient “notification” for
triggering the thirty-day appeal period. To that end, the courts of our Church
might also consider amendments to _BCO_ 42-4 to ensure that the “bright line”
sought by the 41 [st] General Assembly is realized in a way that is most agreeable
with the collective wisdom of our courts.

<a id="ga52-p1024"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1026 printed_page=1024 -->

**CASE 2024-13**

**TE RICHARD COLQUITT**

**v.**

**HOUSTON METRO PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON COMPLAINT**

**October 17, 2024**

**RULING**

TE Colquitt's Complaint is answered by reference to the Standing Judicial
Commission’s October 17, 2024 Ruling in Case 2024-02: _Complaint of Mr._
_Gleason v. Houston Metro Presbytery_ . In TE Colquitt's cover letter to the PCA
Stated Clerk's office, he reported he _"contends that [Presbytery's] Judicial_
_Commission erred in its Decision on the Gleason Complaint."_ Presbytery's
Commission had ruled the Gleason Complaint out of order on the grounds that
he failed to identify any Session action or decision, as required by _BCO_ 43-1.
In Gleason, the SJC reviewed that argument about lack of complainable action,
but more importantly, ruled the Gleason Complaint out of order because
the _BCO_ 43 complaint process is not a proper avenue for accusations
(regardless of whether a Session action or decision is involved, i.e., essentially
the same main issue in the Colquitt Complaint).

**AMENDS** - None

**RECORDING IN MINUTES**

The SJC instructs the Session and Presbytery to record this Decision, and any
Concurring and Dissenting Opinions, in the minutes of the next stated meeting
following its receipt of the full Decision.

The decision was drafted by RE Donohoe and the SJC approved the final
decision as amended by a vote of **21-0-0, with 1 absent, 1 disqualified, and**
**1 recused.**

Bankson _Concur_ Eggert _Concur_ Maynard Absent
Bise _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Neikirk _Concur_

<a id="ga52-p1025"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1027 printed_page=1025 -->

Bruce _Concur_ Garner _Recused_ Pickering _Concur_
Carrell _Concur_ Greco _Disqualified_ Sartorius _Concur_
Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall _Concur_
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Concur_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe _Concur_ Lee _Concur_ White _Concur_

Dowling _Concur_ Marcinak _Concur_ Wilson _Concur_

TE Greco was disqualified because he is a member of this Presbytery.

TE Garner indicated he recused himself “due to his professional relationship
with a member of the Houston Metro Presbytery who features prominently
in these cases, and in accordance with the stipulations of _OMSJC_ 2.10.d,
which assert the necessity of SJC member disqualification when
"impartiality might reasonably be questioned.”

<a id="ga52-p1026"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1028 printed_page=1026 -->

**CASE 2024-16**

**RE WILLIAM MUELLER**

**v.**

**SOUTH FLORIDA PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON COMPLAINT**

**February 19, 2025**

**CASE SUMMARY**

This case came before the SJC through a Complaint by RE William Mueller,
first to the Redlands Community Church Session (RCC), then to the South
Florida Presbytery (SFP), and then finally to the General Assembly. The RCC
Session failed to consider the Complaint, which resulted in RE Mueller
carrying his complaint to SFP per _BCO_ 43-3. SFP denied the Complaint on the
grounds that the RCC Session’s decision was within its discretion under _BCO_
12-5.

The case was received by the SJC and assigned to a Panel. The Panel
conducted a hearing on the case and determined that the Complaint and Record
of the Case did not demonstrate a violation of Constitutional Interpretation
under _BCO_ 39-3(4). Thus, this matter is one of discretion by the lower court
under _BCO_ 39-3(3). As such, it was not necessary to either endorse or
condemn Alcoholics Anonymous (AA). Under the facts available in the
Record of the Case, the Session had not abused its discretion in the use of
RCC’s property by AA.

**II.** **SUMMARY OF FACTS**

10/05/21 The Redlands Community Church Session (RCC) approved a
motion to allow Alcoholics Anonymous (AA) to use church
facilities for weekly meetings.

12/03/21 RE Mueller filed his Complaint with the RCC Session against the
10/05/21 decision. The Complaint alleged “that by allowing AA
to meet in a classroom on church property, RCC has sanctioned
the use of secular and unbiblical methods. Complainant maintains
that sanctioning this unbiblical form of therapy is antithetical to
the mission of the church, namely, the calling of individuals to

<a id="ga52-p1027"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1029 printed_page=1027 -->

repentance and faith in Jesus Christ alone, the Savior of all the
world.”

02/01/23 Complainant carried his Complaint, per _BCO_ 43-3, to South
Florida Presbytery (SFP) when the RCC Session failed, after 14
months, to consider the Complaint.

08/16/23 The Judicial Commission of SFP (JCSFP) voted “to find that the
matter was neither administratively nor judicially in order…and is
therefore denied by the Judicial Commission.” The Commission’s
ruling was based on their conclusion that there was no written
notice of Complaint with supporting reasons and that the
Complaint had not been first filed with the RCC Session.

11/17/23 Complainant was informed of the action of the JCSFP.

11/29/23 Complainant carried his Complaint to the SJC, alleging that SFP
had misunderstood the nature of the Complaint, including the
steps for adjudication.

12/19/23 Complainant responded to RE Glenn Curran, Chairman of JCSFP,
who had requested that Complainant withdraw the Complaint then
before the SJC, stating that he would work with JCSFP to take up
the case and adjudicate it.

02/13/24 JCSFP reported to SFP that the SJC had “remanded the Complaint
by RE William Mueller (Redlands) back to South Florida
Presbytery to be adjudicated.” SFP then acted to appoint a special
commission (SCSFP) to adjudicate the Complaint.

03/13/24 SCSFP “found the record perfected and requested a response from
RCC Session.”

03/17/24 RCC Session submitted its response to SCSFP. In its response
Session denied that its action of allowing AA to use the facilities
of RCC was contrary to the Confession, provided evidence that
other PCA churches allow AA to use their facilities, and denied
allegations that allowing AA to use the facilities of RCC could

<a id="ga52-p1028"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1030 printed_page=1028 -->

cause confusion in the Church or could be seen as allowing
teaching that is contrary to the Faith.

03/18/24 – A series of emails were exchanged between Complainant and TE
03/29/24 Tim Sansbury, Chairman of SCSFP, regarding the propriety of the
decision of SCSFP to solicit or allow a response from the Session
of RCC. Complainant argued that Session, by failing to act on the
Complaint for 14 months, “lost its right to address the issues raised
in this Complaint.” TE Sansbury’s position was SCSFP was
within its rights to solicit a response from the Session of RCC,
particularly given that the filing of briefs is part of the “suggested
process” for dealing with a complaint. He stated “it seems
remarkable to suggest the _BCO_ prohibits the commission from
seeking information about that original decision.” Complainant
reiterated his concerns, noting that Session’s failure to respond to
the original complaint meant there was “no written record of a
Session judicial proceeding that can be included in the ROC,” and
that Session could not now, retroactively, create such a record.
The Chairman’s advice to Complainant was that he include his
concerns ina brief to SCSFP.

05/06/24 RE Mueller submitted a brief to SCSFP explaining further his
argument that the RCC Session erred in allowing AA to use the
facilities of RCC, and requesting that all RCC briefs be struck
from the Record since RCC’s failure to respond to the initial
written Complaint removed their standing to respond to the
Complaint now before Presbytery.

05/15/24 RCC Session submitted an additional brief arguing the _BCO_ gives
Session the right to respond to the Complaint in spite of their
original failure to consider the matter. Session reiterated its earlier
arguments, and further argued that AA is not religious in nature
so allowing AA to meet in the RCC facilities is not contrary to
Christian practice. They finally asked that RE Mueller withdraw
his Complaint in light of his vow to be subject to his brethren.

05/22/24 SCSFP ruled that the three briefs would be included in the Record,
that the email exchanges between RE Mueller and TE Sansbury
would, with TE Sansbury’s consent, be “made available to the

<a id="ga52-p1029"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1031 printed_page=1029 -->

commission and the RCC session, but for information purposes
only and not as a part of the official record of the case,” and it set
the date for the hearing.

07/31/24 SCSFP held the hearing on the Complaint. Complainant
represented himself. TE William Mashburn, Pastor of RCC,
represented Session. Following the hearing SCSFP voted
unanimously “to uphold the RCC Session’s decision to allow AA
to use the church facility as within their proper authority in
accordance with _BCO_ 12-5(c).”

08/13/24 SCSFP presented its final report to SFP.

09/06/24 Complainant carried his Complaint to the General Assembly.

10/21/24 TE Dominic Aquila, representative for Complainant, asked that
the email correspondence noted above be included in the Record.

11/20/24 TE Sansbury, Presbytery’s representative in this matter, responded

as follows to the request that the emails be included in the Record:

The Commission of South Florida Presbytery found on May
22, 2024 that the private email correspondence between Tim
Sansbury and Bill Mueller did not belong in the ROC (see the
meeting minutes, p. 63 in the most recent ROC PDF).

However, as stated in those minutes: “with TE Sansbury’s
personal consent, on 5/31/2024 the private email
correspondence between RE Mueller and TE Sansbury was
made available to the commission and the RCC session, but
for informational purposes only and not as a part of the official
record of the case.”

While the official position of the SFL Presbytery has not
changed regarding the impropriety of including this private
correspondence in the ROC, we consent to the inclusion of the
correspondence officially in the ROC or merely
informationally, as it did not materially affect the process or

<a id="ga52-p1030"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1032 printed_page=1030 -->

decision of the Commission in the case. Therefore, this
disagreement does not warrant a hearing.

12/18/24 The Panel, consisting of TE Fred Greco (Chairman) and REs John
Maynard and Frederick (Jay) Neikirk (Secretary), and alternates
TE Jay Bruce and RE John Pickering, held the hearing on the
Complaint. It was noted that both parties had agreed to an
expedited schedule and that both parties had filed briefs.
Complainant was present and was represented by TE Dominic
Aquila. Respondent Presbytery was represented by TE Tim
Sansbury.

**II. STATEMENT OF THE ISSUE**

Did the RCC Session commit clear error when, on October 5, 2021, it
voted to approve Alcoholics Anonymous to meet at RCC one day a week
ina classroom on church property?

**III. JUDGMENT**

No.

**IV. REASONING AND OPINION**

Three provisions of _The Book of Church Order_ must guide our decision in this
matter. The first is _BCO_ 12-5.e, which gives Sessions authority over “the uses
to which the church building and associated properties may be put.” It further
charges Sessions to “determine the best measures for promoting the spiritual
interests of the church and congregation.” The second is _BCO_ 39-3.3, which
states “A higher court should ordinarily exhibit great deference to a lower court
regarding those matters of discretion and judgment which can only be
addressed by a court with familiar acquaintance of the events and parties.”
That paragraph goes on to enjoin higher courts that they are not to reverse the
judgment of the lower court in such a matter unless there is clear error on the
part of the lower court. The third is _BCO_ 39-3.4, which reminds us that if the
issues raised in the case involve a matter of Constitutional interpretation, then

<a id="ga52-p1031"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1033 printed_page=1031 -->

the higher court is not obliged to grant the same deference to the lower court
and is “to interpret the Constitution of the Church to its best ability and
understanding.”

At the root of Complainant’s case is the belief that if a PCA Session allowed
a group that espouses views that are antithetical to the gospel of Jesus Christ
to meet in its facilities, then that Session would be in violation of its
responsibility to uphold the truth of the Gospel and its responsibility to care
for its Congregation. Thus, if it were demonstrated that the Session of RCC
had allowed such a group to meet in its facilities, then we would need to
evaluate this Complaint according to the constitutional interpretation standard
of _BCO_ 39-3.4 without deferring to the lower court’s interpretation. That also
means, however, that the burden of proof is on Complainant to demonstrate
that AA is, in fact, espousing views that are antithetical to the gospel.

Complainant sought to meet that burden, contending that the principles and
practices of AA make it incompatible with the Gospel. Thus, he argues,
“Introducing a competing system of thought like AA, that putatively would
help human beings in how they addressed their sin (in this instance, various
forms of substance abuse), diminishes the biblical teaching of the salvific
power of the gospel.” he goes onto assert “sanctioning this unbiblical form of
therapy is antithetical to the mission of the church, namely, to glorify God, to
proclaim the gospel to sinners calling them to repentance and faith in Jesus
Christ, to disciple believers in the Scriptures, and to teach what Christ taught
his disciples.” These, however, are assertions, not evidence strictly speaking.
To demonstrate the validity of these assertions, Complainant would need to
have included in the Record specific statements from AA publications and/or
documented practices that provide support for the assertions.

The assertions presented by Complainant certainly raise questions that any
Session should think about if asked to allow AA to meet in their building. We
are not, however, persuaded that the evidence provided demonstrates that the
principles and practices of AA are antithetical to the gospel and our
Confessional Standards such that a Session would necessarily be sinning, or in
violation of the Constitution of the Church, i fit allowed aa tome e ti nits
building. We could reach such a conclusion only if we were sufficiently
persuaded s oas to be able to say “No Session in the PCA could, in conformity
with our doctrinal standards, allow an AA group to meet in their building.”

<a id="ga52-p1032"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1034 printed_page=1032 -->

The evidence and argumentation provided in this case do not allow us to reach
such a conclusion.

That being the case, this matter becomes an issue of Session’s discretion and
judgment about the prudence of allowing AA to meet in their building. Indeed,
even Complainant’s argument seems to grant this proposition. Thus, for
example, in oral argument, Complainant’s representative suggested that it
might be appropriate for a Session to allow AA to meet in their building once
or twice, but it likely would not be appropriate to allow AA to meet regularly
in the building. That distinction makes sense if the matter is one of Session’s
discretion and judgment under _BCO_ 39-3.3. It is not, in our view, tenable if
AA’s principles and practices are antithetical to the gospel, which would
subject the matter to the Constitutional interpretation standard of review in
_BCO_ 39-3.4.

Thus, we conclude that the decision to allow AA to meet at the RCC building
is a matter of discretion and judgment about the proper use of the building and
how best to promote the spiritual interests of the church and the congregation.
Moreover, as _BCO_ 39-3.3 reminds us, such decisions are best addressed by the
“court with familiar acquaintance of the events and parties,” and we should
overturn their decision only if we find “clear error on the part of the lower
court.” Complainant’s concern that allowing AA to meet in the RCC building
“could have the effect of creating confusion for RCC members,” “may also
lead some weaker professing believers astray,” and “has ‘opened the door’ for
unbiblical concepts to potentially influence RCC’s congregation adversely”
suggests only the possibility that the decision could lead to bad consequences
for some or all of the RCC Congregation. But the possibility of bad
consequences does not rise to the standard of clear error.

In sum, while Complainant has certainly raised concerns about the principles
and practices of AA, concerns that at least some from both SCSFP and this
Court share, he has not met his burden of demonstrating that AA espouses a
religious system that is antithetical to the gospel of Jesus Christ, nor has he
demonstrated that Session committed a clear error in the exercise of its

constitutional discretion. For those reasons we deny the Complaint.

Having said that, we wish to offer three observations. First, Complainant’s
representative said in oral argument that part of what Complainant sought was
a fuller discussion of the question of whether the principles and practices of

<a id="ga52-p1033"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1035 printed_page=1033 -->

AA fit within our doctrinal standards. Nothing in this Opinion prevents
Complainant from respectfully raising these questions in the future in Session
or Presbytery, nor does this Opinion in any way prevent broader discussions
of these questions in the Courts of the Church.

Second, in the course of the hearing, Complainant and his representative stated
that Presbytery's decision to seek and then to allow Session formally to
respond to the Complaint, having failed previously to address the Complaint
in a timely manner, was a matter of concern to Complainant, but he did not
intend for it to be part of the Complaint _per se_ . That being the case, and given
that neither the decision of SCSFP nor the decision of the SJC turns on the

material presented in those responses, we are not obliged to determine what, if
any, rights a session loses when it fails to respond ina timely manner to a
Complaint. wed o want to note, however, that it certainly is not appropriate
fora session to introduce new material byway o fa response that was
presented years after the filing of the Complaint. As such, the Panel in this case
excised from the Record any such new information.

Third, and of greater import, we wish to express our concern regarding
Session’s failure to consider RE Mueller’s Complaint for 14 months, thus
leaving RE Mueller with no choice but to follow _BCO_ 43-3 as the only way of
making sure his concerns were formally addressed. Such a delay on the part of
Session, regardless of its cause or motive, is incompatible with _BCO_ 43-2 and
43-3. It also meant that Session lost its best opportunity to share its mind on
the questions involved in the Complaint, which opportunity they would have
had if they had responded to the Complaint, denied it, and set forth their
reasoning for denying it. Further, Session’s failure to act left SFP in an
untenable position. They were forced to make a determination based on no
Record beyond a single paragraph in Session’s minutes setting forth the
request of a member of RCC that AA be allowed to meet at RCC and the single
sentence stating Session’s decision, as well as the materials provided by
Complainant. Further, when SCSFP did ask Session to respond to the
Complaint, Session could only develop new rationale that came almost two
and a half years after the original decision and provide evidence of what
happened at RCC after the action against which Complaint was made. Neither
of those is a proper response to the Complaint. Thus, in the spirit of _BCO_ 436, we exhort Session to be more circumspect in the future.

<a id="ga52-p1034"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1036 printed_page=1034 -->

Further, we urge South Florida Presbytery to heed and facilitate the wise
counsel of the “Final Report” of the Judicial Commission of South Florida
Presbytery for Case No. 23-01: Mueller v. Redlands Community Church.

While the Commission lacks standing to address the failure of the
RCC Session to respond to RE Mueller’s case in an appropriate and
timely manner, (in accordance with _BCO_ 43-2), all evidence suggests
the RCC Session did indeed entirely fail to respond until the
Commission requested written response and [the Session] is
encouraged to ensure future actions are properly addressed.

**V. AMENDS**

None.

**VI. RECORDING IN MINUTES**

The SJC instructs SFP and the RCC Session to record this Decision, and any
Concurring and Dissenting Opinions, in each of their respective minutes of the
next stated meeting following its receipt of the full Decision.

The proposed decision was written by RE Frederick Neikirk and was amended
by the Panel. It was approved by a unanimous vote.

The SJC reviewed each part of the proposed decision and after the approval of
various amendments the vote on the decision was **22-0-0**, with two absent, thus
denying the Appeal.

Bankson _Concur_ Eggert _Concur_ Maynard _Concur_
Bise _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Neikirk _Concur_

Bruce _Concur_ Garner _Absent_ Pickering _Concur_
Carrell _Concur_ Greco _Concur_ Sartorius _Concur_

Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall _Absent_
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Concur_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe _Concur_ Lee _Concur_ White _Concur_

Dowling _Concur_ Marcinak _Concur_ Wilson _Concur_

<a id="ga52-p1035"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1037 printed_page=1035 -->

**CASE 2024-18**

**RE JOHN HARRIS**

**v.**

**WISCONSIN PRESBYTERY**

**DECISION ON COMPLAINT**

**March 6, 2025**

**CASE SUMMARY**

Presbytery received allegations from two former members of a PCA Church
in Wisconsin regarding the Session's alleged improper removal of them from
the rolls and some other unrelated Session decisions. Presbytery appointed an
investigative committee, which eventually reported some advice regarding the
Session, but also recommended Presbytery "commence process" against the
Church's pastor. Presbytery found a strong presumption of guilt and appointed
a nine-member judicial commission and a prosecutor. Three months later, the
prosecutor issued two indictments related to allegations about the quality of
the minister's pastoral care. The TE pled not guilty, and after several pre-trial
meetings, the judicial commission voted 8-0 to dismiss both Charges. The
prosecutor filed a Complaint against those actions. Presbytery heard and
denied the Complaint, and Complainant then carried the Complaint to the SJC.

**I.** **SUMMARY OF THE FACTS**

2020 The Church transferred from the Reformed Church in

America into the PCA.

12/06/22 the sessions en ta letter to a member couplet hat began with
this paragraph:

You have repeatedly and continue to reiterate to the
Session that you have no intention of fulfilling your
membership vows at [the] Church and that you are not
under the authority of the Elders. We have requested

<a id="ga52-p1036"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1038 printed_page=1036 -->

verbally and in written letters for over a year that we
desire to meet with you to discuss your ongoing
prideful, combative, and divisive attitudes and actions
that are not appropriate for members of [the] Church.
You repeatedly have refused. As such you have been
removed from the membership rolls at [the] Church
( _BCO_ 57-5).

12/19/22 Presbytery Clerk RE Heinen received a two-page letter dated
12/15 from the couple, alleging they were improperly
removed from the rolls. The "Subject" line of the letter was
"Right to file an appeal re: our excommunication from [the]
Church." Among other things, it references the
Pastor's/Session's alleged unwillingness to nominate the
husband to be an elder and included criticisms of the Pastor.

The final paragraph said: "We feel the pastor should be
investigated..."

01/28/23 One month later, Presbytery considered the couple's letter at a
Stated Meeting. Below is an excerpt from those Minutes.

[Presbytery] Clerk reported that a Complaint was
received on December 15, 2022, from the [couple] related
to a decision by the Session of [the] Church that was sent
to the [couple] in a letter dated December 6, 2022.

Presbytery appointed a commission to adjudicate the
complaint against the Session (the alleged improper removal
from the rolls) and a committee ("IC") to conduct a _BCO_ 312 investigation of allegations in the complaint regarding the
minister and report to the next stated meeting.

07/22/23 Presbytery Stated Meeting. The Complaint Commission
reported it declined to sustain the Complaint against the
alleged improper removal from the rolls.

The IC recommended some assistance be offered to the

Session regarding some of the Session's responsibilities and
four recommendations were adopted.

<a id="ga52-p1037"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1039 printed_page=1037 -->

The IC, having unanimously found a strong presumption of
guilt regarding the minister, also recommended Presbytery
"institute process against" the minister, which Presbytery
adopted. Presbytery appointed a judicial commission ("JC")
to conduct the case and appointed RE John L. Harris as
prosecutor (now the Complainant). The JC included four TEs
and five REs. Presbytery also adopted a recommendation
from the IC to direct the Pastoral Care Committee ("PCC") to
converse in a private manner with the alleged offender, and
endeavor to bring him to a sense of his guilt, before instituting
actual process.

10/26/23 Three months later, the Prosecutor issued two indictments
against the minister. The first indictment alleged violation of
the 5 [th], 6 [th] and 9 [th] Commandments related to alleged failures
in pastoral care to a woman and her marriage, seven years
prior (May 2017). The second alleged violation of the 5 [th]
Commandment related to alleged failures in pastoral care to a
family whose father was in the hospital.

10/31/23 The initial defense representative, Mr. Weisgarber, submitted
a Motion to Dismiss both charges in the Indictment.

11/08/23 Presbytery's PCC met with the accused minister to discuss the
Indictment.

02/19/24 The JC met via videoconference and determined not to
suspend the accused minister's official functions. ( _BCO_ 3110)

03/09/24 Four months after the Indictments were drawn, the accused
minister pled not guilty at the arraignment held via
videoconference. The Prosecutor was not present at the
arraignment and no reason was provided in the Record. The
JC considered April 13 and 20 for the trial, but because
previous defense counsel was unable to continue, dates in late
May or June were mentioned.

<a id="ga52-p1038"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1040 printed_page=1038 -->

Before the plea, JC Chairman asked the Defendant "if there's
anything you have to say regarding the charges, be sure to—
if you could get it to us in—in writing because that will be
important as well." The Record contained a "Written plea
from [the minister]" attached to the minutes of the
arraignment as shown below.

In 2021, our session asked an antagonistic couple to
repent for causing church disunity. Instead, they began a
campaign of recruiting people and spreading slander in
our church. The session faithfully worked through the
conflict and peace was restored. It was an exhausting
season of ministry. As a result of the couple losing their
membership, they maliciously began recruiting the last
remnant of their movement to look for disgruntled people
to file complaints to the Presbytery. They succeeded and
successfully brought about a second round of chaos and
disunity for [Church] Session at the Presbytery level.
The two indictments filed against me are from people
who have never talked to me or our session personally
about their complaints as written in the indictments. This
has created a dangerous precedent in our Presbytery for it
allows people to leapfrog the basic Biblical peacemaking
principles outlined by Jesus in Matthew 18:15ff in the
local church and go right to Presbytery and a trial setting.
The indictments misrepresent the situations as they
occurred and are slanderous in nature. I plead not guilty.

After pleading Not Guilty to the charges, the Defendant made
several statements to the JC. Below are sample excerpts from the
transcript of the arraignment.

So, can you—any of you in one sentence tell me what
each indictment—what the charge would be for each one?
Is there a simplified version of this?

i think a charge could be written i none or two sentences
and could be easily understood. So, these are very, very
difficult to understand.

<a id="ga52-p1039"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1041 printed_page=1039 -->

You know, I think this stuff could have been hashed out
in an afternoon if people would have sat down, or been
willing to, with a mediator.

05/11/24 The JC met in Sheboygan, WI for 75 minutes to hear
preliminary motions. No witnesses were cited or present.
Prosecutor reported he did not wish to amend the charges.
Defense counsel was TE Dan Breed (Appleton, WI) with
assistance from TE Nathan Kline (Friendly Hills PCA,
Jamestown, NC (Greensboro), Piedmont Triad).

With the consent of the court, the prosecution and defense
agreed to try to reach a mediated resolution.

06/22/24 The JC met in Sheboygan, WI from 9:29 am to 3:06 pm with
defense assistant counsel TE Kline joining via Zoom. The JC
denied a defense motion to dismiss the charges but decided to
hold a hearing later to review procedures used by the IC.

The Prosecutor indicated the "witnesses declined the

invitation to mediation," and based partly on that, the
Prosecutor "was withdrawing from the concept of mediation."
At one point, the Prosecutor said, "We're bordering on starting
the trial. I'm not going to cross-examine." Later in the
meeting, the Prosecutor was asked, "Is there anything else you
would like to say [regarding the motion to dismiss]"? and he
answered "No." At this hearing the Accused was permitted to
give his account of the specifications listed in the two
indictments. His unsworn narrative was occasionally followed
up by additional questions directed to him from some of the
men serving on the JC. There is no record in the transcript of
the Prosecutor specifically lodging an objection to anything
during the meeting.

06/29/24 The JC met in Sheboygan, WI in closed session with each of
the five members of the IC individually via videoconference
to review the IC process. The JC dismissed Indictment 2 and
suggested the subject matter giving rise to the Charge

<a id="ga52-p1040"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1042 printed_page=1040 -->

presented by the witnesses/offended parties should be referred
to the Session. The JC postponed further consideration of
Indictment 1 to its next meeting.

07/02/24 JC met in closed session via videoconference and ruled to

dismiss Indictment 1.

07/09/24 By vote of 8-0, the JC adopted a final decision framed as
follows:

Statement of the Issue: Did [the Pastor] err in neglect and

failure of his ordination vows as per the two
indictments?

Judgment 1: As to Indictment #1: "The neglect and failure
of his ordination vows to be faithful and

diligent in the exercise of all duties as a
Christian and a minister of the Gospel," the
charge is dismissed with the
recommendation that [the Church] hire [a
named private organization] for both training
and policy making.

Judgment 2: As to Indictment #2: "The neglect and failure
of his ordination vows to be zealous and

faithful in maintaining the truths of the
Gospel and the purity and peace and unity of
the church," the charge is dismissed as the
matter should be referred to the lower court.

The Commission provided two pages of rationale.

07/17/24 Prosecutor RE Harris filed a Complaint with Presbytery
alleging the JC erred by dismissing the Indictments.

07/27/24 Presbytery Stated Meeting. Presbytery denied the part of the
Complaint regarding Indictment 1 by vote of 28-4-9 (87%
majority) and denied the second part regarding Indictment 2
by vote of 29-2-11 (93% majority).

<a id="ga52-p1041"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1043 printed_page=1041 -->

08/05/24 Presbytery Clerk notified RE Harris that Presbytery denied his
Complaint.

08/29/24 RE Harris carried his Complaint to the SJC and notified the
Presbytery Clerk.

11/06/24 Complainant received the Record via email. The Panel
members were notified of their appointment and also received
the Record. Members included RE Donahoe (convener), TE
Sartorius and TE Garner, with alternates RE Wilson and TE
Waters. Presbytery had not yet appointed its official
Representative.

11/15/24 The Panel held its Constituting Meeting. The Panel requested
the Presbytery Clerk to provide additional documents for the
Record.

11/20/24 The Presbytery Clerk supplied the documents, and the Panel
notified the Parties that the Panel had added 15 pages to the
Record, called Addition 1.

11/22/24 Panel requested the Presbytery Clerk supply the Session’s
December 6, 2022 letter sent to the couple who sent their
December 15, 2022 letter to the Presbytery. Panel also
requested minutes of the Judicial Commission. These items
were supplied and became nine-page Addition 2.

11/25/24 The Panel was notified that TE Michael Bowman and RE Bob
Petts would represent the Presbytery.

11/24 The Complainant filed several objections/proposed
corrections to the Record and discussed them with the

Respondent.

12/09/24 Presbytery's Representative responded to the Complainant's
ROC objections and the Parties reached agreement on 59page Addition 3.

<a id="ga52-p1042"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1044 printed_page=1042 -->

12/17/24 Panel decided the ROC was complete, with Additions 1, 2 and
3 to the original Record. Panel decided the Case was judicially
in order and ready for a Hearing. The Parties indicated
Thursday morning, January 30, 2024 would work for both,
and the Panel scheduled such and notified the Parties same

day.

01/02/25 Complainant filed his Preliminary Brief.

01/16/25 Presbytery's Representative filed his Preliminary Brief.

01/30/25 Panel Hearing on the Complaint via videoconference.

02/12/25 Panel met via videoconference, adopted a Proposed Decision,
filed it with the SJC, and copied the Parties.

**II. STATEMENT OF THE ISSUES**

1. Did the Presbytery err on July 9, 2024 when its Judicial Commission

dismissed Indictment 1?

2. Did the Presbytery err on July 9, 2024 when its Judicial Commission

dismissed Indictment 2?

**III. JUDGMENT**

1. No.

2. No.

**IV. REASONING AND OPINION**

**1. Dismissal of Indictment 1**

The JC requested, and the Accused voluntarily provided a written plea,
including reasons the Accused pled not guilty. The written plea provided

<a id="ga52-p1043"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1045 printed_page=1043 -->

contextual information related to the Charges. In pretrial meetings, the JC
requested, and the Accused provided, lengthy explanations regarding the
background and circumstances giving rise to the Charges. In consequence of
these meetings, the JC dismissed each indictment.

First, we note that under our Constitution, a court (or judicial commission
assigned to try the case) may, in the exercise of its discretion, dismiss an
indictment where circumstances warrant. See Case 2007-14: _PCA v. Louisiana_
_Presbytery_ ( _M36GA_, p. 128) and Case 2010-16: _Lyons v. Western Carolina_
_Presbytery_ ( _M39GA_, p. 594). [55]

Below is the written judgment from the JC’s report.

As to Indictment #1: The "neglect and failure of his ordination
vows to be faithful and diligent in the exercise of all duties as a
Christian and a minister of the Gospel," the charge is dismissed
with the recommendation that [the Church] hire [a private
organization] for both training and policy making.

The Complainant's primary objection to this dismissal, as expressed in his
Complaint, was the court's reliance upon _BCO_ 32-20.

32-20. The accused or a member of the court may object to the
consideration of a charge, for example, if he thinks the passage
of time since the alleged offense makes fair adjudication
unachievable. The court should consider factors such as the

gravity of the alleged offense as well as what degradations of
evidence and memory may have occurred in the intervening
period.

In its Report, the JC reasoned:

The seven years between the event and the trial, the transition
that occurred at Gibbsville Church moving from the Reformed
Church in America to the Presbyterian Church in America, and
the subsequent changes in how the pastor and elders engage

55 Presbytery's judicial commission dismissed both indictments by vote of 8-0,
and the Complaint against those actions was denied by Presbytery majorities of 87
and 93%.

<a id="ga52-p1044"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1046 printed_page=1044 -->

shepherding, creates a scenario in which the Judicial
Commission concluded that the charge should be dismissed.

The specifications supporting Indictment 1 describe events that occurred in
2017. The SJC judges the JC’s decision to be one in a matter of discretion and
judgment and does not find that such decision was clearly erroneous. Thus, we
afford the great deference required by the standard of review stipulated in _BCO_
39-3.3. The SJC has reached similar conclusions in the application of _BCO_ 3220 in Case 2021-11: _Park et al v. Korean Central Presbytery_ ( _M50GA_, p. 797)
and Case 2023-21: _Kang et al v. Korean Central Presbytery_ (decided Oct 17,
2024).

The lower court was authorized, in the exercise of its discretion, to dismiss the
charges because of the passage of time. Notwithstanding, several of the
reasons articulated by members of the JC in the JC’s pretrial meetings appear
troublesome, if not erroneous. Some members of the court appeared to confuse
the status of persons as witnesses/offended parties and complainants in a prior
case. In addition, some members of the JC expressed skepticism regarding the
motives of some of the witnesses when they declined to participate in
mediation.

The mere filing of a complaint, proffering allegations, or identifying offenses
does not disqualify one from testifying as a witness at trial, nor do any of these
permitted actions automatically mark a person’s testimony as dubious
according to _BCO_ 31-8. _BCO_ 31-8 describes patterns of improper conduct
which are distinct from the presentation of complaints and allegations via
proper process. From their pretrial comments in the transcript, it appears some
of the Commission members did not appreciate this important distinction. Our
standards of review recognize broad discretion afforded the lower court in this
matter, and they require us to defer to the lower court’s decision, even though
the Record shows that some members of the lower court may have held
erroneous reasons leading to the decision.

**2. Dismissal of Indictment 2**

As indicated in the reasoning regarding Indictment 1, the rights of a Prosecutor
to proceed with a case are subject to the proper exercise of judicial power by
a judicial commission. Any ecclesiastical body trying a case has _some_ inherent
power to dismiss a case pretrial.

<a id="ga52-p1045"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1047 printed_page=1045 -->

The JC, in its written judgment dismissing indictment 2, stated:

"The neglect and failure of his ordination vows to be zealous and
faithful in maintaining the truths of the Gospel and the purity and
peace and unity of the church, the charge is dismissed as the matter
should be referred to the lower court.”

Further, in its report, after citing _BCO_ 11-4 and 31-1, the judicial commission

reasoned:

The aggrieved parties must first seek resolution through the court of
original jurisdiction and, if so desired, they may appeal that decision
to the higher court, in this case, the Presbytery.

There is error in this reasoning. Even though ministerial functions of a church
fall on both the ruling elder sofa church and the teaching elders serving a
church ( _BCO_ 12-5) the courts of original jurisdiction for ruling elders and
teaching elders differ. a member o fa church feeling aggrieved by a teaching
elder must first go to the Presbytery and is not compelled to first go to his or
her local Session.

However, despite the fact that the reasoning for dismissal is flawed, the act of
issuing the dismissal is not. The Record indicates that the subject matter of
Indictment 2 was not investigated nor acted upon by the Investigative
Committee. In fact, the indictment itself shows that the alleged facts behind
the indictment did not even occur until after the Investigative Committee was
discharged. As a result, it is apparent that the Prosecutor unilaterally added
this charge to his indictment.

The specifications of Indictment 2 further show that the lone charge of this
indictment arose over what is alleged to be a personal and private offense,
which never came to Presbytery as a “general fame” report. ( _BCO_ 31-2) As
this is a private offense, even if learned about by the Prosecutor through his
interviewing of potential witnesses, the Prosecutor cannot just make the
offense public by putting it in an indictment and then taking the case to trial.
The Prosecutor must first ensure that the accuser addresses the matter with the
minister in private. ( _BCO_ 27-5, 34-3) There is no indication this happened. As
such, the dismissal was authorized.

<a id="ga52-p1046"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1048 printed_page=1046 -->

**V. AMENDS**

No further action is necessary.

**VI. RECORDING IN MINUTES**

The SJC instructs Presbytery to record this Decision and any Concurring and
Dissenting Opinions in the minutes of the next stated meeting following
receipt of those documents.

The proposed decision was written jointly by the Panel, and was adopted
unanimously with the alternates also expressing their agreement.

The SJC reviewed each part of the proposed decision and after the approval of
various amendments the vote on the decision was **18-4-0, with 2 absent.**

Bankson _Concur_ Eggert _Concur_ Maynard Absent
Bise _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Neikirk _Dissent_

Bruce _Concur_ Garner _Concur_ Pickering _Dissent_
Carrell _Dissent_ Greco _Concur_ Sartorius _Dissent_

Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall _Concur_
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Concur_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe _Concur_ Lee _Concur_ White Absent

Dowling _Concur_ Marcinak _Concur_ Wilson _Concur_

**Concurring Opinion**
_Case 2024-18: Harris v. Wisconsin_

RE Howie Donahoe

March 20, 2025

I concur with the SJC Decision denying this Complaint but believe four items
warrant further attention.

**1. Lack of Compliance with Matthew 18**

<a id="ga52-p1047"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1049 printed_page=1047 -->

While the SJC Decision does not cite Matthew 18 in its Reasoning for
Judgment 1, it's as pertinent and important as it was for Judgment 2.
Presbytery’s investigative committee and prosecutor did not ensure that
Matthew 18 was properly followed before judicial action commenced. I would
have dismissed Indictment 1 for this reason alone.

_BCO_ 27-5 explicitly requires church discipline to adhere to several biblical
principles.

Scriptural law is the basis of all discipline because it is the
revelation of God’s Holy will. Proper disciplinary principles
are set forth in the Scriptures and _must be_ followed. They are:

- **a.** Instruction in the Word;
- **b.** Individual’s _responsibility_ to admonish one

another (Matt 18:15, Gal 6:1);
c. If the admonition is rejected, then the calling of

one or more witnesses (Matt 18:16);
d. If rejection persists, then the Church must act

through her court unto admonition, suspension,
excommunication and deposition (See _BCO_ 29,
30 for further explanation).
Steps (a) through (d) _must be followed_ in proper order for the
exercise of discipline. [56] (all emphasis added)

When questioned in the SJC Panel Hearing, the Complainant (prosecutor) said
the accusers did not “feel safe” in a Matthew 18 type engagement. But no
reasonable basis was provided for that assertion. While there are legitimate
instances where victims of abuse (such as minors or physically abused
individuals) may require alternative approaches, and the PCA has amended
_BCO_ 35 to provide such a process, the facts of this Case did not warrant such.

_BCO_ 35-3. A court may, at the request of either party, or at its
own initiative, make reasonable accommodation to prevent inperson contact with the accused: ...

56 cf. OPC's Book of Discipline 3.5. "No charge of a personal private offense
shall be admitted unless the judicatory has assured itself that the person bringing the
charge has faithfully followed the course set forth in Matthew 18:15-17; nor shall a
charge of a private offense which is not personal be admitted unless it appears that
the plaintiff has first done his utmost privately to restore the alleged offender."

<a id="ga52-p1048"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1050 printed_page=1048 -->

(d) The court shall include in the record of the proceedings

_its reasons for this accommodation_ and any objection
from either party. (emphasis added)

The lack of a legitimate exemption from Matthew 18 suggests a significant
procedural failure in this Case. Future courts should ensure that scriptural and
constitutional requirements are appropriately observed before escalating
disputes to formal judicial process.

**2. Receiving Exculpatory Statements**

During the June 22, 2024 pre-trial hearing, the defendant was allowed to speak
extensively, introducing information outside of a formal trial setting. Trial
courts should use great caution in affording such allowances. Nonetheless, an
accused person is permitted by _BCO_ 32-20 to "object to the consideration o fa
charge" for a variety of reasons.

_BCO_ 32-20 The accused or a member of the court may object
to the consideration o fa charge, _for example_, if he thinks the
passage of time since the alleged offense makes fair
adjudication unachievable. The court should consider factors
_such as_ the gravity of the alleged offense as well as what
degradations of evidence and memory may have occurred in the
intervening period. (emphasis added)

It's reasonable for a court to afford the accused an opportunity to express those
reasons. It's also important to note that while _BCO_ 32-20 provides a few
examples of reasons for objecting to the consideration of a charge, they are not
exhaustive. And ordinarily, it might be best to express those reasons in a pretrial brief rather than verbally from the defendant. In either form, the court
needs to guard against those communications becoming quasi-exculpatory
testimony from a defendant who might later decline to take the stand. Finally,
prosecutors should energetically object if communications drift into
exculpatory or non-cross-examinable testimony. In this Case, the prosecutor
did not register a clear objection.

<a id="ga52-p1049"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1051 printed_page=1049 -->

**3. Undue Delay in Proceedings**

The Presbytery and its Judicial Commission ("JC") appeared to take extensive
measures to avoid a trial. While this is commendable in many situations, it can
also delay resolution beyond the time a trial would require. This Case
exemplifies such a delay.

The couple that was removed by the Session from the church membership rolls
sent their letter to the Presbytery in December 2022. _Seven months later_ in July
2023, the Presbytery adopted an investigative committee’s recommendation to
"commence process" against the minister. The arraignment did not take place
until March 2024— _eight months later_ . Such delays are unreasonable,
especially when the person being investigated or indicted is still serving as the
pastor of a church. It is unfair to him and to his church.

While Prosecutors should have liberty in crafting the final indictment and
adequate time to prepare for the trial, additional pre-trial _investigation_ of a
general nature should not be necessary once process has commenced because
the church court has already deemed there is sufficient evidence indicating a
"strong" presumption of guilt. The eight-month delay between the
commencement of process and the arraignment probably explains how the
prosecutor’s Indictment 2 included events that transpired five weeks _after_
Presbytery adopted a motion to commence process.

**4. Anticipating the Likelihood of a Particular Censure**

While expedient trials can be beneficial, it's disheartening to see indictments
on matters unlikely to result in a censure greater than admonition. When the
anticipated outcome is most likely admonition, experienced elders should be
able to resolve such issues through non-judicial means or a _BCO_ 38-1 case
without process. Sometimes, we take _BCO_ 29-1 too far.

_BCO_ 29-1. An offense, _the proper object of judicial process_, is
anything in the doctrines or practice of a Church member
professing faith in Christ which is contrary to the Word of God.
(emphasis added)

<a id="ga52-p1050"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1052 printed_page=1050 -->

Not all offenses warrant judicial process. The PCA might benefit from
considering the approach found in the OPC’s Book of Discipline, shown
below.

OPC 3.7.b An offense which is _serious enough_ to warrant a trial

is:

(1) an offense in the area of conduct and practice which

_seriously_ disturbs the peace, purity, and/or unity of the
church, or
(2) an offense in the area of doctrine for the nonordained

member which would constitute a _denial of a credible_
_profession_ of faith as reflected in his membership vows,

or

(3) an offense in the area of doctrine for the ordained officer

which would constitute a violation of the _system_ of
doctrine contained in the Holy Scriptures as that system
of doctrine is set forth in our Confession of Faith and
Catechisms. [57] (emphasis added)

In this present Case, the accusations stemmed primarily from personal
dissatisfaction with the pastoral care received rather than from misconduct
warranting formal indictment. In my four decades as a PCA elder, I've
observed that such disappointments—particularly in times of church stress or
transition—are common and often do not justify formal judicial process.
Hopefully, the PCA will enact the revision proposed to _BCO_ 32 in this year's

##### Overture 1 from Northern New England Presbytery, shown below (with

emphasis added).

_BCO_ 32-2. Prior to commencing process against an alleged
offender, regardless of how allegations arose, the court shall
investigate to the extent necessary to determine:

- **a.** i fit is a personal or general offense
- **b.** if personal, whether Matthew 18 has been followed
- **c.** _if the nature of the alleged offense rises to a level_

_warranting judicial process_
d. if the accuser is like any of those described in _BCO_ 31-8

57 See also OPC Book of Discipline 3.1, 3.3, 3.6, 3.7.b, 6.a.1, and 6.b.1.
https://opc.org/BCO/BD.html

<a id="ga52-p1051"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1053 printed_page=1051 -->

- **e.** if there is a strong presumption of guilt
- **f.** if it is reasonable to expect the offense can be proven

through judicial process.

/s/ RE Howie Donahoe

**Case 2024-18**
_**Complaint of RE John Harris**_

_**v.**_
_**Wisconsin Presbytery**_

_**Dissenting Opinion on Complaint**_
**By TE Art Sartorius, RE Jay Neikirk, RE Dan Carrell,**

**And RE John Pickering,**

**March 24, 2024**

The SJC in this case determined that the Wisconsin Presbytery did not err on
July 9, 2024 when its Judicial Commission dismissed “Indictment 1” of two
indictments or charges. This dissent takes an opposing view and would state
that not only did the Presbytery err but _clearly erred_ s oas to require an
annulment of the Presbytery’s dismissal on that issue.

This case followed from actions taken by Presbytery at its meeting on July 22,
2022. At that time it received an Investigative Committee report from a _BCO_
31-2 committee established to investigate reports affecting the Christian
character of TE David Krueger. The Investigative Committee unanimously
found there to be a strong presumption of guilt and recommended that the
Presbytery institute process against said teaching elder. A motion to institute
process was then adopted by the Presbytery and a prosecutor was appointed to
prepare an indictment and conduct the case. The hearing of the case was
assigned to a nine-member Judicial Commission (JC).

Thereafter, the JC did not try the case but instead ended the judicial process as
it submitted its July 9, 2024 “Decision on Trial” to the Presbytery. In that
decision the JC stated:

While the indictments and investigative process appeared to be in

<a id="ga52-p1052"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1054 printed_page=1052 -->

order and the considerations were of serious nature, there where ( _sic_ )
procedural considerations that require the Judicial Commission to
dismiss the charges.

The JC in that decision cited more specific reasoning for dismissal by referring
to _BCO_ 32-20. The JC reasoning indicated that it would be “seven years
between the event and the trial” and that during that time the church in which
the matters are alleged to have occurred had moved out of the Reformed
Church in America into the Presbyterian Church in America which led to
“subsequent changes in how the pastor and elders engage shepherding.”

The decision to dismiss Indictment 1 was approved by the JC by a vote of 4-3
on July 2, 2024, 10 days after a lengthy preliminary hearing held on June 22,
2024. Argument at that hearing never suggested _BCO_ 32-20 was being
considered s oas to justify a dismissal. Furthermore, argument and “evidence”
presented at that hearing mitigate against an application of _BCO_ 32-20. More
pointedly, the transcript of the hearing of June 22, 2024 reveals that other
“procedural considerations” were in play which led to the JC improperly
receiving evidence from the Accused while effectively declining to receive
evidence from the Prosecution. Accordingly, the JC clearly erred in entering a
decision to dismiss.

It is helpful in seeing this clear error by first reviewing some of the facts
contained in the Record of the Case. The Accused entered written pleas and
oral pleas of not guilty to both Indictment 1 and Indictment 2. The oral pleas
were also entered at a video hearing held on March 9, 2024. At that hearing,
the JC allotted some time for the Accused to speak beyond his plea, and among
other matters of which he spoke were his complaints about the process which
led to the charges and how the matters of which he was accused could have
been “hashed out” if people would have been willing to sit down “with a
mediator.”

Although the pleas offered at that hearing were accepted, the JC did not take
the normal next step of scheduling a trial and citing parties or witnesses to
appear ( _BCO_ 32-3), but instead, held two more preliminary hearings, which
the Prosecutor, the Accused, and representatives of the Accused were invited
to attend.

At the first of those subsequent hearings held May 11, 2024, the hearing was

<a id="ga52-p1053"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1055 printed_page=1053 -->

called to order by the chairman of the JC who then went on to the identify the
purpose for the hearing. He stated, “The business before us today is to set
expectations” and “to hear preliminary motions.” During this hearing the
emphasis of the Defense was to ask for time for the Accused and his
representatives and the Prosecutor and his accusing witnesses to mediate with
a goal of reaching restoration and reconciliation.

After deliberation following that May 11, 2024 hearing, the JC granted 3
motions, including a motion which “highly recommend[ed]” that the Accused
and his accusing witness use a third-party mediator with the hope of reaching
reconciliation. Such an effort to pause a judicial process so as to possibly reach
a mediated amicable result is noble. Yet, the proposed mediation did not occur,
as the accusing witnesses “declined the invitation to mediation.”

This disinclination to mediate was reported by the Prosecutor at the next, and
last, preliminary hearing held on June 22, 2024. After reporting the position of
the accusing witnesses, the Prosecutor stated that it was “the conviction of my
heart that we should proceed to trial.”

The prosecutor serves as “the representative of the Church, and as such has all
its rights in the case.” ( _BCO_ 31-3) His conviction to proceed to trial after the
finding of a strong presumption is one that should generally be honored. Yet,
it should be acknowledged that a prosecutor’s rights in a case are still subject
to the proper exercise of judicial power by a judicial commission. As such, in
general, any ecclesiastical body trying a case has _some_ inherent power to
dismiss a case pretrial.

In the Case 2010-16: _Lyons v. Western Carolina Presbytery_, for example, the
SJC cited three categories specified within the _BCO_ where discretion could be
exercised s oas to allow dismissal. Further, one could possibly imagine other
hypothetical circumstances beyond those listed in the _BCO_ . Indictment 2 in
this case also presents a not-so-hypothetical where both the SJC and this
Dissent see dismissal as appropriate.

Yet, in this case the JC has abused its discretion in that it allowed the final
preliminary hearing held to become a _de facto_ trial with only one witness—the
Accused himself. This error in procedure is egregious, and it permitted the
Accused the privilege of giving unsworn testimony in which he defended the
actions of which he was accused without cross-examination and without the

<a id="ga52-p1054"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1056 printed_page=1054 -->

Prosecutor having the opportunity to present his own witnesses.

The preliminary hearing of June 22, 2024 found the representatives of the
Accused advancing a continual desire to curtail judicial process, asking the JC
to “... simply hear Dave's [the Accused’s] explanation—even in this particular
setting [before the jc ina preliminary hearing]… [w]ithout a full trial....”

The Prosecutor in response raised questions regarding the propriety of the JC
hearing the Accused’s “explanation of the situation and hear[ing] his
repentance” in lieu of a full trial by saying:

- “I think my question is, how is that possible without a change in plea
when there aren't witnesses present because we're called to process
this with witnesses.”

- “So, to me, it feels like we're skirting around biasing the court,
potentially, because witnesses aren't present.”

The Defense representatives continued to advocate for mediation, or
alternatively, for the JC to hear from the Accused—without moving on to trial.
Eventually the argument moved specifically to consideration of motions to
dismiss which were orally presented to the JC by the defense representatives.
None of those argued grounds for dismissal referenced _BCO_ 32-20.

In making arguments for dismissal, one of the statements made by the defense
was that “it is totally fair for a presbytery to want to investigate if there is ( _sic_ )
issues coming from a church. But they did not do a good job of
investigating....” Furthermore, the defense stated:

Number one, it appears, from our perspective, the investigation
committee only looked at one side and immediately responded to the
emotion on the one side rather than remaining neutral until all the facts
were in and presented. It appeared that the fact-gathering and
interpretation was very one-sided.

This position was taken even though the Record reveals that the Accused
engaged in a “57-minute zoom meeting with the investigative committee.” The
Prosecutor noted in reply to such arguments that “the investigative committee
is not on trial.”

<a id="ga52-p1055"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1057 printed_page=1055 -->

After hearing all arguments for dismissal, the JC chairman in the open hearing
announced the following ruling of the JC:

So, we've heard the motion, and in hearing it, a lot of it sounds like it
revolves around the issue of due process, and that is, I think, an
important decision, it's critical to the case. If due process was not
followed at one point or another, then that's very understandable. With
that in mind, we—we will not entertain the motion. We want to move
forward with this proviso. We want to move forward and focus on the
issue of due process, the arguments. We haven't had a chance to
process it and allow questioning a rebuttal, and doing that before we
hear any witnesses, okay?

This ruling did not end the hearing. And shortly thereafter, a spar to fan
apparent attempt to “focus on the issue of due process,” the JC Chairman
suggested that if the Accused admitted “guilt somewhere that—that shows us
that we don't need to go, you know, to a trial,” something could then be said to
those who might be among the accusers of the Accused.

With that said, the Accused himself responded: “I'm very willing to just walk
you through each case in a succinct, brief way and give you my perspective,
tell you where i think i could have done things better, and then you'd have a
little context with what I am saying. So, I'm willing to do that.”

Thereafter, the Accused was permitted to give his account of the specifications
listed in the two indictments. His unsworn testimony was occasionally
followed up by additional questions directed to him from some of the men
serving on the JC. Nothing was said to the Prosecutor specifically which could
have been construed as giving him an opportunity to cross examine, nor did he
request an opportunity for cross examination. After the unsworn testimony, the
commission went into a closed session to further discuss the matter.

Before that testimony of the Accused was given in the preliminary hearing, the
JC chairman stated: “... what we're trying to do is proceed in a way that affords
the—what we think is the best opportunity to avoid trial ….” After the
testimony, he again stated: “It's been our desire to offer every opportunity to
avoid, you know, a full trial.” The preliminary hearing subsequently ended,
and as stated above, ten days later the JC voted to dismiss Indictment 1.

<a id="ga52-p1056"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1058 printed_page=1056 -->

Although the stated reason for dismissal was based upon _BCO_ 32-20, nothing
in the record supports such a decision. _BCO_ 32-20 states:

32-20. The accused or a member of the court may object to the
consideration of a charge, for example, if he thinks the passage of time
since the alleged offense makes fair adjudication unachievable. The
court should consider factors such as the gravity of the alleged offense
as well as what degradations of evidence and memory may have
occurred in the intervening period.

There is nothing in this Record suggesting at all that “fair adjudication is
unachievable.” The Accused, when allowed to give testimony, spoke with what
appeared to be clear recollection of factual matters in spite of a seven-year
intervening period. Further, the change in denominations does not affect the
possibility of “fair adjudication.” Instead, a zealousness to avoid trial, which
then led to a judicial commission improperly hearing testimony from one party
and not another, clearly underlies the decision made by the JC.

Although this matter reaches the SJC via the mechanism of complaint, _BCO_
42-3 relating to appeals is helpful in understanding procedures which should
be followed by a judicial commission. That provision enumerates some
possible grounds of appeal, including: “receiving improper or declining to
receive proper evidence....” The JC’s improper receiving of evidence and
declining to receive proper evidence in this case is not _de minimis_ . The
Accused was permitted to present his side of the case unopposed. This seems
to have certainly influenced the JC and cannot be condoned, particularly in
light of Proverbs 18:17. This Dissent, therefore, concludes that the JC, acting
for the Presbytery, clearly erred when dismissing this indictment.

<a id="ga52-p1057"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1059 printed_page=1057 -->

**CASE 2024-21**

**TE CHARLES DEWITT**

**v.**

**NASHVILLE PRESBYTERY**

**RULING ON APPEAL**

**February 19, 2025**

On November 21, 2024, the Appellant wrote to the Stated Clerk
requesting to withdraw his Appeal. The case is dismissed according to the
Appellant’s request.

**CASE 2024-29**

**MR. PETER BENYOLA**

**v.**

**CENTRAL FLORIDA PRESBYTERY**

**RULING ON COMPLAINT**

**February 19, 2025**

This case is administratively out of order and cannot be put in order. The
Complainant is not a member of any congregation of the PCA and lacks
standing to bring a complaint ( _BCO_ 43-1). This decision is controlled by the
SJC’s decisions in SJC Cases _Benyola v. Central Florida_ 2022-17, 18, and 19.
The Complaint in this case seeks to address the same issues presented in those
actions. The Complainant’s membership status has not changed since those
decisions. The Complaint is dismissed.

SJC reviewed the proposed ruling and approved the final version of the Ruling
by vote of **21-0-0 with 2 absent and 1 disqualified** .

Bankson _Concur_ Eggert _Concur_ Maynard Disqualified
Bise _Concur_ Evans _Concur_ Neikirk _Concur_

Bruce _Concur_ Garner _Absent_ Pickering _Concur_
Carrell _Concur_ Greco _Concur_ Sartorius _Concur_

<a id="ga52-p1058"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1060 printed_page=1058 -->

Coffin _Concur_ Kooistra _Concur_ Stegall Absent
Dodson _Concur_ Landry _Concur_ Waters _Concur_
Donahoe _Concur_ Lee _Concur_ White _Concur_

Dowling _Concur_ Marcinak _Concur_ Wilson _Concur_

**IV. PROPOSED MANUAL CHANGES**

**Item 1 – Documents included in the Record**

_OMSJC_ 7.2 (c)

Add a new _OMSJC_ 7.2.c and reletter the current sections “c” and “d” to “d”

and “e,” with the new _OMSJC_ 7,2,c to read as follows:

Only documents provided to the Office of the Stated Clerk can be included in
the Record of the Case. No document shall be included in the Record of the

Case that must be accessed through external links, websites, QR codes, filesharing services, or other external digital platforms.

Rationale:

1. In recent years, many Presbyteries have moved to using links, Dropbox
links, or QR codes to allow Presbyters to access documents.
2. Sometimes the Presbytery or parties then continue that convention by
seeking to include document in the record o fa case by including a
reference to a link or code.

3. Members of the SJC do not have access to those links or codes. As a result,
they cannot access any parts of the Record that are included by those links
or codes.

4. Further, even if the SJC is provided with those links or codes, there is no
way to ascertain whether the documents thus accessed, assuming they are
even still available at the link, are the same as the documents that were
originally before the lower court.
5. This amendment simply ensures that all parts of the Record of a Case are
readily available to members of the SJC.

<a id="ga52-p1059"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1061 printed_page=1059 -->

**Item 2 – Objections to the Record**

_OMSJC_ 7.4 (d) and (e)

1. Add a new paragraph d. and reletter the current paragraphs d., e. and f. to
e., f. and g., with the new paragraph d. to read as follows:

d. If a party objects to any portion of the Record, the other party
shall respond to such objection within 15 days of receipt of
notification of the objection. Such response shall be in writing
and must be filed by mail or electronic means with Stated
Clerk and the Panel Chairman, or the Chairman of the
Commission if the case is to be heard by the Commission. The
response must also be copied to the party that lodged the
objection. If a party, having received notice that an objection
to the Record has been filed, fails to respond to that objection
within 15 days they shall be deemed to have acceded to the
objection.

2. Amend the current paragraph d. (to be relettered to e.) as follows:

e. If the other party shall accede to the objection, it shall be so
stipulated in writing and made a part of the Record of the
Case. Such stipulation shall be filed by mail or electronic
means with the Stated Clerk and the Panel Chairman, or the
Chairman of the Commission if the case is to be heard by the
Commission, not more than 30 days after the date the last
party received such Record of the Case from the Stated Clerk.

**Rationale:**

1. The current provisions stipulate what happens if the other party accedes to
an objection or does not accede, but there is nothing explicit about what
happens if the other party fails to respond.
2. It is not evident why parties have only 15 days to lodge an objection to the
ROC but have 30 days to respond to an objection (assuming that is what
the current paragraph d means). Further, allowing 30 days will often
unnecessarily prolong the time needed to adjudicate the Case.

<a id="ga52-p1060"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1062 printed_page=1060 -->

3. The current paragraph d is difficult to follow. If the last clause means the
other party has 30 days to respond to an objection, why is that tied to
receipt of the Record rather than receipt of the objection? What is meant
by “such Record?” etc.
4. Nothing in the current paragraph d requires that the party that lodged the
objection be notified of the other party’s response. If the party lodging an
objection must notify the other party, it seems reasonable that the other
party should have to notify the objecting party of their response.
5. Being clear that failure to respond to an objection will constitute acceding
to that objection will force parties to operate within the deadline.

**Item 3 – Cross References to** _**OMSJC**_ **8.5**

**Amend** _**OMSJC**_ **Sections to correct cross-references to** _**OMSJC**_ **8.5 as**

**follows:**

16.1.e Briefs under these paragraphs shall comply with the
requirements for briefs in _OMSJC_ 8.4 8.5 but shall not
