exceed 5 pages.

17.5 Proposed judgments of a Judicial Panel are not binding on
the parties, but the Stated Clerk shall transmit to the
parties a copy of the panel’s proposed decision and inform
the parties of their right to request a rehearing before the
full Standing Judicial Commission. If any party desires a
rehearing by the full Commission, such request must be
filed with the Stated Clerk within 14 days after receipt of
said Panel’s proposed and recommended decision. Said
party filings uc ha request for re hearing may attach a brief
giving such party’s reasons and arguments for this
request. Such brief must comply with the requirement of
_OMSJC_ 8.3-8.4 8.5 of this Manual as it relates to

supplemental briefs.

18.12.b(4) Concurring or Dissenting opinions shall conform to the
specifications for a preliminary brief ( _OMSJC_ 8.4 8.5.ab.).

<a id="ga52-p1061"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1063 printed_page=1061 -->

19.3.b The briefs (see _OMSJC_ 8.1, 8.2, 8.3, 8.4, 8.5) shall be
made available by electronic means or by inclusion in the
Commissioner Handbook for the next following General
Assembly.

**Rationale:**

1. OMSJC 8 was amended at the 2024 General Assembly with the addition
of a new 8.1. The amendments resulting a renumbering of several
paragraphs . The cross-references found in the OMSJC were not updated
at that time. This amendment accomplishes that updating.

**Item 4 – Amend** _**RAO**_ **17-3 and** _**OMSJC**_ **10.1, 10.2, and 6.2 regarding**
**panel composition**

That _RAO_ 17-3 be amended by strike and add as follows (added text

underscored):

17-3. The Standing Judicial Commission may appoint a judicial panel

of not less than three five of its members to hear the case in

accordance with the provisions of tThe Rules of Discipline in the
BCO and these Rules of Assembly Operations. Such panels shall
be chosen as follows:

a. A pool with the names of teaching elder Standing Judicial

Committee members shall be established, and another pool
with the names of ruling elder members shall be established.
The chairman of the Standing Judicial Commission shall draw
by lot names of panel members and alternates from each pool
until he reaches a combination of three teaching elders and two
ruling elders, or vice versa. and The chairman shall notify the
Stated Clerk of the General Assembly, who shall notify,
immediately, those so chosen.
b. If a panel member so selected shall be disqualified under

Section 2 of the Operating Manual for Standing Judicial
Commission, he shall be replaced by the alternate another of
the same class drawn from the pool.

<a id="ga52-p1062"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1064 printed_page=1062 -->

c. The names of those panel members selected and qualified shall

not be returned to the pool until all names therein have been
exhausted. The name of an alternate, who does not serve as a
panel member, shall be returned to the pool. The names of
those members whose terms expire at the next General
Assembly shall be withdrawn from the pool on March May 1
of that year.
d. Immediately after each General Assembly the name of each

new member selected at such General Assembly shall be added
to his respective the pool.

And that _OMSJC_ 10.1, 10.2 and 6.2 be amended by strike and add as follows

(added text underscored):

10.1 Ordinarily, A Judicial Panel should shall consist of three at least

five qualified ( _OMSJC_ 2.3.d.) members of the Commission,
which should shall include at least one two teaching elders and
one two ruling elders. In more serious cases, larger panels may
be appointed with an approximately equal balance between
teaching and ruling elders.

10.2 At least one ruling elder and at least one teaching elder shall be

named as alternates for each Judicial Panel. An alternate will not

be expected to attend the meeting of the Judicial Panel unless he
is needed to meet the quorum. Should, in the judgment of the
officers, circumstances warrant, larger panels may be appointed
with an approximately equal balance between teaching and ruling
elders.

6.2 a quorum forth e transaction of business at any meeting o fa

Judicial Panel appointed hereunder shall be the number of the
members of the Judicial Panel appointed as principals a majority
of the Panel members, which shall include at least one teaching
elder and one ruling elder.

RATIONALE

Experience has taught us that the current provisions for alternates are
inadequate ina number of ways, e.g., it is critical f oran alternate, that might
be called upon to act in place of an incapacitated qualified member, to be fully

<a id="ga52-p1063"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1065 printed_page=1063 -->

informed of all Panel proceedings, and yet he is not expected to attend Panel
meetings.
However, experience also suggests that the pragmatic reason for
alternates has expired, i.e., that there be members who could fill a quorum for
in-person meetings. The old rule was put in place when SJC Panels flew to
out-of-state, in-person Panel Hearings, which necessitated having a TE and an
RE alternate because rescheduling would be very costly. But those Hearings
are now conducted via videoconference.

The value of a larger Panel has also been demonstrated by experience,
as most of our Panels allow full participation of alternates except for
parliamentary actions. And, most of our Panels are nearly unanimous, even
including the views of the non-voting parties in their reports. A larger Panel
holds promise for a more likely representation of the full Commission, and
thus a more likely approval, and removes all the practical impediments of the
“alternates” system.
It is unlikely that this change will create more work for SJC members
or increase the time it takes to adjudicate a case.

**REPORT OF THE**

**STATED CLERK**

_See 52-7 (p. 18) for the Assembly’s action_

In one generation, American culture has shifted from 90
percent of the population identifying as Christian to less than
half belonging to any church and 95 percent _not_ engaged in
weekly Sunday worship…

Responsible and caring reactions to the loss of a majority
Christian influence in America must include understanding
how great the pressures are upon Christian children,
especially those who are trying to live faithfully in this present
evil age. Christians who are age fifty and older were raised in
an era when the vast majority of Americans thought of
themselves as Christian…. Such adults need to recognize that
a christian young person ina public high school today who is
regular in church attendance, faithful in biblical standards of
sexual purity, and orthodox in theology, may be the only such
“Christian” they know — especially if 30 percent of their
female friends say their gender is nonbinary, all of their male
friends have normalized pornography usage, half of their
church friends are sexually active, and most peers will hide
their faith identity from the university they wish to attend lest
their admission be jeopardized.

In their life context, young people of this generation who
remain faithful to their Savior need to be understood,
supported, and celebrated by the generation that precedes
them. Pastors and churches need to understand younger and
older generations and minister to both.

Extracted from _The Multigenerational_
_Church Crisis: Why We Don’t Understand_
_Each Other and How to Unite in Mission_,

<a id="ga52-p1066"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1068 printed_page=1066 -->

When preparing these reports, my tendency is to think in terms of costs,
practices, and polarities to overcome. Yet, the spiritual realities of our culture
that have been described in the opening paragraphs of this report should
remind us all of the far greater concerns of our labors and the needed revival
that our Lord alone can provide through our dependence upon the gospel of
his grace. in such a time as this, we must work to keep our ministries from
becoming focused upon the number of votes we can rally for a General
Assembly cause that will be forgotten in a year and freshly dedicate ourselves
to being the church that prioritizes the eternal welfare of our children’s
children. We consider the following details of our denomination’s practices
not merely to measure our influence but to assess how best we can be used to
advance Christ’s Kingdom in a lost world.

**F** **IFTY** **-S** **ECOND** **G** **ENERAL** **A** **SSEMBLY**
Registration for the 52 [nd] General Assembly to be held in Chattanooga, June
24-27, opened in early January. As of May 1, almost 1800 commissioners have
registered; our largest number ever at this point. Such large interest and
involvement is deeply encouraging and profoundly important as we address
issues vital to our church’s witness to our culture and seek God’s favor and

guidance for our united mission.

**O** **VERTURES TO THE** **52** **[ND]** **G** **ENERAL** **A** **SSEMBLY**
Fifty (50) overtures have been submitted to the 52 [nd] General Assembly, with
one (Overture 21) withdrawn by the submitting Presbytery.

In addition to the usual overtures relating to Presbytery boundaries, there are
requests for the erection of five ad interim committees: a study committee on
Christian Nationalism, a committee to write a pastoral letter on christian
citizenship and Church/State relations, a committee to revise the Directory for
Worship for adoption with full constitutional authority, a committee to solicit
and receive any recommendations for improvement, optimization, and greater
transparency from the lower courts for all funded General Assembly works,
and a committee on artificial intelligence (AI). Two other overtures do not seek
study committees but call for studies: one directs all agencies and committees,
Presbyteries, and Sessions to study incorporating Bitcoin (a specific brand
within the Cryptocurrency category of finance) into their balance sheets; the
other directs the AC to study GA meeting locations.

One overture proposes amending _BCO_ 14-1.12 to change the voting
membership of the AC by making those members elected to represent the

<a id="ga52-p1067"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1069 printed_page=1067 -->

committees and agencies non-voting advisory members. Six overtures deal
with candidates and officers. Two address voting in congregational meetings.
Eleven seek amendment to the Rules of Discipline. Two others address judicial
cases by amending provisions in the _BCO_ outside of the Rules of Discipline
(regarding how GA would handle a minority decision from the SJC and to
allow a Session to elect one of its members as moderator in judicial cases).
Four seek to amend the Directory for Worship. Two overtures seek General
Assembly to direct permanent committees to take specific actions. Four give
directions regarding the collection of statistics and information regarding the
churches of the PCA. One seeks to change the mission and ministry of byFaith.
The final overture requests the Assembly to encourage all churches,
Presbyteries, officers, and members to discernment and compassion regarding
immigrants.

You can find a complete listing and the text of the overtures submitted to date
in the _Commissioners Handbook_ [Appendix U, p. 1149]. Please go to the
General Assembly website https://pcaga.org/resources /#overtures to keep
abreast of additional overtures as they are received. The deadline for
submitting overtures to this year’s Assembly that do not require CCB review
is May 24 (“one (1) month (31 days) prior to the opening of the General
Assembly” _RAO_ 11-8).

_**BCO**_ **A** **MENDMENT** **V** **OTES BY** **P** **RESBYTERIES**
The 51 [st] General Assembly sent four proposed amendments to the _Book of_
_Church Order_ to Presbyteries for advice and consent this past year. All have
received the required approval from two-thirds of the presbyteries (58) with
significant margins and will be before this Fifty-Second Assembly for a final
vote (requiring approval by a majority of commissioners).

**Presbytery Votes on Amendments Sent Down**

**by the 51** **[st]** **General Assembly**

(as of June 10, 2025)

**For** **Against**
**Item 1** : Amend _BCO_ 13-6 for clarity in transfers of 67 2
ordination

**Item 2** : Amend _BCO_ 13-6 to add personal character
and family management to the examination of
transferring ministers

68 1

<a id="ga52-p1068"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1070 printed_page=1068 -->

**Item 3** : Amend _BCO_ 32-19 to expand representation
of accused persons before church courts

**Item 4** : Amend _BCO_ 43-1 to change the prohibition
against ‘interlocutory appeal’ by complaint

64 5

66 3

For a complete tally of the Presbytery votes as of June 10, please see
Attachment 1. In a recent collaborative handoff, a frequently referenced
amendment tracker managed by TE Scott Edburg is available as a new AC
resource. The _BCO_ [Amendment Tracker (https://www.pcaac.org/dashboard/)](https://www.pcaac.org/dashboard/)
now provides real-time updates as they are officially reported by Presbytery
clerks. The tracker reflects the amendment language, the Assembly’s votes to
send each item to the Presbyteries, as well as the Presbyteries votes.

Votes for _BCO_ amendments may continue to be submitted until the 52 [nd] GA
convenes. Presbyteries should be aware that not voting on a proposed
amendment to the _BCO_ is tantamount to a negative vote ( _BCO_ 26-2) because
the advice and consent of two-thirds of Presbyteries are required and not twothirds of the votes cast. This method of counting votes differs from _Robert’s_
_Rules of Order_, in which abstentions (refraining to vote) are not counted in
determining a majority. The _BCO_ is of higher parliamentary authority than
_Robert’s Rules of Order_ .

**T** **HEOLOGICAL** **E** **XAMINING** **C** **OMMITTEE** **(TEC)**
The AC provides administrative assistance and arrangements for the
Theological Examining Committee (TEC). The TEC is comprised of three
Teaching Elders and three Ruling Elders (with two alternates) who serve the
General Assembly by ensuring that candidates for positions of influence in our
denomination are both gifted for and committed to promoting the glory of God
by promoting the biblical gospel of Jesus Christ. The TEC task, according to
_The_ _Book of Church Order_, is to examine “all first and second level
administrative officers of committees, boards, and agencies, and those acting
temporarily in these positions who are being recommended for first time
employment” ( _BCO_ 14-1.14).

Since the 51 [st] General Assembly, two first- and second-level Committee and
Agency officers have been referred to the TEC to be examined as required by
_BCO_ 14-1.14. The TEC expects to complete these exams in May and report on
them to the General Assembly.

For the Theological Examining Committee Report, see p. 1101 of this
_Handbook_ [Appendix S, p. 1053].

<a id="ga52-p1070"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1072 printed_page=1070 -->

**S** **TANDING** **J** **UDICIAL** **C** **OMMISSION** **(SJC)**
The Standing Judicial Commission is one of the treasures of the PCA, with
members of differing perspectives typically rising above partisan debates and
internet suppositions to issue decisions according to how sworn testimony
aligns with our Standards. The Lord has blessed our entire denomination
through the dedication and sacrificial service of these men pursuing their
responsibilities with integrity.

The SJC is not separately funded but operates administratively as a
subcommittee of the AC ( _RAO_ 17-1). The Stated Clerk serves as Clerk of the
Commission and parliamentarian ( _OMSJC_ 3-8). Since the meeting of the 51 [st]
General Assembly, the 24 members of the SJC have completed 21 cases and
have approximately 24 cases currently in process. These numbers reflect
almost double the cases from the previous year.

For a report on the cases handled by the SJC, see the Standing Judicial
Commission Report, p. 2001 of this _Handbook_ [Appendix Q, p. 755].

**C** **OOPERATIVE** **M** **INISTRIES** **C** **OMMITTEE** **(CMC)**
The Cooperative Ministries Committee (CMC) is designed to unite the leaders
of our various agencies and institutions with the elected leaders of the General
Assembly in coordinated ministry to advance the mission of the PCA as a
whole. The simple goal is to have all the horses in the harness pulling in the
same direction. That goal is achieved by relational camaraderie as well as by
ministry strategizing, denominational assessment, and shared insights
regarding best practices and resources for advancing Christ’s mission in our
challenging times. As Stated Clerk, i ama member and serve as secretary
( _RAO_ 7-4.c).

The CMC met on January 22, 2025. Committee and Agency leaders shared
challenges, goals, and prayers.

For the Cooperative Ministries Committee Report, see p. 381 of this _Handbook_

[Appendix E, p. 257].

**I** **NTERCHURCH** **R** **ELATIONS** **C** **OMMITTEE** **(IRC)**
The Interchurch Relations Committee (IRC), which met on September 10,
2024, and February 25, 2025, corresponds and maintains relationships with
churches and organizations with whom we have fraternal or ecclesiastical
relations. The Committee has invited Fraternal Delegates to address the

<a id="ga52-p1071"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1073 printed_page=1071 -->

General Assembly on a rotational basis due to their number. Delegates will
also be welcomed to the General Assembly at a luncheon hosted by IRC
members and the IRC Committee of Commissioners members. The PCA
Stated Clerk is an ex officio member and secretary of the IRC ( _RAO_ 3-2.j).

Our founding fathers strongly believed that we in the PCA constituted a
blessed branch, but not the only branch, of Christ’s true church. That is why
we, as a denomination, seek cooperative ministry and understanding with likeminded and Bible-believing organizations. Supporting the mission and faith of
those serving Christ with us leverages our resources, furthers our Savior’s
mission, and links us with those of like faith for matters of legal and legislative
protection.

To this end, the PCA maintains _Fraternal_ Relations with other
Presbyterian/Reformed denominations and _Corresponding_ Relations with
other evangelical denominations for exchanging greetings and letters of
encouragement as well as communications on issues of common concern. The
IRC is recommending that we extend Corresponding Relationships to the
presbyterian church of australia and non gu uk ri stu ui se rush a tar (i.e.,
Universal Reformed Christian Church of Nigeria, “NKST”).

For the report of the Interchurch Relations Committee, see p. 901 of this
_Handbook_ [Appendix I, p. 323].

**C** **OMMITTEE ON** **C** **ONSTITUTIONAL** **B** **USINESS**
All overtures proposing amendments to the _BCO_ and _RAO_ ( _RAO_ 11-5) are
referred to the CCB to seek advice for the Assembly regarding their conformity
with our constitution. The Office of the Stated Clerk supports the CCB in its
operations and communications. The Stated Clerk may also seek advice from
the CCB on matters of constitutional import ( _RAO_ 8-2.b.1).

All overtures proposing amendments to the _BCO_ and _RAO_ were referred to
CCB. Two non-judicial references were received and referred to CCB.
Additionally, I requested advice on two matters from the CCB. CCB’s advice
can be found in the Committee on Constitutional Business Report, p. 271 of
this _Handbook_ [Appendix C, p. 223].

<a id="ga52-p1072"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1074 printed_page=1072 -->

**P** **RESBYTERY** **C** **LERKS**
The Stated Clerk’s Office has an important duty of facilitating communication
among clerks for sharing knowledge and experience. One way that this office
fulfills this responsibility is through the annual Presbytery Clerks
Conference, which provides instruction for new clerks as well as a forum
where experienced clerks can compare notes and discuss challenges they face.
This conference took place on December 6-7, 2024.

This office also provides tools to this group (e.g., the Clerks’ Handbook and
Presbytery portal) to help all clerks in their work. The staff of the AC works
hand-in-hand under the supervision of the Stated Clerk with Presbytery clerks
to serve the whole church in a unified effort that enables local presbyteries and
churches to thrive in accordance with our mission and standards.

**T** **HE** **O** **FFICE**

The Stated Clerk’s Office has seen more transitions among its retirement-age
personnel this year—some planned and some not. In November, Margie
Mallow retired after twelve years as the _byFaith_ and RPR Administrative
Assistant. From her desk just inside the office door, Margie was the face of the
office, giving care-filled attention to visitors and callers to the AC. Mrs. Alyssa
Cho, wife of TE Joshua Cho, joined the office to fill the void. Alyssa holds a
MA in Biblical Counseling and an MDiv, both from Westminster Theological
Seminary (PA). She taught five semesters of English at Hapdong Seminary in
Korea, where she and her husband lived for five years. We are blessed to have
an administrative assistant with such excellent qualifications and such an
engaging and spiritually encouraging demeanor.

The Office also recently lost Wayne Herring, who passed away suddenly on
March 11. A minister of the PCA since its founding, Rev. Herring was a former
member and chair of the AC. He joined the Stated Clerk’s Office in 2008
following a 45-year pastoral career. In addition to serving churches in
Mississippi, Alabama, South Carolina, and Tennessee, Rev. Herring taught at
RTS Jackson and was one of the founding leaders of Reformed Youth
Ministries (RYM) and Reformed University Fellowship (RUF). Rev. Herring
left an indelible mark upon the PCA and touched the lives of countless
churches and people.

I report with sadness that long-term Stated Clerk Roy Taylor has continued to
struggle to recuperate from heart surgery last year. His affairs and
communication are now being managed by his son.

<a id="ga52-p1073"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1075 printed_page=1073 -->

**BY** **F** **AITH**

Through productive use of writing resources expressed through digital means,
byFaith’s publishing output i satan all-time high: since the summer of 2024
byFaith has produced one piece of original content every weekday, resulting
in almost 200 articles to date in the new format.

With more content and new editorial leadership, the website is experiencing
vastly increased readership throughout our denomination. The number of
visitors and page views on the website have more than doubled in the past year.
Further, views (i.e., times when a web page is loaded or reloaded ina browser)
are 33 times higher, and clicks (i.e., times when a user clicks on links or ads in
search results) have increased 17 times over the same period. Also, by moving
to a digital format, the budgeted costs of byFaith to the Administrative
Committee have dropped by more than half from 2024 to 2026. Our net costs
are projected to reach a more than decade low of $159k in 2026 and, with
increased attention to advertisers, we expect further reductions

Not only is byFaith being increasingly well received by PCA readers, but the
recognized quality of the articles is allowing the PCA to serve the wider church
and world. Recent articles from byFaith have been referenced by the
_Washington Post_, _Wall Street Journal_, _Atlanta Journal-Constitution_, _U.S._
_News and World Report_, Reuters, _Christianity Today_, The Gospel Coalition,
_World Magazine_, Challies, and Ministry Watch. In short, as byFaith is
fulfilling its General Assembly mandates, production i satan all-time high,
readership is vastly improved, and costs are significantly decreased.

**S** **TATISTICS**
Last year, I reported to the Assembly that church demographer Ryan Burge
had indicated that the PCA was the only significantly sized denomination in
the United Stated to see growth since the Covid pandemic. In each year that I
have reported to you, I have said that our denomination is approximately 400thousand persons. However, that figure was estimated by rounding up. This is
the first year that I can tell you that, by the Lord’s blessing, we are
approximately 400-thousand persons by rounding down. The Lord has now
increased our numbers to over 400-thousand! A number of news sources have

picked up the general aspects of this story of our outlier status as a growing
denomination, but we should add that the particulars of the Lord’s blessing are
just as encouraging: again we were blessed with the Triple-5 dynamics of _more_

<a id="ga52-p1074"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1076 printed_page=1074 -->

_than_ 5000 Adult professions, _more than_ 5000 child professions, and _more than_
5000 infant baptisms. We have also seen overall giving grow ever increasingly
above the billion-dollar mark for the third straight year.

At least two observations are important: First, we cannot be certain about these
figures because only about half of our churches report. So, the numbers could
be worse, but they are likely much better. I would encourage all of our
churches to report as a witness and testimony to what g odis doing ina bible believing body committed to truth and mission. Second, we should give all
glory to the Lord. He blessed Israel to show that he could be gracious to the
least significant and most stiff-necked of people. Sometimes we are those
people in which he displays his grace most boldly in the light of our
unworthiness. Thus, to the extent that he is glorified by blessing people such
as we, let us resolve to honor him in the way that we conduct his business,
address one another, testify to his truth, and pursue his Great Commission.

See Attachment 2 for the 2024 Five-Year Summary of Statistics. You may also
view the statistics online at https://presbyteryportal.pcanet.org/Report/StatsReport.

**F** **INAL** **T** **HOUGHTS**

as we testify to God’s grace, let us also glorify him with profound awareness
and humble thanksgiving for how he displays his faithfulness to nations and
generations:

While studies previously cited indicate that as many as twothirds of young people will leave the church after high school
(at least for a time), these figures are based upon those who
call themselves “Christian,” not necessarily upon those who
have been raised in homes where life patterns and
relationships consistently honor Christ. The children of such
homes are twice as likely to retain their parents’ faith as those
with contradictory or insincere parental examples.

The truth that counters despair over the large exodus of youth
is the fact that the vast majority (60 to 87 percent, depending
on the study) of children continue to embrace their parents’
faith, if their parents live it. Such parental faithfulness is not
simply about insisting on our children’s good behavior
(requiring children to put on their “Jesus Jacket” with its

<a id="ga52-p1075"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1077 printed_page=1075 -->

crushing restrictions and expectations). Instead, parents exert
their greatest influence on their children’s future faith when
demonstrating a loving and trusting relationship with Christ
that is reflected in gracious attitudes and biblically consistent
actions toward those in and outside the home.

When such attitudes and actions are reinforced by models of
faith in church and school, then the likelihood of instilling
lifetime faith in children soars even higher—even into the 90
percent range. Of course, there are no guarantees, but when
home and church partner in providing models of sincere faith
(among adults and peers), then it is more than fair to say that
two-thirds of the children of such backgrounds do not leave
the church after high school; rather, at least two-thirds remain
engaged.

Extracted from _The Multigenerational_
_Church Crisis: Why We Don’t Understand_
_Each Other and How to Unite in Mission_,
2025

The spiritual challenges of our time and culture are great, but not greater than
the spiritual provisions of our Lord. In the face of the great discouragement of
so many young people leaving the church in our time is the profound evidence
of the still-powerful influence of the Christian family and church community.
We who believe in the covenant promises of our sovereign God have no reason
to despair. What God yet displays to us in the statistics of faith-consistent
families is his eternal faithfulness and irrevocable covenant. Our duty and
privilege as elders of the PCA is to continue to work in mutual love and respect
for a church that enables God’s people to live in humble faithfulness to the
One whose grace never fails.

Bryan Chapell
Stated Clerk

<a id="ga52-p1076"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1078 printed_page=1076 -->

Attachment 1

Attachment 1: BCO Amendments

**2024-2025**

BCO **AMENDMENTS SENT DOWN TO PRESBYTERIES**

**by the 51** **[st]** **GENERAL ASSEMBLY**

**for ADVICE AND CONSENT**

NOTE: The Stated Clerk’s Office sends the proposed amendments only in

their final form as approved by the General Assembly.

**ITEM 1:** **Amend** _**BCO**_ **13-6** for clarity in transfers of ordination
(strikethrough for deletions, underlining for additions)

[Overture 2 was answered in the affirmative as amended by the
Overtures Committee.]

**13-6.** Ministers Transferring into the Presbytery

a. a ministers seeking admission to a pres byte ry from

another Presbyteries Presbytery in the Presbyterian
Church in America shall be examined on Christian

experience, and also touching as to his their views in
theology, the Sacraments, and church government. If
the examining Presbytery does not accept the
Minister seeking admission, it shall record this fact
along with its rationale, if one is adopted, in the
minutes, and shall communicate its rationale to his
current Presbytery.

b. If an applicants comes from another denominations,

the Presbytery shall examine him them thoroughly in
knowledge and views as required by the trials listed
in _BCO_ 21-4 and require them him to answer in the
affirmative the questions put to candidates at their
ordination. Ordained ministers from other

denominations being considered by Presbyteries for

<a id="ga52-p1077"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1079 printed_page=1077 -->

reception may come under the extraordinary
provisions set forth in _BCO_ 21-4.

c. In every case, Presbyteries shall also require each

ordained ministers coming from other denominations
entering the Presbytery to state the specific instances
in which they he may differ with the _Confession of_
_Faith_ and _Catechisms_ in any of their statements
and/or propositions, which differences the court shall
judge in accordance with _BCO_ 21-4 (see _BCO_ 21-4.fg (see also _RAO_ 16-3.e.6.a-d).

[ **Stated Clerk’s Note** : The _BCO_ currently reads “ _BCO_ 21-4.e,f.”
However, last year, GA added a new 21-4.e, renumbering thee and f
referred to here so as to become f and g. This GA also amended _RAO_
16-3.e, moving subparagraphs 16-3.e.5.a-d to 16-3.e.6.a-d.]

So that the amended section will read as follows:

**13-6.** Ministers Transferring into the Presbytery

a. a ministers seeking admission to a pres byte ry from

another Presbytery in the Presbyterian Church in
America shall be examined on Christian experience,
and also as to his views in theology, the Sacraments,
and church government. If the examining Presbytery
does not accept the Minister seeking admission, it
shall record this fact along with its rationale, if one is
adopted, in the minutes, and shall communicate its
rationale to his current Presbytery.

b. If an applicant comes from another denominations,

the Presbytery shall examine him thoroughly as
required by the trials listed in _BCO_ 21-4 and require
him to answer in the affirmative the questions put to
candidates at their ordination. Ordained ministers

from other denominations being considered by
Presbyteries for reception may come under the
extraordinary provisions set forth in _BCO_ 21-4.

<a id="ga52-p1078"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1080 printed_page=1078 -->

c. In every case, Presbyteries shall also require each

ordained minister entering the Presbytery to state the
specific instances in which he may differ with the
_Confession of Faith_ and _Catechisms_ in any of their
statements and/or propositions, which differences the
court shall judge in accordance with _BCO_ 21-4.f-g
(see also _RAO_ 16-3.e.6.a-d).

**For: 67** **Against: 2**

government.

management), and also as to his views in theology,

examining Presbytery does not accept the Minister
seeking admission, it shall record this fact along with

**ITEM 3:** **Amend** _**BCO**_ **32-19** to expand representation of accused persons
before church courts (strikethrough for deletions, underlining for
additions)

**[Overture 26** was answered in the affirmative as amended by the
Overtures Committee.]

**32-19.** No professional counsel shall be permitted as such
to appear and plead in cases of process in any court; but
an accused person may, if he desires it, be represented
before the Session or the Presbytery by any member in
good standing of a church in the same Presbytery or by
any Teaching Elder member of that Presbytery, or before
the General Assembly by by any communing member of
the same particular church, or before any other court, by
any member of that court any member in good standing in
the PCA. A member of the court so employed shall not be
allowed to sit in judgment in the case. Courts are
encouraged to suggest to the accused/appellant the names
of potential representatives and potential advisors he
might contact.
So that the amended section will read as follows:

**32-19.** No professional counsel shall be permitted as such
to appear and plead in cases of process in any court; but
an accused person may, if he desires it, be represented
before the Session or the Presbytery by any member in
good standing of a church in the same Presbytery or by
any Teaching Elder member of that Presbytery, or before
the General Assembly by any member in good standing
in the PCA. A member of the court so employed shall not
be allowed to sit in judgment in the case. Courts are
encouraged to suggest to the accused/appellant the names
of potential representatives and potential advisors he
might contact.

**For: 65** **Against: 5**

**ITEM 4: Amend** _**BCO**_ **43-1** to change the prohibition against ‘interlocutory
appeal’ by complaint (strikethrough for deletions, underlining for additions).

[ **Overture 21** was answered in the affirmative as amended by the Overtures
Committee.]

**43-1.** a complaint is a written representation made
against some act or decision of a court of the Church. It is
the right of any communing member of the Church in
good standing to make complaint against any action of a
court to whose jurisdiction he is subject, except that no
complaint is allowable in a judicial case in which an
appeal is pending after process has commenced. If, at any
time after process has commenced, a _BCO_ 43-1 complaint
is timely filed, the court’s adjudication shall be delayed
until after the judicial case has been completed, or, if an
appeal is filed, after it has been fully adjudicated or
withdrawn. During judicial process, the accused may
raise _BCO_ 32-14 objections on a variety of matters and
the original court shall decide each. Those decisions could
be reviewable on appeal.

So that the amended section will read as follows:

**43-1.** a complaint is a written representation made
against some act or decision of a court of the Church. It is
the right of any communing member of the Church in
good standing to make complaint against any action of a
court to whose jurisdiction he is subject, except that no
complaint is allowable in a judicial case after process has
commenced. If, at any time after process has commenced,
a _BCO_ 43-1 complaint is timely filed, the court’s
adjudication shall be delayed until after the judicial case
has been completed, or, i fan appeal is filed, after it has
been fully adjudicated or withdrawn. During judicial
process, the accused may raise _BCO_ 32-14 objections on
a variety of matters and the original court shall decide
each. Those decisions could be reviewable on appeal.

<a id="ga52-p1090"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1092 printed_page=1090 -->

<a id="ga52-p1105"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1107 printed_page=1105 -->

**REPORT OF THE**

**THEOLOGICAL EXAMINING COMMITTEE**

_See 52-13 (p. 22) for the Assembly’s action_

**I. Introduction to the Committee’s Work**

**A. Purpose and Scope of Examinations**

According to our Book of Church Order, Teaching Elders should seek
office “out of a sincere desire to promote the glory of God in the
Gospel of his Son.” In this same spirit, the Theological Examining
Committee (comprising 3 Teaching Elders, 3 Ruling Elders, and 2
alternates) serves the General Assembly by ensuring that candidates
for positions of influence in our denomination are both gifted for and
committed to promoting the glory of God by promoting the biblical
gospel of Jesus Christ. Our task, according to The Book of Church
Order, chapter 14, section 1.14, is to examine “all first and second
level administrative officers of committees, boards, and agencies, and
those acting temporarily in these positions who are being
recommended for first time employment.”

**B. Nature of Examinations**

The examinations we administer resemble those for the ordination of

Teaching Elders in the PCA, covering the following areas: Christian
experience, theology, the sacraments, church government and the
BCO, Bible content, church history, and the history of the PCA. Our
standard procedure is to administer a written examination covering
theological views, followed by an intensive oral examination, which
entails not only views but knowledge in these areas.

**II. Summary of the Committee’s Work**

From July 2024 to March 2025, the committee has conducted no
examinations but expects to complete two examinations in May 2025.

Respectfully submitted,

TE K.J. Drake, Chairman TE Drew Martin, Secretary

<a id="ga52-p1106"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1108 printed_page=1106 -->

Supplemental Report

**SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF THE**

**THEOLOGICAL EXAMINING COMMITTEE**

**I. Summary of the Committee’s Work**

In the past year, the committee has conducted two (2) examinations.

1. On **May 13, 2025**, the committee examined **RE Andrew Rein** for the
position of **RUF Coordinator** . All areas of the exam were sustained and
unanimously approved by the committee.

The committee reviewed the nominee’s written statement of personal
differences with the _Westminster Standards_, included below, and, per _RAO_
8-3 and 16.3.e.5, deemed the following difference to be more than
semantic but neither hostile to the system of doctrine nor striking at the
vitals of religion.

- _WCF_ 21.8, _WLC_ 117, 119, _WSC_ 60-61: “I do embrace the
Confession’s view of the Lord’s Day. It should be a day set aside
that is very different than other days. We should not seek to “do
as we please”, but instead should be characterized by worship,
rest, family, and the enjoyment of God’s marvelous creation.
Further, humanity needs the Sabbath—it is a gift of God. Because
the Sabbath is a gift from God (i.e., “it was made for man;” Mark
2:27) and if we call the Sabbath day a “delight” (Isaiah 58:13) then
I believe there are activities that are worshipful and restful that
may be pursued on the Lord’s Day that would be outside the
definition of recreation as meant by the Assembly. For instance,
enjoying God’s creation through hiking, riding a bike, or
swimming or building family bonds through games and other
recreational activities.”

2. On **May 20, 2025**, the committee examined **RE Rob Wynkoop** for the
position of **VP for Operations and Finance** at Covenant College. All
areas of the exam were sustained and unanimously approved by the
committee.

<a id="ga52-p1107"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1109 printed_page=1107 -->

The committee reviewed the nominee’s written statement of personal
differences with the _Westminster Standards_, included below, and, per _RAO_
8-3 and 16.3.e.5, deemed the following difference to be more than
semantic but neither hostile to the system of doctrine nor striking at the
vitals of religion.

- _WCF_ 21.8, _WLC_ 117, 119, _WSC_ 60-61: I do embrace the
Westminster Confession’s view of the Lord’s Day as the Sabbath.
I am particularly thankful for the emphasis on the preparation of
our hearts and ordering our common affairs beforehand. I agree
and, my family consistently practices, the Sabbath as a day for
worship of our Lord and a time of rest from our normal
employments. I do differ with the _WCF_ ’s view on recreation. I do
not believe that recreation should be completely forbidden on the
Sabbath as God can be glorified and rest can be found through that
recreation.

- _WLC_ 109: “I affirm the fundamental principle that worship is
instituted by God alone. I do believe that artistic depictions,
particularly of Jesus, do not necessarily constitute a violation of
the second commandment if used outside of worship. It is
important to note that they should never be venerated or used as
objects of devotion. They can be used to assist in teaching (outside
of worship) about the birth, life, death and resurrection of Jesus,
particularly with younger children (e.g. _Jesus Storybook Bible_ ).”

Respectfully submitted,

TE K.J. Drake, Chairman TE Drew Martin, Secretary

<a id="ga52-p1108"></a>

**OVERTURES**

**TO THE FIFTY-SECOND GENERAL ASSEMBLY**

**Note:** The following are the original texts of the overtures as submitted by
Presbyteries to the PCA Office of the Stated Clerk. For any changes to these
overtures by the Committees of Commissioners and/or the Assembly, see the
respective Committee of Commissioners Reports in the Daily Journal.

##### OVERTURE 1 from Northern New England Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 32-2 to Clarify that a Court May Investigate Whether
Charges Should Be Filed”

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 32-2 be amended by striking the current language and

replacing it with the following (deletions indicated by strikethrough;
additions by underlining).

Existing language:
**32-2.** Process against an offender shall not be commenced unless
some person or persons undertake to make out the charge; or unless
the court finds it necessary, for the honor of religion, itself to take the
step provided for in _BCO_ 31-2.

Proposed language:
**32-2.** Prior to commencing process against an alleged offender,
regardless of how allegations arose, the court shall investigate to the
extent necessary to determine:

1) i fit is a personal or general offense
2) if personal, whether Matthew 18 has been followed
3) if the nature of the alleged offense rises to a level

warranting judicial process
4) if the accuser is like any of those described in _BCO_ 31-8
5) if there is a strong presumption of guilt
6) if it is reasonable to expect the offense can be proven

through judicial process.

<a id="ga52-p1164"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1166 printed_page=1164 -->

**Rationale**

The current language of _BCO_ 32-2 has sometimes been read to allow an
individual to force a court of the Church to institute process by the filing of
charges, taking the court’s discretion to investigate and determine if discipline
in the restricted and technical sense of judicial process ( _BCO_ 27-1.b) is the
necessary avenue for achieving the ends of discipline as laid out in _BCO_ 27-3.

Thus, in Case 2011-15, the SJC declares that “in general _BCO_ 32-2 requires
that a court commence process upon the filing of charges.” [1] The concurring
opinion to case 2010-04 lays out this two-fold path to judicial process in more
detail.

There are two (2) basic ways for such concerns to be resolved
by a trial.

First, a trial may result from a _BCO_ 31-2 investigation, which
leads to a finding o fa strong presumption of guilt. Upon such a
finding, a prosecutor is appointed, an indictment drawn, Parties
cited, and a trial is conducted.

Second, a trial may result when someone, who believes an
offense has occurred and after practicing Matthew 18:15, files
"charges" under _BCO_ 32-2.

It is at this point that some err in understanding how
our Constitution instructs us to proceed. Some,
incorrectly, believe that when _BCO_ 32-2 “charges” are
made, the Constitution requires (1) a _BCO_ 31-2
investigation and (2) a finding o fa strong presumption of
guilt, before proceeding to trial. The correct interpretation
of the Constitution, as set forth in the Decision, is that
when _BCO_ 32-2 "charges" are made out (and Matthew
18:15 has been practiced and _BCO_ 31-8 does not apply),
there is NO investigation under _BCO_ 31-2, but rather the
Court goes directly to appoint a prosecutor, an indictment
is drawn, the Parties are cited, and a trial is conducted. [2]

1 Case 2011-15: _Complaint of Mr. Hahn v. Philadelphia Metro_, _M42GA_, 2014, 516.
https://www.pcahistory.org/pca/ga/42nd_pcaga_2014.pdf
2 Case 2010-04: _Sartorius et al. v. Siouxlands_, _M39GA_, 2011, Concurring Opinion, 585.
https://www.pcahistory.org/pca/ga/39th_pcaga_2011.pdf

<a id="ga52-p1165"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1167 printed_page=1165 -->

This reading of the current language means that the filing of charges by an
individual is both a necessary and a sufficient cause to institute judicial
process. Thus, a court that has received charges has avery narrow latitude in
not ordering an indictment to be drawn and proceeding to trial (only the
provisions of _BCO_ 31-8 according to the decision in Case 2011-15).

However, previously one of the issues raised in Case 91-6 was “Does a court
have the prerogative of not adjudicating a case once charges have been placed
before it?” At that time, the SJC answered, and GA approved (it was the
procedure at that time for GA to approve SJC decisions), that “A court has the
duty to investigate the allegations to determine if a trial is necessary ( _BCO_ 312).” [3] Thus, GA concluded that a court should have the obligation to investigate
before instituting process. Ramsay agrees:

The whole tone of these Rules is evidently this: that judicial
prosecution i sn otto be originated, either by a court orb ya
voluntary prosecutor, unless the honor of religion requires
this step, all other means to this end having been first
exhausted; but that this means is, of course, to be resorted to
when the honor of religion does require it, the honor of
religion to be preserved at every cost. And the honor of
religion is synonymous with the holiness of the Church. [4]

But by the reading in _Hahn_, an individual may force a court of the Church to
process without the court having the ability to exhaust all other means to
maintain the ends of discipline (the glory of God, the purity of his Church, and
the keeping and reclaiming of lost sinners, _BCO_ 27-3). This removes the
discretion of the court, the use of the wisdom God has given to the shepherds
he has called to care for that portion of the Church.

the amendment makes explicit that bringing allegations before a court is a
necessary but not a sufficient step for moving into judicial process. [5] It makes
explicit that the court always has the authority and the duty to investigate and

3 Case 1991-6: _Lovelace v. Northeast Presbytery_, _M21GA_, 1993, 190.
https://www.pcahistory.org/pca/ga/21st_pcaga_1993.pdf
4 F. P. Ramsay, _An Exposition of the Form of Government and the Rules of Discipline of the_
_Presbyterian Church in the United States_ (Richmond, VA: The Presbyterian Committee of
Publication, 1898), 194.
5 A more in-depth argument for this position and against the reading put forward in the SJC’s
decision on Case 2011-15 may be found in the concurring opinion to that decision filed by RE
Howard Donahoe. _M42GA_, Case 2011-15, 517-528.

<a id="ga52-p1166"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1168 printed_page=1166 -->

pursue the ends of discipline through the appropriate means. The proposed
language maintains judicial process in its proper and historical place, as the
means of last resort when all other means have been exhausted.

_Adopted by the Northern New England Presbytery at its stated meeting, May_

_18, 2024._
_Attested by TE Per Almquist, stated clerk._

##### OVERTURE 2 from South Florida Presbytery (to MNA)

“Expand Boundaries of South Florida Presbytery”

**Whereas**, South Florida Presbytery is the geographically closest PCA

presbytery to the Caribbean Sea,

**Whereas**, the United States has two inhabited territories in the Caribbean Sea,

namely Puerto Rico and the US Virgin Islands, and

**Whereas**, Briarwood Presbyterian Church and Evangel Presbytery established

PCA works in Puerto Rico, and

**Whereas**, Briarwood Presbyterian Church and Evangel Presbytery requested

that South Florida Presbytery assume jurisdiction over the PCA works
in Puerto Rico, and

**Whereas**, South Florida Presbytery received the PCA works in Puerto Rico,

and

**Whereas**, South Florida Presbytery also received inquiries from PCA

members living in the US Virgin Islands about establishing a PCA
work there, and

**Whereas**, South Florida Presbytery already includes the Cayman Islands (a

British territory in the Caribbean Sea),

**Now therefore be it resolved** that the South Florida Presbytery overture the

52 [nd] General Assembly to restructure the boundary of the South
Florida Presbytery to include Puerto Rico and the US Virgin Islands,
effective July 1, 2025.

<a id="ga52-p1167"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1169 printed_page=1167 -->

_Adopted by the South Florida Presbytery at its stated meeting, August 13,_

_2024._

_Attested by /s/ TE Robbie Crouse, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 3 from Arizona Presbytery (to OC, AC)

“Erect Ad Interim Committee on Christian Nationalism”

**Whereas**, the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) has adopted the

American Revision (1788) to the Westminster Confession of Faith,
which represents a substantial change from the Historic Text (1646)
as it relates to the relationship between the church and the civil
magistrate; and

**Whereas**, there has been recent teaching, among those who subscribe to or

have adopted certain aspects of the Reformed Tradition, as to the
nature of this relationship between the church and the civil magistrate,
which have caused confusion, division, and dissension even among
the congregants of PCA churches and affected PCA pastors and
officers; and

**Whereas**, these teachings are from a particular type of Christian Nationalism

which professes conformity to the Reformed Tradition; and

**Whereas**, church courts have the responsibility to determine questions of

doctrine seriously, and in general to maintain truth and righteousness,
condemning erroneous opinions which tend to bring injury to the
peace, purity, or progress of the Church ( _BCO_ 11-4);

**Therefore, be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] PCA General Assembly erect and

convene an Ad Interim Study Committee on Christian Nationalism
( _RAO_ 9-1), to study the relationship between Christian Nationalism,
Theonomic Reconstructionism, and similar viewpoints deemed
necessary by the committee. Further, the committee shall advise on
whether these viewpoints and formulations are in conformity with the
system of doctrine taught in the Westminster Standards or where they
may diverge from the system of doctrine. Further, the committee shall

<a id="ga52-p1168"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1170 printed_page=1168 -->

write a report that gives pastoral guidance when addressing
congregations, new members, and future officers of the PCA.

**Be it further resolved** that the budget for the study committee be set at

$10,000/year and that funds be derived from gifts to the AC designated
for that purpose, with General Assembly appealing to the various
Presbyteries to fund this initiative via such gifts ( _RAO_ 9-3).

**Be it further resolved** that the Arizona Presbytery is committed to offer a

$2,500 gift to assist in funding the work of this Ad Interim Study
Committee.

_Adopted by the Arizona Presbytery at its stated meeting, September 6, 2024._
_Attested by /s/ RE Richard Wolfe, stated clerk._

##### OVERTURE 4 from the Presbytery of South Texas (to OC, AC)

“Erect Ad Interim Study Committee on Christian Nationalism”

**Whereas,** there has been debate among churches in the Presbyterian Church

in America (PCA) concerning the relationship of church and state that
has occurred under the broad term of Christian Nationalism; and

**Whereas,** Christian Nationalism has been promulgated theologically by

Moscow Theology and Postmillennial Theonomic Reconstructionism;
and

**Whereas,** there has been disagreement and confusion regarding what these

various viewpoints actually teach; these variations have caused
confusion, division, and dissension among the congregants of PCA
churches and disturbed PCA pastors and officers; and

**Whereas,** many members of PCA churches continue to encounter, be

influenced by, and be disturbed by these teachings from various online
sources; and

<a id="ga52-p1169"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1171 printed_page=1169 -->

**Whereas,** questions have been raised regarding current conformity of these

viewpoints to the system of doctrine taught in the Westminster
Standards as held by the PCA; and

**Whereas,** the above mentioned viewpoints have brought confusion on

theological definitions and concepts that have disrupted the peace,
purity, and progress of a significant number of families, churches, and
sessions within the PCA; and

**Whereas,** church courts have the responsibility to determine questions of

doctrine seriously, and in general to maintain truth and righteousness,
condemning erroneous opinions which tend to bring injury to the
peace, purity, or progress of the Church ( _BCO_ 11-4);

**Therefore be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] PCA General Assembly erect and

convene a new Ad Interim Study Committee to study Christian
Nationalism ( _RAO_ 9-1), to study the relationship between Christian
Nationalism, Moscow Theology, Postmillennial Theonomic
Reconstructionism, and similar viewpoints deemed necessary by the
committee. Further, the committee shall advise on whether these
viewpoints and formulations are in conformity with the system of
doctrine taught in the Westminster Standards or where they may
diverge from the system of doctrine. Further, the committee shall write
a report that gives pastoral guidance when addressing congregations,
new members, and future officers of the PCA; and

**Be it further resolved**, that the committee shall be appointed by the

moderator; and

**Be it further resolved**, that the budget for the study committee be set at

$10,000/year and that funds be derived from gifts to the AC designated
for that purpose. Presbyteries, churches, and individuals are highly
encouraged to contribute funds to the AC, designated for this work
with members of South Texas Presbytery committed to offer
$5,000/year.

_Adopted South Texas Presbytery at its stated meeting, October 26, 2024._
_Attested by RE Barry McBee, stated clerk._

<a id="ga52-p1170"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1172 printed_page=1170 -->

##### OVERTURE 5 from Calvary Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Grant _BCO_ 60-63 Full Constitutional Status”

**Whereas**, the constitutional documents of our denomination include the

Directory of Public Worship (Preface III, _BCO_ 26-1) with our _Book of_
_Church Order_ ( _BCO_ ) implying the usefulness of and adherence to the
Directory of Public Worship; and

**Whereas**, it was the desire of our founding fathers to have an authoritative

Directory of Public Worship for our beloved denomination; and

**Whereas**, the Third General Assembly placed a temporary preface to the

Directory of Public Worship stating it “does not have the force of law”
which was only to be in place “until a more precise statement can be
prepared by the Constitutional Documents Committee;” and

**Whereas**, the Constitutional Documents Committee was dismissed in 1978

without any report or action pertaining to the Directory of Public
Worship; and

**Whereas**, the 1980 General Assembly tasked the Permanent Committee on

Judicial Business to re-write the Directory of Worship, but this
committee failed to achieve the original goal of authoring an
authoritative Directory of Public Worship beyond chapters 56-58
concerning the sacraments; and

**Whereas**, the most recent changes to our Directory of Public Worship being

the constitutionalizing of _BCO_ 59-3 by the 47 [th] General Assembly;
and

**Whereas**, the current Directory of Public Worship is wrongly considered just

pious advice; and

**Whereas**, the current Directory of Public Worship is often neglected or

ignored due to its lack of constitutionality;

<a id="ga52-p1171"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1173 printed_page=1171 -->

**Therefore, be it resolved,** that the Calvary Presbytery hereby requests the 52 [nd]

General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to declare
_BCO_ chapters 60 through 63 having fully constitutional status.

_Adopted by the Calvary Presbytery at its stated Autumn meeting (CP163),_

_October 24, 2024_
_Attested by /s/ RE Melton L. Duncan, Stated Clerk_

##### OVERTURE 6 from Rocky Mountain Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 18-2 to Allow Flexibility on Applications to Come
Under Care”

**Be it resolved:** That _BCO_ 18-2 be amended by the current language as follows
(underlining for additions, strikethrough for deletions):

**18-2** . Every applicant for the ministry must put himself under the care
of Presbytery, which should ordinarily be the Presbytery that has
jurisdiction of the church of which he is a member. The endorsement
of his Session must be given to the Presbytery, consisting of
testimonials regarding his Christian character and promise of
usefulness in the ministry. The endorsement should also describe the
activities of ministry the applicant has participated in with brief
evaluation.

Every applicant for care shall be a member of the
congregation whose session provides an endorsement for at least six
months before filing his application, except in those cases deemed
extraordinary by the Presbytery.

Every applicant must should file his application with the clerk
of the Presbytery at least one month before the meeting of the
Presbytery (unless a pres byte ry has a different stated application
deadline). An applicant for care may not be received under care and
examined for ordination at the same meeting of the Presbytery, since
he must serve a period of at least one year of internship prior to
ordination (see _BCO_ 19-7 and 21-4). An applicant for internship is
obliged to be under care and may be licensed to preach the Gospel;
further, one who is not already under care may be taken under care, be

<a id="ga52-p1172"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1174 printed_page=1172 -->

licensed to preach the Gospel, and become an intern at the same
meeting of Presbytery.

So that the amended section will read as follows:

**18-2** . Every applicant for the ministry must put himself under the care
of Presbytery, which should ordinarily be the Presbytery that has
jurisdiction of the church of which he is a member. The endorsement
of his Session must be given to the Presbytery, consisting of
testimonials regarding his Christian character and promise of
usefulness in the ministry. The endorsement should also describe the
activities of ministry the applicant has participated in with brief
evaluation.

Every applicant for care shall be a member of the
congregation whose session provides an endorsement for at least six
months before filing his application, except in those cases deemed
extraordinary by the Presbytery.

Every applicant should file his application with the clerk of
the Presbytery at least one month before the meeting of the Presbytery
(unless a pres byte ry has a different stated application deadline). An
applicant for care may not be received under care and examined for
ordination at the same meeting of the Presbytery, since he must serve
a period of at least one year of internship prior to ordination (see _BCO_
19-7 and 21-4). An applicant for internship is obliged to be under care
and may be licensed to preach the Gospel; further, one who is not
already under care may be taken under care, be licensed to preach the
Gospel, and become an intern at the same meeting of Presbytery.

**Rationale**

As previously written _BCO_ 18-2 restricts the ability of Presbyteries to receive
the applications of applicants for the ministry any time after one month before
a Presbytery meeting.

In the case of some Presbyteries, this deadline has delayed the licensure and
ordination process for ministry applicants by setting an inflexible application
deadline. In the case of some Presbyteries (that meet three times a year), the
process for ministry applicants can be delayed more than five (5) months.

<a id="ga52-p1173"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1175 printed_page=1173 -->

The adopted text of the 1 [st] General Assembly required a candidate to file his
application “at least two months before the meeting of the Presbytery.” The
17 [th] General Assembly adopted a change in the requirement, replacing “two
months” with “one month”, thus requiring an applicant to submit an
application “at least one month before the meeting of Presbytery.”

The change adopted by the 17 [th] Assembly was a reasonable change that
reflected the amount of time needed for Stated Clerks to collect and

disseminate applications in preparation for Presbytery meetings. This change
reflected cultural changes in communication with the advent of Fax and email.

Given the ability to disseminate applications more quickly via internet, email,
Dropbox, etc. Another change to 18-2 is reasonable.

This amendment seeks to eliminate an inflexible deadline for ministry
applicants (with a change to _should_ from _must_ ) and give discretion to
individual Presbyteries in setting their own application deadline. This could
alleviate the delay of ordination for some ministry candidates and will better
reflect the current time needed to collect and disseminate applications in
preparation for Presbytery meetings.

_Adopted by the Rocky Mountain Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 25,_

_2024._

_Attested by TE Del Farris, stated clerk._

##### OVERTURE 7 from the Missouri Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 34-1 Regarding Advisors and Original Jurisdiction in
Process Against a TE”

**Whereas** the issue of maintaining high moral and theological standards for

ministers in the PCA continues to be of utmost importance; and

**Whereas** the _Book of Church_ order speaks to how these standards are to be

upheld by Presbyteries; and

**Whereas** the Church of the Lord Jesus Christ and her ministers are under

greater public scrutiny than at any time in recent memory; and

<a id="ga52-p1174"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1176 printed_page=1174 -->

**Whereas** the officers of the church are charged with devising methods and

strategies that enable us to maintain high standards for our ministers
while also protecting the rights of the accused; and

**Whereas** every Presbytery could benefit from the voluntary input,

engagement and concerns of their fellow Presbyteries, especially in
controversial cases; and

**Whereas** it remains important to maintain the principle of original jurisdiction;

and

**Whereas** removal of original jurisdiction should only take place when it can

be grounded in the constitution; and

**Whereas** the PCA has grown considerably since the time _BCO_ 34-1 was last

amended; and

**Whereas** the age of the internet has significantly impacted the way we share

and receive information;

**Therefore, be it resolved** that _BCO_ 34-1 be amended as follows (underlining

for additions, strikethrough for deletions):

**34-1.** Process against a minister shall be entered before the Presbytery
of which he is a member and may include Teaching or Ruling Elders
from other Presbyteries as non-voting advisors. However, if at least
five other Presbyteries believe that the Presbytery of original
jurisdiction has failed to uphold the Constitution the Presbytery
refuses to act in doctrinal cases or cases of public scandal and two
other Presbyteries and if they can cite the specific portion of the
Constitution that may have been violated, they may jointly request the
General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction (to first receive and
initially hear and determine), and the General Assembly shall do so

So that the amended paragraph will read as follows:

**34-1.** Process against a minister shall be entered before the Presbytery
of which he is a member and may include Teaching or Ruling Elders
from other Presbyteries as non-voting advisors. However, if at least

<a id="ga52-p1175"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1177 printed_page=1175 -->

five other Presbyteries believe that the Presbytery of original
jurisdiction has failed to uphold the Constitution in doctrinal cases or
cases of public scandal and can cite the specific portion of the
Constitution that may have been violated, they may jointly request the
General Assembly to assume original jurisdiction (to first receive and
initially hear and determine), and the General Assembly shall do so.

**Rationale for granting the ability to appoint advisors from other**

**Presbyteries:**

Over the last several years far too many ministerial ethics and doctrinal
scandals have undermined the work of the Church. As Presbyteries have
sought to address these scandals critics of their efforts have arisen from both
inside and outside the church. One thing that all these critics seem to have in
common is their concern that Presbyteries are incapable of or unwilling to
sanction their own.

The evidence supporting these claims is uneven, however, there is no doubt
that there is a public perception that Presbyteries are networks of ‘good ole
boys’ who protect their own from appropriate accountability, a perception that
continues to damage the credibility of the Church.

Setting aside the question of whether Presbyteries are actually guilty of
shielding their own from discipline, any change that increases the
accountability ina disciplinary investigation should be viewed as a good thing
if done appropriately.

In an effort to address the charge that Presbyteries are sometimes unwilling to
sanction their own while continuing to uphold the integrity of our system of
polity surrounding discipline, we propose that giving Presbyteries an option of
including Ruling or Teaching Elder advisors from other Presbyteries may
improve the transparency of an investigation which would also be a helpful
step toward reclaiming some of the credibility that has been lost in recent

years.

In offering this amendment we recognize that Presbyteries always have an
option to reference (See _BCO_ 41) to the higher court, however, it is our view
that doing so skips an important step in our normal polity of trial by a jury of
one’s peers in a court that remains as close to the original evidence and

<a id="ga52-p1176"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1178 printed_page=1176 -->

witnesses in the case as possible. Furthermore, this change keeps intact the
important role that appellate courts have in reviewing the original proceedings
and findings in any given case while also enabling presbyteries to receive more
objective feedback from fellow officers on their disciplinary processes.

As to the question of whether the corresponding investigative procedure in a
local congregation should be changed to match the newly proposed language
above, we do not think it is necessary since doctrinal and public scandal almost
always pertains to teaching elders.

**Rationale related to the change from “two” Presbyteries to “five”**
**Presbyteries:**

The rationale here is simply that “two” Presbyteries is far too low of a bar in
the current state of play in the PCA and the age of the internet in which the
“facts of a case” can quickly become diluted, taken out of context or missed
completely.

Originally there was no minimum bar for requesting a change of original
jurisdiction. Then in 1988 the bar was set at two (2) Presbyteries. At that time
the PCA had 44 presbyteries so this meant that about 4.5% of the Presbyteries
would need to overture for the change in original jurisdiction. Today the PCA
has 88 presbyteries. For this reason, our overture proposes that we elevate the
bar for appealing for a change of original jurisdiction to five (5) Presbyteries,
which would amount to only a slightly larger percentage (5.6%) of
Presbyteries than that which existed when this rule was first put in place.

We also added a line that reinforces the five Presbytery threshold by making
certain that there is broad agreement related to the specific constitutional
violation and not five different ideas on what part of the constitution was
violated.

Our view was that the threshold on assuming original jurisdiction should be
high enough to preclude a very small number of Presbyteries from forcing the
case out of its original context and low enough that the burden for doing so is
not inordinate.

**Rational for clarifying the meaning of “act:”**

<a id="ga52-p1177"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1179 printed_page=1177 -->

The final proposed change has to do with clarifying the meaning of the phrase
“refuses to act” which has always been difficult. In the past, this phrase was
used with wide facility and could be taken to mean any of refuses to:
“investigate,” “indict,” “discipline,” or “convict.”

As we wrestled with what to do with this, we at first thought that restricting
the use of the word “act” to “investigate” might be best. However, after further
dialogue we began to see that restricting the word to “investigate” could
preclude other presbyteries from exercising important accountability for a
truly wayward presbytery so long as it had conducted some kind of
investigation.

We then thought that perhaps the word should be elevated to “convict” to make
sure that outside Presbyteries should allow process to fully unfold before
taking action. While this was attractive, we began to worry about some of the
rules for filing complaints and appeals once a case had been decided and
whether other presbyteries would have the time to develop an overture
requesting original jurisdiction within the rules. We also were concerned about
the actions that some presbyteries may take, often for good reasons, to shelter
their records to protect the privacy of their work while it was ongoing and even
after it had been decided.

In the end, we settled on clarifying the meaning of “act” as having “failed to
uphold the constitution.” We reached this conclusion because it preserves a
somewhat wide range of application for concerned presbyteries, but it compels
those wishing to intervene to clarify exactly how the presbytery of original
jurisdiction has “failed to uphold the constitution” rather than simply accusing
them of not “acting.”

_Adopted by Missouri Presbytery at its stated meeting of January 21, 2025._
_Attested by RE Robert Wilkinson, stated clerk_

<a id="ga52-p1178"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1180 printed_page=1178 -->

##### OVERTURE 8 from the Nashville Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 24-1 To Require REs and Deacons to State
Confessional Differences”

**Whereas,** the Presbyterian Church in America is a confessional denomination,

as defined by its Constitution ( _BCO_, Preface, III) and the vows that its
Teaching Elders, Ruling Elders, and Deacons are required to take
concerning that Constitution ( _BCO_ 21-5, Q.2; 24-6, Q.2); and,

**Whereas,** the PCA clarified this confessional position to be “good faith

subscription” at its 30 [th] General Assembly (2002) [6] by adding to the
examination of Teaching Elder candidates what is now _BCO_ 21-4.f,g,
which includes: “While our Constitution does not require the
candidate’s affirmation of every statement and/or proposition of
doctrine in our _Confession of Faith_ and _Catechisms_ ” and that the
Presbytery “shall require the candidate to state the specific instances
in which he may differ with the _Confession of Faith_ and _Catechisms_
in any of their statements and/or propositions.”; and,

**Whereas,** _BCO_ 24-1 is the parallel description of required examinations for

Ruling Elder and Deacon nominees, but does not contain the
clarifications concerning good faith subscription of _BCO_ 21-4.f,g;
and,

**Whereas,** the vow pertaining to the Confession and Catechisms that Ruling

Elders and Deacons take is identical to the vow for Teaching Elders
( _BCO_ 21-5, Q.2; 24-6, Q.2), suggesting that their relationship to the
_Confession_ and _Catechisms_ is the same as that of Teaching Elders;

**Therefore, be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] General Assembly amend _BCO_ 24-1
to include the following two paragraphs:

While our Constitution does not require the nominee’s affirmation of
every statement and/or proposition of doctrine in our _Confession of_
_Faith_ and _Catechisms_, it is the right and responsibility of the Session
to determine if the nominee is out of accord with any of the
fundamentals of these doctrinal standards and, as a consequence, may

6 https://www.pcahistory.org/pca/ga/30th_pcaga_2002.pdf (pgs. 218-221)

<a id="ga52-p1179"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1181 printed_page=1179 -->

not be able in good faith sincerely to receive and adopt the _Confession_
_of Faith_ and _Catechisms_ of this Church as containing the system of
doctrine taught in the Holy Scriptures (cf. _BCO_ 21-5, Q.2; 24-6, Q.2).

Therefore, in examining a nominee for ordination, the Session shall
inquire not only into the nominee’s knowledge and views in the areas
specified above but also shall require the nominee to state the specific
instances in which he may differ with the _Confession of Faith_ and
_Catechisms_ in any of their statements and/or propositions. The court
may grant an exception to any difference of doctrine only if in the
court’s judgment the nominee’s declared difference is not out of
accord with any fundamental of our system of doctrine because the
difference is neither hostile to the system nor strikes at the vitals of
religion.

And enumerate the paragraphs to match the format of _BCO_ 21-4, so that the
amended section will read as follows:

**24-1.** Every church shall elect persons to the offices of ruling elder and
deacon in the following manner: At such times as determined by the Session,
communicant members of the congregation may submit names to the Session,
keeping in mind that each prospective officer should be an active male member
who meets the qualifications set forth in 1 Timothy 3 and Titus 1. Nominees
for the office of ruling elder and/or deacon shall receive instruction in the
qualifications and work of the office.

a. Each nominee shall be examined in:

1. his Christian experience, especially his personal character and

family management (based on the qualifications set out in 1
Timothy 3:1-7 and Titus 1:6-9),
2. his knowledge of Bible content,
3. his knowledge of the system of doctrine, government, discipline

contained in the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in
America ( _BCO_ Preface III, The Constitution Defined),
4. the duties of the office to which he has been nominated, and
5. his willingness to give assent to the questions required for

ordination. ( _BCO_ 24-6).

<a id="ga52-p1180"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1182 printed_page=1180 -->

b. In the examination of the nominee’s personal character, the Session

shall give specific attention to potential notorious concerns. Careful
attention must be given to his practical struggle against sinful actions,
as well as to persistent sinful desires. The nominee must give clear
testimony of reliance upon his union with Christ and the benefits
thereof by the Holy Spirit, depending on this work of grace to make
progress over sin (Psalm 103:2-5, Romans 8:29) and to bear fruit
(Psalm 1:3, Gal. 5:22-23). While imperfection will remain, when
confessing sins and sinful temptations publicly, the nominee must
exercise great care not to diminish the seriousness of those sins in the
eyes of the congregation, as though they were matters of little
consequence, but rather should testify to the work of the Holy Spirit
in his progress in holiness (1 Cor. 6:9-11).

c. Notwithstanding the above, the Session may render a decision on

Christian experience at any point in the process, and based on that
decision, may judge him ineligible for that election.

d. While our Constitution does not require the nominee’s affirmation of

every statement and/or proposition of doctrine in our _Confession of_
_Faith_ and _Catechisms_, it is the right and responsibility of the Session
to determine if the nominee is out of accord with any of the
fundamentals of these doctrinal standards and, as a consequence, may
not be able in good faith sincerely to receive and adopt the _Confession_
_of Faith_ and _Catechisms_ of this Church as containing the system of
doctrine taught in the Holy Scriptures (cf. _BCO_ 21-5, Q.2; 24-6, Q.2).

e. Therefore, in examining a nominee for ordination, the Session shall

inquire not only into the nominee’s knowledge and views in the areas
specified above but also shall require the nominee to state the specific
instances in which he may differ with the _Confession of Faith_ and
_Catechisms_ in any of their statements and/or propositions. The court
may grant an exception to any difference of doctrine only if in the
court’s judgment the nominee’s declared difference is not out of
accord with any fundamental of our system of doctrine because the
difference is neither hostile to the system nor strikes at the vitals of
religion.

<a id="ga52-p1181"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1183 printed_page=1181 -->

f. If there are candidates eligible for the election, the Session shall report

to the congregation those eligible, giving at least thirty (30) days prior
notice of the time and place of a congregational meeting for elections.

g. If one-fourth (1/4) of the persons entitled to vote shall at any time

request the Session to call a congregational meeting for the purpose of
electing additional officers, it shall be the duty of the Session to call
such a meeting on the above procedure. The number of officers to be
elected shall be determined by the congregation after hearing the
Session’s recommendation.

_Adopted by the Nashville Presbytery at its 120_ _[th]_ _Stated Meeting on November_
_12, 2024_
_Attested by R. Neil Spence, Stated Clerk_

##### OVERTURE 9 from the James River Presbytery (to CCB, OC, AC, CC,

CDM, CTS, Geneva, MNA, MTW, PCAF, RH, RUF)
“Amend BCO 14-1 to Make AC Members Appointed by Committees
and Agencies Non-voting Advisory Members”

**Whereas** the permanent committees and agencies of the General Assembly

exist to serve the church and to promote the Great Commission ( _BCO_
14-1.5);

**Whereas** the PCA has, since its founding, been committed to a grass roots

system of church government;

**Whereas** the General Assembly’s Administrative Committee exercises

tremendous influence over the PCA’s national operations, including
the nomination of the Stated Clerk; the preparation of budgets for the
permanent committees and agencies; arranging for the General
Assembly to meet each year; and the hiring, retention, and dismissal
of numerous employees;

**Whereas**, as presently constituted in _BCO_ 14-1.12 and _RAO_ Article V, the

Administrative Committee consists of eleven (11) members elected at
large by the General Assembly ( _BCO_ 14-1.12.a; _RAO_ 5-1.a) and nine

<a id="ga52-p1182"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1184 printed_page=1182 -->

(9) members appointed by the permanent committees and agencies
