( _BCO_ 14-1.12.b, _RAO_ 5-1.b);

**Whereas** this means that 45% of the votes on the Administrative Committee,

as presently constituted, are cast by men appointed to the Committee
rather than elected;

**Whereas**, per _BCO_ 14-1.12 and _RAO_ 5-3, “The chief administrative officers

of the program Committees and Agencies may attend any meeting of
the Administrative Committee. They shall be entitled to the privilege
of the floor, but shall not have a vote and must be excluded when an
executive session is called;” and

**Whereas** the PCA will be better served if the voting members of the

Administrative Committee are directly elected by the General
Assembly, making the Committee more directly accountable to the
will of the Assembly; now, therefore,

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 14-1.12 is amended as follows (underlining for

additions):

By inserting the words “non-voting, advisory” into the stem of _BCO_ 14-1.12.b
between the word “One” and the word “member” and by inserting the
following sentence at the end of the second paragraph: “The non-voting,
advisory members appointed to the Administrative Committee pursuant to
subparagraph (b) above shall have the privilege of the floor but shall not be
entitled to vote and must be excluded when an executive session is called.”

These changes shall result in an amended _BCO_ 14-1.12 that reads:

12. The Assembly permanent committees are the Administrative

Committee of General Assembly, Committee on Discipleship
Ministries, Committee on Mission to North America, Committee
on Mission to the World, and Committee on Reformed
University Fellowship.

The Administrative Committee of General Assembly
(AC) shall consist of twenty (20) members:

a. Eleven members in classes elected through the

standard nomination and election procedure,

<a id="ga52-p1183"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1185 printed_page=1183 -->

b. One non-voting, advisory member each from the

following program committees or agencies:
1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM);
2. Covenant College (CC);
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS);
4. Mission to North America (MNA);
5. Mission to the World (MTW);
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF);
7. Geneva Benefits Group, Inc. (Geneva);
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF);
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH).

The eleven members at large shall serve a term of four
years. The chairman of the Administrative Committee shall be
one of its members at large. The non-voting, advisory members
appointed to the Administrative Committee, pursuant to
subparagraph (b) above, shall have the privilege of the floor but
shall not be entitled to vote and must be excluded when an

executive session is called.

Each program committee and agency shall designate its
member each year at the last meeting of the committee or board
before the meeting of General Assembly. The chief
administrative officers of the program committees and agencies
may attend any meeting of the Administrative Committee. They
shall be entitled to the privilege of the floor but shall not have a
vote and must be excluded when an executive session is called.

Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on
Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the World,
and Committee on Reformed University Fellowship shall consist
of fifteen (15) men divided into five classes of three men each,
with two men being TEs and one RE or two men being REs and
one TE on alternate years, elected to serve five-year terms.
Committees on Discipleship Ministries, Mission to North
America, Mission to the World, and Reformed University
Fellowship shall have one ruling and one teaching elder as
alternates to fill any vacancy that may occur during the year.

Persons who have served for a full term, or for at least two
years of a partial term, on one of the Assembly’s permanent
committees or agencies shall not be eligible for re-election to an

<a id="ga52-p1184"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1186 printed_page=1184 -->

Assembly committee until one year has elapsed. (Exceptions
may be permitted in agency bylaws approved by the Assembly.)

**Be it further resolved** that in order to harmonize the language in the _Rules of_

_Assembly Operation_ with this amendment to the _Book of Church_
_Order_, at the time that this amendment is finally and fully adopted by
the General Assembly, having met with the approval of 2/3 of the
presbyteries, _RAO_ 5-1.b is amended as follows (underlining for
additions):

By inserting the words “non-voting, advisory” in the first line between the
words “One” and “member” such that an amended _RAO_ 5-1 reads:

**5-1.** The Administrative Committee of General Assembly (AC) shall

consist of twenty (20) members:

a. Eleven members in classes elected through the

standard nomination and election procedure,
b. One non-voting, advisory member each from the

following program committees or agencies:
1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM);
2. Covenant College (CC);
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS);
4. Mission to North America (MNA);
5. Mission to the World (MTW);
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF);
7. Geneva Benefits Group, Inc. (Geneva);
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF);
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH).

**Be it further resolved** that in order to harmonize the language in the _Rules of_

_Assembly Operation_ with this amendment to the _Book of Church_
_Order_, at the time that this amendment is finally and fully adopted by
the General Assembly, having met with the approval of 2/3 of the
presbyteries, _RAO_ 5-2 is amended by adding the following sentence at
the end the end of the paragraph, “The non-voting advisory members
appointed to the Administrative Committee pursuant to _RAO_ 5-1.b
shall have the privilege of the floor but shall not be entitled to vote and
must be excluded when an executive session is called.” This

<a id="ga52-p1185"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1187 printed_page=1185 -->

amendment will result in an amended _RAO_ 5-2 that reads (underlining
for additions):

**5-2.** The eleven members at large shall serve a term of four years. The

chairman of the Administrative Committee shall be one of its

members at large. The non-voting, advisory members appointed
to the Administrative Committee, pursuant to _RAO_ 5-1.b, shall
have the privilege of the floor but shall not be entitled to vote and
must be excluded when an executive session is called.

_Adopted by the James River Presbytery at its stated meeting, January 18,_
_2025._

_Attested by J. Christopher Rohde, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 10 from the Central Florida Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 22 to Affirm the ‘Consent of the Governed’ Regarding
Assistant Pastors”

**Whereas** _The Book of Church Order_ 22-1 states that the “various pastoral

relations are Pastor, Associate Pastor, Assistant Pastor”; and

**Whereas** the Pastor and Associate Pastor are elected by the congregation and

become members of the Session ( _BCO_ 22-2) but the Assistant Pastor
is called by the Session and is not a member of the Session ( _BCO_ 223); and

**Whereas** Assistant Pastors are members of Presbytery, vote on both the

church’s higher courts, and exercise ministerial authority in preaching
the Word and administering the Sacraments; and

**Whereas** all Elders, both Teaching and Ruling, being of one class of office

have parity of office (BCO 8-9); and

**Whereas** the Apostles did not appoint church officers without the consent of

the governed (Acts 6:3; Acts 14:23); and

<a id="ga52-p1186"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1188 printed_page=1186 -->

**Whereas** preliminary principle 6 of _The Book of Church Order_ states “the

power to elect persons to the exercise of authority in any particular
society resides in that society” (see also _BCO_ 3-1); and

**Whereas** the idea of an Assistant Pastor being called by the Session and not

being called by the congregation is contrary to historic Presbyterian
polity; and

**Whereas** the first time the Assistant Pastor relation was codified in American

Presbyterianism was in 1945 in the nineteenth edition of the PCUS’
_Book of Church Order_ (see chapter xxiv, paragraph 126); and

**Whereas** Assistant Pastors are not infrequently called to serve PCA churches

for many years—even decades—without the direct consent of the
congregations they shepherd as teaching elders;

**Therefore, be it resolved** to amend the _BCO_ by adding/striking the

following language of _BCO_ 22-3 and 22-4 to read as follows
(underlining for additions; strikethrough for deletions):

**22-3.** An assistant pastor is called by the Session, by the
permission and approval of Presbytery, under the provisions of
_BCO_ 20-1 and 13-2, with Presbytery membership being
governed by the same provisions that apply to pastors. The
Session may call an Assistant Pastor only to serve for a definite
period not exceeding five years. Should the Session wish to
renew the assistant pastor’s call beyond five years, it shall call a
congregational meeting to seek the consent of the governed in
support of his continued ministry. In this case, congregational
consent shall be indicated by a three-fourths (3/4) majority vote.
The renewal of a call as an Assistant Pastor is subject to
Presbytery approval. If no three-fourths majority is secured, the
Session shall conclude that the assistant pastor does not have
adequate support from the congregation for a continued ministry
among them, and shall move immediately to dissolve his call, in
accordance with the provisions of _BCO_ 23. He The assistant
pastor is not a member of the Session, but may be appointed on
special occasions to moderate the Session under the provisions
of _BCO_ 12-4.

<a id="ga52-p1187"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1189 printed_page=1187 -->

**22-4.** The relationship of the associate pastor to the church
is determined by the congregation. The relationship of the
Assistant Pastor to the church is determined by the Session
(though not indefinitely, see _BCO_ 22-3). The dissolution of the
relationship of both is governed by the provision of _BCO_ 23.

So that the amended paragraphs would read:

**22-3.** An assistant pastor is called by the Session, by the
permission and approval of Presbytery, under the provisions of
_BCO_ 20-1 and 13-2, with Presbytery membership being
governed by the same provisions that apply to pastors. The
Session may call an Assistant Pastor only to serve for a definite
period not exceeding five years. Should the Session wish to
renew the assistant pastor’s call beyond five years, it shall call a
congregational meeting to seek the consent of the governed in
support of his continued ministry. In this case, congregational
consent shall be indicated by a three-fourths (3/4) majority vote.
The renewal of a call as an Assistant Pastor is subject to
Presbytery approval. If no three-fourths majority is secured, the
Session shall conclude that the assistant pastor does not have
adequate support from the congregation for a continued ministry
among them, and shall move immediately to dissolve his call, in
accordance with the provisions of _BCO_ 23. The assistant pastor
is not a member of the Session but may be appointed on special
occasions to moderate the Session under the provisions of _BCO_
12-4.

**22-4.** The relationship of the associate pastor to the church
is determined by the congregation. The relationship of the
Assistant Pastor to the church is determined by the Session
(though not indefinitely, see _BCO_ 22-3). The dissolution of the
relationship of both is governed by the provision of _BCO_ 23.

_Adopted by the Central Florida Presbytery at its stated meeting, January 28,_
_2025._

_Attested by Donald L. Mountan, Stated Clerk_

<a id="ga52-p1188"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1190 printed_page=1188 -->

##### OVERTURE 11 from the South Texas Presbytery (to OC, AC, CC,

CDM, CTS, Geneva, MNA, MTW, PCAF, RH, RUF)
“To Study Incorporating Bitcoin into Session, Presbytery, and
Committee & Agency Balance Sheets”

**Whereas** the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) seeks to exercise faithful

stewardship over the resources entrusted to her care, applying biblical
principles of prudence, integrity, and accountability, so that the
ministries of the Church may be sustained and the work of the Gospel
advanced ( _WLC_ 141; cf. _BCO_ 8-3; 8-4); and

**Whereas** Sessions, Presbyteries, and the various Committees and Agencies of

the General Assembly bear responsibility for prudent financial
oversight, seeking to preserve and wisely employ the financial assets
under their stewardship as an expression of obedience to the eighth
commandment’s duties of diligence and faithful increase ( _WLC_ 141–
142); and

**Whereas** changing economic conditions, including inflationary pressures,

currency fluctuations, and market volatility, encourage thoughtful
consideration of diversified asset allocations that may help maintain
or enhance the Church’s long-term financial stability; and

**Whereas** Bitcoin—an innovative, decentralized digital asset—has emerged as

a globally recognized store of value and medium of exchange,
operating independently from traditional banking systems, thereby
offering opportunities to diversify holdings beyond reliance on any
single national currency; and

**Whereas** Bitcoin’s underlying blockchain technology, characterized by

transparency and verifiability, may offer benefits such as reduced
transaction costs, enhanced financial accountability, and potentially
increased donor confidence in the stewardship of Church resources;
and

**Whereas** an increasing number and variety of organizations and individuals

have adopted Bitcoin as part of their financial strategies, citing its
resilience in economic downturns and its capacity to hedge against
certain systemic risks; and

<a id="ga52-p1189"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1191 printed_page=1189 -->

**Whereas** the pursuit of wise stewardship requires careful study and

discernment, so that decisions regarding financial assets—whether
conventional or innovative—are informed by Scripture, the Reformed
confessional standards, and a thorough understanding of potential
risks, benefits, and long-term implications;

**Therefore be it resolved** that the 52nd General Assembly of the Presbyterian

Church in America encourage Sessions, Presbyteries, and the
Committees and Agencies under its authority to study the wisdom,
biblical stewardship implications, financial prudence, and potential
benefits and risks of incorporating Bitcoin into their balance sheets;
and

**Be it further resolved** that these ecclesiastical bodies be encouraged, as they

deem appropriate and in accordance with their capacities, to share
their findings, insights, and recommendations with one another and at
future Assemblies, so that the whole Church might collectively gain
understanding, exercise sound judgment, and glorify God through the
faithful and prudent management of all resources entrusted to her care.

_Adopted by the South Texas Presbytery at its stated meeting, January 25,_
_2025._

_Attested by RE Barry McBee, Stated Clerk_

##### OVERTURE 12 from the Providence Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 58-5 to Specify Only Officers May Distribute the
Lord’s Supper”

**Whereas** our _Confession of Faith_ (29.3), _Catechisms_ ( _Larger Catechism_ 169,

173, and 176) and the declarations of our own denomination* call for
the Supper to be administered by ministers,

**And whereas** the keys of the Kingdom and the sacraments are to be in the

hands of the ordained offices of the church (Matthew 16:19),

<a id="ga52-p1190"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1192 printed_page=1190 -->

**And whereas** the role of the Session in discipline impinges upon the serving

of the Supper ( _BCO_ 30-3; cf. _WCF_ 30:1-4)

**And whereas** not since the beginning of Presbyterianism has the

administering of the sacraments been in the hands of the non-ordained
membership (see also the Scots Confession, Chapter 22**),

**And whereas** in the Presbyterian church the passing of the elements by the

laity has historically been done while seated, while those standing in
front of the congregation represent the administering and serving role,
and having non-ordained people standing with the minister and
serving the Supper confuses the role of ordained office,

**And whereas** there are no positive Scriptural mandates to allow non-ordained

members to administer and serve communion that would encourage
us to change our practice,

**And whereas** the Directory of Worship (58-5) only implies (or assumes) the

role of the officers in the serving of the Supper, not foreseeing the
cultural trends in our contemporary society,

**Be it resolved** that we overture the 52 [nd] General Assembly to amend the

Directory of Worship in _BCO_ 58-5, third paragraph as follows
(underlining for additions):

Here the bread is to be distributed by the minister or to other
officers for distribution. After having given the bread, he shall
take the cup, and say:

**In the same manner, He also took the cup, and**
**having given thanks as has been done in His name, He gave**
**it to the disciples, saying, “This cup is the new covenant in**
**My blood, which is shed for many for the remission of**
**sins. Drink from it, all of you.”**

While the minister is repeating these words, let him give the
cup directly to the people or to the officers for distribution.

<a id="ga52-p1191"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1193 printed_page=1191 -->

_Adopted by the Providence Presbytery at its stated meeting, February 4,_
_2025._

_Attested by TE Adam Tisdale, Stated Clerk_

Sources Referenced

_**WCF**_ **29.3** The Lord Jesus hath, in this ordinance, appointed his ministers to
declare his word of institution to the people; to pray, and bless the elements of
bread and wine, and thereby to set them apart from a common to an holy use;
and to take and break the bread, to take the cup, and (they communicating also
themselves) to give both to the communicants; but to none who are not then
present in the congregation.

_**WLC**_ **169** . How hath Christ appointed bread and wine to be given and received
in the sacrament of the Lord’s Supper? A. Christ hath appointed the ministers
of his Word, in the administration of this sacrament of the Lord’s Supper, to
set apart the bread and wine from common use, by the word of institution,
thanksgiving, and prayer; to take and break the bread, and to give both the
bread and the wine to the communicants: who are, by the same appointment,
to take and eat the bread, and to drink the wine, in thankful remembrance that
the body of Christ was broken and given, and his blood shed, for them.

_**WLC**_ **173** . May any who profess the faith, and desire to come to the Lord’s
Supper, be kept from it? A. Such as are found to be ignorant or scandalous,
notwithstanding their profession of the faith, and desire to come to the Lord’s
Supper, may and ought to be kept from that sacrament, by the power which
Christ hath left in his church, until they receive instruction, and manifest their
reformation.

_**WLC**_ **176** . Wherein do the sacraments of baptism and the Lord’s Supper agree?
A. The sacraments of baptism and the Lord’s Supper agree, in that the author
of both is God; the spiritual part of both is Christ and his benefits; both are
seals of the same covenant, are to be dispensed by ministers of the gospel, and
by none other; and to be continued in the church of Christ until his second
coming.

**Matthew 16:19** I will give you the keys of the kingdom of heaven,
and whatever you bind on earth shall be bound in heaven, and whatever you
loose on earth shall be loosed [[[a]]](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=matthew%252016:19&version=ESV#fen-ESV-23691a) in heaven.” (English Standard Version)

<a id="ga52-p1192"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1194 printed_page=1192 -->

_**BCO**_ **30-3.** Suspension from Sacraments is the temporary exclusion from those
ordinances, and is indefinite as to its duration. There is no definite suspension
from the Sacraments. Suspension from office is the exclusion of a church
officer from his office. This may be definite or indefinite as to its duration.
With respect to church officers, suspension from Sacraments shall always be
accompanied by suspension from office. But suspension from office is not
always necessarily accompanied with suspension from Sacraments. Definite
suspension from office is administered when the credit of religion, the honor
of Christ, and the good of the delinquent demand it, even though the delinquent
has given satisfaction to the court. Indefinite suspension is administered to the
impenitent offender until he exhibits signs of repentance, or until by his
conduct, the necessity of the greatest censure be made manifest. In the case of
indefinite suspension from office imposed due to scandalous conduct, the
procedure outlined in BCO 34- 8 shall be followed.

_**WCF**_ **30** .1. The Lord Jesus, as King and Head of his church, hath therein
appointed a government, in the hand of church officers, distinct from the civil
magistrate.
2. To these officers the keys of the kingdom of heaven are committed; by virtue
whereof, they have power, respectively, to retain, and remit sins; to shut that
kingdom against the impenitent, both by the Word, and censures; and to open
it unto penitent sinners, by the ministry of the gospel; and by absolution from
censures, as occasion shall require.
3. Church censures are necessary, for the reclaiming and gaining of offending
brethren, for deterring of others from the like offenses, for purging out of that
leaven which might infect the whole lump, for vindicating the honor of Christ,
and the holy profession of the gospel, and for preventing the wrath of God,
which might justly fall upon the church, if they should suffer his covenant, and
the seals thereof, to be profaned by notorious and obstinate offenders.
4. For the better attaining of these ends, the officers of the church are to
proceed by admonition; suspension from the sacrament of the Lord’s Supper
for a season; and by excommunication from the church; according to the nature
of the crime, and demerit of the person.

***** Ruling Elders Administering Sacraments (pcahistory.org)
“Recommendation No. 8: That the General Assembly affirm that in keeping
with the Confessional Standards of the Church, only properly ordained

<a id="ga52-p1193"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1195 printed_page=1193 -->

Teaching Elders may administer the Sacraments. Adopted. (Minutes of the
Seventh General Assembly, 1979, pp. 83-84).”

** **Scots Confession, Chapter 22: Of the Right Administration of the**
**Sacraments**

That sacraments be rightly ministered, we judge two things requisite: the one,
that they be ministered by lawful ministers, whom we affirm to be only they
that are appointed to the preaching of the word, or into whose mouths God has
put some sermon of exhortation, they being men lawfully chosen thereto by
some kirk. The other, that they be ministered in such elements, and in such
sort, as God has appointed; else, we affirm that they cease to be right
sacraments of Christ Jesus.

And therefore it is that we flee the society of the Papistical kirk, in participation
of their sacraments: first, because their ministers are no ministers of Christ
Jesus; yea (which is more horrible) they suffer women, whom the Holy Ghost
will not suffer to teach in the congregation, to baptize. And, secondly, because
they have so adulterated both the one sacrament and the other with their own
inventions, that no part of Christ's action abides in the original purity: for oil,
salt, spittle, and suchlike in baptism, are but men's inventions. Adoration,
veneration, bearing through streets and towns, and keeping of bread in boxes
or buists [chests], are profanation of Christ's sacraments, and no use of the
same. For Christ Jesus said, Take, eat, etc. do ye this in remembrance of
me. By which words and charge he sanctified bread and wine, to be the
sacrament of his body and blood, to the end that the one should be eaten, and
that all should drink of the other; and not that they should be kept to be
worshipped, and honoured as God, as the blind Papists have done heretofore,
who also committed sacrilege, stealing from the people the one part of the
sacrament: to wit, the blessed cup.

Moreover, that the sacraments be rightly used, it is required that the end and
cause why the sacraments were instituted be understood and observed, as well
of the minister, as the receivers. For if the opinion be changed in the receiver,
the right use ceases: which is most evident by the rejection of the sacrifices; as
also if the teacher plainly teaches false doctrine, which were odious and
abominable before God (albeit they were his own ordinances), because that
wicked men use them to another end than God has ordained. The same affirm

we of the sacraments in the Papistical kirk, in which we affirm the whole action
of the Lord Jesus to be adulterated, as well in the external form, as in the end

<a id="ga52-p1194"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1196 printed_page=1194 -->

and opinion. What Christ Jesus did, and commanded to be done, is evident by
the evangelists, and by Saint Paul. What the priest does at his altar we need
not rehearse. The end and cause of Christ's institution, and why the selfsame
should be used, is expressed in these words: doth i sin remembrance of me.
As oft as ye shall eat of this bread and drink of this cup, ye shall show forth,
that is, extol, preach, magnify, and praise, the Lord's death till he come. But to
what end, and in what opinion, the priests say their Mass, let the words of the
same, their own doctors and writings witness: to wit, that they, as mediators
betwixt Christ and his kirk, do offer unto God the Father a sacrifice
propitiatory for the sins of the quick and the dead. Which doctrine, as
blasphemous to Christ Jesus, and making derogation to the sufficiency of his
only sacrifice, once offered for purgation of all those that shall be
sanctified, we utterly abhor, detest, and renounce.

_**BCO**_ **58-5.** The table, on which the elements are placed, being decently
covered, and furnished with bread and wine, and the communicants orderly
and gravely sitting around it (or in their seats before it), the elders in a
convenient place together, the minister should then set the elements apart by
prayer and thanksgiving.

The bread and wine being thus set apart by prayer and thanksgiving,
the minister is to take the bread, and break it, in the view of the people, saying:

**That the Lord Jesus Christ on the same night in which He was**
**betrayed took bread; and when He had given thanks, He broke it,**
**gave it to His disciples, as I, ministering in His name, give this**
**bread to you, and said, "Take, eat; this is My body which is for**
**you; doth i sin remembrance of Me."** (Some other biblical account
of the institution of this part of the Supper may be substituted here.)

Here the bread is to be distributed. After having given the bread, he
shall take the cup, and say:

**In the same manner, He also took the cup, and having given**
**thanks as has been done in His name, he gave it to the disciples,**
**saying, "This cup is the new covenant in My blood, which is shed**
**for many for the remission of sins. Drink from it, all of you."**

While the minister is repeating these words, let him give the cup.

<a id="ga52-p1195"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1197 printed_page=1195 -->

##### OVERTURE 13 from the Great Lakes Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 25-1 to Specify Only Members in Good Standing May
Vote”

**Whereas** _BCO_ 24-3 states that “All communing members in good and regular

standing, but no others, are entitled to vote.”

**Whereas** _BCO_ 25-1 states that all communing members are entitled to vote

and does not qualify that only members in good and regular standing
may vote, it is inconsistent with the _BCO_ .

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 25-1 be amended to specify which communing

members are entitled to vote (underlining for additions; strikethrough
for deletions):

**25-1.** The congregation consists of all the communing members
of a particular church, and they only members in good and
regular standing are entitled to vote.

So that the amended paragraph would read:

**25-1.** The congregation consists of all the communing members
of a particular church, and only members in good and regular
standing are entitled to vote.

_Adopted by the Great Lakes Presbytery at its stated meeting, January 11, 2025._
_Attested by Ryan Potter, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 14 from the Great Lakes Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 38-1 to Indicate Ways a Court May Interact with an
Offended Party”

**Whereas** _BCO_ 38-1 requires that “in any instances… pertinent to the offense

against him or her;” and

**Whereas** informing offended person(s), inviting offended person(s) to provide

the court comment, and encouraging offended person(s) to enlist the

<a id="ga52-p1196"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1198 printed_page=1196 -->

help of an advisor in preparing such comments are all the
responsibility of the court; and

**Whereas** compliance with these responsibilities ordinarily requires multiple

meetings of the court; and

**Whereas** in some circumstances it may be preferable for a designated

individual to interact with an offended person rather than the court
itself;

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 38-1 be amended by inserting the following

language (underlining for additions; strikethrough for deletions):

In any instances involving a personal offense ( _BCO_ 29-3), the
court Moderator of the court or his designee shall attempt to
inform the offended person(s) of that part of the Confession the
court deems pertinent to the offense against him or her. The court
Moderator of the court or his designee shall invite the offended
person to provide the court comment on the Confession prior to
final approval of the Confession by the confessor and the court.
The court Moderator of the court or his designee shall encourage
the offended person to enlist the help of an advisor in preparing
any such comments. In all such instances, the court shall report
the way such offended persons were informed of the parts of the
Confession pertinent to them.

So that the amended paragraph would read:

In any instances involving a personal offense ( _BCO_ 29-3), the
Moderator of the court or his designee shall attempt to inform the
offended person(s) of that part of the Confession the court deems
pertinent to the offense against him or her. The Moderator of the
court or his designee shall invite the offended person to provide
the court comment on the Confession prior to final approval of
the Confession by the confessor and the court. The Moderator of
the court or his designee shall encourage the offended person to
enlist the help of an advisor in preparing any such comments. In
all such instances, the court shall report the way such offended

<a id="ga52-p1197"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1199 printed_page=1197 -->

persons were informed of the parts of the Confession pertinent to
them.

_Adopted by the Great Lakes Presbytery at its stated meeting, January 11,_
_2025._

_Attested by Ryan Potter, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 15 from Mississippi Valley Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 36-4 to Add Language for the Administration of
Definite Suspension from Office”

**Whereas** the censure of definite suspension from office is “administered to an

accused who, upon conviction, satisfies the court as to his repentance
and makes such restitution as is appropriate” ( _BCO_ 30-1), and

**Whereas** the censure of definite suspension from office is “administered when

the credit of religion, the honor of Christ, and the good of the
delinquent demand it, even though the delinquent has given
satisfaction to the court” ( _BCO_ 30-3), and

**Whereas** _BCO_ 36-4 gives no specific language by which the censure of

definite suspension from office is to be administered and the offending
brother is to be addressed, necessitating Courts to determine for
themselves how to communicate the censure, and

**Whereas** _BCO_ 37-1 currently contains language to declare to a brother when

he is “restored by the court at the end of the term of his suspension,”

**Therefore, be it resolved** that _BCO_ 36-4 be amended by adding the following

language (underlining for additions):

**36-4.** Definite suspension from office should be administered
in the presence of the court alone or in open session of the court, as it
may deem best, and public announcement thereof shall be at the
court’s discretion. When the court has resolved to pass this sentence,
the moderator shall address the offending brother to the following

purpose:

<a id="ga52-p1198"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1200 printed_page=1198 -->

**Whereas, you, ___________________ (here describe**
**the brother as a teaching elder, ruling elder, or deacon) are**
**convicted by sufficient proof (or are guilty by your own**
**confession) of the sin of ______________ (here insert the**
**offense), and have satisfied the court as to your repentance**
**by the grace of God; yet for the credit of religion, the honor**
**of** **Christ,** **and** **your** **own** **good** **we** **the**
**_______________________ Presbytery (or Church Session)**
**in the name and by the authority of the Lord Jesus Christ,**
**do now declare you suspended from the exercise of your**
**office,** **and** **all** **the** **functions** **thereof,** **for**
**__________________ (here state the definite period of time**
**of the suspension).**

To this shall be added such advice or admonition as may be
judged necessary, and the whole shall be concluded with prayer to
almighty God that He would follow this act of discipline with His
blessing.

So that the amended section will read as follows:

**36-4.** Definite suspension from office should be administered
in the presence of the court alone or in open session of the court, as it
may deem best, and public announcement thereof shall be at the
court’s discretion. When the court has resolved to pass this sentence,
the moderator shall address the offending brother to the following

purpose:

**Whereas, you, ___________________ (here describe**
**the brother as a teaching elder, ruling elder, or deacon) are**
**convicted by sufficient proof (or are guilty by your own**
**confession) of the sin of ______________ (here insert the**
**offense), and have satisfied the court as to your repentance**
**by the grace of God; yet for the credit of religion, the honor**
**of** **Christ,** **and** **your** **own** **good** **we** **the**
**_______________________ Presbytery (or Church Session)**
**in the name and by the authority of the Lord Jesus Christ,**
**do now declare you suspended from the exercise of your**
**office,** **and** **all** **the** **functions** **thereof,** **for**

<a id="ga52-p1199"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1201 printed_page=1199 -->

**__________________ (here state the definite period of time**
**of the suspension).**

To this shall be added such advice or admonition as may be
judged necessary, and the whole shall be concluded with prayer to
almighty God that He would follow this act of discipline with His
blessing.

_Adopted by the Mississippi Valley Presbytery at its stated meeting, February_
_4, 2025._
_Attested by TE Chris Wright, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 16 from Mississippi Valley Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 36-5 to Conform the Language to _BCO_ 37-3 re
Suspension from Office”

**Whereas** the censure of indefinite suspension is “administered to an accused

who, upon conviction, remains impenitent” ( _BCO_ 30-1), and is
administered “until he exhibits signs of repentance, or until by his
conduct, the necessity of the greatest censure be made manifest” ( _BCO_
30-3), and

**Whereas** with respect to church officers, “suspension from Sacraments shall

always be accompanied by suspension from office. But suspension
from office is not always necessarily accompanied with suspension
from Sacraments” ( _BCO_ 30-3), and

**Whereas** the language stipulated in _BCO_ 36-5 to be used for the administration

of the censure of indefinite suspension does not address the case when
an officer is indefinitely suspended from his office but not from the
sacraments as clearly a sit could, and

**Whereas** the language stipulated in _BCO_ 37-3 to be used for the restoration of

one who has been indefinitely suspended more clearly addresses the
case when an officer is indefinitely suspended from his office but not
from the Sacraments, and

<a id="ga52-p1200"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1202 printed_page=1200 -->

**Whereas** there is wisdom in bringing the language of _BCO_ 36-5 into

conformity with the language of _BCO_ 37-3,

**Therefore, be it resolved** that _BCO_ 36-5 be amended by adding “/or” to the

word “and” in “(and from the exercise of your office)” (underlining
for additions):

**Whereas, you, ___________________ (here describe the**
**person as a teaching elder, ruling elder, deacon, or private**
**member of the church) are convicted by sufficient proof (or are**
**guilty** **by** **your** **own** **confession)** **of** **the** **sin** **of**
**___________________ (here insert the offense), we the**
**___________________ Presbytery (or Church Session) in the**
**name and by the authority of the Lord Jesus Christ, do now**
**declare you suspended from the Sacraments of the Church**
**(and/or from the exercise of your office), until you give**
**satisfactory evidence of repentance.**

So that the amended section will read as follows:

**Whereas, you, ___________________ (here describe the**
**person as a teaching elder, ruling elder, deacon, or private**
**member of the church) are convicted by sufficient proof (or are**
**guilty** **by** **your** **own** **confession)** **of** **the** **sin** **of**
**___________________ (here insert the offense), we the**
**___________________ Presbytery (or Church Session) in the**
**name and by the authority of the Lord Jesus Christ, do now**
**declare you suspended from the Sacraments of the Church**
**(and/or from the exercise of your office), until you give**
**satisfactory evidence of repentance.**

_Adopted by the Mississippi Valley Presbytery at its stated meeting, February_
_4, 2025._
_Attested by TE Chris Wright, stated clerk_

<a id="ga52-p1201"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1203 printed_page=1201 -->

##### OVERTURE 17 from Mississippi Valley Presbytery (to MNA)

“Change the Boundaries of Covenant and Mississippi Valley
Presbyteries”

**Whereas** the geographic center of Covenant Presbytery has shifted

significantly north and west in recent years as evidenced by the
frequency of stated meetings in the Memphis area and parts of
Arkansas; and

**Whereas** Lowndes, the county in Mississippi where Main Street Presbyterian

Church (the only PCA church in the county) is located, is on the
southernmost border of Covenant Presbytery and borders The
Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley on its southern border; and

**Whereas** the Session of Main Street Presbyterian Church wants to have more

regular participation in the stated meetings of Presbytery; and

**Whereas** the Session of Main Street Presbyterian Church finds this

participation difficult due to the locations where many of the stated
meetings of Covenant Presbytery are held; and

**Whereas** the locations of the stated meetings of The Presbytery of the

Mississippi Valley are usually closer to Columbus, MS, and often
significantly so, than the locations of the stated meetings of Covenant
Presbytery; and

**Whereas** The Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley has in the past acted in

similar situations to allow the transfer of churches in Winona, MS;
Ackerman, MS; Delhi, LA and Rustin, LA, into the Presbytery

**Therefore, be it resolved** that the Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley

overtures the 2025 General Assembly to transfer Lowndes County in
Mississippi from the geographic bounds of Covenant Presbytery to
the geographic bounds of The Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley.

_Adopted by the Mississippi Valley Presbytery at its stated meeting, February_
_4, 2025._
_Attested by TE Chris Wright, stated clerk_

<a id="ga52-p1202"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1204 printed_page=1202 -->

##### OVERTURE 18 from the Gulf Coast Presbytery (to RUF)

“Direct RUF to Update “Campus Ministry Manual” for GA Approval”

**Be it resolved** Gulf Coast Presbytery overtures the 52 [nd] General Assembly

(2025) to direct the Permanent Committee of RUF, with the RUF
Coordinator, to revise the 1979 “Campus Ministry Manual” in order
to reflect the 2024 approved Affiliation Agreement and previously
issued Assembly directives, and to present the same to the 54 [th] General
Assembly (2027) through its Committee of Commissioners. This
updated manual shall also include, but not be limited to, policies
regarding: recruiting, assessing, hiring or promoting prospective TEs
to the positions of Campus Minister and area Coordinator, starting
new works and closing works (including for international and crosscultural works), yearly RUF training, Summer Conference, etc.

**Rationale:**
Reformed University Fellowship, since its inception, has expanded to over 195
works worldwide. Through much hard work and collaboration, RUF has
recently passed through the General Assembly its most recent Affiliation
Agreement. However, the last policy manual RUF presented for approval by
the PCA was in 1979 (nearly half a century ago). _BCO_ 14-1.12 recognizes
RUF is a permanent committee of the General Assembly. And according to
_BCO_ 14-1 the General Assembly is the court for RUF. But its current structure
and practices do not reflect the 1979 “Campus Ministry Manual.” Since the
year 2000 (when RUF became a Permanent Committee of the PCA), no
updates to its manual have been presented, though required by the General
Assembly. Therefore, a policy manual that accurately reflects RUF’s current
practices is needed.

_Adopted by the Gulf Coast Presbytery at its stated meeting, February 10,_
_2025._

_Attested by Joe Grider, stated clerk_

<a id="ga52-p1203"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1205 printed_page=1203 -->

##### OVERTURE 19 from the Eastern Carolina Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 57-5 to Revise the Membership Vows”

**Whereas** our current five membership vows have been unchanged for many

years without review;

**Whereas** our current five membership vows have some antiquated language

that must be explained;

**Whereas** our current five membership vows are inherited from other

Reformed denominations, and we are an “ _ecclesia reformata, semper_
_reformanda_ ” denomination;

**Whereas** our current five membership vows mention nothing of the love of

God for sinners;

**Whereas** our current five membership vows give no indication of what human

beings are saved unto, just what we are saved from;

**Therefore, be it resolved** that the General Assembly amend _BCO_ 57-5 to

revise our membership vows to include 2 addenda and one language
update (underlining for additions; strikethrough for deletions):

Add an entire new first vow for membership and renumber the existing

vows.

**1.** **Do you agree that you are made in the image of God,**

**designed by God to glorify God and enjoy Him forever?**

Insert the words “both beloved and” into the former vow #1.

**12. Do you acknowledge yourselves to be both beloved and**

**sinners in the sight of God, justly deserving His**
**displeasure, and without hope save in His sovereign**
**mercy?**

Change the word “study” to “promote” in the final vow.

**56. Do you submit yourselves to the government and**

**discipline of the Church, and promise to study promote**
**its purity and peace?**

<a id="ga52-p1204"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1206 printed_page=1204 -->

So that the amended vows would read as follows:

**1.** **Do you agree that you are made in the image of God,**

**designed by God to glorify God and enjoy Him forever?**
**2.** **Do you acknowledge yourselves to be both beloved and**
**sinners in the sight of God, justly deserving His**
**displeasure, and without hope save in His sovereign**
**mercy?**
**3.** **Do you believe in the Lord Jesus Christ as the Son of**
**God, and Savior of sinners, and do you receive and rest**
**upon Him alone for salvation as He is offered in the**
**Gospel?**
**4.** **Do you now resolve and promise, in humble reliance**
**upon the grace of the Holy Spirit, that you will endeavor**
**to live as becomes the followers of Christ?**
**5.** **Do you promise to support the Church in its worship**
**and work to the best of your ability?**
**6.** **Do you submit yourselves to the government and**
**discipline of the Church, and promise to promote its**
**purity and peace?**

_Adopted by the Eastern Carolina Presbytery at its stated meeting, January_
_25, 2025_
_Attested by TE Wes Martin, stated clerk_

“Change the Boundaries of Covenant and Mississippi Valley

significantly north and west in recent years as evidenced by the
frequency of stated meetings in the Memphis area and parts of
Arkansas; and

**Whereas** Lowndes, the county in Mississippi where Main Street Presbyterian

Church (the only PCA church in the county) is located, is on the

<a id="ga52-p1205"></a>

southernmost border of Covenant Presbytery and borders The
Presbytery of the Mississippi Valley on its southern border; and

regular participation in the stated meetings of Presbytery; and

##### OVERTURE 21 from the Covenant Presbytery (to MNA)

“Change the Boundaries of Covenant and Hills & Plains Presbyteries”

**Whereas** Sebastian County, Arkansas, where Covenant Presbyterian Church

of Fort Smith is located is on farthest western border of Covenant

##### OVERTURE 22 from the Pacific Northwest Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 20-3, 24-3 and 25-1 to clarify "Regular Standing" re
minimum voting age”

**A.** **Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 20-3, 24-3, and _BCO_ 25-1 be amended as
shown below.

Proposed additions are indicated by underlining.

20-3. when a congregation is convened forth e election o fa pastor ...
All communing members in good and regular standing, but no others,
are entitled to vote in the churches to which they are respectively
attached. (cf. _BCO_ 25-1)

24-3. All communing members in good and regular standing, but no
others, are entitled to vote in the election of church officers in the
churches to which they respectively belong. A majority vote of those
present is required for election. (cf. _BCO_ 25-1)

25-1. The congregation consists of all the communing members of a
particular church, and they only are entitled to vote. A congregation

<a id="ga52-p1207"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1209 printed_page=1207 -->

may establish a rule setting a minimum voting age (regular standing),
but it must be adopted by a two-thirds (2/3) majority at a
congregational meeting that is called with at least 30 days' notice. (cf.
_BCO_ 20-3, 24-3)

**B.** **Be it also resolved** that if GA adopts this, or an amended version, the
Stated Clerk will make this full Rationale available when the

Amendment Ballot is sent to Presbyteries.

**Rationale** - The need for this Overture arises from an SJC Decision in March
2024, where, by a vote of 15-5, the SJC ruled that the _BCO_ does not permit a
congregation to set a minimum voting age. [7] This Overture seeks to permit
such. It argues that such has historically been common in American
Presbyterianism. It's part of what was, and is, meant by "regular" standing. The
_Psiaki_ Decision did not define what the word "regular" means in the phrase
"good and regular standing" and this Overture seeks to treat it as it has
historically been understood. When the PCA was formed in 1973 it adopted
the PCUS _BCO_ of 1925. Minor communicants did not vote in 1925, as they
were not regarded as being in "regular" standing. Our _BCO_ 25-1 and 24-3
remain unchanged from 1925. The sentence above in _BCO_ 20-3 remains
unchanged from the PCUS _BCO_ of 1879. [ 8]

This Overture’s rationale provides many and varied arguments for why the
PCA should allow such freedom, which is generally described below in
Robert's Rules.

Some organized societies define additional classes of "membership" that do
not entail all of these rights. Whenever the term member is used in this book

[RONR], it refers to full participating membership in the assembly, unless
otherwise specified. Such members are also described as "voting members"
when it is necessary to make a distinction. (RONR (12th ed.) 1:4)

7 Case 2023-11: _Complaint of Psiaki v. Pacific Northwest_ ( _M51GA_, p. 877). This included the
SJC Decision, a Concurring Opinion, three Dissenting Opinions, and an Objection (34 pages
total).
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1o6nAccxzIkLBHVNlL_v7d_A7YDPyURK/view?usp=sharing
8 _**BCO**_ **20-3 = PCUS 1879, VI, §3-4.** _**BCO**_ **24-3 = PCUS 1925, XXVI, §145.**
_BCO_ 25-1 = **PCUS 1925, XXVII, §152. The 1879 PCUS Book used "all communicants"**
**instead of "all communing members" for the provision in** _**BCO**_ **20-3.**
**https://www.pcahistory.org/bco/index.html**

<a id="ga52-p1208"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1210 printed_page=1208 -->

If this Amendment is enacted, _no church_ will be required to change _any_ current
practice. It simply adds an option for churches that believe a minimum voting
age is prudent. Further, while enactment might increase the average age of
minors who vote, it should not have any effect on the average age when
covenant children are admitted to the Lord's Supper (unless a Session is
unreasonably delaying communion until a child has the maturity to vote.)

In the pages that follow are many reasons why this Overture should be adopted.

1. Scripture and the “DEA” 11. PCA Polity Differences
2. Good and "Regular" Standing 12. De Facto Minimum Age
3. PCUS Allowance for Voting Age 13. Church Corporations
4. 1982 Reception of RPCES 14. _BCO_ Preliminary Principle 6
5. 2024 _Psiaki v. Pacific NW_ 15. Other Voting Restrictions?
6. No Conflict with BCO 6-4 16. No Change to Pby/GA
Representation
7. Common Grace & Societal Rules 17. No Change to Judicial Rights
8. Status Quo 18. Related Considerations
9. 1996-1997 Strong Support 19. Further Reading
10. 2000 Pittsburgh Overture

As one considers these many arguments, it's important to understand that
disagreement with some does not invalidate the persuasive power of the others.

**1. Scripture and the "Discernment Equivalency Assertion"**

Some maintain that if a child has the discernment necessary to be admitted to
the Lord's Supper, he thereby has the discernment necessary to vote on _any_
church matter (i.e., what we'll call the Discernment Equivalency Assertion or
DEA). However, this view lacks advocacy among the churchmen most
responsible for developing Presbyterian polity. [9] These consistently find

9 Alexander T. McGill, _Church Government: A Treatise_ (Philadelphia, PA: Presbyterian Board

of Publication, 1888), 127-136. Cf., Samuel Rutherford, _Due Right of Presbyteries_ (London:
Printed by E. Griffin for Richard Whittaker and Andrew Crook), 38-39, 201, 204. George
Gillespie, George Gillespie, _Miscellaneous Questions_ (Edinburgh: Gedeon Lithgow, 1649),
24. Thomas Smyth, _Manual for the Use of the Members of the Second Presbyterian Church,_
_Charleston_ (Jenkins & Hussey, 1838), 104-107, 121. William Cunningham, _Discussions on_

<a id="ga52-p1209"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1211 printed_page=1209 -->

rational and Scriptural grounds for limiting church suffrage to communicants
with adult maturity, self-sovereignty, and ability to commit and contribute to
the local church.

a. _Adult Understanding_ —Jesus tells us that minors can, at some point prior

to adulthood, make a genuine profession of faith in Him (Matt. 18:6; Mk.
9:42). And yet in the same breath He warns His disciples that believing
minors are especially capable of being led astray, as they retain a particular
degree of naivety. Although they might be sufficiently discerning to
partake of sacramental meals (Ex. 12:26-32; Lk. 2:41-47), their relative
lack of wisdom requires that they remain under the unique tutelage and
discipline of parents (Gen. 18:19; Deut. 6:7; Prov. 22:6; Eph. 6:4). Thus,
_BCO_ 28-1 affirms that even after becoming communicants, “the spiritual
nurture, instruction and training of the children of the Church are
committed by God primarily to their parents.”

Prov. 22:15 Folly is bound up in the heart of a child, but the rod
of discipline drives it far from him. (ESV here and
below.)
Prov. 29:15 The rod and reproof give wisdom, but a child left to
himself brings shame to his mother.
1 Cor. 13:11 when i was a child, i spoke l ikea child, I thought like
a child, i reasoned l ikea child. when i became a man,
I gave up childish ways.
1 Cor. 14:20 Brothers, do not be children in your thinking. Be
infants in evil, but in your thinking be mature.

To discern that a prospective church officer possesses careful discretion;
courage to take difficult stands; excellent household management skills;
empathy to comfort the downcast; etc. may, in the wise judgment of a
church, require greater understanding than belongs to minors. Most would
probably also agree there is a difference between the ability of children to
understand the Gospel ( _BCO_ 57-2) and their ability to understand things

_Church Principles: Popish, Erastian, Presbyterian_ (Edinburgh: T & T Clark, 1863), 290-291.
Stuart Robinson & Thomas E. Peck, “What are the Constituent Elements of the Church of
God, as Organized and Visible?” in _The Presbyterial Critic and Monthly Review_, Dec 1855,
566-573. Charles Hodge, “Who May Vote in the Election of Pastor,” in _The Church and Its_
_Polity_ (New York: Thomas Nelson and Sons, 1879), 244-246.

<a id="ga52-p1210"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1212 printed_page=1210 -->

like church debt, elder qualification, dissolution of TE/RE calls, and
denominational affiliation.

Some have, nevertheless, argued that if minors are qualified to make the
most consequential life-decision of receiving Christ as Savior, they are
necessarily qualified to make decisions of lesser consequence, such as
choosing church officers. [10] This line of reasoning entails a _non sequitur_ .
The _positive consequences_ of accepting Jesus Christ are surely greater than
that involved in choosing a particular man for office. The former procures
eternal life, the latter a particular officer fora limited season. However,
the potentially _negative ramifications_ of selecting an officer are always
greater than those involved in accepting Christ as Lord. Whereas Christ
cannot sin against His church (2 Cor. 5:21; Heb. 4:15), officers can.

Whereas Christ cannot prevaricate, would-be officers can. Hence, it
requires a greater level of discernment to decipher the character of the
latter than the former (Matt. 7:15ff.; 24:24; 2 Cor. 11:3-4, 13-15; 1 Thess.
5:19-21; 1 John 4:1ff.; 2 Pet. 2:1ff.; Rev. 13:11). Analogously, even
though admission to the Lord’s Supper and right to marry must both follow
reaching “years of discretion” ( _BCO_ 28-3; 54-1), none suppose that the
age at which one may be communed is identical with that at which he may
enter into a marriage covenant. The _BCO_ recognizes that the “age and
intelligence” necessary to testify in a church court may be greater than that
necessary to participate in the Lord’s Supper:

35-1. All persons of _proper age_ and intelligence are
competent witnesses, except such as do not believe in the
existence of God, or a future state of rewards and
punishments. Either party has the right to challenge a witness
whom he believes to be incompetent, and the court shall
examine and decide upon his competency.

b. _Adult Self-Sovereignty_ —The demands of the Fifth Commandment (Ex.

20:12; Deut. 5:16; Lev. 19:3; Matt. 15:4; 19:19; Eph. 6:2-4) supply strong

10 Morton Smith, for example, understood PCA _BCO_ 24-3 to confer suffrage to minor
communicants, and he suggests as its likely rationale something along these lines. Smith,
_Commentary on the PCA Book of Church Order_ (Taylors, SC: Presbyterian Press, 2007), 257.
However, the original (1879) authors of the language retained in _BCO_ 24-3 do not correlate
the understanding necessary to be communed with that necessary to vote in congregational
meetings.

<a id="ga52-p1211"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1213 printed_page=1211 -->

grounds for doubting the propriety of minors voting in church meetings.
The specific nature and degree to which minors are required to submit to
parents renders it questionable that they may assert their voting rights
against parental prohibitions or exercise them in contradiction to how
parents instruct them vote. It is reasonable, therefore, for congregations to
limit voting to adults, with a view to neither (a) encouraging minors to
influence their church independently of parental guidance; nor (b)
effectively granting parents the ability to wield multiple votes, by
requiring their minors to second their perspective in all matters. Of course,
minors may continue to exert an influence on congregational decisions in
consultation with parents by whom they are well-represented.

Notably, a communing minor's lack of independence has also been
recognized in judicial cases. In Case 2002-5: _Plowman v. Philadelphia_, a
Session declared a mother automatically contumacious for "refusing to
force" her 14 and 16-year-old communing daughters to testify at trial
"even though they had been duly cited by the Session." The Session
suspended the mother from the sacraments, but did not censure the
communing minors for failing to obey the citation. ( _BCO_ 35-14) [11]

_BCO_ 35-3 was revised in 2023 to provide an option to treat minors
differently than adults when testifying, even if the minor is a communing
member and even if not a victim:

35-3. A court may, at the request of either party, or at its own
initiative, make reasonable accommodation to prevent inperson contact with the accused: ...
(c.) In all cases where such accommodation has
been made, videoconference testimony by witnesses under
the age of 18 shall be taken by written interrogatory to be read
to the witness by a person appointed by the court in
accordance with the applicable provisions of _BCO_ 35-11.

c. _Adult Commitment_ —In the Old Covenant, the only unambiguous parties

involved in formally selecting (or reciprocating God’s selection of)
ecclesiastical and civil leaders were adults (Num. 8:9-10, 14 with Lev. 9:1;
cf. Deut. 17:14-16 with 1 Sam. 8:4-9; 2 Sam. 5:3; 1 Chron. 12:38; etc.).

11 The Case was largely decided on procedural grounds, but the SJC was silent on whether a
Session needed parental permission to cite communicant minors to testify. ( _M32GA_, p. 62)

<a id="ga52-p1212"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1214 printed_page=1212 -->

There existed, therefore, a strong correlation between the body of electors
and those adults who especially bore the responsibility to support,
materially, both the ministry (Ex. 30:12-14; 38:26; Num. 18:8-32) and the
civil government (1 Sam. 8:11ff.). Since the same burden to support for
the ministry exists today (Luke 10:7; 1 Cor. 9:7-1; 8; Gal. 6:6; 1 Tim.
5:22), and no passage requires that minors be granted voting privileges, it
is reasonable for churches to follow the only Biblical pattern, confine
voting to those adults who may generally be expected to underwrite the
ministry. This policy does not deny that minors participate in the sacrificial
burden of supporting the church through the faithful contributions of their
parents. Instead, it achieves consistency, recognizing that the same minors
may also be represented in congregational voting through their parents.

Many TEs, REs, and the churches whom they represent are not persuaded
by the Discernment Equivalency Assertion. And preventing those
churches from setting a reasonable voting age will logically require them
to make one of the two undesirable choices below.

1. _Delayed Admission_ - These churches will delay admission to the

Lord's Supper for an eligible, non-communing minor until he
reaches a reasonable age to have the sufficient discernment,
maturity, and independence to vote on church matters. These
churches will tolerate delayed communion to ensure voting
maturity.

2. _Premature Voter_ - These churches won't delay admission to the

Supper for eligible covenant children but will ignore the legitimate
concern about the minor's discernment, maturity, and
independence for voting. These churches will tolerate voting
immaturity in order to admit eligible covenant children to the

sacrament.

Neither are prudent choices, and neither have biblical support. Some
churches may try to split the difference, but that's a compromise suffering
the negative consequences of both those unreasonable choices (albeit,
perhaps, for fewer years). If this Overture is enacted, it will have no effect
on the average communing age in the PCA. It's only likely to increase the

<a id="ga52-p1213"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1215 printed_page=1213 -->

average _voting_ age of "carefully examined" communing minors ( _BCO_ 572). 12

**2. The History of "Good and Regular Standing" –** _**BCO**_ **20-3 and 24-3**

Since their adoption in 1879 ( _BCO_ 6:3:4), the elector conditions “good and
regular standing” (PCA _BCO_ 20-3, 24-3) did not guarantee voting privileges
to all communicants, or disallow local congregations from observing
additional voting rules. A member in “good standing” has a right to the Lord’s
Super in both his home church and neighboring congregations. This voter
condition excludes parties under censure of suspension or strong presumption
of guilt (cf. PCA _BCO_ 33-4; 42-6). “Regular standing” is the status members
enjoy in their home congregation and is indicative of their right to exercise
congregation-specific privileges (e.g. solicitation o fa letter of transfer;
eligibility for office; pursuit of judicial process, etc.) _exercised in according to_
_the rules of that congregation_ . This voter condition excludes non-members,
members with an abnormal relationship to the church (e.g. non-resident,
dismissed, or associate members—cf., PCA _BCO_ 25-3; 38-3, 46-4), and those
who fall short of the voting conditions in local church bylaws and/or applicable
civil laws (cf., _BCO_ 25-11). This means that the voter conditions “good and
regular standing” imply that congregations may observe additional voting
rules. It does not mean that all communicants must be counted as voters.

This interpretation of “good and regular standing” is confirmed by the
following:

a. _Antecedent Voter Conditions_ —The original 1788 voting provision laid

down two conditions that, on the understanding of many, could allow some
_non-communicants_ to vote: “No person shall be entitled to vote, who
refuses to submit to the censures of the Church, regularly administered; or
who does not contribute his just proportion, according to his own
engagements, or the rules of that Church” (1788, _Form of Government_,
14:3) The first condition, requiring submission to censure, was the

12 One example of what our Standards regard as too-early communion is called
paedocommunion, which is the view that covenant children should be admitted to the Supper
on the same basis they're admitted to Baptism. But that view is entirely unrelated to this
Overture. For example, eight SJC members cited earlier in this Overture are favorable towards
allowing congregations the option of a voting age restriction, but none have written or spoken
publicly in favor of the paedocommunion view.

<a id="ga52-p1214"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1216 printed_page=1214 -->

antecedent of “good standing.” The second requiring monetary
contribution as required by local church “rules” was the antecedent of
“regular standing.” The marginal summary calls those who meet these
conditions, “regular members.” In 1865 the Old School GA of the PCUSA
would expressly deny that it is “consistent with regular standing” for
members to neglect “to support the church to which they belong.” [13]
Moreover, it was normative for congregations throughout 18 [th] and 19 [th]
centuries to observe a variety of additional rules, confining voting to
adults, heads of household, persons who had attended a church fora
minimum period of time, etc. [14] By confining voting to communicants, the

<a id="ga52-p1215"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1217 printed_page=1215 -->

1879 BCO was more specific than its predecessor. Yet, by adopting the
general conditions “good and regular standing,” the 1879 BCO secured
greater freedom for congregations to define eligible voters with or without
reference to monetary contribution.

b. _BCO Context_ —The indispensable corollary of electing a minister is

subscribing to a minister’s call, by pledging monetary support (PCA _BCO_
20-5, 20-6). Historically, voters were required to pledge payment of a
specific amount of a minister’s salary in the same congregational meeting
in which they elected him (and to renew their subscription annually). [15]
Therefore, the original readers of the 1879 _BCO_ would have concluded
that indeed all communicants may vote, but only if they are able to perform
the adult duty without which a pastoral election is incomplete.

c. _Other BCO Provisions_ —Historic and contemporary _BCO_ provisions

governing congregational meetings are equally compatible with minimum
voting age. For example, _BCO_ 25-1 (following 1933 PCUS _BCO_ 27:152)
makes communicant membership a _necessary_, but not a _sufficient_
condition for congregation voting: “The congregation consists of all the
communing members of a particular church, and they only are entitled to
vote.” This provision is equivalent to the statement: “The pride consists
of all the lions of a particular region, and they only birth lions.” _BCO_ 251 no more states that all communicants are entitled to vote, than the latter

Virginia, 40-42, 70. (21) _Manual of North Presbyterian Church Buffalo, NY_ (Buffalo, NY:

_Presbyterian Church_ (Philadelphia, PA: Crissy & Markley, 1853), 5-6. (23) _Bylaws of the_
_Presbyterian Church of the City of Charleston_ (Philadelphia, PA: Adam Waldie, 1838), 3,
18. (24) _Historical Manual of the Presbyterian Church of Glen’s Falls, NY_ (Glen's Falls, NY:
Messenger Printing, 1876), 50. (25) The Charter and Bylaws of the Presbyterian Church of
Harrisburg (Harrisburg, PA: np, 1860), 4-5. (26) _Manual of the Second Presbyterian Church,_
_Indianapolis_ (Indianapolis, IN: I.W. Holman & Co., 1872), 10, 16. (27) _A History of the First_
_Presbyterian Church of Ithica, NY_ (Ithaca, NY: Andrus & Church), 47. (28) _Manual of the_
_North Presbyterian Church in the City of New York_ (New York, NY: John A. Gray, 1858),
6-8, 12.
15 First Presbyterian (Charleston, SC) called its initial minister in 1724, thus: “forty-three
persons, probably heads of families…subscribed a call inviting the Rev. Nathan Bassett to be
their minister.” F.D. Jones and W.H. Mills, _History of the Presbyterian Church in South_
_Carolina_ (Columbia, SC: R. L. Bryan Company, 1926), 12, cf. 1026. At its 1898
congregational meeting, First Presbyterian (Charleston, WV) sought to extract funding for a
minister’s salary, and “Deacons were to call upon all members who did not ‘then and there’
subscribe” to carry out their duty. Joe B. Overmeyer, _History of the Presbytery of Kanawha,_
_1895-1956_ (Charleston, WV: Jarrett Printing, 1956), 274.

<a id="ga52-p1216"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1218 printed_page=1216 -->

implies that all lions are female. Therefore, _BCO_ 25-1 is distinctly
compatible with congregational voting conditions.

d. _Reception_ —The universal report about the 1879 voter conditions was that

they _confined_ voting to communicant members, never that they had
secured voting privileges for every communicant. [16] Commentator F.P.
Ramsay limits his discussion to which parties are definitively excluded
from voting by the _BCO_ conditions—non-members, non-communing
members, and parties under censure—he does not suggest that all other
communicants must be included. [17]

e. _Application_ —For the decades following the adoption of the 1879 _BCO_,

Southern Presbyterian churches retained historic voter requirements as
entirely consistent with _BCO_ voter provisions. In 1895, Second
Presbyterian Charleston continued to limit voting in pastoral elections to
adult men who had contributed to the church budget by renting a pew. [18]
In 1907, one author who wished to see both “young and old” participate
in Southern Presbyterian officer elections reports that the opposite practice
was common: “Unhappily, it is far too often the case that when an election
is ordered by the authorities of the church” such parties are found “leaving
the church building.” [19] The 1891 PCUS GA stated, “It is the settled
doctrine of our church that women…are prohibited from speaking by way
of exhortation, or leading in prayer, or discussing any question publicly in
the meetings of the church or congregation as a mixed assembly.” [20]
Commenting on this policy, it was reported at the 1956 GA that the
“traditional position of our church” was to “forbid women to speak or vote
in congregational meetings.” [21] Indeed, it was not until 1920 that the PCUS
GA would adopt the still very conservative policy: “any mission, if it so
desires, may by majority vote make its women missionaries members of

_What is Presbyterian Law as Defined by the Church Courts?_ (Philadelphia, PA: Presbyterian
Board of Publication, 1882), 56.
17 Ramsey, _An Exposition of the Form of Government_, 129.
18 Gilbert Robbins Brackett, _Manual for the Use of the Members of the Second Presbyterian_

19 Clement Read Vaughan, “The People in Church Elections” in _The Union Seminary_
_Magazine_ Vol. 18, No. 4 Apr-May, 1907, 284.
20 1891 _PCUS Minutes_, 260.
21 1956 _PCUS Minutes_, 141-142.

<a id="ga52-p1217"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1219 printed_page=1217 -->

the mission technically so called, and accord them the privilege of voting.”

22 Especially in their business transactions, PCUS churches understood it
to be their right to regulate suffrage as they saw fit. In 1926, suffrage in
the business meetings of Carrolton Presbyterian, New Orleans was
extended to those “who subscribe and contribute annually.” [23] In 1954,
Conservative Presbyterians were reticent to unite with the UPC and
PCUSA, because these Churches denied the “generally accepted policy in
our Presbyterian Church (U.S.) that each individual church owns and
manages its church property as the congregation shall so direct, without
any help or advice from any Church Court or agency.” [24] After all, each
edition of the PCUS _BCO_ (cf., 1942 _BCO_, 13:52) affirmed the session
representing a congregation has “the power to establish rules for the
government, discipline, and extension of the Church” (cf. PCA _BCO_ 112)

f. _Contrast_ —Progressive churches intent on encouraging universal
communicant suffrage recognized that association of voting privileges
with membership in “good and regular standing” was insufficient for that
end. The Biblical, rational, and historical precedents for limiting suffrage
to adult communicants were so widely recognized, that it was necessary
to expressly prohibit that limitation. Hence, next to non-communicant
contributors, the 1897 PCUSA GA would define eligible voters as “all
communicant members in good standing, _of whatever age_ ” (emphasis
added), and its official polity manuals throughout the twentieth century
would consistently adopt the same language. [25]

22 1920, _PCUS Minutes_, 73.
23 “Charter of Carrolton Presbyterian Church,” as amended December 26, 1926, Article III. In
1926, the “Corporation” of First Presbyterian, Columbia, SC was “comprised of all male
members 21 years old and above who contribute to the support of the church.” Jones &
Mills, _History of the Presbyterian Church in South Carolina_, 651.
24
Randolph B. Lee, “Analysis of the Proposed Plan of Union,” in _Southern Presbyterian Journal_,
Sep 15, 1954, 6.
