**make known to your Presbytery the change which has**
**taken place in your views since the assumption of this**
**ordination vow?**
**3.** **Do you approve of the form of government and**

**discipline of the Presbyterian Church in America, in**
**conformity with the general principles of Biblical**
**polity?**
**4.** **Do you promise subjection to your brethren in the**

**Lord?**

**5.** **Have you been induced, as far as you know your own**

**heart, to seek the office of the holy ministry from love**
**to God and a sincere desire to promote His glory in the**
**Gospel of His Son?**
**6.** **Do you promise to be zealous and faithful in**

**maintaining the truths of the Gospel and the purity**
**and peace and unity of the Church, whatever**
**persecution or opposition may arise unto you on that**
**account?**
**7.** **Do you engage to be faithful and diligent in the**

**exercise o fall your duties as a christian and a minister**
**of the Gospel, whether personal or relational, private**
**or public; and to endeavor by the grace of God to**
**adorn the profession of the Gospel in your manner of**
**life, and to walk with exemplary piety before the flock**
**of which God shall make you overseer?**
**8.** **Do you now undertake this Needful Work, and do you**

**promise, in reliance on God for strength, to be faithful**
**in the discharge of all the duties incumbent on you as**
**a minister of the Gospel of the Lord Jesus Christ?**

<a id="ga52-p1255"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1257 printed_page=1255 -->

The candidate having answered these questions in the
affirmative, the presiding minister shall propose to the calling
entity the following questions:

**1.** **Do you, the people of this calling entity, continue to**

**profess** **your** **readiness** **to** **receive**
_________________, **whom you have called to this**
**Needful Work?**
**2.** **Do you promise to receive the word of truth from his**

**mouth with meekness and love?**
**3.** **Do you promise to encourage him in his labors, and**

**to assist his endeavors for your instruction and**
**spiritual edification?**
**4.** **Do you engage to continue to him while he is in this**

**call that competent worldly maintenance which you**
**have promised, and to furnish him with whatever you**
**may see needful for the honor of religion and for his**
**comfort among you?**

The people of the calling entity having answered these
questions in the affirmative, by holding up their right hands, the
candidate shall kneel, and the presiding minister shall, with prayer
and the laying on of hands of the Presbytery, according to the
apostolic example, solemnly set him apart to the holy office of the
Gospel ministry. Prayer being ended, he shall rise from his knees;
and the minister who presides shall first, followed by all members
of the Presbytery, take him by the right hand, saying, in words to
this effect:

**We give you the right hand of fellowship, to take part in**
**this ministry with us.**

The presiding minister shall then say:

**I now pronounce and declare that __________________**
**has been regularly elected, and ordained, agreeable to the**
**Word of God, and according to the Constitution of the**
**Presbyterian Church in America; and that as such he is**
**entitled to all support, encouragement, honor, and**

<a id="ga52-p1256"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1258 printed_page=1256 -->

**obedience in the Lord: In the name of the Father, and of**
**the Son, and of the Holy Ghost. Amen.**

After which the minister presiding, or some other teaching or
ruling elder appointed for the purpose, shall give a solemn charge
to the minister to persevere in the discharge of his duties, and then
after prayer and the singing of a psalm, or hymn, the congregation
shall be dismissed with the benediction. The Presbytery shall duly
record its proceedings.

**c.** **Installation to a Needful Work.** In the installation of an ordained

minister to a Needful Work, the following questions are to be
substituted for those addressed to a candidate for ordination,
namely:

**Questions for Installation**

**1.** **Are you now willing to take charge of this Needful**

**Work, agreeable to your declaration in accepting the**
**call?**

**2.** **Do you conscientiously believe and declare, as far as**

**you know your own heart, that, in taking upon you this**
**charge, you are influenced by a sincere desire to**
**promote the glory of God and the good of the Church?**
**3.** **Do you solemnly promise that, by the assistance of the**

**grace of God, you will endeavor faithfully to discharge**
**all the duties of this Needful Work, and will be careful**
**to maintain a deportment in all respects becoming a**
**minister of the Gospel of Christ, agreeable to your**
**ordination engagements?**

The candidate having answered these questions in the
affirmative, the presiding minister shall propose to the calling entity
the following questions:

**1.** **Do you, the people of this calling entity, continue to**

**profess your readiness to receive _________________,**
**whom you have called to this Needful Work?**

<a id="ga52-p1257"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1259 printed_page=1257 -->

**2.** **Do you promise to receive the word of truth from his**

**mouth with meekness and love?**
**3.** **Do you promise to encourage him in his labors, and to**

**assist his endeavors for your instruction and spiritual**
**edification?**
**4.** **Do you engage to continue to him while he is in this**

**call that competent worldly maintenance which you**
**have promised, and to furnish him with whatever you**
**may see needful for the honor of religion and for his**
**comfort among you?**

**d.** **Transfer of ma nina needful Work.** when a man who has a call

to a Needful Work desires to move from one field of labor to

another, if the field of labor is in another Presbytery, the minister
must be examined and approved by the Presbytery to labor in the
field to which he is being transferred, and must be released for
transfer by his present Presbytery (see _BCO_ 13-6).

**Be it further resolved** that _BCO_ 23-1 be amended by adding the following at

the end of the current section as a new paragraph (underlining for
additions).

In the case of a needful work ( _BCO_ 21-12), when any minister shall
tender the resignation of his call to his Presbytery, the Presbytery shall
cite the calling entity to appear, to show cause why the Presbytery
should or should not accept the resignation. If the calling entity fails
to appear, or if its reasons for retaining the minister be deemed
insufficient, his resignation shall be accepted and the relation
dissolved. If any calling entity desires to be relieved of the minister
called to the particular needful work, a similar procedure shall be
observed. In any case, the minister must not physically leave the field
until the Presbytery or its commission empowered to handle
uncontested requests for dissolution has dissolved the relation.

So that _BCO_ 8-4, 20-1, 21-1, 21-12, and 23-1, as amended, would read:

**8-4.** As the Lord has given different gifts to men and has
committed to some special gifts and callings, the Church is authorized
to call and appoint some to labor as teaching elders in such works as

<a id="ga52-p1258"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1260 printed_page=1258 -->

may be needful to the Church ( _cf_ . BCO 21-12). When a teaching elder
is called to such needful work, it shall be incumbent upon him to make
full proof of his ministry by disseminating the Gospel for the
edification of the Church. He shall make a report to the Presbytery at
least once each year.

**20-1** . Before a candidate, or licentiate, can be ordained to the office
of the ministry, he must receive a call to a definite work. Ordinarily
the call must come from a church, Presbytery, a permanent Committee
or Agency (see BCO 14-1.12), or the General Assembly of this
denomination. If the call comes from another source, the Presbytery
shall always make a record of the reasons why it considers the work
t ob ea valid christian ministry. (See also BCO 8-7 and 21-1).
A proper call must be written and in the hands of the
Presbytery prior to being acted upon by a Presbytery. It must include
financial arrangements (such as salary, vacation, insurance,
retirement, etc.) between those calling and the one called, and
assurance that the definite work will afford the liberty to proclaim and
practice fully and freely the whole counsel of God, as contained in the
Scriptures and understood in the _Westminster Confession of Faith_ . It
shall be in accord with the _BCO_ 8.

**21-1.** No minister, licentiate or candidates hall receive a call from a
church ora call to a needful work ( _BCO_ 21-12) but by the permission
of his Presbytery. When a call has been presented to the Presbytery, if
found in order and the Presbytery deems it for the good of the Church,
they shall place it in the hands of the person to whom it is addressed.
Ordinarily a candidate or licentiate may not be granted
permission by the Presbytery to move on to the field to which he has
been called, prior to his examination for licensure or ordination.
Likewise an ordained minister from another Presbyterian Church in
America Presbytery or another denomination, ordinarily shall not
move on to the field to which he has been called until examined and

received by Presbytery.

**21-12.** **Needful Works and Calls from Outside the PCA** .

a. A minister, licentiate, or candidate that receives a call from a body

other than ap ca church shall be considered to have a call to a

<a id="ga52-p1259"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1261 printed_page=1259 -->

Needful Work ( _cf._ _BCO_ 8-4). Such calls shall include (without
limitation): (i) calls from the General Assembly or a Permanent
Committee or Agency, (ii) chaplaincy calls from a presbytery,
MNA, other Permanent Committee or Agency, or other body, (iii)
missionary calls from MTW or other missionary society, (iv)
campus minister calls from RUF, (v) calls from outside the PCA,
and (vi) calls t ob ea teacher ina school of divinity or college or
university.
b. **Ordination to a Needful Work** . The day appointed for the

ordination having come, and the Presbytery being convened, a
sermon suitable for the occasion shall be preached by a person
appointed or invited by the Presbytery. The Presbytery member
appointed to preside shall afterwards briefly recite from the pulpit
the proceedings of the Presbytery preparatory to the ordination; he
shall point out the nature and importance of the ordinance, and
endeavor to impress the audience with a proper sense of the
solemnity of the transaction.

**Questions for Ordination**

Then, addressing himself to the candidate, he shall propose to
him the following questions:

**1.** **Do you believe the Scriptures of the Old and New**
**Testaments, as originally given, to be the inerrant**
**Word of God, the only infallible rule of faith and**
**practice?**
**2.** **Do you sincerely receive and adopt the** _**Confession of**_
_**Faith**_ **and the** _**Catechisms**_ **of this Church, as containing**
**the system of doctrine taught in the Holy Scriptures;**
**and do you further promise that if at any time you find**
**yourself out of accord with any of the fundamentals of**
**this system of doctrine, you will on your own initiative,**
**make known to your Presbytery the change which has**
**taken place in your views since the assumption of this**
**ordination vow?**
**3.** **Do you approve of the form of government and**
**discipline of the Presbyterian Church in America, in**
**conformity with the general principles of Biblical**
**polity?**

<a id="ga52-p1260"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1262 printed_page=1260 -->

**4.** **Do you promise subjection to your brethren in the**
**Lord?**
**5.** **Have you been induced, as far as you know your own**
**heart, to seek the office of the holy ministry from love**
**to God and a sincere desire to promote His glory in the**
**Gospel of His Son?**
**6.** **Do you promise to be zealous and faithful in**
**maintaining the truths of the Gospel and the purity**
**and peace and unity of the Church, whatever**
**persecution or opposition may arise unto you on that**
**account?**
**7.** **Do you engage to be faithful and diligent in the**
**exercise o fall your duties as a christian and a minister**
**of the Gospel, whether personal or relational, private**
**or public; and to endeavor by the grace of God to**
**adorn the profession of the Gospel in your manner of**
**life, and to walk with exemplary piety before the flock**
**of which God shall make you overseer?**
**8.** **Do you now undertake this Needful Work, and do you**
**promise, in reliance on God for strength, to be faithful**
**in the discharge of all the duties incumbent on you as**
**a minister of the Gospel of the Lord Jesus Christ?**

The candidate having answered these questions in the
affirmative, the presiding minister shall propose to the calling
entity the following questions:

**1.** **Do you, the people of this calling entity, continue to**
**profess** **your** **readiness** **to** **receive**
_________________, **whom you have called to this**
**Needful Work?**
**2.** **Do you promise to receive the word of truth from his**
**mouth with meekness and love?**
**3.** **Do you promise to encourage him in his labors, and**
**to assist his endeavors for your instruction and**
**spiritual edification?**
**4.** **Do you engage to continue to him while he is in this**
**call that competent worldly maintenance which you**
**have promised, and to furnish him with whatever you**

<a id="ga52-p1261"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1263 printed_page=1261 -->

**may see needful for the honor of religion and for his**
**comfort among you?**

The people of the calling entity having answered these
questions in the affirmative, by holding up their right hands, the
candidate shall kneel, and the presiding minister shall, with prayer
and the laying on of hands of the Presbytery, according to the
apostolic example, solemnly set him apart to the holy office of the
Gospel ministry. Prayer being ended, he shall rise from his knees;
and the minister who presides shall first, followed by all members
of the Presbytery, take him by the right hand, saying, in words to
this effect:

**We give you the right hand of fellowship, to take part in**
**this ministry with us.**

The presiding minister shall then say:

**I now pronounce and declare that __________________**
**has been regularly elected, and ordained, agreeable to the**
**Word of God, and according to the Constitution of the**
**Presbyterian Church in America; and that as such he is**
**entitled to all support, encouragement, honor, and**
**obedience in the Lord: In the name of the Father, and of**
**the Son, and of the Holy Ghost. Amen.**

After which the minister presiding, or some other teaching or
ruling elder appointed for the purpose, shall give a solemn charge
to the minister to persevere in the discharge of his duties, and then
after prayer and the singing of a psalm, or hymn, the congregation
shall be dismissed with the benediction. The Presbytery shall duly
record its proceedings.

**c.** **Installation to a Needful Work.** In the installation of an ordained

minister to a Needful Work, the following questions are to be
substituted for those addressed to a candidate for ordination,
namely:

**Questions for Installation**

<a id="ga52-p1262"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1264 printed_page=1262 -->

**1.** **Are you now willing to take charge of this Needful**
**Work, agreeable to your declaration in accepting the**
**call?**
**2.** **Do you conscientiously believe and declare, as far as**
**you know your own heart, that, in taking upon you this**
**charge, you are influenced by a sincere desire to**
**promote the glory of God and the good of the Church?**
**3.** **Do you solemnly promise that, by the assistance of the**
**grace of God, you will endeavor faithfully to discharge**
**all the duties of this Needful Work, and will be careful**
**to maintain a deportment in all respects becoming a**
**minister of the Gospel of Christ, agreeable to your**
**ordination engagements?**

The candidate having answered these questions in the
affirmative, the presiding minister shall propose to the calling entity
the following questions:

**1.** **Do you, the people of this calling entity, continue to**
**profess your readiness to receive _________________,**
**whom you have called to this Needful Work?**
**2.** **Do you promise to receive the word of truth from his**
**mouth with meekness and love?**
**3.** **Do you promise to encourage him in his labors, and to**
**assist his endeavors for your instruction and spiritual**
**edification?**
**4.** **Do you engage to continue to him while he is in this**
**call that competent worldly maintenance which you**
**have promised, and to furnish him with whatever you**
**may see needful for the honor of religion and for his**
**comfort among you?**

**d.** **Transfer of ma nina needful Work.** when a man who has a call

to a Needful Work desires to move from one field of labor to

another, if the field of labor is in another Presbytery, the minister
must be examined and approved by the Presbytery to labor in the
field to which he is being transferred, and must be released for
transfer by his present Presbytery (see _BCO_ 13-6).

<a id="ga52-p1263"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1265 printed_page=1263 -->

**23-1** . When any minister shall tender the resignation of his pastoral
charge to his Presbytery, the Presbytery shall cite the church to appear
by its commissioners, to show cause why the Presbytery should or
should not accept the resignation. If the church fails to appear, or if
its reasons for retaining its pastor be deemed insufficient, his
resignation shall be accepted and the pastoral relation dissolved.

If any church desires to be relieved of its pastor, a similar
procedure shall be observed. But whether the minister or the church
initiates proceedings for a dissolution of the relation, there shall
always be a meeting of the congregation called and conducted in the
same manner as the call of the pastor. In any case, the minister must
not physically leave the field until the Presbytery or its commission
empowered to handle uncontested requests for dissolution has
dissolved the relation.

The associate or assistant pastors may continue to serve a
congregation when the pastoral relation of the senior pastor is
dissolved, but they may not normally succeed the senior pastor
without an intervening term of service in a different field of
labor. however a congregation by a secret ballot with four-fifths (4/5)
majority vote may petition Presbytery for an exception which by a
three-fourths (3/4) majority vote Presbytery may grant. Presbytery
needs to determine if the dissolution of the pastoral relationship with
the senior pastor was brought about in Christian love and good order
on the part of the parties concerned.
In the case of a needful work ( _BCO_ 21-12), when any minister
shall tender the resignation of his call to his Presbytery, the Presbytery
shall cite the calling entity to appear, to show cause why the
Presbytery should or should not accept the resignation. If the calling
entity fails to appear, or if its reasons for retaining the minister be
deemed insufficient, his resignation shall be accepted and the relation
dissolved. If any calling entity desires to be relieved of the minister
called to the particular needful work, a similar procedure shall be
observed. In any case, the minister must not physically leave the field
until the Presbytery or its commission empowered to handle
uncontested requests for dissolution has dissolved the relation.

<a id="ga52-p1264"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1266 printed_page=1264 -->

**RATIONALE:**

The current provisions of the BCO allow for calls to come from entities other
than congregations but do not specify how those calls should be presented and
prosecuted or dissolved. Additionally, the ordination and installation questions
presume a mani s being ordained and installed into a pastoral role ina
congregation. That means that the questions are inapplicable in the context of
a campus minister ora chaplain, for example.

Current _BCO_ 8-4 is the result of an editing process from material in the _BCO_
of the PCUS, which included additional material describing “works as may be
needful to the Church” as follows:

“When a minister is appointed t ob ea teacher ina school of divinity, or to give
instruction in the doctrines and duties of religion to youth assembled in a
college or university, it appertains to his office to take a pastoral oversight of
those committed to his charge, and to be diligent' in sowing the seed of the
Word and gathering the fruit thereof, as one who watches for souls.”

There are several common categories of calls to a Teaching Elder that are not
to a congregation. Among them would be campus minister (RUF), military and
other chaplains (MNA), missionary (MTW), and teachers (CTS and CC).
Other permanent committees and agencies (e.g., Ridge Haven, Geneva
Benefits) may or could issue calls to a teaching elder. The Stated Clerk ( _RAO_
3-1) is called by the General Assembly. All of these calls are legitimate to the
ministry (teaching elder) and require a minister to hold his membership ina
Presbytery ( _BCO_ 13-2). The Overture seeks to regularize how those calls are
presented and dissolved and to avoid the confusion that has sometimes resulted
from a lack of clarity.

The Overture sacrifices brevity for the sake of clarity in the ordination and
installation of men in the case of a needful work. It adds a new subsection to

_BCO_ 21 to set forth questions applicable in a non-congregational context to
avoid numerous footnotes or an entire new chapter to the _BCO_ that would
necessitate renumbering of all subsequent chapters.

Finally, the Overture seeks to give the permanent committees and agencies the
same flexibility with respect to calls that congregations currently enjoy while

<a id="ga52-p1265"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1267 printed_page=1265 -->

underscoring the responsibility of Presbyteries to approve or dissolve calls,
just as they do with congregational pastoral calls.

_Adopted by Houston Metro Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 14, 2025_
_Attested by RE /s/ Eric Manthei, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 31 from the Houston Metro Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 31-10 to Require Vote of the Entire Court to Suspend
an Officer’s Official Functions during Process”

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 31-10 is amended as follows (underlining for

additions):

**31-10** . when a member o fa church court i sunder process, all his
official functions may be administratively suspended at the court’s
discretion; but this shall never be done in the way of censure, and this
requires a two-thirds (2/3) majority of the entire court, even if the
process is being conducted by a commission.

So that _BCO_ 31-10 as amended would read:

**31-10** . when a member o fa church court i sunder process, all his
official functions may be administratively suspended at the court’s
discretion; but this shall never be done in the way of censure, and this
requires a two-thirds (2/3) majority of the court itself, even if the
process is being conducted by a commission.

**RATIONALE:**

There are occasions when a church officer (that is, “a member of a church
court”) comes under process. Such process could potentially take a period of
time to conclude. At the same time, it may be necessary for a number of
reasons, including the honor of religion, the health of the court, and even the
well-being of the accused, to suspend the accused from his official functions.
Current _BCO_ 31-10 rightly points out that this is not a censure but is only an
administrative suspension.

<a id="ga52-p1266"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1268 printed_page=1266 -->

Often process is conducted by a _BCO_ 15-3 commission rather than the entire
court. Such a commission may be relatively small when compared to the entire
court: a Presbytery commission must be at least four ( _BCO_ 15-2), but a Session
commission could be as few as two members. In the case of a Teaching Elder,
suspending him could result in a significant effect on both the Teaching Elder
and his congregation. For example, the church could be required to find pulpit
supply during the Teaching Elder’s suspension, or a church could be unable to
celebrate the sacraments if another Teaching Elder is unavailable to them.

Because a commission is acting _as the court_ ina case of process, the provision
of _BCO_ 31-10 can currently be enacted by the members of the commission
itself, rather than the larger court. in the case o fa six-member commission, for
example, that would mean that four members of the larger court could suspend
a man from “all his official functions.” This could occur even in a Presbytery
which had membership of more than 100 (Teaching Elders and Ruling Elder
commissioners). If a court is going to take the step of suspending an officer
from “all his official functions,” this should be done by a greater number of
officers of the court than a 2/3 majority of a commission.

_Adopted by Houston Metro Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 14, 2025_
_Attested by RE /s/ Eric Manthei, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 32 from the Houston Metro Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 36 to Provide for Elevation of a Censure without an

Entirely New Trial”

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 36 be amended to add a new paragraph, _BCO_ 36-8,

as follows (underlining for additions):

**36-8** . When members of a court wish to consider the elevation of an

indefinite suspension, whether from office or from the Sacraments
( _BCO_ 36-5) to the censure of deposition or excommunication, it shall
not be required to initiate process _de novo_ according to _BCO_ 31-2 or
_BCO_ 32-2. The indefinitely suspended person is not in good standing
and has already been found impenitent, resulting in indefinite
suspension ( _BCO_ 30-3). However, the indefinitely suspended person
has aright to a hearing in the matter. The court supervising the

<a id="ga52-p1267"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1269 printed_page=1267 -->

indefinite suspension may consider a motion to find him “incorrigible
and contumacious” ( _cf_ . _BCO_ 30-4) and present evidence to that effect.
The court shall give at least ten (10) days’ notice of such a motion to
the indefinitely suspended person and permit him the privilege of the
floor to plead and give a defense before the original trial court. The
court, upon completing its hearing, shall vote to determine whether or
not to elevate the indefinite suspension to deposition or
excommunication.

So that _BCO_ 36-8 as amended would read:

**36-8** . When members of a court wish to consider the elevation of an

indefinite suspension, whether from office or from the Sacraments
( _BCO_ 36-5) to the censure of deposition or excommunication, it shall
not be required to initiate process _de novo_ according to _BCO_ 31-2 or
_BCO_ 32-2. The indefinitely suspended person is not in good standing
and has already been found impenitent, resulting in indefinite
suspension ( _BCO_ 30-3). However, the indefinitely suspended person
has aright to a hearing in the matter. The court supervising the
indefinite suspension may consider a motion to find him “incorrigible
and contumacious” ( _cf_ . _BCO_ 30-4) and present evidence to that effect.
The court shall give at least ten (10) days’ notice of such a motion to
the indefinitely suspended person and permit him the privilege of the
floor to plead and give a defense before the original trial court. The
court, upon completing its hearing, shall vote to determine whether or
not to elevate the indefinite suspension to deposition or
excommunication.

**RATIONALE:**

The _Book of Church Order_ currently does not include any express provision
for the elevating of censure of indefinite suspension from either office or the
Sacraments to a greater censure of deposition or excommunication. This has
led to confusion as to what is the proper process for elevating the censure for
a person whom the court has deemed unrepentant. Should such a person be
entitled to completely _de novo_ process, that is, the court must charge the person
anew, marshal evidence and witnesses, and conduct a trial as if the person were
presumed innocent? While a censured person is entitled to some rights, clearly
he is not entitled to all rights under the Rules of Discipline. One who has been

<a id="ga52-p1268"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1270 printed_page=1268 -->

judged guilty by a court doe snot have the right to a presumption of innocence.
There is no person to make out a _BCO_ 32-2 charge, nor are there common
fame reports to provoke a _BCO_ 31-2 investigation. The court has already been
dealing with the indefinitely suspended person for some time, and by its
previous action, the court has already found indefinitely suspended person
impenitent.

The SJC revisited this issue, first decided in Case 2001-25 _TE Anthony_
_Dallison v. North Florida Presbytery_ ( _M30GA,_ 2002, pages 156-162). In that
case, the SJC decided that _no_ process beyond a vote of the court was required
to elevate a censure. In Case 2023-09 _TE Aaron Myers v. Illiana Presbytery_
( _M51GA_, 2024, pages 824-834), the SJC rejected two possible options: either
a _de novo_ process with a new trial or elevation by pure legislative act. Instead,
it reasoned that a process which permitted the censured person to present
evidence of his penitence without giving him the full rights of an uncensured
person under the Rules of Discipline was the best path forward. This
amendment would codify this path in our constitution.

_Adopted by Houston Metro Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 14, 2025_
_Attested by RE /s/ Eric Manthei, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 33 from TE Fred Greco (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 57-2 re the Examination of Young Persons for
Admission to the Sealing Ordinances”

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 57-2 be amended as follows (underlining for

additions):

**57-2** . The time when young persons come to understand the Gospel
cannot be precisely fixed. This must be left to the prudence of the
Session, whose office it is to judge, after careful examination, the
qualifications of those who apply for admission to sealing ordinances.
The examination shall include an evaluation of the young person’s
credible profession of faith, the young person’s understanding of the
meaning of the Lord’s Supper, and the young person’s understanding
of the questions in _BCO_ 57-5.

<a id="ga52-p1269"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1271 printed_page=1269 -->

So that _BCO_ 57-2 as amended would read:

**57-2** . The time when young persons come to understand the Gospel
cannot be precisely fixed. This must be left to the prudence of the
Session, whose office it is to judge, after careful examination, the
qualifications of those who apply for admission to sealing ordinances.
The examination shall include an evaluation of the young person’s
credible profession of faith, the young person’s understanding of the
meaning of the Lord’s Supper, and the young person’s understanding
of the questions in _BCO_ 57-5.

**RATIONALE:**

Current _BCO_ 57-2 properly highlights the fact that examining a young person
for membership is an inexact matter. There is no mandatory age for admission
to sealing ordinances. Sessions have discretion to use “prudence” in their
examinations and the admission of young persons to sealing ordinances.

Guidance as to what would be the minimal elements of such an examination

would be helpful for Sessions, as well as reminding Sessions that such
elements are mandatory: hence the “shall” in the proposed language.

This language is also a helpful companion to the proposed changes to _BCO_ 203, 24-3 and 25-1 in Overture 22. Concerns that a young person would be more
readily admitted to sealing ordinances without sufficient understanding
because he could be prohibited from voting on various matters (e.g., a pastoral
call) would be relieved by the explicit setting forth of the content of the
examination of young persons.

_Rejected by Houston Metro Presbytery at its stated meeting on April 14,_
_2025_

_Attested by RE /s/ Eric Manthei, stated clerk_

<a id="ga52-p1270"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1272 printed_page=1270 -->

##### OVERTURE 34 from the Savannah River Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _RAO_ 16 to Require Reporting on Session and Diaconate
Membership and Duties”

**Whereas** the 45 [th] General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America

adopted the 4th recommendation from the Study Committee on the
Role of Women in the Ministry of the Church which, in its final form
stated: “That sessions, if possible, establish a diaconate of qualified
ordained men.”; and

**Whereas** the rationale in the body of the report explaining this

recommendation stated that “There is at least anecdotal evidence that

some PCA churches are choosing not to establish an ordained
diaconate, even with qualified candidates, because the church wishes
to be free to establish a body of unordained servants, both male and
female ( _Book of Church Order_ 9-7)”, and similar reports of this
practice persist within our denomination; and

**Whereas** _BCO_ 9-7 recognizes an unofficial and unordained role within the

church called “assistants to the deacons” comprised of “godly men
andS women of the congregation to assist the deacons in the caring for
the sick, the widow, the orphans, the prisoners, and other who may be
in distress or need”; and

**Whereas** at its 50 [th] General Assembly, the PCA approved an amendment to

the _Book of Church Order_ ( _BCO_ ) 7-3 and affirmed it at its 51 [st] General
Assembly which prohibits assigning the titles connected to the
ecclesial offices of pastor, elder, or deacon to non-ordained members;

**Therefore be it resolved** the Savannah River Presbytery respectfully

overtures the 52 [nd] General Assembly of the PCA to protect the
constitutional practice of ordaining a Diaconate by adding the
following to _RAO_ 16-4.c.1 (underlining for additions; strikethrough
for deletions):

A current Directory of Presbytery, including (1) a list of all teaching
elders, with their addresses and their status; and of all churches and
missions with the address of the church, and the name and address of
the Clerk of Session, (2) the list of persons who comprise the Session

<a id="ga52-p1271"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1273 printed_page=1271 -->

and the Diaconate of each church arranged by their ordained or given
titles; and if no Diaconate exists, a report why it has not been
established and whether or not the duties have devolved upon the
ruling elders, unordained diaconal assistants, or any other committee
or member of the church, (23) a listing of all officers and committee
members of the regular committees of presbytery, and (34) a list
identifying all candidates under care, interns, and licentiates of the
presbytery with their addresses and their status.

**Be it further resolved** to add the following to _RAO_ 16-6.c.1:

Exceptions of substance: Apparent violations of the Scripture or
serious irregularities from the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church
in America, actions out of accord with the deliverances of the General
Assembly, and matters of impropriety and important delinquencies,
and any non-compliance with _RAO_ 16-3.e.5-6 and _RAO_ 16-4.c.1
should shall be reported under this category.

_Adopted by the Savannah River Presbytery at its Stated Meeting, April 15,_
_2025._

_Attested by RE W. Glynn Dyer, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 35 from the Pacific Northwest Presbytery (to AC)

“Request AC to Study and Report on GA Meeting Locations”

**Be it resolved,** the 52 [nd] General Assembly (GA) requests the Administrative

Committee (AC) to research and report to the 54 [th] GA on necessary
considerations and best practices for selecting and affording
appropriate host cities and venues, and the impact of location on broad
commissioner attendance across the PCA.

**Rationale** — We understand that in the post-COVID environment, the
hospitality industry has experienced significant cost pressures, coinciding with
historic highs in GA Commissioner attendance over the last four years. With
growth comes the need for larger spaces. Therefore, it is important to
determine the best locations for future GAs, considering several factors. For
example, should we consider larger cities with hub airports or continue to

<a id="ga52-p1272"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1274 printed_page=1272 -->

gather ina mix of mid-size markets in the Southeast and elsewhere? Would
commissioners prefer to meet, for example, in ATL every year?
Additionally, the AC should evaluate how well we're representing the
entire PCA at GA as the denomination continues to expand beyond the
Southeast. This includes considering site selection across the country and its
impact on reaching commissioner attendance that broadly represents the PCA.
Two years ago, at the 50 [th] GA in Memphis, 38% of our 87 Presbyteries
had between 0 and 4 Ruling Elder (RE) commissioners, totaling just 42 RE
commissioners. This is the same number of REs who attended from Covenant

Presbytery alone. Put another way, 38% of our Presbyteries supplied 6% of the
RE commissioners. Last year, at the 51 [st] GA in Richmond, just 8 of our
Presbyteries (9%) provided over 33% of all RE commissioners. This disparity
may be related, at least in part, to where g ais held.
Only one of 52 GA has been held west of the Rockies (1989 La
Mirada, CA). Only 7 have been west of the Mississippi River (5 in Texas).
Because many commissioners in the western half of the US probably need to
fly to GA, having GA in a city with an airline hub could be beneficial. This
also means very few western churches or Presbyteries have had the
opportunity to plan or lead a GA worship service. Below are the 16 states
where our 52 GAs have been held. Sixty percent have been in six states:
Tennessee, Alabama, Georgia, Texas, Florida, and Virginia (32 of 53; 60%).
Numbers correspond to the GA number:

TN 16, 33, 35, 38, 43, 50, 52 MI 6, 10, 15
AL 1, 19, 30, 34, 44, 49 NC 7, 31, 45
GA 2, 5, 8, 18, 22, 46 SC 4, 21, 41
TX 23, 29, 36, 42, 47 PA 14, 32
FL 9, 24, 28, 37 CA 17
VA 11, 20, 39, 51 LA 12
KY 27, 40, 53 CO 25
MO 13, 26, 48 MS 1

_The initial version of this Overture was approved at Pacific NW's stated meeting_

_on Feb 7, 2025_
_A PNW Commission was authorized to revise and file when it deemed most_

_prudent to do so._
_Attested to by: TE Jerid Krulish, Pacific NW Presbytery Clerk_

<a id="ga52-p1273"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1275 printed_page=1273 -->

##### OVERTURE 36 from the Georgia Foothills Presbytery (to MNA)

“Change the Boundaries of Georgia Foothills and Metro Atlanta
Presbyteries”

**Whereas** Lumpkin County, GA is the farthest northern point of Metro Atlanta

Presbytery; and

**Whereas** it lies geographically in the northeast Georgia mountains, near many

churches within the current boundary of Georgia Foothills Presbytery;
and

**Whereas** Georgia Foothills Presbytery desires to start an RUF campus

ministry at University of North Georgia located in the city of
Dahlonega in Lumpkin County; and

**Whereas** the Session of Creekstone Church, the only PCA church located in

Lumpkin County, is agreeable to come into Georgia Foothills
Presbytery; and

**Whereas** the minutes of the 46 [th] General Assembly established the boundary

of Georgia Foothills Presbytery to be the Georgia counties of Hall,
Barrow, Jackson, Clarke, Oconee, Habersham, and Union counties;
also the counties of Oglethorpe, Elbert, Madison, Hart, Franklin,
Banks, White, Stephens, Rabun, and Towns; and Gwinnett county
north of the defined boundaries, from east to west, of Highway 316,
then Sugarloaf Parkway to Peachtree Industrial Boulevard, then an
imaginary line westward from the intersection of Peachtree Industrial
Boulevard and Sugarloaf Parkway to the Chattahoochee River; and

**Whereas** the Minutes of the 46 [th] General Assembly established the boundary

of Metro Atlanta Presbytery to be the Georgia counties of Dekalb,
Fulton, Clayton, Cobb (East of I-75), Dawson, Lumpkin, Coweta,
Heard, Spalding, Troup, Merriweather, Pike, Fayette, Forsyth,
Rockdale, Newton, Henry, and Walton; and Gwinnett county south of
the defined boundaries, from east to west, of Highway 316, then
Sugarloaf Parkway to Peachtree Industrial Boulevard, then an
imaginary line westward from the intersection of Peachtree Industrial
Boulevard and Sugarloaf Parkway to the Chattahoochee River;

<a id="ga52-p1274"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1276 printed_page=1274 -->

**Therefore, be it resolved** that Georgia Foothills Presbytery overtures the

52 [nd] General Assembly as follows:

- That Lumpkin County be transferred from Metro Atlanta Presbytery
to Georgia Foothills Presbytery;

- By adding Lumpkin County to the list of Georgia counties included
within the boundary of Georgia Foothills Presbytery; and

- By removing Lumpkin County from the list of Georgia counties
included within the boundary of Metro Atlanta Presbytery.

_Adopted by Georgia Foothills Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 15,_
_2025_

_Attested by TE Don Aldin, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 37 from the Southeast Alabama Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 12-3 so that a Session May Elect One of Its Members
as Moderator in Judicial Cases”

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 12-3 is amended as follows (underlining for additions;

strikethrough for deletions):

**12-3.** when a church is without a pastor, the moderator of
the Session may be either a minister appointed for that purpose
by the Presbytery, with consent of the Session, or one invited
by the Session to preside on a particular occasion, or one of its
own members elected to preside. In judicial cases, the Session
may elect one of its members to preside as moderator shall be
a or invite a minister of the pres byte ry to which the church
belongs to preside as moderator.

So that _BCO_ 12-3 as amended would read:

**12-3.** when a church is without a pastor, the moderator of
the Session may be either a minister appointed for that purpose
by the Presbytery, with consent of the Session, or one invited
by the Session to preside on a particular occasion, or one of its
own members elected to preside. In judicial cases, the Session
may elect one of its members to preside as moderator or invite

<a id="ga52-p1275"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1277 printed_page=1275 -->

a minister of the Presbytery to which the church belongs to
preside as moderator.

**RATIONALE:**

Sessions should have discretion to elect a moderator in judicial cases. Some
Sessions may have ruling elders who are highly experienced in church
discipline and cases of process, and they should have the liberty to elect such
a ruling elder to preside. In instances where this is not the case, the revised
provision retains the right of the Session to invite a minister of the Presbytery
to preside.

_Adopted by the Southeast Alabama Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 22,_
_2025_

_Attested by TE Kevin Corley, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 38 from the Southeast Alabama Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 30-4 to Require a New Case with Process for Elevation
of Indefinite Suspension to Excommunication”

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 30-4 be amended as follows (underlining for

additions):

**30-4.** Excommunication is the excision of an offender from

the communion of the Church. This censure is to be inflicted

only on account of gross crime or heresy and when the offender
shows himself incorrigible and contumacious. Elevating
indefinite suspension from the Sacraments to
excommunication shall require a new case of process
establishing that the matter is a gross crime or heresy and that
the offender has shown himself incorrigible and contumacious.
The design of this censure is to operate on the offender as a
means of reclaiming him, to deliver the church from the
scandal of his offense, and to inspire all with fear by the
example of his discipline.

So that _BCO_ 30-4 as amended would read:

<a id="ga52-p1276"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1278 printed_page=1276 -->

**30-4.** Excommunication is the excision of an offender from

the communion of the Church. This censure is to be inflicted

only on account of gross crime or heresy and when the offender
shows himself incorrigible and contumacious. Elevating
indefinite suspension from the Sacraments to
excommunication shall require a new case of process
establishing that the matter is a gross crime or heresy and that
the offender has shown himself incorrigible and contumacious.
The design of this censure is to operate on the offender as a
means of reclaiming him, to deliver the church from the
scandal of his offense, and to inspire all with fear by the
example of his discipline.

**RATIONALE:**

The SJC recently decided _TE Aaron Myers v. Iliana Presbytery_ ( _M51GA_,
2024, pages 824-834) in which it concluded that where there is a previous
finding of guilt and imposition of censure, a censured person is not entitled to
full process _de novo_ for the court to find him “incorrigible and contumacious”
( _BCO_ 30-4). The SJC, invoking the “intrinsic powers” of our courts and
“parliamentary law,” prescribed the following procedure:

the indefinitely suspended person has aright to a hearing in
the matter: he must be charged by the court supervising the
indefinite suspension with being “incorrigible and
contumacious,” he must be presented with the evidence to that
effect, he must be called upon to plead before the court, and he
would have aright to a defense before the original trial court.
The court, upon completing its hearing, would be called upon
to consider a motion to amend a matter previously adopted, to
elevate the indefinite suspension to excommunication. Passage
would require a two thirds majority (2/3), unless previous
notice were given o fan intent to offer a motion to amend a
matter previously adopted, the notice framed in such a way as
to avoid undermining the impartiality of the maker and thereby
disqualifying him from participation in the hearing.

<a id="ga52-p1277"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1279 printed_page=1277 -->

This procedure is not expressly prescribed anywhere in our Rules of
Discipline.

t hes jc previously treated a decision to elevate censure as a matter o fits
discretion without the necessity of further process in _Dallison v. North Florida_
_Presbytery_, ( _M30GA_ 2002, page 156, 160-161). As in _Myers_, _Dallison_ denied
that the Constitution requires a “new trial for new charges” for elevating
censure but afforded the court “discretion” in such elevations of censure

(subject to the “clear error” standard). But _Dallison_ never mentioned limiting
that discretion by a parliamentary procedure as the SJC only recently held in
_Myers_, evidencing an ongoing lack of certainty and uniformity in the standards
our courts should apply when imposing the Church’s highest censure in
elevation cases.

Contrary to the variable holdings on display in _Myers_ and _Dallison_, before a
member of the Church is excommunicated, he should be afforded a regular
case of process per our Rules of Discipline to guarantee that those conditions
peculiarly pertinent to excommunication have been established in accordance
with the time-tested procedures that Presbyterianism has long recognized as
decent and fair.

Such procedures ensure a meaningful record is made detailing the ground for
the court’s decision and affording an excommunicated person a meaningful
final review of his excommunication. Since the accused’s restoration to the

Sacraments is the intended result of indefinite suspension and
excommunication alike, the real difference between the two censures is
whether the accused has any right of review, a hollow right indeed in the
absence of the protections afforded by a case of process. Importantly, a person
under indefinite suspension from the Sacraments has no right to complain
against a court’s refusal to restore him, however meritorious his case may be,
since he i snot in “good standing.” ( _BCO_ 43-1). Therefore, an appeal from his
excommunication is the censured person’s last and only opportunity to seek
relief from a censuring court that has made an error about whether he is truly
“incorrigible and contumacious.” In deciding to elevate censure to
excommunication it is possible for a court to act irregularly, refuse reasonable
indulgence, receive improper evidence or decline to receive proper evidence,
hurry to a decision, manifest prejudice, make a mistake, or commit injustice in
the censure. ( _BCO_ 42-3). But if the censuring court’s actions are not regulated
by the familiar and well-defined boundaries afforded by a case of process, how

<a id="ga52-p1278"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1280 printed_page=1278 -->

can the accused obtain, and the higher courts afford him, a meaningful review
of his excommunication? The weightiness of this last opportunity for review
is evident when we understand that once he is excommunicated, he thereafter
lacks any kind of standing either to complain or appeal should the court of
original jurisdiction refuse to restore him, thus instantiating and insulating any
error of the censuring court, perhaps permanently.

This proposed amendment to _BCO_ 30-4 provides that the court, having
reckoned the case unfit for excommunication in the first instance, and having
fulfilled its responsibility to “frequently converse with” the accused “as well
as pray with him and for him, that it would please God to give him repentance”
( _BCO_ 37-2), must thereafter demonstrate that the censure of indefinite
suspension from the Sacraments should be elevated because those peculiar
conditions justifying the different and greater censure of excommunication
(i.e., that the case involves a “gross crime or heresy” and that the offender has
shown himself “incorrigible and contumacious”) have been established before
taking the extraordinary step of “excising him from the communion of the
Church.”

_Adopted by the Southeast Alabama Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 22,_
_2025_

_Attested by TE Kevin Corley, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 39 from the Southeast Alabama Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 34-8 and add _BCO_ 33-5 to Clarify the Process for
Elevating Suspension from Office to Deposition”

**Be it resolved** that _BCO_ 34-8 be amended as follows (underlining for

additions; strikethrough for deletions):

**34-8.** A minister under indefinite suspension from his office
or deposed for scandalous conduct shall not be restored, even
on the deepest sorrow for his sin, until he shall exhibit for a
considerable time such an eminently exemplary, humble and
edifying life and testimony as shall heal the wound made by
his scandal. A deposed minister shall in no case be restored
until it shall appear that the general sentiment of the Church is

<a id="ga52-p1279"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1281 printed_page=1279 -->

strongly in his favor, and demands his restoration; and then
only by the court inflicting the censure, or with that court’s
consent. The removal of deposition requires a three-fourths
(3/4) vote of the court inflicting the censure, or a three-fourths
(3/4) vote of the court to which the majority of the original
court delegates that authority.

Likewise, if it shall appear in time that any minister suspended
from office fails to satisfy the requirements of _BCO_ 34-8
and/or _BCO_ 37-8, Presbytery may depose him from office, a
majority of three-quarters (3/4) being necessary for this
purpose. In such a case, the clerk shall under the order of the
Presbytery forthwith deliver to the minister concerned a
written note that, at the next stated meeting, the question of his
being so dealt with is to be considered. This notice shall
distinctly state the grounds for this proceeding. The party thus
notified shall be heard in his own defense; and if the decision
pass against him he may appeal, as if he had been tried after
the usual forms.

**Be it further resolved** that a new _BCO_ 33-5 be added as follows (underlining

for new wording):

**33-5.** When a ruling elder or deacon is indefinitely
suspended from office after a case of processor abc o 38-1
case without process, he shall not be restored, even on the
deepest sorrow for his sin, until he shall exhibit for a
considerable time such an eminently exemplary, humble and
edifying life and testimony as shall heal any wound made by
his scandal. The removal of deposition requires a three-fourths
(3/4) vote of his Session.

If it shall appear in time that any ruling elder or deacon
suspended from office fails to satisfy the above requirements,
the Session may depose him from office, a majority of threequarters (3/4) being necessary for this purpose. In such a case,
the clerk shall under the order of the Session forthwith deliver

to the Ruling Elder or Deacon concerned a written note that, at
the next stated meeting, the question of his being so dealt with

<a id="ga52-p1280"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1282 printed_page=1280 -->

is to be considered. This notice shall distinctly state the
grounds for this proceeding. The party thus notified shall be
heard in his own defense; and if the decision pass against him
he may appeal, as if he had been tried after the usual forms.

So that _BCO_ 34-8 as amended would read:

**34-8.** A minister under indefinite suspension from his office
or deposed for scandalous conduct shall not be restored, even
on the deepest sorrow for his sin, until he shall exhibit for a
considerable time such an eminently exemplary, humble and
edifying life and testimony as shall heal the wound made by
his scandal. A deposed minister shall in no case be restored
until it shall appear that the general sentiment of the Church is
strongly in his favor and demands his restoration; and then only
by the court inflicting the censure, or with that court’s consent.
The removal of deposition requires a three-fourths (3/4) vote
of the court inflicting the censure, or a three-fourths (3/4) vote
of the court to which the majority of the original court
delegates that authority.

Likewise, if it shall appear in time that any minister suspended
from office fails to satisfy the requirements of _BCO_ 34-8
and/or _BCO_ 37-8, Presbytery may depose him from office, a
majority of three-quarters (3/4) being necessary for this
purpose. In such a case, the clerk shall under the order of the
Presbytery forthwith deliver to the minister concerned a
written note that, at the next stated meeting, the question of his
being so dealt with is to be considered. This notice shall
distinctly state the grounds for this proceeding. The party thus
notified shall be heard in his own defense; and if the decision
pass against him he may appeal, as if he had been tried after
the usual forms.

So that _BCO_ 33-5 as amended would read:

**33-5.** When a ruling elder or deacon is indefinitely
suspended from office after a case of processor abc o 38-1
case without process, he shall not be restored, even on the

<a id="ga52-p1281"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1283 printed_page=1281 -->

deepest sorrow for his sin, until he shall exhibit for a
considerable time such an eminently exemplary, humble and
edifying life and testimony as shall heal any wound made by
his scandal. The removal of deposition requires a three-fourths
(3/4) vote of his Session.

If it shall appear in time that any ruling elder or deacon
suspended from office fails to satisfy the above requirements,
the Session may depose him from office, a majority of threequarters (3/4) being necessary for this purpose. In such a case,
the clerk shall under the order of the Session forthwith deliver

to the Ruling Elder or Deacon concerned a written note that, at
the next stated meeting, the question of his being so dealt with
is to be considered. This notice shall distinctly state the
grounds for this proceeding. The party thus notified shall be
heard in his own defense; and if the decision pass against him
he may appeal, as if he had been tried after the usual forms.

**RATIONALE:**

Indefinite suspension from office is a distinct and insular censure of the Church
( _BCO_ 30-3) and is subject to review by the higher courts via appeal or
complaint.

By contrast, the procedural mechanism and kind of process applicable to
_elevating_ indefinite suspension from office to deposition from office is less
clear as has been demonstrated recently in the SJC case _TE Aaron Myers v._
_Iliana Presbytery_ ( _M51GA_, 2024, page 824) a swell as formerly in _Dallison v._
_North Florida Presbytery_ ( _M30GA_, 2002, page 156).

It would therefore be wise for the Assembly to enact changes to our Rules of
Discipline to promote clarity and uniformity regarding agreeable and
appropriate procedures for elevating indefinite suspension from office to
deposition from office.

**Elevation of Censure to Deposition for Teaching Elders**

With respect to teaching elders, indefinite suspension from office serves the
Church to protect the integrity of the gospel ministry. Since a Presbytery may

<a id="ga52-p1282"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1284 printed_page=1282 -->

conclude that the continuance of a convicted minister in office would

scandalize or otherwise hurt the Church (or even the minister), it may choose
indefinite suspension, adopting a “wait and see” approach particularly where
the Presbytery sees reasonable prospects for his repentance and restoration to
the ministry.

_BCO_ 37-8, governing the “Removal of Censure” in cases of suspension from
office, mandates that the Presbytery “proceed with great caution,” advancing
through deliberate stages of a gradual restoration, first admitting him to the
Sacraments (if he was suspended from them), and only later giving him “the
privilege of preaching on probation for a time, so as to test the sincerity of his
repentance and prospect of his usefulness.” It continues, “When satisfied in
these respects, the Presbytery shall take steps to restore him to his office,”
noting that “the case shall always be under judicial consideration until the
declaration of restoration has been pronounced. Notably, _BCO_ 37-8 prescribes
no procedural mechanism defining what the phrase “take steps to restore him
to his office” should entail, and there is no mention at all what procedural
“steps” might be involved in _elevating_ his suspension from office to deposition
from office.

Additionally, _BCO_ 30-3 (“Church Censures”) prescribes, “In the case of
indefinite suspension from office imposed due to scandalous conduct, the
procedure outlined in _BCO_ 34-8 (“Special Rules Pertaining to Process Against
a Minister”) shall be followed.” That provision says:

A minister under indefinite suspension from his office or
deposed for scandalous conduct shall not be restored, even on
the deepest sorrow for his sin, until he shall exhibit for a
considerable time such an eminently exemplary, humble and
edifying life and testimony as shall heal the wound made by
his scandal.

It adds:

A deposed minister shall in no case be restored until it shall
appear that the general sentiment of the Church is strongly in
his favor, and demands his restoration; and then only by the
court inflicting the censure, or with that court’s consent. The
removal of deposition requires a three-fourths (3/4) vote of the

<a id="ga52-p1283"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1285 printed_page=1283 -->

court inflicting the censure, or a three-fourths (3/4) vote of the
court to which the majority of the original court delegates that
authority.

Interestingly, while _BCO_ 34-8 prescribes a procedural mechanism for
restoration to office after deposition from office, it is frustratingly silent about
what steps should be taken to either elevate suspension from office to
deposition or to remove the same.

Clearly, the duration of a minister’s indefinite suspension from office is related
to the elevation of that censure to deposition, for once a minister is indefinitely
suspended, he must either be restored in time or be deposed. There are no other
options, and because the duration of the censure is presently governed by the
various considerations described in _BCO_ 34-8 and _BCO_ 37-8, the particular
facts and circumstances must guide the Presbytery’s judgment about how best
to proceed.

The question of a man’s deposition from the ministry is different in important
ways from excommunication. A minister both suspended from office and the
Sacraments might be deemed unsuitable to be restored to office yet remain a
genuine Christian and therefore be restored to the Sacraments upon
satisfactory evidence of repentance ( _BCO_ 36-5), sometimes well before his
Presbytery finds it appropriate to restore him to the ministry.

Elevating suspension from the Sacraments to excommunication as compared
to elevating suspension from office to deposition is like comparing apples and
oranges. Every Christian has a duty to repent of sin, but Christians have neither
a manifest duty to be eligible for gospel ministry nor to pursue (or be restored
to) the same. excommunication involves a determination that a man has
committed the offense of being “incorrigible and contumacious” ( _BCO_ 30-4),
matters regularly assessed in cases of process, but the determination of those
factors governing whether a man should be restored to the ministry as we find
them in _BCO_ 34-8 and _BCO_ 37-8 are not readily susceptible to adjudication in
a case of process with the appointment o fa prosecutor in the way that the
refusal to repent of sin is, nor are the stakes as high since deposition from office
does not remove a man from the communion of the Church.

The situation of an indefinitely suspended minister, particularly as time
marches on, is analogous to the irregularity we address in cases where a

<a id="ga52-p1284"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1286 printed_page=1284 -->

minister is “divested” from the ministry because he has been “without call” for
an extended period as governed by _BCO_ 34-10. The key difference is that
divestment “without censure” is expressly _not_ available to an indefinitely
suspended minister because the cause of his ineligibility arises from his having
committed an _offense_ rather than an inculpable “lack of acceptance to the
Church.”

This proposed amendment would adopt the general form of parliamentary
procedure used in divestiture cases as the procedure governing the elevation
of indefinite suspension from office to deposition from office which, while
less formal than a case of process, is both tested and familiar, requiring notice
to the suspended minister and an opportunity for him to be heard as well as a
proposed three-quarters (3/4) majority vote to obtain his deposition from office
rather than the lesser two-thirds (2/3) vote applicable to divestiture cases. This
heightened vote requirement affords parity as it corresponds to the same supermajority vote needed for a man’s _restoration_ to the ministry in such cases as
he has been deposed from office per existing _BCO_ 34-8. The heightened vote
requirement also mitigates whatever protection might be lost to the minister
resulting from the more informal nature of such proceedings. The deposed
minister is also protected by the right of appeal from the deposition, which
would mean that the reviewing court would overturn the ruling if “clear error”
were demonstrated. ( _BCO_ 39-3.3).

**Elevation of Censure to Deposition for Ruling Elders and Deacons**

A corresponding solution is proposed for the elevation of suspension from
office to deposition for ruling elders and deacons. Unlike the steps described
in _BCO_ 34-8 and _BCO_ 37-8, the present Rules of Discipline provide no specific
guidelines governing the restoration to office of ruling elders and deacons after
they have been indefinitely suspended.

The instant proposal would treat ruling elders and deacons the same as it treats
teaching elders, adopting the same standard for restoration from suspension
from office applicable to ministers under _BCO_ 34-8 and subjecting both their
restoration to office and deposition from office to a three-fourths (3/4) vote of
their Session pursuant to a parliamentary procedure subject to appeal.

<a id="ga52-p1285"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1287 printed_page=1285 -->

_Adopted by the Southeast Alabama Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 22,_
_2025_

_Attested by TE Kevin Corley, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 40 from the Southeast Alabama Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 35-9 to Require Recording All Parts of a Trial”

**Be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] General Assembly revise _BCO_ 35-9 as shown

below (underlining for additions; strikethrough for deletions):

**35-9.** All testimony parts of a trial other than deliberations
shall be recorded (by transcription, audiotape, videotape, or
some other electronic means) and witnesses informed of such
prior to testifying. Such recording becomes part of the Record
of the Case. However, in order to be referenced in written or
oral briefs, such recording must be transcribed and the
transcription authenticated by the trial court. The court of final
appeal may assess the cost of transcription equitably among
the parties

So that _BCO_ 35-9 as amended would read:

**35-9.** All parts of a trial other than deliberations shall be
recorded (by transcription, audiotape, videotape, or some other
electronic means) and witnesses informed of such prior to
testifying. Such recording becomes part of the Record of the
Case. However, in order to be referenced in written or oral
briefs, such recording must be transcribed and the transcription
authenticated by the trial court. The court of final appeal may
assess the cost of transcription equitably among the parties

**Rationale**

1. This clarifies, for example, that objections raised by the parties during a

trial and the court's ruling on those objections, are recorded and available
for transcription in the event of an appeal

<a id="ga52-p1286"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1288 printed_page=1286 -->

_Adopted by the Southeast Alabama Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 22,_
_2025_

_Attested by TE Kevin Corley, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 41 from the Calvary Presbytery (to AC)

“Direct the AC to Collect & Report Each Congregation’s Public
Worship Service Times”

**Whereas** the worship of God, and particularly the corporate public worship of

God, is vital to the Christian faith ( _WLC_ 117); and,

**Whereas** proper observance of the Fourth Commandment requires due

diligence and preparation ( _WLC_ 121); and

**Whereas** due diligence and preparation requires knowing when particular

congregations will gather for public worship; and,

**Whereas** the charge of keeping the Sabbath is more specially directed to

superiors (WLC 118); and

**Whereas** information about public worship service times are at present

occasionally unavailable and often tedious for individuals to collect
through publicly accessible channels; and,

**Therefore, be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] General Assembly direct the

Administrative Committee to collect and report each congregation’s
public worship service times (inclusive of all stated worship services)
on the church directory map available at
https://www.pcaac.org/churchdirectory/.

**Therefore, be it further resolved** that the 52 [nd] General Assembly encourage

the Administrative Committee to seek the assistance of the several

Presbyteries in this task.

_Adopted by Calvary Presbytery at its stated meeting of April 24, 2025._
_Attested by /s/ RE Melton L. Duncan, Stated Clerk_

<a id="ga52-p1287"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1289 printed_page=1287 -->

##### OVERTURE 42 from the Calvary Presbytery (to AC)

“Direct the Stated Clerk Not to Collect Statistical Data Pertaining to
Age or Ethnicity”

**Whereas** _RAO_ 4-11 authorizes the Administrative Committee to collect

statistical information for the purpose of providing and publishing
“guidelines for the Partnership Share Giving Program of the PCA,
including the Ministry Ask” and for no other stated reason; and,

**Whereas** _RAO_ 4-11 specifies that said “guidelines shall include statistical

information expressed both in per capita giving terms and in
proportional (percentage) giving terms to demonstrate the giving
necessary to attain full funding for budgets of the General Assembly
Committees and Agencies” and nothing more; and,

**Whereas** the Office of the Stated Clerk, through a note contained ina box on

the bottom of page 2 of the 2024 Annual Statistical Report Form,
informed the congregations of the PCA that “two new line items”
regarding “communicant member ethnicity and age” would appear
“on the 2025 Annual Statistical Report Form” to be sent out for
completion in 2026; and,

**Whereas** the practical difficulties of collecting information on “communicant

member ethnicity and age” are greater than perhaps the Stated Clerk’s
Office has yet considered due to members’ reticence to share their
ages or uncertain of how best to categorize their ethnicity, with many
of our members being the children of interracial/multiethnic unions;
and,

**Whereas** the theological commitments of the PCA derived from Scripture and

expressed in the PCA’s subordinate Standards and Constitute militate
against the collection of data pertaining to ethnicity, for the PCA
confesses with Scripture that though our members come from many
different backgrounds (ethnic and otherwise), our Triune God has
united us together as “one body” (1 Cor. 12:12), with Christ Himself
being “our peace, who made both groups into one and broke down the
barrier of the dividing wall” (Eph. 2:14), baptizing all “by one
Spirit…into one body, whether Jews or Greeks, whether slaves of
free,” and making all “to drink of one Spirit” (1 Cor. 12:13), even

<a id="ga52-p1288"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1290 printed_page=1288 -->

while acknowledging that Old Testament historical narratives,
ceremonial laws, and prophecies frequently refer to people groups by
familial origin, tribal associations, ethnic affinities, nations, and what
some commentators might refer to as racial distinctions, yet never in
such a way so as to accept as appropriate the continuing use of such
distinctions in the formal categorization of members of the church of
Jesus Christ; and,

**Whereas** the kingdoms of this world may collect (and even act upon) certain

demographic information, yet God has neither directed nor authorized
the church to collect such information pertaining to ethnicity; and,

**Whereas** there are two classes of people with which the church should be

concerned, in its formal statistical record-keeping: covenant-keepers
and covenant-breakers ( _BCO_ 56-4.j.), recorded in the PCA as those
who are in covenant with God through their own “profession of faith
in Christ” ( _BCO_ 6-2; see also 57-3, 4) or “through the covenant and
by right of birth” as “non-communing members of the church” ( _BCO_
6-1), thus furnishing the church with two classes of those who are
deemed in covenant with God—communing members and noncommuning members (WCF 25.2; WLC 62)—and one additional
record of those who break covenant with God and come under the

censure of excommunication ( _BCO_ 30-1, 4); and,

**Whereas** there is no clear usefulness or theological justification for further

subdividing either of these two categories (i.e., covenant-keepers and
covenant-breakers) in terms of either age or ethnicity; and,

**Whereas** the collection of data pertaining to age or ethnicity gives the

appearance of the sins of partiality (James 2:9) and pride, which must
not be indulged even at the first stirring of the same (James 1:14-15),
as illustrated in King David’s sinful (and deadly) census recorded in 2
Samuel 24 and cited in 1 Chronicles 21; and,

**Whereas** the resolution of the conflict between Hellenistic Jews and native

Hebrews in Acts 6 does not constitute an approved example that would
lead us by necessary inference to collect denomination-wide
demographic data pertaining to ethnicity or age; and,

<a id="ga52-p1289"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1291 printed_page=1289 -->

**Whereas** the collection of demographic data pertaining to ethnicity or age is

not necessary for the undershepherds called and ordained by Christ to
know the flock in their care; and,

**Whereas** the church is called to “examine everything carefully; hold fast to

that which is good; abstain from every form of evil” (1 Thess. 5:2122).

**Therefore, be it resolved** that Calvary Presbytery hereby overtures the 52 [nd]

General Assembly to direct the Office of the Stated Clerk through the
Administrative Committee not to collect statistical data pertaining to
age or ethnicity in annual statistical reports

_Adopted by Calvary Presbytery at its stated meeting of April 24, 2025._
_Attested by /s/ RE Melton L. Duncan, Stated Clerk_

##### OVERTURE 43 from the Calvary Presbytery (to CCB, OC, AC)

“Amend _RAO_ 4-11 regarding Data on Age and Ethnicity”

**Whereas** _RAO_ 4-11 authorizes the Administrative Committee to collect

statistical information for the purpose of providing and publishing
“guidelines for the Partnership Share Giving Program of the PCA,
including the Ministry Ask” and for no other stated reason; and,

**Whereas** _RAO_ 4-11 specifies that said “guidelines shall include statistical

information expressed both in per capita giving terms and in
proportional (percentage) giving terms to demonstrate the giving
necessary to attain full funding for budgets of the General Assembly
Committees and Agencies” and nothing more; and,

**Whereas** the Office of the Stated Clerk, through a note contained ina box on

the bottom of page 2 of the 2024 Annual Statistical Report Form,
informed the congregations of the PCA that “two new line items”
regarding “communicant member ethnicity and age” would appear
“on the 2025 Annual Statistical Report Form” to be sent out for
completion in 2026; and,

<a id="ga52-p1290"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1292 printed_page=1290 -->

**Whereas** the practical difficulties of collecting information on “communicant

member ethnicity and age” are greater than perhaps the Stated Clerk’s
Office has yet considered due to members’ reticence to share their
ages or uncertain of how best to categorize their ethnicity, with many
of our members being the children of interracial/multiethnic unions;
and,

**Whereas** the theological commitments of the PCA derived from Scripture and

expressed in the PCA’s subordinate Standards and Constitute militate
against the collection of data pertaining to ethnicity, for the PCA
confesses with Scripture that though our members come from many
different backgrounds (ethnic and otherwise), our Triune God has
united us together as “one body” (1 Cor. 12:12), with Christ Himself
being “our peace, who made both groups into one and broke down the
barrier of the dividing wall” (Eph. 2:14), baptizing all “by one
Spirit…into one body, whether Jews or Greeks, whether slaves of
free,” and making all “to drink of one Spirit” (1 Cor. 12:13), even
while acknowledging that Old Testament historical narratives,
ceremonial laws, and prophecies frequently refer to people groups by
familial origin, tribal associations, ethnic affinities, nations, and what
some commentators might refer to as racial distinctions, yet never in
such a way so as to accept as appropriate the continuing use of such
distinctions in the formal categorization of members of the church of
Jesus Christ; and,

**Whereas** the kingdoms of this world may collect (and even act upon) certain

demographic information, yet God has neither directed nor authorized
the church to collect such information pertaining to ethnicity; and,

**Whereas** there are two classes of people with which the church should be

concerned, in its formal statistical record-keeping: covenant-keepers
and covenant-breakers ( _BCO_ 56-4.j.), recorded in the PCA as those
who are in covenant with God through their own “profession of faith
in Christ” ( _BCO_ 6-2; see also 57-3, 4) or “through the covenant and
by right of birth” as “non-communing members of the church” ( _BCO_
6-1), thus furnishing the church with two classes of those who are
deemed in covenant with God—communing members and noncommuning members (WCF 25.2; WLC 62)—and one additional

<a id="ga52-p1291"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1293 printed_page=1291 -->

record of those who break covenant with God and come under the

censure of excommunication ( _BCO_ 30-1, 4); and,

**Whereas** there is no clear usefulness or theological justification for further

subdividing either of these two categories (i.e., covenant-keepers and
covenant-breakers) in terms of either age or ethnicity; and,

**Whereas** the collection of data pertaining to age or ethnicity gives the

appearance of the sins of partiality (James 2:9) and pride, which must
not be indulged even at the first stirring of the same (James 1:14-15),
as illustrated in King David’s sinful (and deadly) census recorded in 2
Samuel 24 and cited in 1 Chronicles 21; and,

**Whereas** the resolution of the conflict between Hellenistic Jews and native

Hebrews in Acts 6 does not constitute an approved example that would
lead us by necessary inference to collect denomination-wide
demographic data pertaining to ethnicity or age; and,

**Whereas** the collection of demographic data pertaining to ethnicity or age is

not necessary for the undershepherds called and ordained by Christ to
know the flock in their care; and,

**Whereas** the church is called to “examine everything carefully; hold fast to

that which is good; abstain from every form of evil” (1 Thess. 5:2122).

**Therefore, be it resolved** that Calvary Presbytery hereby overtures the 52 [nd]

General Assembly to amend _RAO_ 4-11 by adding the following to the
third paragraph (underlining for additions):

Such statistical information shall not include data pertaining to age or
ethnicity.

So that the amended section will read as follows:

**4-11.** The budget for each permanent Committee and
Agency shall be submitted to the Administrative Committee of
General Assembly, which shall independently evaluate the
proposed budget of each permanent Committee and Agency

<a id="ga52-p1292"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1294 printed_page=1292 -->

and report to the Assembly its considered opinion on the
adoption. To protect the ministry and fiduciary responsibilities
of the Committees and Agency Boards, the financial
coordination and independent evaluation of the budgets by the
Administrative Committee shall be for review, discussion, and
recommendation to the Assembly.

Should modifications in budgets be deemed necessary by the
Assembly, special care shall be taken that changes not be made
in such a way as to threaten the continuity or effectiveness of
the Committee’s or Agency’s ministry. Budgets of permanent
Committees and Agencies that are agreed upon by the
Administrative Committee may be changed only by a twothirds vote of the Assembly commissioners present and voting
at the time the budget is submitted for adoption. In the event
the Administrative Committee disagrees with the budget
submitted by one of the Committees or Boards, in whole or any
item thereof, the General Assembly may adopt either the
Committee’s or Board’s budget or the Administrative
Committee’s recommended budget by simple majority. The
requirement of a two-thirds vote of the General Assembly
