applies only to changes not recommended by the
Administrative Committee, initiated on the floor of the
Assembly. With respect to the Committees and Agencies,
financial coordination is through the Administrative
Committee to the Assembly and from the General Assembly
to the Committees and Agencies. The Administrative
Committee is a “filter upward” and has no downward
authority.

The Administrative Committee shall annually provide and
publish guidelines for the Partnership Share Giving Program
of the PCA, including the Ministry Ask. These guidelines shall
include statistical information expressed both in per capita
giving terms and in proportional (percentage) giving terms to
demonstrate the giving necessary to attain full funding for
budgets of the General Assembly Committees and Agencies.
Such statistical information shall not include data pertaining to
age or ethnicity.

<a id="ga52-p1293"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1295 printed_page=1293 -->

The Committees and Agencies receiving support through the
PCA Partnership Share Giving Program shall present their
financial needs and their Ministry Ask to the Administrative
Committee for review and recommendation in the course of

the regular budgeting process. Each Committee and Agency
shall then be free to request from the PCA churches and
constituency the contributions necessary to fund its approved
budget.

While all churches remain free to give their contributions as
they by God’s guidance determine, all churches are
encouraged to give, as they are able, at least ten percent (10%)
of their tithes and offerings to the PCA causes of presbyteries
and General Assembly.

The Administrative Committee shall maintain a manual

containing the Affirmation of Core Financial Standards. This
information, by which each PCA General Assembly ministry
(Committee or Agency) shall function and by which the budget
process is governed for each Committee and Agency, shall be
available to all constituencies

_Adopted by Calvary Presbytery at its stated meeting of April 24, 2025._
_Attested by /s/ RE Melton L. Duncan, Stated Clerk_

##### OVERTURE 44 from the Pee Dee Presbytery (to AC)

“Transition byFaith to a Press-Release-Based Publication”

**Whereas** byFaith magazine was established as the official publication of the

Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) to serve as a voice for the
denomination; and

**Whereas** the PCA is committed to maintaining a clear distinction between

denominational communications and editorial commentary, ensuring
that official communications remain impartial and representative of
the church as a whole; and

<a id="ga52-p1294"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1296 printed_page=1294 -->

**Whereas** the purpose of denominational communication should be to provide

clear and accurate information regarding the actions, policies, and
ministries of the PCA without editorializing or presenting particular
viewpoints that may not reflect the consensus of the denomination (1
Corinthians 14:40); and

**Whereas** byFaith magazine has, at times, functioned as more than a source of

official denominational news, engaging in commentary and editorial
perspectives that may not always represent the collective voice of the
General Assembly ( _BCO_ 14-1.5; _WCF_ 31.3); and

**Whereas** many churches and Presbyteries would benefit from an official PCA

publication that focuses on disseminating essential information, press
releases, and updates from ministries and Presbyteries in a way that
prioritizes clarity, transparency, and denominational unity (Ephesians
4:3); and

**Whereas** transitioning byFaith into a press-release-based platform would

enhance trust in denominational communications by ensuring that its
content remains neutral, factual, and reflective of the entire PCA rather
than selective viewpoints; and

**Whereas** such transition and narrowing of focus would be accompanied by a

decrease in expenditures for byFaith, which currently is ~$400,000
( _M51GA_, 2024, Appendix A, 139), assisting the Administrative
Committee in meeting its budget; and

**Whereas** the reputation of Christ and His Church should not be compromised

by unnecessary controversies or unbalanced representation in
denominational communications (2 Corinthians 8:21);

**Therefore, be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] General Assembly of the Presbyterian

Church in America directs the Administrative Committee to transition

byFaith magazine from its current editorial format to a press-releasebased publication, limited to the following types of content:

1. Official press releases from the Administrative Committee and
other denominational agencies regarding actions taken by the

<a id="ga52-p1295"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1297 printed_page=1295 -->

General Assembly, significant initiatives, and denominational
decisions.

2. Ministry updates from PCA churches, Presbyteries, and
denominational agencies, highlighting the work of the church in
missions, discipleship, and service.
3. Announcements and reports from the General Assembly and
Presbyteries, ensuring transparency and accessibility of
denominational proceedings.

**Be it further resolved** that the Administrative Committee develop and

implement a plan to restructure byFaith within one year of the
adoption of this overture, ensuring that the new format aligns with the
PCA’s commitment to truth, transparency, and denominational unity

_Adopted by the Pee Dee Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 26, 2025_
_Attested by TE Mark Horne, Stated Clerk_

##### OVERTURE 45 from the Hills and Plains Presbytery (to MNA)

“Change the Boundaries of Covenant and Hills & Plains Presbyteries”

_**OVERTURE 21 WITHDRAWN BY COVENANT PRESBYTERY**_

**Whereas**, the general guidelines for dividing Presbyteries approved by the 41 [st]

General Assembly of the PCA state “Presbytery boundaries should be
such that her member churches have a common commitment to the

region within their boundaries and a deep commitment to their shared
responsibility to cover the region with the Gospel through evangelism
and church planting.”

**Whereas**, the same general guidelines for dividing presbyteries further state

“A Presbytery should have regional cohesiveness. The Presbytery
should have a geographical spread that is as reasonable as
possible to facilitate planting churches, encouraging fellowship,
participating together in shared ministry and conducting the necessary
business of the Presbytery.”

**Whereas**, the 16 [th] General Assembly of the PCA adopted guidelines for

Presbytery boundaries stating, “A presbytery should have a radius of
2 1/2 hours maximum driving distance.”

<a id="ga52-p1296"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1298 printed_page=1296 -->

**Whereas**, Sebastian County, Arkansas, where Covenant Presbyterian Church

of Fort Smith is located on the farthest western border of Covenant

Presbytery and is adjacent to the border of Hills & Plains Presbytery,
and

**Whereas**, the Session of Covenant Presbyterian Church of Fort Smith wants

to regularly attend Presbytery meetings but finds it difficult at times
due to extended travel, and

**Whereas**, the locations of the stated meetings of Hills & Plains Presbytery are

much closer to Fort Smith than the stated meetings of Covenant
Presbytery, and

**Whereas**, the Session of Covenant Presbyterian Church of Fort Smith,

Arkansas has petitioned Covenant Presbytery and Hills & Plains
Presbytery to so act,

**Now therefore, be it resolved** that Hills and Plains Presbytery, in concurrence

with the Session of Covenant Presbyterian Church of Fort Smith and
Covenant Presbytery, overtures the 2025 General Assembly to transfer
Sebastian, Scott, Crawford, Franklin, Johnson, Logan, and Polk
counties from the geographic bounds of Covenant Presbytery to the
geographic bounds of Hills and Plains Presbytery, and that Covenant
Presbyterian Church of Fort Smith and her Teaching Elder(s) be
received into the membership of Hills and Plains Presbytery.

_Adopted by the Hills and Plains Presbytery at its stated meeting, May 6,_
_2025_

_Attested by /s/ TE Aaron Raines, stated clerk_

<a id="ga52-p1297"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1299 printed_page=1297 -->

##### OVERTURE 46 from the Metro Atlanta Presbytery (to MNA)

“Change the Boundaries of Georgia Foothills and Metro Atlanta
Presbyteries”

**Whereas** Lumpkin County, GA is the farthest northern point of Metro Atlanta

Presbytery; and

**Whereas** it lies geographically in the northeast Georgia mountains, near many

churches within the current boundary of Georgia Foothills Presbytery;
and

**Whereas** Georgia Foothills Presbytery desires to start an RUF campus

ministry at University of North Georgia located in the city of
Dahlonega in Lumpkin County; and

**Whereas** the Session of Creekstone Church, the only PCA church located in

Lumpkin County, is agreeable to come into Georgia Foothills
Presbytery; and

**Whereas** the minutes of the 46 [th] General Assembly established the boundary

of Georgia Foothills Presbytery to be the Georgia counties of Hall,
Barrow, Jackson, Clarke, Oconee, Habersham, and Union counties;
also the counties of Oglethorpe, Elbert, Madison, Hart, Franklin,
Banks, White, Stephens, Rabun, and Towns; and Gwinnett county
north of the defined boundaries, from east to west, of Highway 316,
then Sugarloaf Parkway to Peachtree Industrial Boulevard, then an
imaginary line westward from the intersection of Peachtree Industrial
Boulevard and Sugarloaf Parkway to the Chattahoochee River; and

**Whereas** the minutes of the 46 [th] General Assembly established the boundary

of Metro Atlanta Presbytery to be the Georgia counties of Dekalb,
Fulton, Clayton, Cobb (East of I-75), Dawson, Lumpkin, Coweta,
Heard, Spalding, Troup, Merriweather, Pike, Fayette, Forsyth,
Rockdale, Newton, Henry, and Walton; and Gwinnett county south of
the defined boundaries, from east to west, of Highway 316, then
Sugarloaf Parkway to Peachtree Industrial Boulevard, then an
imaginary line westward from the intersection of Peachtree Industrial
Boulevard and Sugarloaf Parkway to the Chattahoochee River;

<a id="ga52-p1298"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1300 printed_page=1298 -->

**Therefore be it resolved** that Metro Atlanta Presbytery overtures the 52 [nd]

General Assembly as follows:

- That Lumpkin County be transferred from Metro Atlanta Presbytery
to Georgia Foothills Presbytery;

- By adding Lumpkin County to the list of Georgia counties included
within the boundary of Georgia Foothills Presbytery; and

- By removing Lumpkin County from the list of Georgia counties
included within the boundary of Metro Atlanta Presbytery.

_Adopted by the Metro Atlanta Presbytery at its stated meeting, May 6, 2025_
_Attested by TE Randy Schlichting, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 47 from the Great Lakes Presbytery (to OC, AC)

“Erect Ad Interim Committee on Christian Nationalism

**Whereas**, there is a question whether those that hold to the Historic _WCF_

(1647) concerning the magistrate ( _WCF_ 23-3) need to take an
exception because of differences from the version of the _WCF_ adopted
by the PCA (1788); and

**Whereas**, some in the PCA have labeled the historic _WCF_ ’s views concerning

the magistrate as a form of Christian Nationalism; and

**Whereas**, some also hold to a version of Christian Nationalism that they

describe as Ethno-Nationalism, or label themselves as Heritage
Americans; and

**Whereas**, those that hold these teachings profess conformity to the Reformed

Tradition; and

**Whereas**, these have caused confusion, division, and dissension even among

the congregants of PCA churches and affected PCA pastors and
officers; and

**Whereas**, church courts have the responsibility to determine questions of

doctrine seriously, and in general to maintain truth and righteousness,
condemning erroneous opinions which tend to bring injury to the eace,
purity, or progress of the Church ( _BCO_ 11-4);

<a id="ga52-p1299"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1301 printed_page=1299 -->

**Therefore, be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] PCA General Assembly erect and

convene an Ad Interim Study Committee on Christian Nationalism
( _RAO_ 9-1), to study the relationship between Christian Nationalism,
Ethno-Nationalism, and related teachings. Further, the committee
shall advise on whether these teachings and formulations are in
conformity with the system of doctrine taught in the _Westminster_
_Standards_ or where they may diverge from the system of doctrine.

**Further**, the committee shall write a report that gives pastoral guidance when

addressing congregations, new members, and future officers of the
PCA.

**Be it further resolved** that the General Assembly’s Moderator be empowered

as a commission to populate the ad interim committee with three (3)
Teaching Elders and four (4) Ruling Elders.

**Be it further resolved** that the budget for the study committee be set at

$10,000/year and that funds be derived from gifts to the AC designated
for that purpose, with the General Assembly appealing to the various
Presbyteries to fund this initiative via such gifts ( _RAO_ 9-3).

**Be it further resolved** that the Great Lakes Presbytery is committed to offer

a $1,000 gift to assist in funding the work of this Ad Interim Study
Committee.

_Adopted by the Great Lakes Presbytery at its stated meeting, May 3, 2025_
_Attested by TE Ryan Potter, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 48 from the Tennessee Valley Presbytery (to OC, AC)

“Erect an Ad Interim Committee for a Pastoral Letter on Christian

Citizenship and Church-State Relations”

**Whereas,** the duties of Christian citizenship are often disputed and prove

perennially difficult for both elders and church members to discern;
and

<a id="ga52-p1300"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1302 printed_page=1300 -->

**Whereas**, confusion exists about the proper relation between church and state;

and

**Whereas**, due to recent movements, publications, popular teachings, political

strife, and societal stresses, the need for guidance concerning
Christian citizenship and church-state relations has intensified; and

**Whereas**, the _Westminster Standards_, as adopted by the Presbyterian Church

in America and the Preface to the Presbyterian Church in America’s
_Book of Church Order_ provide substantial, biblical guidance
concerning Christian citizenship and the relationship of the church to
the state; and

**Whereas**, an explication of principles may prove more helpful than a focus on

persons or rapidly changing movements, and may be helpful to the
church in decades to come,

**Therefore, be it resolved** that the 52 [nd] General Assembly of the Presbyterian

Church in America direct its moderator to appoint an unfunded ad
interim committee to produce a pastoral letter [45] or short paper
applying the doctrine of the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church
in America to questions of Christian citizenship and church-state
relations for the edification of the Church. The committee will be

composed of four teaching elders and three ruling elders and will set
its own procedures and timelines.

_Adopted by the Tennessee Valley Presbytery at its stated meeting, May 10,_
_2025_

_Attested by TE Ryan Frank Biese, stated clerk_

45 An example of a previous PCA pastoral letter:
[https://www.pcahistory.org/documents/pastoralletter.html](http://www.pcahistory.org/documents/pastoralletter.html)

<a id="ga52-p1301"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1303 printed_page=1301 -->

##### OVERTURE 49 from the Pacific Northwest Presbytery (to OC, AC, CC,

CDM, CTS, Geneva, MNA, MTW, PCAF, RH, RUF)
“Erect ana d interim committee on AI”

**Be it resolved** that the General Assembly erect a study committee of seven

members, consisting of teaching or ruling elders, with an annual
budget of $15,000 (funded by gifts to the Administrative Committee),
tasked to:

a. Write a report by the 54th General Assembly (June 2027) on the

teaching of Scripture and the _Westminster Standards_, with a
particular reference to our anthropology ( _WCF_ 4, 7) and
ecclesiology ( _WCF_ 25), and suggest general principles for the use
or limits of artificial intelligence technology (AI) in the Church;

b. Then apply these general principles to the following suggested

areas:

i. _Pastoral Ministry_ : AI use in preaching, teaching, and
pastoral care
ii. _Church Administration_ : AI use in local churches,
Presbyteries, and the General Assembly, also addressing
security concerns
iii. _Church Discipline_ : suggest criteria for assessing AIgenerated evidence in church courts, especially in
disciplinary cases
iv. _Christian Vocation and Diaconal Ministry_ : a Reformed
approach to work and service amid AI-driven changes in the
workplace and economy, with diaconal strategies for
affected church members

v. _Christian Education:_ implications for PCA institutions
(e.g., Covenant College, Covenant Seminary), especially on
education philosophy and practice
vi. _Medical Ethics_ : boundaries for AI use in medical practice
with particular emphasis in recovering natural function vs.
enhancing human ability beyond natural limitations
vii. _Evangelism and Apologetics_ : strategies to proclaim the
gospel and encourage Christian discipleship in contrast to
AI-influenced ideologies (i.e., transhumanism, etc.)

<a id="ga52-p1302"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1304 printed_page=1302 -->

viii. _Ethical Issues_ : copyrighted materials illicitly used to train

AIs; the impact of over-use of energy and water by server
farms on poor communities; the use of AI to create
simulated relationship and pornography
ix. Any other significant applications the committee finds
during this study.

c. Consult theological, technical, or legal specialists as needed
to aid in this study

d. Propose amendments to the _Book of Church Order_ ( _BCO_ ) or
changes to agency policies, if warranted by their findings.

**Be it further resolved** that all General Assembly permanent committees,

commissions, and agencies submit a report to the study committee
within six months (December 31, 2025) specifying their current and
proposed AI usage to help inform the study committee, and include
that report in their annual reports to the 53th General Assembly (June
2026).

**Rationale**

The revolutionary character of artificial intelligence technology (AI)
is already having an inevitable impact on every aspect of society and therefore
on the Church and individual Christian living. AI presents opportunities and
challenges requiring a serious engagement grounded within our confessional
heritage. Scripture and our Standards affirm God’s sovereignty over all
creation, the sufficiency of Scripture to guide to our faith and practice, and our
call to exercise dominion with wisdom, which includes taking responsibility
for technological innovations and their intended or unintended effects. AI’s
growing influence is wide-reaching, ranging from sermon or educational
preparation tools to major career realignments to potential “deepfake”
evidence, and many other areas. It impacts pastoral and church ministry,
Christian vocation and education, apologetic confrontations with new
ideologies, significant impacts on the quality of human relationships, and other
ethical and theological questions that need attention. There is a serious risk of
losing critical thinking, knowledge, and wisdom as such skills are surrendered
to AI. The PCA must shepherd its members and proclaim the gospel faithfully
amidst these rapid changes, and a modest investment of $15,000 annually can
enable a study to help the church with a biblical guide for their christian
witness under these new conditions.

<a id="ga52-p1303"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1305 printed_page=1303 -->

_Adopted by the Pacific Northwest Presbytery at its stated meeting, May 9,_
_2025_

_Attested by TE Jerid Krulish, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 50 from the Chesapeake Presbytery (to OC)

“Encouragement to Discernment and Compassion regarding
Immigrants”

**Whereas**, Scripture commands believers to submit to and pray for governing

authorities as instituted by God for the good of society and the restraint
of evil (Romans 13:1–7; 1 Peter 2:13–17; 1 Timothy 2:1–2); and

**Whereas**, the moral law of God, revealed in both Old and New Testaments,

calls for the protection of human life and the care of the vulnerable—
including the fatherless, the widow, the sojourner, and the oppressed
(Exodus 22:21; Deuteronomy 10:18–19; Proverbs 31:8–9; Matthew
25:35–40); and

**Whereas**, the _Westminster Larger Catechism_ teaches that the sixth

commandment requires “all careful studies, and lawful endeavors, to
preserve the life of ourselves and others” ( _WLC_ Q.135), and forbids
“the neglecting or withdrawing the lawful and necessary means of
preservation of life” ( _WLC_ Q.136); and

**Whereas**, the Lord Jesus Himself was once a political refugee, having fled as

an infant with His parents into Egypt to escape the violent policies of
Herod (Matthew 2:13–15), and He taught that welcoming the stranger
is integral to the life of discipleship (Matthew 25:35–36); and

**Whereas**, many immigrants in the United States have entered due to political

instability, persecution, or extreme poverty, and live peacefully and
productively in our communities—often without access to legal
status—yet remain vulnerable to exploitation, fear, and injustice; and

**Whereas**, God's law commands compassion and justice for the sojourner:

“You shall not wrong a sojourner or oppress him… You shall treat the

<a id="ga52-p1304"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1306 printed_page=1304 -->

stranger who sojourns with you as the native among you, and you shall
love him as yourself” (Exodus 22:21; Leviticus 19:34); and

**Whereas**, the people of God are themselves former aliens, now brought near

by the blood of Christ, and made one new humanity in Him (Ephesians
2:12–19); and

**Whereas**, Christ commissioned His Church to make disciples of all nations

(Matthew 28:18–20), and Scripture affirms that God's saving purposes
include all people—immigrants, refugees, and sojourners among them
(Isaiah 56:6–8; Revelation 7:9–10); and

**Whereas**, Acts 17:26-27 states that God allows the movement of people “so

that they would seek him and perhaps reach out for him and find him,”;
and

**Whereas**, God is bringing the nations to us, many of whom are Christians or

likely to become Christians, according to missiologists; and

**Whereas**, the 5 [th] and 14 [th] Amendments to the Constitution of our nation were

added to ensure due process protections for all those living within its
boundaries, and informing persons of how they can avail themselves
of those due process protections is good and just;

**Therefore be it resolved** that the Church should view immigrants, regardless

of documentation status, primarily as neighbors and image-bearers
whom Christ has called us to love, evangelize, disciple, and enfold
into His body; and

**Be it further resolved**, that the 52 [nd] General Assembly of the Presbyterian

Church in America encourages all churches, Presbyteries, officers,
and members to exercise gospel-informed discernment and
compassion in responding to their new neighbors, including refugees,
immigrants, asylum seekers, international students, and those
currently without legal documentation; and

**Be it further resolved**, that churches and members of the PCA be exhorted to

respect and pray for civil authorities (1 Timothy 2:1–2), obey just laws
(Romans 13:1–7), and encourage immigrants toward legal pathways

<a id="ga52-p1305"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1307 printed_page=1305 -->

when available, while also recognizing the divine mandate to preserve
life, show mercy, and seek justice (Micah 6:8; Proverbs 31:8–9); and

**Be it further resolved**, that PCA churches be encouraged to offer tangible

care and support to immigrants—including compassionate outreach,
ESL tutoring, vocational assistance, legal aid referrals, and inclusion
in church fellowship—recognizing them as image-bearers of God and
neighbors in need; and

**Be it further resolved**, that the PCA encourages its churches to speak and act

toward the sojourner in ways shaped not by political rhetoric but by
the gospel, remembering our own identity as once-strangers, now
brought near by grace (Ephesians 2:13); and

**Be it further resolved**, that the PCA affirm the call to love, evangelize,

disciple, and enfold immigrants into the body of Christ, remembering
that the Great Commission includes all nations and peoples—
regardless of legal status—as worthy recipients of the gospel of grace
(Matthew 28:19–20; Isaiah 56:8; Revelation 7:9); and

**Be it further resolved**, that when our churches and agencies interact with

individuals who have no valid legal pathway to attain legal immigrant
status, we should exercise pastoral wisdom by advising them to obey
US immigrant laws, which might include voluntarily departing the US
for their home country, o rift his would be life-threatening, to a nation
(if any) that might allow them legal residence; and

**Be it finally resolved**, that the PCA commends to all believers a life of faithful

citizenship—honoring authority, paying taxes, and living peaceably
(Romans 13:6–7; 1 Peter 2:12)—while also walking courageously in
obedience to God (Acts 5:29), bearing witness to the love, justice, and
mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ.

_Adopted by the Chesapeake Presbytery at its stated meeting, May 13, 2025_
_Attested by TE Donald M. Dove_ _, stated clerk_

<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1308 printed_page=1306 -->

***Psalm of Praise – Psalm 100** | TUNE : OLD HUNDREDTH

All people that on earth do dwell,
sing to the Lord with cheerful voice;
him serve with fear, his praise forth tell,
come ye before him and rejoice.

The Lord ye know is God indeed;
without our aid he did us make;
we are his folk, he doth us feed,
and for his sheep he doth us take.

O enter then his gates with praise,
approach with joy his courts unto;
praise, laud, and bless his name always,
for it is seemly soto do.

For why? The Lord our God is good,
his mercy is forever sure;
his truth at all times firmly stood,
and shall from age to age endure.

*** Prayer of Adoration and Invocation**

**Reading of the Law** – Romans 12:3-21 (ESV)

For by the grace given to me I say to everyone among you not to think of
himself more highly than he ought to think, but to think with sober judgment,
each according to the measure of faith that God has assigned. For as in one
body we have many members, and the members do not all have the same
function, so we, though many, are one body in Christ, and individually
members one of another. Having gifts that differ according to the grace given
to us, let us use them: if prophecy, in proportion to our faith; if service, in our
serving; the one who teaches, in his teaching; the one who exhorts, in his
exhortation; the one who contributes, in generosity; the one who leads, with
zeal; the one who does acts of mercy, with cheerfulness.

*Please stand as you are able

<a id="ga52-p1309"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1311 printed_page=1309 -->

Let love be genuine. Abhor what is evil; hold fast to what is good. Love one
another with brotherly affection. Outdo one another in showing honor. Do
not be slothful in zeal, be fervent in spirit, serve the Lord. Rejoice in hope,
be patient in tribulation, be constant in prayer. Contribute to the needs of the
saints and seek to show hospitality.

Bless those who persecute you; bless and do not curse them. Rejoice with
those who rejoice, weep with those who weep. Live in harmony with one
another. Do not be haughty, but associate with the lowly. Never be wise in
your own sight. Repay no one evil for evil, but give thought to do what is
honorable in the sight of all. If possible, sofa ra sit depends on you, live
peaceably with all.

Beloved, never avenge yourselves, but leave it to the wrath of God, for it is
written, “Vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord.” To the contrary, “if
your enemy is hungry, feed him; if he is thirsty, give him something to drink;
for by so doing you will heap burning coals on his head.” Do not be overcome
by evil, but overcome evil with good.

**Unison Confession of Sins** – from Psalm 51

Have mercy on us, O God, according to your steadfast love; according to your
abundant mercy blot out our transgressions. Wash us thoroughly from our
iniquity and cleanse us from our sin! For we know our transgressions, and
our sin is ever before us. Against you, you only, have we sinned and done
what is evil in your sight, so that you may be justified in your words and
blameless in your judgment. Create in us clean hearts, O God, and renew a
right spirit within us. Restore to us the joy of your salvation and uphold us
with a willing spirit. Then we will teach transgressors your ways, and sinners
will return to you.

**The Assurance of Pardon** – 1 John 2:1-2

My little children, I am writing these things to you so that you may not sin.
But if anyone does sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the
righteous. He is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also
for the sins of the whole world.

**The Confession of Faith** – The Nicene Creed

We believe in one God, the Father Almighty, Maker of heaven and earth, of
all things visible and invisible.

And in one Lord Jesus Christ, the only begotten Son of God, begotten of his
Father before all worlds, God of God, Light of Light, very God of very God,
begotten, not made, being of one substance with the Father; by whom all

<a id="ga52-p1310"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1312 printed_page=1310 -->

things were made; who for us and for our salvation came down from heaven,
and was incarnate by the Holy Spirit of the virgin Mary, and was made man;
and was crucified also for us under Pontius Pilate; he suffered and was buried;
and the third day he rose again according to the Scriptures, and ascended into
heaven, and is seated on the right hand of the Father; and he shall come again,
with glory, to judge both the living and the dead; whose kingdom shall have
no end.

And we believe in the Holy Spirit, the Lord and giver of life, who proceeds
from the Father and the Son; who with the Father and the Son together is
worshiped and glorified; who spoke by the prophets; and we believe in one
holy catholic and apostolic church; we acknowledge one baptism for the
remission of sins; and we look for the resurrection of the dead, and the life of
the world to come. Amen.

**Hymn of Trust** – I Hear the Words of Love | TUNE : ST . MICHAEL

I hear the words of love;
I gaze upon the blood;
I see the mighty sacrifice,
and I have peace with God.

‘Tis everlasting peace,
sure as Jehovah’s name;
’tis stable as His steadfast throne,
forevermore the same.

The clouds may come and go,
and storms may sweep my sky,
this blood-seal’d friendship changes not;
the cross is ever nigh.

My love is oft-times low;
my joy still ebbs and flows;
but peace with Him remains the same;
no change Jehovah knows.

I change—He changes not;
the Christ can never die;
His love, not mine, the resting place;
His truth, not mine, the tie.

But as for you, continue in what you have learned and have firmly believed,
knowing from whom you learned it and how from childhood you have been

<a id="ga52-p1312"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1314 printed_page=1312 -->

acquainted with the sacred writings, which are able to make you wise for
salvation through faith in Christ Jesus. All Scripture is breathed out by God
and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in

work.

I charge you in the presence of God and of Christ Jesus, who is to judge the
living and the dead, and by his appearing and his kingdom: preach the word;
be ready in season and out of season; reprove, rebuke, and exhort, with
complete patience and teaching. For the time is coming when people will not
endure sound teaching, but having itching ears they will accumulate for

from where shall come my aid?

who heav’n and earth has made.

He will not let your foot be moved;
your keeper will not sleep.

**Scripture Reading** – Revelation 3:14-22

“And to the angel of the church in Laodicea write: ‘The words of the Amen,
the faithful and true witness, the beginning of God’s creation.

*Please stand as you are able

<a id="ga52-p1313"></a>

“‘I know your works: you are neither cold nor hot. Would that you were either

spit you out of my mouth. For you say, I am rich, I have prospered, and I need
nothing, not realizing that you are wretched, pitiable, poor, blind, and naked.
I counsel you to buy from me gold refined by fire, so that you may be rich,

who conquers, I will grant him to sit with me on my throne, as I also

***Hymn of Preparation** – How Sweet and Awesome is the Place | TUNE : ST . COLUMBA

How sweet and awesome is the place
with Christ within the doors,
while everlasting love displays
the choicest of her stores.

While all our hearts and all our songs
join to admire the feast,
each of us cries, with thankful tongue,
“Lord, why w asia guest?”

“Why was I made to hear your voice,
and enter while there’s room,
when thousands make a wretched choice,
and rather starve than come?”

Twas the same love that spread the feast
that sweetly drew us in;
else we had still refused to taste,
and perished in our sin.

Pity the nations, O our God,
constrain the earth to come;

<a id="ga52-p1314"></a>

send your victorious Word abroad,
and bring the strangers home.

We long to see your churches full,
that all the chosen race

may, with one voice and heart and soul,
sing your redeeming grace.

_Verse 1_

_Verse 2_
For nothing good have I whereby Thy grace to claim.
I’ll wash my garments white in the blood of Calv’ry’s Lamb.

**WEDNESDAY LITURGY**

June 25, 2025

4:45 pm

**Music Leader:** Dr. Scott Finch, Chattanooga Valley Presbyterian Church |

FLINTSTONE, GA
**Liturgist:** TE Seth Hammond, Christ Covenant Church | KNOXVILLE, TN

***Praise Gathering**

Glory be to the Father and to the Son and to the Holy Ghost; a si twa sin the
beginning, is now and ever shall be; world without end. Amen! Amen!

***Prayer of Convocation**

O Lord, our Rock and our Redeemer, you hold the universe in the palm of
your hand and your voice commands the seas according to the purposes of
your will yet you are mindful of us as our Father. Thank you for giving us
breath to experience your new mercies in Christ Jesus. Thank you for your
Holy Spirit who has drawn us in so that we belong. In this hour we pray that
you would help us see more clearly by faith rather than by sight. Give us
strength to worship you in Spirit and in Truth. We pray in the powerful name
of Jesus. Amen.

**Welcome and Salutation**

Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

**Meditation** – Zechariah 10:6-12

“I will strengthen the house of Judah, and I will save the house of Joseph. I
will bring them back because I have compassion on them, and they shall be
as though I had not rejected them, for i am the lord their god and i will
answer them. Then Ephraim shall become like a mighty warrior, and their
hearts shall be glad as with wine. Their children shall see it and be glad; their
hearts shall rejoice in the Lord. “I will whistle for them and gather them in,
for I have redeemed them, and they shall be as many as they were before.
Though I scattered them among the nations, yet in far countries they shall
remember me, and with their children they shall live and return. I will bring
them home from the land of Egypt, and gather them from Assyria, and I will
bring them to the land of Gilead and to Lebanon, till there is no room for
them. He shall pass through the sea of troubles and strike down the waves of

*Please stand as you are able

<a id="ga52-p1317"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1319 printed_page=1317 -->

the sea, and all the depths of the Nile shall be dried up. The pride of Assyria
shall be laid low, and the scepter of Egypt shall depart. I will make them
strong in the Lord, and they shall walk in his name,” declares the Lord.

**Musical Meditation** | COVENANT COLLEGE CHAPEL TEAM

***Call to Worship** – Psalm 118:19-29

**LEADER** : Open to me the gates of righteousness, that I may enter through
them and give thanks to the Lord.
**ALL** **: This is the gate of the Lord; the righteous shall enter through it. I**
**thank you that you have answered me and have become my salvation.**
**LEADER** : The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone.
This is the Lord’s doing; it is marvelous in our eyes. This is the day that the
Lord has made; let us rejoice and be glad in it.
**ALL** **: Save us, we pray, O Lord! O Lord, we pray, give us success! Blessed**
**is he who comes in the name of the Lord! We bless you from the house**
**of the Lord.**
**LEADER** : The Lord is God, and he has made his light to shine upon us. Bind
the festal sacrifice with cords, up to the horns of the altar!
**ALL** **: You are my God, and I will give thanks to you; you are my God; I**
**will extol you. Oh give thanks to the Lord, for he is good; for his steadfast**
**love endures forever!**

**LEADER** : This is the Word of the LORD!

**ALL** **: Thanks be to God!**

***Song of Adoration** – Praise to the Lord, the Almighty

Praise to the Lord, the Almighty, the King of creation!
O my soul, praise him, for he is thy health and salvation!
All ye who hear, now to his temple draw near,
join me in glad adoration.

Praise to the Lord, who o’er all things so wondrously reigneth,
shelters thee under his wings, yea, so gently sustaineth!
Hast thou not seen how thy desires e’er have been
granted in what he ordaineth?

Praise to the Lord, who doth prosper thy work and defend thee!
Surely his goodness and mercy here daily attend thee;
ponder anew what the Almighty will do,
if with his love he befriend thee.

Praise to the Lord, who with marvelous wisdom hath made thee,
decked thee with health, and with loving hand guided and stayed thee.

<a id="ga52-p1318"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1320 printed_page=1318 -->

How oft in grief hath not he brought thee relief,
spreading his wings to o’ershade thee!

Praise to the Lord! O let all that is in me adore him!
All that hath life and breath, come now with praises before him!
Let the amen sound from his people again;
gladly fore’er we adore him.

**Prayer of Adoration & the Lord’s Prayer**

**Confession of Sin and Assurance of Pardon**

**ALL** **: Our Father, we have sinned against you this past week in thought,**
**word and deed. Our secret desires have not been aligned with your holy**
**calling in our lives. We are often selfish, impatient and rude to those you**
**have placed in our lives. We have grown weary of doing good and for**
**this we repent. Cleanse us from our sin and help us to look up to you for**
**healing and strength. Lord have mercy; Christ have mercy, Lord have**

**mercy.**

_A time for silent confession._

**LEADER** : Hear now, O Church, who testify to the world as ambassadors of
Christ Jesus. We have been made clean. As the angel of the Lord touched the
unclean lips of Isaiah and made the man whole in word and deed, readying
him to do his will, he has done the same for us by the cleansing touch of his
Son, Jesus Christ. In him, the disparity between our word and deed is made
right. In him, our unkindness in action and in thought has been forgiven.
Serve now in the purity of the King to speak the Gospel through actions of
justice, mercy, grace, and love.

***Song of Preparation** – In Christ Alone

In Christ alone my hope is found;
He is my light, my strength, my song;
this Cornerstone, this solid ground,
firm through the fiercest drought and storm.
What heights of love, what depths of peace,
when fears are stilled, when strivings cease!
My Comforter, my All in All –
here in the love of Christ I stand.

In Christ alone, who took on flesh,
fullness of God in helpless babe!
This gift of love and righteousness,

<a id="ga52-p1319"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1321 printed_page=1319 -->

scorned by the ones He came to save.
‘Til on that cross as Jesus died,
the wrath of God was satisfied;
for every sin on Him was laid –
here in the death of Christ I live.

There in the ground His body lay,
Light of the world by darkness slain;
then, bursting forth in glorious day,
up from the grave He rose again!
and ashe stands in victory,
sin’s curse has lost its grip on me;
for I am His and He is mine –

bought with the precious blood of Christ.

No guilt in life, no fear in death –
this is the power of Christ in me;
from life’s first cry to final breath,
Jesus commands my destiny.
No pow’r of hell, no scheme of man
can ever pluck me from His hand;
‘til He returns or calls me home –

here in the power of Christ I’ll stand.

**Scripture Reading** – Isaiah 6:1-6

in they earth at king uzzi ah died i saw the lord sitting upon a throne, high
and lifted up; and the train of his robe filled the temple. Above him stood the
seraphim. Each had six wings: with two he covered his face, and with two he
covered his feet, and with two he flew. And one called to another and said:

“Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of hosts;
the whole earth is full of his glory!”

And the foundations of the thresholds shook at the voice of him who called,
and the house was filled with smoke. And I said: “Woe is me! For I am lost;
for ia mama n of unclean lips, and i dwell in the midst o fa people of unclean
lips; for my eyes have seen the King, the Lord of hosts!”

Then one of the seraphim flew to me, having in his hand a burning coal that
he had taken with tongs from the altar.

TE Dennis Louis, Chattanooga Valley Presbyterian Church | FLINTSTONE, GA

***Song of Response** – Be Thou My Vision

Be Thou my vision, o lord of my heart;
naught be all else to me, save that thou art thou my best thought, by day orb y night,
waking or sleeping, thy presence my light.

Be thou my wisdom, and thou my true word;
I ever with thee and thou with me, Lord;
thou my great Father, I thy true son;
thou in me dwelling, and I with thee one.

Be thou my battle shield, sword for my fight;
be thou my dignity, thou my delight,
thou my soul’s shelter, thou my high tow’r;
raise thou me heav’nward, O Pow’r of my pow’r.

Riches I heed not, nor man’s empty praise,
thou mine inheritance, now and always:
thou and thou only, first in my heart,
High King of Heaven, my treasure thou art.

High King of heaven, my victory won,
may I reach heaven’s joys, O bright heav’n’s Sun!
Heart of my own heart, whatever befall,
still be my vision, O Ruler of all.

**Confession of Faith** | ADAPTED FROM THE WESTMINSTER CONFESSION OF FAITH, CH . 18

**LEADER** : Although hypocrites and other unregenerate men may vainly
deceive themselves with false hopes and fleshly presumptions that they are
in God’s favor and in a state of salvation, this hope of theirs will perish.
**ALL** **: Nevertheless, those who truly believe on the Lord Jesus, love him**
**sincerely, and strive to live in all good conscience before him, may in this**
**life be certainly assured that they are in the state of grace and may**
**rejoice in the hope of the glory of God, a hope that shall never make them**
**ashamed.**

**LEADER** : This certainty is not merely a conjectural and probable persuasion
grounded on a fallible hope, but an infallible assurance of faith, founded on
the divine truth of the promises of salvation,
**ALL** **: On the evidence in our hearts that the promised graces are present,**
**and on the fact that the Spirit of adoption witnesses with our spirits that**
**we are God’s children. The Holy Spirit, by whom we are sealed for the**
**day of redemption, is the pledge of our inheritance.**

<a id="ga52-p1321"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1323 printed_page=1321 -->

Holy, holy, holy! Merciful and mighty!
God in three Persons, blessed Trinity! Amen!

safe and secure from all alarms;
leaning, leaning,

oh, how bright the path grows from day to day,
leaning on the everlasting arms. (Refrain)

What have I to dread, what have I to fear,
leaning on the everlasting arms?
I have blessed peace with my Lord so near,
leaning on the everlasting arms. (Refrain)

***Scripture Reading** – Matthew 9:35-38

And Jesus went throughout all the cities and villages, teaching in their
synagogues and proclaiming the gospel of the kingdom and healing every
disease and every affliction. When he saw the crowds, he had compassion for
them, because they were harassed and helpless, like sheep without a
shepherd. Then he said to his disciples, “The harvest is plentiful, but the
laborers are few; therefore pray earnestly to the Lord of the harvest to send
out laborers into his harvest.”

**Confession of Sin**

Almighty God, Lord of the Harvest, forgive us for our inconsistent affections.
At times we sing your praises, hear your invitations into harvest fields, and
follow your call. But when we get discouraged, our mood shifts. Forgive us
for our doubt of your purposes, our disillusionment in our work, our despair
over your people, and our disappointment at your providence. Renew us by

<a id="ga52-p1325"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1327 printed_page=1325 -->

your Holy Spirit today. Restore to us a joy in the work of the kingdom. And
empower us to give all for the honor of our savior, King Jesus, in whose
glorious name we pray. Amen.

**Assurance of Grace** – Matthew 11:27-30

“All things have been handed over to me by my Father, and no one knows
the Son except the Father, and no one knows the Father except the Son and
anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him. Come to me, all who labor
and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you, and
learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for
your souls. For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light.”

<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1328 printed_page=1326 -->

19 The woman said to him, “Sir, i perceive that you area prophet. 20 Our
fathers worshiped on this mountain, but you say that in Jerusalem is the place
where people ought to worship.” [21] Jesus said to her, “Woman, believe me,
the hour is coming when neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem will you
worship the Father. [22] You worship what you do not know; we worship what
we know, for salvation is from the Jews. [23] But the hour is coming, and is
now here, when the true worshipers will worship the Father in spirit and truth,
for the Father is seeking such people to worship him.

24 God is spirit, and those who worship him must worship in spirit and truth.”
25 The woman said to him, “I know that Messiah is coming (he who is called
Christ). When he comes, he will tell us all things.” [26] Jesus said to her, “I who
speak to you am he.”

27 Just then his disciples came back. They marveled that he was talking with
a woman, but no one said, “What do you seek?” or, “Why are you talking
with her?” [28] So the woman left her water jar and went away into town and
said to the people, [29] “Come, see a man who told meal l that i ever did. Can
this be the Christ?” [30] They went out of the town and were coming to him.

31 Meanwhile the disciples were urging him, saying, “Rabbi, eat.” 32 But he
said to them, “I have food to eat that you do not know about.” [33] So the
disciples said to one another, “Has anyone brought him something to eat?” [34]
Jesus said to them, “My food is to do the will of him who sent me and to
accomplish his work. [35] Do you not say, ‘There are yet four months, then
comes the harvest’? Look, I tell you, lift up your eyes, and see that the fields
are white for harvest. [36] Already the one who reaps is receiving wages and
gathering fruit for eternal life, so that sower and reaper may rejoice together.
37 For here the saying holds true, ‘One sows and another reaps.’ 38 I sent you
to reap that for which you did not labor. Others have labored, and you have
entered into their labor.”

39 Many Samaritans from that town believed in him because of the woman’s
testimony, “He told me all that I ever did.” [40] So when the Samaritans came
to him, they asked him to stay with them, and he stayed there two days. [41]
And many more believed because of his word. [42] They said to the woman, “It
is no longer because of what you said that we believe, for we have heard for
ourselves, and we know that this is indeed the Savior of the world.”

**Sermon:** “Lift Up Your Eyes, See the Harvest”

PART IV

CORRECTIONS TO PREVIOUS MINUTES

OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY

**Corrections to the** _**Minutes of the 51**_ _**[st]**_ _**General Assembly**_
The minutes of the commission appointed by the Moderator of the 50 [th] General
Assembly to draft a humble petition to government officials regarding the
protection of minor children from the damages of gender reassignment were
inadvertently left out of the Assembly’s minutes. Their minutes are included
here.

**REPORT OF COMMISSION**
to consider the Motion passed by the 50 [th] General Assembly to:
“Draft a humble petition ( _WCF_ 31-4), which the PCA Stated Clerk shall send
to the President of the United States, the majority and minority party leaders
in the U.S. Senate and House of Representatives, and the Chief Justice of the
United States; and recommend the Presbytery Clerks to send the petition to the
Governors, the majority and minority party leaders in the State Legislatures,
and the Chief Justice of the State Supreme Courts in their regions, condemning
the practice of surgical and medical gender reassignment, especially of
minors.”

The Commission, having been appointed on September 7, 2023 by the
Moderator of the 50 [th] General Assembly, TE Fred Greco, met on September
27, 2023, at 9:07 a.m. virtually.

Commission members present were:
Teaching Elders: Hans Madueme, Nathan Newman, Blair Smith,
Ruling Elders: Bill Whitaker

The Commission meeting was opened with prayer by TE Madueme. TE
Newman was elected chairman.

<a id="ga52-p1330"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1332 printed_page=1330 -->

The Chairman presided over a meeting that considered rules of engagement,
task and scope, responsibility, process, requirements, and next steps of the
commission.

TE Newman motioned to adjourn at 9:57 a.m. and closed in prayer.

/s/ TE Nathan Newman

Chairman

**REPORT OF COMMISSION**
to consider the Motion passed by the 50 [th] General Assembly to:
“Draft a humble petition (WCF 31-4), which the PCA Stated Clerk shall send
to the President of the United States, the majority and minority party leaders
in the U.S. Senate and House of Representatives, and the Chief Justice of the
United States; and recommend the Presbytery Clerks to send the petition to the
Governors, the majority and minority party leaders in the State Legislatures,
and the Chief Justice of the State Supreme Courts in their regions, condemning
the practice of surgical and medical gender reassignment, especially of
minors.”

The Commission met on November 1, 2023, at 11:00 a.m. virtually.

Commission members present were:
Teaching Elders: Bruce Baugus, Hans Madueme, Nathan Newman, Blair
Smith,
Ruling Elders: Jim Lopez, Bill Whitaker

The Commission meeting was opened with prayer by RE Jim Lopez. The
meeting was conducted by Chairman Nathan Newman.

The minutes of the previous meeting were approved.

The writing philosophy and process was discussed at length. Bruce Baugus,
Jim Lopez, and Blair Smith were unanimously appointed primary authors of
Part I of the Humble Petition.

The Commission decided that the deadline for completion of the final version
of the Humble Petition is no later than March 30, 2024.

<a id="ga52-p1331"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1333 printed_page=1331 -->

CORRECTIONS

Chairman Newman adjourned the meeting at 11:58 a.m. and TE Smith closed
in prayer.

/s/ Bill Whitaker

Secretary

**REPORT OF COMMISSION**
to consider the Motion passed by the 50 [th] General Assembly to:
“Draft a humble petition (WCF 31-4), which the PCA Stated Clerk shall send
to the President of the United States, the majority and minority party leaders
in the U.S. Senate and House of Representatives, and the Chief Justice of the
United States; and recommend the Presbytery Clerks to send the petition to the
Governors, the majority and minority party leaders in the State Legislatures,
and the Chief Justice of the State Supreme Courts in their regions, condemning
the practice of surgical and medical gender reassignment, especially of
minors.”

The Commission met on December 15, 2023, at 11:00 a.m. virtually.

Commission members present were:
Teaching Elders: Bruce Baugus, Hans Madueme, Nathan Newman, Blair
Smith,
Ruling Elders: Jim Lopez, Bill Whitaker

The Commission meeting was opened with prayer by RE Jim Lopez. The
meeting was conducted by the chairman.

The minutes of the previous meeting were approved.

The draft of the first part of the letter was discussed. TE Madueme agreed to
serve as the primary author of Part II of the Humble Petition. He will add more
commission members to help draft the second part of the letter.

Chairman Newman adjourned the meeting at 12:05 p.m. and TE Baugus closed
in prayer.

/s/ Bill Whitaker

Secretary

<a id="ga52-p1332"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1334 printed_page=1332 -->

**REPORT OF COMMISSION**
to consider the Motion passed by the 50 [th] General Assembly to:
“Draft a humble petition (WCF 31-4), which the PCA Stated Clerk shall send
to the President of the United States, the majority and minority party leaders
in the U.S. Senate and House of Representatives, and the Chief Justice of the
United States; and recommend the Presbytery Clerks to send the petition to the
Governors, the majority and minority party leaders in the State Legislatures,
and the Chief Justice of the State Supreme Courts in their regions, condemning
the practice of surgical and medical gender reassignment, especially of
minors.”

The Commission met on December 29, 2023, at 10:00 a.m. virtually.

Commission members present were:
Teaching Elders: Bruce Baugus, Hans Madueme, Nathan Newman, Blair
Smith,
Ruling Elders: Jim Lopez, Bill Whitaker

The Commission meeting was opened with prayer by RE Bill Whitaker. The
meeting was conducted by the chairman.

The minutes of the previous meeting were approved.

The draft of the second part of the letter was discussed. RE Whitaker motioned
to approve the full text of the letter as follows. RE Lopez seconded the motion.
It was unanimously approved by the commission.

Dear Leaders of the United States Government:

We, the Presbyterian Church in America, the largest body of confessional
Presbyterian and Reformed churches in North America, consisting of more
than 1,500 congregations and 374,000 members across the United States and
Canada, humbly petition you to protect the lives and welfare of minor children
from the physical, mental, and emotional harms associated with medical and
surgical interventions for the purpose of gender reassignment. Furthermore,
we call upon you to use your positions to promote the health, bodily integrity,
and wellbeing of minors who are suffering from gender dysphoria and related
conditions.

We recognize the growing nationwide distress and concern over
interventionist practices targeting children. While we acknowledge

<a id="ga52-p1333"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1335 printed_page=1333 -->

CORRECTIONS

complexities around these issues, we share those concerns and urgently appeal
to you to protect the Nation’s children. The basis for our appeal is that all
people—young and old, male and female—are created in the image of God
(Genesis 1:26–27; James 3:9). This unique status accords all human beings
with inherent dignity, a dignity that extends to both soul and body. For over
two thousand years, the Christian Church in all her branches has stood on the
teaching that the value of the human body arises from its source, which is from
God, and its purpose, which is to bear God’s image. We believe current gender
reassignment interventions for children are not in keeping with the high value
of human bodies—a value determined not by circumstance, ability, or human
judgment, but by the determination of our wise Creator who constituted each
person a body-soul unity (Genesis 2:7; Psalm 139:13–16).

We also ground our humble petition in God’s love for children. He expressed
his love when the Son of God said, “Let the children come to me; do not hinder
them, for to such belongs the kingdom of God” (Mark 10:14 ESV). Indeed, his
deep love for children is revealed in comparing them to those who are greatest
in the kingdom of heaven. Because of this love, it is a grave sin to harm
children (Matthew 18:1-6). In his kindness, God has provided parents and the
civil magistrate for the protection of children (Ephesians 6:1–4; Romans 13:1–
4).

Until recently, an obligation to protect children has been widely acknowledged
in Western society. The duty to protect children from harm is to be met by
authorities in familial and civic contexts who recognize the vulnerabilities
unique to childhood. While these vulnerabilities can be preyed upon by
powerful external forces, they are also susceptible to the internal confusions
and instabilities often accompanying childhood.

As Christians, we recognize that we live in a fallen world in which some
children and adults experience a perceived incongruence between their
biological sex and their internal sense of gender. These feelings of gender
incongruence cause severe psychological distress often associated with
debilitating anxiety and depression. We genuinely sympathize with the parents
and loved ones of those who experience this kind of suffering—many of them
in our churches.

However, experts disagree on the nature and causes of gender dysphoria.
Persons who try to change their biological sex through the process of
transitioning—including psychotherapy, lifelong hormonal treatments, and
extensive nongenital and genital surgeries—are attempting the impossible.

<a id="ga52-p1334"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1336 printed_page=1334 -->

This reality merely reflects the divine design, as God created human beings
distinctly male and female (Genesis 1:27; Matthew 19:4; Mark 10:6). Since
the sexual binary is rooted in creation and determined by God, it cannot be
changed; therefore, it is not surprising that transition attempts carry many longterm risks. Among these risks, which are often irreversible, include conditions
such as sterility, infertility, cancer, cardiovascular disease, strokes, blood clots,
pituitary apoplexy, pseudotumor cerebri, and diminished bone density.

For children, the stakes are even higher. Since the brain has not yet fully
developed during puberty and adolescence, minor children are not mentally
and emotionally ready to give informed consent to life-altering and nonreversible medical procedures. And with the increase in depression, anxiety,
isolation, and alienation of children and adolescents in the United States and
Canada—exacerbated by the use of social media and, more recently, COVID19 policies—young people often experience fluctuating emotions and internal
confusion. Teenage girls especially have been susceptible to rapid onset
gender dysphoria, a recent phenomenon involving large numbers of teen girls
claiming to have gender dysphoria. The increased diagnosis of gender
dysphoria in girls has been driven by social contagion from current social,
educational, and cultural influences. Children whose minds and personalities
are still developing do not yet possess the perspective or maturity to make
these irreversible decisions; they should be given time to accept their
biological sex, which occurs in the majority of teens allowed to progress
through natural puberty.

Although we respect the expertise of medical professionals, it is striking that
traditionally, medical students had to affirm the Hippocratic Oath, which
includes the commitment: “I will abstain from all intentional wrong-doing and
harm, especially from abusing the bodies of man or woman.” This priority of
not harming others is ultimately grounded in the triune God who is love (1
John 4:8), from whence he calls us to love each other. Reflecting this nature
of God, one of the greatest commandments is that we love our neighbors as
ourselves (Matthew 22:39). As Scripture states, “Love does no harm to a
neighbor” (Romans 13:10 NIV; cf. Exodus 20:13). Providing medical
intervention for the purposes of transitioning does irreversible harm and
injustice to all people, but especially minor children.

For these reasons, we condemn the practice of surgical and medical gender
reassignment, especially of minors, and we humbly petition you to protect the
lives and welfare of minor children.

<a id="ga52-p1335"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1337 printed_page=1335 -->

CORRECTIONS

TE Smith adjourned the meeting at 11:05 a.m. and closed in prayer.

/s/ Bill Whitaker

Secretary

**REPORT OF COMMISSION**
to consider the Motion passed by the 50 [th] General Assembly to:
“Draft a humble petition (WCF 31-4), which the PCA Stated Clerk shall send
to the President of the United States, the majority and minority party leaders
in the U.S. Senate and House of Representatives, and the Chief Justice of the
United States; and recommend the Presbytery Clerks to send the petition to the
Governors, the majority and minority party leaders in the State Legislatures,
and the Chief Justice of the State Supreme Courts in their regions, condemning
the practice of surgical and medical gender reassignment, especially of
minors.”

The Commission met on February 16, 2024, at 11:00 a.m. virtually.

The following Commission members present were:
Chairman Nathan Newman, Secretary Bill Whitaker, RE Jim Lopez, and RE
Bruce Baugus

RE Bill Whitaker opened in prayer.

The Commission approved a statement on January 16, 2024 to be given to the
Stated Clerk’s office in case of media inquiry about the Commission’s letter:
“It is more important than ever to protect children from the harms that come
from rejecting biological sex. God created humanity male and female; persons
who try to change their biological sex are attempting the impossible. Children,
above all, must be protected and given time to progress through natural
puberty. The PCA’s letter affirms the Bible’s care for children as a reflection
of God’s love and asks leaders of the United States government to protect the
lives and welfare of the most vulnerable among us.”

The Commission also permitted the Stated Clerk’s office to make minor
editorial changes in coordination with the Chairman including the final
signature of the letter.

<a id="ga52-p1336"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1338 printed_page=1336 -->

The final set of minutes by the Commission, including these minutes, were
approved.

The Commission finished the business it was assigned by the 50th General
Assembly and RE Jim Lopez adjourned at 11:19 a.m. with prayer. The
effective date of the dismissal of the Commission is February 16, 2024.

/s/ Bill Whitaker

Secretary

<a id="ga52-p1337"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1339 printed_page=1337 -->

**Psalm 133**

Behold how good a thing it is,

And how becoming well
When those that brethren are delight

In unity to dwell.

<a id="ga52-p1344"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=52 pdf_page=1346 printed_page=1344 -->

For it is like the precious oil
Poured out on Aaron’s head,
That, going down upon his beard,

Upon his garments spread.

Like Hermon’s dew upon the hills

Of Zion that descends;
The Lord commands His blessing there,

Ev’n life that never ends.
